options.A: Best eye opening (max 4), verbal response (max 5), motor response (max 6); total range 3–15
options.B: Eye (max 6), verbal (max 4), motor (max 5); range 0–12
options.C: Eye, verbal, motor each scored 0–10; range 0–30
options.D: A single 1–15 consciousness score

exp: The GCS scores best eye opening (max 4), verbal response (max 5), and motor response (max 6), for a total range of 3–15. The other options misassign the component maxima or the range.
hy: GCS: eye 4, verbal 5, motor 6; total 3–15.

stem: Which component of the GCS has been found to be most reflective of outcomes after traumatic brain injury?

options.A: The motor response score
options.B: The eye-opening score
options.C: The verbal response score
options.D: The pupil size

exp: The motor response component of the GCS has been found to be the most reflective of outcomes after traumatic brain injury. Eye opening, verbal response, and pupil size are not the cited best predictor.
hy: GCS motor score best predicts TBI outcome.

stem: A trauma patient opens his eyes to voice, is confused when spoken to, and localizes to painful stimulus. What is his Glasgow Coma Scale score?
exp: Eyes opening to voice = E3, confused speech = V4, and localizing to pain = M5, summing to a GCS of 12. The other totals reflect different component scores.
hy: E3 + V4 + M5 = GCS 12.

stem: Which three variables make up the Revised Trauma Score?

options.A: Glasgow Coma Scale, systolic blood pressure, and respiratory rate
options.B: Heart rate, hemoglobin, and base deficit
options.C: Age, mechanism of injury, and ISS
options.D: Pupil size, temperature, and urine output

exp: The Revised Trauma Score is a physiologic score combining the Glasgow Coma Scale, systolic blood pressure, and respiratory rate (each coded, total 0–12). The other variable sets are not the RTS.
hy: RTS = GCS + systolic BP + respiratory rate.

stem: On the Revised Trauma Score, which physiologic value earns the maximum coded points (4)?

options.A: A systolic blood pressure greater than 89 mm Hg (and a GCS of 13–15)
options.B: A systolic blood pressure of 1–49 mm Hg
options.C: A respiratory rate of 0
options.D: A GCS of 3

exp: On the RTS, the top coded value (4) corresponds to a systolic blood pressure >89 mm Hg (and, separately, a GCS of 13–15 and respiratory rate 10–29). A systolic BP of 1–49 (B), a respiratory rate of 0 (C), and a GCS of 3 (D) all score the lowest (0).
hy: RTS: SBP >89 (and GCS 13–15) earn the max 4 points.

stem: What did the National Study on the Costs and Outcomes of Trauma (NSCOT) demonstrate about care at trauma centers?

options.A: Care at a trauma center was associated with ~20%–25% lower mortality than at a non-trauma center, after adjusting for injury severity
options.B: There was no mortality difference between trauma and non-trauma centers
options.C: Trauma centers increased mortality due to overtreatment
options.D: Mortality benefit was seen only in pediatric patients

exp: NSCOT (>5000 patients, 69 hospitals) showed that, after correcting for injury severity, care at a trauma center was associated with a 20% reduction in in-hospital mortality and a 25% reduction in 1-year mortality versus non-trauma centers. There is a clear benefit (B, C wrong), and it was not limited to children (D).
hy: Trauma-center care: ~20–25% lower mortality (NSCOT).

stem: Which prehospital principles does the chapter emphasize?

options.A: Minimize scene time (target <15 minutes) and recognize that prehospital hypotension (<90 mm Hg), GCS ≤8, and nonextremity firearm injury predict higher mortality
options.B: Spend at least 30 minutes stabilizing at the scene before transport
options.C: Definitive operative repair should occur in the field
options.D: Prehospital hypotension has no prognostic value

exp: Prehospital care emphasizes minimizing scene time (target <15 minutes) with a "load and go" approach, and recognizes that prehospital hypotension (<90 mm Hg), GCS ≤8, and nonextremity firearm injury predict higher mortality with increasing prehospital time. Prolonged scene stabilization (B), field definitive repair (C), and ignoring hypotension (D) are wrong.
hy: Scene time <15 min; prehospital hypotension/GCS ≤8 predict death.

stem: How does ATLS define shock, and what is the most common cause in the injured patient?

options.A: Evidence of end-organ hypoperfusion on physical exam; hemorrhage is by far the most common cause
options.B: A systolic blood pressure below 90 mm Hg; sepsis is the most common cause
options.C: A heart rate above 120 bpm; neurogenic shock is most common
options.D: Loss of consciousness; cardiogenic shock is most common

exp: ATLS defines shock clinically as evidence of end-organ hypoperfusion on physical exam, and hemorrhage is by far the most common cause in trauma — acute blood loss must be excluded before other causes. Hypotension is a late sign (B), and neurogenic/cardiogenic causes are far less common (C, D).
hy: Shock = end-organ hypoperfusion; hemorrhage most common in trauma.

stem: By the ATLS classification of hemorrhagic shock, what blood-volume loss defines Class III?

options.B: Less than 15%
options.D: Greater than 40%

exp: ATLS Class III hemorrhagic shock corresponds to a 30%–40% blood-volume loss; Class I is <15%, Class II is 15%–30%, and Class IV is >40%.
hy: Class III hemorrhage = 30–40% volume loss.

stem: Which statement about the ATLS hemorrhagic shock classes is correct?

options.A: Blood pressure typically remains normal until Class III; Class II shows tachycardia and a narrowed pulse pressure with a still-normal blood pressure
options.B: Hypotension appears in Class I
options.C: Urine output is unaffected even in Class IV
options.D: Class I requires a massive transfusion protocol

exp: Blood pressure typically remains normal until Class III hemorrhage; Class II shows tachycardia and a narrowed (decreased) pulse pressure while systolic BP is still normal. Hypotension does not appear in Class I (B), urine output falls by Class III–IV (C), and Class I needs only monitoring, not MTP (D).
hy: BP stays normal until Class III; Class II = tachycardia + narrow pulse pressure.

stem: What is the ATLS-recommended initial fluid resuscitation, and what crystalloid threshold increases the risk of death?

options.A: 1 L of warmed crystalloid initially, moving to blood-product resuscitation; crystalloid beyond 1.5 L increases the risk of death
options.B: 4 L of crystalloid before any blood products
options.C: Vasopressors first, before any fluid
options.D: Unlimited crystalloid until blood pressure normalizes

exp: ATLS recommends initial resuscitation with 1 L of warmed crystalloid (via two large-bore IVs, an IO, or central access), moving to product-based resuscitation in nonresponders; crystalloid beyond 1.5 L increases the risk of death. Large crystalloid volumes (B, D) and vasopressors-first (C) are incorrect.
hy: Start 1 L warmed crystalloid; >1.5 L crystalloid increases mortality.

stem: What are the major anatomic locations through which life-threatening blood loss may occur in trauma?

options.A: Chest, abdomen, retroperitoneum, pelvis, and long-bone fractures (plus external loss)
options.B: Only the chest and abdomen
options.C: The cranium and scalp
options.D: The bladder and ureters

exp: Exsanguination in trauma occurs through five major sites: the chest, abdomen, retroperitoneum, pelvis, and long-bone fractures (plus identifiable external loss). The narrower options miss the retroperitoneum/pelvis/long bones; cranial and bladder bleeding are not the major exsanguination sites.
hy: Five bleeding sites: chest, abdomen, retroperitoneum, pelvis, long bones (+ external).

stem: The Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma (FAST) assesses which spaces for fluid?

options.A: The hepatorenal (Morison pouch), splenorenal, and pelvic spaces, plus the pericardium
options.B: Only the bladder
options.C: The pleural apices bilaterally
options.D: The retroperitoneal aorta

exp: The FAST examination assesses the hepatorenal space (Morison pouch), the splenorenal space, the pelvis, and the pericardium for fluid, which in trauma is presumed to be blood; it is fast and repeatable. The other single-space answers are incorrect.
hy: FAST: hepatorenal, splenorenal, pelvic, and pericardial windows.

stem: How should a suspected tension pneumothorax be managed during the primary survey?

options.A: It is a clinical diagnosis requiring immediate decompression (needle/finger thoracostomy then tube), not waiting for a chest radiograph
options.B: Confirm with CT before any intervention
options.C: Observe and obtain serial chest radiographs
options.D: Treat with diuretics and oxygen alone

exp: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis (hypotension, decreased breath sounds, poor compliance) requiring immediate decompression (needle or finger thoracostomy followed by a chest tube) — not waiting for imaging. Confirming with CT (B) or serial films (C), or treating with diuretics (D), would be dangerous delays.
hy: Tension PTX: clinical diagnosis → immediate decompression, no imaging delay.

stem: Which clinical triad suggests pericardial tamponade, and what bedside test/procedure evaluates the pericardium?

options.A: Beck triad (hypotension, distended neck veins, muffled heart sounds); ultrasound, with a subxiphoid pericardial window if inconclusive
options.B: Cushing triad; bronchoscopy
options.C: Charcot triad; paracentesis
options.D: Virchow triad; pericardiocentesis only

exp: Pericardial tamponade is suggested by the Beck triad (hypotension, distended neck veins, muffled heart sounds), though it is inconsistently present; ultrasound rapidly assesses the pericardium, and a subxiphoid pericardial window is used when ultrasound is inconclusive (e.g., with a hemothorax). The other triads/procedures are unrelated.
hy: Tamponade = Beck triad; ultrasound ± subxiphoid window.

stem: Which statement about neurogenic shock in the trauma patient is correct?

options.A: It requires a cervical or high thoracic spinal cord injury; if the patient can move the upper extremities, neurogenic shock is much less likely, and hemorrhage must still be excluded
options.B: It is the most common cause of shock in trauma
options.C: A lumbar fracture is the usual cause
options.D: It is diagnosed before excluding hemorrhage

exp: Neurogenic shock requires a cervical or high thoracic spinal cord injury; if the patient can move the upper extremities, neurogenic shock is much less likely — and because hemorrhage is so common in trauma, blood loss must be excluded before attributing shock to the cord injury. It is not the most common cause (B), a lumbar fracture does not cause it (C), and hemorrhage must be excluded first (D).
hy: Neurogenic shock needs a cervical/high-thoracic SCI; exclude hemorrhage first.

stem: Which three derangements form the "lethal triad" (terrible triad of death) in trauma, prompting aggressive rewarming after exposure?

options.A: Hypothermia, acidosis, and coagulopathy
options.B: Hypoxia, hypercarbia, and hypotension
options.C: Hyperkalemia, acidosis, and hypocalcemia
options.D: Fever, tachycardia, and leukocytosis

exp: The lethal (terrible) triad of death in trauma is hypothermia, acidosis, and coagulopathy; hypothermia is countered with blankets, warming devices (e.g., Bair Hugger), a warm room, and warmed fluids during the exposure phase. The other triads are incorrect.
hy: Lethal triad: hypothermia + acidosis + coagulopathy.

stem: Resuscitative thoracotomy in the emergency department provides which therapeutic maneuvers?

options.A: Release of cardiac tamponade, temporary repair of cardiac injury, cross-clamping the descending thoracic aorta, and control of intrathoracic bleeding
options.B: Definitive coronary bypass
options.C: Pulmonary lobectomy
options.D: Repair of the abdominal aorta

exp: Resuscitative thoracotomy provides four therapeutic maneuvers: release of cardiac tamponade, temporary repair of a cardiac injury, cross-clamping of the descending thoracic aorta, and management of intrathoracic bleeding. It is not a definitive bypass, lobectomy, or abdominal aortic repair.
hy: RT: relieve tamponade, repair heart, cross-clamp aorta, control thoracic bleeding.

stem: Which patients have the best survival after resuscitative thoracotomy?

options.A: Those with penetrating thoracic injury and signs of life on arrival (~21% survival), versus blunt trauma without signs of life (<1%)
options.B: Blunt trauma patients without signs of life
options.C: Patients over age 57 with blunt arrest
options.D: Patients with isolated extremity injury

exp: The best survival after resuscitative thoracotomy is in patients with penetrating thoracic injury and signs of life on arrival (~21.3%, vs ~8.3% without signs of life), whereas blunt trauma fares poorly (4.6% with, 0.7% without signs of life). The other groups have poor or futile outcomes.
hy: Best RT survival: penetrating chest + signs of life (~21%).

stem: What does the NTDB review show about resuscitative thoracotomy in older patients?

options.A: Essentially 100% mortality in both blunt and penetrating mechanisms above age 57, favoring bilateral thoracostomy with conservative measures instead
options.B: Improved survival with advancing age
options.C: No relationship between age and outcome
options.D: Best outcomes occur in patients over 70

exp: An NTDB review of 11,380 patients found essentially 100% mortality after resuscitative thoracotomy in both blunt and penetrating mechanisms above age 57, so bilateral thoracostomy tubes with conservative transfusion measures are more appropriate in that group. Survival does not improve with age (B, D), and age clearly matters (C).
hy: RT is futile (~100% mortality) above age 57.

stem: How does resuscitative endovascular balloon occlusion of the aorta (REBOA) work in the decompensating trauma patient?

options.A: A balloon introduced via the common femoral artery is advanced proximal to the level of injury and inflated, shunting blood to the heart and brain while decreasing distal hemorrhage
options.B: A balloon is placed in the inferior vena cava to increase venous return
options.C: It permanently replaces the injured aortic segment
options.D: It is inserted through the radial artery into the ascending aorta

exp: REBOA introduces a balloon via the common femoral artery, advances it proximal to the level of injury, and inflates it — shunting blood to the heart and brain while decreasing distal hemorrhage in the decompensating patient. It is not placed in the IVC (B), does not replace the aorta (C), and is not a radial/ascending-aorta technique (D).
hy: REBOA: femoral balloon proximal to injury shunts blood to heart/brain.
src: Sabiston Ch 17, "Resuscitative Thoracotomy and Endovascular Aortic Occlusion"

stem: What is the central concept of damage control surgery?

options.A: Abbreviated initial operation for hemostasis and contamination control with temporary closure, followed by ICU resuscitation and delayed definitive reconstruction
options.B: Definitive repair of all injuries at the index operation regardless of physiology
options.C: Avoiding any operation until the patient is fully resuscitated
options.D: Routine primary anastomosis of all bowel injuries immediately

exp: Damage control replaces definitive index-operation repair with an abbreviated operation for rapid hemostasis and contamination control plus temporary closure, followed by ICU resuscitation and delayed definitive reconstruction — preventing the deterioration seen with prolonged surgery in physiologically deranged patients. The other options describe the abandoned definitive-first or non-operative approaches.
hy: Damage control: abbreviated hemostasis → ICU resuscitation → delayed reconstruction.

stem: Damage control resuscitation is defined by which principles?

options.A: Permissive hypotension, early balanced transfusion, treatment of coagulopathy, and minimization of crystalloid
options.B: Aggressive crystalloid loading to normalize blood pressure
options.C: Withholding all blood products until the OR
options.D: Routine vasopressor infusion as first-line therapy

exp: Damage control resuscitation is defined by permissive hypotension, early balanced (1:1:1) transfusion, treatment of coagulopathy, and minimization of crystalloid — the resuscitation counterpart to damage control surgery. Aggressive crystalloid (B), withholding products (C), and routine vasopressors (D) are contrary to it.
hy: DCR: permissive hypotension, balanced transfusion, treat coagulopathy, minimize crystalloid.

stem: What component ratio do massive transfusion protocols use to approximate whole blood?

options.A: 1:1:1 — plasma, platelets, and packed red blood cells
options.B: 3:1 crystalloid to blood
options.C: 4:1 packed red cells to plasma
options.D: Platelets only

exp: Massive transfusion protocols transfuse plasma, platelets, and packed red blood cells in an equivalent 1:1:1 ratio to approximate whole blood. The crystalloid ratio (B), red-cell-heavy ratio (C), and platelets-only (D) are wrong.
hy: MTP ratio = 1:1:1 plasma:platelets:PRBC.

stem: The Assessment of Blood Consumption (ABC) score predicts the need for massive transfusion using which four criteria?

options.A: Penetrating mechanism, positive FAST, arrival systolic BP ≤90 mm Hg, and arrival heart rate >120 bpm (≥2 predicts MTP need)
options.B: Age, GCS, lactate, and base deficit
options.C: Hemoglobin, INR, platelets, and fibrinogen
options.D: Mechanism, ISS, RTS, and TRISS

exp: The ABC score uses four criteria — penetrating mechanism, positive FAST, arrival systolic BP ≤90 mm Hg, and arrival heart rate >120 bpm; a score of ≥2 predicts the need for massive transfusion (≈75% sensitivity, 86% specificity). The other variable sets are not the ABC score.
hy: ABC score: penetrating, +FAST, SBP ≤90, HR >120; ≥2 → MTP.

stem: On thromboelastography (TEG), which abnormality is matched to the correct treatment?

options.A: An elevated LY30 (fibrinolysis) is treated with tranexamic acid; a prolonged R time with plasma; a decreased maximum amplitude with platelets
options.B: A prolonged R time is treated with platelets
options.C: An elevated LY30 is treated with cryoprecipitate alone
options.D: A decreased maximum amplitude is treated with vitamin K

exp: On TEG, an elevated LY30 indicates fibrinolysis and is treated with tranexamic acid; a prolonged R time (delayed clot formation) is treated with plasma; and a decreased maximum amplitude (low clot strength/platelet dysfunction) is treated with platelets. The other pairings are reversed/incorrect.
hy: TEG: ↑LY30→TXA; prolonged R→plasma; ↓MA→platelets.

stem: Which statement about tranexamic acid (TXA) in trauma is correct?

options.A: It is a lysine analog that inhibits fibrinolysis and (per CRASH-2) reduced all-cause mortality and death from bleeding without increasing vascular occlusive events
options.B: It is a procoagulant factor concentrate that markedly raises thrombosis risk
options.C: It works by activating plasmin
options.D: It is only useful after 24 hours from injury

exp: Tranexamic acid is a synthetic lysine derivative that inhibits fibrinolysis by blocking plasmin binding to fibrin; in CRASH-2 it reduced all-cause mortality (14.5% vs 16%) and death due to bleeding without increasing vascular occlusive events, making it a standard resuscitation adjunct. It is not a procoagulant concentrate (B), does not activate plasmin (C), and is most effective early, not after 24 h (D).
hy: TXA = antifibrinolytic lysine analog; CRASH-2 lowered mortality, no extra thrombosis.

stem: What does the Monro-Kellie doctrine describe in traumatic brain injury?

options.A: Because the skull is a fixed compartment, any increase in intracranial volume (blood/edema) eventually causes an exponential rise in intracranial pressure, reducing cerebral perfusion and risking herniation
options.B: Cerebral blood flow is independent of intracranial pressure
options.C: The brain can expand freely without raising pressure
options.D: Cerebrospinal fluid production increases to offset any bleeding

exp: The Monro-Kellie doctrine holds that the skull is a fixed compartment, so once compensatory reserve is exhausted, any further increase in intracranial volume (extravascular blood or edema) causes an exponential rise in ICP, reducing cerebral perfusion pressure and risking herniation. The other statements deny this fixed-volume relationship.
hy: Monro-Kellie: fixed skull → added volume → exponential ICP rise.

stem: How is cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) defined, and when is an ICP monitor indicated?

options.A: CPP = mean arterial pressure − intracranial pressure; ICP monitoring is indicated with a GCS <8 and evidence of injury on CT
options.B: CPP = systolic BP − diastolic BP; monitor all GCS 15 patients
options.C: CPP = ICP − MAP; monitor only penetrating injuries
options.D: CPP equals the mean arterial pressure regardless of ICP

exp: Cerebral perfusion pressure equals the mean arterial pressure minus the intracranial pressure (CPP = MAP − ICP); ICP monitoring is indicated in patients with a GCS <8 and evidence of injury on CT. The other formulas/indications are incorrect.
hy: CPP = MAP − ICP; monitor ICP if GCS <8 with CT injury.

stem: Which features are characteristic of an epidural hematoma?

options.A: A lens-shaped (biconvex) collection that may follow a "lucid interval" and is often associated with relatively preserved underlying brain
options.B: A crescentic collection with severe underlying brain injury
options.C: Blood within the ventricles only
options.D: Diffuse thin blood following the brain's contour

exp: An epidural hematoma is a lens-shaped (biconvex) collection (classically from a middle meningeal artery tear) that may follow a "lucid interval" before the hematoma expands, and it is often associated with relatively preserved underlying brain. A crescentic collection with severe brain injury describes a subdural (B); intraventricular (C) and contour-following thin blood (D) are not epidural.
hy: Epidural: biconvex, lucid interval, less underlying brain injury.

stem: How does a subdural hematoma differ from an epidural hematoma?

options.A: It follows the contour of the brain (crescentic) and is commonly associated with severe underlying brain tissue injury
options.B: It is biconvex and respects suture lines
options.C: It never causes mass effect
options.D: It is always managed nonoperatively

exp: A subdural hematoma follows the contour of the brain (crescentic) and is commonly associated with severe underlying brain tissue injury, contrasting with the biconvex epidural. It is not biconvex (B), can cause mass effect (C), and frequently requires operative decompression (D).
hy: Subdural: crescentic, follows brain contour, severe underlying injury.

stem: Which measures are used to manage elevated intracranial pressure after TBI?

options.A: Head-of-bed elevation, mild hyperventilation, mannitol, and hypertonic saline (with craniotomy for a hematoma causing mass effect)
options.B: Trendelenburg positioning and aggressive hypotonic fluids
options.C: Deliberate hypoventilation to raise PaCO₂
options.D: Routine prophylactic craniectomy in all TBI

exp: Elevated ICP after TBI is managed with head-of-bed elevation, mild hyperventilation, mannitol, and hypertonic saline, with craniotomy to evacuate a hematoma causing mass effect. Trendelenburg/hypotonic fluids (B), deliberate hypoventilation (C), and routine prophylactic craniectomy (D) are wrong (craniectomy for severe TBI is rarely needed).
hy: ICP control: HOB up, mild hyperventilation, mannitol, hypertonic saline.

stem: Why is immediate airway control and ventilatory support critical after traumatic brain injury?

options.A: Transient episodes of hypoxia after TBI may increase mortality up to fourfold
options.B: Hypoxia has no effect on TBI outcomes
options.C: Permissive hypoxia improves cerebral perfusion
options.D: Intubation is contraindicated in TBI

exp: Airway control and ventilatory support are critical immediately after TBI because transient episodes of hypoxia may increase mortality up to fourfold; avoiding secondary hypoxic insult is paramount. Hypoxia is harmful (B, C wrong), and intubation is often required (D).
hy: Post-TBI hypoxia can increase mortality up to 4×.

stem: A C1 ring fracture in multiple locations (blow-out of the ring) is which eponymous fracture, and what determines management?

options.A: A Jefferson fracture; management depends on transverse-ligament integrity (hard collar if stable, traction/surgery if unstable)
options.B: A hangman fracture; always requires fusion
options.C: A Chance fracture; managed with a brace
options.D: An odontoid type I fracture; requires a halo vest

exp: A Jefferson fracture is a burst/blow-out of the C1 ring in multiple locations; management depends on the integrity of the transverse ligament — a hard collar if the ligament is intact (stable), and traction or surgery if it is disrupted (unstable). The other eponyms/fractures are misassigned.
hy: Jefferson (C1 burst): collar if ligament stable, traction/surgery if unstable.

stem: How are odontoid (dens) fractures classified and managed?

options.A: Type I (tip) — hard collar; Type II (through the base) — halo vest or surgery; Type III (into the C2 body) — halo vest
options.B: Type I — surgery; Type II — observation; Type III — collar only
options.C: All odontoid fractures are managed with a soft collar
options.D: Type II fractures uniformly heal in a collar without intervention

exp: Odontoid fractures are classified as Type I (tip, managed with a hard collar), Type II (through the base, managed with a halo vest or surgery given high nonunion risk), and Type III (extending into the C2 body, managed with a halo vest). The other management schemes are incorrect — Type II in particular does not reliably heal in a collar.
hy: Odontoid: I collar, II halo/surgery, III halo vest.

stem: A flexion-distraction avulsion of the posterior elements of a lumbar vertebra associated with seat-belt use is which fracture, and how is it managed?

options.A: A Chance fracture, typically requiring surgical stabilization
options.B: A Jefferson fracture, managed with a hard collar
options.C: A compression fracture, never requiring surgery
options.D: A hangman fracture, managed with traction

exp: A Chance fracture is a flexion-distraction injury with avulsion of the posterior elements of a lumbar vertebra, classically from seat-belt use, and typically requires surgical stabilization. The other eponyms (Jefferson, hangman) and "never surgery" are incorrect.
hy: Chance fracture (seat-belt, posterior-element avulsion) → surgical stabilization.

stem: What is the role of methylprednisolone in acute spinal cord injury per the chapter?

options.A: It is a controversial, potentially therapeutic short-course option if started within 8 hours of injury (with increased sepsis/pneumonia at 48 h), decided in consultation with neurosurgery
options.B: It is mandatory for all spinal cord injuries
options.C: It is given only after 24 hours from injury
options.D: It has no associated infectious risk

exp: Methylprednisolone in acute SCI is a controversial, potentially therapeutic short-course option if begun within 8 hours of injury (NASCIS trials showed some motor improvement), with higher rates of severe sepsis/pneumonia at the 48-hour regimen; its use is decided in consultation between trauma and neurosurgery. It is not mandatory (B), is given early not late (C), and does carry infectious risk (D).
hy: SCI steroids: controversial short course within 8 h; infection risk.

stem: When does surgical spinal cord decompression best improve functional outcomes?

options.A: When performed within 24 hours of injury in appropriate candidates
options.B: Only after 2 weeks of immobilization
options.C: Decompression has no effect on outcomes
options.D: Only for stable fractures without deficit

exp: In appropriate candidates, spinal cord decompression has been shown to improve functional outcomes when performed within 24 hours of injury. The other options (2 weeks, no effect, only stable fractures) are incorrect.
hy: Spinal cord decompression within 24 h improves function.

stem: Which imaging modality is the modern study of choice for evaluating the cervical spine for bony injury, and what is MRI's role?

options.A: CT is the modality of choice for bony injury; MRI better characterizes soft tissue/cord injury (e.g., neck pain with normal radiographs)
options.B: Plain radiography is superior to CT for the cervicothoracic junction
options.C: MRI is the first-line study for bony fractures
options.D: Ultrasound is the study of choice for the cervical spine

exp: CT is the modality of choice for evaluating cervical-spine bony injury (superior sensitivity, rapid, and better at the cervicothoracic junction than plain films), while MRI better characterizes soft tissue and cord injury — useful with neck pain and normal radiographs (ideally within 72 hours). Plain films are not superior (B), MRI is not first-line for fractures (C), and ultrasound is not used (D).
hy: CT for bony c-spine; MRI for cord/soft tissue.

stem: The Le Fort classification describes which injuries?

options.A: Three patterns of midface fracture disrupting the maxilla from the surrounding facial bones
options.B: Grades of splenic laceration
options.C: Cervical spine fracture types
options.D: Patterns of pelvic ring disruption

exp: The Le Fort classification describes three patterns of midface fracture that disrupt the maxilla from the surrounding facial bones. It is not a splenic, cervical-spine, or pelvic classification.
hy: Le Fort = three midface fracture patterns.

stem: What is the foremost concern in significant maxillofacial trauma?

options.A: Airway compromise from soft-tissue/bony injury and bleeding, sometimes requiring early intubation before edema worsens
options.B: Cosmetic deformity, which mandates immediate repair
options.C: Imaging of the facial bones before addressing life threats
options.D: Routine antibiotic prophylaxis as the first priority

exp: In significant maxillofacial trauma, the foremost concern is airway compromise from soft-tissue/bony injury and bleeding — early intubation can be lifesaving before edema worsens. Cosmesis (B) and facial imaging (C) are deferred until life threats are managed, and antibiotics (D) are not the first priority.
hy: Facial trauma: secure the airway first (edema worsens fast).

stem: Which finding is an indicator of high risk for blunt cerebrovascular injury?

options.A: A severe midface (Le Fort II/III) fracture or a basilar skull fracture involving the carotid canal
options.B: An isolated distal radius fracture
options.C: A simple nasal contusion
options.D: A normal neurologic exam with no risk factors

exp: Indicators of high risk for blunt cerebrovascular injury include a severe midface fracture (Le Fort II/III), a basilar skull fracture involving the carotid canal, diffuse axonal injury with GCS ≤6, significant cervical-spine fracture/ligamentous injury, and an anterior-neck seat-belt sign. A distal radius fracture (B), nasal contusion (C), and a normal exam without risk factors (D) are not high-risk indicators.
hy: BCVI risk: Le Fort II/III, basilar skull/carotid canal fracture, c-spine injury.

stem: How are penetrating neck injuries managed in the current ("no-zone") approach?

options.A: Hard signs of vascular or aerodigestive injury mandate immediate operative exploration; stable patients without hard signs undergo CT angiographic evaluation regardless of zone
options.B: All zone II injuries are explored and all zone I/III are observed
options.C: CT angiography is contraindicated in penetrating neck trauma
options.D: Hard signs are managed expectantly with serial exams

exp: In the modern "no-zone" approach to penetrating neck trauma, hard signs of vascular or aerodigestive injury mandate immediate operative exploration, while hemodynamically stable patients without hard signs undergo CT angiographic evaluation regardless of anatomic zone. The strict zone-based and "no CTA" approaches (B, C) and expectant management of hard signs (D) are incorrect.
hy: Neck: hard signs → explore; otherwise CT angiography ("no-zone").

stem: In a hypotensive trauma patient with decreased breath sounds and poor pulmonary compliance, which diagnosis must be treated immediately during the primary survey?

options.A: Tension pneumothorax
options.B: Rib contusion
options.C: Simple atelectasis
options.D: Costochondritis

exp: A hypotensive patient with decreased breath sounds and poor pulmonary compliance has a possible tension pneumothorax, which must be decompressed immediately during the primary survey. Rib contusion, atelectasis, and costochondritis are not immediately life-threatening.
hy: Shock + decreased breath sounds + poor compliance = tension PTX.

stem: Which are traditional indications for immediate thoracotomy after chest-tube placement for hemothorax?

options.A: >1500 mL of blood drained immediately, or 150–200 mL/hr for 2–4 consecutive hours, or persistent hemodynamic instability with ongoing transfusion need
options.B: Any blood in the chest tube
options.C: >100 mL drained over 24 hours
options.D: A single reading of 500 mL with stable vitals

exp: Traditional indications for immediate thoracotomy after chest-tube placement are >1500 mL of blood drained immediately, 150–200 mL/hr for 2–4 consecutive hours, or persistent hemodynamic instability with ongoing transfusion need — although these derive from older data and patient physiology should guide the decision ("is bleeding" vs "has bled"). Any blood (B), >100 mL/24 h (C), and a single stable 500-mL reading (D) are not indications.
hy: Thoracotomy: >1500 mL initial, or 150–200 mL/hr ×2–4 h, or instability.

stem: Which statement about penetrating cardiac injury is correct?

options.A: Injuries in the "cardiac box" raise suspicion for cardiac wounds; ultrasound assesses the pericardium, and a subxiphoid window is used when ultrasound is inconclusive (e.g., with hemothorax)
options.B: The cardiac box refers to the upper abdomen
options.C: Ultrasound has no role in penetrating chest trauma
options.D: Pericardial blood is best left undrained

exp: Penetrating wounds in the "cardiac box" raise suspicion for cardiac injury; ultrasound rapidly assesses the pericardium for blood, and a subxiphoid pericardial window is performed when ultrasound is inconclusive or potentially falsely negative (as with a hemothorax). The cardiac box is the precordium, not the abdomen (B); ultrasound is valuable (C); and pericardial blood requires drainage/repair (D).
hy: Cardiac box → ultrasound; subxiphoid window if inconclusive.

stem: How is blunt cardiac injury evaluated, and what rules it out?

options.A: ECG and troponin I at initial workup; if both are negative, blunt cardiac injury is ruled out (a new arrhythmia warrants monitored admission)
options.B: A chest radiograph alone rules it out
options.C: It always requires emergency sternotomy
options.D: Echocardiography is the first-line screening test for all patients

exp: Blunt cardiac injury is evaluated with an ECG and troponin I at initial workup; if both are negative, blunt cardiac injury is effectively ruled out, while a new arrhythmia (commonly tachyarrhythmia) warrants admission for continuous monitoring. A chest film alone does not rule it out (B), it does not always need sternotomy (C), and echocardiography is reserved for instability/structural concern, not first-line screening (D).
hy: Blunt cardiac injury: negative ECG + troponin rules it out.

stem: Which statement about blunt thoracic aortic injury (BTAI) is correct?

options.A: ~80% die before reaching the hospital; the tear typically occurs at the aortic isthmus near the ligamentum arteriosum from rapid deceleration
options.B: Most patients survive to hospital and the injury is benign
options.C: The ascending aorta at the valve is the typical site
options.D: It is caused by slow venous congestion

exp: In blunt thoracic aortic injury, ~80% of patients die before reaching the hospital, and the tear typically occurs at the aortic isthmus near the ligamentum arteriosum as a result of rapid deceleration. Most do not survive to hospital with a benign course (B), the ascending aorta/valve is not the typical site (C), and it is a deceleration—not venous-congestion—injury (D).
hy: BTAI: ~80% die prehospital; tear at isthmus/ligamentum arteriosum (deceleration).

stem: On CT angiography, how is aortic injury graded, and what is a key management principle before repair?

options.A: Grade I intimal disruption, II intramural hematoma, III pseudoaneurysm, IV rupture; impulse/blood-pressure control limits wall stress until repair
options.B: Grades are based on the spleen injury scale
options.C: Immediate full anticoagulation is the priority
options.D: Blood pressure should be deliberately raised before repair

exp: On CT angiography, aortic injury is graded I (intimal disruption), II (intramural hematoma), III (pseudoaneurysm), IV (rupture); a key principle is impulse/blood-pressure control to limit aortic wall stress until repair (pseudoaneurysms require repair due to risk of progression to rupture). It is not graded by the spleen scale (B), anticoagulation is not the priority (C), and BP should be controlled/lowered, not raised (D).
hy: BTAI grades I–IV; control BP/impulse before repair.

stem: How is the unstable versus stable patient with suspected abdominal injury triaged?

options.A: A nonresponder with intraabdominal fluid on FAST requires emergent laparotomy; a responder with normalized physiology can often be managed with CT and selective nonoperative management
options.B: All abdominal trauma requires immediate laparotomy
options.C: CT should be obtained in all unstable patients before any operation
options.D: FAST findings never alter management

exp: A nonresponder to resuscitation with intraabdominal fluid on FAST requires emergent laparotomy, whereas a responder who normalizes physiologically can often undergo CT and selective nonoperative management. Not all abdominal trauma needs laparotomy (B), unstable patients should not be delayed for CT (C), and FAST does guide management (D).
hy: Unstable + FAST-positive → laparotomy; stable → CT/selective nonop.

stem: On the AAST spleen injury scale, a Grade I injury is defined by which findings?

options.A: A subcapsular hematoma <10% of surface area, a parenchymal laceration <1 cm deep, or a capsular tear
options.B: A shattered spleen
options.C: A hilar vascular injury devascularizing the spleen
options.D: A laceration involving segmental vessels with >25% devascularization

exp: An AAST Grade I spleen injury is a subcapsular hematoma <10% of surface area, a parenchymal laceration <1 cm deep, or a capsular tear. A shattered spleen or hilar devascularization is Grade V (B, C), and segmental-vessel injury with >25% devascularization is Grade IV (D).
hy: Spleen Grade I: subcapsular hematoma <10%, laceration <1 cm, or capsular tear.

stem: Which AAST spleen injury grade is defined by a hilar vascular injury that devascularizes the spleen, or a shattered spleen?

options.A: Grade V
options.B: Grade I
options.C: Grade II
options.D: Grade III

exp: AAST spleen Grade V is defined by a hilar vascular injury that devascularizes the spleen, a shattered spleen, or active bleeding extending beyond the spleen into the peritoneum. Grades I–III are the smaller hematomas/lacerations.
hy: Spleen Grade V: hilar devascularization or shattered spleen.

stem: Which statement about nonoperative management of blunt splenic injury is correct?

options.A: Success approaches ~90% at high-volume centers; active extravasation/pseudoaneurysm or high grades (III–V) prompt angioembolization; most delayed bleeding occurs within 72 hours
options.B: Nonoperative management succeeds in under 20% of cases
options.C: Embolization is contraindicated in splenic trauma
options.D: Delayed bleeding essentially never occurs

exp: Nonoperative management of blunt splenic injury succeeds in roughly 90% of cases at high-volume centers; active extravasation/pseudoaneurysm or high-grade injuries (III–V) prompt angiography and embolization, and most delayed bleeding occurs within 72 hours, warranting monitoring. Success is not <20% (B), embolization is used not contraindicated (C), and delayed bleeding does occur (D).
hy: Nonop spleen ~90% success; embolize extravasation/high grade; watch 72 h.

stem: On the AAST liver injury scale, a Grade II injury includes which finding?

options.A: A subcapsular hematoma of 10%–50% surface area or a laceration 1–3 cm deep and ≤10 cm long
options.B: A capsular tear only (Grade I)
options.C: A subcapsular hematoma >50% surface area (Grade III)
options.D: Hepatic avulsion (Grade VI)

exp: An AAST Grade II liver injury includes a subcapsular hematoma of 10%–50% of surface area, an intraparenchymal hematoma <10 cm, or a laceration 1–3 cm deep and ≤10 cm long. A capsular tear alone is Grade I (B), a subcapsular hematoma >50% is Grade III (C), and avulsion is the highest grade (D).
hy: Liver Grade II: subcapsular hematoma 10–50% or laceration 1–3 cm.

stem: Which CT finding distinguishes a Grade III from a Grade I–II liver injury?

options.A: A subcapsular hematoma >50% of surface area (or a ruptured subcapsular/parenchymal hematoma), versus the smaller hematomas/lacerations of Grades I–II
options.B: Only a capsular tear
options.C: An intraparenchymal hematoma <10 cm
options.D: A parenchymal laceration <1 cm

exp: A Grade III liver injury is distinguished by a subcapsular hematoma >50% of surface area (or a ruptured subcapsular/parenchymal hematoma) and deeper lacerations, versus the smaller hematomas/lacerations of Grades I–II. A capsular tear (B), an intraparenchymal hematoma <10 cm (C, Grade II), and a laceration <1 cm (D, Grade I) are lower grades.
hy: Liver Grade III: subcapsular hematoma >50% / ruptured hematoma.

stem: How are most hepatic injuries managed today?

options.A: Increasingly nonoperatively (in stable patients), with angioembolization for active arterial bleeding and ICU surveillance
options.B: With routine immediate hepatic lobectomy
options.C: Operatively in all cases regardless of stability
options.D: With observation only, never embolization

exp: Most hepatic injuries are now managed nonoperatively in stable patients, with angioembolization for active arterial bleeding and ICU surveillance — mirroring the shift away from routine operative management. Routine lobectomy (B), operative management of all (C), and observation-only without embolization (D) are incorrect.
hy: Liver: nonoperative + angioembolization for active bleeding in stable patients.

stem: How is a pelvic fracture with hemodynamic instability initially managed?

options.A: Immediate reduction of pelvic volume with a binder or sheet, followed by angiographic embolization for arterial bleeding and/or external fixation
options.B: Immediate definitive open reduction and internal fixation in the ED
options.C: Observation with serial radiographs
options.D: Diuresis to reduce pelvic edema

exp: A pelvic fracture with hemodynamic instability is managed by immediately reducing pelvic volume with a binder or sheet, followed by angiographic embolization for arterial bleeding and/or external fixation. Immediate ED open fixation (B), observation (C), and diuresis (D) are wrong.
hy: Unstable pelvis: binder/sheet → angioembolization ± external fixation.

stem: What is the "gold standard" approach when a definitive airway is needed in trauma, and what is the surgical airway of choice for laryngeal injury?

options.A: Endotracheal intubation (often rapid-sequence) is preferred; for laryngeal injury a tracheotomy is used because cricothyroidotomy may be ineffective
options.B: A nasopharyngeal airway is the definitive airway in all trauma
options.C: Cricothyroidotomy is preferred for laryngeal injury
options.D: Bag-mask ventilation alone is definitive

exp: Endotracheal intubation (often rapid-sequence) is the gold standard when a definitive airway is needed; for a laryngeal injury, a tracheotomy is preferred because cricothyroidotomy may be ineffective. A nasopharyngeal airway (B) and bag-mask ventilation (D) are not definitive, and cricothyroidotomy is not preferred with laryngeal injury (C).
hy: Definitive airway = ETT (RSI); laryngeal injury → tracheotomy, not cricothyroidotomy.

stem: Why must permissive hypotension be applied cautiously in patients with traumatic brain injury?

options.A: Hypotension reduces cerebral perfusion pressure and worsens secondary brain injury, so the TBI patient needs adequate blood pressure rather than deliberate hypotension
options.B: TBI patients tolerate hypotension better than other trauma patients
options.C: Permissive hypotension improves intracranial pressure
options.D: Blood pressure is irrelevant in TBI

exp: Although permissive hypotension is part of damage control resuscitation, it must be applied cautiously in TBI because hypotension reduces cerebral perfusion pressure and worsens secondary brain injury — the TBI patient needs adequate blood pressure. TBI patients do not tolerate hypotension better (B), hypotension does not help ICP (C), and blood pressure is highly relevant (D).
hy: In TBI, avoid permissive hypotension — maintain CPP.

stem: What is the ATLS secondary survey?

options.A: A thorough head-to-toe examination and history performed after the primary survey in stable patients, including log-roll of the back, rectal exam, and distal pulse/perfusion checks
options.B: The first 10 seconds of patient contact
options.C: Limited to a neurologic examination only
options.D: Performed before the primary survey in unstable patients

exp: The ATLS secondary survey is a thorough head-to-toe examination and history performed after the primary survey in stable patients, including a log-roll to inspect the back/spine, a rectal exam, and assessment of distal pulses/perfusion (e.g., ankle-brachial indices). It is not the first seconds of contact (B), not neuro-only (C), and is performed after—not before—the primary survey (D).
hy: Secondary survey = head-to-toe exam + history after the primary survey.

stem: Which intraoperative physiologic derangements drive the decision to use a damage-control (abbreviated) operation?

options.A: Progressive hypothermia, coagulopathy, and metabolic acidosis
options.B: Mild hypertension and bradycardia
options.C: Normal temperature and pH with controlled bleeding
options.D: Isolated hyperglycemia

exp: The decision to use a damage-control (abbreviated) operation is driven by progressive intraoperative physiologic derangement — hypothermia, coagulopathy, and metabolic acidosis (the lethal triad). Mild hypertension/bradycardia (B), normal physiology (C), and isolated hyperglycemia (D) are not the triggers.
hy: Damage control triggers: hypothermia, coagulopathy, acidosis.

stem: Under what condition should resuscitative thoracotomy be performed?

options.A: Only in locations with readily available surgical support for definitive repair if return of spontaneous circulation is achieved
options.B: In any setting, regardless of surgical backup
options.C: Only in the operating room after full imaging
options.D: Only after the patient is hemodynamically stable

exp: Resuscitative thoracotomy should only be performed in locations with readily available surgical support for definitive repair of thoracic injuries if return of spontaneous circulation is achieved. It is not appropriate in any setting without backup (B), is an ED (not OR-after-imaging) procedure (C), and is for arrest/peri-arrest, not stable patients (D).
hy: Perform RT only where definitive surgical repair is available.

stem: A stable blunt-trauma patient's CT shows a contrast "blush" within the splenic parenchyma. What does this represent and prompt?

options.A: Active extravasation (or pseudoaneurysm), prompting angiography and embolization rather than immediate splenectomy in the stable patient
options.B: A normal finding requiring no action
options.C: A mandatory indication for immediate splenectomy regardless of stability
options.D: Contrast in the renal collecting system, which is ignored

exp: A contrast "blush" within injured splenic parenchyma represents active extravasation (or a pseudoaneurysm); in a hemodynamically stable patient this prompts angiography and embolization rather than immediate splenectomy. It is not a normal finding (B), does not mandate splenectomy in the stable patient (C), and is intrasplenic rather than in the collecting system (D).
hy: Splenic contrast blush in a stable patient → angioembolization.

stem: A 6-year-old falls and a forearm radiograph shows a cortical break on one side of the radius with bowing of the opposite cortex. What is this fracture called?

options.A: Greenstick fracture
options.B: Transverse fracture
options.C: Pathologic fracture
options.D: Segmental fracture

exp: A greenstick fracture is an incomplete cortical disruption: the bone breaks through one cortex while the opposite cortex only bows (plastic deformation or a buckle). It is unique to children because of their thick periosteum and increased bone plasticity. A transverse fracture (B) is a complete break perpendicular to the long axis, a pathologic fracture (C) occurs through diseased bone, and a segmental fracture (D) breaks at two levels.
hy: Greenstick = one cortex broken, the other only bowed (pediatric).

stem: A child sustains an axial load to the wrist and the distal radial cortex shows a small circumferential buckle without a discrete fracture line. What is the correct term?

options.A: Buckle (torus) fracture
options.B: Spiral fracture
options.C: Open fracture
options.D: Insufficiency fracture

exp: A buckle (torus) fracture results from an axial load that crushes the cortex into a small circumferential bulge without a discrete fracture line, and is classic in children's distal radius. A spiral fracture (B) comes from torsion, an open fracture (C) communicates with the environment, and an insufficiency fracture (D) occurs in weakened bone from low energy. The intact periosteum makes the buckle fracture stable.
hy: Buckle/torus = axial crush buckling the cortex, no fracture line.

stem: A pediatric fracture involves the growth plate. Which classification system is used to describe it?

options.A: Salter-Harris classification
options.B: Gustilo-Anderson classification
options.C: Young and Burgess classification
options.D: Denis three-column classification

exp: Physeal (growth-plate) fractures are described by the Salter-Harris classification, which matters because disruption of an ossification center can cause future growth arrest, length discrepancy, or angular deformity. Gustilo-Anderson (B) grades open fractures, Young-Burgess (C) grades pelvic ring injuries, and Denis (D) describes spinal column injury. A high index of suspicion is needed because radiographs can be normal.
hy: Growth-plate fractures → Salter-Harris classification.

stem: An 80-year-old woman fractures her distal radius after a fall from standing height onto osteoporotic bone. How is this injury best termed?

options.A: Insufficiency (fragility) fracture
options.B: High-energy comminuted fracture
options.C: Stress fracture from overuse
options.D: Avulsion fracture

exp: A fracture through osteoporotic bone from a low-energy mechanism (a fall from standing height) is an insufficiency or fragility fracture — technically a pathologic fracture, but conventionally named for the weakened bone. Hip, vertebral compression, and distal radius fractures in older adults are classic examples. A high-energy comminuted fracture (B) requires much greater force, a stress fracture (C) is from repetitive overuse, and an avulsion fracture (D) is a ligament/tendon pulling off bone.
hy: Low-energy fracture in osteoporotic bone = fragility/insufficiency fracture.

stem: Which statement about the definitive grading of an open fracture is correct?

options.A: The final Gustilo grade is assigned in the operating room after thorough debridement and assessment of the soft tissue envelope
options.B: The grade is fixed at the scene by the size of the skin laceration alone
options.C: Open fractures never require emergent treatment
options.D: The grade is determined solely by the fracture pattern on radiograph

exp: Although the skin laceration is the obvious clue, the true Gustilo grade is assigned in the operating room after thorough debridement reveals the full extent of muscle devitalization and periosteal stripping. The scene/ED size of the wound (B) underestimates deep injury and has only 53%–60% interobserver reliability. Open fractures do require urgent care (C is wrong), and grading depends on soft tissue, not the fracture pattern alone (D).
hy: Final open-fracture grading happens in the OR after debridement.

stem: How does the reduction goal for a displaced intraarticular fracture differ from that of a diaphyseal (shaft) fracture?

options.A: Intraarticular fractures require anatomic reduction and rigid fixation; a shaft fracture needs only restoration of alignment and length
options.B: Both require only restoration of alignment and length
options.C: Shaft fractures require a perfectly smooth surface; intraarticular ones do not
options.D: Neither requires reduction if the patient is elderly

exp: A displaced intraarticular fracture must be anatomically reduced and rigidly fixed because the joint surface has to be perfectly smooth to avoid posttraumatic arthritis. A diaphyseal (shaft) fracture, by contrast, needs only restoration of mechanical alignment, length, and rotation — it can heal with an imperfect appearance if mechanically sound. Option B ignores the articular requirement, C reverses the two, and D is false (reduction is still needed).
hy: Joint surfaces need anatomic reduction; shafts need alignment/length.

stem: A long bone subjected to a torsional (twisting) force most characteristically produces which fracture pattern?

options.A: Spiral fracture
options.B: Transverse fracture
options.C: Greenstick fracture
options.D: Compression fracture

exp: Bone is weakest in torsion, so a twisting force produces a spiral fracture. Transverse and oblique fractures (B) result from directly applied bending/off-axis forces, a greenstick (C) is a pediatric incomplete fracture, and a compression fracture (D) follows axial loading of cancellous bone (e.g., vertebra).
hy: Torsion → spiral fracture (bone is weakest in twisting).

stem: When a bending moment is applied to a long bone and a "butterfly" (wedge) fragment forms, where does the bone fail first?

options.A: On the tension (convex) side, with the fracture then propagating toward the compressed side
options.B: On the compression (concave) side first
options.C: Simultaneously across the entire cortex
options.D: Only at the physis

exp: Under a bending moment, the convex (tension) side fails first because bone is weaker in tension; as the fracture propagates toward the compressed concave side it splits around the compressed bone, creating the wedge/butterfly fragment. Failing first on the compression side (B) is backwards, simultaneous failure (C) does not occur, and the physis (D) is irrelevant to butterfly formation.
hy: Butterfly fragment: bone fails on the tension (convex) side first.

stem: A ligament is completely ruptured, producing significant instability of the joint. How is this graded?

options.A: Grade III sprain
options.B: Grade I sprain
options.C: Grade II sprain
options.D: Strain

exp: A Grade III sprain is a complete ligament rupture producing significant joint instability; avulsion fractures at a ligament insertion fall here too. Grade I (B) is a stretch without instability (e.g., simple ankle sprain), Grade II (C) is a partial rupture with minor instability, and a strain (D) is a muscle/tendon — not ligament — injury.
hy: Grade III sprain = complete ligament rupture with instability.

stem: Which statement about a strain is correct?

options.A: It is an injury to a muscle or tendon, usually from overuse, treated with rest, ice, compression, and elevation
options.B: It is a complete ligament rupture requiring emergent surgery
options.C: It is a disruption of the growth plate
options.D: It is a fracture through pathologic bone

exp: A strain is an injury to a muscle or tendon, usually from overuse, and is managed with rest, ice, compression, and elevation; a rupture needs more urgent (often operative) care. A complete ligament rupture (B) is a sprain, a growth-plate disruption (C) is a physeal fracture, and a fracture through pathologic bone (D) is unrelated. Proper joint positioning matters so a healing tendon scars in a functional position.
hy: Strain = muscle/tendon injury; RICE for the mainstay.

stem: A severe lumbar disc herniation causes urinary retention, saddle anesthesia, and reduced rectal tone. What does this represent?

options.A: Cauda equina syndrome — a surgical emergency
options.B: A simple sprain managed with rest
options.C: A stable compression fracture
options.D: A self-limited radiculopathy needing only physical therapy

exp: Severe lumbar disc herniation can impinge the cauda equina, producing urinary retention (early), saddle anesthesia, lower-extremity weakness, and reduced rectal tone (late) — cauda equina syndrome, a surgical emergency requiring urgent MRI and decompression. A simple sprain (B), a stable compression fracture (C), and ordinary radiculopathy (D) do not threaten the cauda equina. Most disc herniations resolve with physical therapy, but this presentation does not.
hy: Retention + saddle anesthesia + low rectal tone = cauda equina = emergency.

stem: Among the methods of skeletal stabilization, which is the only form of fixation that does NOT attach directly to bone?

options.A: Splinting
options.B: Skeletal traction
options.C: Intramedullary nailing
options.D: External fixation

exp: Splinting is the easiest, most common form of limb stabilization and the only one that does not attach directly to bone, which is why it carries no implant-related infection risk. Skeletal traction (B), intramedullary nailing (C), and external fixation (D) all create a pathway to or contact with bone. The closer the fixation lies to the bone, the better the control but the greater the infection risk.
hy: Splinting is the only fixation not directly attached to bone.

stem: Skeletal traction is best described by which statement?

options.A: A transosseous pin with a traction weight, used for temporary stabilization of femur and pelvis fractures
options.B: A plaster or fiberglass device that accommodates soft tissue swelling
options.C: A nail placed inside the medullary canal for definitive fixation
options.D: Plates and screws applied to articular fractures

exp: Per Table 19.2, skeletal traction is a transosseous pin with an attached traction weight, used for temporary stabilization of femur and pelvis fractures; it helps control pain and bleeding and maintains length. A plaster/fiberglass device (B) is a splint, a medullary nail (C) is intramedullary nailing, and plates/screws for articular fractures (D) describe ORIF. Its downside is indirect bone control and loss of patient mobilization.
hy: Skeletal traction = transosseous pin + weight; temporizes femur/pelvis.

stem: Which statement comparing intramedullary nailing (IMN) and open reduction internal fixation (ORIF) is correct?

options.A: ORIF allows direct reduction and absolute stability but causes larger soft tissue disruption; IMN gives excellent mechanical stability with limited soft tissue disruption
options.B: IMN requires the largest soft tissue dissection of any method
options.C: ORIF is preferred for diaphyseal long-bone fractures because it disturbs less soft tissue
options.D: Intraarticular fractures are best treated with closed intramedullary nailing

exp: Per Table 19.2, ORIF (plates/screws) permits direct reduction and absolute stability — ideal for articular and upper-extremity long-bone fractures — at the cost of larger soft tissue disruption, whereas IMN gives excellent mechanical stability with limited soft tissue disruption for long-bone shafts. IMN does not require the largest dissection (B), ORIF is not preferred for simple diaphyseal fractures over nailing (C), and intraarticular fractures need direct visualization, not closed nailing (D).
hy: ORIF = direct/absolute stability (more dissection); IMN = stable, soft-tissue-sparing for shafts.

stem: Following the ATLS "life before limb" principle, how should a hemodynamically unstable multiply-injured patient be approached?

options.A: Address airway, breathing, and circulation first, and assume hemorrhagic shock until proven otherwise
options.B: Reduce all fractures before any resuscitation
options.C: Obtain MRI of every injured extremity before resuscitation
options.D: Assume neurogenic shock as the default cause of hypotension

exp: ATLS dictates "life before limb": the airway, breathing, and circulation are addressed before any orthopedic evaluation, and a hemodynamically unstable patient is assumed to be in hemorrhagic shock until proven otherwise, prompting a search of chest, abdomen, retroperitoneum, pelvis, and extremities. Reducing fractures first (B) or imaging extremities before resuscitation (C) violates the primary survey. Neurogenic shock (D) is a diagnosis of exclusion after hemorrhage.
hy: Life before limb: ABCs first; unstable = hemorrhagic shock until proven otherwise.

stem: A patient who fell from a height lands on his feet. Which constellation of associated injuries should be specifically sought?

options.A: Calcaneus, tibial plateau, hip/acetabular, and vertebral burst fractures
options.B: Distal radius and posterior elbow dislocation only
options.C: Axillary nerve injury and shoulder dislocation
options.D: Isolated clavicle fracture

exp: A fall from height transmits axial load up the skeleton, so feet-first landings classically cause calcaneus, tibial plateau, hip/acetabular, and vertebral burst fractures (the "feet-to-spine" pattern). Distal radius/posterior elbow (B) follow a fall on an outstretched hand, axillary nerve/shoulder dislocation (C) follow shoulder trauma, and an isolated clavicle fracture (D) is not the pattern. Recognizing the mechanism prompts a targeted search.
hy: Fall from height → calcaneus, tibial plateau, hip, vertebral burst.

stem: A restrained driver sustains a dashboard (axial femur) injury in a head-on collision. Which injury and at-risk structure are classically associated?

options.A: Posterior hip dislocation with sciatic (peroneal division) nerve injury
options.B: Anterior shoulder dislocation with axillary nerve injury
options.C: Supracondylar humerus fracture with brachial artery injury
options.D: Open-book pelvic fracture with bladder rupture

exp: A dashboard/axial-load injury to the flexed knee drives the femoral head posteriorly, producing a posterior hip dislocation that classically endangers the sciatic nerve (peroneal division — foot drop). Anterior shoulder dislocation/axillary nerve (B) and supracondylar humerus/brachial artery (C) are different mechanisms, and an open-book pelvis/bladder rupture (D) is an AP-compression pattern. The limb is held flexed, internally rotated, and shortened.
hy: Dashboard injury → posterior hip dislocation → sciatic (peroneal) nerve.

stem: What is the correct trauma-room handling of a sterile dressing placed over an open fracture wound?

options.A: Leave it in place until the patient reaches the operating room, as this lowers infection rates versus repeated redressing
options.B: Remove and replace it every 2 hours in the trauma bay
options.C: Remove it and leave the wound open to air
options.D: Replace it with a povidone-iodine soak only after the patient is in the ICU

exp: A sterile dressing applied to an open fracture in the trauma room should be left undisturbed until the patient reaches the operating room, because this practice lowers infection rates compared with repeated redressing in the trauma bay. Redressing every 2 hours (B) or exposing the wound (C) increases contamination, and waiting until the ICU for a povidone soak (D) is not the protocol. Emergent care still includes antibiotics, tetanus prophylaxis, gross debridement, and irrigation.
hy: Don't redress an open fracture wound until the OR.

stem: An ecchymotic, fluctuant lesion over the greater trochanter representing a closed subcutaneous degloving injury is known as what, and what is it associated with?

options.A: A Morel-Lavallée lesion, frequently associated with acetabular fractures
options.B: A Morison pouch collection, associated with splenic injury
options.C: A sail sign, associated with elbow fractures
options.D: A dimple sign, associated with knee dislocation

exp: The Morel-Lavallée lesion is a closed subcutaneous degloving injury — an ecchymotic, fluctuant collection over the greater trochanter — and is frequently associated with acetabular fractures. Morison pouch (B) is the hepatorenal FAST window, the sail sign (C) is an elbow fat-pad finding, and the dimple sign (D) suggests an irreducible knee dislocation. Recognition matters because it signals significant underlying energy and complicates surgical incisions.
hy: Morel-Lavallée = subcutaneous degloving over the trochanter; think acetabular fracture.

stem: On a lateral elbow radiograph after trauma, the presence of a posterior fat pad sign most strongly indicates what?

options.A: An occult intraarticular fracture (hemarthrosis displacing the fat pads)
options.B: A normal finding requiring no further evaluation
options.C: A complete radial nerve transection
options.D: Chronic osteomyelitis

exp: A posterior fat pad sign (and the "sail sign" anteriorly) appears when hemarthrosis from an intraarticular fracture extrudes the fat pads from the coronoid/olecranon fossae; the anterior fat pad can be normal, but a visible posterior fat pad strongly suggests occult fracture and warrants CT if clinically indicated. It is not a normal finding (B), does not indicate nerve transection (C), and is not a sign of chronic osteomyelitis (D).
hy: Posterior fat pad/sail sign on lateral elbow = occult intraarticular fracture.

stem: A normal Bohler angle on a lateral foot radiograph is which range, and what does a decrease suggest?

options.A: 20–40 degrees; a decrease suggests a calcaneal fracture with depression of the posterior facet
options.B: 60–90 degrees; a decrease suggests a normal variant
options.C: 5–15 degrees; a decrease suggests a talar neck fracture
options.D: 90–110 degrees; a decrease suggests a stress fracture

exp: Bohler angle, measured on the lateral foot film, is normally 20–40 degrees; a decrease indicates a calcaneal fracture with depression of the posterior facet. The other ranges (B, C, D) are incorrect, and a flattened angle — not an increased one — signals injury. Comparison views of the uninjured foot help when in doubt.
hy: Normal Bohler angle 20–40°; flattening = depressed calcaneal fracture.

stem: A knee dislocation is identified. What is the reported rate of associated vascular (popliteal) injury, and how should the limb be managed?

options.A: 18%–30%; vascular injury is assumed until proven otherwise, with serial ABIs and a low threshold for CT/MR angiography
options.B: Under 1%; vascular imaging is never indicated
options.C: About 50%; immediate amputation is mandatory
options.D: About 5%; a single normal pulse fully excludes injury

exp: Traumatic knee dislocation carries an 18%–30% rate of popliteal artery injury, so vascular injury is assumed until proven otherwise, with serial vascular exams/ABIs and a low threshold for CT or MR angiography. The rate is far above 1% (B), amputation is not automatic (C), and a single normal pulse does not exclude injury (D) because collateral flow can mask occlusion. The popliteal artery is tethered at the adductor hiatus and soleus.
hy: Knee dislocation → 18–30% popliteal injury; image, don't rely on pulses.

stem: In a patient with a femoral shaft fracture, what is the approximate incidence of an associated ipsilateral femoral neck fracture?

options.A: As high as 9%, warranting dedicated intraoperative fluoroscopic views
options.B: Essentially 0%, so screening is unnecessary
options.C: About 50% in all patients
options.D: Roughly 80%, making it the most common associated injury

exp: The incidence of an ipsilateral femoral neck fracture in patients with a femoral shaft fracture is as high as 9%, and these occult injuries are easily missed on preoperative radiographs/CT — hence dedicated intraoperative fluoroscopic rotation views. It is not 0% (B), not 50% (C), and not 80% (D). Missing it converts a relatively simple problem into a far more morbid one.
hy: ~9% of femoral shaft fractures have an occult ipsilateral femoral neck fracture.

stem: Why is circumferential casting in the acute setting potentially dangerous, and what technique mitigates it?

options.A: Swelling increases for 48–72 hours and a circular cast can cause pressure necrosis or compartment syndrome; bi-valving the cast allows for swelling
options.B: Casts are too loose acutely; they should be reinforced with extra plaster
options.C: Swelling resolves within 2 hours, so casts are always safe immediately
options.D: Casts should be applied tighter to prevent any swelling

exp: Injured-extremity swelling increases for 48–72 hours, so an acute circumferential cast can become constrictive and cause pressure necrosis or compartment syndrome; bi-valving (cutting the cast longitudinally on two sides) lets the limb swell while maintaining reduction. Reinforcing a cast (B), assuming swelling resolves in 2 hours (C), or applying it tighter (D) all increase the danger. Acute injuries are usually splinted first.
hy: Acute circumferential casts risk compartment syndrome — bivalve to allow 48–72 h of swelling.

stem: Which statement about traction weight limits is correct?

options.A: Skin traction over ~8 pounds for an extended period damages skin, whereas skeletal traction can carry up to ~10% of body weight
options.B: Skin traction can carry up to 50% of body weight safely
options.C: Skeletal traction is limited to 2 pounds
options.D: Skin and skeletal traction tolerate identical weights

exp: Skin (cutaneous) traction over roughly 8 pounds for any extended period damages skin, limiting it to geriatric hip fractures and pediatric injuries; skeletal traction can carry up to ~10% of body weight on a lower-extremity pin. Skin traction cannot bear 50% of body weight (B), skeletal traction is not limited to 2 pounds (C), and the two do not tolerate identical weights (D).
hy: Skin traction ≤ ~8 lb; skeletal traction up to ~10% body weight.

stem: When placing skeletal traction pins, which directions are correct for avoiding neurovascular structures?

options.A: Distal femur — pass the pin medial to lateral (avoid the femoral vessels in the adductor hiatus); proximal tibia — pass it lateral to medial (avoid the common peroneal nerve)
options.B: Distal femur — lateral to medial; proximal tibia — medial to lateral
options.C: Both pins are passed anterior to posterior regardless of site
options.D: Pin direction is irrelevant to neurovascular safety

exp: To avoid neurovascular injury, the distal femoral pin is passed medial to lateral (keeping the entry away from the femoral artery/nerve in the adductor hiatus), and the proximal tibial pin is passed lateral to medial (away from the common peroneal nerve around the fibular head). Reversing these directions (B) risks those structures, an anteroposterior pass (C) is not the rule, and direction is far from irrelevant (D). The principle is to start from the side nearest the known at-risk structure.
hy: Distal femur pin medial→lateral; proximal tibia pin lateral→medial.

stem: In evaluating a lower extremity for possible vascular injury, which ankle-brachial index value warrants further workup?

options.A: An ABI less than 0.9
options.B: An ABI less than 0.5 only
options.C: An ABI greater than 1.2
options.D: The ABI has no role in extremity trauma

exp: An ankle-brachial index below 0.9 in a traumatized lower extremity warrants further workup for vascular injury. An ABI <0.5 threshold (B) would miss significant injuries, an ABI >1.2 (C) is not the cutoff, and the ABI is in fact a recommended tool (D is wrong). CT angiography has largely supplanted conventional angiography as the definitive test.
hy: Lower-extremity ABI <0.9 → evaluate for vascular injury.

stem: A Gustilo-Anderson Type I open fracture is defined by which description?

options.A: A skin opening <1 cm, clean, with minimal muscle contusion and a simple fracture pattern
options.B: A laceration >10 cm with massive contamination
options.C: Any open fracture with a vascular injury requiring repair
options.D: Extensive periosteal stripping requiring soft tissue flap coverage

exp: A Gustilo-Anderson Type I open fracture has a skin opening less than 1 cm, is clean (usually an inside-to-outside puncture), with minimal muscle contusion and a simple fracture pattern. A laceration >10 cm with massive contamination (B) is Type III, a vascular injury requiring repair (C) is IIIC, and periosteal stripping requiring flap coverage (D) is IIIB.
hy: Gustilo I = clean wound <1 cm, minimal soft-tissue injury.

stem: How are Gustilo Type IIIB and IIIC fractures distinguished?

options.A: IIIB requires formal soft tissue (flap) coverage of exposed bone; IIIC is any open fracture with a vascular injury requiring repair
options.B: IIIB involves a vascular injury; IIIC requires flap coverage
options.C: IIIB has a skin opening <1 cm; IIIC has a laceration >1 cm
options.D: They are graded purely by the bone involved, not the soft tissues

exp: Type IIIB denotes extensive soft tissue loss with periosteal stripping and bone exposure that requires formal soft tissue (flap) coverage, whereas IIIC is any open fracture with a vascular injury requiring repair. Option B reverses the two definitions, option C describes Grades I vs II, and grading is driven by the soft tissues, not the bone alone (D). IIIC carries the highest infection rate.
hy: IIIB needs flap coverage; IIIC has a vascular injury needing repair.

stem: How does infection risk vary across Gustilo grades?

options.A: Roughly 0%–2% for Grade I, 2%–10% for Grade II, and 10%–50% for Grade III (highest in IIIC)
options.B: Identical (~5%) across all grades
options.C: Highest in Grade I because of delayed recognition
options.D: Negligible in Grade III because of aggressive debridement

exp: Each successive Gustilo grade carries a higher infection risk: roughly 0%–2% for Grade I, 2%–10% for Grade II, and 10%–50% for Grade III, with IIIC the highest. Rates are not identical across grades (B), Grade I is the lowest not the highest (C), and Grade III risk is substantial, not negligible (D). This prognostic gradient is the scheme's main validated value.
hy: Infection: I 0–2%, II 2–10%, III 10–50% (IIIC worst).

stem: The authors note that, in practice, the Gustilo grades can be simplified into which scheme?

options.A: Low-energy injuries (≈ Grades I–II) versus high-energy injuries (≈ Grades IIIA–C)
options.B: Pediatric versus adult injuries
options.C: Open versus closed injuries
options.D: Diaphyseal versus metaphyseal injuries

exp: The authors find it more reliable, especially for non-orthopedic providers, to simplify open fractures into low-energy (≈ Gustilo I–II) versus high-energy (≈ Gustilo IIIA–C) injuries, since energy can be judged from the radiograph, soft tissue appearance, and mechanism. Pediatric/adult (B), open/closed (C), and diaphyseal/metaphyseal (D) are different distinctions. High-energy patterns mean the soft tissues and bone absorbed large forces.
hy: Practical shortcut: low-energy ≈ I–II, high-energy ≈ IIIA–C.

stem: What is the single most important early step in the treatment of an open fracture, and the recommended timing?

options.A: Administration of antibiotics, ideally within 60 minutes of arrival, with cefazolin (gram-positive coverage) for all grades
options.B: Definitive internal fixation within 1 hour
options.C: Withholding antibiotics until intraoperative cultures return
options.D: Vancomycin alone, started 24 hours after admission

exp: Early antibiotic administration is the single most important step in open-fracture care, with the general target of giving antibiotics within 60 minutes of arrival (some centers start prehospital); cefazolin for gram-positive coverage is given to all grades. Definitive fixation within an hour (B) is not the priority, withholding antibiotics for cultures (C) is wrong, and vancomycin alone started late (D) is inappropriate. Evidence suggests the 60-minute clock really starts at injury.
hy: Open fracture: antibiotics within 60 min; cefazolin for all grades.

stem: For higher-grade (II–III) open fractures, which antibiotic addition is standard, and why?

options.A: Add an aminoglycoside for gram-negative coverage, based on data showing morbid late gram-negative infections
options.B: Add an antifungal because fungal infection predominates
options.C: Remove the cephalosporin and use penicillin alone
options.D: No additional coverage is ever indicated beyond cefazolin

exp: Grades II and III open fractures standardly add an aminoglycoside for gram-negative coverage, based on historical data that late gram-negative infections markedly increase morbidity, operations, and hospital/antibiotic duration. Antifungals (B) are not routine, removing the cephalosporin for penicillin alone (C) is wrong, and additional coverage is indeed indicated beyond cefazolin (D). Some centers substitute piperacillin-tazobactam given aminoglycoside nephrotoxicity.
hy: Add an aminoglycoside (gram-negative) for Gustilo II–III.
src: Sabiston Ch 19, "Open Fractures: Initial Management" / Table 19.4

stem: What irrigation volumes does expert consensus recommend for surgical debridement of Grade I, II, and III open fractures, respectively?

options.A: 3 L, 6 L, and 9 L of normal saline
options.B: 1 L, 2 L, and 3 L
options.C: 9 L, 6 L, and 3 L
options.D: 500 mL for all grades

exp: Expert consensus uses approximately 3 L of normal saline for Grade I, 6 L for Grade II, and 9 L for Grade III open fractures. The smaller volumes (B) and the reversed order (C) are incorrect, and a flat 500 mL (D) is far too little for higher grades. No study has truly defined an optimal volume, but the volume scales with grade.
hy: Irrigation: 3 / 6 / 9 L for Gustilo I / II / III.

stem: Regarding irrigation pressure for open fracture wounds, what does the evidence support?

options.A: Low-pressure lavage produces lower rebound bacterial levels than high-pressure lavage, which may drive contamination deeper
options.B: High-pressure lavage is clearly superior at reducing infection
options.C: Antibiotic or detergent additives outperform normal saline
options.D: The wound should not be irrigated at all

exp: Animal data show low-pressure lavage produces lower rebound bacterial levels at 48 hours than high-pressure lavage, which is thought to drive contamination deeper into the wound. High-pressure superiority (B) is therefore wrong, antibiotic/detergent additives (C) showed no benefit over plain saline, and not irrigating (D) is unacceptable. Irrigation is typically done by gravity with regular surgical tubing.
hy: Low-pressure saline lavage beats high-pressure for open fractures.

stem: If primary closure is not possible, by when should definitive soft tissue coverage (skin graft or muscle flap) ideally be performed?

options.A: Within the first 72 hours, before secondary colonization and wound fibrosis develop
