stem: What did sequencing of clinically NORMAL, sun-exposed human skin reveal?

options.A: It already harbors clonally expanded keratinocytes/melanocytes with high UV-mutation burdens and positively selected oncogenic drivers
options.B: It is genetically pristine, with driver mutations appearing only once a tumor becomes clinically visible
options.C: It carries inherited germline cancer variants but no acquired somatic mutations
options.D: It contains only passenger mutations, which never undergo positive selection

exp: Sequencing of clinically normal sun-exposed skin showed it already contains clonally expanded keratinocytes and melanocytes carrying a high UV-mutation burden and positively selected drivers — so cancer-genome evolution begins long before a tumor appears. The distractors are the intuitive-but-wrong alternatives the data overturned: B (pristine until visible disease), C (germline-only, no somatic change), and D (passengers only, no selection) are each contradicted by the finding of selected somatic drivers in normal skin.
hy: Normal sun-exposed skin already harbors clonal, positively-selected driver mutations.

stem: In cancer genomes, copy-number changes typically follow which pattern?

options.A: Amplification of regions encoding oncogenes (e.g. BRAF, MYC, cyclins) and deletion of regions encoding tumor suppressors (e.g. TP53, CDKN2A)
options.B: Amplification of tumor-suppressor regions and deletion of oncogene regions
options.C: Uniform single-copy loss of every chromosome arm across the genome
options.D: Selective amplification of housekeeping genes required for basal metabolism

exp: Cancers recurrently amplify oncogene-bearing regions (BRAF, MYC, cyclins) and delete tumor-suppressor regions (TP53, CDKN2A) — selection acting at the copy-number level. The distractors invert or misstate this: B reverses the logic (amplifying suppressors, deleting oncogenes would be counter-selected); C (uniform arm-level loss) is not a selected pattern; and D (amplifying housekeeping genes) confers no growth advantage and is not observed.
hy: Amplify oncogenes (BRAF/MYC/cyclins), delete suppressors (TP53/CDKN2A).

stem: Which pair of pathways are the prominent oncogenic growth-factor signaling pathways highlighted in the chapter?

options.A: The RTK–RAS–MAPK pathway and the PI3K–AKT–mTOR pathway
options.B: The Hedgehog (PTCH–SMO–GLI) and Notch (Delta/Jagged–NICD) developmental pathways
options.C: The p53–Rb cell-cycle checkpoint and the DNA-damage-response pathways
options.D: The cGAS–STING cytosolic-DNA-sensing and type-I-interferon pathways

exp: The chapter highlights the RTK–RAS–MAPK and PI3K–AKT–mTOR growth-factor pathways as the prominent oncogenic proliferative signaling routes. The distractors are real pathways with different jobs: B (Hedgehog/Notch) are developmental pathways that remodel the tissue ecosystem (and drive BCC/cSCC); C (p53–Rb) is the cell-cycle checkpoint/DNA-damage axis, not a growth-factor pathway; and D (cGAS–STING) is an innate-immune DNA-sensing pathway.
hy: Proliferative growth-factor pathways = RTK-RAS-MAPK + PI3K-AKT-mTOR.

stem: How do oncogenic RAS mutations alter RAS protein behavior?

options.A: They impair RAS GTPase activity, locking it in a constitutively active GTP-bound state independent of upstream receptors
options.B: They convert RAS into a tumor suppressor whose loss removes a brake on proliferation
options.C: They abolish GTP binding entirely, silencing the downstream MAPK cascade
options.D: They delete the membrane-anchoring domain so RAS can no longer localize to the cell surface

exp: Oncogenic RAS mutations cripple its intrinsic GTPase, so RAS stays GTP-bound and "on," signaling to MAPK without upstream receptor input. The distractors describe other (wrong) molecular logics: B treats RAS as a tumor suppressor (it is an oncogene — gain, not loss, of function); C (abolishing GTP binding) would silence RAS, the opposite of oncogenic activation; and D (loss of membrane anchoring) would mislocalize and inactivate RAS rather than activate it.
hy: Oncogenic RAS = GTPase-dead → locked GTP-bound "on."

stem: Which metabolic adaptation of cancer cells is the "Warburg effect"?

options.A: Aerobic glycolysis — conversion of glucose to lactate even under oxygen-rich conditions, supplying energy and biosynthetic building blocks
options.B: Mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation — the efficient ATP pathway normal cells use in oxygen-rich conditions
options.C: Beta-oxidation of fatty acids as the dominant fuel source
options.D: Glutaminolysis — using glutamine to replenish tricarboxylic-acid-cycle intermediates

exp: The Warburg effect is aerobic glycolysis: tumor cells ferment glucose to lactate even with oxygen available, gaining both ATP and biosynthetic precursors for new macromolecules. The distractors are genuine metabolic pathways that are not the Warburg effect: B (oxidative phosphorylation) is the efficient route normal cells favor in oxygen; C (beta-oxidation) burns fatty acids; and D (glutaminolysis) replenishes TCA intermediates — all real cancer-relevant metabolism, but the Warburg phenomenon specifically denotes aerobic glycolysis.
hy: Warburg = aerobic glycolysis (glucose→lactate despite O₂).

stem: Which statement explains why p53 is the "guardian of the genome"?

options.A: It senses irreparable DNA damage and triggers senescence or apoptosis; loss-of-function TP53 mutations are the single most frequent oncogenic alteration across tumor types
options.B: It is the catalytic DNA polymerase that proofreads and corrects replication errors during S phase
options.C: It is a cell-surface growth-factor receptor whose activating mutations drive constitutive proliferation
options.D: It is the spindle-assembly-checkpoint kinase that delays anaphase until all kinetochores are attached

exp: p53 earns "guardian of the genome" because it detects irreparable DNA damage and forces senescence or apoptosis, and TP53 loss is the most frequent oncogenic alteration across cancers. The distractors are real molecules doing other jobs: B describes a replicative DNA polymerase/proofreader (e.g. POLE), not p53; C describes a receptor tyrosine kinase oncogene; and D describes a spindle-assembly-checkpoint kinase (e.g. BUB/MAD family). Only p53 is the damage-sensing tumor suppressor.
hy: p53 = damage sensor → senescence/apoptosis; most commonly mutated tumor suppressor.

stem: Which set of relationships at the G1/S checkpoint is correct?

options.A: Cyclin D–CDK4/6 phosphorylates Rb to release E2F and permit S-phase entry, and p16 (a CDKN2A product) inhibits CDK4/6 to enforce the checkpoint
options.B: p16 activates CDK4/6 to accelerate cells through the checkpoint, while Rb phosphorylation arrests the cycle
options.C: p53 directly phosphorylates Rb, and E2F sequestration by cyclin E halts DNA synthesis
options.D: Cyclin B–CDK1 controls G1/S entry, and the anaphase-promoting complex licenses S phase

exp: Mitogenic signals drive Cyclin D–CDK4/6 to phosphorylate Rb, releasing E2F to license S-phase entry, while p16 (a CDKN2A product) brakes CDK4/6 to hold the G1/S checkpoint. The distractors scramble these real players: B inverts p16's role (it inhibits, not activates, CDK4/6) and Rb phosphorylation's effect (it releases E2F, promoting the cycle); C miscasts p53 as an Rb kinase; and D assigns G1/S control to Cyclin B–CDK1 and the APC, which actually govern the M phase / mitotic exit.
hy: Cyclin D-CDK4/6 → phosphorylate Rb → release E2F; p16 brakes CDK4/6.

stem: Which tumor-suppressor pathways drive cellular senescence, and what is the significance of high p53/p16 in preneoplastic cells?

options.A: The p14ARF/MDM2/p53 and p16/pRb pathways; elevated p53 and p16 mark preneoplastic cells and form a barrier to malignant progression
options.B: The RTK–RAS–MAPK pathway; elevated p53/p16 are markers of fully transformed, immortalized cells
options.C: The Hedgehog and Notch developmental pathways; elevated p53/p16 promote rather than restrain transformation
options.D: The PI3K–AKT–mTOR pathway; elevated p53/p16 reflect normal proliferation and carry no prognostic meaning

exp: Senescence is enforced by the p14ARF/MDM2/p53 and p16/pRb pathways, and high p53/p16 in preneoplastic cells is a barrier whose inactivation permits progression. The distractors misassign the biology: B (RTK-RAS-MAPK) is a proliferative pathway, and high p53/p16 mark a barrier, not the transformed state; C (Hedgehog/Notch) are developmental pathways and the claim that p53/p16 promote transformation is backwards; D (PI3K-AKT-mTOR) is a survival/growth pathway and elevated p53/p16 are far from prognostically meaningless.
hy: Senescence via p14ARF/MDM2/p53 + p16/pRb; high p53/p16 = preneoplastic barrier.

stem: The CDKN2A locus encodes which protein product(s)?

options.A: Both p16 (which inhibits CDK4/6, feeding the Rb pathway) and p14ARF (which stabilizes p53 by inhibiting MDM2)
options.B: The Rb protein, which binds and sequesters E2F transcription factors
options.C: Cyclin D and CDK4, the kinase complex that phosphorylates Rb
options.D: MDM2, the E3 ligase that targets p53 for proteasomal degradation

exp: Uniquely, CDKN2A encodes two proteins from alternate reading frames — p16 (inhibits CDK4/6 → Rb pathway) and p14ARF (inhibits MDM2 → stabilizes p53) — so its loss cripples both checkpoints at once. The distractors are the partner molecules in the same circuit, encoded elsewhere: B (Rb) is encoded by RB1; C (Cyclin D/CDK4) are separate genes that p16 inhibits; and D (MDM2) is the E3 ligase that p14ARF inhibits.
hy: CDKN2A = p16 (→Rb) AND p14ARF (→p53); one locus, two checkpoints.

stem: In intrinsic apoptosis, which statement is correct?

options.A: Loss of p53 leaves the pro-apoptotic protein Bax inhibited, impairing apoptosis; the cascade is launched by initiator caspases 8 and 9
options.B: Loss of p53 hyperactivates Bax, causing excessive apoptosis and tissue loss
options.C: Effector caspase 3 alone initiates the cascade, independent of upstream signals
options.D: Anti-apoptotic Bcl-2 and Mcl-1 trigger the cascade by permeabilizing the mitochondrial membrane

exp: Without p53, pro-apoptotic Bax stays inhibited, so apoptosis is impaired and cells survive; the cascade is normally launched by initiator caspases 8 and 9, which activate effector caspases. The distractors misstate the players: B reverses p53's effect (its loss impairs, not hyperactivates, apoptosis); C names caspase 3, which is an effector (downstream), not an initiator; and D miscasts Bcl-2/Mcl-1, which are anti-apoptotic guardians of the mitochondrial membrane, as triggers of death.
hy: p53 loss → Bax inhibited → apoptosis impaired; initiators = caspase 8 & 9.

stem: Besides classic apoptosis, which trio of programmed cell-death pathways does the chapter highlight?

options.A: Ferroptosis, necroptosis, and pyroptosis
options.B: Autophagy, anoikis, and mitotic catastrophe
options.C: Senescence, quiescence, and terminal differentiation
options.D: Necrosis, oncosis, and accidental cell lysis

exp: The chapter singles out ferroptosis, necroptosis, and pyroptosis as newer regulated death pathways with distinct tissue effects. The distractors mix in non-death or different processes: B lists autophagy (a recycling/survival process), anoikis (matrix-detachment death, discussed separately), and mitotic catastrophe; C lists growth-arrest states (senescence/quiescence/differentiation), not death; and D lists necrosis/oncosis/lysis, which are unregulated/accidental rather than the programmed pathways named.
hy: Newer programmed death: ferroptosis, necroptosis, pyroptosis.

stem: Which event is described as a frequent EARLY step predisposing cells with defective checkpoints to chromosomal instability?

options.A: Whole-genome doubling, which then permits progressive aneuploidy
options.B: Telomere elongation by reactivated telomerase, granting replicative immortality
options.C: Global DNA hypomethylation silencing tumor-suppressor promoters
options.D: Mitochondrial DNA depletion forcing a switch to glycolysis

exp: Whole-genome doubling is described as a frequent early event that lets checkpoint-deficient cells tolerate and accumulate further chromosomal changes, driving aneuploidy/instability. The distractors are real cancer mechanisms that serve other hallmarks: B (telomerase reactivation) grants replicative immortality; C (global hypomethylation) is an epigenetic change that can de-repress genes; and D (mtDNA depletion → glycolysis) relates to metabolic reprogramming — none is the early step toward chromosomal instability described here.
hy: Whole-genome doubling = early step to aneuploidy/instability.

stem: What is the role of the cGAS–STING pathway in cancer cells with chromosomal instability?

options.A: It senses cytosolic DNA from ruptured micronuclei and makes interferons; early it alerts innate immunity, but later it drives proinflammatory responses favoring immune evasion and metastasis
options.B: It is the homologous-recombination machinery that repairs the double-strand breaks created during mis-segregation
options.C: It is a receptor tyrosine kinase that activates RAS in response to extracellular growth factors
options.D: It is the spindle checkpoint that prevents the chromosome mis-segregation in the first place

exp: cGAS–STING senses cytosolic DNA leaking from ruptured micronuclei and makes interferons; early it alerts innate immunity, but later it drives proinflammatory signaling that supports immune evasion and metastasis. The distractors are different machineries: B describes homologous-recombination repair (e.g. BRCA/RAD51), not DNA sensing; C describes a receptor tyrosine kinase activating RAS; and D describes the spindle-assembly checkpoint that would prevent mis-segregation — cGAS–STING acts after the instability, not to prevent it.
hy: cGAS-STING senses cytosolic DNA → interferons; later fosters evasion/metastasis.

stem: The chapter likens advanced cancer tissue — with its inflammation, neovascularization, and remodeled matrix — to what?

options.A: A chronic wound that does not heal
options.B: A normally regenerating epidermis returning to homeostasis after injury
options.C: A mature granuloma walling off an inert foreign body
options.D: A terminally differentiated tissue that has permanently exited the cell cycle

exp: Because advanced tumor tissue shows persistent inflammation, neovascularization, and matrix remodeling — the same features as a healing wound that never resolves — the chapter calls cancer "a chronic wound that does not heal." The distractors describe tissue states that do reach resolution or arrest: B (normal regenerating epidermis) returns to homeostasis; C (a granuloma) walls off and contains; and D (terminally differentiated tissue) has permanently exited the cycle — none captures the unresolving, self-perpetuating tumor ecosystem.
hy: Cancer = "a chronic wound that does not heal."

stem: In NORMAL Hedgehog signaling and its physiologic activation, which statement is correct?

options.A: Unbound Patched silences Smoothened; when Hedgehog binds Patched, that repression is relieved and Smoothened signals via GLI to the nucleus
options.B: Unbound Patched activates Smoothened, and Hedgehog binding shuts the pathway off
options.C: The membrane ligand Delta binds Notch, releasing an intracellular fragment (NICD) that enters the nucleus
options.D: A growth factor binds a receptor tyrosine kinase, switching on RAS and the MAPK cascade

exp: In resting Hedgehog signaling, Patched tonically silences Smoothened; ligand binding to Patched relieves that brake, letting Smoothened signal through GLI to the nucleus. The distractors describe other pathways or an inverted logic: B reverses the Patched–Smoothened relationship; C is the Notch pathway (Delta ligand → NICD cleavage → nucleus); and D is the RTK–RAS–MAPK growth-factor pathway. Only A states Hedgehog correctly.
hy: Patched normally silences SMO; Hedgehog binding releases SMO → GLI.

stem: Which genetic lesion drives constitutive Hedgehog signaling in a sporadic BCC that has an INTACT PTCH1 gene?

options.A: An activating mutation in SMO (Smoothened) that signals even though Patched is functional
options.B: An inactivating mutation in PTCH1 that produces dysfunctional Patched unable to repress Smoothened
options.C: An amplification of GLI that floods the nucleus with the terminal transcription factor
options.D: A loss-of-function mutation in SUFU, the negative regulator that normally restrains GLI

exp: When PTCH1 is intact, a sporadic BCC instead acquires an activating SMO mutation that fires the pathway despite functional Patched — which is why SMO inhibitors can work. The distractors are real Hedgehog lesions seen in other settings: B (PTCH1 loss-of-function) is the more common driver, but the stem stipulates intact PTCH1; C (GLI amplification) and D (SUFU loss) are additional, less-common ways to activate Hedgehog (SUFU loss is notable in medulloblastoma), but the lesion fitting "intact PTCH1 in BCC" is activating SMO.
hy: Intact PTCH1 + active Hedgehog → activating SMO mutation.

stem: Vismodegib and sonidegib treat advanced BCC by which mechanism, and what limits their durability?

options.A: They inhibit Smoothened (SMO); at least 50% of advanced BCCs develop resistance, commonly by acquiring SMO mutations
options.B: They inhibit BRAF/MEK in the MAPK pathway; resistance arises through MAPK reactivation
options.C: They block PD-1 on T cells to restore antitumor immunity; resistance reflects antigen loss
options.D: They inhibit VEGF/VEGFR2 to starve the tumor of new vessels; resistance reflects alternative proangiogenic signals

exp: Vismodegib and sonidegib are Smoothened (SMO) inhibitors for locally advanced/metastatic BCC and Gorlin syndrome, and ≥50% of advanced BCCs become resistant, usually via new SMO mutations. Each distractor is the mechanism of a different skin-cancer therapy: B (BRAF/MEK inhibitors) treat BRAF-mutant melanoma, with resistance from MAPK reactivation; C (PD-1 blockade, e.g. cemiplimab) treats advanced cSCC/melanoma/Merkel cell, with resistance from antigen loss; and D (anti-VEGF) is an antiangiogenic strategy used in other cancers.
hy: Vismodegib/sonidegib = SMO inhibitors; ≥50% advanced BCC resist via SMO mutation.

stem: In normal skin, physiologic Hedgehog signaling is chiefly responsible for what?

options.A: Developmental regulation of hair follicles and sebaceous glands (and stem-cell maintenance/repair)
options.B: Terminal keratinocyte differentiation and formation of the cornified envelope
options.C: Melanocyte pigment production and transfer of melanosomes to keratinocytes
options.D: Patterning of cutaneous nerves and neuroendocrine (Merkel) cell development

exp: In skin, physiologic Hedgehog signaling governs hair-follicle and sebaceous-gland development and helps maintain epithelial/neural stem cells — which is why its oncogenic activation produces BCC (a follicular-germinative tumor). The distractors are the jobs of other programs: B (terminal keratinocyte differentiation/cornified envelope) is driven largely by Notch and calcium gradients; C (melanosome transfer/pigmentation) is melanocyte–keratinocyte biology (MC1R/MITF); and D (cutaneous nerve/Merkel-cell patterning) is neural-crest/neuroendocrine development.
hy: Cutaneous Hedgehog → hair follicles & sebaceous glands; Notch → keratinocyte differentiation.

stem: Immune-checkpoint blockade — the breakthrough that reshaped melanoma therapy — uses antibodies against which T-cell receptors?

options.A: CTLA-4 and PD-1
options.B: VEGFR2 and EGFR on endothelial and epithelial cells
options.C: Patched and Smoothened in the Hedgehog pathway
options.D: CD20 and CD19 on B-lineage cells

exp: Checkpoint blockade uses antibodies against the inhibitory T-cell receptors CTLA-4 and PD-1, releasing the brakes on antitumor T cells. The distractors target other receptors/cells: B (VEGFR2/EGFR) are angiogenesis/epithelial-growth targets (antiangiogenic and anti-EGFR therapies); C (Patched/Smoothened) are Hedgehog components targeted in BCC; and D (CD20/CD19) are B-cell antigens targeted in lymphoma (rituximab) and CAR-T — not the T-cell checkpoints.
hy: Checkpoint blockade = anti-CTLA-4 and anti-PD-1.

stem: Why does the efficacy of T-cell immunotherapy correlate with a tumor's mutational burden?

options.A: Mutated peptides (neoantigens) are presented on MHC like viral antigens, so more mutations create more neoantigens for T cells to recognize
options.B: A higher mutational burden destabilizes the tumor genome until the cells die spontaneously without immune help
options.C: More mutations increase MHC down-regulation, making high-burden tumors easier to ignore
options.D: Mutational burden reflects proliferation rate, which alone determines chemotherapy (not immunotherapy) response

exp: Neoantigens — mutated peptides — are displayed on MHC like viral antigens, so a higher mutational burden yields more neoantigens and more T-cell targets, explaining the burden–response correlation. The distractors are plausible-sounding but wrong: B (spontaneous death from instability) is not why immunotherapy works; C inverts the relationship (high burden generally means more targets, not easier immune ignorance); and D conflates proliferation rate with chemo sensitivity, which is a different axis from immunotherapy response.
hy: More mutations → more neoantigens on MHC → better immunotherapy response.

stem: Which mechanism allows cancer cells to evade established T-cell immunity?

options.A: Loss-of-function mutations in MHC molecules plus dedifferentiation that down-regulates antigen presentation
options.B: Up-regulation of MHC and neoantigen display to recruit more cytotoxic T cells
options.C: Increased secretion of interferons that amplify antigen presentation
options.D: Forced entry into apoptosis whenever a T cell engages the tumor cell

exp: Tumors escape T cells by mutating/down-regulating MHC and antigen-presentation genes and by dedifferentiating into stem-like states that display fewer antigens. The distractors would enhance recognition, not evade it: B (up-regulating MHC/neoantigens) and C (interferon-driven antigen presentation) make cells more visible to T cells; and D (forced apoptosis on T-cell contact) describes effective immune killing, the opposite of evasion.
hy: Immune evasion = lose MHC/antigen presentation + dedifferentiate.

stem: Which molecule is the principal regulator of tumor angiogenesis, and what triggers its production?

options.A: VEGF, produced by cancer cells under hypoxia and acting on VEGFR2 on endothelial cells, yielding abnormal, leaky vessels
options.B: PDGF, produced by platelets and recruiting pericytes to stabilize mature vessels
options.C: E-cadherin, expressed under normoxia to maintain endothelial cell–cell junctions
options.D: Angiopoietin-1, which acts through Tie2 to keep quiescent vessels tightly sealed

exp: VEGF is the principal angiogenic regulator: hypoxic tumor cells secrete it, and it acts on endothelial VEGFR2 to sprout new — but abnormal, leaky, poorly perfused — vessels. The distractors are real vascular molecules with different roles: B (PDGF) recruits pericytes to stabilize vessels; C (E-cadherin) is an epithelial adhesion molecule, not an endothelial angiogenic factor; and D (angiopoietin-1/Tie2) quiets and seals mature vessels — opposite to initiating sprouting.
hy: Hypoxia → VEGF → VEGFR2 → abnormal leaky tumor vessels.

stem: The "seed and soil" hypothesis of organ-specific metastasis is credited to whom?

options.A: Stephen Paget — metastatic tropism reflects favorable interactions between cancer cells ("seed") and specific organ microenvironments ("soil")
options.B: James Ewing — metastatic distribution is dictated purely by mechanical/anatomic patterns of vascular and lymphatic drainage
options.C: Rudolf Virchow — chronic inflammation ("irritation") at a site is the primary driver of cancer and its spread
options.D: Thomas Hunt Morgan — organ tropism follows the developmental Notch program first seen in fruit-fly wing notching

exp: Stephen Paget proposed "seed and soil": metastatic organ tropism reflects compatibility between the cancer cell ("seed") and a receptive organ microenvironment ("soil"). The distractors are real historical alternatives: B (Ewing) championed the mechanical/anatomic view that drainage patterns alone dictate spread; C (Virchow) is tied to the inflammation/irritation theory of cancer; and D (Morgan) is the geneticist who named Notch from fruit-fly wing notching — unrelated to metastatic tropism.
hy: Paget = "seed and soil"; Ewing = mechanical/anatomic spread.

stem: Which adhesion-molecule change is the critical step that initiates carcinoma invasion (epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition)?

options.A: A switch from E-cadherin to N-cadherin, with matrix metalloproteinases degrading the extracellular matrix
options.B: A switch from N-cadherin to E-cadherin, restoring tight epithelial junctions
options.C: Up-regulation of desmoglein-1, strengthening desmosomal keratinocyte adhesion
options.D: Loss of integrin expression, detaching the cell from all matrix contact at once

exp: EMT is initiated by a cadherin switch from E-cadherin to N-cadherin, loosening epithelial adhesion, while matrix metalloproteinases digest the basement membrane/ECM to allow invasion. The distractors are the reverse or unrelated adhesion changes: B (N→E-cadherin) is mesenchymal-to-epithelial reversion, the opposite of invasion onset; C (desmoglein-1 up-regulation) would strengthen keratinocyte desmosomes; and D (total integrin loss) would detach the cell entirely rather than enable directed invasion.
hy: EMT = E-cadherin → N-cadherin switch + MMP-mediated ECM breakdown.

stem: What is "anoikis," and how do metastasizing cells handle it?

options.A: An apoptosis-like death triggered by loss of cell–matrix contact; metastasis-initiating cells adapt their adhesion networks to resist it while in the circulation
options.B: Uncontrolled proliferation triggered when a cell loses contact inhibition from its neighbors
options.C: The fusion of a circulating tumor cell with a platelet to form a protective embolus
options.D: A dormant, slow-cycling state entered after a cell colonizes a distant perivascular niche

exp: Anoikis is an apoptosis-like death triggered when a cell loses matrix contact; metastasis-initiating cells remodel their adhesion/survival networks to resist it while circulating. The distractors describe different metastasis-related concepts: B is loss of contact inhibition (a proliferation control, not a death program); C describes tumor-cell–platelet cloaking in the circulation (a real but separate survival trick); and D describes dormancy in a perivascular niche — a later step, not the definition of anoikis.
hy: Anoikis = death from lost matrix contact; metastatic cells resist it.

stem: Which statement about metastasis-initiating cells and mutation timing is correct?

options.A: They are stem-like and plastic, can lie dormant in perivascular niches, and most oncogenic driver mutations are acquired BEFORE dissemination
options.B: They are terminally differentiated, non-plastic cells that cannot survive in the circulation
options.C: They acquire essentially all of their driver mutations only AFTER reaching the distant organ
options.D: They proliferate immediately upon arrival and are incapable of entering a dormant state

exp: Metastasis-initiating cells are stem-like and plastic, can lie dormant in perivascular niches, and — importantly — most oncogenic driver mutations are present before dissemination (chromosomal instability/copy-number change accrues later). The distractors contradict this: B (terminally differentiated, can't circulate) is wrong because plasticity is required; C (all drivers acquired after arrival) is the opposite of the data; and D (immediate outgrowth, no dormancy) ignores the well-documented dormant phase.
hy: Metastatic cells: stem-like, dormancy-capable; most drivers precede spread.

stem: Which statement about keratinocyte carcinomas is correct?

options.A: Highest mutational burden of any cancer (a UV signature) with TP53 mutated in nearly all; BCCs are locally destructive but rarely metastasize, while cSCCs metastasize more often and cause most keratinocyte-carcinoma deaths
options.B: One of the lowest mutational burdens of any cancer with no UV signature; TP53 is rarely involved, and both BCC and cSCC are indolent tumors that essentially never metastasize
options.C: A high mutational burden driven mainly by tobacco rather than UV; BCC is the keratinocyte carcinoma that most often metastasizes and causes most deaths, while cSCC stays purely local
options.D: A high UV-signature burden with frequent TP53 mutation, but it is cSCC that stays purely local while BCC accounts for nearly all keratinocyte-carcinoma deaths through metastasis

exp: Keratinocyte carcinomas carry the highest mutational burden of any cancer (UV signature) with TP53 mutated in essentially all; clinically BCCs are locally destructive but rarely metastasize, whereas cSCCs metastasize more often and cause most keratinocyte-carcinoma deaths. Each distractor inverts a specific fact: B reverses both the mutational burden (it is the highest, not lowest) and TP53 involvement; C wrongly assigns a tobacco (G→T) rather than UV signature and wrongly makes BCC the chief metastasizer; and D reverses the clinical behavior of the two tumors — cSCC, not BCC, is the one that metastasizes and causes most deaths.
hy: KCs: highest mutation burden, TP53 in all; BCC rarely metastasizes, cSCC drives most KC deaths.

stem: Linkage studies of which inherited syndrome first implicated the Hedgehog pathway in BCC, and which gene did they identify?

options.A: Basal cell nevus (Gorlin) syndrome → PTCH1, a Hedgehog-pathway inhibitor acting as a tumor suppressor
options.B: Familial atypical multiple mole melanoma syndrome → CDKN2A (p16)
options.C: Li-Fraumeni syndrome → germline TP53
options.D: Xeroderma pigmentosum → a nucleotide-excision-repair gene such as XPA or XPC

exp: Linkage analysis of basal cell nevus (Gorlin) syndrome — multiple early BCCs — identified PTCH1, a Hedgehog inhibitor and tumor suppressor, whose loss also occurs in sporadic BCC. The distractors are real syndrome–gene pairs for other skin cancers: B (FAMMM syndrome → CDKN2A/p16) predisposes to melanoma; C (Li-Fraumeni → germline TP53) gives diverse cancers; and D (xeroderma pigmentosum → nucleotide-excision-repair genes, e.g. XPA/XPC) causes UV-hypersensitive multiple skin cancers — but the Gorlin/Hedgehog/PTCH1 link is specific to BCC.
hy: Gorlin syndrome → PTCH1 (Hedgehog); FAMMM → CDKN2A; XP → NER genes.

stem: Beyond Hedgehog activation (~85% of BCCs), which additional driver alterations cooperate to produce aggressive BCC?

options.A: Mutations in TP53, the Notch pathway, N-Myc (MYCN), and Hippo-YAP signaling
options.B: Activating BRAF V600E plus NRAS mutations of the MAPK pathway, as in melanoma
options.C: BCR-ABL fusion and JAK2 mutations, as in myeloproliferative disease
options.D: Germline BRCA1/2 loss with homologous-recombination deficiency, as in breast/ovarian cancer

exp: Beyond Hedgehog activation (~85% of BCCs), cooperating drivers include TP53, the Notch pathway, N-Myc (MYCN), and Hippo-YAP — and combined Hedgehog + Notch + N-Myc changes drive aggressive growth. The distractors are driver sets of other cancers: B (BRAF V600E/NRAS, MAPK) typifies melanoma; C (BCR-ABL/JAK2) typifies myeloproliferative neoplasms/CML; and D (germline BRCA1/2, HR deficiency) typifies hereditary breast/ovarian cancer.
hy: BCC drivers beyond Hedgehog: TP53, Notch, N-Myc, Hippo-YAP.

stem: What is the role of Notch signaling in cutaneous SCC, and how frequent are NOTCH1 mutations?

options.A: Notch is a tumor SUPPRESSOR in skin (it drives keratinocyte differentiation), so loss-of-function NOTCH1 mutations are oncogenic and occur in roughly half (~55%) of cSCCs — the second most frequent driver group after p53/Rb
options.B: Notch is an ONCOGENE in skin, so activating NOTCH1 mutations (as in T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia) drive cSCC
options.C: NOTCH1 mutations are essentially absent from cSCC, which is driven instead by Hedgehog activation
options.D: Notch loss is protective against cSCC by forcing premature keratinocyte apoptosis

exp: In skin, Notch is a tumor suppressor that promotes keratinocyte differentiation, so loss-of-function NOTCH1 mutations are oncogenic and appear in roughly half (~55%) of cSCCs — the second most frequent driver group after p53/Rb. The distractors capture real but misapplied facts: B is true of T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia, where Notch is an oncogene with activating mutations — context-dependence is the point; C is false (NOTCH1 mutations are common in cSCC); and D inverts the biology (Notch loss promotes, not prevents, cSCC).
hy: Notch = skin tumor suppressor (loss-of-function in ~55% cSCC); oncogenic (gain) in T-ALL.

stem: In the classic two-stage chemical-carcinogenesis mouse model (e.g. DMBA/TPA), which initiating mutation and which cooperating loss were identified for cSCC?

options.A: An initiating oncogenic HRAS mutation, followed by loss-of-function p53 mutations that enable chromosomal instability
options.B: An initiating PTCH1 loss (Hedgehog activation) cooperating with gain of p16
options.C: An initiating BRAF V600E (MAPK activation) cooperating with gain of E-cadherin
options.D: An initiating NOTCH1 amplification cooperating with loss of VEGF

exp: In the two-stage DMBA/TPA mouse model, an initiating oncogenic HRAS mutation starts the tumor and subsequent loss-of-function p53 mutations then license chromosomal instability and progression — findings later confirmed in human cSCC. Each distractor swaps in the wrong driver pair: B (PTCH1 loss + p16 gain) mixes the BCC Hedgehog driver with an implausible p16 "gain"; C (BRAF V600E + E-cadherin gain) is melanoma's driver plus a nonsensical pairing; and D (NOTCH1 amplification + VEGF loss) inverts Notch's loss-of-function role in skin.
hy: Two-stage cSCC model: HRAS initiates, p53 loss enables instability.

stem: A surgeon broke prevailing surgical dogma by combining an intracranial and extracranial approach to correct hypertelorbitism, persisting despite early operative deaths, and is regarded as having created an entirely new surgical specialty. Who is this founding father of craniofacial surgery?

options.A: Paul Tessier
options.B: Joseph McCarthy
options.C: Paul Manson
options.D: Harold Gillies

exp: Paul Tessier is named as the founding father of craniofacial surgery: he defied dogma by combining intracranial and extracranial exposure to correct hypertelorbitism, and pressed on despite early deaths, ultimately creating a new specialty spanning plastic, maxillofacial, ophthalmic and neurosurgery. The distractors are real figures with different legacies: B, Joseph McCarthy, adapted distraction osteogenesis to the craniofacial skeleton; C, Paul Manson, (with Gruss) pioneered rigid fixation and primary bone grafting of facial fractures; D, Harold Gillies, is a father of modern reconstructive/WWI plastic surgery, not the craniofacial-combined-approach innovator described.
hy: Tessier = founder of craniofacial surgery (intracranial + extracranial approach to hypertelorism).
chname: Plastic Surgery and Innovation in Medicine

stem: The first reported clinical use of tissue expansion (1957) employed a progressively distended subcutaneous balloon (a Foley catheter) to recruit skin for reconstruction of which structure?

options.A: The ear
options.B: The breast
options.C: The scalp
options.D: The nose

exp: The first clinical tissue expansion (Neumann, 1957) distended a subcutaneous balloon/Foley catheter to grow skin for ear reconstruction. The distractors are each a *different* tissue-expansion milestone or site: B, the breast, is where Radovan later reintroduced the temporary expander ~20 years on; C, the scalp, is a common modern expansion site but not the first reported use; D, the nose, is reconstructed with forehead/expanded skin but is not the 1957 case.
hy: First tissue expansion = Neumann 1957, balloon for ear reconstruction.
chname: Plastic Surgery and Innovation in Medicine

stem: Tissue expansion lay dormant for ~20 years after its first description until a surgeon revived the concept, popularizing the temporary expander for post-mastectomy breast reconstruction. Who reintroduced tissue expansion into mainstream practice?

options.A: Radovan
options.B: Neumann
options.C: Khouri
options.D: Coleman

exp: Radovan revived tissue expansion two decades after Neumann, popularizing the temporary expander for post-mastectomy breast reconstruction (he died before seeing it become mainstream). The distractors anchor other parts of the same story: B, Neumann, gave the *first ever* use (ear, 1957); C, Khouri, devised external suction expansion (Brava) plus fat grafting; D, Coleman, introduced structural fat grafting — a separate technique entirely.
hy: Radovan reintroduced tissue expansion → breast reconstruction with the temporary expander.
chname: Plastic Surgery and Innovation in Medicine

stem: The introduction of structural fat grafting, later found to have unanticipated benefits such as skin rejuvenation, is credited to which surgeon?

options.A: Sydney Coleman
options.B: Gino Rigotti
options.C: Roger Khouri
options.D: Yves-Gérard Illouz

exp: Sydney Coleman introduced structural fat grafting, a technique applied to both aesthetic and reconstructive surgery and later found to have unanticipated benefits (skin rejuvenation via stem cells, amelioration of radiation damage). The distractors are neighbors in the fat-grafting story: B, Rigotti, specifically reported reversal of *radiation* tissue damage by lipoaspirate; C, Khouri, combined external expansion (Brava) with fat injection; D, Illouz, is historically associated with liposuction technique, not the structural fat-grafting concept credited here.
hy: Structural fat grafting = Coleman.
chname: Plastic Surgery and Innovation in Medicine

stem: Beyond simple volume augmentation, autologous fat grafting was reported to reverse or ameliorate the tissue damage of prior radiotherapy, an effect attributed to adipose-derived stem cells. Which investigator is credited with demonstrating this healing effect of lipoaspirate transplant on radiated tissue?

options.A: Gino Rigotti
options.B: Sydney Coleman
options.C: Roger Khouri
options.D: Geoffrey Gurtner

exp: Gino Rigotti reported that lipoaspirate transplant ameliorates radiotherapy tissue damage through a healing process mediated by adipose-derived adult stem cells — one of fat grafting's "unanticipated benefits." The distractors are adjacent contributors: B, Coleman, introduced structural fat grafting itself; C, Khouri, paired external expansion with fat to reconstruct (including in radiated patients) but the *radiation-reversal observation* is Rigotti's; D, Gurtner, is the volume's basic-science editor (biologic brachytherapy), not the fat-and-radiation investigator.
hy: Fat grafting reverses radiation damage (stem-cell mediated) = Rigotti.
chname: Plastic Surgery and Innovation in Medicine

stem: One surgeon combined external suction-cup expansion of the breast with multi-vector fat injection to achieve breast reconstruction by external tissue expansion plus fat grafting alone (the "Brava" approach). Who devised this technique?

options.A: Roger Khouri
options.B: Sydney Coleman
options.C: Charles Radovan
options.D: Carl Neumann

exp: Roger Khouri put the concepts together: his Brava device produced external (suction) tissue expansion of the breast, and he recognized the swollen tissue as an ideal medium for retaining large-volume, multi-vector fat injection — achieving reconstruction by external expansion plus fat grafting alone. The distractors are the people whose individual ideas Khouri *combined*: B, Coleman, gave structural fat grafting; C, Radovan, gave (internal) temporary-expander tissue expansion; D, Neumann, gave the very first balloon expansion.
hy: Brava = external expansion + fat grafting (Khouri).
chname: Plastic Surgery and Innovation in Medicine

stem: The biological principle later adapted into craniofacial surgery as distraction osteogenesis — that bone can be lengthened by gradual mechanical traction — was originally discovered in 1951 by which orthopedic surgeon?

options.A: Gavril Ilizarov
options.B: Joseph McCarthy
options.C: Paul Tessier
options.D: Ian Taylor

exp: Gavril Ilizarov discovered in 1951 that bone can be lengthened by gradual mechanical traction — the foundation later adapted as craniofacial distraction osteogenesis. The distractors are downstream or unrelated: B, McCarthy, *adapted* the principle to the craniofacial skeleton (he did not discover it); C, Tessier, founded craniofacial surgery by a different (combined intracranial/extracranial) route; D, Taylor, mapped the body's vascular territories (angiosome).
hy: Bone lengthening / distraction principle discovered by Ilizarov, 1951.
chname: Plastic Surgery and Innovation in Medicine

stem: Who adapted the principle of gradual bone lengthening to the craniofacial skeleton — first lengthening the human mandible by gradual distraction — thereby making obsolete several more morbid older operations for craniofacial anomalies?

options.A: Joseph McCarthy
options.B: Gavril Ilizarov
options.C: Paul Tessier
options.D: Joe Gruss

exp: Joseph McCarthy adapted Ilizarov's gradual-distraction principle to the craniofacial skeleton, first lengthening the human mandible by gradual distraction and rendering obsolete several more morbid older operations. The distractors are the source or siblings of the idea: B, Ilizarov, *discovered* limb bone lengthening but did not bring it to the face; C, Tessier, founded craniofacial surgery via the combined cranial approach; D, Gruss, (with Manson) advanced rigid fixation/bone grafting of facial fractures — a different craniofacial advance.
hy: Distraction osteogenesis brought to the craniofacial skeleton (mandible) by McCarthy.
chname: Plastic Surgery and Innovation in Medicine

stem: For decades random pedicled skin flaps were designed according to a fixed maximum length-to-width ratio. Which investigator demonstrated that this length-to-width ratio rule was a fallacy?

options.A: Milton
options.B: Pontén
options.C: McGregor
options.D: Cormack

exp: Milton disproved the long-held length-to-width ratio "rule" for random pedicled skin flaps, showing flap survival depends on perfusion pressure and vascular anatomy, not a fixed geometric ratio. The distractors each advanced flaps in a *different* way: B, Pontén, introduced the fasciocutaneous flap; C, McGregor (with Jackson), introduced the axial groin flap; D, Cormack (with Lamberty), classified fasciocutaneous vascular supply. Only Milton is credited with debunking the ratio fallacy.
hy: Length:width ratio of random flaps = a fallacy (Milton).
chname: Plastic Surgery and Innovation in Medicine

stem: The first true axial flap — one in which the blood supply and the axis of flow through it were actually known — was the groin flap, introduced in 1972. Which flap concept did this represent compared with what preceded it?

options.A: The first axial-pattern flap, succeeding the era of random-pattern flaps
options.B: The first musculocutaneous flap, defined by an underlying muscle pedicle
options.C: The first fasciocutaneous flap, based on the fascial plexus
options.D: The first perforator flap, based on a single septocutaneous perforator

exp: The groin flap (McGregor & Jackson, 1972) was the first *axial-pattern* flap — the first in which the blood supply and axis of flow were actually known — succeeding the era of random-pattern flaps and opening modern flap surgery. The distractors name later flap classes that the chapter attributes to others: B, the musculocutaneous flap, became mainstream after Mathes & Nahai's muscle classification; C, the fasciocutaneous flap, was introduced by Pontén; D, the perforator flap, was introduced by Koshima in 1989.
hy: Groin flap = first axial flap (known blood supply/axis).
chname: Plastic Surgery and Innovation in Medicine

stem: The groin flap found new life as the SCIP flap. On a perforator of which vessel is the SCIP flap based?

options.A: Superficial circumflex iliac artery
options.B: Deep inferior epigastric artery
options.C: Superficial inferior epigastric artery
options.D: Deep circumflex iliac artery

exp: The SCIP flap is based on a perforator of the superficial circumflex iliac artery (the same axial vessel of the old groin flap), introduced by Koshima in 2004. The distractors are real but different abdominal-wall donor vessels: B, the deep inferior epigastric artery, supplies the DIEP flap; C, the superficial inferior epigastric artery, supplies the SIEA flap; D, the deep circumflex iliac artery, supplies the iliac-crest osteocutaneous (DCIA) flap.
hy: SCIP = Superficial Circumflex Iliac artery Perforator.
chname: Plastic Surgery and Innovation in Medicine

stem: The perforator flap concept — raising a skin flap on a perforator while sparing the underlying muscle, exemplified by the inferior epigastric artery skin flap without rectus abdominis — was introduced in 1989 by which surgeon?

options.A: Isao Koshima
options.B: Ian Taylor
options.C: Stephen Mathes
options.D: Bengt Pontén

exp: Isao Koshima introduced the perforator flap concept in 1989 with the inferior epigastric artery skin flap raised *without* the rectus abdominis — sparing muscle while keeping the cutaneous perforator. The distractors contributed earlier/adjacent flap milestones: B, Taylor, mapped angiosomes (1987); C, Mathes, (with Nahai) classified muscle vascular anatomy; D, Pontén, introduced the fasciocutaneous flap.
hy: Perforator flap concept = Koshima 1989 (muscle-sparing inferior epigastric skin flap).
chname: Plastic Surgery and Innovation in Medicine

stem: Using a series of lead-oxide injection studies, one surgeon mapped the blood supply of the entire integument and in 1987 published the theory of the vascular territories of the body. Which concept did he introduce?

options.A: The angiosome
options.B: The perforasome
options.C: The musculocutaneous classification
options.D: The fasciocutaneous classification

exp: Ian Taylor's 1987 lead-oxide injection studies mapped the integument's vascular territories and produced the angiosome concept — a three-dimensional block of tissue supplied by a source artery. The distractors are related but distinct ideas: B, the perforasome, is a later refinement (the territory of a single perforator); C and D, the musculocutaneous and fasciocutaneous classifications, are Mathes & Nahai's and Cormack & Lamberty's contributions, respectively, not Taylor's integument map.
hy: Angiosome = Taylor 1987 (lead-oxide vascular-territory mapping).
chname: Plastic Surgery and Innovation in Medicine

stem: Myocutaneous flaps became mainstream only after two surgeons performed the anatomic dissections that produced a precise classification of the vascular anatomy of muscles. Which pair is credited with this muscle vascular classification?

options.A: Mathes and Nahai
options.B: Cormack and Lamberty
options.C: McGregor and Jackson
options.D: Manson and Gruss

exp: Mathes and Nahai performed the anatomic dissections that yielded the classification of muscle vascular anatomy, which made myocutaneous flaps reliable and mainstream. The distractors are real eponymous pairs from the same chapter assigned to other advances: B, Cormack and Lamberty, classified fasciocutaneous vascular supply; C, McGregor and Jackson, introduced the axial groin flap; D, Manson and Gruss, advanced rigid fixation of facial fractures.
hy: Muscle vascular classification = Mathes & Nahai.
chname: Plastic Surgery and Innovation in Medicine

stem: Building on axial and muscle flaps, which surgeon took the next step by introducing the fasciocutaneous flap for soft-tissue defects of the lower leg?

options.A: Bengt Pontén
options.B: Ian Taylor
options.C: Isao Koshima
options.D: Stephen Mathes

exp: Bengt Pontén introduced the fasciocutaneous flap, using the deep fascial vascular plexus to reconstruct soft-tissue defects of the lower leg. The distractors advanced *different* flap classes: B, Taylor, gave the angiosome map; C, Koshima, gave the perforator flap; D, Mathes, (with Nahai) classified muscle flaps. Only Pontén is credited with the fasciocutaneous flap here.
hy: Fasciocutaneous flap = Pontén (lower leg).
chname: Plastic Surgery and Innovation in Medicine

stem: A plastic surgeon who began by studying the immunology of skin-graft rejection went on to perform the world's first successful kidney transplant (between identical twins) in 1954 and was awarded the 1990 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine. Who was he?

options.A: Joseph Murray
options.B: Christiaan Barnard
options.C: Harry Buncke
options.D: Paul Tessier

exp: Joseph Murray — a plastic surgeon who entered transplantation through his interest in skin-graft rejection — performed the first successful kidney transplant (identical twins, the Herrick brothers, 1954) and won the 1990 Nobel Prize. The distractors are adjacent figures: B, Christiaan Barnard, performed the first *heart* transplant (in South Africa); C, Harry Buncke, is a father of microsurgery; D, Paul Tessier, founded craniofacial surgery.
hy: Joe Murray = first successful kidney transplant (1954, twins) + Nobel 1990; was a plastic surgeon.
chname: Plastic Surgery and Innovation in Medicine

stem: Vascularized composite allotransplantation returned transplantation to plastic surgery. According to the chapter, the first hand transplant and the first face transplant were performed, respectively, in which years?
exp: The chapter dates the first hand transplant to 1998 and the first face transplant to 2005, marking the modern era of vascularized composite allotransplantation — still limited chiefly by the hazards of immunosuppression. The distractor year-pairs are simply incorrect against the text: B (1990/2000), C (1985/1998), and D (2005/2010) do not match the chapter's stated 1998 and 2005.
hy: First hand transplant 1998; first face transplant 2005 (VCA).
chname: Plastic Surgery and Innovation in Medicine

stem: Working largely in his garage, one surgeon figured out how to swage a fine needle onto a microsuture, experimented with replantation in animals, and performed an early free omental transfer to a scalp defect — helping create modern microsurgery. Who was he?

options.A: Harry Buncke
options.B: Isao Koshima
options.C: Joseph Murray
options.D: Geoffrey Gurtner

exp: Harry Buncke, often working in his garage, solved how to swage a fine needle onto a microsuture (by metallizing the suture end), experimented with replantation in animals, and performed an early free omental transfer to a scalp defect (1972) — foundational steps of microsurgery. The distractors are other innovators: B, Koshima, introduced perforator flaps decades later; C, Murray, pioneered kidney transplantation; D, Gurtner, is a modern basic-science researcher (biologic brachytherapy).
hy: Harry Buncke = pioneer of microsurgery (swaged microneedle, free omental transfer).
chname: Plastic Surgery and Innovation in Medicine

stem: Independently of each other, two surgeons developed the concept of rigid fixation and primary bone grafting for craniomaxillofacial trauma — decried at the time but now the standard of care for facial fractures. Which pair is credited with this advance?

options.A: Manson and Gruss
options.B: Mathes and Nahai
options.C: Tessier and McCarthy
options.D: Cormack and Lamberty

exp: Paul Manson and Joe Gruss independently developed rigid fixation and primary bone grafting for craniomaxillofacial trauma — heretical when proposed, now the standard of care for facial fractures. The distractors are real eponymous pairs credited elsewhere in the chapter: B, Mathes and Nahai, classified muscle vascular anatomy; C, Tessier and McCarthy, are craniofacial pioneers but for the combined cranial approach and distraction osteogenesis respectively, not fracture fixation; D, Cormack and Lamberty, classified fasciocutaneous flaps.
hy: Rigid fixation + primary bone grafting of facial fractures = Manson & Gruss.
chname: Plastic Surgery and Innovation in Medicine

stem: The chapter argues that the world's first heart transplant occurred outside the United States largely because strict U.S. regulation of experimental surgery drove the innovation elsewhere. In which country was that first heart transplant performed?

options.A: South Africa
options.B: United States
options.C: United Kingdom
options.D: Ireland

exp: The chapter cites the first heart transplant as having been performed in South Africa by Christiaan Barnard, arguing it did not happen first in the U.S. because of the comparatively strict U.S. regulation of experimental surgery — an example of regulation displacing innovation. The distractors are the contrast cases: B, the United States, is precisely where the chapter says regulation *prevented* it occurring first; C, the United Kingdom, and D, Ireland, are not credited with the first heart transplant.
hy: First heart transplant = Barnard, South Africa (U.S. regulation delayed it there).
chname: Plastic Surgery and Innovation in Medicine

stem: In 1597 a Bolognese professor of surgery published *De Curtorum Chirurgia per Insitionem*, illustrating step-by-step nasal reconstruction, and defined the discipline as restoring "what Nature has given and chance has taken away." Who is considered the founder of plastic surgery?

options.A: Gaspare Tagliacozzi
options.B: Ambroise Paré
options.C: Aulus Cornelius Celsus
options.D: Heinrich von Pfolspeundt

exp: Gaspare Tagliacozzi, professor of surgery at Bologna, published *De Curtorum Chirurgia per Insitionem* (Venice, 1597) — the work usually taken to mark the beginning of plastic surgery — and gave the discipline its enduring definition (restoring what nature gave and chance took away). The distractors are real but different figures: B, Ambroise Paré, was the great Renaissance battlefield surgeon (gunshot-wound care, first printed cleft-lip suture); C, Celsus, was the earliest *writer* on plastic-surgical topics (Roman, *De Medicina*); D, von Pfolspeundt was a 15th-century German who described nose repair but is not credited as the founder.
hy: Tagliacozzi = founder of plastic surgery (*De Curtorum Chirurgia*, 1597; Italian-method nasal reconstruction).
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: The word "plastic," as in plastic surgery, derives from a Greek root. What does that root (*plastikós*) mean?

options.A: To mold or shape
options.B: To cut away
options.C: To restore or repair
options.D: To graft or implant

exp: "Plastic" comes from the Greek *plastikós*, meaning moldable / to mold or shape — capturing the discipline's aim of reshaping tissue. The distractors describe related but distinct ideas: B (to cut away) is closer to *-ectomy*; C (to restore/repair) is the *goal* of plastic surgery but not the literal meaning of the root; D (to graft/implant) describes one *technique* (e.g., Tagliacozzi's *insitionem* = grafting), not the etymology of "plastic."
hy: "Plastic" = Greek *plastikós* = to mold/shape.
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: The earliest detailed account of reconstructive procedures of the face, including nasal repair, appears in a Sanskrit surgical text possibly dating to ~600 BCE. To which ancient surgeon is this text (the *Samhita*) attributed?

options.A: Sushruta
options.B: Hippocrates
options.C: Galen
options.D: Albucasis

exp: The *Samhita*, a Sanskrit surgical text possibly from ~600 BCE, is attributed to Sushruta and contains early accounts of facial reconstruction, including nasal repair (relevant because amputation of the nose was used as a punishment in ancient India). The distractors are physicians of other traditions: B, Hippocrates, was the great Greek clinician of Kos; C, Galen, was the Roman anatomist who mapped cranial nerves; D, Albucasis, was the Arabian surgeon-author of *Al-Tasrif* — none of whom authored the *Samhita*.
hy: Sushruta's *Samhita* = earliest detailed facial/nasal reconstruction (India).
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: The Indian forehead-flap technique for nasal reconstruction reached the Western world at the end of the eighteenth century (a letter in the *Gentleman's Magazine*, 1794, describing the case of Cowasjee). Which surgeon then popularized the operation in the West with his 1816 account of restoring a lost nose from the integuments of the forehead?

options.A: Joseph Carpue
options.B: Carl Ferdinand von Graefe
options.C: Johann Dieffenbach
options.D: Gaspare Tagliacozzi

exp: Joseph Carpue popularized the Indian forehead-flap nose reconstruction in the West, publishing his 1816 *Account of Two Successful Operations for Restoring a Lost Nose from the Integuments of the Forehead*; knowledge of the technique had reached Europe via the 1794 *Gentleman's Magazine* letter (the Cowasjee case). The distractors are different contributors: B, von Graefe, wrote an early-19th-century *Rhinoplastik* (German method) but is not the one credited here with the forehead-flap account; C, Dieffenbach, advanced 19th-century reconstructive and reduction-rhinoplasty technique; D, Tagliacozzi, used the *arm* (Italian-method), not the forehead, for nasal reconstruction.
hy: Carpue (1816) brought the Indian forehead-flap rhinoplasty to the West.
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: Which Roman author of *De Medicina* is considered the earliest writer on plastic surgery and described the four cardinal signs of acute inflammation (redness, swelling, heat, and pain)?

options.A: Celsus
options.B: Galen
options.C: Oribasius
options.D: Paulus of Aegina

exp: Aulus Cornelius Celsus, author of *De Medicina* (~30 CE), is considered the earliest writer on plastic-surgical topics (lip/ear/nose repair by advancement flaps) and described the four cardinal signs of inflammation — *rubor, tumor, calor, dolor*. The distractors are other ancient authors: B, Galen, contributed anatomy/neurology (he added *functio laesa* in later tradition, not the original four signs); C, Oribasius, and D, Paulus of Aegina, were *Byzantine* compilers who described facial repairs but came centuries after Celsus and are not credited with the cardinal signs.
hy: Celsus = earliest writer on plastic surgery; four signs = rubor, tumor, calor, dolor.
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: The most representative Arabian surgeon, author of *Al-Tasrif* (translated into Latin, with over 200 illustrated instruments), was a proponent of cautery and is credited with first using a syringe with a piston. Who was he?

options.A: Albucasis (Abū-l-Qāsim)
options.B: Avicenna
options.C: Galen
options.D: William of Saliceto

exp: Albucasis (Abū-l-Qāsim) wrote *Al-Tasrif*, illustrating over 200 instruments (many his own), favored cautery, and is credited with the first use of a syringe with a piston. The distractors are different figures: B, Avicenna, authored the *Canon of Medicine* (an internal-medicine encyclopedia), not the surgical *Al-Tasrif*; C, Galen, is Roman; D, William of Saliceto, was a later medieval author whose *La Ciroxia* (1474) reintroduced the surgical knife over cautery — essentially the opposite emphasis from Albucasis.
hy: Albucasis = *Al-Tasrif*; first syringe with a piston; champion of cautery.
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: Our knowledge of ancient Egyptian surgery — including conservative wound care and accurate treatment of nasal fractures — comes largely from which document, regarded as the most ancient surgical text and describing 48 cases?

options.A: The Edwin Smith papyrus
options.B: The Corpus Hippocraticum
options.C: The Code of Hammurabi
options.D: The Ebers papyrus

exp: The Edwin Smith papyrus — a ~1650 BCE transcription of a much older text — is the most ancient surgical document, describing 48 cases (wounds, fractures, dislocations, tumors) and including accurate management of nasal fractures. The distractors are real but different sources: B, the Corpus Hippocraticum, is the Greek collection attributed to Hippocrates; C, the Code of Hammurabi (~1700 BCE), is a *legal* code (surgical malpractice penalties), not a surgical manual; D, the Ebers papyrus, is another Egyptian medical text but is chiefly *medical/pharmacologic* rather than the surgical 48-case document cited here.
hy: Edwin Smith papyrus = oldest surgical text (48 cases).
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: The celebrated Renaissance barber-surgeon Ambroise Paré advanced wound care chiefly by which contribution?

options.A: Abandoning cauterization of gunshot wounds with boiling oil in favor of a gentle soothing dressing
options.B: Performing the first autologous skin graft to close a granulating wound
options.C: Introducing the tubed pedicle flap for coverage of facial defects
options.D: Codifying the steps of aesthetic reduction rhinoplasty

exp: Ambroise Paré's key advance was abandoning the brutal cauterization of gunshot wounds with boiling oil in favor of a gentle soothing dressing (egg yolk, oil of roses, turpentine), concluding that less-invasive care gave far better results. The distractors belong to others: B, the first autologous skin graft, was Baronio's (1804, ram); C, the tubed pedicle flap, was Filatov's (systematized by Gillies in WWI); D, the codification of aesthetic rhinoplasty, was Jacques Joseph's — all centuries removed from Paré.
hy: Paré = abandoned boiling-oil cautery of gunshot wounds (gentle dressing).
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: The principle that a piece of skin fully separated from its donor site can survive when transplanted elsewhere was first demonstrated in 1804 by an experiment performed on a ram. Which surgeon performed this first autologous skin graft?

options.A: Giuseppe Baronio
options.B: Jacques Reverdin
options.C: Louis Ollier
options.D: Carl Thiersch

exp: Giuseppe Baronio of Milan performed the first autologous skin graft in 1804, transplanting skin on a ram and proving that fully detached skin can survive at a new site. The distractors advanced grafting *later*: B, Reverdin, did the first successful *human* epidermic graft (1869); C, Ollier, transferred large split-thickness grafts; D, Thiersch, refined split-thickness grafting — all building on Baronio's proof of principle.
hy: First autologous skin graft = Baronio, 1804 (ram).
src: Neligan I.2 · Origin of modern skin grafting
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: The first successful epidermic (pinch) skin graft on a human being was carried out at the Hôpital Necker in Paris in 1869, opening a new era in wound management. Who performed it?

options.A: Jacques Reverdin
options.B: Giuseppe Baronio
options.C: John Wolfe
options.D: Carl Thiersch

exp: Jacques Reverdin performed the first successful epidermic (pinch) graft on a human at the Hôpital Necker, Paris, in 1869 — 65 years after Baronio's animal experiment — opening the modern era of grafting for granulating wounds. The distractors are adjacent: B, Baronio, did the first *animal* graft (1804); C, Wolfe, is associated with full-thickness grafting; D, Thiersch, with split-thickness grafting — but the first *human* graft is Reverdin's.
hy: First successful human skin graft = Reverdin, 1869 (Necker, Paris).
src: Neligan I.2 · Origin of modern skin grafting
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: Building on the first human grafts, which surgeon is credited in the chapter with transferring a large piece of split-thickness skin (including superficial layers and underlying dermis)?

options.A: Louis Ollier
options.B: Giuseppe Baronio
options.C: Jacques Reverdin
options.D: Hippolyte Morestin

exp: Louis Ollier is credited in the chapter with transferring a large piece of split-thickness skin (superficial layers plus underlying dermis), an advance beyond the small pinch grafts of Reverdin. The distractors are other grafting names: B, Baronio (first animal graft) and C, Reverdin (first human pinch graft), precede this larger-sheet advance; D, Morestin, was a WWI facial-reconstruction pioneer, unrelated to this grafting step.
hy: Ollier = large split-thickness skin transfer.
src: Neligan I.2 · Origin of modern skin grafting
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: The "lip switch" technique for upper-lip reconstruction — a cross-lip flap — was reported in 1838 by which surgeon?

options.A: Pietro Sabattini
options.B: Victor von Bruns
options.C: Johann Fricke
options.D: Germanicus Mirault

exp: Pietro Sabattini reported the lip-switch (cross-lip) technique for upper-lip reconstruction in 1838, a forerunner of the modern Abbe flap. The distractors are sibling 19th-century lip/eyelid surgeons: B, von Bruns (1857), used double lateral flaps for lower-lip/oral-sphincter restoration; C, Fricke (1829), described the temporal/cheek eyelid flap; D, Mirault (with Malgaigne, 1844), advanced cleft-lip repair — different operations.
hy: Lip-switch (cross-lip) flap = Sabattini, 1838.
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: In 1829 a surgeon described a pedicled skin flap raised from the ipsilateral temple or cheek to correct upper or lower eyelid defects — the flap that still bears his name. Who was he?

options.A: Johann Fricke
options.B: Pietro Sabattini
options.C: Victor von Bruns
options.D: Carl Thiersch

exp: Johann Fricke (1829) described a pedicled flap from the ipsilateral temple or cheek to reconstruct upper or lower eyelid defects — the eponymous Fricke flap. The distractors are contemporaries credited with different repairs: B, Sabattini, the lip switch; C, von Bruns, double lateral lower-lip flaps; D, Thiersch, split-thickness skin grafting — none of which is the temporal eyelid flap.
hy: Fricke flap = temporal/cheek pedicled flap for eyelid (1829).
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: The latissimus dorsi musculocutaneous flap was originally described by which Italian surgeon, predating its modern application by decades?

options.A: Iginio Tansini
options.B: Gustavo Sanvenero Rosselli
options.C: Gaspare Tagliacozzi
options.D: Pietro Sabattini

exp: Iginio Tansini originally described the latissimus dorsi musculocutaneous flap (~1906), decades before its modern revival for breast and trunk reconstruction. The distractors are other Italians/figures: B, Sanvenero Rosselli, led the Milan "Pavilion for Facial Cripples"; C, Tagliacozzi, did Renaissance arm-flap nasal reconstruction; D, Sabattini, the lip switch — none described the latissimus flap.
hy: Latissimus dorsi musculocutaneous flap = Tansini (~1906).
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: The clinical definition of the cutaneous vascular territories nourished by a single vessel — a forerunner of the angiosome concept — was first identified in 1889 by which anatomist?

options.A: Carl Manchot
options.B: Ian Taylor
options.C: Iginio Tansini
options.D: Michel Salmon

exp: Carl Manchot first mapped the cutaneous vascular territories supplied by single vessels in 1889 (*Die Hautarterien des menschlichen Körpers*), the conceptual ancestor of the modern angiosome. The distractors are related vascular mappers from other eras: B, Taylor, formalized the *angiosome* in 1987; C, Tansini, described the latissimus flap; D, Salmon, refined Manchot's cutaneous-artery maps in the 1930s — but the *first* identification cited here is Manchot's.
hy: Cutaneous vascular territories first mapped by Manchot, 1889.
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: The tubed (tubular pedicle) flap, which allowed coverage of large skin defects and was systematized by Gillies during World War I, was described by which Russian surgeon?

options.A: Vladimir Filatov
options.B: Harold Gillies
options.C: Johannes Esser
options.D: Erich Lexer

exp: The tubed pedicle flap, used to carry skin to distant defects, was described by the Russian Vladimir Filatov and famously systematized by Gillies for WWI facial reconstruction. The distractors are the surgeons who *used or paralleled* it rather than originating it: B, Gillies, popularized but did not first describe the tube; C, Esser, codified other flaps (cheek rotation, bilobed, island); D, Lexer, was a maxillofacial-surgery founder — neither originated the tubed flap.
hy: Tubed pedicle flap = Filatov (systematized by Gillies).
src: Neligan I.2 · Origin of modern plastic surgery
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: World War I maxillofacial injuries drove the birth of reconstructive surgery. Which New Zealand–born surgeon established the referral center for face and jaw injuries at Queen's Hospital, Sidcup (1917) and authored *Plastic Surgery of the Face* (1920)?

options.A: Harold Gillies
options.B: Hippolyte Morestin
options.C: Vilray Blair
options.D: Archibald McIndoe

exp: Harold Gillies, a New Zealand–born otolaryngologist, established the face-and-jaw referral center at Queen's Hospital, Sidcup (1917) and wrote *Plastic Surgery of the Face* (1920) — central to the birth of modern plastic surgery. The distractors are contemporaries with different roles: B, Morestin, inspired Gillies and pioneered the Paris team approach; C, Blair, built the first US unit and the American Board; D, McIndoe, was Gillies's cousin who later led WWII burn care at East Grinstead.
hy: Gillies = Sidcup (1917), *Plastic Surgery of the Face* (1920); father of modern plastic surgery.
src: Neligan I.2 · Origin of modern plastic surgery
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: Between 1916 and 1918 the Dutch surgeon Johannes Esser codified several flaps still used today (cheek rotation, bilobed, island, and arterialized flaps). What did Esser call his arterialized flaps, and which term did he coin as a substitute for "plastic"?

options.A: "Biological" flaps; he coined the term "structive"
options.B: "Axial" flaps; he coined the term "reconstructive"
options.C: "Random" flaps; he coined the term "autoplastic"
options.D: "Tubed" flaps; he coined the term "pedicled"

exp: Johannes Esser codified the cheek-rotation, bilobed, island, and arterialized flaps; he named the arterialized ones "biological" flaps and coined the term "structive" as (he felt) a more accurate word than "plastic" for the repairing concept. The distractors mismatch the terms: B, C, and D pair the wrong flap name and the wrong coined word (Esser did not coin "reconstructive," "autoplastic," or "pedicled," and his arterialized flaps were "biological," not axial/random/tubed).
hy: Esser = "biological" (arterialized) flaps; coined "structive."
src: Neligan I.2 · Origin of modern plastic surgery
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: Which German surgeon, named in the chapter as one of the founders of maxillofacial surgery, built extensive experience repairing the face, mandible, and eye socket with cartilage, bone, skin, and fat grafts?

options.A: Erich Lexer
options.B: Jacques Joseph
options.C: Johannes Esser
options.D: Eugen Holländer

exp: Erich Lexer of Germany is named among the founders of maxillofacial surgery, repairing the face, mandible, and orbit with cartilage, bone, skin, and fat grafts (book, 1920). The distractors are other early-20th-century figures with different niches: B, Jacques Joseph, was the Berlin father of aesthetic rhinoplasty; C, Esser, the Dutch flap-codifier; D, Holländer, credited with the first facelift — not the maxillofacial reconstructive founder described.
hy: Erich Lexer = a founder of maxillofacial surgery (composite grafts).
src: Neligan I.2 · Origin of modern plastic surgery
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: Who, working with the dentist Charles Valadier at Val-de-Grâce, first realized the importance of the plastic-surgeon/oral-surgeon team approach to facial wounds and is considered a pioneer of facial reconstructive surgery?

options.A: Hippolyte Morestin
options.B: Harold Gillies
options.C: Vilray Blair
options.D: Johannes Esser

exp: Hippolyte Morestin, working with the dentist Charles Valadier at Val-de-Grâce, first grasped the value of the surgeon–dental team approach to facial wounds and is regarded as a pioneer of facial reconstructive surgery (he in turn inspired Gillies). The distractors had distinct roles: B, Gillies, carried the team model to Sidcup; C, Blair, organized US units and certification; D, Esser, codified flaps — but the team-approach pioneer here is Morestin.
hy: Morestin (+ Valadier) = pioneer of the facial-reconstruction team approach.
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: Which American surgeon, trained at Sidcup, established the first independent US unit for complex maxillofacial injuries (Walter Reed), organized the first US training program at Washington University in St. Louis, and in 1937 organized the American Board to certify plastic surgeons?

options.A: Vilray Blair
options.B: Truman Brophy
options.C: Jacques Maliniak
options.D: Jerome Webster

exp: Vilray Blair, trained at Sidcup, established the first independent US maxillofacial unit (Walter Reed), created the first US training program at Washington University in St. Louis, and organized the American Board (1937) to certify plastic surgeons. The distractors founded other things: B, Brophy, started the first society (1921); C, Maliniak, founded the American Society of Plastic Surgeons (1931); D, Webster, was a founding father/historian of US plastic surgery but not the Board's organizer.
hy: Vilray Blair = first US unit + American Board (1937).
src: Neligan I.2 · Plastic surgery in the US
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: The first scientific society in the field, the American Association of Oral and Plastic Surgeons (1921, initially requiring both MD and DDS degrees), was established by whom?

options.A: Truman Brophy
options.B: Jacques Maliniak
options.C: Vilray Blair
options.D: Maurice Coelst

exp: Truman Brophy established the first scientific society — the American Association of Oral and Plastic Surgeons (1921) — and championed cooperation between oral and plastic surgeons, initially requiring both MD and DDS degrees. The distractors founded *later/other* bodies: B, Maliniak, the American Society of Plastic Surgeons (1931); C, Blair, the American Board (1937); D, Coelst, the European *Société de Chirurgie Structive* (1936) and its journal.
hy: First society (1921, oral + plastic) = Brophy.
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: The American Society of Plastic Surgeons was founded in 1931 by which surgeon?

options.A: Jacques Maliniak
options.B: Truman Brophy
options.C: Eastman Sheehan
options.D: Jerome Webster

exp: Jacques Maliniak founded the American Society of Plastic Surgeons in 1931 (and was a prolific author with a large New York nose-and-breast practice). The distractors founded different institutions: B, Brophy, the first (oral + plastic) society in 1921; C, Sheehan, was an influential course director/society president but not the ASPS founder; D, Webster, a founding father of US plastic surgery, not the society's founder.
hy: American Society of Plastic Surgeons (1931) = Maliniak.
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: Anesthesia for facial surgery improved considerably after World War I thanks to a clinician who developed nasal and endotracheal intubation. Who was he?

options.A: Ivan Magill
options.B: Archibald McIndoe
options.C: Rainsford Mowlem
options.D: Pomfret Kilner

exp: Ivan Magill developed nasal and endotracheal intubation, greatly improving anesthesia for facial surgery after WWI (his name endures in the Magill forceps and tube). The distractors are British plastic surgeons of the era, not the anesthesia innovator: B, McIndoe; C, Mowlem; and D, Kilner were three of the "Big Four" reconstructive surgeons (with Gillies), but the intubation advance is Magill's.
hy: Endotracheal/nasal intubation for facial surgery = Magill.
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: The chapter dates the origin of aesthetic surgery to 1845, when a surgeon described external incisions to reduce large hanging noses (altering the nasolabial angle and size). Who was this surgeon?

options.A: Johann Dieffenbach
options.B: Jacques Joseph
options.C: John Orlando Roe
options.D: Charles Miller

exp: The chapter dates the origin of aesthetic surgery to 1845, when Johann Dieffenbach described external incisions to reduce large hanging noses (changing the nasolabial angle and size). The distractors come later: B, Jacques Joseph, codified aesthetic rhinoplasty around 1900; C, John Orlando Roe, did the first intranasal reduction (1887); D, Charles Miller, was the early-1900s "featural imperfections"/paraffin figure — all after Dieffenbach's 1845 origin.
hy: Origin of aesthetic surgery = Dieffenbach, 1845 (nose reduction).
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: Correction of prominent ears, performed in 1881 by the New York surgeon Edward Ely, is regarded as among the first purely cosmetic operations. What procedure does this represent?

options.A: Otoplasty
options.B: Rhinoplasty
options.C: Blepharoplasty
options.D: Rhytidectomy

exp: Edward Ely's 1881 correction of prominent ears — an otoplasty — is cited among the first purely cosmetic operations. The distractors are other cosmetic procedures credited to different pioneers: B, rhinoplasty (Roe/Joseph); C, blepharoplasty (e.g., Bourguet's transconjunctival approach); D, rhytidectomy/facelift (Holländer, 1901) — none of which is Ely's prominent-ear operation.
hy: Edward Ely (1881) = early otoplasty (one of the first cosmetic operations).
src: Neligan I.2 · Aesthetic surgery — first cosmetic procedures
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: In 1887 an otolaryngologist from Rochester, NY, demonstrated that reduction of a bulbous "pug nose" could be done intranasally, under local anesthesia, on an outpatient basis. Who pioneered this endonasal (intranasal) approach to rhinoplasty?

options.A: John Orlando Roe
options.B: Jacques Joseph
options.C: Robert Weir
options.D: Johann Dieffenbach

exp: John Orlando Roe, a Rochester otolaryngologist, showed in 1887 that a bulbous "pug nose" could be reduced *intranasally*, under local anesthesia, on an outpatient basis — pioneering the endonasal approach. The distractors are different rhinoplasty figures: B, Jacques Joseph, codified the European technique somewhat later; C, Robert Weir, described alar-base (external) excision; D, Dieffenbach, used *external* incisions in 1845 — not the intranasal route.
hy: First intranasal (endonasal) rhinoplasty = John Orlando Roe, 1887.
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: The excision of the alar base to lower an overprojected nose is eponymously named after which New York general surgeon who described it in 1892?

options.A: Robert Weir
options.B: John Orlando Roe
options.C: Edward Ely
options.D: Jacques Joseph

exp: Alar-base excision to lower an overprojected nose is the eponymous "Weir operation," described by the New York general surgeon Robert Weir (1892). The distractors are adjacent rhinoplasty pioneers: B, Roe, did intranasal hump reduction; C, Ely, did prominent-ear correction; D, Joseph, codified aesthetic rhinoplasty in Berlin — but alar-base excision is Weir's.
