hy: Weir operation = alar-base excision.
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: In Berlin, which surgeon codified the steps of aesthetic rhinoplasty in a rigorous sequence still essentially used today, and authored the monumental *Nasenplastik* (1931) — earning recognition as the father of modern aesthetic rhinoplasty?

options.A: Jacques Joseph
options.B: Johann Dieffenbach
options.C: John Orlando Roe
options.D: Eugen Holländer

exp: Jacques Joseph of Berlin codified aesthetic rhinoplasty into a rigorous, reproducible sequence (still essentially used) and authored the monumental *Nasenplastik* (1931), earning recognition as the father of modern aesthetic rhinoplasty. The distractors precede or differ from him: B, Dieffenbach, gave the 1845 origin; C, Roe, the first intranasal reduction (1887); D, Holländer, the first facelift — none codified rhinoplasty as Joseph did.
hy: Jacques Joseph = father of modern aesthetic rhinoplasty (*Nasenplastik*, 1931).
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: Which Berlin surgeon is credited with the first reported facelift (rhytidectomy), excising skin along the hairline of an aging patient (a Polish aristocrat) in 1901, and also reported the first cases of facial atrophy treated by fat injection?

options.A: Eugen Holländer
options.B: Jacques Joseph
options.C: Suzanne Noël
options.D: Julien Bourguet

exp: Eugen Holländer is credited with the first reported facelift (1901, excising hairline skin of an aging Polish aristocrat) and with the first literature account of facial atrophy treated by fat injection. The distractors are other aesthetic figures: B, Jacques Joseph, dominated rhinoplasty; C, Suzanne Noël, wrote the first aesthetic-surgery text by a woman and ran a facial-rejuvenation practice; D, Bourguet, introduced transconjunctival blepharoplasty — but the first facelift is Holländer's.
hy: First facelift (and first fat-injection report) = Holländer, 1901.
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: The transconjunctival approach for the correction of baggy lower eyelids was first presented in 1929 by which Parisian cosmetic surgeon?

options.A: Julien Bourguet
options.B: Suzanne Noël
options.C: Raymond Passot
options.D: Eugen Holländer

exp: Julien Bourguet, a Parisian cosmetic surgeon, first presented the transconjunctival approach to baggy lower-eyelid correction in 1929. The distractors are contemporaries with different contributions: B, Suzanne Noël, focused on outpatient facial rejuvenation and authored an early text; C, Raymond Passot, advanced breast-ptosis/abdomen/face techniques; D, Holländer, did the first facelift — none described the transconjunctival blepharoplasty.
hy: Transconjunctival lower blepharoplasty = Bourguet, 1929.
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: Charles C. Miller published *The Correction of Featural Imperfections* (1907) and made extensive use of paraffin injections as a filler. He is alternately regarded as which of the following?

options.A: The "father of modern cosmetic surgery" by some, and an unscrupulous charlatan by others
options.B: The founder of the first plastic surgery scientific society
options.C: The first surgeon to perform an autologous skin graft
options.D: The codifier of modern aesthetic rhinoplasty technique

exp: Charles C. Miller is the polarizing figure regarded as the "father of modern cosmetic surgery" by some and an unscrupulous charlatan by others; his 1907 *The Correction of Featural Imperfections* illustrated facial operations, and he relied heavily on paraffin injections (later abandoned for paraffinomas/embolism). The distractors belong to others: B, the first society, was Brophy's; C, the first autologous skin graft, was Baronio's; D, the codification of aesthetic rhinoplasty, was Jacques Joseph's.
hy: Charles Miller = controversial "father of modern cosmetic surgery" (paraffin era).
chname: History of Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery

stem: A 24-year-old requests a third rhinoplasty for a nasal "deformity" that the surgeon and two prior surgeons cannot appreciate; she spends hours daily checking mirrors and avoids work because of it. Body dysmorphic disorder is suspected. What is the approximate prevalence of BDD reported in cosmetic clinics (and notably higher in rhinoplasty clinics)?
exp: The chapter puts BDD prevalence at around 15% in cosmetic clinics, and explicitly higher in rhinoplasty clinics — meaning roughly one affected patient per clinic or more. The distractors misstate the figure: B (~2%) is closer to the *general-population* BDD prevalence, not the cosmetic-clinic rate; C (~30%) and D (~50%) overstate it (50% is the kind of figure quoted for *postoperative dissatisfaction* among BDD patients, not BDD prevalence itself).
hy: BDD ≈ 15% in cosmetic clinics, higher in rhinoplasty.
chname: Applying Psychology to Routine Plastic Surgery Practice

stem: For the same patient, screening confirms body dysmorphic disorder. According to the chapter, what is the recommended management?

options.A: Screen out of the surgical pathway and offer cognitive behavior therapy and/or medication
options.B: Proceed with the requested rhinoplasty since a good technical result will resolve the concern
options.C: Offer a smaller "compromise" procedure to partially satisfy the patient
options.D: Defer all treatment indefinitely with no referral, as BDD is untreatable

exp: Because BDD patients are heavily over-represented among those dissatisfied after surgery, the recommendation is to screen them out of the surgical pathway and treat with cognitive behavior therapy and/or medication (the procedure of choice). The distractors are the classic traps: B assumes a good technical result satisfies a distorted body image (it usually does not — the patient does not perceive the feature as the surgeon does); C, a "compromise" procedure, still exposes a high-risk patient to surgery; D wrongly calls BDD untreatable when CBT/medication are evidence-based.
hy: BDD → screen out of surgery; first-line = CBT and/or medication.
src: Neligan I.3 · Managing mental health issues
chname: Applying Psychology to Routine Plastic Surgery Practice

stem: How does the chapter conceptualize body dysmorphic disorder, and why is it the single most important psychological factor to detect before cosmetic surgery?

options.A: It is an extreme form of body-image anxiety causing significant distress/impairment, and BDD patients are markedly over-represented among those dissatisfied postoperatively
options.B: It is a transient dissatisfaction that reliably resolves once the targeted feature is surgically altered
options.C: It is a delusional disorder that legally bars any medical or psychological treatment
options.D: It is a mood disorder whose core feature is anhedonia rather than appearance preoccupation

exp: BDD is framed as an extreme form of body-image anxiety, with symptoms severe enough to cause significant distress and functional impairment, and its detection matters because BDD patients are very clearly over-represented among the postoperatively dissatisfied. The distractors are wrong characterizations: B describes the *false hope* that surgery resolves the concern (it typically does not); C overstates BDD as a treatment-barring delusional disorder; D confuses BDD with major depression (anhedonia), whereas BDD's core is appearance preoccupation.
hy: BDD = severe body-image anxiety; top predictor of postop dissatisfaction.
chname: Applying Psychology to Routine Plastic Surgery Practice

stem: A 16-year-old requests cosmetic breast augmentation, supported by a parent. Citing consensus from professional bodies, what does the chapter advise regarding aesthetic procedures and age?

options.A: Aesthetic procedures are contraindicated for people under the age of 18
options.B: Aesthetic procedures are acceptable at any age with parental consent
options.C: Aesthetic procedures are contraindicated only under the age of 16
options.D: Aesthetic procedures are permitted from age 14 if physical development is complete

exp: Professional-body consensus cited in the chapter holds that aesthetic procedures are contraindicated under the age of 18 — adolescents' identity, self-esteem, and even physical development (e.g., breast size) may be incomplete. The distractors loosen this: B (any age with parental consent), C (only under 16), and D (from 14 if developed) all contradict the stated under-18 contraindication.
hy: Aesthetic procedures contraindicated under age 18.
chname: Applying Psychology to Routine Plastic Surgery Practice

stem: The chapter notes a markedly higher burden of anxiety in cosmetic settings. Approximately what proportion of cosmetic patients are reported to be taking an anxiolytic or antidepressant at the time of referral, compared with the general population?

options.A: Up to 18%, versus about 5% in the general population
options.B: About 5%, versus about 1% in the general population
options.C: About 40%, versus about 20% in the general population
options.D: About 50%, versus about 25% in the general population

exp: The chapter reports up to 18% of cosmetic patients on an anxiolytic or antidepressant at referral, versus about 5% of the general population — a roughly three- to four-fold excess. The distractors invert or shrink the gap: B understates the cosmetic-clinic figure; C and D inflate both numbers well beyond what is cited.
hy: Anxiolytic/antidepressant use: ~18% cosmetic vs ~5% general population.
chname: Applying Psychology to Routine Plastic Surgery Practice

stem: Among people living with a visible difference (disfigurement), approximately what proportion experience significant psychological difficulties (transient or enduring)?

options.A: About one-third
options.B: Nearly all (over 90%)
options.C: About two-thirds
options.D: A small minority (under 5%)

exp: About one-third of people with a visible difference experience significant difficulties, transiently or enduringly — and, importantly, the degree of distress is poorly predicted by the physical severity of the disfigurement. The distractors over- or under-state it: B (>90%) and C (two-thirds) exaggerate the proportion, while D (<5%) minimizes a substantial burden.
hy: ~one-third with a visible difference have significant distress.
chname: Applying Psychology to Routine Plastic Surgery Practice

stem: The chapter describes appearance dissatisfaction in the general population as now "normative." Approximately what proportion of young people and adults report dissatisfaction with one or more aspects of their looks?

options.A: In excess of two-thirds
options.B: About one-quarter
options.C: About one-tenth
options.D: Fewer than one in twenty

exp: In excess of two-thirds of young people and adults report dissatisfaction with one or more aspects of their appearance — a level now considered "normative" and rising year on year. The distractors understate a phenomenon the chapter stresses is widespread: B (one-quarter), C (one-tenth), and D (<1 in 20) are all far below the cited figure.
hy: >two-thirds report appearance dissatisfaction (now normative).
chname: Applying Psychology to Routine Plastic Surgery Practice

stem: Which factor is described as among the STRONGEST predictors of how well a person adjusts to a visible difference?

options.A: The person's dispositional optimism/pessimism and reliance of self-worth on others' opinions
options.B: The etiology and anatomical site of the disfigurement
options.C: The objective severity and extent of the disfigurement
options.D: The length of time elapsed since the disfigurement appeared

exp: The strongest predictors of adjustment are dispositional/psychosocial: optimism vs pessimism, how much self-worth depends on others' opinions, satisfaction with social support, a sense of life meaning, and social skill. The distractors are precisely the variables the chapter calls *poor* predictors: B (etiology/site), C (severity/extent), and D (time elapsed) all correlate weakly with actual adjustment.
hy: Best predictors of adjustment = dispositional optimism + social/psychological factors, not the physical defect.
chname: Applying Psychology to Routine Plastic Surgery Practice

stem: Contrary to many clinicians' assumptions, which group of variables does the chapter identify as POOR predictors of psychological adjustment to a disfigurement?

options.A: The "physical" parameters — etiology, severity, extent, and the body part affected
options.B: The patient's degree of dispositional optimism
options.C: The patient's satisfaction with social support
options.D: The patient's sense that life has meaning and purpose

exp: The chapter states plainly that the "physical" parameters — etiology, severity, extent, and the body part affected — are in fact poor predictors of adjustment, and that time is not necessarily a great healer. The distractors (B optimism, C social-support satisfaction, D sense of meaning/purpose) are the *strong* predictors, i.e., the opposite of what the question asks.
hy: Physical disfigurement parameters = poor predictors of adjustment.
chname: Applying Psychology to Routine Plastic Surgery Practice

stem: A surgeon wants a brief instrument specifically to flag signs and symptoms of body dysmorphic disorder in cosmetic patients. Which tool is the 9-item scale designed for this purpose?

options.A: The Cosmetic Procedures Screening Questionnaire (COPS)
options.B: The BREAST-Q
options.C: The Patient Assessment Tools (PATs)
options.D: The FACE-Q

exp: The Cosmetic Procedures Screening Questionnaire (COPS) is the 9-item scale designed to detect signs and symptoms of BDD. The distractors are outcome/other tools: B, the BREAST-Q, and D, the FACE-Q, are procedure-specific PROMs (not BDD screens); C, the PATs, are a structured-interview *framework* for goals/risk, not a brief 9-item BDD scale.
hy: COPS = 9-item BDD screening questionnaire.
chname: Applying Psychology to Routine Plastic Surgery Practice

stem: Which assessment approach is described as a structured-interview framework (with pre- and post-procedure versions) that identifies a patient's aesthetic and psychosocial goals and links the response profile to evidence-based management suggestions?

options.A: The Patient Assessment Tools (PATs)
options.B: The Cosmetic Procedures Screening Questionnaire (COPS)
options.C: The QPROMS suite
options.D: A standard depression rating scale

exp: The Patient Assessment Tools (PATs) are a structured-interview framework with pre- and post-procedure versions that elicit aesthetic and psychosocial goals, flag risk factors, and link the response profile to evidence-based management suggestions. The distractors differ in function: B, COPS, is a brief BDD screen; C, QPROMS, measure outcomes/satisfaction; D, a depression rating scale, assesses mood, not cosmetic goals/risk.
hy: PATs = structured-interview goals/risk framework (pre- and post-op).
chname: Applying Psychology to Routine Plastic Surgery Practice

stem: The procedure-specific QPROMS — including the FACE-Q, BODY-Q, and BREAST-Q — serve which purpose according to the chapter?

options.A: They are patient-reported outcome measures of surgical impact and satisfaction, NOT generic screening tools for psychological vulnerability
options.B: They are validated generic screening tools for detecting body dysmorphic disorder preoperatively
options.C: They are clinician-rated scales of technical operative success
options.D: They are diagnostic instruments for depression and anxiety disorders

exp: QPROMS such as the FACE-Q, BODY-Q, and BREAST-Q are patient-reported outcome measures of surgical impact and satisfaction, and the chapter explicitly notes they are NOT designed as generic screening tools for psychological vulnerability. The distractors miscast them: B (BDD screen), C (clinician-rated technical success), and D (depression/anxiety diagnosis) all assign a role the chapter says these PROMs do not serve.
hy: QPROMS (FACE-Q/BODY-Q/BREAST-Q) = outcome measures, not vulnerability screens.
chname: Applying Psychology to Routine Plastic Surgery Practice

stem: Two psychological processes are said to characterize people who intend to pursue cosmetic surgery: their degree of susceptibility to sociocultural appearance messages, and the degree to which they perceive those messages as personally relevant. What is the second process called?

options.A: Internalization
options.B: Catastrophization
options.C: Habituation
options.D: Dissociation

exp: The second process is internalization — the degree to which a person perceives sociocultural appearance messages as personally relevant (paired with susceptibility, the degree of exposure/responsiveness to those messages). The distractors are unrelated psychological terms: B (catastrophization) is a cognitive distortion in anxiety/depression; C (habituation) is decreasing response with repeated exposure; D (dissociation) is detachment from experience — none is the process described.
hy: Susceptibility + internalization characterize surgery-seekers.
chname: Applying Psychology to Routine Plastic Surgery Practice

stem: A severely depressed patient insists on cosmetic surgery, believing it will lift their mood. Beyond the lack of evidence that surgery treats depression, what specific concern does the chapter raise about proceeding?

options.A: Depression impairs attention, memory, and information processing, and a severely depressed patient may lack the capacity to give informed consent
options.B: Antidepressants are an absolute contraindication to all elective surgery
options.C: Depression guarantees an excellent objective result but a poor subjective one
options.D: Depressed patients are legally prohibited from undergoing any elective procedure

exp: Depression directly impairs attention, memory, and the capacity to process and absorb procedural and risk information, so a severely depressed patient may lack the capacity to give informed consent — a reason to pause beyond the absence of evidence that surgery treats mood. The distractors overreach: B (antidepressants as absolute contraindication) and D (legal prohibition) are false blanket claims; C wrongly promises an excellent objective result.
hy: Severe depression can impair capacity for informed consent.
chname: Applying Psychology to Routine Plastic Surgery Practice

stem: The chapter comments on the value of mandatory "cooling-off" periods before cosmetic surgery. What is its position?

options.A: They are advised but have no psychological value on their own and must be used constructively
options.B: They are the single most effective tool for reducing postoperative dissatisfaction
options.C: They are unnecessary and should be abandoned
options.D: They reliably cause most vulnerable patients to abandon surgery

exp: Cooling-off periods are advised but, the chapter stresses, have no psychological value on their own — they help only if used constructively (e.g., to consult information or discuss with others). The distractors overstate them: B (most effective single tool) and D (reliably deter vulnerable patients) credit them with power they lack, while C (abandon them) goes too far the other way.
hy: Cooling-off periods: advised but valueless unless used constructively.
chname: Applying Psychology to Routine Plastic Surgery Practice

stem: The chapter recommends dividing a patient's goals into "physical" and "psychosocial" categories. What is the central reason for this distinction?

options.A: The surgeon can influence physical goals but has no direct control over psychosocial outcomes such as confidence or feeling "more feminine"
options.B: Physical goals are unimportant and only psychosocial goals should guide surgery
options.C: Psychosocial goals are always achieved once the physical change is made
options.D: Only psychosocial goals can be measured objectively, unlike physical goals

exp: Goals are split into physical and psychosocial because the surgeon can influence only the physical change (bigger, smaller, more symmetrical) and has no direct control over psychosocial outcomes such as confidence, reduced anxiety, or "feeling more feminine." The distractors invert this: B dismisses physical goals; C wrongly assumes psychosocial gains automatically follow; D reverses measurability (physical goals are the measurable ones).
hy: Separate physical (surgeon-controlled) from psychosocial (not surgeon-controlled) goals.
chname: Applying Psychology to Routine Plastic Surgery Practice

stem: Cosmetic surgery is described as more likely to be effective when patient motivation is of which type?

options.A: Intrinsic — undertaken for personal reasons without undue pressure from others
options.B: Extrinsic — primarily to please a partner or gain professional advantage
options.C: Driven by comparison with celebrities and influencers
options.D: Driven by promotional advertising from cosmetic providers

exp: Outcomes are better when motivation is intrinsic — pursued for personal reasons without undue external pressure. The distractors are the poorer-prognosis motivators the chapter contrasts against intrinsic drive: B (extrinsic — to please a partner/career), C (celebrity/influencer comparison), and D (provider advertising) all predict greater risk of dissatisfaction.
hy: Intrinsic motivation → better cosmetic-surgery outcomes.
chname: Applying Psychology to Routine Plastic Surgery Practice

stem: A normal-weight patient requests liposuction, expressing intense preoccupation with body fat. Why does the chapter flag eating disorders as requiring additional assessment in this scenario?

options.A: The request for surgery (e.g., liposuction) may be a reflection of the underlying eating disorder rather than a true surgical indication
options.B: Eating disorders make patients ideal liposuction candidates
options.C: Liposuction is an evidence-based treatment for eating disorders
options.D: Eating disorders only affect anesthetic risk, not the surgical indication

exp: A request for liposuction in a preoccupied, normal-weight patient may be a manifestation of an underlying eating disorder rather than a genuine surgical indication — hence the call for additional assessment. The distractors are dangerous inversions: B and C wrongly treat the eating disorder as an indication for or beneficiary of surgery; D minimizes it to an anesthetic-only issue, ignoring the psychiatric driver of the request.
hy: Liposuction request may be a symptom of an eating disorder — assess first.
src: Neligan I.3 · Managing mental health issues
chname: Applying Psychology to Routine Plastic Surgery Practice

stem: According to the synthesis of evidence, cosmetic surgery is MORE likely to be effective under which of the following circumstances?

options.A: When a specific, targeted feature is addressed rather than a general improvement in body image
options.B: When it is offered as a first-line treatment for depression
options.C: When the patient holds broad expectations of overall body-image transformation
options.D: When signs of BDD are present but left uninvestigated

exp: Synthesized evidence shows surgery is more effective when a specific, targeted feature is addressed, rather than when patients expect a general improvement in body image. The distractors are the explicitly *unfavorable* conditions: B (first-line for depression), C (broad body-image expectations), and D (uninvestigated BDD) are each listed as settings where surgery is less likely to succeed.
hy: Target a specific feature → better outcomes than chasing global body-image change.
chname: Applying Psychology to Routine Plastic Surgery Practice

stem: The chapter emphasizes that a surgeon's manner strongly shapes patient satisfaction. Which statement reflects its guidance on the consultation?

options.A: Nonverbal behavior (eye contact, posture, unrushed manner) is more powerful than words, and the surgeon should not rush the patient
options.B: A well-rehearsed verbal script matters far more than nonverbal behavior
options.C: Filling silences quickly with a menu of procedure options reassures patients
options.D: Efficient, brief consultations consistently produce the most satisfied patients

exp: The chapter stresses that nonverbal behavior — eye contact, posture, spatial orientation, an unrushed and interested manner — is more powerful than words, and that the surgeon should not rush the patient (silence is acceptable). The distractors contradict this: B over-prioritizes a verbal script; C (filling silence with a procedure menu) is named as a pitfall linked to later dissatisfaction; D (brief, efficient consults) runs against the "don't rush" guidance.
hy: Nonverbal rapport > words; don't rush the consultation.
chname: Applying Psychology to Routine Plastic Surgery Practice

stem: A residency curriculum frames every clinical-ethics discussion around four moral principles first articulated by Beauchamp and Childress in 1979. Which set of four principles defines this framework ("principlism")?

options.A: Respect for autonomy, beneficence, nonmaleficence, and justice
options.B: Confidentiality, fidelity, veracity, and consent
options.C: Utility, equity, solidarity, and dignity
options.D: Competence, compassion, advocacy, and stewardship

exp: US medical ethics most commonly follows "principlism," articulated by Beauchamp and Childress (1979), which reasons from four moral principles: respect for autonomy (the patient's right to self-determination and to accept or refuse care), beneficence (promoting the patient's good and preventing harm), nonmaleficence (not intentionally inflicting harm), and justice (fair, equal distribution of benefits, risks, and costs). The distractors borrow real concepts from elsewhere: the confidentiality/fidelity/veracity grouping names downstream professional duties rather than the four foundational principles; the utility/equity/solidarity set evokes public-health and bioethics vocabulary but is not principlism; and the competence/compassion set describes virtues/professionalism traits, not the Beauchamp–Childress framework.
hy: Principlism = autonomy, beneficence, nonmaleficence, justice (Beauchamp & Childress, 1979).
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: During informed-consent teaching a resident is asked which principle is violated when a surgeon *intentionally* inflicts harm on a patient. Which principle does this describe?

options.A: Nonmaleficence
options.B: Beneficence
options.C: Respect for autonomy
options.D: Distributive justice

exp: Nonmaleficence is the principle that a physician must not intentionally inflict harm — the classic "first, do no harm." The distractors are the other three principles, each defined differently: beneficence is the positive duty to *do and promote good* (not merely avoid harm); respect for autonomy concerns the patient's right to self-determination and informed refusal; and distributive justice concerns the fair, equal distribution of benefits and burdens across patients. The discriminating point is that nonmaleficence is a *negative* duty (refrain from harming) whereas beneficence is its positive counterpart.
hy: Nonmaleficence = do not intentionally inflict harm.
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: A systematic review of the plastic surgery literature found the four core ethical principles are NOT given equal attention. According to the chapter, which principle is the most fully explored, and which the least?

options.A: Respect for autonomy is most explored; distributive justice is least
options.B: Beneficence is most explored; respect for autonomy is least
options.C: Nonmaleficence is most explored; beneficence is least
options.D: Distributive justice is most explored; nonmaleficence is least

exp: A systematic review of the plastic surgery literature found respect for autonomy to be the most fully explored core principle (it encompasses informed consent, photography, and marketing), while distributive justice is discussed least often — though it grows in importance with diversity, equity, and inclusion initiatives. The chapter ranks the middle two as beneficence (second most common) and nonmaleficence (third). The distractors invert or scramble this published ordering: beneficence, nonmaleficence, and justice are each named, but none is the *most* explored, and autonomy is never the least.
hy: PS literature: autonomy explored most, distributive justice least.
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: A surgeon weighs how to allocate limited operating-room time so that the benefits, risks, and costs of care are distributed equally and patients are treated similarly and fairly. Which ethical principle does this most directly invoke?

options.A: Distributive justice
options.B: Respect for autonomy
options.C: Beneficence
options.D: Nonmaleficence

exp: Distributive justice dictates that patients be treated similarly and fairly so that benefits, risks, and costs are distributed equally among them — exactly the lens for allocating a scarce resource like OR time. The distractors are the other three principles applied to different problems: respect for autonomy governs the individual patient's consent and self-determination; beneficence is the duty to promote a given patient's good; and nonmaleficence is the duty to avoid inflicting harm — none of which is fundamentally about *fair allocation across* patients.
hy: Distributive justice = fair, equal allocation of benefits/risks/costs.
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: The chapter illustrates early religious hostility to plastic surgery with the fate of one founding figure of the specialty, whose body was exhumed from consecrated ground after the Church excommunicated him posthumously. Who was this surgeon?

options.A: Gaspare Tagliacozzi
options.B: Ambroise Paré
options.C: Heinrich von Pfolspeundt
options.D: Aulus Cornelius Celsus

exp: The chapter uses Gaspare Tagliacozzi — widely considered one of the fathers of the specialty — to illustrate early religious hostility: church authorities excommunicated him after his death and exhumed his body from the church cemetery, reburying it in unconsecrated ground. The distractors are real historical surgeons with different stories: Paré was the Renaissance battlefield surgeon who abandoned boiling-oil cautery; von Pfolspeundt was a 15th-century German who described nose repair; and Celsus was the Roman author regarded as the earliest writer on plastic-surgical topics — none of whom suffered Tagliacozzi's posthumous excommunication.
hy: Tagliacozzi excommunicated and exhumed — emblem of early religious objection to plastic surgery.
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: According to the chapter, plastic surgery only gained true legitimacy as a specialty during which historical period?

options.A: The two World Wars, driven by the need for reconstructive procedures
options.B: The Renaissance, with Tagliacozzi's published nasal reconstruction
options.C: The late nineteenth century, with the advent of antisepsis and anesthesia
options.D: The 1960s–70s, with the rise of microsurgery and replantation

exp: The chapter states that the field only gained true legitimacy during the two World Wars, when the sheer need for reconstructive procedures for facial and limb injuries established plastic surgery as essential. The distractors name genuine milestones that did *not* confer that legitimacy in the chapter's account: Tagliacozzi's Renaissance work was met with religious objection rather than acceptance; antisepsis and anesthesia advanced surgery broadly but are not credited here with legitimizing the specialty; and microsurgery is a much later technical development.
hy: Plastic surgery gained legitimacy during the two World Wars (reconstructive need).
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: Reflecting evolving breast-implant regulation, the FDA now requires a specific step before prosthetic breast implantation to ensure the patient understands the risks. What does the FDA require?

options.A: A checklist completed by both the patient and the operating surgeon prior to implantation
options.B: A mandatory 30-day waiting period between consent and surgery
options.C: An independent second-surgeon opinion documented in the chart
options.D: Annual serologic surveillance for implant-associated illness

exp: Because regulators concluded that surgeons and device companies could not be relied upon to police themselves, the FDA now requires a checklist to be completed by *both* the patient and the operating surgeon before prosthetic breast implantation, to document shared understanding of the risks. The distractors are plausible regulatory mechanisms that the chapter does not describe: a fixed waiting period, a mandatory second-surgeon opinion, and routine serologic surveillance are all real-sounding safeguards but are not the FDA's stated requirement here.
hy: FDA requires a patient-and-surgeon checklist before breast-implant surgery.
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: When the FDA reviewed public comments related to breast implants, how were patients' chief concerns distributed?

options.A: Nearly half cited inadequacy of informed consent, about a third the content/language of forms, and about a quarter physician-provided education
options.B: Nearly all comments praised the existing consent process as adequate
options.C: The majority focused on implant cost rather than the consent process
options.D: Concerns were evenly split one-third each among surgical skill, anesthesia, and cost

exp: Reviewing public comments on its breast-implant website, the FDA found that nearly half expressed concern over inadequacy of informed consent, nearly one-third denigrated the content and language of existing forms, and about one-quarter disparaged physician-provided patient education — a portrait of widespread dissatisfaction with the consent *process*, not the implants alone. The distractors misrepresent this distribution: comments did not praise the process, did not center on cost, and were not split evenly across skill/anesthesia/cost.
hy: FDA comments: ~½ consent inadequacy, ~⅓ form content, ~¼ physician education.
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: A practice in which the *critical* portions of more than one operation occur simultaneously under a single senior surgeon has been curtailed, with only very limited emergency exceptions. What is this practice called?

options.A: Concurrent surgery
options.B: Overlapping surgery
options.C: Delegated surgery
options.D: Tag-team surgery

exp: Concurrent surgery — in which the *critical* portions of more than one procedure occur simultaneously under a single senior surgeon — has been curtailed, with only very limited emergency exceptions, because no patient should have the surgeon absent for the key part of their operation. The distractor "overlapping surgery" is the deliberately contrasted term (only *non-critical* portions coincide and it remains permissible with disclosure); "delegated" and "tag-team" surgery are not the chapter's defined terms. The discriminating point is the word *critical*: simultaneity of the critical portions is what makes it concurrent and impermissible.
hy: Concurrent surgery = critical portions overlap → curtailed.
src: Neligan I.4 · Concurrent and overlapping surgery
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: A senior surgeon begins closing one patient while a second patient is being positioned and prepped in an adjacent room, such that only *non-critical* portions of the two procedures coincide. Which term best describes this arrangement?

options.A: Overlapping surgery
options.B: Concurrent surgery
options.C: Ghost surgery
options.D: Itinerant surgery

exp: Overlapping surgery is the more nuanced, still-permissible practice in which only the *non-critical* portions of two procedures coincide (e.g., closing one case while the next is positioned), so the surgeon is present for each operation's critical part. The distractor "concurrent surgery" is its impermissible counterpart (critical portions overlap); "ghost surgery" (operating without the patient's knowledge of who is operating) and "itinerant surgery" (operating then leaving aftercare to others) are different ethical problems not used here.
hy: Overlapping surgery = only non-critical portions overlap (permissible with disclosure).
src: Neligan I.4 · Concurrent and overlapping surgery
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: The chapter notes that relationships with industry are pervasive in plastic surgery. Approximately what proportion of plastic surgeons are estimated to have such industry relationships?

options.A: Well over half
options.B: Fewer than one in ten
options.C: About one-quarter
options.D: Essentially none, since disclosure rules prohibit them

exp: The chapter states that estimates put well over half of plastic surgeons in relationships with industry, profit centers, or research efforts — making conflict of interest pervasive rather than exceptional. The distractors understate or deny this: fewer-than-one-in-ten and about-a-quarter both fall short of the cited majority, and the claim that disclosure rules eliminate such relationships is contradicted by the chapter, which notes routine *absence* of disclosure historically.
hy: Well over half of plastic surgeons have industry relationships.
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: A professional society is drafting a policy to address members' financial conflicts of interest. Consistent with the chapter's account of current best practice, what approach do most current policies emphasize?

options.A: Disclosure and management of conflicts (e.g., recusal, removal from discussion, abstention from voting) rather than their elimination
options.B: Complete elimination of all industry relationships as a condition of membership
options.C: Confidential internal reporting with no public disclosure of any conflict
options.D: Reliance on individual surgeons to self-police without any society oversight

exp: Current policy looks to *disclosure and management* of conflicts rather than their elimination — recognizing that industry relationships are pervasive and often productive. Management tools named include complete recusal, removal from discussion, and abstention from voting, and disclosure before/during presentations and in publications. The distractors describe approaches the chapter rejects or does not endorse: outright elimination of all relationships, purely confidential reporting with no public disclosure, and unsupervised self-policing (the very failure that prompted external regulation).
hy: COI → disclose and manage (recuse/abstain), not eliminate.
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: The chapter cites evidence that financial conflict of interest can bias the scientific record. What specific association is described?

options.A: Investigators with a financial COI were significantly more likely to publish positive conclusions than non-funded counterparts
options.B: Funded investigators were significantly less likely to complete and publish their studies
options.C: Financial COI had no measurable effect on the direction of published conclusions
options.D: Funded studies were significantly more likely to report negative or null results

exp: The chapter cites evidence that investigators with a financial COI were significantly more likely to publish positive conclusions than their non-funded counterparts — the core reason financial conflicts threaten the integrity of the literature (e.g., the breast-implant evidence base). The distractors invert or neutralize this finding: funding is not associated with *failure* to publish, does not leave conclusions unchanged, and does not bias toward negative/null results.
hy: Financial COI → more positive published conclusions.
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: Patient awareness of medical imperfection increased markedly after a landmark 2000 report on patient safety. Which report does the chapter credit with this shift?

options.A: The Institute of Medicine's *To Err Is Human: Building a Safer Health System*
options.B: The Patient Safety and Quality Improvement Act
options.C: *Forgive and Remember*, by Charles Bosk
options.D: The Daubert Supreme Court opinion

exp: The Institute of Medicine's 2000 report *To Err Is Human: Building a Safer Health System* is credited with sharply increasing patient (and professional) awareness of medical error and launching the modern patient-safety movement. The distractors are related but distinct: the Patient Safety and Quality Improvement Act is a 2005 *law* promoting error reporting; *Forgive and Remember* is Charles Bosk's sociological study of surgical error; and Daubert is the expert-testimony case — none is the landmark 2000 awareness report.
hy: *To Err Is Human* (IOM, 2000) = landmark patient-safety report.
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: A sociologist who studied error in surgical training concluded that some level of medical error is inevitable and that maintaining a humane environment for patients and providers is essential. Who was this sociologist?

options.A: Charles Bosk
options.B: Tom Beauchamp
options.C: Atul Gawande
options.D: Elizabeth Haiken

exp: The sociologist Charles Bosk (author of *Forgive and Remember*) studied ways to reduce error in medicine and concluded that some level of error is inevitable, so maintaining a humane environment for patients and providers is essential. The distractors are other named figures with different roles: Beauchamp co-authored the principlism framework; Atul Gawande is the surgeon-author quoted in the chapter's epigraph; and Elizabeth Haiken is the historian cited on the specialty's troubled social history — none is the sociologist of medical error.
hy: Charles Bosk: medical error is inevitable; keep care humane.
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: Among efforts to encourage error reporting for the purpose of education and quality improvement (rather than blame), the chapter specifically names which 2005 measure?

options.A: The Patient Safety and Quality Improvement Act of 2005
options.B: The Affordable Care Act of 2005
options.C: The Federal Rules of Evidence of 2005
options.D: The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 2005

exp: The chapter names the Patient Safety and Quality Improvement Act of 2005 as an effort to encourage error reporting for education and quality improvement rather than blame. The distractors are real or plausible statutes mismatched to this purpose and year: the Affordable Care Act addressed coverage (and is from 2010); the Federal Rules of Evidence govern expert testimony, not error reporting; and HIPAA (1996) governs privacy — none is the 2005 patient-safety reporting law.
hy: Patient Safety and Quality Improvement Act of 2005 = blame-free error reporting.
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: Although many physicians fear that apologizing for an error will prompt litigation, the chapter notes increasing adoption of policies built around three linked actions to increase transparency. Which set of actions?

options.A: Disclosure, apology, and offer of compensation
options.B: Documentation, deferral, and defense
options.C: Denial, delay, and settlement
options.D: Referral, recusal, and reassignment

exp: Increasingly adopted policies pair three actions — disclosure of the error, apology, and an offer of compensation — to increase transparency, which may benefit both quality improvement and professional well-being, despite physicians' fear that apology invites litigation. The distractors describe the opposite, defensive postures the chapter contrasts against transparency: document/defer/defend, deny/delay/settle, and refer/recuse/reassign all avoid candid disclosure rather than embrace it.
hy: Modern error policy = disclosure + apology + offer of compensation.
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: A plaintiff's expert proposes to testify using a novel, untested analytic method. Under which 1993 U.S. Supreme Court decision did the Court set the standard for the admissibility of such expert testimony?

options.A: Daubert v. Merrell Dow Pharmaceuticals, Inc.
options.B: Roe v. Wade
options.C: Canterbury v. Spence
options.D: Tarasoff v. Regents of the University of California

exp: In Daubert v. Merrell Dow Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (1993) the US Supreme Court set the standard for the admissibility of expert testimony, giving courts guidelines to judge whether an expert's methodology is valid and to exclude unreliable "junk science." The distractors are landmark cases on other issues: Roe v. Wade (abortion), Canterbury v. Spence (the informed-consent "reasonable patient" standard), and Tarasoff (the duty to warn/protect third parties) — none concerns the admissibility of expert scientific testimony.
hy: Daubert (1993) = admissibility standard for expert testimony.
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: Under the Daubert standard, a court weighs several factors to judge whether an expert's methodology is valid. Which of the following is one of the Daubert factors?

options.A: Whether the theory or technique has a known or potential error rate and has been subjected to peer review and publication
options.B: Whether the expert is board-certified in the same subspecialty as the defendant
options.C: Whether the expert charges a fee below a fixed statutory cap
options.D: Whether a majority of a lay jury finds the testimony persuasive

exp: A recognized Daubert factor is whether the theory or technique has a known or potential error rate and has been subjected to peer review and publication (other factors include testability, maintenance of operating standards, and general acceptance in the relevant scientific community) — all aimed at filtering out unreliable methods. The distractors are not Daubert factors: subspecialty board match, a fee cap, and lay-jury persuasiveness concern who may serve or how testimony lands, not whether the *methodology* is scientifically valid.
hy: Daubert factors test the method (error rate, peer review, testability, acceptance).
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: Under Federal Rule 702, on what basis may a person be qualified to offer expert opinion testimony?

options.A: Knowledge, skill, experience, training, or education, provided the testimony rests on sufficient facts and reliable methods applied reliably to the case
options.B: Holding any current state medical license, regardless of field or experience
options.C: Prior service as an expert witness in at least ten comparable cases
options.D: Agreement by both parties' attorneys to waive challenges to the witness

exp: Under Federal Rule 702, a person qualified by knowledge, skill, experience, training, or education may give expert opinion if the testimony will help the trier of fact, is based on sufficient facts or data, is the product of reliable principles and methods, and those methods are reliably applied to the case. The distractors substitute lesser proxies for that standard: a bare medical license, a count of prior testimony, or attorney agreement none of which, by itself, satisfies Rule 702's reliability requirements.
hy: Rule 702: qualified by knowledge/skill/experience/training/education + reliable method reliably applied.
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: The chapter lists several areas from which malpractice liability can arise — negligence, insufficiently informed consent, purposeful misconduct, breach of contract, defamation, and breach of confidentiality. Which of these is identified as the *most common*?

options.A: Negligence
options.B: Breach of contract
options.C: Defamation
options.D: Purposeful misconduct

exp: Among the enumerated sources of malpractice liability — negligence, insufficiently informed consent, purposeful misconduct, breach of contract, defamation, breach of confidentiality, and failure to prevent foreseeable injury — negligence is identified as the most common. The distractors are genuine liability categories from the same list but are not the most frequent: breach of contract, defamation, and purposeful misconduct each arise in narrower circumstances than ordinary negligence.
hy: Negligence = most common basis of malpractice liability.
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: A patient sues after a technically sound elective procedure yields an unexpectedly poor cosmetic result. Applying the chapter's account of malpractice law, which statement is correct?

options.A: A poor outcome alone does not define malpractice; liability requires violation of the standard of care causally related to a distinct injury
options.B: Any poor cosmetic outcome after elective surgery is, by definition, malpractice
options.C: Standard of care is defined solely by the individual surgeon's personal best practice
options.D: The surgeon must prove their own innocence beyond a reasonable doubt

exp: A poor outcome alone does not define malpractice; the case hinges on violation of the standard of care (reasonable or ordinary care provided by local peers with similar practices) causally related to a distinct injury within a physician–patient relationship. The distractors misstate the law: a bad cosmetic result is not automatically malpractice; the standard of care is set by peer practice, not by one surgeon's personal ideal; and the burden is a civil preponderance on the plaintiff, not "beyond a reasonable doubt" on the defendant.
hy: Poor outcome ≠ malpractice; need standard-of-care breach + causation + injury.
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: In a malpractice case, the violation of the standard of care must be demonstrated to a particular evidentiary threshold. What is that threshold ("preponderance of the evidence")?

options.A: Greater than 50% (about 51%) likelihood — more likely than not
options.B: Beyond a reasonable doubt (about 99%)
options.C: Clear and convincing evidence (about 75%)
options.D: Any non-zero probability that harm occurred

exp: Civil malpractice is proven by a preponderance of the evidence — greater than 50% (about 51%), i.e., "more likely than not." The distractors are the wrong evidentiary standards: "beyond a reasonable doubt" (~99%) is the *criminal* standard; "clear and convincing evidence" is an intermediate civil standard used for specific issues; and any non-zero probability is far below the required threshold.
hy: Malpractice standard of proof = preponderance (>50%, ~51%).
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: The chapter examines plastic surgeons' use of social media as a practice-building tool. Which statement reflects its assessment of the content actually posted?

options.A: A large majority of posts have been judged purely promotional rather than educational, and patient confidentiality is difficult to preserve when images are used
options.B: The large majority of posts are rigorously educational and authored by board-certified plastic surgeons
options.C: Social media content is reliably unbiased and easily regulated by professional societies
options.D: Volume of posts weighs far more heavily toward medical professionals than toward patients

exp: The chapter judges that a large majority of plastic surgery social-media posts are purely promotional rather than educational, and warns that patient confidentiality is very difficult to preserve when images are used (consent currency is hard to maintain). The distractors reverse the chapter's findings: posts are *not* mostly rigorous education by board-certified surgeons; social media is described as a wide, often biased spectrum that *defies* easy regulation; and the volume of posts weighs far more heavily toward *patients* than toward medical professionals.
hy: Most PS social-media posts are promotional, not educational; confidentiality is hard to protect.
src: Neligan I.4 · Social media and advertising
chname: The Role of Ethics in Plastic Surgery and Medico-legal Issues

stem: A plastic surgery group wants to analyze the competitive environment of its local healthcare market — established rivals, potential new entrants, substitutes, suppliers, and buyers. Which classic framework does the chapter present as the most established model for this analysis?

options.A: Porter's 5-Forces model of competition
options.B: The Boston Consulting Group growth–share matrix
options.C: The McKinsey 7-S framework
options.D: A SWOT (strengths/weaknesses/opportunities/threats) analysis

exp: Porter's 5-Forces is presented as the most established and respected model of the business environment, analyzing established competitors, the threat of new entrants, the threat of substitutes, supplier bargaining power, and buyer bargaining power. The distractors are real strategy tools used for different purposes: the BCG growth–share matrix sorts a *portfolio* of products (stars/cash cows/dogs); the McKinsey 7-S framework examines *internal* organizational alignment; and SWOT is a general situational scan, not the five-force industry-structure model the chapter names.
hy: Porter's 5-Forces = competitors, new entrants, substitutes, suppliers, buyers.

stem: Rather than fighting rivals for a larger slice of a fixed, crowded market, a company discovers an uncontested but related market space, rendering competitors obsolete and generating new demand. The chapter calls this approach:

options.A: Blue ocean strategy
options.B: Judo strategy
options.C: Rising-tide strategy
options.D: Loss-leader strategy

exp: Blue ocean strategy seeks uncontested but related market space, rendering rivals obsolete and creating new demand — the opposite of fighting over a fixed market. The distractors are adjacent concepts from the same section: "judo strategy" is using focus/agility to undermine a dominant firm within the existing market; the "rising tide" describes attracting customers via a slowly growing market; and a "loss leader" is a mission-critical product run at negative margin — none is the uncontested-new-space idea.
hy: Blue ocean strategy = create uncontested new market space.

stem: The chapter describes the entire axis from supplier to consumer — inbound logistics, operations, outbound logistics, marketing/sales, and customer service — along which value is added at each step. What is this axis called?

options.A: The value chain
options.B: The supply curve
options.C: The income statement
options.D: The hurdle rate

exp: The value chain is the supplier-to-consumer axis along which primary activities (inbound logistics, operations, outbound logistics, marketing/sales, customer service) each add value, coordinated by support activities. The distractors are different constructs: the supply curve is an economics relationship of price to quantity supplied; the income statement is an accounting report of period transactions; and the hurdle rate is a finance threshold for accepting investments — none describes the value-adding activity axis.
hy: Value chain = supplier→consumer axis where value is added at each step.

stem: The rules used to prepare, present, and report financial statements — required of publicly-traded firms by the US Securities and Exchange Commission — are known by which name?

options.A: Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP)
options.B: Managerial accounting standards
options.C: The DMAIC standard
options.D: Sarbanes–Oxley provisions

exp: GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) are the rules for preparing, presenting, and reporting financial statements; the SEC requires public firms to follow them. The distractors name real but different things: managerial accounting (used to allocate cost/overhead) explicitly does *not* follow GAAP; DMAIC is the Six Sigma improvement cycle; and Sarbanes–Oxley is a 2002 securities law, not the accounting-principles framework itself.
hy: GAAP = SEC-mandated financial-reporting rules for public firms.

stem: On a balance sheet, owner's equity (net worth) is defined in relation to assets and liabilities. Which equation expresses this relationship?

options.A: Equity = Assets − Liabilities (equivalently, Assets = Liabilities + Equity)
options.B: Equity = Liabilities − Assets
options.C: Equity = Assets + Liabilities
options.D: Equity = Revenue − Operating costs

exp: Owner's equity (net worth) is the difference between assets and liabilities, so Equity = Assets − Liabilities, conventionally rearranged as the balancing identity Assets = Liabilities + Equity (or Assets = Debt + Equity). The distractors break the identity: subtracting assets from liabilities, adding liabilities to assets, or using the revenue-minus-costs (income-statement) relationship all violate the balance-sheet equation that must always balance.
hy: Assets = Liabilities + Equity (Equity = Assets − Liabilities).

stem: A hospital administrator wants to know the value a specific procedure creates independent of overhead and fixed costs. The chapter highlights "contribution margin" for this. How is contribution margin defined?

options.A: Revenue minus variable costs
options.B: Revenue minus all fixed and variable costs
options.C: Net income divided by total assets
options.D: Gross profit divided by revenue

exp: Contribution margin is defined as revenue less *variable* costs; because it strips out fixed costs and overhead (which are often misallocated), it isolates the value a specific product or service creates and lets you compare service lines. The distractors are other metrics: revenue minus *all* costs is operating/net income; net income ÷ total assets is return on assets; and gross profit ÷ revenue is gross margin — none is contribution margin.
hy: Contribution margin = revenue − variable costs.

stem: The chapter contrasts the income statement with the balance sheet. Which statement correctly describes the balance sheet?

options.A: It is a snapshot of what the company owns and owes at a single point in time
options.B: It describes financial transactions over a defined period such as a quarter or year
options.C: It tracks only the inflow and outflow of cash across three activity categories
options.D: It expresses the numerical relationship between two financial categories as a ratio

exp: The balance sheet is a snapshot of what the company owns and owes at a single point in time (and shows little about operational performance). The distractors describe the *other* accounting instruments: a report of transactions over a defined period is the income statement; the inflow/outflow across three activity categories is the summary of cash flows; and a numerical relationship between two categories is a financial ratio.
hy: Balance sheet = point-in-time snapshot of assets and liabilities.

stem: The chapter stresses that "profit is NOT the same as cash." Into which three categories does it subdivide a company's overall cash flows?

options.A: Operations, investing, and financing
options.B: Revenue, expenses, and taxes
options.C: Assets, liabilities, and equity
options.D: Fixed, variable, and marginal

exp: Because profit is not the same as cash, the chapter subdivides overall cash flows by their conduit into operations (CFO), investing (CFI), and financing (CFF), summing to total cash flow. The distractors borrow line items from other statements: revenue/expenses/taxes are income-statement components; assets/liabilities/equity are balance-sheet components; and fixed/variable/marginal are cost classifications — none is the three-way cash-flow split.
hy: Cash flows = operations + investing + financing.

stem: The chapter calls a particular measure "the best source of capital for developing new products, acquiring companies, paying dividends, and reducing debt." How is free cash flow (FCF) defined in its simplest form?

options.A: Cash flow from operations minus capital expenditures
options.B: Net income plus depreciation
options.C: Revenue minus variable costs
options.D: Total assets minus total liabilities

exp: Free cash flow is defined simply as cash flow from operations minus capital expenditures — the cash a firm generates after funding the maintenance/growth of its asset base, and the best source of capital for new products, acquisitions, dividends, and debt reduction. The distractors are incomplete or different: net income plus depreciation is only part of the longer FCF expression; revenue minus variable costs is contribution margin; and assets minus liabilities is equity.
hy: Free cash flow = CFO − capital expenditures.

stem: On the income statement, the chapter pairs two non-cash charges: depreciation and amortization. What does each apply to?

options.A: Depreciation applies to assets; amortization applies to leases
options.B: Depreciation applies to leases; amortization applies to assets
options.C: Both apply only to inventory
options.D: Depreciation applies to revenue; amortization applies to taxes

exp: On the income statement the chapter pairs depreciation *of assets* with amortization *of leases* as the two non-cash charges against revenue. The distractors swap or misassign them: amortization is not applied to physical assets nor depreciation to leases; neither applies to inventory; and neither is charged against revenue or taxes in the way described.
hy: Depreciation → assets; amortization → leases.

stem: "A dollar today is worth slightly more tomorrow and a lot more in 10 years." Which financial concept does this express, and why does money carry it?

options.A: The time value of money — owing to postponement of consumption and expectations of inflation
options.B: Opportunity cost — owing to the next-best forgone alternative
options.C: Contribution margin — owing to variable cost behavior
options.D: Price elasticity — owing to the responsiveness of demand to price

exp: The time value of money holds that a dollar today is worth more than a dollar later; economists attribute this to postponement of consumption and expectations of inflation (interest rates hedge against that depreciation), captured by PV = FV/(1 + r)^n. The distractors are different finance/economics ideas: opportunity cost is the next-best forgone alternative; contribution margin is an accounting figure; and price elasticity measures demand's responsiveness to price — none explains why money appreciates over time.
hy: Time value of money: PV = FV/(1+r)^n; driven by deferred consumption + inflation.

stem: Before tying up time and money in a new project, a surgeon should weigh the benefit forgone by not investing those resources elsewhere. The chapter defines this as:

options.A: Opportunity cost — the potential benefit forgone from not following the financially optimal course of action
options.B: Sunk cost — money already spent and unrecoverable
options.C: Marginal cost — the cost of producing one additional unit
options.D: The hurdle rate — the minimum acceptable rate of return

exp: Opportunity cost is the potential benefit forgone from not following the financially optimal course of action — which is why, for a surgeon, any activity that takes them out of the OR should be weighed against what they could earn by operating. The distractors are other cost concepts: a sunk cost is already spent and irrecoverable; a marginal cost is the cost of one more unit; and the hurdle rate is a required rate of return — none is the forgone-next-best-alternative idea.
hy: Opportunity cost = benefit forgone from the next-best alternative.

stem: A practice evaluates a capital purchase by summing the time-series of discounted future cash flows to obtain a net present value (NPV). According to the chapter's decision rule, when should the project be accepted?

options.A: When NPV > 0, because the investment would add value to the firm
options.B: When NPV < 0, because a negative figure signals tax advantages
options.C: When NPV equals the purchase price exactly
options.D: Only when NPV equals the internal rate of return

exp: The NPV decision rule: if NPV > 0 the investment adds value and may be accepted; if NPV < 0 it subtracts value and should be rejected. The distractors invert or distort the rule: a negative NPV is a reason to reject (not a tax benefit), and the project decision does not hinge on NPV equaling the purchase price or matching the IRR exactly.
hy: Accept when NPV > 0; reject when NPV < 0.

stem: In an NPV calculation, the discount rate selected is often the firm's blended cost of debt and equity. What is this blended rate called?

options.A: The weighted average cost of capital (WACC)
options.B: The internal rate of return (IRR)
options.C: The price elasticity coefficient
options.D: The payback period

exp: The discount rate in an NPV calculation is often the firm's weighted average cost of capital (WACC), which blends the cost of debt (borrowing) with the cost of equity (shareholders' expected returns). The distractors are related but distinct: the IRR is the return the cash flows themselves imply (compared *against* the hurdle rate/WACC); price elasticity is an economics coefficient; and the payback period is a separate, simpler ROI measure.
hy: WACC = blended cost of debt + equity; the usual discount rate.

stem: The chapter describes the internal rate of return (IRR) as an alternative to assuming a discount rate. Conceptually, what does the IRR represent?

options.A: The hurdle rate that would make the project's NPV equal to zero
options.B: The time required for cash flow to repay the original investment
options.C: The gain from investment divided by its cost, expressed as a percentage
options.D: The blended cost of debt and equity for the firm

exp: The chapter states that IRR represents the hurdle rate that would make NPV equal to zero — i.e., the actual return implied by the projected cash flows, then compared with the firm's hurdle rate. The distractors describe *other* ROI tools from the same passage: time to repay the original investment is the payback method; gain-over-cost as a percentage is yield; and the blended cost of debt and equity is WACC.
hy: IRR = the rate that makes NPV = 0.

stem: The chapter quantifies how quantity demanded changes with price using the coefficient of price elasticity of demand (Ed). Which interpretation is correct?

options.A: Ed < −1 indicates elastic demand, whereas Ed = 0 indicates perfectly inelastic demand
options.B: Ed = 0 indicates elastic demand, whereas Ed < −1 indicates perfectly inelastic demand
options.C: Ed = −1 indicates perfectly elastic demand with no response to price
options.D: A higher absolute Ed means demand is less responsive to price changes

exp: Using the chapter's elasticity scale, Ed < −1 is elastic demand (small price change → large quantity change) and Ed = 0 is perfectly inelastic (quantity does not respond to price). The distractors reverse or misread the scale: Ed = 0 is *not* elastic, Ed < −1 is *not* perfectly inelastic, Ed = −1 is unitary (not perfectly elastic), and a higher absolute Ed means *more* responsiveness, not less.
hy: Ed = 0 perfectly inelastic; Ed < −1 elastic; |Ed| up = more responsive.

stem: A patient whose surgery is fully paid by third-party insurance is relatively insensitive to its price. According to the chapter, how does third-party payment affect the price elasticity of demand for healthcare?

options.A: It makes demand relatively inelastic; adding a copay or deductible increases elasticity
options.B: It makes demand perfectly elastic, since price no longer matters at all
options.C: It has no effect on elasticity, which depends only on the good itself
options.D: It makes demand more elastic, because patients shop more aggressively

exp: When a third party (insurer) pays, the consumer does not feel the price, so demand for healthcare becomes relatively inelastic; adding a copay or deductible shifts some cost to the consumer and raises elasticity. The distractors overstate or deny the effect: third-party payment does not make demand *perfectly* elastic, does not leave elasticity unchanged, and does not make patients more price-sensitive (the opposite is true).
hy: Third-party payment → inelastic demand; cost-sharing raises elasticity.

stem: The chapter notes that the textbook supply-and-demand model requires "perfect competition," a condition it says does not actually exist. Which set of assumptions defines perfect competition?

options.A: Identical products, no strategic manipulation, rational and fully-informed participants, and no barriers to entry or exit
options.B: A single dominant firm setting prices, with high barriers to entry protecting it
options.C: Heavily differentiated products and asymmetric information favoring sellers
options.D: Government price-setting that fixes both supply and demand

exp: Perfect competition (which the chapter says does not actually exist) requires identical products, no strategic manipulation of supply/demand, rational and fully-informed participants, and no barriers to entry or exit. The distractors describe *imperfect* markets: a single dominant price-setter with entry barriers is monopoly; differentiated products with asymmetric information is monopolistic/real-world competition; and government price-fixing is direct intervention — none meets the perfect-competition assumptions.
hy: Perfect competition = identical goods, no manipulation, rational/informed players, free entry/exit.

stem: The chapter reviews marketing strategy through a classic, if oversimplified, framework. What are the "4 Ps" of marketing?

options.A: Product, price, promotion, and placement
options.B: People, planet, profit, and purpose
options.C: Plan, produce, promote, and profit
options.D: Position, package, publicize, and price

exp: The classic (if oversimplified) marketing framework is the 4 Ps: product, price, promotion, and placement. The distractors borrow from other frameworks: people/planet/profit is the sustainability triple bottom line; plan/produce/promote/profit and position/package/publicize/price are invented look-alikes that do not match the chapter's 4 Ps.
hy: 4 Ps = product, price, promotion, placement.

stem: To find how many units must be sold to cover costs, the chapter gives a break-even formula. Break-even unit volume equals:

options.A: Fixed costs ÷ (sales price − variable costs)
options.B: Variable costs ÷ (sales price − fixed costs)
options.C: Fixed costs × (sales price − variable costs)
options.D: (Sales price − variable costs) ÷ fixed costs

exp: Break-even unit volume = fixed costs ÷ (sales price − variable costs); the denominator (price minus variable cost) is the per-unit contribution that pays down fixed costs. The distractors scramble the formula: dividing variable costs by (price − fixed costs), multiplying rather than dividing, or inverting the ratio all give nonsensical results that don't yield a unit count to cover fixed costs.
hy: Break-even units = fixed costs ÷ (price − variable cost).

stem: A production system targets the elimination of any expenditure that does not add value to the customer, attacking seven sources of waste (overproduction, transportation, inventory, motion, defects, over-processing, and waiting). What is this approach, and which company originated it?

options.A: Lean manufacturing, originally developed by Toyota
options.B: Six Sigma, originally developed by Motorola
options.C: The theory of constraints, originally developed by Apple
options.D: Total quality management, originally developed by Ford

exp: Lean manufacturing, originated by Toyota, targets elimination of anything that does not add customer value, attacking seven wastes (overproduction, transportation, inventory, motion, defects, over-processing, waiting). The distractors are real but different: Six Sigma (Motorola) reduces defect *variability*; the theory of constraints addresses bottlenecks (and was not an Apple creation); and TQM is a separate quality philosophy not credited to Ford here.
hy: Lean (Toyota) = eliminate the 7 wastes.

stem: A quality methodology uses the DMAIC model (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) and aims to reduce defects to fewer than ~3.4 per million (six standard deviations from target). What is it, and where did it originate?

options.A: Six Sigma, originally developed by Motorola
options.B: Lean manufacturing, originally developed by Toyota
options.C: Queueing theory, originally developed by Bell Labs
options.D: Kaizen, originally developed by Honda

exp: Six Sigma, developed by Motorola, uses the DMAIC cycle (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) and drives defects below ~3.4 per million (six standard deviations from target). The distractors misattribute or mismatch: Lean (Toyota) eliminates waste rather than statistically minimizing defects; queueing theory models waiting lines; and "Kaizen" (continuous improvement) is not the DMAIC/3.4-per-million method described.
hy: Six Sigma (Motorola) = DMAIC, <3.4 defects/million (6σ).

stem: The chapter gives a relationship stating that inventory equals the average flow rate multiplied by the average flow time. By what name is this principle known?

options.A: Little's Law
options.B: Moore's Law
options.C: The Pareto principle
options.D: The bullwhip effect

exp: Little's Law states that inventory equals average flow rate multiplied by average flow time (Inventory = flow rate × flow time). The distractors are unrelated laws/effects: Moore's Law concerns transistor density doubling; the Pareto principle is the 80/20 rule; and the bullwhip effect describes amplifying demand variability along a supply chain — none is the inventory relationship.
hy: Little's Law: Inventory = flow rate × flow time.

stem: When matching supply with demand, the chapter warns that running stations at nearly 100% capacity is unsustainable. What utilization level does it offer as a reasonable target, and how is a bottleneck defined?

options.A: About 80% utilization; the bottleneck is the resource with the lowest capacity
options.B: About 95% utilization; the bottleneck is the resource with the highest capacity
options.C: About 50% utilization; the bottleneck is whichever station has the most workers
options.D: About 100% utilization; there is no single bottleneck in a balanced system

exp: The chapter gives ~80% utilization as a reasonable target (running near 100% is unsustainable) and defines the bottleneck as the resource with the lowest capacity — a process technically has only one at a time. The distractors misstate both: 95% or 100% utilization is not sustainable, the bottleneck is the lowest-capacity (not highest-capacity or most-staffed) station, and even a "balanced" system has a constraining resource.
hy: ~80% utilization target; bottleneck = lowest-capacity resource.

stem: The chapter describes the sequence by which a new technology spreads through a market and warns that most companies fail at "crossing the chasm." Which sequence of adopter groups does it use?

options.A: Innovators, early adopters, early majority, late majority, and laggards
options.B: Pioneers, settlers, migrants, defectors, and laggards
options.C: Apostles, loyalists, mercenaries, hostages, and terrorists
options.D: Start-ups, scale-ups, shape-ups, and stand-ups

exp: The diffusion-of-innovation sequence the chapter uses is innovators → early adopters → early majority → late majority → laggards, with "crossing the chasm" (early adoption → market penetration) being where most companies fail. The distractors are other taxonomies from the chapter: pioneers/settlers/migrants come from the market-space discussion; apostles/loyalists/mercenaries/hostages/terrorists are the customer-loyalty groups; and start-ups/scale-ups/shape-ups/stand-ups are the entrepreneurship business-model categories.
hy: Adopters: innovators, early adopters, early/late majority, laggards; failure point = crossing the chasm.

stem: The chapter cites Peter Drucker's claim that only two functions of a company create real value, while all other processes are costs. Which two functions did Drucker name?

options.A: Innovation and marketing
options.B: Finance and operations
options.C: Accounting and human resources
options.D: Strategy and leadership

exp: The chapter quotes Drucker that the two essential functions of a company are innovation and marketing, because these create real value while all other processes are costs. The distractors pair real business functions that Drucker did *not* name as the value-creating two: finance/operations, accounting/HR, and strategy/leadership are all important but, in this framing, fall under "costs" rather than the value-creating pair.
hy: Drucker: innovation + marketing create value; the rest are costs.

stem: "Green business," or sustainable enterprise, is described as meeting a new "triple bottom line." What three elements make up this triple bottom line?

options.A: People, planet, and profit
options.B: Price, place, and promotion
options.C: Quality, access, and cost
options.D: Reduce, reuse, and recycle

exp: Sustainable ("green") enterprise is built around a triple bottom line of people, planet, and profit. The distractors are other trios from the book: price/place/promotion are three of the marketing 4 Ps; quality/access/cost are the dimensions of healthcare value; and reduce/reuse/recycle is the older environmental slogan the chapter says has been superseded by the triple bottom line.
hy: Triple bottom line = people, planet, profit.

stem: Reviewing basic forms of business ownership, the chapter notes a tax disadvantage shared by corporations and cooperatives that proprietorships and partnerships avoid. What is this disadvantage?

options.A: Double taxation — profit is taxed once as entity net income and again as returns to stakeholders
options.B: Personal liability of every owner for all business debts
options.C: A legal prohibition on having more than one shareholder
options.D: Mandatory dissolution of the entity each fiscal year

exp: Corporations and cooperatives suffer double taxation: profit is taxed once as the entity's net income and again as returns to stakeholders (dividends, capital gains), whereas a sole proprietorship or partnership is taxed only once. The distractors describe other ownership features: unlimited personal liability is the hallmark of proprietorships/partnerships (not the corporate disadvantage asked about); corporations require *multiple* shareholders (not a prohibition on more than one); and entities are not dissolved annually.
hy: Corporations/cooperatives → double taxation (entity + stakeholder).

stem: The chapter teaches that negotiation begins by defining your "walk-away position" — the lowest you will go if no agreement is reached — before negotiations start. What is this position called?

options.A: The BATNA — the best alternative to a negotiated agreement
options.B: The ZOPA — the zone of possible agreement
options.C: The reservation premium
options.D: The anchor point

exp: The walk-away position — the lowest you will go, set before negotiating — is the BATNA, the best alternative to a negotiated agreement.
