The distractors describe the BREAST-Q (breast), the Boston Carpal Tunnel Questionnaire (carpal tunnel), and a generic profile (PROMIS-29).
hy: FACE-Q (2013): facial aesthetic outcomes across satisfaction/HRQL/sequelae/process-of-care scales.

why.A: Correct — the FACE-Q targets facial aesthetic procedure outcomes.
why.B: Wrong — breast reconstruction/reduction is the BREAST-Q.
why.C: Wrong — carpal tunnel severity is the Boston Carpal Tunnel Questionnaire.
why.D: Wrong — a seven-domain generic profile is PROMIS-29.

stem: Per Table 12.2, the CLEFT-Q (2018) is intended for which population?

options.A: People aged 8–29 with cleft lip and/or palate, across facial appearance, facial function, and quality-of-life domains
options.B: Adults undergoing augmentation mammaplasty
options.C: Patients with mammary hypertrophy seeking reduction
options.D: Any patient with a congenital or acquired hand condition

exp: Per Table 12.2, the CLEFT-Q (2018; Klassen, Wong-Riff, et al.) is for people aged 8–29 with cleft lip and/or palate and includes 12 scales falling under three domains: facial appearance, facial function, and quality of life. The other options describe the Breast Evaluation Questionnaire (augmentation), the Breast Reduction Assessed Severity Scale (mammary hypertrophy), and the HAND-Q (any hand condition).
hy: CLEFT-Q (2018): ages 8–29 cleft; appearance/function/QoL domains.

why.A: Correct — CLEFT-Q's age range, condition, and three domains.
why.B: Wrong — augmentation mammaplasty is the Breast Evaluation Questionnaire.
why.C: Wrong — mammary hypertrophy is the Breast Reduction Assessed Severity Scale.
why.D: Wrong — any hand condition is the HAND-Q.

stem: Per Table 12.2, which correctly describes the generic SF-36?

options.A: A 1992 (Ware) 36-item questionnaire across eight health domains, on which lower scores (<50) represent worse outcomes
options.B: A 2008 condition-specific breast questionnaire scored 0–100
options.C: A 2011 instrument with four items from each of seven PROMIS domains
options.D: A 1990 EuroQol 5-dimension instrument

exp: Per Table 12.2, the SF-36 (1992; Ware & Sherbourne) is a generic 36-item questionnaire spanning eight health domains (physical functioning, social functioning, role limitations from physical and emotional health, bodily pain, mental health, vitality, general health), on which lower scores (<50) represent a worse outcome. The distractors describe the BREAST-Q, PROMIS-29, and EQ-5D.
hy: SF-36 (1992, Ware): generic 36-item; lower scores (<50) = worse.

why.A: Correct — SF-36's year, length, and "lower = worse" scoring.
why.B: Wrong — a 0–100 breast questionnaire is the BREAST-Q.
why.C: Wrong — four items from seven PROMIS domains describes PROMIS-29.
why.D: Wrong — a five-dimension EuroQol instrument is the EQ-5D.

stem: Per Table 12.2, the EQ-5D (1990, EuroQol Group) measures health status across which five dimensions?

options.A: Mobility, self-care, usual activity, pain/distress, and depression/anxiety
options.B: Pain, fatigue, depression, anxiety, and sleep
options.C: Appearance, function, HRQL, experience of care, and treatment
options.D: Physical, social, role, vitality, and general health perception

exp: Per Table 12.2, the EQ-5D (1990, EuroQol Group) describes health status across five dimensions — mobility, self-care, usual activity, pain/distress, and depression/anxiety — plus a visual analogue rating scale, yielding an index ranging from 0 to 100. The other sets list the PROMIS domains, the CLEFT-Q/HAND-Q domains, and SF-36 components, not the EQ-5D dimensions.
hy: EQ-5D (1990): mobility, self-care, usual activity, pain/distress, depression/anxiety.

why.A: Correct — the five EQ-5D dimensions.
why.B: Wrong — pain/fatigue/depression/anxiety/sleep are PROMIS-29 domains.
why.C: Wrong — appearance/function/HRQL describe condition-specific PROMs.
why.D: Wrong — physical/social/role/vitality are SF-36 components, not EQ-5D dimensions.

stem: Per Table 12.2, the PROMIS-29 (2011) is built from four items in each of seven core domains. Which set lists those domains?

options.A: Pain, fatigue, depression, anxiety, sleep, physical function, and sexual function
options.B: Mobility, self-care, usual activity, pain, and anxiety
options.C: Appearance, facial function, and quality of life
options.D: Satisfaction with breasts, physical, psychosocial, and sexual well-being

exp: Per Table 12.2, PROMIS-29 (2011) contains four items from each of seven core PROMIS domains — pain, fatigue, depression, anxiety, sleep, physical function, and sexual function — plus an 11-point pain-intensity rating. These cover the most relevant self-reported health areas for people with chronic illness. The distractors list EQ-5D dimensions, CLEFT-Q domains, and BREAST-Q scales.
hy: PROMIS-29 (2011): 7 domains × 4 items (pain, fatigue, depression, anxiety, sleep, physical & sexual function).

why.A: Correct — the seven PROMIS-29 core domains.
why.B: Wrong — those five are EQ-5D dimensions.
why.C: Wrong — appearance/facial function/QoL are CLEFT-Q domains.
why.D: Wrong — satisfaction with breasts etc. are BREAST-Q scales.

stem: Per Table 12.2, which instrument is a hand/upper-extremity PROM?

options.A: The Disabilities of the Arm, Shoulder and Hand (DASH) questionnaire — a composite measure of upper-extremity disability scored 0–100
options.B: The BREAST-Q
options.C: The FACE-Q
options.D: The EQ-5D

exp: Per Table 12.2, the Disabilities of the Arm, Shoulder and Hand (DASH, 1996) questionnaire is a hand/upper-extremity PROM — a 30-item, 5-point Likert composite measure of upper-extremity disability scored 0–100, with an optional work/sport module. The BREAST-Q (breast), FACE-Q (face), and EQ-5D (generic) are not hand instruments.
hy: DASH = upper-extremity disability PROM (30 items, 0–100).

why.A: Correct — the DASH is the upper-extremity/hand instrument.
why.B: Wrong — the BREAST-Q is a breast PROM.
why.C: Wrong — the FACE-Q is a facial PROM.
why.D: Wrong — the EQ-5D is a generic health-status instrument.

stem: The FDA recommends what kind of process for developing a PROM?

options.A: A multi-stage, iterative process (hypothesize framework → draft and cognitively debrief → field-test → finalize)
options.B: A single cross-sectional survey validated once
options.C: An expert-only consensus process without patient involvement
options.D: A purely statistical exercise applied to existing administrative data

exp: The FDA recommends a multi-stage, iterative process for developing a PROM, summarized in the chapter as four steps: hypothesize the conceptual framework; adjust the framework and draft the instrument with cognitive debriefing; confirm the framework by field-testing and assessing measurement properties; and finalize the instrument (RMT analysis, item reduction, translation). A single one-off survey, an expert-only process, or a purely statistical exercise on administrative data all omit the iterative, patient-grounded development the FDA advises.
hy: FDA PROM development = multi-stage iterative 4-step process.

why.A: Correct — the FDA's iterative four-step development process.
why.B: Wrong — a single validated survey is not the recommended process.
why.C: Wrong — patient involvement is essential; expert-only is insufficient.
why.D: Wrong — administrative data alone cannot develop a PROM.

stem: Before developing a brand-new PROM, what helpful first step does the chapter recommend?

options.A: Perform a systematic review to identify whether any PROMs already exist in the topic area of interest
options.B: Immediately field-test a draft instrument in a large sample
options.C: Translate an existing instrument into multiple languages
options.D: Finalize scoring rules before any patient input is obtained

exp: A helpful first step before developing a new PROM is to perform a systematic review to identify whether any PROMs already exist in the topic area, avoiding duplication and informing the draft conceptual framework. Jumping straight to field-testing, translating an existing tool, or finalizing scoring before patient input all skip this foundational reconnaissance.
hy: Step 1: systematic review first to find existing PROMs.

why.A: Correct — a systematic review identifies existing instruments first.
why.B: Wrong — field-testing comes much later (Step 3).
why.C: Wrong — translation is a finalization step, not the first one.
why.D: Wrong — scoring is not finalized before patient input.

stem: In developing the BREAST-Q, the initial systematic literature review of PROMs in cosmetic and reconstructive breast surgery identified how many existing PROMs to examine for quality and concepts?
exp: To develop the BREAST-Q, a systematic literature review of PROMs used in cosmetic and reconstructive breast surgery identified 7 existing PROMs, which were then examined for quality and the concepts they included. The distractor values (31, 55, 0) correspond to item counts of unrelated instruments or are simply incorrect. This illustrates Step 1's systematic-review groundwork in a concrete example.
hy: BREAST-Q development: 7 existing PROMs found on systematic review.

why.A: Correct — seven existing PROMs were identified and examined.
why.B: Wrong — 31 is the BRECON-31 item count, not this figure.
why.C: Wrong — 55 is the Breast Evaluation Questionnaire's item count.
why.D: Wrong — existing PROMs did exist; the count was not zero.

stem: Patient input during the conceptual-framework phase is critical chiefly because it establishes which property?

options.A: Content validity
options.B: Test–retest reliability
options.C: Internal-consistency reliability
options.D: Responsiveness

exp: Patient input during the conceptual-framework phase is critical chiefly because it establishes content validity — ensuring the instrument captures the outcomes and concerns most relevant to patients in language they understand. Test–retest and internal-consistency reliability are assessed later via field-testing statistics, and responsiveness is the ability to detect change over time — none of which is what early patient interviews establish.
hy: Early patient interviews → content validity.

why.A: Correct — patient input establishes content validity.
why.B: Wrong — test–retest reliability is a later field-testing property.
why.C: Wrong — internal-consistency reliability is computed from field-test data.
why.D: Wrong — responsiveness is the ability to detect change, assessed later.

stem: When conducting qualitative patient interviews to develop a PROM, what determines the number of interviews needed?

options.A: Saturation — interviewing continues until no new relevant information emerges
options.B: A fixed quota of exactly 100 interviews regardless of content
options.C: The number of languages the PROM will be translated into
options.D: The number of items in the existing legacy instrument

exp: The number of qualitative interviews is governed by saturation — interviewing continues until no new relevant information emerges, meaning further data would not deepen understanding of the concept. A fixed quota, the number of translation languages, or the legacy instrument's item count are not what determines interview number. Saturation is the qualitative-research principle anchoring this phase.
hy: Qualitative interview number = guided by saturation (no new information).

why.A: Correct — saturation determines when interviewing can stop.
why.B: Wrong — there is no fixed quota of 100 interviews.
why.C: Wrong — translation count is unrelated to interview number.
why.D: Wrong — legacy item count does not set interview number.

stem: Roughly how many cognitive-debriefing interviews are estimated to be sufficient to confirm patient comprehensibility of items and establish content validity (with saturation as the ultimate guide)?

options.A: Seven to ten interviews
options.B: One to two interviews
options.C: Fifty to sixty interviews
options.D: Two hundred interviews

exp: The chapter estimates that seven to ten cognitive-debriefing interviews are sufficient to confirm patient comprehensibility of items and establish content validity, though saturation should again serve as the ultimate guide. The other figures (1–2, 50–60, 200) are too few or far more than the cited estimate. In these interviews patients explain in their own words what each item asks, testing readability.
hy: ~7–10 cognitive-debriefing interviews for comprehensibility/content validity.

why.A: Correct — seven to ten interviews is the cited estimate.
why.B: Wrong — one to two is too few to confirm comprehensibility.
why.C: Wrong — 50–60 far exceeds the cited estimate.
why.D: Wrong — 200 is far more than needed for cognitive debriefing.

stem: The literacy demand of a PROM is recommended not to exceed what reading level?

options.A: A 6th-grade reading level (ages 11–12) or lower
options.B: A 12th-grade reading level
options.C: A college reading level
options.D: A 1st-grade reading level

exp: The literacy demand of a PROM is recommended not to exceed a 6th-grade reading level (ages 11–12) or lower, so that items are accessible to the broad patient population and responses are not biased by misunderstanding. A 12th-grade or college level would exclude many patients, and a 1st-grade level is unnecessarily restrictive and not what is cited.
hy: PROM reading level ≤ 6th grade (ages 11–12).

why.A: Correct — 6th-grade (ages 11–12) or lower is the recommendation.
why.B: Wrong — 12th grade would exclude many patients.
why.C: Wrong — a college level is far too high.
why.D: Wrong — 1st grade is not the cited threshold.

stem: Field-testing in Step 3 is performed to assess validity, reliability, and responsiveness. To run a test–retest study, participants complete the PROM twice within what interval?

options.A: 3–14 days, excluding anyone whose health changed in the interim
options.B: The same day, within one hour
options.C: 6–12 months apart
options.D: 2–3 years apart

exp: In Step 3, field-testing assesses validity, reliability, and responsiveness; for a test–retest reproducibility study, participants complete the refined PROM twice within a short interval of 3–14 days, and anyone whose health changed in the interim is excluded. Same-day repetition risks recall, and intervals of months or years risk true health-state change — both of which would invalidate the reproducibility estimate.
hy: Test–retest interval = 3–14 days (exclude those whose health changed).

why.A: Correct — a 3–14-day interval, excluding changed-health patients.
why.B: Wrong — same-day repetition invites recall bias.
why.C: Wrong — 6–12 months risks genuine health-state change.
why.D: Wrong — years apart would measure change, not reproducibility.

stem: After RMT analysis and item reduction, the BREAST-Q Reconstruction Module illustrates which step of finalization?

options.A: Translation and cultural adaptation — it is now available in 45 languages, some with multiple dialects
options.B: Initial generation of the item pool from patient interviews
options.C: The first systematic review of existing instruments
options.D: Cognitive debriefing of draft items

exp: Step 4 (finalize) includes RMT analysis and item reduction, then translation and cultural adaptation to other languages; the BREAST-Q Reconstruction Module exemplifies this, now available in 45 languages, some with multiple dialects (e.g., distinct German versions for Austria, Germany, and Switzerland). Item-pool generation and the initial systematic review belong to earlier steps, and cognitive debriefing is a Step 2 activity.
hy: Step 4 finalization: translation/cultural adaptation (BREAST-Q in 45 languages).

why.A: Correct — translation/cultural adaptation, BREAST-Q in 45 languages.
why.B: Wrong — item-pool generation is a Step 2 activity.
why.C: Wrong — the systematic review is a Step 1 activity.
why.D: Wrong — cognitive debriefing of draft items occurs in Step 2.

stem: Translation and cultural adaptation of a finalized PROM should follow guidelines such as those from which organization?

options.A: ISPOR (International Society for Pharmacoeconomics and Outcomes Research)
options.B: The American College of Surgeons
options.C: The EuroQol Group
options.D: The National Quality Forum

exp: Translation and cultural adaptation of a finalized PROM should follow established guidelines such as those from ISPOR (the International Society for Pharmacoeconomics and Outcomes Research), ensuring the instrument retains its measurement properties across languages and cultures. The American College of Surgeons, EuroQol Group, and National Quality Forum are real organizations but are not the cited source of translation/cultural-adaptation guidance.
hy: PROM translation/cultural adaptation → ISPOR guidelines.

why.A: Correct — ISPOR provides translation/cultural-adaptation guidance.
why.B: Wrong — the ACS is a surgical college, not the translation-guideline source.
why.C: Wrong — the EuroQol Group develops the EQ-5D, not these guidelines.
why.D: Wrong — the National Quality Forum endorses performance metrics, not this.

stem: What does a computerized adaptive test (CAT) do?

options.A: Uses an algorithm to select the most appropriate next question based on the patient's previous answers, continuing until a prespecified measurement precision is reached
options.B: Presents every item in the full-length scale to every patient in fixed order
options.C: Replaces patient responses with clinician estimates
options.D: Randomly shuffles items so no two patients answer the same questions

exp: A computerized adaptive test uses an algorithm to select the most appropriate next question for each individual based on their previous answers, refining the estimate of where the patient sits on the "Rasch ruler" until a prespecified level of measurement precision is reached — so different patients may answer different subsets of items yet have directly comparable scores. Presenting all items in fixed order, substituting clinician estimates, or randomly shuffling items all describe non-adaptive or invalid approaches.
hy: CAT = algorithm picks next item from prior answers until target precision.

why.A: Correct — adaptive item selection to a precision stopping rule.
why.B: Wrong — fixed full-length administration is non-adaptive testing.
why.C: Wrong — CAT uses patient responses, not clinician estimates.
why.D: Wrong — items are chosen by algorithm, not randomly shuffled.

stem: Applying CAT to the BREAST-Q Satisfaction with Breasts (Reconstruction) scale shortened it substantially. Which statement is correct?

options.A: It produced a 38–75% reduction in length while maintaining a reliable outcome; already-short scales (e.g., the 6-item Sexual Well-being scale) benefit little
options.B: It lengthened the questionnaire to improve reliability
options.C: It eliminated the need for any psychometric validation
options.D: Short scales benefit most from CAT, long scales least

exp: Applying CAT to the BREAST-Q Satisfaction with Breasts (Reconstruction) scale produced a 38–75% reduction in length depending on the patient while still maintaining a reliable outcome. PROMs unlikely to benefit from CAT are those already short, such as the six-item Sexual Well-being scale. CAT shortens rather than lengthens questionnaires, does not remove the need for validation, and helps long scales more than short ones.
hy: CAT on BREAST-Q: 38–75% shorter, reliable; short scales benefit least.

why.A: Correct — 38–75% reduction with maintained reliability; short scales benefit little.
why.B: Wrong — CAT shortens, it does not lengthen.
why.C: Wrong — CAT does not remove the need for psychometric validation.
why.D: Wrong — long scales benefit most from CAT, short scales least.

stem: Validity is the ability of a PROM to measure the concept it intends to measure. What are its three main types?

options.A: Content validity, criterion validity, and construct validity
options.B: Internal-consistency, test–retest, and inter-observer validity
options.C: Distribution-based, anchor-based, and responder validity
options.D: Generic, disease-specific, and adaptive validity

exp: Validity is the ability of a PROM to measure the concept or attribute it intends to measure, and its three main types are content validity, criterion validity, and construct validity. The other groupings list reliability subtypes (internal-consistency, test–retest, inter-observer), MID methods (distribution/anchor), or PROM categories (generic/disease-specific) — none of which are the three validity types.
hy: Three validity types: content, criterion, construct.

why.A: Correct — content, criterion, and construct are the three validity types.
why.B: Wrong — those are reliability subtypes, not validity types.
why.C: Wrong — distribution/anchor are MID-estimation methods.
why.D: Wrong — generic/disease-specific are PROM categories.

stem: Which type of validity is considered the most important, and when is it typically established?

options.A: Content validity — established in the early qualitative phase of PROM development
options.B: Criterion validity — established only after translation into other languages
options.C: Construct validity — established solely by computerized adaptive testing
options.D: Face validity — established by administrative data review

exp: Content validity is considered the most important aspect of validity — the degree to which the items include the most relevant and important aspects of the concept being measured — and is typically established in the early qualitative phase of development (systematic review, expert opinion, and especially patient interviews). Criterion validity involves a gold standard, construct validity is assessed via RMT, and "face validity" by administrative review is not the cited answer.
hy: Content validity = most important; established in early qualitative phase.

why.A: Correct — content validity is most important, set in the qualitative phase.
why.B: Wrong — criterion validity (gold standard) is a different, later type.
why.C: Wrong — construct validity is assessed by RMT, and is not "most important."
why.D: Wrong — face validity via administrative review is not the cited concept.

stem: Criterion validity refers to which of the following?

options.A: The degree to which PROM scores are an adequate reflection of a true value or the best existing "gold standard"
options.B: The degree to which items reflect the most relevant aspects of the concept measured
options.C: The degree to which scores are consistent with the theoretical framework of the concept
options.D: The degree to which repeated measurements agree over time

exp: Criterion validity is the degree to which the scores of a PROM are an adequate reflection of a true value or the best existing "gold standard," assessed by correlating the new PROM with that standard (or, where none exists, with a longer/shorter version of itself). Reflecting the most relevant aspects of the concept is content validity, consistency with the theoretical framework is construct validity, and agreement of repeated measures is reliability.
hy: Criterion validity = agreement with a gold standard.

why.A: Correct — criterion validity compares scores to a gold standard.
why.B: Wrong — covering relevant aspects of the concept is content validity.
why.C: Wrong — consistency with the theoretical framework is construct validity.
why.D: Wrong — agreement of repeated measures is reliability.

stem: The COSMIN initiative released a 2010 checklist used for what purpose?

options.A: To evaluate the methodological quality of studies measuring patient-reported outcomes (including validity)
options.B: To translate PROMs into other languages
options.C: To set the $50,000/QALY adoption threshold
options.D: To define the four phases of clinical trials

exp: The COSMIN (Consensus-based Standards of Health Status Management Instruments) study released a checklist in 2010 to evaluate the methodological quality of studies measuring PROs, used as a guide to assess validity among other properties. It is not a translation tool, has nothing to do with the $50,000/QALY threshold, and does not define clinical-trial phases.
hy: COSMIN 2010 checklist = methodological-quality appraisal of PRO studies.

why.A: Correct — COSMIN's 2010 checklist appraises PRO study quality.
why.B: Wrong — translation guidance comes from ISPOR, not the COSMIN checklist.
why.C: Wrong — the $50,000/QALY threshold is from economic analysis (Ch 11).
why.D: Wrong — clinical-trial phases are unrelated to COSMIN.

stem: What does reliability of a PROM describe?

options.A: The degree to which measurement is free from error, yielding consistent and reproducible results
options.B: Whether the instrument measures the concept it intends to measure
options.C: Whether the instrument detects clinically important change over time
options.D: The ease of applying the instrument in clinical practice

exp: Reliability is the degree to which measurement is free from error — both random and systematic — so that the instrument yields consistent and reproducible results when applied on different occasions or by different observers. Measuring the intended concept is validity, detecting clinically important change is responsiveness, and ease of application is feasibility.
hy: Reliability = consistent, reproducible, error-free measurement.

why.A: Correct — reliability is freedom from error / reproducibility.
why.B: Wrong — measuring the intended concept is validity.
why.C: Wrong — detecting clinically important change is responsiveness.
why.D: Wrong — ease of application is feasibility.

stem: Per the COSMIN steering committee, what is the preferred statistic for internal-consistency reliability, and what value is considered good?

options.A: Cronbach's alpha (range 0–1), with a good value higher than 0.70
options.B: The intraclass correlation coefficient, with a good value below 0.30
options.C: Pearson's r, with a good value of exactly 0.50
options.D: Cohen's d, with a good value higher than 0.80

exp: Per the COSMIN steering committee, the preferred statistic for internal-consistency reliability (how well items in a multi-item scale measure the same concept) is Cronbach's alpha, whose values range from 0 to 1, with a good value higher than 0.70. The ICC is used for test–retest, Pearson's r for inter-observer agreement, and Cohen's d is an effect-size measure for responsiveness — none is the internal-consistency statistic with the stated cutoff.
hy: Internal consistency = Cronbach's alpha (0–1), good > 0.70.

why.A: Correct — Cronbach's alpha, good above 0.70, for internal consistency.
why.B: Wrong — the ICC is a test–retest statistic, and good values are high, not <0.30.
why.C: Wrong — Pearson's r relates to inter-observer agreement, not a fixed 0.50.
why.D: Wrong — Cohen's d is an effect size for responsiveness, not internal consistency.

stem: In Rasch Measurement Theory, which index is the equivalent of Cronbach's alpha, measuring how spread out a sample is on a scale?

options.A: The Person Separation Index
options.B: The intraclass correlation coefficient
options.C: The minimal important difference
options.D: The standardized response mean

exp: In Rasch Measurement Theory, the Person Separation Index is the equivalent of Cronbach's alpha and measures how spread out a sample is along a scale. The intraclass correlation coefficient is a test–retest/inter-observer statistic, the minimal important difference is an interpretability metric, and the standardized response mean is a responsiveness statistic — none is the RMT analogue of Cronbach's alpha.
hy: Person Separation Index = RMT equivalent of Cronbach's alpha.

why.A: Correct — the Person Separation Index is the RMT analogue.
why.B: Wrong — the ICC is a reproducibility statistic, not the RMT alpha analogue.
why.C: Wrong — the MID is about interpretability, not internal consistency.
why.D: Wrong — the standardized response mean quantifies responsiveness.

stem: For test–retest reliability to be considered acceptable, the chapter cites what minimum intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) and sample size?

options.A: A minimum ICC of 0.70 with at least 50 patients
options.B: A minimum ICC of 0.30 with at least 10 patients
options.C: A minimum ICC of 0.95 with at least 500 patients
options.D: A minimum ICC of 0.50 with at least 20 patients

exp: For test–retest reliability to be considered acceptable, the chapter states a study should meet a minimum intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) of 0.70 with at least 50 patients. The lower thresholds (0.30/10, 0.50/20) are too lax, and 0.95/500 is stricter than the cited standard. Test–retest itself uses a 3–14-day interval, excluding patients whose health changed.
hy: Acceptable test–retest: ICC ≥ 0.70 with ≥ 50 patients.

why.A: Correct — minimum ICC of 0.70 with at least 50 patients.
why.B: Wrong — 0.30/10 is well below the cited standard.
why.C: Wrong — 0.95/500 exceeds the cited requirement.
why.D: Wrong — 0.50/20 is below the cited threshold.

stem: Inter-observer reliability is best described as which of the following?

options.A: The degree of consistency between different observers assessing the same patients at the same time
options.B: The agreement of one patient's responses on two occasions over time
options.C: The degree to which items within one scale measure the same concept
options.D: The ability of the instrument to detect change after surgery

exp: Inter-observer reliability measures the degree of consistency between different observers assessing the same patients at the same time, commonly quantified with Pearson's r or the intraclass correlation coefficient. Agreement of one patient's responses on two occasions over time is test–retest reliability, consistency among items within a scale is internal-consistency reliability, and detecting post-surgical change is responsiveness.
hy: Inter-observer reliability = consistency between different observers, same patients/time.

why.A: Correct — agreement between different observers of the same patients.
why.B: Wrong — one patient on two occasions is test–retest reliability.
why.C: Wrong — agreement among items is internal-consistency reliability.
why.D: Wrong — detecting change after surgery is responsiveness.

stem: Responsiveness is the ability of a PROM to detect clinically important change over time. Effect size is commonly interpreted using Cohen's criteria — what are the thresholds?

options.A: Small 0.2, moderate 0.5, large 0.8
options.B: Small 0.5, moderate 0.7, large 0.9
options.C: Small 0.05, moderate 0.5, large 0.95
options.D: Small 0.1, moderate 0.3, large 0.6

exp: Responsiveness — a PROM's ability to detect a meaningful or clinically important change over time — is often quantified with effect sizes interpreted using Cohen's arbitrary criteria: small 0.2, moderate 0.5, large 0.8. The other threshold sets do not match Cohen's conventional cut points. Distribution-based responsiveness analysis typically uses paired t-tests, effect sizes, and standardized response means.
hy: Cohen's effect size: small 0.2, moderate 0.5, large 0.8.

why.A: Correct — Cohen's 0.2/0.5/0.8 small/moderate/large thresholds.
why.B: Wrong — 0.5/0.7/0.9 are not Cohen's criteria.
why.C: Wrong — 0.05/0.5/0.95 are not Cohen's criteria.
why.D: Wrong — 0.1/0.3/0.6 are not Cohen's criteria.

stem: What is the minimal important difference (MID) of a PROM?

options.A: The smallest score difference in a domain of interest that patients perceive as beneficial and that would mandate a change in management
options.B: The largest score change ever recorded in a clinical trial
options.C: The difference between the highest and lowest possible scale scores
options.D: The number of items needed to reach measurement precision

exp: The minimal important difference (MID) is the smallest difference in score in a domain of interest that patients perceive as beneficial and which would mandate — absent troublesome side effects and excessive cost — a change in the patient's management. It is not the largest change observed, the full scale range, or the number of items needed for precision. Knowing the MID lets clinicians judge whether a statistically significant change is also clinically meaningful.
hy: MID = smallest patient-perceived beneficial change that would alter management.

why.A: Correct — the smallest patient-meaningful change altering management.
why.B: Wrong — the MID is the smallest important change, not the largest observed.
why.C: Wrong — the full score range is not the MID.
why.D: Wrong — items-to-precision is a CAT concept, not the MID.

stem: The two main approaches to estimate the MID are distribution-based and anchor-based. Which is recommended, and why?

options.A: Anchor-based methods are recommended because distribution-based methods do not reflect the patient's perspective
options.B: Distribution-based methods are recommended because they incorporate the patient's perspective
options.C: Both are discouraged in favor of expert consensus
options.D: Anchor-based methods are discouraged because they require no external measure

exp: The two MID approaches are distribution-based (using statistical variability such as effect size or standardized response mean) and anchor-based (correlating PROM change with an external, patient-relevant anchor). Anchor-based estimation is recommended because distribution-based methods do not reflect the patient's perspective — they rely only on the statistical characteristics of the data set. Distribution-based values can serve as supportive evidence or a fallback.
hy: MID: anchor-based preferred (patient perspective); distribution-based ignores it.

why.A: Correct — anchor-based is preferred because it reflects the patient perspective.
why.B: Wrong — distribution-based methods do NOT incorporate the patient perspective.
why.C: Wrong — MID estimation is recommended, not abandoned for expert consensus.
why.D: Wrong — anchor-based methods do use an external measure, which is their strength.

stem: Knowing a PROM's MID lets investigators conduct a responder analysis, which can be transformed into which clinically useful metric?

options.A: The number needed to treat (NNT) to achieve an MID-equivalent change in one patient
options.B: The Cronbach's alpha of the scale
options.C: The intraclass correlation coefficient
options.D: The Person Separation Index

exp: Knowing the MID lets investigators perform a responder analysis — calculating the proportion of patients improving by more than the MID — which can then be transformed into a number needed to treat (NNT): the number of patients needed to achieve an MID-equivalent change in one patient over a given period. Cronbach's alpha, the ICC, and the Person Separation Index are reliability statistics, not the metric derived from a responder analysis.
hy: MID + responder analysis → number needed to treat (NNT).

why.A: Correct — a responder analysis converts to the NNT.
why.B: Wrong — Cronbach's alpha is an internal-consistency statistic.
why.C: Wrong — the ICC is a reproducibility statistic.
why.D: Wrong — the Person Separation Index is an RMT reliability index.

stem: How do "feasibility" and "burden" differ as considerations when selecting a PROM?

options.A: Feasibility is the practical ease of applying the PROM (not a psychometric property); burden is the time, effort, and demands placed on respondents or administrators
options.B: Feasibility and burden are both psychometric properties like validity and reliability
options.C: Feasibility is the time placed on respondents; burden is the ease of application
options.D: Both refer solely to the monetary cost of the instrument

exp: Feasibility is the ease of applying a PROM in its intended context (e.g., cost or time to complete) and is a practical characteristic, not a psychometric property. Burden is the time, effort, and other demands placed on those completing the PROM (respondent burden) or administering it (clinician/administrative burden). They are related but distinct, and neither is a psychometric property like validity or reliability, nor solely about monetary cost.
hy: Feasibility = practical ease of use; burden = time/effort demanded.

why.A: Correct — feasibility is practical ease; burden is the demand placed on people.
why.B: Wrong — neither is a psychometric property like validity/reliability.
why.C: Wrong — the definitions are swapped in this option.
why.D: Wrong — both are broader than monetary cost alone.

stem: What are the three main future priorities for PROs in plastic surgery research and clinical care?

options.A: Incorporate PRO data into decision-making, integrate it into the electronic health record, and use it to measure and improve quality of care
options.B: Replace all clinical outcomes with PROs, eliminate generic PROMs, and ban legacy instruments
options.C: Limit PROs to research only, exclude them from the EHR, and avoid quality comparisons
options.D: Standardize a single PROM for all conditions, mandate paper administration, and abolish CAT

exp: The three main future priorities for PROs in plastic surgery research and clinical care are to incorporate PRO data into the decision-making process for patient care, integrate PRO data into the electronic health record, and use PRO data to measure and improve quality of care. The other options describe extreme or contrary positions (replacing all clinical outcomes, restricting PROs from the EHR, mandating one paper PROM) that the chapter does not advocate.
hy: PRO priorities: decision-making, EHR integration, quality measurement/improvement.

why.A: Correct — decision-making, EHR integration, and quality improvement.
why.B: Wrong — the chapter does not call for replacing all clinical outcomes or banning legacy tools.
why.C: Wrong — the goal is to integrate PROs into the EHR, not exclude them.
why.D: Wrong — it does not mandate one PROM or abolish CAT.

stem: Administering PROMs through a patient portal enables a "measure once, cut twice" approach. What does this mean?

options.A: The same data collected for clinical care can also be used for real-world comparative-effectiveness research and quality improvement
options.B: Each PROM must be administered twice to be valid
options.C: Surgeons should operate twice after a single measurement
options.D: PRO data must be discarded after a single clinical use

exp: A "measure once, cut twice" approach means that PRO data collected via a patient portal for routine clinical care can simultaneously be used for real-world comparative-effectiveness research and quality-improvement studies — one collection serving two purposes. It does not mean administering a PROM twice for validity, operating twice, or discarding the data after one use. This efficiency is a key argument for embedding PROs in the EHR.
hy: "Measure once, cut twice" = one PRO collection serves both care and research/QI.

why.A: Correct — a single collection serves both clinical care and research/QI.
why.B: Wrong — it does not mean administering the PROM twice.
why.C: Wrong — it is a data-reuse metaphor, not a surgical instruction.
why.D: Wrong — the point is to reuse, not discard, the data.

stem: How does the AHRQ characterize health services research (HSR), distinguishing it from traditional clinical research?

options.A: A multidisciplinary field studying how social factors, financing systems, organizational structures, technologies, and behaviors affect access, quality, cost, and ultimately health — taking a broader view than clinical research
options.B: The study of disease mechanisms at the molecular and cellular level
options.C: Randomized controlled trials of surgical devices under tightly monitored conditions
options.D: The bench-to-bedside translation of basic-science discoveries into first-in-human trials

exp: Per the AHRQ, health services research is a multidisciplinary field studying how social factors, financing systems, organizational structures and processes, health technologies, and personal behaviors affect access to care, the quality and cost of care, and ultimately health and well-being — a deliberately broader lens than clinical research, considering real-world delivery rather than tightly monitored trials. Molecular/cellular disease study (basic science), device RCTs (clinical trials), and bench-to-bedside translation are different research enterprises.
hy: HSR (AHRQ) = how social/financing/organizational/technology/behavior factors affect access, quality, cost, health.
src: Neligan I.13 · What is health services research?
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — the AHRQ multidisciplinary definition spanning access/quality/cost/health.
why.B: Wrong — molecular/cellular mechanism is basic science, not HSR.
why.C: Wrong — tightly monitored device RCTs are traditional clinical research.
why.D: Wrong — bench-to-bedside translation is translational science, not HSR.

stem: The AMA and CDC recommend a shift in terminology in health-equity work. Which change do they endorse?

options.A: From "disparities" to "inequities" (unjust and avoidable differences), and from "high-risk groups" to "groups that have been historically marginalized or made vulnerable"
options.B: From "inequities" to "disparities," because the former implies blame
options.C: From "social determinants" to "biological determinants" of health
options.D: From "marginalized groups" to "high-risk groups," to emphasize clinical risk

exp: The AMA and CDC recommend shifting from "disparities" to "inequities" (defined as unjust and avoidable differences) and from stigmatizing labels like "high-risk groups" to "groups that have been historically marginalized or made vulnerable," to avoid unintentional blame. Option B reverses the recommended direction, C swaps social for biological framing (the opposite of guidance), and D reverts to the discouraged "high-risk" label.
hy: AMA/CDC: "inequities" (unjust/avoidable) and "historically marginalized," not "high-risk."
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — inequities and "historically marginalized/made vulnerable" are the endorsed terms.
why.B: Wrong — the shift is toward "inequities," not back to "disparities."
why.C: Wrong — race/structure is framed as social, not biological.
why.D: Wrong — "high-risk groups" is the term being moved away from.

stem: The chapter argues that adjusting a statistical model for race can be problematic. Why?

options.A: Race is a social, not biological, construct, so adjusting for it can obscure the role of structural racism on the outcome
options.B: Race is a purely biological variable that should never be included in any model
options.C: Adjusting for race always eliminates confounding and is therefore mandatory
options.D: Race has no measurable association with surgical outcomes

exp: The chapter states that race is a social, not biological, construct, so statistically adjusting a model for race can ignore — and thereby obscure — the role of structural racism on the outcome; there is not yet an established quantitative method to account for historic/current structural disadvantage. Option B wrongly calls race biological, C overstates adjustment as always-correct, and D denies the well-documented associations.
hy: Race = social construct; adjusting for it can mask structural racism's effect.
src: Neligan I.13 · Considering structural root causes
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — race is social; naive adjustment obscures structural racism.
why.B: Wrong — race is not a biological variable here.
why.C: Wrong — adjusting for race does not automatically remove confounding.
why.D: Wrong — race is associated with outcomes via structural factors.

stem: The Neighborhood Atlas project (University of Wisconsin) lets researchers merge their datasets with which geographic measure of socioeconomic disadvantage?

options.A: The Area Deprivation Index (ADI), built from income, education, and employment metrics
options.B: The Gini coefficient of national income inequality
options.C: The body mass index (BMI) distribution by ZIP code
options.D: The Charlson comorbidity index

exp: The Neighborhood Atlas (University of Wisconsin) lets researchers merge their datasets with the Area Deprivation Index (ADI), a geographic marker of relative socioeconomic disadvantage built from traditional metrics of income, education, and employment; it has been used for risk-adjustment and to study residential burn-injury risk. The Gini coefficient, BMI distribution, and Charlson comorbidity index are unrelated indices.
hy: Neighborhood Atlas → Area Deprivation Index (ADI): income/education/employment.
src: Neligan I.13 · Considering structural root causes
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — the ADI is the Neighborhood Atlas socioeconomic measure.
why.B: Wrong — the Gini coefficient measures national income inequality, not this.
why.C: Wrong — BMI distribution is unrelated to the ADI.
why.D: Wrong — the Charlson index measures comorbidity, not deprivation.

stem: Of particular relevance to plastic surgeons, which 1998 federal policy increased rates of breast reconstruction (though without fixing systemic barriers beyond insurance coverage)?

options.A: The Women's Health and Cancer Rights Act
options.B: The Affordable Care Act
options.C: The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)
options.D: The Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program

exp: The 1998 Women's Health and Cancer Rights Act increased rates of breast reconstruction by mandating insurance coverage, but the chapter stresses it did not fix systemic barriers to reconstruction beyond coverage. The Affordable Care Act (2010) is a different, broader law; HIPAA governs privacy/portability; and the HRRP is a CMS readmissions-penalty program — none is the 1998 breast-reconstruction coverage act.
hy: Women's Health and Cancer Rights Act (1998) → ↑breast reconstruction (coverage only).
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — the 1998 WHCRA increased reconstruction via coverage.
why.B: Wrong — the ACA (2010) is a separate, broader law.
why.C: Wrong — HIPAA addresses privacy/portability, not reconstruction coverage.
why.D: Wrong — the HRRP is a readmissions-penalty program.

stem: The CMS Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program (HRRP) and Merit-Based Incentive Payment System (MIPS) are payment/penalty programs that aim to incentivize quality and control cost. What unintended risk does the chapter highlight?

options.A: They risk disproportionately impacting hospitals and surgeons serving under-resourced communities when resources are scarce
options.B: They guarantee increased reimbursement for all surgeons regardless of outcomes
options.C: They apply only to cosmetic, self-pay procedures
options.D: They eliminate all variation in surgical quality

exp: CMS programs like the HRRP and MIPS aim to incentivize quality and control cost, but the chapter warns they risk disproportionately impacting hospitals and surgeons serving under-resourced communities when resources are scarce. They do not guarantee higher reimbursement for everyone (B), are not limited to cosmetic self-pay procedures (C), and do not eliminate quality variation (D).
hy: HRRP/MIPS risk: penalizing safety-net providers in under-resourced settings.
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — the equity risk to under-resourced providers.
why.B: Wrong — they tie payment to performance, not guaranteed increases.
why.C: Wrong — they apply broadly, not only to cosmetic procedures.
why.D: Wrong — they do not abolish quality variation.

stem: The chapter describes two primary approaches to policy research. Which pair is correct?

options.A: Motivating policy-makers with data, or using data to investigate an existing policy
options.B: Lobbying legislators directly, or funding political campaigns
options.C: Running randomized controlled trials of laws, or conducting bench experiments
options.D: Avoiding policy entirely, or deferring to hospital administrators

exp: The chapter names two primary approaches to policy research: motivating policy-makers with data (using evidence to drive new policy) or using data to investigate an existing policy's effects. Direct lobbying/campaign funding (B), randomized trials of laws or bench experiments (C), and avoiding policy altogether (D) are not the described research approaches.
hy: Policy research: motivate policy with data, or study existing policy with data.
src: Neligan I.13 · How to engage with policy?
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — the two described data-driven approaches.
why.B: Wrong — lobbying/campaign funding are advocacy, not research methods.
why.C: Wrong — RCTs of laws are infeasible; bench work is unrelated.
why.D: Wrong — engaging with policy is encouraged, not avoided.

stem: Why are randomized controlled trials generally not used to evaluate health policy, prompting reliance on "natural" or "quasi" experiments?

options.A: RCTs are not feasible at the level of policy implementation, so econometric/observational methods are used to infer causality
options.B: RCTs are unethical for any non-pharmacologic question
options.C: Policy effects are too large to require statistical analysis
options.D: Observational data cannot address policy questions at all

exp: Randomized controlled trials are not feasible at the level of policy implementation, so health services researchers use econometric/observational methods — "natural" or "quasi" experiments — to infer causality between a policy and outcomes. RCTs are not unethical for all non-drug questions (B), policy effects still require careful statistical analysis (C), and observational data can in fact address policy questions (D).
hy: Policy RCTs infeasible → natural/quasi experiments (econometrics) for causal inference.
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — quasi-experimental econometric methods substitute for infeasible RCTs.
why.B: Wrong — non-drug RCTs are not categorically unethical.
why.C: Wrong — policy analysis still needs statistics.
why.D: Wrong — observational data can address policy questions.

stem: In a difference-in-difference (DID) analysis of a policy's impact, what does the result actually represent, and what key assumption underlies it?

options.A: The difference between the change in the exposed group and the change in an unexposed group, relying on the assumption that the two trends would have been parallel without the policy
options.B: The absolute outcome in the exposed group only, with no comparison group
options.C: The ratio of outcomes between two randomized arms
options.D: The difference between pre- and post-policy outcomes in the exposed group alone, ignoring secular trends

exp: A difference-in-difference analysis estimates the difference between the change in the exposed (policy) group and the change in an unexposed control group, which controls for underlying secular trends (e.g. national screening changes); it relies on the parallel-trends assumption — that without the policy the two groups' trends would have moved in parallel. Looking at the exposed group alone (B, D) ignores secular trends, and it is not a ratio of randomized arms (C).
hy: DID = (change in exposed) − (change in unexposed); parallel-trends assumption.
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — the difference of differences with the parallel-trends assumption.
why.B: Wrong — DID requires a comparison group, not the exposed group alone.
why.C: Wrong — it is a difference of changes, not a ratio of randomized arms.
why.D: Wrong — exposed-only pre/post ignores secular trends DID controls for.

stem: The Institute of Medicine's 2001 report Crossing the Quality Chasm called for health care that has which six aims?

options.A: Safe, effective, patient-centered, timely, efficient, and equitable
options.B: Cheap, fast, profitable, scalable, automated, and standardized
options.C: Innovative, proprietary, exclusive, premium, marketable, and branded
options.D: Centralized, federalized, rationed, audited, penalized, and reported

exp: The IOM's 2001 Crossing the Quality Chasm called for health care that is safe, effective, patient-centered, timely, efficient, and equitable — the six aims for improvement. The other lists (cheap/fast/profitable; innovative/proprietary; centralized/rationed) are not the IOM aims and in places contradict them.
hy: IOM six aims: safe, effective, patient-centered, timely, efficient, equitable.
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — the six IOM quality aims.
why.B: Wrong — profit/speed framing is not the IOM aims.
why.C: Wrong — proprietary/premium branding contradicts the aims.
why.D: Wrong — rationing/penalizing is not the IOM framework.

stem: Per Table 13.1 (Donabedian domains applied to plastic surgery quality), which example is correctly matched to its domain?

options.A: Case volume and trained microsurgeons are Structure; pain-control education/prescribing and pre-mastectomy referral are Process; patient-reported satisfaction and flap loss are Outcome
options.B: Flap loss is Structure; case volume is Outcome; satisfaction is Process
options.C: Patient satisfaction is Structure; trained microsurgeons are Outcome; flap loss is Process
options.D: All three examples belong to the Process domain

exp: Per Table 13.1 (Donabedian applied to plastic surgery), Structure examples are case volume and trained microsurgeons; Process examples are pain-control education/prescribing and referral to a reconstructive surgeon before mastectomy; and Outcome examples are patient-reported satisfaction and flap loss. The other options scramble the domain assignments.
hy: Table 13.1: Structure (volume/microsurgeons), Process (education/referral), Outcome (satisfaction/flap loss).
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — the correct Structure/Process/Outcome example mapping.
why.B: Wrong — flap loss is Outcome, not Structure.
why.C: Wrong — satisfaction is Outcome, not Structure.
why.D: Wrong — the examples span all three domains, not just Process.

stem: Per Table 13.1, what is a stated disadvantage of structure metrics and of outcome metrics, respectively?

options.A: Structure metrics are not direct targets for improvement; large databases rarely capture procedure-specific outcomes
options.B: Structure metrics are impossible to measure; outcome metrics are irrelevant to surgeons
options.C: Structure metrics are always procedure-specific; outcome metrics are easiest to measure
options.D: Both have no disadvantages and are interchangeable

exp: Per Table 13.1, a disadvantage of Structure metrics is that they are not direct targets for improvement, and a disadvantage of Outcome metrics is that large databases rarely capture procedure-specific outcomes. Structure metrics are actually easy to measure (not impossible), outcome metrics do matter to surgeons, and both have real disadvantages — so the other options are wrong.
hy: Structure = not direct improvement targets; Outcome = databases miss procedure-specific outcomes.
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — the stated disadvantages of structure and outcome metrics.
why.B: Wrong — structure metrics are easy to measure, and outcomes do matter.
why.C: Wrong — structure metrics are not procedure-specific; outcomes aren't easiest.
why.D: Wrong — each domain has a stated disadvantage.

stem: The ASPS founded the Tracking Operations and Outcomes in Plastic Surgery (TOPS) program in 2002. What distinguishes the data it captures from the American College of Surgeons NSQIP?

options.A: TOPS captures outcomes of specific interest to plastic surgeons (e.g. flap loss) that NSQIP extractors do not report; participation is voluntary and data are free to ASPS members
options.B: TOPS is a mandatory federal registry that replaces NSQIP
options.C: TOPS captures only medical complications and ignores surgical outcomes
options.D: TOPS and NSQIP capture identical data, making comparison pointless

exp: TOPS (Tracking Operations and Outcomes in Plastic Surgery), founded by the ASPS in 2002, captures outcome metrics of specific interest to plastic surgeons — notably flap loss — that the ACS-NSQIP extractors do not report; participation is voluntary and aggregated data are provided free to ASPS members. It is not a mandatory federal registry (B), captures surgical outcomes (C), and differs meaningfully from NSQIP (D).
hy: TOPS (ASPS, 2002): voluntary/free, captures plastic-specific outcomes (flap loss) NSQIP misses.
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — plastic-specific outcomes, voluntary and free to members.
why.B: Wrong — TOPS is voluntary, not a mandatory federal replacement.
why.C: Wrong — TOPS captures surgical outcomes like flap loss.
why.D: Wrong — TOPS and NSQIP differ, which is the point of comparison.

stem: Khavanin et al. compared TOPS and NSQIP for a matched breast-surgery cohort. What did they find?

options.A: TOPS captured more wound dehiscence and reconstructive failure, whereas medical complications were better captured in NSQIP
options.B: NSQIP captured more flap loss and reconstructive failure than TOPS
options.C: The two databases captured identical complication profiles
options.D: Neither database captured any surgical complications

exp: Khavanin et al. compared matched breast-surgery cohorts and found that TOPS captured more wound dehiscence and reconstructive failure, whereas medical complications were better captured in NSQIP — illustrating that the research question should steer dataset choice. Option B reverses the finding, C wrongly claims identical capture, and D denies any surgical-complication capture.
hy: Khavanin: TOPS > dehiscence/reconstructive failure; NSQIP > medical complications.
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — TOPS for surgical, NSQIP for medical complications.
why.B: Wrong — the database strengths are reversed.
why.C: Wrong — the databases captured different profiles.
why.D: Wrong — both captured complications, just different types.

stem: The chapter cites readmission rates after plastic surgery (a metric central to the HRRP). What were the approximate outpatient and inpatient figures?

options.A: ~1.9% after outpatient plastic surgery and ~7.1% after inpatient plastic surgery
options.B: ~20% after outpatient and ~50% after inpatient surgery
options.C: ~0.01% after outpatient and ~0.1% after inpatient surgery
options.D: Identical (~5%) for both settings

exp: The chapter cites readmission (the focus of the HRRP) as approximately 1.9% after outpatient plastic surgery and 7.1% after inpatient plastic surgery, underscoring the need for procedure-specific data and attention to outcomes that matter to patients. The other figures grossly over- or under-state these rates or wrongly equate the two settings.
hy: Plastic-surgery readmission: ~1.9% outpatient, ~7.1% inpatient.
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — 1.9% outpatient and 7.1% inpatient.
why.B: Wrong — 20%/50% grossly overstate the rates.
why.C: Wrong — 0.01%/0.1% grossly understate them.
why.D: Wrong — the two settings differ (1.9% vs 7.1%).

stem: Birkmeyer et al.'s seminal volume–outcome work, and its follow-up, concluded which of the following?

options.A: High hospital volume was associated with lower mortality, and surgeon volume mediated most of the hospital-volume effect — as surgeon volume rose, mortality fell across procedures
options.B: Hospital and surgeon volume had no relationship to mortality
options.C: Low-volume hospitals consistently outperformed high-volume hospitals
options.D: Only nurse staffing, not surgeon volume, affected outcomes

exp: Birkmeyer et al. first reported lower mortality at high-volume hospitals, and subsequent work showed surgeon volume mediated most of the hospital-volume effect — as surgeon volume increased, mortality decreased across the procedures studied, motivating discussion of regionalization/minimum-volume thresholds. The other options deny the volume–outcome relationship or invert it.
hy: Birkmeyer: high volume → lower mortality; surgeon volume mediates the hospital effect.
src: Neligan I.13 · The future of assessment
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — surgeon volume mediates the hospital volume–mortality benefit.
why.B: Wrong — a clear volume–mortality relationship exists.
why.C: Wrong — high-volume centers generally do better, not worse.
why.D: Wrong — surgeon volume, not just nurse staffing, mattered.

stem: Yoon, Chung et al. developed a proficiency scoring system for surgeons performing digit replantation. What did it show relative to case volume?

options.A: Surgeon proficiency score had a stronger association with outcomes than surgeon volume, suggesting attention should focus on skill over volume
options.B: Surgeon volume predicted outcomes better than any measure of skill
options.C: Proficiency and volume were both unrelated to outcomes
options.D: Years of experience was the single best predictor, outperforming both

exp: Yoon, Chung et al. developed a proficiency scoring system for digit replantation and found the surgeon proficiency score had a stronger association with outcomes than surgeon volume — suggesting quality efforts should focus on skill rather than volume alone. Option B reverses this, C denies any association, and D wrongly elevates years of experience.
hy: Digit-replantation proficiency score > surgeon volume for predicting outcomes.
src: Neligan I.13 · The future of assessment
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — proficiency outperformed volume, favoring skill focus.
why.B: Wrong — proficiency, not volume, was the stronger predictor.
why.C: Wrong — proficiency was associated with outcomes.
why.D: Wrong — years of experience was not the best predictor.

stem: Plastic surgery has led the development of validated patient-reported outcome instruments. Which set are the Q-suite PROs named in the chapter?

options.A: WOUND-Q, FACE-Q, BODY-Q, and BREAST-Q (with GENDER-Q under development)
options.B: SF-36, EQ-5D, PROMIS-29, and HUI
options.C: APACHE, SOFA, Glasgow, and Charlson
options.D: CONSORT, STROBE, PRISMA, and CHEERS

exp: Plastic surgery has led the development of validated patient-reported outcome instruments, including the WOUND-Q, FACE-Q, BODY-Q, and BREAST-Q (the rigorous GENDER-Q for gender-affirming care is under development); these are free to non-profit users. SF-36/EQ-5D/PROMIS-29/HUI are generic health-status/utility tools, APACHE/SOFA/Glasgow/Charlson are severity/comorbidity scores, and CONSORT/STROBE/PRISMA/CHEERS are reporting guidelines.
hy: Plastic-surgery PROs: WOUND-Q, FACE-Q, BODY-Q, BREAST-Q (+ GENDER-Q in progress).
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — the Q-suite plastic-surgery PROs.
why.B: Wrong — those are generic health-status/utility instruments.
why.C: Wrong — APACHE/SOFA/Glasgow/Charlson are severity/comorbidity scores.
why.D: Wrong — CONSORT/STROBE/PRISMA/CHEERS are reporting guidelines.

stem: How many modules does the BREAST-Q comprise, and what are they?

options.A: Three modules — augmentation, breast cancer, and reduction — each with multiple scales the researcher can select from
options.B: A single universal module used for all breast procedures
options.C: Two modules — cosmetic and reconstructive — with no sub-scales
options.D: Five modules, one for each Fitzpatrick skin type

exp: The BREAST-Q comprises three modules — augmentation, breast cancer, and reduction — each containing multiple scales from which researchers select those most relevant to their study and population. It is not a single universal module (B), not a two-module cosmetic/reconstructive split (C), and not organized by Fitzpatrick skin type (D).
hy: BREAST-Q = 3 modules (augmentation, breast cancer, reduction), multi-scale.
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — augmentation, breast cancer, and reduction modules.
why.B: Wrong — it is modular, not a single universal questionnaire.
why.C: Wrong — there are three modules, not a two-way cosmetic/reconstructive split.
why.D: Wrong — modules are by procedure, not skin type.

stem: The chapter stresses the distinction between effectiveness and efficacy, and the trial spectrum that mirrors it. Which statement is correct?

options.A: Effectiveness refers to real-world outcomes (assessed by pragmatic trials), whereas efficacy is measured under tightly controlled randomized conditions (explanatory trials)
options.B: Effectiveness is measured in placebo-controlled RCTs, whereas efficacy refers to real-world outcomes
options.C: Effectiveness and efficacy are synonyms with no practical difference
options.D: Explanatory trials assess effectiveness and pragmatic trials assess efficacy

exp: Effectiveness refers to real-world outcomes and is assessed by pragmatic trials, whereas efficacy is measured under tightly controlled randomized conditions and is assessed by explanatory trials — the explanatory-to-pragmatic spectrum mirrors the efficacy-to-effectiveness distinction. Option B reverses the trial settings, C wrongly calls them synonyms, and D swaps which trial type assesses which.
hy: Efficacy = explanatory/controlled; effectiveness = pragmatic/real-world.
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — pragmatic→effectiveness, explanatory→efficacy.
why.B: Wrong — the RCT/real-world assignments are reversed.
why.C: Wrong — efficacy and effectiveness are distinct.
why.D: Wrong — explanatory assesses efficacy, pragmatic assesses effectiveness.

stem: What distinguishes a collaborative quality improvement (CQI) initiative in its incentive structure?

options.A: It emphasizes pay-for-participation over pay-for-performance, forming groups of hospitals with shared interest in improvement
options.B: It penalizes hospitals financially for every readmission
options.C: It pays surgeons only when risk-adjusted mortality falls below a threshold
options.D: It relies solely on public reporting without any collaboration

exp: Collaborative quality improvement (CQI) initiatives emphasize pay-for-participation over pay-for-performance, forming groups of hospitals with a shared interest in improvement that launch initiatives and collect feedback together rather than relying on public reporting alone. It is not a per-readmission penalty (B), not threshold-based mortality pay (C), and explicitly more than public reporting (D).
hy: CQI = pay-for-participation, collaborative (not pay-for-performance/public reporting alone).
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — pay-for-participation, collaborative improvement.
why.B: Wrong — CQI is not a per-readmission penalty scheme.
why.C: Wrong — it is not threshold-based mortality payment.
why.D: Wrong — it adds collaboration beyond bare public reporting.

stem: A cohort study within the Michigan Collaborative Hand Initiative for Quality in Surgery (MCHIQS) examined electrodiagnostic testing variation. What did it find?

options.A: 31% of testing variation occurred at the surgeon level and 8% at the practice level, without association with clinical presentation
options.B: All variation was explained by differences in patients' clinical presentation
options.C: Variation occurred only at the practice level, never the surgeon level
options.D: There was no measurable variation between surgeons or practices

exp: A cohort study within the Michigan Collaborative Hand Initiative for Quality in Surgery (MCHIQS) found that 31% of electrodiagnostic-testing variation occurred at the surgeon level and 8% at the practice level, without association with clinical presentation — evidence of practice variation unexplained by patient factors. Options B–D deny or misattribute this variation.
hy: MCHIQS: 31% of EDX-testing variation surgeon-level, 8% practice-level, unrelated to presentation.
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — 31% surgeon-level and 8% practice-level variation.
why.B: Wrong — clinical presentation did not explain the variation.
why.C: Wrong — variation occurred at the surgeon level too.
why.D: Wrong — there was substantial measured variation.

stem: The PDSA cycle used by collaborative quality initiatives stands for which sequence?

options.A: Plan, Do, Study, Act
options.B: Plan, Deliver, Score, Adjust
options.C: Predict, Design, Sample, Analyze
options.D: Prepare, Deploy, Standardize, Audit

exp: The continuous quality-improvement cycle used by collaborative quality initiatives is PDSA — Plan, Do, Study, Act (Fig 13.3): plan from collaborative meetings, do/implement an initiative, study the data for trends, and act on feedback to change practice. The other expansions are not the PDSA cycle.
hy: PDSA = Plan, Do, Study, Act (CQI cycle).
src: Neligan I.13 · Collaborative quality improvement / Fig 13.3
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — Plan, Do, Study, Act.
why.B: Wrong — "Deliver/Score/Adjust" is not the PDSA cycle.
why.C: Wrong — "Predict/Design/Sample/Analyze" is fabricated.
why.D: Wrong — "Prepare/Deploy/Standardize/Audit" is not PDSA.

stem: How does implementation science differ from local quality improvement, and what outcomes does it emphasize?

options.A: Its goal is generalizable knowledge about launching an intervention (vs the local effect of QI), and its outcomes center on acceptability, feasibility, and sustainability
options.B: It focuses only on the local effect of an intervention at a single site
options.C: It measures only mortality and length of stay
options.D: It is identical to quality improvement, with no meaningful distinction

exp: Implementation science differs from local quality improvement in that its goal is generalizable knowledge about how to launch and scale an intervention (not just the local effect at one site), and its outcomes most often center on acceptability, feasibility, and sustainability — whether other surgeons will accept, employ, and continue the change. The other options confine it to one site, to mortality/LOS, or equate it with QI.
hy: Implementation science = generalizable scaling knowledge; outcomes = acceptability/feasibility/sustainability.
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — generalizable knowledge; acceptability/feasibility/sustainability.
why.B: Wrong — that describes local quality improvement, not implementation science.
why.C: Wrong — it is broader than mortality/LOS metrics.
why.D: Wrong — it is distinct from quality improvement.

stem: The TRIP (Translating Evidence into Practice) model of implementation has four components. Which sequence is correct?

options.A: Summarize the evidence; engage stakeholders to identify barriers; track uptake of the intervention; ensure patients are receiving the intervention
options.B: Randomize patients; blind assessors; analyze; publish
options.C: Diagnose; prescribe; monitor; discharge
options.D: Hypothesize; experiment; replicate; peer-review

exp: The TRIP (Translating Evidence into Practice) model has four components: summarize the evidence; engage all stakeholders to identify barriers to implementation; track the intervention's uptake by the team; and ensure patients are actually receiving the intervention. The other sequences describe RCT conduct, clinical care, or the scientific method — not TRIP.
hy: TRIP: summarize evidence → engage stakeholders/identify barriers → track uptake → ensure patients receive it.
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — the four TRIP components in order.
why.B: Wrong — randomize/blind/analyze is RCT conduct, not TRIP.
why.C: Wrong — diagnose/prescribe/monitor is clinical care.
why.D: Wrong — hypothesize/experiment is the generic scientific method.

stem: As an example of legislation affecting practice, the chapter notes a Michigan state law on opioid prescribing. What does it limit?

options.A: Prescriptions for acute pain to a 7-day supply
options.B: All opioid prescriptions to a single tablet
options.C: Opioid prescribing exclusively to pain specialists
options.D: Postoperative prescriptions to a 90-day supply

exp: As an example of legislation changing practice, the chapter notes a Michigan state law limiting prescriptions for acute pain to a 7-day supply; such legislation has reduced prescribing, though state involvement varies and changing patterns does not always require a legislative mandate. The other limits (single tablet, specialists-only, 90-day) are not the cited law.
hy: Michigan law: acute-pain opioid prescriptions limited to a 7-day supply.
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — a 7-day acute-pain supply limit.
why.B: Wrong — it is a 7-day supply, not a single tablet.
why.C: Wrong — it limits supply, not who may prescribe.
why.D: Wrong — it restricts to 7 days, not 90.

stem: How does qualitative research fundamentally differ from quantitative research in its starting point?

options.A: It does not begin with a hypothesis and is often hypothesis-generating, remaining open to theories rather than testing data against an existing theory
options.B: It always begins with a pre-specified hypothesis to be statistically tested
options.C: It requires a randomized control group
options.D: It can only confirm, never generate, theories

exp: Qualitative research is fundamentally distinct in that it does not begin with a hypothesis and is often hypothesis-generating — it remains open to theories emerging from the data rather than testing data against a pre-existing theory. It does not start with a fixed hypothesis (B), needs no randomized control group (C), and can generate, not merely confirm, theories (D).
hy: Qualitative research = hypothesis-generating, theory emerges from data.
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — no starting hypothesis; it generates theory.
why.B: Wrong — it does not begin with a hypothesis to test.
why.C: Wrong — no control group is required.
why.D: Wrong — it generates theories, not only confirms them.

stem: "The discovery of theory from data," one of the most used qualitative sociological approaches (alongside phenomenology), is known as which method?

options.A: Grounded theory
options.B: Difference-in-difference
options.C: Comparative effectiveness research
options.D: The Donabedian model

exp: "The discovery of theory from data" describes grounded theory, one of the most used qualitative sociological approaches alongside phenomenology. Difference-in-difference and comparative effectiveness research are quantitative methods, and the Donabedian model is a quality framework — none is the data-driven theory-generation approach described.
hy: Grounded theory = "discovery of theory from data."
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — grounded theory generates theory from data.
why.B: Wrong — difference-in-difference is a quantitative policy method.
why.C: Wrong — CER is a quantitative comparative method.
why.D: Wrong — Donabedian is a quality-of-care framework.

stem: In qualitative sampling, what distinguishes purposeful from convenient sampling, and what is the strength of purposeful sampling?

options.A: Purposeful sampling uses deliberate invitations to build a representative sample (its strength is centering marginalized voices), whereas convenient sampling interviews interested existing patients
options.B: Purposeful sampling interviews whoever is available; convenient sampling is randomized
options.C: Both are forms of probability sampling identical to RCT randomization
options.D: Purposeful sampling is always biased and should be avoided

exp: Purposeful sampling uses deliberate invitations to create a representative sample — its strength is centering marginalized voices — whereas convenient sampling interviews interested existing patients. Option B swaps the definitions, C wrongly equates them with RCT randomization, and D dismisses purposeful sampling, which is in fact a deliberate strength for under-represented populations.
hy: Purposeful = deliberate/representative (centers marginalized voices); convenient = available patients.
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — purposeful (deliberate) vs convenient (available), with purposeful's strength.
why.B: Wrong — the two sampling definitions are reversed.
why.C: Wrong — neither is probability/RCT randomization.
why.D: Wrong — purposeful sampling is a deliberate strength, not to be avoided.

stem: Mixed-methods research combines qualitative and quantitative methods in one of three designs. Which correctly describes the sequential designs?

options.A: Exploratory sequential uses qualitative research to inform subsequent quantitative research; explanatory sequential uses qualitative research to explain prior quantitative findings
options.B: Exploratory sequential uses quantitative research to inform qualitative; explanatory sequential is purely qualitative
options.C: Both sequential designs collect qualitative and quantitative data simultaneously with no ordering
options.D: Sequential designs never combine the two methods

exp: In mixed-methods research, exploratory sequential designs use qualitative research to inform subsequent quantitative research, whereas explanatory sequential designs use qualitative research to explain (follow up on) prior quantitative findings; the third design, convergent, collects both simultaneously. Option B reverses the qualitative/quantitative ordering, C describes only the convergent design, and D denies that mixed methods combine the two.
hy: Exploratory sequential = qual→quant; explanatory sequential = quant→qual explanation.
chname: Health Services Research in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — exploratory (qual informs quant) vs explanatory (qual explains quant).
why.B: Wrong — the ordering is reversed.
why.C: Wrong — simultaneous collection is the convergent design, not sequential.
why.D: Wrong — mixed methods by definition combine both.

stem: Tumors grow logarithmically, quantified by the doubling time. What range of doubling times and detection threshold does the chapter cite?

options.A: Doubling times vary between 2 and 200 days, and tumors are generally not detectable until ~1 cm in diameter
options.B: Doubling times are fixed at 30 days for all tumors, detectable at 1 mm
options.C: Doubling times vary between 2 and 200 hours, detectable at 10 cm
options.D: Tumors are detectable only once they exceed 5 cm regardless of doubling time

exp: Tumor cells lose normal growth restraint and proliferate logarithmically, with doubling times that vary widely — between 2 and 200 days — so a tumor may be present for years before becoming palpable; tumors are generally not detectable until they reach ~1 cm in diameter. Understanding this affords time to evaluate patients thoroughly before rushing therapy. The other options misstate the range, units, or detection threshold.
hy: Doubling time 2–200 days; tumors detectable at ~1 cm.
src: Neligan I.14 · Cancer diagnosis and treatment planning

why.A: Correct — 2–200-day doubling time and ~1 cm detection threshold.
why.B: Wrong — doubling time is not fixed at 30 days, nor 1 mm detection.
why.C: Wrong — the units are days, not hours, and detection is ~1 cm.
why.D: Wrong — tumors are detectable around 1 cm, not only above 5 cm.
