stem: A surgeon removes an entire tumor mass with a small margin of surrounding normal tissue, often with curative intent. Which biopsy term describes this?

options.A: Excisional biopsy (wide local incision)
options.B: Incisional biopsy
options.C: Core-needle biopsy (CNB)
options.D: Fine-needle aspiration (FNA)

exp: An excisional biopsy (wide local incision) removes the entire tumor mass with a small margin of normal tissue and is often performed with curative intent (no further surgery needed for characteristic lesions). An incisional biopsy removes only a portion, a core-needle biopsy obtains a tissue core, and FNA samples only cells.
hy: Excisional biopsy = whole mass + small margin (often curative intent).
src: Neligan I.14 · Cancer presentation and initial evaluation

why.A: Correct — whole-mass removal with a small margin is excisional.
why.B: Wrong — an incisional biopsy removes only part of the tumor.
why.C: Wrong — CNB obtains a core, not the entire mass.
why.D: Wrong — FNA samples only cells.

stem: Which statement about fine-needle aspiration (FNA) versus core-needle biopsy (CNB) is correct?

options.A: FNA obtains only a sample of cells and a negative result may not rule out malignancy, whereas CNB obtains less tissue than open biopsy but often suffices for definitive surgical planning
options.B: FNA reliably excludes malignancy when negative
options.C: CNB obtains more tissue than an excisional biopsy
options.D: FNA always provides full architectural/histologic information

exp: FNA is minimally invasive and obtains only a sample of cells, so a positive result can guide treatment but a negative result may not rule out malignancy; CNB obtains considerably less tissue than excisional/incisional biopsy yet often provides enough for definitive surgical planning. Option B overstates FNA's negative predictive value, C reverses the tissue yield, and D wrongly credits FNA with architectural detail.
hy: FNA = cells only (negative doesn't exclude cancer); CNB = core, often planning-sufficient.
src: Neligan I.14 · Cancer presentation and initial evaluation

why.A: Correct — FNA cells-only with limited negative value; CNB often sufficient.
why.B: Wrong — a negative FNA does not reliably exclude malignancy.
why.C: Wrong — CNB obtains less, not more, tissue than excisional biopsy.
why.D: Wrong — FNA gives cytology, not full architecture.

stem: The chapter notes that even with "negative" margins, recurrences occur. Which finding illustrates the body's capacity to destroy small numbers of residual tumor cells?

options.A: Basal cell cancers with positive margins recur in only ~20–30% of cases
options.B: Basal cell cancers with positive margins recur in ~100% of cases
options.C: Negative-margin rectal cancers never recur
options.D: All tumors with positive margins recur within one year

exp: Review of basal cell cancers with positive margins shows recurrence in only ~20–30% of cases, which the chapter cites as evidence of the body's capacity to destroy small numbers of residual cancer cells (and a rationale for the immune system's role and for not always re-excising). The other options overstate recurrence or wrongly claim negative margins guarantee cure.
hy: BCC positive-margin recurrence only ~20–30% → body clears some residual cells.

why.A: Correct — ~20–30% recurrence despite positive margins.
why.B: Wrong — recurrence is far below 100%.
why.C: Wrong — negative-margin tumors can still recur.
why.D: Wrong — not all positive-margin tumors recur.

stem: In rectal cancers resected with negative margins, where do locally recurrent tumors predominantly arise, illustrating undetected microscopic deposits?

options.A: ~50% occur at the suture line despite negative histology
options.B: ~5% occur, all at distant sites
options.C: None recur once margins are negative
options.D: Recurrences occur only in irradiated tissue

exp: In rectal cancers resected with negative margins, evaluation of locally recurrent disease shows ~50% occur at the suture line despite negative histology — attributable to microscopic tumor deposits not detected pathologically, which justifies adjuvant therapy. The other options deny recurrence or misstate its frequency/location.
hy: Rectal cancer: ~50% of local recurrences at the suture line despite negative margins.

why.A: Correct — ~50% suture-line recurrence despite negative histology.
why.B: Wrong — recurrence is not a uniformly distant ~5%.
why.C: Wrong — negative margins do not eliminate recurrence.
why.D: Wrong — recurrence is not limited to irradiated tissue.

stem: In the TNM staging system, what do T, N, and M represent?

options.A: T = extent of the primary tumor (size/depth/anatomic involvement); N = lymph node involvement; M = distant metastasis
options.B: T = tumor grade; N = necrosis; M = mitotic rate
options.C: T = treatment; N = number of cycles; M = modality
options.D: T = thickness only; N = nuclear grade; M = margin status

exp: In the TNM system, T denotes the extent of the primary tumor (size, depth of invasion, or degree of anatomic involvement), N enumerates lymph-node involvement (number/size of affected nodes), and M reflects distant-organ metastasis; TNM combinations aggregate into stage groups I–IV. The other options misdefine the letters.
hy: TNM: T = primary tumor extent, N = nodal involvement, M = distant metastasis.

why.A: Correct — the standard TNM definitions.
why.B: Wrong — grade/necrosis/mitoses are not the TNM letters.
why.C: Wrong — treatment/cycles/modality are unrelated to TNM.
why.D: Wrong — TNM is broader than thickness/nuclear grade/margin.

stem: Which classification system is correctly matched to its tumor and basis?

options.A: Lund's classification stratifies basal cell carcinomas by descriptive features (nodular/ulcerative/morphea/sclerosing), with morphea or sclerosing features predicting a more aggressive course
options.B: Lund's classification grades melanoma by Breslow thickness
options.C: Lund's classification stages breast cancer by hormone-receptor status
options.D: Lund's classification categorizes salivary tumors by cell type

exp: Lund's classification stratifies basal cell carcinomas by descriptive morphology — nodular (well-defined margins), ulcerative, morphea, and sclerosing (indistinct margins) — with morphea or sclerosing features predicting a more aggressive, problematic course. It is not a melanoma-thickness, breast-receptor, or salivary cell-type system.
hy: Lund = BCC morphology (morphea/sclerosing = aggressive).

why.A: Correct — Lund stratifies BCC by morphology; sclerosing/morphea aggressive.
why.B: Wrong — Breslow thickness grades melanoma, not Lund.
why.C: Wrong — hormone receptors stage breast cancer, not Lund.
why.D: Wrong — salivary cell-type grading is a different system.

stem: How do Clark's and Breslow's systems for melanoma differ?

options.A: Clark's system categorizes by anatomic level of invasion (papillary/reticular dermis, subcutaneous fat) and was subjective; Breslow's more objectively quantitates tumor thickness with an ocular micrometer
options.B: Clark's measures thickness in millimeters; Breslow's grades by level of invasion
options.C: Both measure mitotic rate per high-power field
options.D: Both classify by hormone-receptor expression

exp: Clark's system categorizes melanoma by the anatomic level of invasion (into papillary dermis, reticular dermis, subcutaneous fat) but proved subjective with poor inter-rater reliability; Breslow's system more objectively quantitates tumor thickness using a micrometer attached to the microscope. Option B reverses them, and C/D misattribute mitotic-rate or receptor bases.
hy: Clark = level of invasion (subjective); Breslow = objective thickness.

why.A: Correct — Clark levels (invasion) vs Breslow thickness (measured).
why.B: Wrong — the thickness/level attributions are reversed.
why.C: Wrong — neither is based on mitotic rate per HPF.
why.D: Wrong — neither uses hormone-receptor expression.

stem: The clinically aggressive "triple-negative" breast tumor is defined by the absence of which three receptors?

options.A: Estrogen receptor (ER), progesterone receptor (PR), and HER2
options.B: EGFR, VEGF, and CD20
options.C: CTLA-4, PD-1, and PD-L1
options.D: BRAF, KIT, and ALK

exp: Triple-negative breast tumors lack the estrogen receptor (ER), progesterone receptor (PR), and HER2, and carry a reproducible, highly specific molecular signature associated with clinically aggressive behavior. The other receptor triads belong to other targeted/immunotherapy or driver-mutation contexts, not the triple-negative definition.
hy: Triple-negative breast = ER−/PR−/HER2−.

why.A: Correct — absence of ER, PR, and HER2.
why.B: Wrong — EGFR/VEGF/CD20 are therapeutic targets, not the definition.
why.C: Wrong — CTLA-4/PD-1/PD-L1 are immune checkpoints.
why.D: Wrong — BRAF/KIT/ALK are driver mutations, not this definition.

stem: Cancer treatments are divided into local and systemic. Which grouping is correct?

options.A: Local: surgery, radiation therapy, ablative strategies (e.g. cryoablation); Systemic: chemotherapy, targeted therapy, immunotherapy, systemic radionuclide therapy
options.B: Local: chemotherapy and immunotherapy; Systemic: surgery and cryoablation
options.C: Local: targeted therapy only; Systemic: radiation only
options.D: There is no meaningful distinction between local and systemic treatment

exp: Local treatments act on the tumor and its immediate region — surgery, radiation therapy, and other ablative strategies (e.g. cryoablation) — whereas systemic treatments circulate throughout the body: chemotherapy, targeted therapies, immunotherapy, and systemically administered (radionuclide) radiation therapy. The other options scramble these categories.
hy: Local = surgery/RT/ablation; Systemic = chemo/targeted/immuno/radionuclide.

why.A: Correct — the local vs systemic grouping.
why.B: Wrong — chemo/immunotherapy are systemic, surgery is local.
why.C: Wrong — both targeted therapy and radiation are mischaracterized.
why.D: Wrong — the local/systemic distinction is fundamental.

stem: What is the defining timing of neoadjuvant therapy, and one of its main goals?

options.A: Chemotherapy and/or radiation given before tumor excision, used to shrink unresectable tumors or reduce the magnitude of ablation needed for negative margins
options.B: Therapy given only after complete surgical excision to treat micrometastases
options.C: Therapy given during the operation itself
options.D: Therapy reserved exclusively for palliation of metastatic disease

exp: Neoadjuvant therapy is chemotherapy and/or radiation given before tumor excision, used to shrink unresectable tumors to render them resectable or to decrease the magnitude of ablation needed to achieve negative margins (and to downstage). Therapy given only after excision is adjuvant (B), intraoperative therapy (C) and palliation-only (D) are different concepts.
hy: Neoadjuvant = before surgery (shrink/downstage → resectable, smaller ablation).

why.A: Correct — preoperative therapy to shrink/downstage tumors.
why.B: Wrong — post-excision therapy is adjuvant, not neoadjuvant.
why.C: Wrong — neoadjuvant is before, not during, the operation.
why.D: Wrong — it is not reserved for palliation.

stem: When no tumor is identified on the surgical specimen after neoadjuvant chemotherapy, this is termed what?

options.A: Pathologic complete response
options.B: R0 resection
options.C: Sublethal damage repair
options.D: Kinetic resistance

exp: When no tumor is identified on the surgical specimen following completion of neoadjuvant chemotherapy, it is termed a pathologic complete response — a recognition that has prompted study of whether ablative surgery can be obviated in that setting. R0 resection refers to clear margins at surgery, sublethal damage is a radiation concept, and kinetic resistance is a chemotherapy cell-cycle concept.
hy: No residual tumor on specimen after neoadjuvant chemo = pathologic complete response.

why.A: Correct — pathologic complete response = no tumor on the specimen.
why.B: Wrong — R0 describes clear resection margins, not a chemo response.
why.C: Wrong — sublethal damage is a radiation-repair concept.
why.D: Wrong — kinetic resistance relates to cell-cycle-specific chemo.

stem: Mehrara et al.'s review of microvascular breast reconstructions identified neoadjuvant chemotherapy as a risk factor for wound complications. What timing threshold did they highlight?

options.A: Increased wound complications if surgery was performed less than 6 weeks after completing chemotherapy
options.B: Increased complications only if surgery was delayed beyond 1 year
options.C: No relationship between chemotherapy timing and complications
options.D: Complications occurred only if surgery was within 24 hours of chemotherapy

exp: In a review of nearly 1200 microsurgical breast reconstructions, Mehrara et al. found neoadjuvant chemotherapy was an independent risk factor for postoperative complications, with increased wound complications if surgery was performed less than 6 weeks after completing chemotherapy. The other options deny the relationship or misstate the timing.
hy: Wait ≥6 weeks after neoadjuvant chemo before microvascular breast reconstruction (Mehrara).

why.A: Correct — increased wound complications if <6 weeks post-chemo.
why.B: Wrong — the risk relates to a short, not long, interval.
why.C: Wrong — there was a clear timing relationship.
why.D: Wrong — the threshold is 6 weeks, not 24 hours.

stem: For T3 and T4 rectal tumors, what did randomized trials show about the timing of chemoradiation relative to surgery?

options.A: Lower recurrence rates when chemoradiation is delivered preoperatively (neoadjuvant) rather than postoperatively
options.B: Lower recurrence when chemoradiation is given only postoperatively
options.C: No benefit to chemoradiation in any sequence
options.D: Chemoradiation is contraindicated for rectal tumors

exp: Randomized trials demonstrate lower recurrence rates for T3 and T4 rectal tumors when chemoradiation is delivered preoperatively (neoadjuvant) rather than postoperatively; for distal tumors near the anus, neoadjuvant therapy can also convert abdominoperineal resections to sphincter-preserving low anterior resections. The other options reverse or deny this benefit.
hy: T3/T4 rectal: preoperative chemoradiation → lower recurrence than postoperative.

why.A: Correct — preoperative chemoradiation lowers recurrence in T3/T4 rectal cancer.
why.B: Wrong — postoperative-only is inferior here.
why.C: Wrong — chemoradiation does benefit these tumors.
why.D: Wrong — it is a standard part of treatment, not contraindicated.

stem: The Halsted radical mastectomy exemplifies which historical surgical philosophy, now largely superseded?

options.A: The "bigger is better" approach (removal of pectoralis major, all breast tissue, and axillary nodes), since shown unnecessary for many cancers
options.B: Minimal lumpectomy with sentinel node biopsy
options.C: Radiation monotherapy without surgery
options.D: Neoadjuvant immunotherapy followed by observation

exp: The Halsted radical mastectomy (removal of the pectoralis major muscle, all breast tissue, and axillary nodes) epitomizes the early-twentieth-century "bigger is better" philosophy that wider anatomic excision gives the best chance of cure — an approach since shown unnecessary for many cancers as limited resections (often with multimodality therapy) proved equivalent. The other options describe modern minimal/non-surgical approaches.
hy: Halsted radical mastectomy = "bigger is better" era (now largely superseded).

why.A: Correct — the radical "bigger is better" historical approach.
why.B: Wrong — lumpectomy/sentinel node is the modern minimal approach.
why.C: Wrong — Halsted was a radical surgical, not radiation-only, approach.
why.D: Wrong — it predates neoadjuvant immunotherapy entirely.

stem: In cutaneous melanoma, what did studies show about wide excision margins greater than 2 cm?

options.A: Margins greater than 2 cm showed no benefit
options.B: Margins greater than 2 cm halved recurrence
options.C: Margins must always exceed 5 cm
options.D: Margin width has no studied relationship to melanoma outcome

exp: Studies showed that excision margins greater than 2 cm provide no additional benefit in the treatment of cutaneous melanoma, supporting the move away from ultra-radical excision toward narrower, evidence-based margins. The other options overstate the benefit of wide margins or deny the studied relationship.
hy: Cutaneous melanoma: margins >2 cm confer no benefit.

why.A: Correct — >2 cm margins showed no benefit.
why.B: Wrong — wider margins did not improve recurrence.
why.C: Wrong — margins need not exceed 5 cm.
why.D: Wrong — the margin–outcome relationship has been studied.

stem: The "R" classification of resection margins is defined how?

options.A: R0 = no gross or microscopic tumor at any margin; R1 = positive microscopic margin; R2 = positive macroscopic (gross) margin
options.B: R0 = gross residual tumor; R1 = no residual tumor; R2 = microscopic residual
options.C: R0, R1, R2 refer to the number of radiation fractions delivered
options.D: R0 = stage I; R1 = stage II; R2 = stage III

exp: Resection completeness is graded as R0 (no gross or microscopic tumor at any margin), R1 (positive microscopic margin), and R2 (positive macroscopic/gross margin). The other options invert the definitions or wrongly tie "R" to radiation fractions or stage.
hy: R0 = clear; R1 = microscopic positive; R2 = gross positive margin.

why.A: Correct — R0/R1/R2 margin definitions.
why.B: Wrong — the definitions are inverted.
why.C: Wrong — "R" denotes residual tumor, not radiation fractions.
why.D: Wrong — "R" is not the stage grouping.

stem: Surgical "metastectomy" can be curative in selected patients. Which cited 5-year survival figures are correct?

options.A: Pulmonary resection for extremity sarcoma metastases up to ~25%; hepatic lobectomy for colorectal metastasis ~25–40% in selected patients
options.B: Both procedures yield ~90% 5-year survival universally
options.C: Neither offers any survival benefit
options.D: Pulmonary metastectomy yields ~80%; hepatic resection ~0%

exp: Surgical metastectomy can be curative in selected patients with limited metastases: pulmonary resection for extremity-sarcoma metastases yields 5-year survival up to ~25%, and hepatic lobectomy for colorectal metastasis ~25–40% in selected patients. The other options grossly over- or under-state these figures.
hy: Metastectomy 5-yr survival: sarcoma lung ~25%; colorectal liver ~25–40%.

why.A: Correct — ~25% (pulmonary sarcoma) and ~25–40% (hepatic colorectal).
why.B: Wrong — survival is not ~90% universally.
why.C: Wrong — metastectomy does offer a survival benefit in selected cases.
why.D: Wrong — hepatic resection survival is ~25–40%, not 0%.

stem: What is the SI unit of absorbed radiation dose, and its definition?

options.A: The Gray (Gy), where 1 Gy = 1 J/kg of tissue
options.B: The roentgen, defined as 1 J/kg
options.C: The Sievert, defined as electrons per atom
options.D: The curie, defined as energy per roentgen

exp: The SI unit of absorbed radiation dose is the Gray (Gy), defined as 1 joule of energy absorbed per kilogram of tissue (1 Gy = 1 J/kg) — the clinically relevant measure of dose delivered to the tumor. The roentgen measures exposure (ionization in air), not absorbed dose; the Sievert and curie are different quantities.
hy: Gray (Gy) = 1 J/kg (absorbed dose).

why.A: Correct — the Gray, 1 J/kg.
why.B: Wrong — the roentgen measures exposure, not absorbed dose as J/kg.
why.C: Wrong — the Sievert is equivalent dose, not "electrons per atom."
why.D: Wrong — the curie measures radioactivity, not absorbed dose.

stem: What is "exposure" in radiation terminology, and how is it measured?

options.A: The quantity of ionization produced in air by a beam of radiation, measured in roentgens
options.B: The energy absorbed per unit mass of tumor, measured in Grays
options.C: The number of fractions delivered, measured in Sieverts
options.D: The half-life of the radioactive source, measured in curies

exp: "Exposure" is the quantity of ionization produced in air by a beam of radiation and is measured in roentgens; the more clinically relevant quantity is the absorbed dose (energy per unit mass, in Grays). The other options conflate exposure with absorbed dose, fraction number, or source half-life.
hy: Exposure = ionization in air (roentgens); absorbed dose = Gray.

why.A: Correct — exposure is air ionization measured in roentgens.
why.B: Wrong — energy absorbed per mass (Gray) is absorbed dose, not exposure.
why.C: Wrong — fractions are not measured in Sieverts.
why.D: Wrong — half-life (curies relate to activity) is unrelated to exposure.

stem: The principle of "sublethal damage" underlies fractionated radiation. What is the rationale and typical daily fraction size?

options.A: Normal cells repair sublethal (single-strand) DNA damage between fractions better than tumor cells; daily fractions typically range from 1.8 to 2.5 Gy
options.B: Tumor cells repair damage better than normal cells, so single large doses are preferred
options.C: Fractions are typically 20–25 Gy per day
options.D: Fractionation has no biological rationale and is purely logistical

exp: Fractionated radiation exploits sublethal damage: cells readily repair single-strand DNA breaks, and normal cells repair this sublethal damage between fractions better than tumor cells, so dividing the dose spares normal tissue while accumulating lethal (double-strand) damage in tumor cells; daily fractions typically range from 1.8 to 2.5 Gy. The other options invert the repair advantage, misstate the dose, or deny the rationale.
hy: Fractionation: normal cells repair sublethal damage better; ~1.8–2.5 Gy/fraction.

why.A: Correct — normal-cell sublethal-damage repair; 1.8–2.5 Gy fractions.
why.B: Wrong — normal cells, not tumor cells, repair better.
why.C: Wrong — fractions are ~1.8–2.5 Gy, not 20–25 Gy.
why.D: Wrong — fractionation has a clear radiobiologic rationale.

stem: Why is ionizing radiation most effective in oxygen-rich environments and least effective in the center of necrotic tumor?

options.A: Oxygen increases the half-life of reactive free-radical species that damage DNA, so well-oxygenated (peripheral) cells are more readily killed than the hypoxic avascular core
options.B: Oxygen directly shields DNA from radiation
options.C: Hypoxic cells have no DNA to damage
options.D: Radiation only works in the complete absence of oxygen

exp: Ionizing radiation kills indirectly by generating free radicals from water, and oxygen increases the half-life of these reactive radical species — so radiation is most effective in well-oxygenated (peripheral) tumor and least effective in the hypoxic, avascular necrotic center. The other options misstate oxygen's role.
hy: Oxygen prolongs free-radical half-life → radiation best in oxygen-rich tissue, poor in hypoxic core.

why.A: Correct — oxygen stabilizes damaging free radicals.
why.B: Wrong — oxygen does not physically shield DNA.
why.C: Wrong — hypoxic cells have DNA but are radioresistant.
why.D: Wrong — radiation works better with, not without, oxygen.

stem: Which radiation-delivery modality is correctly described?

options.A: Brachytherapy places radioactive sources directly into or adjacent to tumor tissue, allowing high local dose while sparing surrounding/external tissue; external beam (teletherapy) delivers radiation from a distance
options.B: Brachytherapy delivers radiation from a distant external source
options.C: Teletherapy implants radioactive seeds within the tumor
options.D: IMRT is a form of brachytherapy using blue dye

exp: Brachytherapy places radioactive sources directly into or adjacent to tumor tissue, delivering a high local dose while sparing surrounding and external tissue (and can sometimes be used even after prior external beam radiotherapy); external beam radiotherapy (teletherapy) delivers radiation from a distance. The other options reverse these definitions or miscategorize IMRT.
hy: Brachytherapy = sources in/adjacent to tumor; teletherapy/EBRT = from a distance.

why.A: Correct — brachytherapy is close-source; teletherapy is distant.
why.B: Wrong — brachytherapy is not distant external delivery.
why.C: Wrong — teletherapy does not implant seeds.
why.D: Wrong — IMRT is external beam, not blue-dye brachytherapy.

stem: In proton beam therapy, what does the "Bragg peak" refer to?

options.A: The precise depth at which a proton beam delivers its maximum radiation dose, allowing targeting of tumors at a specific depth with limited exit dose
options.B: The peak serum drug level after chemotherapy
options.C: The maximum tolerated radiation exposure in roentgens
options.D: The point of maximal oxygenation in a tumor

exp: The Bragg peak is the precise depth at which a proton beam deposits its maximum energy/dose; because protons release most energy at this defined depth with little exit dose, proton therapy can target tumors at a specific depth while sparing tissue beyond. The other options misdefine it as a drug level, exposure limit, or oxygenation point.
hy: Bragg peak = depth of maximal proton dose (sharp fall-off beyond).

why.A: Correct — the depth of peak proton dose with limited exit dose.
why.B: Wrong — it is not a pharmacokinetic peak.
why.C: Wrong — it is not a roentgen exposure limit.
why.D: Wrong — it is a dose-deposition, not oxygenation, concept.

stem: Electron beam radiation has which characteristic use, owing to its tissue-penetrance profile?

options.A: Low tissue penetrance relative to photons/protons, so it is mainly used to treat superficial (cutaneous) cancers
options.B: Very deep penetrance, ideal for central abdominal tumors
options.C: No tissue penetrance, used only for imaging
options.D: Penetrance identical to proton beams, used for deep tumors

exp: Electron beams have low tissue penetrance relative to photons and protons, so they are mainly used to treat superficial (cutaneous) cancers, depositing dose near the surface. The other options overstate or deny their penetrance.
hy: Electron beam = low penetrance → superficial/cutaneous tumors.

why.A: Correct — low penetrance suits superficial cancers.
why.B: Wrong — electrons do not penetrate deeply.
why.C: Wrong — electron beams do deliver therapeutic dose superficially.
why.D: Wrong — their penetrance is far less than protons.

stem: Intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) is best described as which of the following?

options.A: A form of external beam radiotherapy that uses 3D CT and computer-modulated beam intensity to conform dose to 3D tumor shapes, sparing surrounding tissue (and reducing cardiac/pulmonary toxicity in left-sided breast cancer)
options.B: A systemic radionuclide infused intravenously
options.C: Implantation of radioactive seeds into the prostate
options.D: A photosensitizer activated by visible light

exp: Intensity-modulated radiation therapy is a form of external beam radiotherapy that combines 3D CT imaging with computer-modulated beam intensity to conform the dose to three-dimensional tumor shapes, minimizing injury to surrounding regions; it is favored for prostate, lung, head and neck, and brain lesions and can reduce cardiac/pulmonary toxicity in left-sided breast cancer. It is not a systemic radionuclide, seed implantation, or photodynamic therapy.
hy: IMRT = computer-modulated EBRT conforming dose to 3D tumor shape.

why.A: Correct — modulated-intensity conformal external beam therapy.
why.B: Wrong — IMRT is external beam, not a systemic radionuclide.
why.C: Wrong — seed implantation is brachytherapy, not IMRT.
why.D: Wrong — light-activated therapy is photodynamic therapy.

stem: How does photodynamic therapy achieve tumor cell killing, and what limits its use?

options.A: Photosensitizers preferentially taken up by cancer cells produce free radicals when exposed to light, causing thermal damage; because it requires light, its utility is limited to superficial tumors
options.B: It uses systemic radionuclides effective at any depth
options.C: It works by implanting radioactive sources, useful for deep tumors
options.D: It relies on antibody-mediated complement fixation

exp: In photodynamic therapy, photosensitizers preferentially taken up by cancer cells produce free radicals when exposed to light, and the accumulated energy causes thermal damage and cell killing; because it requires light to be activated, its utility is limited to superficial tumors (e.g. advanced esophageal and non-small-cell lung tumors via endoscopic light). The other options describe radionuclide, brachytherapy, or antibody mechanisms.
hy: PDT = light-activated photosensitizer → free radicals; superficial tumors only.

why.A: Correct — light-activated photosensitizer producing free radicals, superficial use.
why.B: Wrong — PDT is not a depth-independent systemic radionuclide.
why.C: Wrong — implanting radioactive sources is brachytherapy.
why.D: Wrong — complement fixation is an antibody mechanism, not PDT.

stem: Conventional cytotoxic chemotherapy exploits which fundamental difference between normal and cancer cells, and what does the log-kill concept imply?

options.A: Normal cells repair DNA damage and survive better than cancer cells; because an agent killing 99.99% of 10⁹ cells (≈a 1-cm mass) still leaves ~10⁵ cells, chemotherapy is given in several rounds
options.B: Cancer cells repair DNA better, so a single dose eradicates the tumor
options.C: Chemotherapy kills 100% of cells in one administration
options.D: Cytotoxic agents act only on normal cells, sparing the tumor

exp: Cytotoxic chemotherapy works because normal cells repair DNA damage and survive better than cancer cells; the log-kill model means an agent killing 99.99% of 10⁹ cells (≈the number in a 1-cm mass) still leaves ~10⁵ cells, so chemotherapy must be given in several rounds for maximal cell killing. The other options invert the repair advantage or claim single-dose eradication.
hy: Log-kill: 99.99% of 10⁹ still leaves ~10⁵ → repeated cycles needed.

why.A: Correct — fractional log-kill mandates multiple rounds.
why.B: Wrong — normal cells, not cancer cells, repair DNA better.
why.C: Wrong — a single dose does not kill 100% of cells.
why.D: Wrong — cytotoxic agents target dividing tumor cells, not only normal cells.

stem: Per Table 14.1, cytotoxic chemotherapy agents are grouped into four mechanistic categories. Which set is correct?

options.A: DNA-interactive (alkylating) agents; antitumor antibiotics and topoisomerase poisons; antimetabolites; mitotic spindle inhibitors
options.B: Protein kinase inhibitors; mTOR inhibitors; proteasome inhibitors; HDAC inhibitors
options.C: Checkpoint inhibitors; CAR-T cells; vaccines; cytokines
options.D: Hormonal agents; radiosensitizers; photosensitizers; radionuclides

exp: Per Table 14.1, cytotoxic agents fall into four mechanistic categories: DNA-interactive (alkylating) agents; antitumor antibiotics and topoisomerase poisons; antimetabolites; and mitotic spindle (antimitotic) inhibitors. The other lists are targeted-therapy classes, immunotherapy modalities, or adjunctive agents — not the cytotoxic chemotherapy categories.
hy: Chemo categories: alkylating; antitumor abx/topoisomerase; antimetabolites; antimitotics.

why.A: Correct — the four Table 14.1 cytotoxic categories.
why.B: Wrong — those are targeted-agent classes (Table 14.2).
why.C: Wrong — checkpoint inhibitors/CAR-T/vaccines are immunotherapy.
why.D: Wrong — hormonal/radiosensitizer/radionuclide are different modalities.

stem: Which agent is correctly matched to its Table 14.1 chemotherapy category?

options.A: Vincristine and paclitaxel are antimitotic (mitotic spindle) agents, whereas methotrexate and 5-fluorouracil are antimetabolites
options.B: Cyclophosphamide is an antimetabolite; methotrexate is an alkylating agent
options.C: Bleomycin and doxorubicin are mitotic spindle inhibitors
options.D: Cisplatin is an antimitotic agent; vinblastine is an alkylating agent

exp: Per Table 14.1, vincristine and paclitaxel are antimitotic (mitotic-spindle) agents, while methotrexate and 5-fluorouracil are antimetabolites. The distractors miscategorize cyclophosphamide/methotrexate (alkylating vs antimetabolite), bleomycin/doxorubicin (antitumor antibiotics, not spindle inhibitors), and cisplatin/vinblastine (platinum/DNA-interactive vs antimitotic).
hy: Vincristine/paclitaxel = antimitotic; methotrexate/5-FU = antimetabolite.

why.A: Correct — antimitotic vincristine/paclitaxel vs antimetabolite MTX/5-FU.
why.B: Wrong — cyclophosphamide is alkylating; methotrexate is an antimetabolite.
why.C: Wrong — bleomycin/doxorubicin are antitumor antibiotics, not spindle agents.
why.D: Wrong — cisplatin is a platinum (DNA-interactive) agent; vinblastine is antimitotic.

stem: Regional chemotherapy administration increases local drug dose while reducing systemic toxicity. Which examples does the chapter give?

options.A: Isolated limb perfusion for melanoma in-transit metastases, and hepatic artery infusion pumps for colorectal liver metastasis
options.B: Oral capecitabine and subcutaneous insulin
options.C: Whole-brain radiation and total body irradiation
options.D: Inhaled bleomycin and topical 5-FU

exp: Regional administration delivers higher local drug doses with reduced systemic toxicity; the chapter's examples are isolated limb perfusion for melanoma in-transit metastases and hepatic artery infusion pumps for colorectal liver metastasis. The other options describe systemic/oral routes, radiation, or topical agents, not regional chemotherapy.
hy: Regional chemo: isolated limb perfusion (melanoma in-transit); hepatic artery infusion (colorectal liver mets).

why.A: Correct — the two cited regional-chemotherapy examples.
why.B: Wrong — capecitabine/insulin are systemic/oral, not regional chemo.
why.C: Wrong — whole-brain/total-body irradiation is radiation, not chemo.
why.D: Wrong — inhaled/topical agents are not these regional examples.

stem: For which settings is single-modality chemotherapy notably effective, per the chapter?

options.A: Hematologic malignancies (leukemia, lymphoma) and specific solid tumors such as subtypes of testicular cancer and the Nigro protocol (chemoradiation) for anal cancer
options.B: As curative monotherapy for the majority of solid tumors
options.C: Only for palliation, never for cure in any setting
options.D: Exclusively for basal cell carcinoma

exp: Single-modality chemotherapy is notably effective for hematologic malignancies (leukemia, lymphoma) and certain solid tumors — subtypes of testicular cancer and combined chemoradiotherapy such as the Nigro protocol for anal cancer — but generally has not borne out as curative single-modality treatment for most solid tumors. The other options overstate or understate its role.
hy: Chemo curative-ish: heme malignancies, testicular subtypes, Nigro protocol (anal).

why.A: Correct — heme cancers, testicular subtypes, and the Nigro protocol.
why.B: Wrong — it is not curative monotherapy for most solid tumors.
why.C: Wrong — it can be curative in the cited settings, not palliative-only.
why.D: Wrong — its single-modality efficacy is not limited to BCC.

stem: The introduction of neoadjuvant chemotherapy transformed the management of extremity osteosarcoma in what way?

options.A: It increased rates of limb preservation (vs prior amputation) with equivalent long-term survival
options.B: It made amputation mandatory in all cases
options.C: It eliminated any role for surgery
options.D: It had no effect on limb salvage rates

exp: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy transformed extremity osteosarcoma management: patients previously treated with amputation can now often undergo limb preservation with equivalent long-term survival (combined with functional reconstruction using allografts or endoprostheses). The other options claim mandatory amputation, no surgery, or no effect.
hy: Neoadjuvant chemo → limb salvage in osteosarcoma with equivalent survival.

why.A: Correct — limb preservation with equivalent survival.
why.B: Wrong — it enabled salvage, not mandatory amputation.
why.C: Wrong — surgery remains part of treatment.
why.D: Wrong — it markedly increased limb-salvage rates.

stem: How do targeted agents fundamentally differ from conventional cytotoxic chemotherapy?

options.A: They regulate distinct molecular targets crucial to tumor mechanisms rather than globally causing macromolecular lesions, combining efficacy with decreased toxicity
options.B: They cause more widespread DNA damage than cytotoxic agents
options.C: They act only by stimulating the immune system
options.D: They are identical in mechanism to alkylating agents

exp: Targeted agents regulate distinct molecular targets crucial to tumor proliferation/survival rather than globally causing macromolecular (DNA) lesions as cytotoxic chemotherapy does, theoretically combining efficacy with decreased side effects and toxicity. The other options wrongly attribute broader DNA damage, pure immune stimulation, or an alkylating mechanism.
hy: Targeted agents = specific molecular targets (less global toxicity), unlike cytotoxic chemo.

why.A: Correct — specific molecular targeting with decreased toxicity.
why.B: Wrong — they cause less, not more, global macromolecular damage.
why.C: Wrong — they are not purely immune stimulants.
why.D: Wrong — they are not alkylating agents.

stem: Imatinib (Gleevec), an early rationally designed targeted biologic, was developed to block which abnormal gene product?

options.A: The constitutively active Bcr-Abl tyrosine kinase of the Philadelphia chromosome
options.B: The HER2/neu receptor overexpressed in breast cancer
options.C: The PD-1 checkpoint receptor on T cells
options.D: The VEGF ligand driving angiogenesis

exp: Imatinib (Gleevec), an early rationally designed biologic, was developed to block the constitutively active Bcr-Abl tyrosine kinase produced by the Philadelphia chromosome (its clinical success spurred targeting of other oncogenes). The other targets correspond to trastuzumab (HER2), checkpoint inhibitors (PD-1), and bevacizumab (VEGF).
hy: Imatinib → Bcr-Abl tyrosine kinase (Philadelphia chromosome).

why.A: Correct — imatinib blocks Bcr-Abl (Philadelphia chromosome).
why.B: Wrong — HER2 is the trastuzumab target.
why.C: Wrong — PD-1 is targeted by checkpoint inhibitors.
why.D: Wrong — VEGF is the bevacizumab target.

stem: Trastuzumab is a monoclonal antibody against which target, and in what fraction of breast cancers is that target overexpressed?

options.A: The HER2/neu receptor, overexpressed in ~25% of breast cancers
options.B: VEGF, overexpressed in ~90% of breast cancers
options.C: CD20, overexpressed in ~50% of breast cancers
options.D: EGFR, overexpressed in ~5% of breast cancers

exp: Trastuzumab is a monoclonal antibody that binds the extracellular segment of the HER2/neu receptor, which is overexpressed in approximately 25% of breast cancers; it (with pertuzumab) is commonly used in neoadjuvant regimens. The other options misstate the target or its prevalence.
hy: Trastuzumab → HER2/neu (overexpressed in ~25% of breast cancers).

why.A: Correct — HER2/neu, ~25% of breast cancers.
why.B: Wrong — VEGF is bevacizumab's target, and ~90% is wrong.
why.C: Wrong — CD20 is rituximab's target.
why.D: Wrong — EGFR is cetuximab/panitumumab's target.

stem: Per Table 14.3, which immunotherapy agent is correctly matched to its molecular target?

options.A: Ipilimumab targets CTLA-4, while pembrolizumab and nivolumab target PD-1
options.B: Rituximab targets HER2/neu; trastuzumab targets CD20
options.C: Bevacizumab targets EGFR; cetuximab targets VEGF
options.D: Ipilimumab targets PD-1; nivolumab targets CTLA-4

exp: Per Table 14.3, ipilimumab targets CTLA-4, while pembrolizumab and nivolumab target PD-1 (atezolizumab targets the PD-1/PD-L1 axis). The distractors swap rituximab/trastuzumab (CD20 vs HER2), bevacizumab/cetuximab (VEGF vs EGFR), or invert ipilimumab/nivolumab targets.
hy: Ipilimumab = CTLA-4; pembrolizumab/nivolumab = PD-1.

why.A: Correct — CTLA-4 (ipilimumab) vs PD-1 (pembrolizumab/nivolumab).
why.B: Wrong — rituximab targets CD20 and trastuzumab HER2 (not reversed).
why.C: Wrong — bevacizumab targets VEGF and cetuximab EGFR (not reversed).
why.D: Wrong — ipilimumab is CTLA-4 and nivolumab is PD-1, not the reverse.

stem: The best-known nonspecific active immunostimulant, an attenuated bovine tuberculosis bacillus with proven benefit in superficial bladder cancer, is which agent?

options.A: Bacillus Calmette–Guérin (BCG)
options.B: Interleukin-2
options.C: Ipilimumab
options.D: Sipuleucel-T

exp: Bacillus Calmette–Guérin (BCG), an attenuated form of the bovine tuberculosis bacillus, is the best-known nonspecific active immunostimulant; though its mechanism is unclear, it has proven benefit in superficial bladder cancer (with less promising results in melanoma). Interleukin-2 is a cytokine, ipilimumab is a checkpoint antibody, and sipuleucel-T is a specific vaccine.
hy: BCG = nonspecific active immunostimulant; superficial bladder cancer.

why.A: Correct — BCG, the attenuated bovine TB bacillus, for bladder cancer.
why.B: Wrong — IL-2 is a cytokine, a different nonspecific approach.
why.C: Wrong — ipilimumab is a specific checkpoint antibody.
why.D: Wrong — sipuleucel-T is a specific therapeutic vaccine.

stem: The sentinel lymph node concept (introduced by Morton et al.) is based on which principle, and what is its measured impact on lymphedema?

options.A: Tissues drain to regional nodes in a reproducible, orderly fashion, so the first (sentinel) node reflects the basin; extremity lymphedema fell to ~5% with sentinel-node mapping versus ~35% for complete lymphadenectomy
options.B: All nodes drain randomly, so the sentinel node is unreliable; lymphedema is unchanged
options.C: The sentinel node is always the largest palpable node; lymphedema rises to 80%
options.D: Sentinel mapping requires removing at least 12 nodes; it does not affect lymphedema

exp: The sentinel lymph node concept (Morton et al.) is based on tissues draining to regional nodes in a reproducible, orderly fashion, so the first (sentinel) node — found via radioactive colloid or blue dye — reflects the status of the whole basin; using it instead of complete dissection reduced extremity lymphedema to ~5% versus ~35% for complete lymphadenectomy. The other options deny the orderly-drainage basis or misstate the lymphedema figures.
hy: Sentinel node (Morton): orderly drainage; lymphedema ~5% vs ~35% for complete LND.

why.A: Correct — orderly drainage basis; lymphedema 5% vs 35%.
why.B: Wrong — drainage is orderly/reproducible, making the SLN reliable.
why.C: Wrong — the sentinel node is the first-draining, not largest palpable, node.
why.D: Wrong — SLN reduces lymphedema and does not require ≥12 nodes.

stem: Regarding elective (prophylactic) lymph node dissection (ELND) versus therapeutic lymph node dissection (TLND), which statement is correct?

options.A: ELND is not routinely recommended because it increases morbidity (e.g. lymphedema) and has not clearly improved survival over TLND for malignancies such as melanoma; it is reserved for high-probability occult-spread cases
options.B: ELND is mandatory for all melanomas because it clearly improves survival
options.C: TLND is performed before any tumor is detected
options.D: ELND carries no morbidity and is always preferred

exp: Elective (prophylactic) lymph node dissection (ELND) — done at the time of primary resection — is not routinely recommended because it increases morbidity (e.g. lymphedema, nerve injury) and has not clearly improved survival over therapeutic lymph node dissection (TLND, done when nodal recurrence appears) for malignancies such as melanoma; ELND is reserved for cases with high probability of occult spread (e.g. large head-and-neck primaries). The other options wrongly mandate ELND, misdefine TLND, or deny its morbidity.
hy: ELND not routine (morbidity, no clear survival benefit vs TLND); reserve for high occult-spread risk.

why.A: Correct — ELND morbidity without clear survival gain; reserved for high-risk occult spread.
why.B: Wrong — ELND is not mandatory and has not clearly improved melanoma survival.
why.C: Wrong — TLND is done at nodal recurrence, not before any tumor.
why.D: Wrong — ELND does carry real morbidity.

stem: A fair-skinned 70-year-old man has had several keratinocyte carcinomas removed. Among White individuals, what is the approximate distribution of these tumors?

options.A: ~75–80% are basal cell carcinomas and up to ~25% are squamous cell carcinomas
options.B: ~75–80% are squamous cell carcinomas and up to ~25% are basal cell carcinomas
options.C: They occur in roughly equal numbers, a 1:1 ratio of BCC to SCC
options.D: The great majority are melanomas, with BCC and SCC each under 10%

exp: Among White individuals, keratinocyte carcinomas are the most common malignancy, and the chapter states that ~75–80% are BCCs and up to ~25% are cSCCs — BCC predominates because intermittent UV exposure (which most fair-skinned people accumulate) is the dominant driver of BCC. Melanoma, although responsible for most skin-cancer deaths in younger adults, is far less common than either keratinocyte carcinoma. Recognizing this baseline ratio frames the rest of the chapter, since the ratio inverts with darker skin and with immunosuppression.
hy: Fair skin: ~75–80% BCC, ~25% cSCC; BCC is the most common skin cancer in humans.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter gives ~75–80% BCC and up to ~25% SCC in fair-skinned individuals.
why.B: Wrong — this reverses the ratio; SCC-predominance is seen in chronic immunosuppression (e.g. transplant), not in the general fair-skinned population.
why.C: Wrong — a roughly 1:1 BCC:SCC ratio is characteristic of darkly pigmented individuals, not White individuals.
why.D: Wrong — melanoma is the minority of skin cancers (>95% of skin cancers are NMSC); it causes disproportionate mortality but not incidence.

stem: How does the basal cell : squamous cell carcinoma ratio differ between lightly and darkly pigmented individuals?

options.A: ~4:1 (BCC predominant) in lightly pigmented vs ~1:1 to 2:1 in darkly pigmented individuals
options.B: ~1:1 in lightly pigmented vs ~4:1 (BCC predominant) in darkly pigmented individuals
options.C: ~4:1 (SCC predominant) in lightly pigmented vs ~1:1 in darkly pigmented individuals
options.D: Identical ~2:1 ratio in both groups, since pigmentation does not affect tumor type

exp: In lightly pigmented individuals the BCC:SCC ratio is ~4:1, whereas in darkly pigmented individuals it falls to ~1:1 to 2:1 — the relative rise of SCC in darker skin reflects that SCC is driven more by chronic scarring/cumulative injury (which is skin-type-independent) while BCC tracks tightly with UV susceptibility of fair skin. This is why SCC, not BCC, is the most common skin cancer in some darkly pigmented populations. The absolute NMSC incidence is also vastly higher in light skin (230 vs 3.4 per 100,000).
hy: BCC:SCC ≈ 4:1 in light skin vs ~1:1–2:1 in dark skin; NMSC incidence 230 vs 3.4/100k.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — Table 108.1 lists 4:1 (light) and 1.1:1–2:1 (dark).
why.B: Wrong — neither value is correct; darkly pigmented skin does not have a BCC-predominant 4:1 ratio.
why.C: Wrong — the 4:1 ratio in light skin is BCC-predominant, not SCC-predominant.
why.D: Wrong — pigmentation strongly affects tumor type and absolute incidence; the ratios are not identical.

stem: In darkly pigmented individuals, what proportion of cutaneous SCCs arise within scars and chronic non-healing ulcers, and how does this compare with lightly pigmented individuals?

options.A: 30–40% in darkly pigmented vs <2% in lightly pigmented individuals
options.B: <2% in darkly pigmented vs 30–40% in lightly pigmented individuals
options.C: About 50% in both groups equally
options.D: <5% in both groups, since scar-related SCC is rare regardless of skin type

exp: In darkly pigmented individuals, 30–40% of cSCCs arise in scars and chronic non-healing ulcers, versus <2% in lightly pigmented individuals — because in the relative absence of UV-driven tumors, chronic inflammation/scarring (a skin-type-independent carcinogenic stimulus) becomes the proportionally dominant cause of SCC. This is the basis for vigilance over chronically scarred or ulcerated skin (including Marjolin ulcers) in patients with darker skin.
hy: SCC in scars/chronic ulcers: 30–40% in dark skin vs <2% in light skin.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — Table 108.1 gives 30–40% (dark) vs <2% (light).
why.B: Wrong — this reverses the figures; light skin has the LOW (<2%) scar-SCC fraction.
why.C: Wrong — no figure approaches 50% in either group.
why.D: Wrong — scar-related SCC is proportionally very common in darkly pigmented individuals (30–40%), not rare.

stem: What is the approximate median age at diagnosis of basal cell carcinoma, and which demographic shift in incidence has been noted?

options.A: 68 years, with a disproportionate rise in incidence among young women
options.B: 40 years, with a disproportionate rise among elderly men
options.C: 80 years, with declining incidence across all groups
options.D: 25 years, occurring almost exclusively in immunosuppressed patients

exp: The median age at BCC diagnosis is 68 years, and a disproportionate increase in incidence has been observed among young women — partly attributed to recreational/indoor tanning exposure in that group. BCC incidence rises with age and is higher in men overall (1.5–2:1), but the noted demographic shift is the rise in young women. Knowing the median age anchors the expectation that BCC is predominantly a tumor of older, chronically sun-exposed skin.
hy: Median BCC age ≈ 68; disproportionate incidence rise in young women.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter gives a median age of 68 and a disproportionate rise among young women.
why.B: Wrong — 40 is far too young for the median, and the rise is in young women, not elderly men.
why.C: Wrong — 80 overstates the median and incidence is rising, not declining.
why.D: Wrong — 25 years and exclusive immunosuppression describe xeroderma pigmentosum/transplant settings, not typical BCC.

stem: Actinic keratoses are extremely common in fair-skinned populations. What is the estimated prevalence?

options.A: Up to ~12% of individuals in the US, and 39% of men / 24% of women in an Austrian screening study
options.B: Under 1% of the US population, almost entirely in organ-transplant recipients
options.C: About 90% of all adults regardless of skin phototype
options.D: Roughly equal to the prevalence of invasive SCC, ~0.4% per year

exp: AKs are extremely common: up to ~12% of individuals in the US have them, and an Austrian screening study of outpatients over 30 found a prevalence of 39% in men and 24% in women, rising with age — reflecting cumulative UV damage in fair-skinned populations. The much lower figure for invasive SCC underscores that most AKs do not progress. This high background prevalence is why "field" therapy and surveillance are emphasized.
hy: AK prevalence: ~12% US; 39% men / 24% women (Austria), increasing with age.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter cites ~12% (US) and 39%/24% (Austria).
why.B: Wrong — AKs are extraordinarily common in fair skin, not <1% and not transplant-limited.
why.C: Wrong — 90% of all adults overstates prevalence and ignores skin phototype.
why.D: Wrong — AK prevalence (~12%) is far higher than the annual incidence of invasive SCC.

stem: A patient asks why his dermatologist links his nodular BCCs to childhood sunburns at the beach but his forearm SCCs to a lifetime of outdoor farm work. Which statement best captures the UV-exposure pattern for each tumor?

options.A: BCC is associated with intermittent intense UV exposure and sunburns; cSCC and AKs with cumulative long-term UV exposure
options.B: BCC is associated with cumulative long-term UV exposure; cSCC with intermittent intense exposure and sunburns
options.C: Both BCC and cSCC are associated only with cumulative dose, with sunburn history irrelevant to either
options.D: Neither tumor is UV-related; the link is to ionizing radiation in both cases

exp: BCC is linked to intermittent intense UV exposure and sunburns (at any age), whereas cSCC and AKs are linked to cumulative long-term UV exposure — the two tumors have genuinely different UV epidemiology, which is why a recreational-sunburn history points toward BCC and an occupational/lifetime-dose history toward SCC. Childhood sunburns specifically raise the risk of both cSCC/AKs and BCC, and early-life exposure has a greater influence than later exposure.
hy: BCC = intermittent/sunburn UV; cSCC + AK = cumulative UV.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — matches the chapter's stated UV-pattern distinction.
why.B: Wrong — this reverses the two patterns; cumulative dose drives SCC/AK, not BCC.
why.C: Wrong — sunburn history is specifically relevant to BCC (and childhood SCC risk), not irrelevant.
why.D: Wrong — UVR is the predominant cause of both tumors; ionizing radiation is a far less common contributor.

stem: In the study by Karagas et al., any use of tanning devices was associated with which odds ratios for skin cancer, even after adjusting for sun exposure?

options.A: 2.5 for SCC and 1.5 for BCC
options.B: 8.6 for SCC and 3.0 for BCC
options.C: 1.04 for SCC and 1.09 for BCC
options.D: 4800 for SCC and 4.0 for BCC

exp: Karagas et al. found that any use of tanning devices carried odds ratios of 2.5 for SCC and 1.5 for BCC, persisting after adjustment for sunburn and sun-exposure history — establishing artificial UV as an independent risk factor, with a stronger effect on SCC than BCC. A subsequent meta-analysis of 12 studies confirmed these findings, prompting tanning-bed legislation.
hy: Indoor tanning OR: 2.5 (SCC) / 1.5 (BCC).
src: Bologna Ch108 · Risk factors – indoor tanning
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — these are the Karagas odds ratios cited in the chapter.
why.B: Wrong — 8.6 is the PUVA relative risk for cSCC, not the tanning-bed OR.
why.C: Wrong — 1.04/1.09 are the hydrochlorothiazide hazard rates for SCC/BCC.
why.D: Wrong — 4800× is the xeroderma pigmentosum risk multiplier, unrelated to tanning beds.

stem: Long-term PUVA (psoralen + UVA) therapy for psoriasis carries a significant dose-related risk of which cancer, and at what magnitude?

options.A: cSCC, with an adjusted relative risk of 8.6 for 100–337 treatments
options.B: BCC, with an adjusted relative risk of 8.6 for a single treatment course
options.C: Melanoma, with an odds ratio of 2.5 independent of dose
options.D: Merkel cell carcinoma, with a hazard ratio of 1.09

exp: Long-term PUVA — psoralen photosensitization plus UVA — carries a significant, dose-related risk of cSCC, with an adjusted relative risk of 8.6 for an accumulated 100–337 treatments; a smaller increased BCC risk emerges only with prolonged therapy. The mechanism combines direct UVA-photosensitized DNA damage with PUVA-induced immunosuppression. This dose dependence is why cumulative treatment number is tracked in PUVA patients.
hy: PUVA: adjusted RR for cSCC = 8.6 (100–337 treatments); dose-related.
src: Bologna Ch108 · Risk factors – therapeutic UVR
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter gives an adjusted RR of 8.6 for cSCC with PUVA.
why.B: Wrong — the dominant PUVA risk is cSCC (not BCC), and it is dose-related, not from a single course.
why.C: Wrong — the 8.6 risk is for cSCC, not melanoma, and is dose-dependent.
why.D: Wrong — 1.09 is the hydrochlorothiazide hazard rate for BCC, not a PUVA/Merkel figure.

stem: A man treated decades ago with X-ray therapy for tinea capitis develops multiple BCCs of the scalp. Which statement about ionizing-radiation–induced skin cancer is correct?

options.A: It roughly triples BCC/cSCC risk, usually appears ~20 years after exposure, and larger fractionated doses (>12–15 Gy) are thought necessary to induce tumors
options.B: It halves the risk of BCC/cSCC because radiation sterilizes precancerous clones
options.C: Tumors appear within days of exposure, and only single low doses (<1 Gy) are carcinogenic
options.D: It causes melanoma rather than keratinocyte carcinoma, with no latency period

exp: Therapeutic ionizing radiation roughly triples BCC/cSCC risk in proportion to dose; larger fractionated doses (>12–15 Gy) are thought necessary to induce tumors, and most arise after a long latency, typically ~20 years. In the tinea-capitis X-ray cohort, the relative risk for head-and-neck BCC among irradiated White individuals was 3.6. The long latency explains why radiation-induced tumors present decades after exposure.
hy: Ionizing radiation: ~3× risk, >12–15 Gy, ~20-yr latency (tinea-capitis BCC RR 3.6).
src: Bologna Ch108 · Risk factors – ionizing radiation
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — matches the chapter's threefold risk, dose threshold, and ~20-year latency.
why.B: Wrong — radiation increases, not decreases, keratinocyte-carcinoma risk.
why.C: Wrong — the latency is ~20 years, not days, and larger (not sub-Gy) doses are implicated.
why.D: Wrong — the tumors are predominantly BCC/cSCC with a long latency, not immediate melanoma.

stem: A patient with chronic arsenic exposure develops multiple SCCs. Which finding is the clinical clue to arsenic exposure, and what is the typical latency to tumor development?

options.A: Palmoplantar arsenical keratoses, with a latency of 20–40 years
options.B: A single rapidly growing crateriform nodule, with a latency of a few weeks
options.C: Diffuse facial telangiectasias, with a latency of 1–2 years
options.D: Periungual verrucous plaques, with a latency of several days

exp: Palmoplantar arsenical keratoses are the clinical clue to arsenic exposure, and arsenic-related skin cancers (classically SCC, but also BCC) typically develop after a long latency of 20–40 years — reflecting arsenic's slow carcinogenic accumulation. Arsenical SCC in situ also tends to be multifocal and to favor sun-protected trunk skin. Recognizing the palmoplantar keratoses can prompt a search for internal arsenic-related malignancy.
hy: Arsenic: palmoplantar arsenical keratoses; 20–40-yr latency; multifocal SCC.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter names palmoplantar arsenical keratoses and a 20–40-year latency.
why.B: Wrong — a rapidly growing crateriform nodule over weeks describes a keratoacanthoma, not arsenicism.
why.C: Wrong — diffuse facial telangiectasias suggest Rombo/Bazex–Dupré–Christol or rosacea, not arsenic.
why.D: Wrong — periungual verrucous plaques suggest HPV/verrucous lesions; arsenic latency is decades, not days.

stem: By how much is the incidence of cutaneous SCC and BCC increased in solid-organ transplant recipients relative to the general population?

options.A: cSCC increased 30–250× and BCC increased 5–10×
options.B: cSCC increased 3× and BCC increased 3×
options.C: cSCC increased 5–10× and BCC increased 30–250×
options.D: Both increased 2.5×, identical to the risk from tanning beds

exp: Solid-organ transplant recipients have a 30–250× increased incidence of cSCC and a 5–10× increased incidence of BCC, so the normal BCC-predominant ratio flips toward SCC — because chronic immunosuppression cripples T-cell tumor surveillance, immunosuppressive drugs (especially calcineurin inhibitors) are directly carcinogenic, and oncogenic HPV co-infection adds to UV damage. Risk scales with the degree and duration of immunosuppression. Switching to the mTOR inhibitor sirolimus or to belatacept reduces subsequent cSCC.
hy: Transplant: cSCC 30–250× / BCC 5–10×; ratio flips SCC-predominant; sirolimus/belatacept lower risk.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter gives cSCC 30–250× and BCC 5–10× in transplant recipients.
why.B: Wrong — 3× is the magnitude for therapeutic ionizing radiation, not transplantation.
why.C: Wrong — this reverses the two multipliers; cSCC (not BCC) carries the huge 30–250× increase.
why.D: Wrong — 2.5× is the tanning-bed odds ratio for SCC, far smaller than the transplant risk.

stem: A melanoma patient on a selective BRAF^V600 inhibitor (e.g. vemurafenib) develops several keratoacanthomas. Which statement is correct?

options.A: Up to 25% of patients develop cSCCs or KAs, and adding a MEK inhibitor markedly reduces this
options.B: About 1% develop cSCCs, and adding a MEK inhibitor increases the rate further
options.C: The lesions are caused by BRAF-inhibitor–induced immunosuppression and resolve with steroids
options.D: These are metastatic melanoma deposits, not new keratinocyte tumors

exp: Up to 25% of patients on a selective BRAF^V600 inhibitor (vemurafenib, dabrafenib) develop cSCCs or keratoacanthomas, often within weeks and on photodamaged skin — because BRAF inhibition paradoxically activates the MAPK pathway in RAS-mutant keratinocytes. Adding a MEK inhibitor (now routine) blocks that downstream reactivation and markedly reduces SCC/KA formation. This is why BRAF/MEK combination therapy is standard.
hy: BRAF inhibitor: ~25% develop cSCC/KA via paradoxical MAPK activation; MEK inhibitor rescues.
src: Bologna Ch108 · Risk factors – BRAF inhibitors
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter cites up to 25% and the protective effect of adding a MEK inhibitor.
why.B: Wrong — the rate is ~25%, not ~1%, and MEK inhibition reduces rather than increases it.
why.C: Wrong — the lesions arise from paradoxical MAPK activation, not drug-induced immunosuppression, and do not resolve with steroids.
why.D: Wrong — these are new keratinocyte tumors (cSCC/KA), not metastatic melanoma.

stem: Because of a small but statistically significant increase in NMSC risk, the FDA revised the labeling of hydrochlorothiazide in 2020. What were the reported hazard rates?

options.A: 1.04 for SCC and 1.09 for BCC
options.B: 8.6 for SCC and 3.0 for BCC
options.C: 2.5 for SCC and 1.5 for BCC
options.D: 30–250 for SCC and 5–10 for BCC

exp: Hydrochlorothiazide carries a small but significant NMSC risk — hazard rates of 1.04 for SCC and 1.09 for BCC — prompting the FDA's 2020 labeling change; the proposed mechanism is HCTZ's photosensitizing effect amplifying UV damage. Discontinuing HCTZ is one of the interventions recommended in high-risk (e.g. transplant) patients. The modest magnitude explains why it is a labeling caution rather than a contraindication.
hy: HCTZ hazard rate: 1.04 (SCC) / 1.09 (BCC); photosensitizer; FDA relabeled 2020.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter gives hazard rates of 1.04 (SCC) and 1.09 (BCC).
why.B: Wrong — 8.6 is the PUVA relative risk for cSCC, not the HCTZ hazard rate.
why.C: Wrong — 2.5/1.5 are the tanning-device odds ratios for SCC/BCC.
why.D: Wrong — 30–250/5–10 are the transplant risk multipliers, not the modest HCTZ hazards.

stem: Which gene is most strongly tied to the red-hair/fair-skin phenotype that predisposes to photocarcinogenesis?

options.A: MC1R (melanocortin-1 receptor), which is highly polymorphic, with nine common variant alleles linked to red hair/fair skin
options.B: PTCH1, a Hedgehog-pathway tumor suppressor mutated in basal cell nevus syndrome
options.C: TGFBR1, mutated in the multiple self-regressing keratoacanthomas of Ferguson–Smith syndrome
options.D: TMC6/TMC8, mutated in epidermodysplasia verruciformis

exp: MC1R (the melanocortin-1 receptor on melanocytes) is highly polymorphic, and in White individuals nine common variant alleles are strongly associated with the red-hair/fair-skin phenotype that raises photocarcinogenesis risk — because variant MC1R shifts melanin synthesis toward poorly photoprotective pheomelanin. Pigmentation is polygenic, but MC1R is the key gene named. Multiple pigmentation loci also appear among the ~30 BCC and ≥11 cSCC susceptibility loci identified by GWAS.
hy: MC1R = red hair/fair skin gene; pheomelanin shift raises skin-cancer risk.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter names MC1R and its nine common red-hair/fair-skin variant alleles.
why.B: Wrong — PTCH1 is the basal cell nevus syndrome (Hedgehog) gene, not the pigmentation gene.
why.C: Wrong — TGFBR1 underlies Ferguson–Smith multiple self-regressing KAs.
why.D: Wrong — TMC6/TMC8 cause epidermodysplasia verruciformis, an HPV-susceptibility disorder.

stem: In xeroderma pigmentosum, at what age do keratinocyte carcinomas typically appear, and how elevated is the risk in patients under 20?

options.A: Median age ~8 years, with a risk ~4800× that of the general population under age 20
options.B: Median age ~68 years, with a risk ~3× the general population
options.C: Median age ~40 years, with a risk ~30–250× the general population
options.D: Median age ~25 years, with a risk identical to fair-skinned controls

exp: In xeroderma pigmentosum — an autosomal recessive defect in nucleotide-excision (unscheduled DNA) repair — keratinocyte carcinomas appear at a median age of ~8 years, and in patients under 20 the risk is ~4800× that of the general population, because unrepaired UV photoproducts accumulate from early childhood. Strict lifelong UV avoidance dramatically lowers tumor formation. The extreme early onset distinguishes XP from ordinary photoaging-related skin cancer.
hy: XP: median age ~8 yr; <20-yr risk ~4800×; NER defect; UV avoidance is protective.
src: Bologna Ch108 · Genetic syndromes – xeroderma pigmentosum
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter gives a median age of 8 and a 4800× risk under 20.
why.B: Wrong — 68 years/3× describe ordinary BCC age and ionizing-radiation risk, not XP.
why.C: Wrong — 30–250× is the transplant cSCC multiplier, not the XP figure.
why.D: Wrong — XP tumors begin in early childhood with a vastly elevated risk, not at 25 with control-level risk.

stem: Basal cell nevus (Gorlin) syndrome is caused by an inactivating mutation in which gene, and what is its inheritance?

options.A: PTCH1 (rarely PTCH2), inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern
options.B: TP53, inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern
options.C: BLM (RecQL3 helicase), inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern
options.D: ACTRT1, inherited in an X-linked dominant pattern

exp: Basal cell nevus (Gorlin) syndrome is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by an inactivating mutation in PTCH1 (rarely PTCH2), a Hedgehog-pathway tumor suppressor — loss of PTCH1 de-represses Smoothened and constitutively activates Hedgehog signaling, driving multiple early-onset BCCs. SUFU mutations can instead cause multiple hereditary infundibulocystic BCC syndrome. Because of this pathway, patients are exquisitely radiosensitive and SMO inhibitors are therapeutic.
hy: BCNS = AD, inactivating PTCH1 (Hedgehog tumor suppressor).
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter states BCNS is autosomal dominant due to inactivating PTCH1 (rarely PTCH2).
why.B: Wrong — germline TP53 causes Li–Fraumeni syndrome, not BCNS.
why.C: Wrong — BLM/RecQL3 mutations cause Bloom syndrome.
why.D: Wrong — ACTRT1 (X-linked dominant) underlies Bazex–Dupré–Christol syndrome.

stem: Per the diagnostic criteria, how is basal cell nevus syndrome diagnosed clinically?

options.A: Two major criteria plus one minor, or one major plus three minor criteria
options.B: Any single major criterion alone, with no minor criteria required
options.C: Five minor criteria, with no major criterion permitted
options.D: Three major criteria only, regardless of minor criteria

exp: The diagnosis of basal cell nevus syndrome requires two major criteria plus one minor, OR one major plus three minor — a weighted scheme reflecting that no single feature is specific. Major criteria include a BCC before age 20 (or excessive BCCs), odontogenic keratocysts of the jaw, ≥2 palmar/plantar pits, falx cerebri calcification before 20, childhood medulloblastoma, and a first-degree relative with BCNS. Identifying a germline PTCH1 or SUFU variant alongside one major criterion can also establish the diagnosis.
hy: BCNS dx = 2 major + 1 minor, or 1 major + 3 minor.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — these are the diagnostic-criteria combinations in Table 108.3.
why.B: Wrong — a single major criterion alone is not sufficient (unless paired with a pathogenic PTCH1/SUFU variant).
why.C: Wrong — minor criteria alone, without a major criterion, do not establish the diagnosis.
why.D: Wrong — three major criteria is not the stated threshold; the scheme mixes major and minor.

stem: Bazex–Dupré–Christol syndrome features follicular atrophoderma, milia, and multiple facial BCCs. What is its genetic basis?

options.A: X-linked dominant inheritance with mutations in ACTRT1, causing aberrant Hedgehog activation in primary cilia
options.B: Autosomal dominant inheritance with mutations in TGFBR1
options.C: Autosomal recessive inheritance with mutations in TMC6/TMC8
options.D: Autosomal dominant inheritance with mutations in PTCH1

exp: Bazex–Dupré–Christol syndrome — follicular atrophoderma (dorsal hands/feet), hypotrichosis, milia, epidermoid cysts, and multiple facial BCCs that often look trichoepithelioma-like — is inherited X-linked dominantly with mutations in ACTRT1, producing aberrant Hedgehog activation within primary cilia. BCCs appear during the second decade. The trichoepithelioma-like histology reflects its follicular-Hedgehog basis.
hy: Bazex–Dupré–Christol = X-linked dominant, ACTRT1, follicular atrophoderma + facial BCCs.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter gives X-linked dominant ACTRT1 mutations causing ciliary Hedgehog activation.
why.B: Wrong — autosomal dominant TGFBR1 mutation is Ferguson–Smith syndrome.
why.C: Wrong — autosomal recessive TMC6/TMC8 mutation is epidermodysplasia verruciformis.
why.D: Wrong — autosomal dominant PTCH1 is basal cell nevus (Gorlin) syndrome, not Bazex–Dupré–Christol.

stem: Two inherited keratoacanthoma syndromes are distinguished by their behavior. Which pairing is correct?

options.A: Ferguson–Smith = multiple self-regressing KAs (TGFBR1); Grzybowski = generalized eruptive KAs with hundreds of papules and mask-like facies
options.B: Ferguson–Smith = generalized eruptive KAs; Grzybowski = multiple self-regressing KAs (TGFBR1)
options.C: Both are caused by PTCH1 mutations and present identically with a single giant KA
options.D: Ferguson–Smith = sebaceous KAs of Muir–Torre; Grzybowski = arsenical keratoses

exp: Ferguson–Smith syndrome is autosomal dominant (TGFBR1, encoding TGF-β receptor type 1) and produces multiple keratoacanthomas that characteristically self-regress over weeks to months; Grzybowski type presents instead as generalized eruptive KAs — hundreds of papules resembling milia — often with scarring, ectropion, and a mask-like facies. Distinguishing them matters because the regressing Ferguson–Smith course differs from the disfiguring Grzybowski one. Both are KA syndromes, not BCC syndromes.
hy: Ferguson–Smith = regressing KAs (TGFBR1); Grzybowski = generalized eruptive KAs + mask-like facies.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — matches the chapter's descriptions of the two KA syndromes.
why.B: Wrong — this swaps the two phenotypes (Ferguson–Smith regresses; Grzybowski is eruptive).
why.C: Wrong — neither is a PTCH1 disorder, and they do not present as a single giant KA.
why.D: Wrong — sebaceous KAs belong to Muir–Torre, and arsenical keratoses are a chemical exposure, not these syndromes.

stem: Why does treatment of severely photodamaged skin (e.g. the bald scalp) target the entire affected field rather than only visible tumors?

options.A: The field contains multiple clusters of p53-mutated keratinocytes, clinically apparent or not, from which new tumors arise
options.B: The field is colonized by Merkel-cell polyomavirus, which seeds tumors uniformly
options.C: Topical agents work only when applied to large continuous areas for pharmacokinetic reasons
options.D: Visible tumors are always benign, so only the surrounding normal skin needs treatment

exp: Field cancerization describes photodamaged skin (e.g. the bald scalp) harboring multiple clusters of p53-mutated keratinocytes — clinically apparent or subclinical — from which new AKs and SCCs continually arise; the presence of multiple AKs signals the field. This is why effective treatment must address the entire affected anatomical site, not just individual visible tumors. The p53 mutations are an early UV signature of the field defect.
hy: Field cancerization = p53-mutated keratinocyte clones across a photodamaged field → treat the whole field.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter attributes field cancerization to clusters of p53-mutated keratinocytes.
why.B: Wrong — Merkel-cell polyomavirus drives Merkel cell carcinoma, not keratinocyte field cancerization.
why.C: Wrong — the rationale is the underlying mutated field, not a pharmacokinetic application requirement.
why.D: Wrong — visible tumors in the field are often malignant/premalignant; the surrounding field is also affected.

stem: AKs are regarded as precursors of invasive cSCC. What is the estimated likelihood that a solitary AK progresses to invasive cSCC?

options.A: Difficult to predict, with estimates ranging widely from 0.1% to 20%
options.B: A fixed 50% within one year of appearance
options.C: Essentially 100%, so every AK must be excised
options.D: Zero, because AKs are entirely benign and never progress

exp: AKs are precursors of cSCC, but the likelihood that a single AK becomes invasive is hard to predict, with published estimates ranging widely from 0.1% to 20% — the uncertainty reflects spontaneous regression of many AKs alongside progression of others, and the difficulty of tracking individual lesions. This is why management is risk-based (treat the field, follow high-risk patients) rather than excising every AK. AKs are also markers of overall increased cSCC risk.
hy: Solitary AK → invasive cSCC: estimated 0.1–20%; unpredictable, often regresses.
src: Bologna Ch108 · Actinic keratosis – natural history
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter gives the 0.1–20% range and emphasizes unpredictability.
why.B: Wrong — no fixed 50%/1-year figure is given; progression is variable and usually low.
why.C: Wrong — AKs do not invariably progress; many regress, so universal excision is not indicated.
why.D: Wrong — AKs are precursors with real (if low) malignant potential, not entirely benign.

stem: Which description correctly matches an AK subtype?

options.A: Actinic cheilitis = AK-type change on the lower vermilion lip, with a relatively higher potential to become invasive SCC than AKs elsewhere
options.B: Atrophic AK = a thick hyperkeratotic papule that may form a cutaneous horn
options.C: Hypertrophic AK = a flat pink macule with minimal surface change and an atrophic epidermis
options.D: Pigmented AK = a band-like lymphocytic infiltrate with overlying erythema and pruritus

exp: Actinic cheilitis is AK-type change on the lower vermilion lip (well-demarcated erythema/scale, sometimes leukoplakia), and its potential to evolve into invasive SCC is relatively higher than for AKs at other sites — because the lip's thin epithelium and high cumulative UV make it a higher-risk field. The other subtypes have distinct features: hypertrophic AKs are thick/hyperkeratotic (and can form a cutaneous horn), atrophic AKs are flat pink macules with an atrophic epidermis, and lichenoid AKs show a band-like infiltrate with erythema/pruritus.
hy: Actinic cheilitis (lower lip) carries a higher invasive-SCC potential than AKs elsewhere.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — matches the chapter's description of actinic cheilitis.
why.B: Wrong — a thick hyperkeratotic papule forming a cutaneous horn describes a hypertrophic AK, not an atrophic one.
why.C: Wrong — a flat pink macule with an atrophic epidermis is an atrophic AK, not hypertrophic.
why.D: Wrong — a band-like lymphocytic infiltrate with erythema/pruritus describes a lichenoid AK, not pigmented.

stem: On dermoscopy of the face, which pattern is characteristic of an actinic keratosis?

options.A: A "strawberry pattern" — an erythematous pseudonetwork around follicular openings filled with yellowish keratotic plugs
options.B: Arborizing (tree-like) vessels with foci of microulceration
options.C: A blue–white veil over a pigment network with irregular streaks
options.D: A parallel-ridge pattern of pigmentation on acral skin

exp: On the face, AKs classically show a "strawberry pattern" on dermoscopy: an erythematous pseudonetwork surrounding hair-follicle openings that are filled with yellowish keratotic plugs — the network corresponds to telangiectatic vessels between follicles, the plugs to follicular hyperkeratosis. This pattern (with surface scale and dotted vessels at other sites) helps distinguish AK from its mimics.
hy: Facial AK dermoscopy = "strawberry pattern" (erythematous pseudonetwork + keratotic follicular plugs).
src: Bologna Ch108 · In vivo imaging – dermoscopy
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter names the strawberry pattern as characteristic of facial AK.
why.B: Wrong — arborizing vessels with microulceration are the dermoscopic hallmark of BCC.
why.C: Wrong — a blue–white veil over an irregular pigment network suggests melanoma.
why.D: Wrong — a parallel-ridge pattern is an acral-melanoma dermoscopic sign.

stem: Which histopathologic feature is characteristic of an actinic keratosis (rather than SCC in situ)?

options.A: Partial-thickness atypia of keratinocytes (especially basal) with sparing of the acrosyringia/acrotrichia, producing orthokeratosis over those ostia
options.B: Full-thickness epidermal atypia over a broad zone with frequent mitoses throughout
options.C: A downward proliferation of lobules of glassy eosinophilic keratinocytes with keratin pearls invading the dermis
options.D: Aggregations of basaloid cells with peripheral palisading and stromal retraction clefts

exp: An AK shows partial-thickness keratinocyte atypia, most marked in the basal layer, with characteristic sparing of the acrosyringia and acrotrichia — so orthokeratosis sits above those adnexal ostia while parakeratosis overlies the atypical interfollicular epidermis (the alternating "pink and blue" cornified layer).
