This partial-thickness, adnexa-sparing atypia is what separates AK from full-thickness SCC in situ. Solar elastosis is also present in the dermis.
hy: AK = partial-thickness atypia sparing adnexal ostia (orthokeratosis over acrosyringia).
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — matches the chapter's histology of AK.
why.B: Wrong — full-thickness atypia over a broad zone with frequent mitoses defines SCC in situ (Bowen disease).
why.C: Wrong — dermal lobules of glassy keratinocytes with keratin pearls describe invasive well-differentiated SCC.
why.D: Wrong — basaloid aggregations with peripheral palisading and retraction clefts describe BCC.

stem: A long-standing erythematous scaly plaque on the lower leg, previously treated as eczema, shows full-thickness epidermal atypia on biopsy. What is the diagnosis?

options.A: Bowen disease (cutaneous SCC in situ)
options.B: Invasive well-differentiated cutaneous SCC
options.C: Superficial basal cell carcinoma
options.D: Hypertrophic actinic keratosis

exp: Full-thickness epidermal atypia in a chronic erythematous scaly plaque is SCC in situ (Bowen disease) — by definition the atypia spans the entire epidermal thickness over a broad zone, with frequent mitoses and dyskeratosis, but no dermal invasion. Lower-leg Bowen disease is classically misdiagnosed and treated as eczema or psoriasis for years. Invasion through the basement membrane would make it invasive SCC.
hy: Full-thickness epidermal atypia, no invasion = Bowen disease (SCC in situ).
src: Bologna Ch108 · SCC in situ – Bowen disease
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — full-thickness atypia without invasion is SCC in situ (Bowen disease).
why.B: Wrong — invasive SCC requires dermal invasion (lobules of atypical keratinocytes through the basement membrane).
why.C: Wrong — superficial BCC shows basaloid buds, not full-thickness squamous atypia.
why.D: Wrong — hypertrophic AK shows only partial-thickness atypia, not full-thickness.

stem: Which pairing of an SCC-in-situ variant with its description is correct?

options.A: Erythroplasia of Queyrat = SCC in situ presenting as a glistening red plaque on the glans penis
options.B: Bowenoid papulosis = arsenic-induced multifocal SCC in situ of the sun-protected trunk
options.C: Erythroplasia of Queyrat = HPV-16–associated small papules of young adults that can regress
options.D: Bowenoid papulosis = SCC in situ of the lower vermilion lip from chronic sun exposure

exp: Erythroplasia of Queyrat is SCC in situ of the glans penis, presenting as a glistening, dull-red, well-demarcated plaque — the mucosal counterpart of Bowen disease. The distractors describe other in-situ variants: bowenoid papulosis is HPV-16–associated small papules of young adults that can regress; arsenical SCC in situ is multifocal on the sun-protected trunk; and lower-lip in-situ change from UV is actinic cheilitis. Keeping these mapped prevents misdiagnosing penile SCC in situ as balanitis.
hy: Erythroplasia of Queyrat = SCC in situ of the glans penis.
src: Bologna Ch108 · SCC in situ – variants
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter equates erythroplasia of Queyrat with SCC in situ of the glans penis.
why.B: Wrong — arsenic-induced multifocal trunk SCC in situ is arsenical SCC in situ, not bowenoid papulosis.
why.C: Wrong — HPV-16 papules of young adults that regress describe bowenoid papulosis, not erythroplasia of Queyrat.
why.D: Wrong — in-situ change of the lower vermilion lip from sun is actinic cheilitis, not bowenoid papulosis.

stem: Bowenoid papulosis presents as small papules of the anogenital region in young adults and can regress spontaneously, yet biopsy is taken seriously. Why?

options.A: It is HPV-16–associated and its histology is that of a high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion / SCC in situ
options.B: It is a melanocytic lesion that frequently transforms into nodular melanoma
options.C: It is a benign viral wart with no malignant potential whatsoever
options.D: It is a manifestation of internal arsenic toxicity requiring chelation

exp: Bowenoid papulosis is linked to human papillomavirus, most commonly HPV-16, and although it favors young adults and can regress spontaneously, its histopathology is that of a high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion / SCC in situ — so it cannot be dismissed as a benign wart. This mismatch between an indolent clinical course and worrisome histology is the teaching point. Management balances the malignant histology against the tendency to regress.
hy: Bowenoid papulosis = HPV-16; clinically may regress but histology = SCC in situ.
src: Bologna Ch108 · SCC in situ – bowenoid papulosis
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter ties bowenoid papulosis to HPV-16 with high-grade SIL/SCC-in-situ histology.
why.B: Wrong — it is a squamous intraepithelial lesion, not a melanocytic lesion progressing to melanoma.
why.C: Wrong — despite a viral cause and possible regression, the histology is SCC in situ, not a benign wart.
why.D: Wrong — internal arsenic toxicity is associated with arsenical keratoses/SCC, not bowenoid papulosis.

stem: A patient with an SCC on the cheek reports paresthesias and a burning pain over the lesion. What does this most likely indicate?

options.A: Perineural invasion
options.B: Secondary bacterial infection
options.C: Spontaneous regression of the tumor
options.D: A benign neuroma unrelated to the tumor

exp: New paresthesias, anesthesia, or pain over a cSCC are signs of perineural invasion — tumor tracking along nerve sheaths produces neurologic symptoms and portends aggressive, recurrence-prone disease. This clinical clue should prompt imaging and aggressive (often Mohs ± adjuvant radiation) management. PNI is also a high-risk staging feature in both the AJCC and Brigham systems.
hy: Paresthesia/pain over a cSCC → suspect perineural invasion (aggressive).
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter lists paresthesia, anesthesia, and pain as signs of perineural invasion.
why.B: Wrong — secondary infection causes purulence/cellulitis, not the neuropathic symptoms described.
why.C: Wrong — spontaneous regression (seen in keratoacanthoma) does not produce paresthesias.
why.D: Wrong — these symptoms arise from tumor perineural spread, not an incidental benign neuroma.

stem: On dermoscopy of keratinizing tumors such as invasive cSCC and keratoacanthoma, which feature is described as the hallmark?

options.A: A whitish halo surrounding the vessels
options.B: A negative pigment network with a central scar-like depigmentation
options.C: Multiple blue–gray ovoid nests
options.D: A parallel-furrow pattern on volar skin

exp: The dermoscopic hallmark of keratinizing tumors (invasive cSCC, keratoacanthoma, and also seborrheic keratoses) is a whitish halo surrounding the vessels — corresponding to the keratin-rich tumor stroma around the vascular structures. Invasive cSCC additionally shows polymorphous/atypical vessels (linear-irregular, hairpin, dotted). Recognizing the white halo helps flag a keratinizing malignancy.
hy: Keratinizing-tumor dermoscopy hallmark = whitish halo around vessels.
src: Bologna Ch108 · In vivo imaging – dermoscopy
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter calls the whitish perivascular halo the hallmark of keratinizing tumors.
why.B: Wrong — a negative network with scar-like depigmentation suggests melanoma regression, not a keratinizing tumor.
why.C: Wrong — blue–gray ovoid nests are a pigmented-BCC dermoscopic feature.
why.D: Wrong — a parallel-furrow pattern is a benign acral-nevus sign.

stem: Using the AJCC 8th-edition staging for head-and-neck cSCC, a 3-cm tumor with no other high-risk feature is classified as which T stage?

options.A: T2 (≥2 cm but <4 cm)
options.D: T4 (cortical bone or skull-base invasion)

exp: In the AJCC 8th-edition TNM for head-and-neck cSCC, T2 is a tumor ≥2 cm but <4 cm, so a 3-cm tumor without other high-risk features is T2. T1 is <2 cm and T3 is ≥4 cm (or deep invasion >6 mm/beyond fat, PNI, or minor bone erosion); T4 requires cortical bone/marrow or skull-base invasion. Size alone does not reach T3 until 4 cm, which is why a 3-cm low-risk tumor stays T2.
hy: cSCC AJCC: T1 <2 cm, T2 ≥2 to <4 cm, T3 ≥4 cm or deep invasion/PNI/minor bone.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — 3 cm falls in the ≥2 to <4 cm T2 band.
why.B: Wrong — T1 is reserved for tumors <2 cm.
why.C: Wrong — T3 requires ≥4 cm or a deep-invasion/PNI/bone feature, absent here.
why.D: Wrong — T4 requires cortical bone/marrow or skull-base invasion.

stem: In the AJCC system for cSCC, which finding upstages a tumor to T3 regardless of its diameter?

options.A: Deep invasion — beyond the subcutaneous fat or >6 mm thickness — or perineural invasion or minor bone erosion
options.B: Any tumor located on the ear or lip
options.C: A single ipsilateral lymph node ≤3 cm in size
options.D: Poor histologic differentiation alone

exp: A tumor is upstaged to T3 in the AJCC cSCC system by deep invasion — defined as extension beyond the subcutaneous fat or thickness >6 mm — or by perineural invasion or minor bone erosion, independent of diameter; so a small tumor with deep invasion can be T3. PNI qualifying for T3 means tumor in a nerve sheath deeper than the dermis or a nerve caliber ≥0.1 mm. These features let "small but dangerous" tumors stage appropriately.
hy: T3 triggers: deep invasion (>6 mm/beyond fat), PNI, or minor bone erosion — any diameter.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — deep invasion/PNI/minor bone erosion define T3 regardless of size.
why.B: Wrong — ear/lip location is a clinical high-risk feature but does not by itself assign T3 in the AJCC T table.
why.C: Wrong — a single ipsilateral node ≤3 cm is a nodal (N1) descriptor, not a T-stage criterion.
why.D: Wrong — poor differentiation is a Brigham/clinical high-risk factor, not an AJCC T3 trigger by itself.

stem: The Brigham and Women's Hospital system stages cSCC by counting four high-risk factors. Which set lists those four factors and the T2b cutoff correctly?

options.A: Diameter ≥2 cm, poor differentiation, PNI of nerves ≥0.1 mm, invasion beyond fat — with T2b = 2–3 high-risk factors
options.B: Diameter ≥4 cm, desmoplasia, ulceration, immunosuppression — with T2b = 1 high-risk factor
options.C: Location on ear, thickness >2 mm, recurrence, lymphovascular invasion — with T2b = ≥4 high-risk factors
options.D: Macrocephaly, palmar pits, falx calcification, medulloblastoma — with T2b = 2 high-risk factors

exp: The Brigham and Women's Hospital system counts four high-risk factors — tumor diameter ≥2 cm, poor differentiation, perineural invasion of nerves ≥0.1 mm, and invasion beyond the subcutaneous fat — and stages accordingly: T1 = 0 factors, T2a = 1, T2b = 2–3, T3 = ≥4 factors or bone invasion. This factor-counting scheme stratifies metastatic risk better than diameter alone (the basis of the AJCC). The T2b group (2–3 factors) carries the bulk of poor outcomes.
hy: Brigham HRFs: ≥2 cm, poor diff, PNI ≥0.1 mm, beyond fat → T1=0, T2a=1, T2b=2–3, T3=≥4/bone.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — these are the four Brigham high-risk factors and the T2b = 2–3 cutoff.
why.B: Wrong — the diameter threshold is ≥2 cm (not ≥4), and desmoplasia/ulceration/immunosuppression are not the four counted Brigham factors.
why.C: Wrong — these are not the Brigham four factors, and T2b is 2–3 factors, not ≥4.
why.D: Wrong — macrocephaly/palmar pits/falx calcification/medulloblastoma are basal cell nevus syndrome criteria, not cSCC staging.

stem: In a large prospective study, how did metastatic risk vary with cSCC tumor thickness?

options.A: Tumors >6 mm thick had a 16% metastatic rate vs 4% for 2.1–6 mm; tumors ≤2 mm had a low risk
options.B: Tumors ≤2 mm thick had the highest metastatic rate (16%), decreasing with thickness
options.C: All cSCCs had an identical 30% metastatic rate regardless of thickness
options.D: Thickness had no relationship to metastasis; only diameter mattered

exp: In a large prospective study, metastatic risk rose steeply with cSCC thickness: tumors >6 mm thick had a 16% metastatic rate versus 4% for tumors 2.1–6 mm, while tumors ≤2 mm thick carried a low risk (only tumors >2 mm had a significant metastatic risk) — because deeper tumors have greater access to dermal lymphovascular and perineural routes. Tumor thickness and a desmoplastic stroma were also local-recurrence risk factors. This thickness gradient underlies aggressive treatment of thick tumors.
hy: cSCC metastasis by thickness: ≤2 mm low, 2.1–6 mm 4%, >6 mm 16%.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — matches the study's 16% (>6 mm) vs 4% (2.1–6 mm) figures with low risk ≤2 mm.
why.B: Wrong — risk rises with thickness; thin (≤2 mm) tumors have the lowest, not highest, metastatic rate.
why.C: Wrong — overall metastasis was ~4%, not a uniform 30%, and it varies with thickness.
why.D: Wrong — thickness is strongly related to metastasis; it is not irrelevant.

stem: Which histopathologic SCC feature is specifically associated with a markedly increased risk of local recurrence?

options.A: A desmoplastic stroma (local recurrence ~24% vs ~1% for non-desmoplastic SCC)
options.B: The presence of keratin pearls in a well-differentiated tumor
options.C: A dense peritumoral lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate
options.D: Orthokeratosis overlying the acrosyringia

exp: A desmoplastic stroma in cSCC is specifically associated with a high local-recurrence risk — in the cited series 24% of desmoplastic SCCs recurred locally versus ~1% of non-desmoplastic SCCs — because the dense reactive stroma accompanies infiltrative, hard-to-clear tumor margins. Tumor thickness is the other named recurrence risk factor. Keratin pearls and a brisk infiltrate are not adverse-prognosis features.
hy: Desmoplastic cSCC: ~24% local recurrence vs ~1% non-desmoplastic.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter gives 24% vs 1% recurrence for desmoplastic vs non-desmoplastic SCC.
why.B: Wrong — keratin pearls indicate good differentiation, not a high-recurrence feature.
why.C: Wrong — a dense lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate is not cited as a recurrence risk factor.
why.D: Wrong — orthokeratosis over the acrosyringia is a benign AK histologic feature, unrelated to SCC recurrence.

stem: A lesion on the dorsal hand grew over a few weeks into a sharply circumscribed crateriform nodule with a central keratotic core, then began to involute. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Keratoacanthoma
options.B: Morpheaform basal cell carcinoma
options.C: Verrucous carcinoma
options.D: Bowen disease

exp: A rapidly enlarging, sharply circumscribed crateriform nodule with a central keratotic core that develops over a few weeks and then involutes is the classic keratoacanthoma — considered by some a low-grade/"pseudomalignant" variant of cSCC. Most occur on the head/neck or sun-exposed extremities and resolve over months leaving an atrophic scar. The rapid growth-then-involution course distinguishes it from the indolent, persistent mimics.
hy: KA = rapid crateriform nodule with keratotic core, then involution.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — rapid growth, crater with keratotic core, and involution define keratoacanthoma.
why.B: Wrong — morpheaform BCC is a slowly enlarging scar-like indurated plaque, not a fast crateriform nodule.
why.C: Wrong — verrucous carcinoma is a large, slowly enlarging exophytic warty tumor, not a rapidly involuting nodule.
why.D: Wrong — Bowen disease is a chronic flat scaly plaque (SCC in situ), not a crateriform nodule.

stem: Which histopathologic architecture is typical of a keratoacanthoma?

options.A: A "volcano-like" crater of cornified material lined by well-differentiated keratinocytes with glassy eosinophilic cytoplasm
options.B: Full-thickness epidermal atypia with frequent mitoses and no crater
options.C: Thin anastomosing strands of basaloid cells in a fenestrated pattern
options.D: A pushing, broad, deeply penetrating well-differentiated proliferation with minimal atypia

exp: A keratoacanthoma has a "volcano-like" architecture: a central crater of cornified material flanked by epithelial "lips" of well-differentiated keratinocytes with brightly eosinophilic, glassy cytoplasm, usually with little cytologic atypia. As it regresses the dome flattens and the base fibroses. Marked nuclear atypia or abnormal mitoses would instead prompt the diagnosis of an invasive KA-like SCC.
hy: KA histology = "volcano" crater with glassy eosinophilic well-differentiated keratinocytes.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter describes the volcano-like crater with glassy eosinophilic keratinocytes.
why.B: Wrong — full-thickness atypia with frequent mitoses and no crater describes Bowen disease (SCC in situ).
why.C: Wrong — thin anastomosing basaloid strands in a fenestrated pattern describe fibroepithelioma of Pinkus.
why.D: Wrong — a pushing, deeply penetrating well-differentiated proliferation with minimal atypia describes verrucous carcinoma.

stem: Verrucous carcinoma is a well-differentiated low-grade SCC variant with three major subtypes. Which option matches a subtype to its site correctly?

options.A: Epithelioma cuniculatum = plantar surface of the foot
options.B: Buschke–Löwenstein tumor (giant condyloma acuminatum) = plantar foot
options.C: Oral florid papillomatosis = genital skin
options.D: Epithelioma cuniculatum = oral mucosa

exp: Verrucous carcinoma — a well-differentiated, low-grade SCC variant — has three major subtypes defined by site: epithelioma cuniculatum on the plantar foot, Buschke–Löwenstein tumor (giant condyloma acuminatum) on the genitalia, and oral florid papillomatosis on the oral mucosa. "Cuniculatum" refers to the rabbit-burrow-like crevices of the plantar tumor. Mapping each subtype to its site is the testable point.
hy: Verrucous CA: cuniculatum=plantar, Buschke–Löwenstein=genital, oral florid papillomatosis=oral.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — epithelioma cuniculatum is the plantar-foot subtype.
why.B: Wrong — Buschke–Löwenstein (giant condyloma) is the genital subtype, not plantar.
why.C: Wrong — oral florid papillomatosis is the oral-mucosa subtype, not genital.
why.D: Wrong — epithelioma cuniculatum is plantar, not oral.

stem: Which statement about verrucous carcinoma is correct?

options.A: It is well differentiated and rarely metastasizes, but radiotherapy is generally avoided because anaplastic transformation may worsen prognosis
options.B: It is poorly differentiated and metastasizes early, so radiotherapy is first-line
options.C: It is reliably diagnosed on a small superficial shave biopsy
options.D: It never recurs after attempted removal

exp: Verrucous carcinoma is well differentiated, has a pushing rather than infiltrating border, and rarely metastasizes — but it frequently recurs locally, and radiotherapy is generally avoided because anaplastic transformation after irradiation can worsen the prognosis. Diagnosis often requires a large excisional biopsy, since the size and architecture matter more than cytology and a superficial sample can miss the diagnosis. These features drive a surgery-first, radiation-avoiding approach.
hy: Verrucous CA: rarely metastasizes, recurs locally, avoid RT (anaplastic transformation), needs large excisional biopsy.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — matches the chapter: rarely metastasizes, RT avoided due to anaplastic transformation.
why.B: Wrong — it is well differentiated and rarely metastasizes; radiotherapy is avoided, not first-line.
why.C: Wrong — a small superficial shave is inadequate; diagnosis needs a large excisional biopsy.
why.D: Wrong — verrucous carcinoma often recurs after attempted removal.

stem: A squamous cell carcinoma that develops within a chronic non-healing wound or an old thermal-burn scar is known by which name?

options.A: Marjolin ulcer
options.B: Buschke–Löwenstein tumor
options.C: Erythroplasia of Queyrat
options.D: Bowenoid papulosis

exp: A Marjolin ulcer is an SCC that arises in a chronic wound or scar, including an old thermal-burn scar — chronic inflammation and repeated repair in damaged skin promote malignant transformation over time. The distractors are unrelated entities: Buschke–Löwenstein is genital verrucous carcinoma, erythroplasia of Queyrat is penile SCC in situ, and bowenoid papulosis is HPV-16 anogenital SCC in situ. The chronic-wound/burn-scar context is the trigger to think Marjolin.
hy: Marjolin ulcer = SCC in a chronic wound, scar, or burn scar.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter defines Marjolin ulcer as SCC in a chronic wound/scar including thermal burns.
why.B: Wrong — Buschke–Löwenstein tumor is genital giant condyloma (verrucous carcinoma).
why.C: Wrong — erythroplasia of Queyrat is SCC in situ of the glans penis.
why.D: Wrong — bowenoid papulosis is HPV-16–associated anogenital SCC in situ.

stem: A poorly differentiated dermal spindle-cell tumor must be distinguished from cSCC. Which immunoprofile supports cutaneous SCC rather than the mimics?

options.A: Cytokeratin-positive, distinguishing it from melanoma (SOX-10/S100/Melan-A+) and atypical fibroxanthoma (cytokeratin−, CD10+)
options.B: SOX-10– and S100-positive, like a spindle-cell melanoma
options.C: Cytokeratin-negative but CD10-positive, like an atypical fibroxanthoma
options.D: Smooth-muscle-actin– and desmin-positive, like a leiomyosarcoma

exp: A poorly differentiated cSCC is cytokeratin-positive, which separates it from its principal spindle-cell mimics: spindle-cell melanoma (Melan-A/SOX-10/S100-positive, cytokeratin-negative), atypical fibroxanthoma (cytokeratin-negative, CD10-positive), and leiomyosarcoma (smooth-muscle-actin/desmin-positive). Because poorly differentiated tumors lose overt keratinization, immunohistochemistry is required to confirm squamous lineage. The cytokeratin-positive/melanoma-and-AFX-negative profile clinches cSCC.
hy: Poorly diff cSCC = cytokeratin+; melanoma SOX10/S100+, AFX CK−/CD10+, leiomyosarcoma SMA/desmin+.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — cytokeratin positivity (with negative melanoma and AFX markers) supports cSCC.
why.B: Wrong — SOX-10/S100 positivity characterizes spindle-cell melanoma, not SCC.
why.C: Wrong — cytokeratin-negative/CD10-positive describes atypical fibroxanthoma.
why.D: Wrong — smooth-muscle-actin/desmin positivity characterizes leiomyosarcoma.

stem: What is the most common subtype of basal cell carcinoma, and its classic presentation?

options.A: Nodular BCC (~50% of all BCCs) — a shiny pearly papule/nodule with arborizing telangiectasias and a rolled border
options.B: Superficial BCC (~50%) — a scar-like indurated white plaque with ill-defined borders
options.C: Morpheaform BCC (~50%) — an erythematous scaly patch on the trunk
options.D: Fibroepithelial BCC (~50%) — a pedunculated polyp on the lower back

exp: Nodular BCC is the most common subtype, accounting for ~50% of all BCCs, and classically presents as a shiny "pearly" papule or nodule with arborizing telangiectasias and an elevated rolled border (with central ulceration it becomes the "rodent ulcer") — the pearly translucency and surface vessels reflect the basaloid tumor nests with overlying thin epidermis. It favors the face. The other subtypes have distinct looks: superficial = trunk patch, morpheaform = scar-like plaque, fibroepithelioma = lower-back plaque.
hy: Nodular BCC ~50%; pearly papule + arborizing telangiectasia + rolled border.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — nodular BCC is ~50% of BCCs with the classic pearly/rolled-border morphology.
why.B: Wrong — a scar-like white plaque with ill-defined borders describes morpheaform BCC, not superficial.
why.C: Wrong — an erythematous scaly trunk patch describes superficial BCC, not morpheaform.
why.D: Wrong — fibroepithelioma of Pinkus is a sessile lower-back plaque but is not the ~50% most-common subtype.

stem: Superficial BCC differs from other BCC subtypes in age and site. Which statement is correct?

options.A: It has the youngest mean age at diagnosis (mid-50s) and favors the trunk and extremities
options.B: It has the oldest mean age (mid-70s) and favors the central face
options.C: It is the most aggressive subtype, with frequent bone invasion
options.D: It is essentially never associated with multiple lesions

exp: Superficial BCC has the youngest mean age at diagnosis (mid-50s) — and is the most common subtype in younger age groups — and favors the trunk and extremities, presenting as a well-circumscribed erythematous patch/thin plaque. Its growth is primarily horizontal, but subclinical lateral spread accounts for its notable recurrence rate after routine excision, and multiple lesions are common. The younger age + truncal site distinguish it from face-predominant nodular BCC.
hy: Superficial BCC: youngest (mid-50s), trunk/extremities, horizontal spread → recurs.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter gives a mid-50s mean age and trunk/extremity predilection.
why.B: Wrong — mid-50s is younger than other BCCs, and it favors the trunk, not the central face.
why.C: Wrong — it is indolent/horizontal-growing, not the most aggressive bone-invading subtype (that is morpheaform/infiltrating).
why.D: Wrong — multiple superficial BCCs are commonly present, not essentially never.

stem: A slowly enlarging, ill-defined, scar-like indurated white plaque on the cheek shows strands and cords of basaloid cells in a dense fibrous stroma. Which subtype and behavior is this?

options.A: Morpheaform BCC — biologically more aggressive with extensive local destruction and relative radioresistance
options.B: Superficial BCC — indolent with purely horizontal growth
options.C: Nodular BCC — the least aggressive subtype
options.D: Fibroepithelioma of Pinkus — a benign trichoblastoma variant

exp: A scar-like, ill-defined, indurated white-to-pink plaque with strands/cords of basaloid cells in a dense fibrous stroma is morpheaform (sclerosing/infiltrating) BCC, which behaves more aggressively with extensive local destruction and is relatively radioresistant — the infiltrative cords extend well beyond the clinical border, so margins are deceptive. An elevated pearly border is typically absent. This aggressive, ill-defined behavior is why Mohs is preferred and radiation avoided.
hy: Morpheaform BCC = scar-like, infiltrative cords, aggressive, radioresistant.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — matches the morpheaform subtype's scar-like look and aggressive/radioresistant behavior.
why.B: Wrong — purely horizontal indolent growth describes superficial BCC.
why.C: Wrong — nodular BCC is a pearly nodule and is not the scar-like infiltrative subtype.
why.D: Wrong — fibroepithelioma of Pinkus is a soft lower-back plaque, not an aggressive scar-like cheek lesion.

stem: Fibroepithelioma of Pinkus is a BCC variant. Which description is correct?

options.A: A skin-colored to pink sessile plaque, usually on the lower back, histologically thin anastomosing strands of tumor cells in a fenestrated pattern; some regard it as a trichoblastoma variant
options.B: A pearly nodule of the nose with arborizing telangiectasias and a rolled border
options.C: A pigmented plaque of the trunk arising in arsenic-exposed skin
options.D: A rapidly growing crateriform nodule with a central keratotic core

exp: Fibroepithelioma of Pinkus presents as a skin-colored to pink, soft, sessile plaque (sometimes pedunculated), usually on the lower back, and histologically shows thin anastomosing strands and cords of tumor cells in a fenestrated pattern within a fibrovascular stroma, with peripheral palisading and retraction less prominent than in typical BCC; some experts consider it a trichoblastoma variant. It often coexists with multiple superficial BCCs. The lower-back, fenestrated-strand picture is the giveaway.
hy: Fibroepithelioma of Pinkus = lower-back sessile plaque, fenestrated anastomosing strands; ?trichoblastoma variant.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — matches the chapter's description of fibroepithelioma of Pinkus.
why.B: Wrong — a pearly nasal nodule with arborizing telangiectasia and rolled border is nodular BCC.
why.C: Wrong — a pigmented arsenic-related trunk plaque suggests arsenical SCC in situ/superficial BCC, not Pinkus.
why.D: Wrong — a rapidly growing crateriform nodule with a keratotic core is a keratoacanthoma.

stem: Basosquamous (metatypical) carcinoma has features of both BCC and SCC. Why is it clinically important?

options.A: It behaves more aggressively, like an SCC — with greater likelihood of recurrence and metastasis — and constitutes ~1% of keratinocyte carcinomas
options.B: It is the most indolent BCC variant and never metastasizes
options.C: It is a benign reactive proliferation requiring no treatment
options.D: It is a melanocytic tumor mistaken for BCC

exp: Basosquamous (metatypical, keratotic) carcinoma has histologic features of both BCC and cSCC and tends to behave more like an SCC — a greater likelihood of both recurrence and metastasis — and is estimated to make up ~1% of keratinocyte carcinomas. This aggressive potential means it should be treated like a high-risk tumor rather than a routine BCC. When it metastasizes it may resemble a poorly differentiated SCC.
hy: Basosquamous BCC ~1% of KCs; behaves like SCC (recurs/metastasizes).
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter states it behaves like SCC and is ~1% of keratinocyte carcinomas.
why.B: Wrong — it is among the more aggressive BCC variants, not the most indolent.
why.C: Wrong — it is a malignant tumor requiring aggressive treatment, not a benign reactive process.
why.D: Wrong — it is a keratinocyte (not melanocytic) tumor with mixed basal/squamous features.

stem: Which two histopathologic features are characteristic of BCC and useful for distinguishing it from simulants?

options.A: Peripheral palisading of basaloid nuclei plus retraction (clefting) of the fibromyxoid stroma around the tumor islands
options.B: Keratin pearls plus intercellular bridges throughout the tumor
options.C: Full-thickness epidermal atypia plus diffuse parakeratosis
options.D: A volcano-like crater plus glassy eosinophilic cytoplasm

exp: The two characteristic BCC features are peripheral palisading of the basaloid nuclei at the edge of the tumor islands and retraction (clefting) of the surrounding fibromyxoid stroma away from those islands — the stromal retraction artifact, when present, is especially useful in separating BCC from histologic simulants. The tumor cells have uniform nuclei with scant cytoplasm and indistinct borders. These features anchor the histologic diagnosis.
hy: BCC histology = peripheral palisading + peritumoral stromal retraction (clefts) in fibromyxoid stroma.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — palisading plus stromal retraction clefts are the BCC histologic hallmarks.
why.B: Wrong — keratin pearls and intercellular bridges are features of squamous (SCC) differentiation.
why.C: Wrong — full-thickness atypia with diffuse parakeratosis describes Bowen disease.
why.D: Wrong — a volcano crater with glassy eosinophilic cytoplasm describes keratoacanthoma.

stem: Trichoblastoma can simulate BCC histologically. Which feature favors trichoblastoma over BCC?

options.A: Clefts located within the stroma (not around the tumor islands), follicular germs/papillae, and PHLDA1 positivity
options.B: Retraction clefts immediately around the basaloid islands and absent PHLDA1
options.C: Cribriform architecture with EMA positivity
options.D: CK20-positive punctate cytoplasmic staining

exp: Trichoblastoma — a benign follicular tumor that closely simulates BCC — is distinguished by clefts located within the stroma (rather than as retraction around the basaloid islands as in BCC), the presence of follicular germs and papillae simulating rudimentary hair follicles, and PHLDA1 positivity (PHLDA1 is a marker of epithelial follicular stem cells, stronger/more diffuse in trichoblastoma/trichoepithelioma than in BCC). These cues prevent over-diagnosing BCC.
hy: Trichoblastoma vs BCC: stromal (not peritumoral) clefts, hair germs, PHLDA1+.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — intra-stromal clefts, follicular germs, and PHLDA1 positivity favor trichoblastoma.
why.B: Wrong — peritumoral retraction clefts and absent PHLDA1 describe BCC, not trichoblastoma.
why.C: Wrong — a cribriform, EMA-positive pattern describes adenoid cystic carcinoma.
why.D: Wrong — CK20-positive punctate cytoplasmic staining indicates Merkel cell carcinoma.

stem: What is the dermoscopic hallmark of basal cell carcinoma?

options.A: Well-focused arborizing vessels with foci of microulceration, and an absent pigment network
options.B: A whitish halo surrounding the vessels with surface scale
options.C: A blue–white veil over an irregular pigment network
options.D: A central crater with hairpin vessels (red starburst pattern)

exp: The dermoscopic hallmark of BCC is well-focused arborizing (tree-like branching) vessels together with foci of microulceration; a pigment network is absent, and additional high-specificity clues include blue–gray ovoid nests, multiple blue–gray globules, leaf-like (maple-leaf) structures, and spoke-wheel areas. The sharply focused branching vessels distinguish BCC from the out-of-focus vessels and scale of keratinizing tumors. This pattern underlies dermoscopy's diagnostic benefit in BCC.
hy: BCC dermoscopy = arborizing vessels + microulceration; no pigment network.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — arborizing vessels with microulceration and absent network are the BCC dermoscopic hallmark.
why.B: Wrong — a whitish perivascular halo with scale is the hallmark of keratinizing tumors (SCC/KA).
why.C: Wrong — a blue–white veil over an irregular network suggests melanoma.
why.D: Wrong — a central keratin mass with hairpin vessels (red starburst) is described for AK/keratinizing lesions, not the BCC hallmark.

stem: Multiple hereditary infundibulocystic BCC syndrome has been associated with a germline mutation in which gene?

options.A: SUFU
options.B: TP53
options.C: TGFBR1
options.D: MC1R

exp: Multiple hereditary infundibulocystic BCC syndrome has been associated with a germline mutation in SUFU — a negative regulator of the Hedgehog pathway, the same pathway disrupted (via PTCH1) in Gorlin syndrome. SUFU loss de-represses GLI and activates Hedgehog signaling, producing multiple BCCs. This places it among the Hedgehog-driven BCC syndromes.
hy: Multiple hereditary infundibulocystic BCC syndrome → germline SUFU.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the chapter links this syndrome to germline SUFU mutation.
why.B: Wrong — germline TP53 causes Li–Fraumeni syndrome.
why.C: Wrong — TGFBR1 underlies Ferguson–Smith multiple regressing keratoacanthomas.
why.D: Wrong — MC1R variants relate to red-hair/fair-skin pigmentation risk, not this syndrome.

stem: What surgical margins are recommended for a low-risk cutaneous SCC and for a non-morpheaform BCC <2 cm?

options.A: cSCC: 4–6 mm; non-morpheaform BCC <2 cm: 4 mm (adequate in ~98% of cases)
options.B: cSCC: 1 mm; BCC: 1 mm
options.C: cSCC: 15–20 mm; BCC: 15–20 mm
options.D: cSCC: 4 mm; BCC: no margin needed, curettage only

exp: Low-risk cSCC is excised with a 4–6 mm margin, and a non-morpheaform BCC <2 cm in diameter is adequately removed with a 4 mm margin in ~98% of cases — these margins reflect the typically limited subclinical spread of low-risk tumors. Facial and high-risk lesions need wider margins or Mohs, because subclinical extension is greater. Knowing these standard margins distinguishes routine from high-risk excisions.
hy: Margins: low-risk cSCC 4–6 mm; non-morpheaform BCC <2 cm = 4 mm (98% adequate).
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — matches the chapter's 4–6 mm (cSCC) and 4 mm (BCC <2 cm) margins.
why.B: Wrong — 1 mm margins are inadequate for either tumor.
why.C: Wrong — 15–20 mm is far wider than recommended for low-risk tumors (closer to high-risk BCC European margins).
why.D: Wrong — BCC requires an actual surgical margin, not curettage alone, for standard excision.

stem: For primary BCC, which treatment has the lowest reported 5-year recurrence rate?

options.A: Mohs micrographic surgery (~1%), vs excision 10%, curettage-and-electrodesiccation 8%, radiation 9%, cryotherapy 7.5%
options.B: Cryotherapy (~1%), vs Mohs 10%
options.C: Radiation therapy (~1%), vs Mohs 8%
options.D: Curettage-and-electrodesiccation (~1%), vs excision 7.5%

exp: For primary BCC, Mohs micrographic surgery has the lowest 5-year recurrence (~1%) — complete circumferential peripheral and deep-margin assessment maps the entire margin rather than sampling it — compared with excision 10%, curettage-and-electrodesiccation 8%, radiation 9%, and cryotherapy 7.5% in the cited series. This margin control is why Mohs leads for difficult or recurrence-prone tumors. The advantage is even larger for recurrent BCCs.
hy: Primary-BCC 5-yr recurrence: Mohs 1% < cryo 7.5% < C&E 8% < RT 9% < excision 10%.
src: Bologna Ch108 · Treatment – modality cure rates
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — Mohs ~1% is the lowest, with the comparator rates as listed.
why.B: Wrong — cryotherapy recurrence was 7.5%, not 1%; Mohs is lower, not 10%.
why.C: Wrong — radiation recurrence was ~9%, not 1%.
why.D: Wrong — C&E recurrence was ~8% for primary BCC, not 1%.

stem: For which BCC scenario is Mohs micrographic surgery specifically preferred?

options.A: Morpheaform, poorly demarcated, incompletely removed, or recurrent BCCs, and sites requiring tissue conservation
options.B: Small well-defined nodular BCCs of the trunk in young patients
options.C: Superficial BCCs suitable for topical therapy
options.D: Any BCC for which radiation is also an option, to save cost

exp: Mohs is specifically preferred for morpheaform, poorly demarcated, incompletely removed, or recurrent BCCs, and for sites where tissue conservation and reliable clear margins are paramount — exactly the situations with the most subclinical spread and the highest stakes for recurrence. It is similarly indicated for high-risk primary or recurrent cSCC. Small, well-defined, low-risk truncal tumors do not require Mohs.
hy: Mohs preferred: morpheaform, poorly demarcated, incompletely removed, recurrent BCC; tissue-sparing sites.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — these are the chapter's stated Mohs indications.
why.B: Wrong — small well-defined nodular truncal BCCs are low-risk and do not need Mohs.
why.C: Wrong — superficial BCCs amenable to topical therapy are not the Mohs-preferred scenario.
why.D: Wrong — Mohs is chosen for margin control in high-risk tumors, not merely as a cheaper alternative to radiation.

stem: Radiation therapy should generally be avoided in which situations?

options.A: Verrucous carcinoma (anaplastic transformation), morpheaform BCC (radioresistant), and basal cell nevus syndrome (X-rays → second-PTCH-allele mutation)
options.B: Elderly patients who cannot tolerate surgery
options.C: Small nodular BCCs in poor surgical candidates
options.D: Recurrent cSCC with perineural involvement

exp: Radiation therapy is generally avoided for verrucous carcinoma (risk of anaplastic transformation worsening prognosis), morpheaform BCC (relatively radioresistant), and basal cell nevus syndrome (X-rays can mutate the second PTCH allele and induce further tumors — BCNS patients are exquisitely radiosensitive). For frail patients who cannot tolerate surgery, radiation is in fact a useful option. These three contraindications are the high-yield exceptions.
hy: Avoid RT in: verrucous carcinoma, morpheaform BCC, BCNS.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — these are the chapter's stated radiation contraindications.
why.B: Wrong — radiation is actually advantageous for elderly patients unfit for surgery.
why.C: Wrong — small nodular BCCs in poor surgical candidates are reasonable radiation targets.
why.D: Wrong — radiation is used as adjuvant therapy for high-risk cSCC with perineural involvement, not avoided.

stem: Which mechanism-of-action pairing for an AK topical agent is correct?

options.A: Imiquimod = Toll-like receptor 7 agonist; tirbanibulin = Src-kinase and tubulin-polymerization inhibitor
options.B: Imiquimod = Src-kinase inhibitor; tirbanibulin = Toll-like receptor 7 agonist
options.C: 5-Fluorouracil = Toll-like receptor 7 agonist; diclofenac = thymidylate synthase inhibitor
options.D: Diclofenac = tubulin inhibitor; tirbanibulin = NSAID

exp: Imiquimod is a Toll-like receptor 7 agonist (it stimulates an innate immune/inflammatory response against the lesion), whereas tirbanibulin inhibits Src-kinase signaling and tubulin polymerization (a 1% ointment given for 5 days, complete clearance 44–54%). The other AK agents differ again: 5-FU is a thymidylate-synthase/antimetabolite and diclofenac is an NSAID. Matching each drug to its true mechanism is the testable point.
hy: Imiquimod = TLR7 agonist; tirbanibulin = Src-kinase + tubulin inhibitor.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — imiquimod is a TLR7 agonist and tirbanibulin inhibits Src kinase and tubulin polymerization.
why.B: Wrong — this swaps the two mechanisms.
why.C: Wrong — 5-FU is an antimetabolite (thymidylate synthase), not a TLR7 agonist; diclofenac is an NSAID, not a thymidylate-synthase inhibitor.
why.D: Wrong — diclofenac is an NSAID (not a tubulin inhibitor), and tirbanibulin is the Src/tubulin agent (not an NSAID).

stem: In a randomized controlled trial comparing imiquimod with surgery for BCC, what were the tumor-clearance rates?

options.A: 82.5% with imiquimod vs 98% with surgery
options.B: 98% with imiquimod vs 82.5% with surgery
options.C: 50% with imiquimod vs 75% with surgery
options.D: Equal clearance (~90%) with both

exp: In a randomized controlled trial of 501 BCC patients, tumor clearance was 82.5% with imiquimod versus 98% with surgery — so surgery remained superior, but topical imiquimod (a TLR7 agonist, FDA-approved at 5% for superficial BCC) achieved meaningful clearance in selected superficial tumors. This quantifies the trade-off when avoiding surgery. The gap (≈82.5% vs 98%) is the figure to remember.
hy: BCC clearance: imiquimod 82.5% vs surgery 98%.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the trial gave 82.5% (imiquimod) vs 98% (surgery).
why.B: Wrong — this reverses the values; surgery (not imiquimod) achieved 98%.
why.C: Wrong — imiquimod clearance was much higher than 50%.
why.D: Wrong — clearance was not equal; surgery clearly outperformed imiquimod.

stem: Regarding intralesional therapy of NMSC, which statement is correct?

options.A: Intralesional 5-FU (50 mg/ml) is given at a maximum of 50–100 mg per lesion per session; methotrexate requires checking renal function first
options.B: Intralesional 5-FU has no maximum dose per session
options.C: Methotrexate is injected without any renal assessment
options.D: Both agents are injected intravenously rather than into the lesion

exp: Intralesional 5-FU (50 mg/ml) is given at a maximum of 50–100 mg per lesion per session (weekly for 3–8 treatments), and intralesional methotrexate (25 mg/ml, max 1 ml) requires checking renal function before administration because MTX is renally cleared — both are injected into the base of the lesion until it blanches, with overall response rates ~80%. These are options for selected NMSCs not suited to standard surgery. The dose ceiling and renal check are the practical safety points.
hy: Intralesional 5-FU max 50–100 mg/lesion/session; MTX needs renal-function check.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — matches Table 108.9 dosing and the methotrexate renal precaution.
why.B: Wrong — 5-FU has a stated 50–100 mg per-session maximum.
why.C: Wrong — methotrexate specifically requires a renal-function check before administration.
why.D: Wrong — these are intralesional (injected into the lesion), not intravenous, therapies.

stem: What is first-line systemic therapy for advanced (unresectable/metastatic) cutaneous SCC, and its approximate efficacy?

options.A: Anti-PD-1 antibodies (e.g. cemiplimab), with overall response rates >40% (cemiplimab ~44–45%)
options.B: Hedgehog pathway inhibitors (vismodegib), with response rates ~90%
options.C: BRAF inhibitors (vemurafenib), with response rates ~80%
options.D: Intralesional interferon, with response rates ~99%

exp: First-line systemic therapy for advanced (unresectable or metastatic) cSCC is an anti-PD-1 antibody such as cemiplimab, which releases T-cell checkpoint inhibition against the UV-mutated, neoantigen-rich tumor; overall response rates exceed 40% (cemiplimab ~44% locally advanced / ~45% metastatic). Anti-PD-1 outperforms chemotherapy and tyrosine-kinase inhibitors, and cetuximab (anti-EGFR) is a second-line option. The >40% ORR is the figure to anchor.
hy: Advanced cSCC: anti-PD-1 (cemiplimab) ORR >40% (~44–45%).
src: Bologna Ch108 · Systemic therapy – advanced cSCC
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — anti-PD-1 antibodies (cemiplimab) are first-line with ORR >40%.
why.B: Wrong — hedgehog inhibitors (vismodegib) treat advanced BCC, not cSCC.
why.C: Wrong — BRAF inhibitors treat BRAF-mutant melanoma and actually can induce cSCC.
why.D: Wrong — intralesional interferon (~80% response in selected cases) is not the first-line systemic therapy for advanced cSCC.

stem: Vismodegib and sonidegib are used for advanced BCC. What is their mechanism, and a characteristic side effect?

options.A: They inhibit Smoothened (Hedgehog pathway); dysgeusia occurs in >40% of patients and they are teratogenic
options.B: They block PD-1 on T cells; the main toxicity is immune colitis
options.C: They inhibit EGFR; the main toxicity is an acneiform rash
options.D: They inhibit BRAF; the main toxicity is new keratoacanthomas

exp: Vismodegib and sonidegib inhibit Smoothened (SMO), shutting down the aberrantly activated Hedgehog pathway that drives BCC; characteristic side effects include dysgeusia in >40% of patients (from altered turnover of tongue epithelium), muscle spasms, alopecia, and weight loss, and they are teratogenic (can cause birth defects or fetal death). Adverse effects lead ~30% of patients to discontinue. The SMO mechanism plus dysgeusia/teratogenicity are the high-yield facts.
hy: Vismodegib/sonidegib = SMO (Hedgehog) inhibitors; dysgeusia >40%; teratogenic.
src: Bologna Ch108 · Systemic therapy – advanced BCC + Table 108.10
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — they are Smoothened inhibitors with dysgeusia >40% and teratogenicity.
why.B: Wrong — PD-1 blockade with immune colitis describes checkpoint inhibitors (cemiplimab), not vismodegib.
why.C: Wrong — EGFR inhibition with acneiform rash describes cetuximab.
why.D: Wrong — BRAF inhibition causing keratoacanthomas describes vemurafenib/dabrafenib.

stem: When a locally advanced BCC develops resistance to a hedgehog pathway inhibitor, which FDA-approved option is available, and its approximate efficacy?

options.A: Cemiplimab (anti-PD-1), with an overall response rate of ~31%
options.B: A second hedgehog inhibitor, with response rates ~90%
options.C: Cetuximab (anti-EGFR), with response rates ~80%
options.D: Methotrexate chemotherapy, with response rates ~70%

exp: When a locally advanced BCC becomes resistant to a hedgehog pathway inhibitor, cemiplimab (an anti-PD-1 antibody) is FDA-approved second-line and produced an overall response rate of ~31% in that setting — BCCs carry a high UV-induced mutational/neoantigen burden, providing the rationale for checkpoint blockade. This gives a salvage option after SMO-inhibitor failure. The ~31% ORR is the figure to recall.
hy: Post-hedgehog-inhibitor advanced BCC: cemiplimab (anti-PD-1) ORR ~31%.
src: Bologna Ch108 · Systemic therapy – advanced BCC
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — cemiplimab is FDA-approved after hedgehog-inhibitor therapy, ORR ~31%.
why.B: Wrong — switching to a second hedgehog inhibitor is not the cited ~90%-response option after resistance.
why.C: Wrong — cetuximab (anti-EGFR) is a second-line option for advanced cSCC, not the approved post-hedgehog BCC therapy.
why.D: Wrong — methotrexate chemotherapy is not the FDA-approved second-line therapy described here.

stem: Which preventive statement about keratinocyte carcinoma is correct?

options.A: Consistent sunscreen use decreases the incidence of SCC but has no or only a weak effect on BCC; low-dose acitretin reduces cSCCs and nicotinamide reduces AKs/NMSCs
options.B: Sunscreen reduces BCC but not SCC; acitretin increases cSCCs
options.C: Sunscreen has no effect on either tumor; only oral isotretinoin prevents both
options.D: Nicotinamide reduces melanoma but increases AKs

exp: Consistent sunscreen use has been shown to decrease the incidence of SCC but to have no or only a weak effect on BCC — consistent with SCC being driven by cumulative UV (which sunscreen limits) and BCC by intermittent exposure and other factors. For chemoprophylaxis in high-risk patients, low-dose acitretin reduces the number of cSCCs and nicotinamide reduces AKs and NMSCs (though not in transplant recipients in one study). These differential effects are the prevention high-yields.
hy: Sunscreen ↓SCC but not BCC; acitretin ↓cSCC; nicotinamide ↓AK/NMSC.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — matches the chapter: sunscreen reduces SCC (not BCC), acitretin reduces cSCC, nicotinamide reduces AK/NMSC.
why.B: Wrong — sunscreen reduces SCC (not BCC), and acitretin reduces (not increases) cSCCs.
why.C: Wrong — sunscreen does reduce SCC; the claim of no effect on either is incorrect.
why.D: Wrong — nicotinamide reduces AKs/NMSCs; it is not a melanoma-specific agent that increases AKs.

stem: A 70-year-old man has a slowly enlarging lesion on the infraorbital cheek shown below. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Nodular basal cell carcinoma
options.B: Amelanotic nodular melanoma
options.C: Sebaceous hyperplasia
options.D: Intradermal melanocytic nevus

exp: This is a nodular basal cell carcinoma — the most common BCC subtype (~50%) — presenting as a translucent ("pearly") papulonodule with prominent arborizing telangiectasias and a rolled border; the pearly translucency and surface vessels reflect basaloid tumor nests under a thin epidermis. With time it may ulcerate ("rodent ulcer") but the rolled border persists. The face, especially the cheeks and nose, is the site of predilection.
hy: Nodular BCC = pearly papule + arborizing telangiectasia + rolled border.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma
imgcap: Translucent papulonodule on the cheek

why.A: Correct — pearly translucency, arborizing telangiectasias, and rolled border are classic nodular BCC.
why.B: Wrong — amelanotic melanoma lacks the pearly translucency/arborizing vessels and would more likely show an atypical vascular/pigment pattern.
why.C: Wrong — sebaceous hyperplasia shows yellowish lobules with a central umbilication, not a pearly telangiectatic nodule.
why.D: Wrong — an intradermal nevus is a soft skin-colored papule without telangiectasias or a rolled translucent border.

stem: This man has multiple erythematous patches and thin plaques on the back (he had arsenic exposure decades earlier). What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Superficial basal cell carcinomas
options.B: Guttate psoriasis
options.C: Nummular dermatitis
options.D: Pityriasis rosea

exp: These are superficial basal cell carcinomas — well-circumscribed erythematous patches/thin plaques that favor the trunk and extremities, often multiple, and here associated with prior arsenic exposure (a recognized BCC/SCC risk factor). Superficial BCC has the youngest mean age at diagnosis (mid-50s) and spreads horizontally with subclinical lateral extension. A thin rolled border and fine scale help distinguish it from inflammatory mimics.
hy: Multiple truncal erythematous thin plaques (esp. post-arsenic) = superficial BCCs.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma
imgcap: Numerous erythematous scaly patches on the back

why.A: Correct — multiple well-circumscribed erythematous thin plaques on the trunk, with an arsenic history, are superficial BCCs.
why.B: Wrong — guttate psoriasis produces small droplet papules with silvery scale, typically after streptococcal infection, not arsenic-associated thin plaques.
why.C: Wrong — nummular dermatitis gives intensely pruritic coin-shaped eczematous plaques, not the indolent bordered plaques shown.
why.D: Wrong — pityriasis rosea is a self-limited herald-patch eruption along skin lines, not chronic arsenic-related plaques.

stem: The dermoscopic image below is from a pigmented lesion. Which combination of features points to the diagnosis?

options.A: Blue–gray ovoid nests/globules and arborizing telangiectasias with an absent pigment network — basal cell carcinoma
options.B: A blue–white veil over an atypical pigment network — melanoma
options.C: Milia-like cysts and comedo-like openings — seborrheic keratosis
options.D: A central whitish halo around glomerular vessels — Bowen disease

exp: The dermoscopic hallmark of BCC is well-focused arborizing vessels, and pigmented BCCs additionally show blue–gray ovoid nests, multiple blue–gray globules, leaf-like (maple-leaf) structures, and spoke-wheel areas — crucially with an ABSENT pigment network. Together these features identify a pigmented BCC and help separate it from melanoma. The absence of a pigment network is a key discriminator.
hy: Pigmented-BCC dermoscopy = blue–gray nests/globules + arborizing vessels, NO pigment network.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma
imgcap: Dermoscopic image of a pigmented lesion

why.A: Correct — blue–gray nests/globules with arborizing vessels and no pigment network define pigmented BCC.
why.B: Wrong — a blue–white veil over an atypical pigment network is a melanoma feature; BCC lacks a pigment network.
why.C: Wrong — milia-like cysts and comedo-like openings are seborrheic-keratosis features.
why.D: Wrong — a whitish halo around glomerular vessels is a Bowen-disease/keratinizing-tumor feature, not pigmented BCC.

stem: This H&E section shows a dermal tumor. Which two features identify it?

options.A: Peripheral palisading of basaloid nuclei with retraction (clefting) of the surrounding stroma — basal cell carcinoma
options.B: Keratin pearls and intercellular bridges — squamous cell carcinoma
options.C: Full-thickness epidermal atypia with diffuse parakeratosis — Bowen disease
options.D: A volcano-like keratin-filled crater — keratoacanthoma

exp: This is a basal cell carcinoma: the diagnostic histology is aggregations of basaloid keratinocytes with peripheral palisading of the nuclei and retraction (clefting) of the surrounding fibromyxoid stroma away from the tumor islands. The stromal retraction artifact, when present, is especially useful for distinguishing BCC from its simulants. The cells have uniform nuclei with scant cytoplasm and indistinct borders.
hy: BCC histology = peripheral palisading + peritumoral stromal retraction clefts.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma
imgcap: H&E of a basaloid dermal tumor

why.A: Correct — palisading plus stromal retraction clefts are the histologic hallmarks of BCC.
why.B: Wrong — keratin pearls and intercellular bridges indicate squamous (SCC) differentiation.
why.C: Wrong — full-thickness epidermal atypia with diffuse parakeratosis describes Bowen disease.
why.D: Wrong — a volcano-like keratin-filled crater describes keratoacanthoma.

stem: An elderly woman with severe photodamage has the lesions shown on her forehead. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Actinic keratoses
options.B: Seborrheic keratoses
options.C: Discoid lupus erythematosus
options.D: Flat warts (verruca plana)

exp: These are actinic keratoses — rough erythematous macules/papules with white-to-yellow scale on the most cumulatively sun-exposed skin (forehead, scalp, dorsal hands), here in a woman with severe photodamage. They are partial-thickness atypical keratinocyte proliferations and potential precursors of cSCC. Palpation (a "sandpaper" feel) often detects them better than inspection.
hy: Rough scaly erythematous macules on photodamaged skin = actinic keratoses.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma
imgcap: Multiple rough erythematous scaly macules on the forehead

why.A: Correct — multiple rough scaly erythematous macules on sun-exposed forehead are actinic keratoses.
why.B: Wrong — seborrheic keratoses are waxy "stuck-on" brown papules with comedo-like openings, not rough erythematous macules.
why.C: Wrong — discoid lupus shows scarring plaques with follicular plugging and dyspigmentation, not gritty AKs.
why.D: Wrong — flat warts are smooth skin-colored papules of younger patients, not scaly photodamage-related lesions.

stem: A large, rapidly enlarging fungating nodule developed on the dorsum of the hand in a chronically sun-exposed man. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Invasive cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma
options.B: Pyogenic granuloma
options.C: Nodular basal cell carcinoma
options.D: Dermatofibroma

exp: This is an invasive cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma — a rapidly enlarging, often fungating/papulonodular tumor arising on chronically sun-damaged skin such as the dorsal hand. cSCC can be hyperkeratotic, eroded, or ulcerated, and pain/paresthesia would raise concern for perineural invasion. Unlike BCC, cSCC has meaningful metastatic potential that rises with thickness.
hy: Rapidly growing fungating nodule on sun-damaged skin = invasive cSCC.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma
imgcap: Large fungating nodule on the dorsum of the hand

why.A: Correct — a fungating, rapidly enlarging nodule on photodamaged skin is classic invasive cSCC.
why.B: Wrong — a pyogenic granuloma is a small friable bleeding papule that grows over days, not a large fungating cancerous nodule.
why.C: Wrong — nodular BCC is a pearly telangiectatic nodule, not a fungating keratotic mass.
why.D: Wrong — a dermatofibroma is a small firm dermal papule with a positive dimple sign, not a fungating tumor.

stem: This sharply circumscribed nodule with a central keratotic core grew rapidly over a few weeks on sun-exposed skin. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Keratoacanthoma
options.B: Morpheaform basal cell carcinoma
options.C: Merkel cell carcinoma
options.D: Pyoderma gangrenosum

exp: This is a keratoacanthoma — a sharply circumscribed, crateriform nodule with a central keratotic core that grows rapidly over a few weeks and often involutes over months, considered by some a low-grade/"pseudomalignant" variant of cSCC. It favors the head/neck and sun-exposed extremities. The rapid growth-then-involution and central keratin plug distinguish it from the indolent mimics.
hy: KA = rapidly growing crateriform nodule with central keratotic core, may involute.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma
imgcap: Crateriform nodule with a central keratotic core

why.A: Correct — a crateriform nodule with a keratotic core and rapid growth is a keratoacanthoma.
why.B: Wrong — morpheaform BCC is a slowly enlarging scar-like indurated plaque, not a crateriform nodule.
why.C: Wrong — Merkel cell carcinoma is a rapidly growing violaceous dermal nodule without a keratotic crater.
why.D: Wrong — pyoderma gangrenosum is an ulcer with an undermined violaceous border, not a keratin-cored nodule.

stem: A slowly enlarging exophytic verrucous tumor with crevices is present on the plantar surface of the foot. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Verrucous carcinoma (epithelioma cuniculatum)
options.B: Plantar verruca vulgaris
options.C: Eccrine poroma
options.D: Acral melanoma

exp: This is a verrucous carcinoma — specifically epithelioma cuniculatum, the plantar-foot subtype — a well-differentiated, low-grade variant of SCC presenting as a large, slowly enlarging exophytic warty tumor with rabbit-burrow-like crevices ("cuniculatum"). It has a pushing border, recurs locally, but rarely metastasizes; HPV is often associated. Diagnosis usually needs a large excisional biopsy because cytology is bland.
hy: Plantar exophytic verrucous tumor = verrucous carcinoma (epithelioma cuniculatum).
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma
imgcap: Exophytic verrucous tumor on the plantar foot

why.A: Correct — a plantar exophytic verrucous tumor with crevices is epithelioma cuniculatum, a verrucous carcinoma.
why.B: Wrong — a plantar wart is a smaller, well-demarcated hyperkeratotic papule with thrombosed capillaries, not a large invasive tumor.
why.C: Wrong — an eccrine poroma is a vascular pink papule (often acral) but not a large verrucous burrowing tumor.
why.D: Wrong — acral melanoma is a pigmented macule/nodule with an irregular pattern, not a verrucous exophytic mass.

stem: A fair-skinned, red-haired 8-year-old has multiple 1–3 mm light-brown macules on the cheeks and dorsal forearms that darken each summer and fade in winter. If one were biopsied, which histologic finding would be expected?

options.A: Increased basal-layer melanin with a normal number of melanocytes
options.B: An increased number of singly arranged basal melanocytes without nesting
options.C: Nests of melanocytes at the dermal–epidermal junction
options.D: Dendritic melanocytes dispersed among mid-dermal collagen bundles

exp: Ephelides (freckles) show hyperpigmentation of the basal layer with a NORMAL number of melanocytes; the melanocytes are simply larger, more dendritic, and more DOPA-positive, so they make and transfer more melanin to keratinocytes without proliferating. This is the defining contrast with the other circumscribed pigmented lesions and explains why ephelides darken with sun and fade in winter rather than persisting. Ephelides favor sun-exposed skin in fair, often red-haired individuals (MC1R variants).
hy: Ephelide = ↑basal melanin, melanocyte NUMBER normal (larger/more dendritic cells).
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — basilar hyperpigmentation with no increase in melanocyte number is the histologic hallmark of an ephelide.
why.B: Wrong — a slight increase in singly arranged (non-nested) melanocytes describes lentigo simplex, not an ephelide.
why.C: Wrong — junctional nests define a junctional melanocytic nevus; ephelides have no nesting and no melanocyte proliferation.
why.D: Wrong — dendritic melanocytes scattered in the mid/deep dermis describe dermal melanocytosis (Mongolian spot)/nevus of Ota.

stem: On the dorsal hand of a 68-year-old golfer are two tan macules: one is 2 mm, has been present since early childhood, darkens markedly in summer and fades in winter; the other is 11 mm, first appeared in his 50s, and does not fade in winter. Which statement is correct?

options.A: The small fading macule is an ephelide and the larger persistent one is a solar lentigo
options.B: Both are solar lentigines that differ only in size
options.C: The small macule is a solar lentigo and the larger one is an ephelide
options.D: Both are ephelides; solar lentigines do not occur on the dorsal hands

exp: Per Table 112.1, ephelides appear in early childhood, are 1–5 mm, occur in fair skin, darken in summer and fade in winter and with age; solar lentigines appear by 20–30 years (and later), are 5–15 mm or larger, arise at sites of chronic sun exposure (including the dorsal hands), and persist for life without winter fading. The two key discriminators in this vignette are onset age/size and the seasonal behavior. Both can have moth-eaten borders, so border shape alone does not separate them.
hy: Ephelide: childhood, small, fades in winter; solar lentigo: older, larger, persists, chronic sun sites incl. dorsal hands.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — small + childhood-onset + winter-fading = ephelide; larger + later-onset + non-fading = solar lentigo.
why.B: Wrong — ephelides and solar lentigines differ in onset age, seasonal behavior, and size, not size alone.
why.C: Wrong — this reverses the two: the childhood-onset fading macule is the ephelide.
why.D: Wrong — solar lentigines characteristically DO occur on the dorsal hands (a site of chronic sun exposure).

stem: A healthy 4-year-old has a single, well-circumscribed 3 cm uniformly light-brown macule with a regular border on the trunk and no other abnormalities. Which is the most appropriate counseling point?

options.A: A solitary café-au-lait macule occurs in 10–20% of the normal population and alone does not indicate a genetic disorder
options.B: Even one café-au-lait macule of this size already fulfills a diagnostic criterion for neurofibromatosis type 1
options.C: Café-au-lait macules show an increased melanocyte density, which distinguishes them from ephelides
options.D: Demonstrating melanin macroglobules on biopsy would confirm neurofibromatosis type 1

exp: A single café-au-lait macule (CALM) is found in 10–20% of the normal population, so one typical CALM in an otherwise healthy child does not signal a genetic disorder; it is MULTIPLE CALMs that raise that possibility. NF1 requires ≥6 CALMs (≥5 mm prepubertal or ≥15 mm postpubertal) among other criteria, so one lesion cannot fulfill the criterion. Histologically a CALM is basilar hyperpigmentation WITHOUT increased melanocyte density.
hy: Solitary CALM in 10–20% of normals; ≥6 CALMs needed before invoking NF1.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — a solitary CALM is common (10–20%) and not by itself a marker of disease.
why.B: Wrong — NF1 requires six or more CALMs of defined size; a single CALM does not meet any diagnostic criterion.
why.C: Wrong — CALMs show NO increase in melanocyte density (only increased melanin); ephelides likewise lack a melanocyte increase, so this is not a distinguishing feature.
why.D: Wrong — melanin macroglobules are seen in NF-associated CALMs but are non-specific (also in normal skin and isolated CALMs), so they do not confirm NF1.

stem: A child has a large café-au-lait macule with midline demarcation that is larger and fewer in number than the macules typically seen in neurofibromatosis type 1. Which molecular event most likely underlies this lesion?

options.A: A postzygotic activating mutation in GNAS (encoding Gsα)
options.B: Biallelic inactivation of NF1 in lesional melanocytes
options.C: A germline PTPN11 mutation
options.D: A postzygotic ACTB (β-actin) mutation

exp: In McCune–Albright syndrome the skin contains postzygotic activating mutations in GNAS (encoding Gsα), and its CALMs are characteristically fewer, larger, unilateral, and show midline demarcation — the features described. In neurofibromatosis 1 the CALMs instead carry biallelic NF1 inactivation in melanocytes and tend to be more numerous with smoother borders. Recognizing the GNAS/Gsα association is the high-yield discriminator.
hy: McCune–Albright CALM = postzygotic GNAS (Gsα); fewer/larger/midline. NF1 CALM = biallelic NF1 loss.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — McCune–Albright skin harbors activating GNAS (Gsα) mutations, and its CALMs are larger, fewer, and midline-demarcated.
why.B: Wrong — biallelic NF1 inactivation produces the CALMs of neurofibromatosis 1, which are typically more numerous and not midline-demarcated.
why.C: Wrong — PTPN11 germline mutations cause LEOPARD/Noonan syndrome (multiple lentigines), not McCune–Albright CALMs.
why.D: Wrong — postzygotic ACTB (β-actin) mutations cause Becker melanosis, not café-au-lait macules.

stem: A 15-year-old boy develops a unilateral, block-like hyperpigmented patch on the upper chest that over a year becomes thicker and darker and grows coarse dark hairs. Which statement about this lesion is most accurate?

options.A: It is androgen-dependent, harbors a postzygotic ACTB (β-actin) mutation, and malignant transformation has not been reported
options.B: It carries a lifetime melanoma risk proportional to its surface area
options.C: It results from biallelic NF1 inactivation and predicts neurofibromatosis
options.D: It is driven by an activating GNAQ mutation, as in blue nevi

exp: Becker melanosis is an androgen-dependent organoid hamartoma; the great majority carry a postzygotic ACTB (β-actin) mutation (linked to Hedgehog-pathway activation), which fits its pubertal onset, thickening, and hypertrichosis. It is benign — malignant transformation has not been reported. The lesion is not a melanocytic proliferation (melanin increase without nests), so it carries none of the melanoma risk of a congenital nevus.
hy: Becker = androgen-dependent, ACTB (β-actin) mutation, benign (no reported malignant transformation).
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — ACTB mutation + androgen dependence + benign course is the Becker melanosis profile.
why.B: Wrong — size-proportional lifetime melanoma risk describes large/giant congenital melanocytic nevi, not Becker melanosis.
why.C: Wrong — biallelic NF1 inactivation underlies NF1-associated CALMs, not Becker melanosis.
why.D: Wrong — activating GNAQ mutations drive blue nevi and nevus of Ota, not Becker melanosis.

stem: A girl has a Becker melanosis on the trunk together with ipsilateral breast hypoplasia and a thoracic scoliosis. Which statement best fits this presentation?

options.A: This is Becker nevus syndrome, in which the usual male predominance is reversed and hypoplastic developmental anomalies occur
options.B: Becker melanosis essentially never occurs in females
options.C: The breast hypoplasia is the cutaneous sign of an underlying smooth muscle hamartoma
options.D: These hypoplastic anomalies signal a high risk of malignant transformation of the patch

exp: Becker nevus syndrome refers to Becker melanosis accompanied by hypoplastic developmental anomalies — ipsilateral hypoplasia of the breast/areola/nipple/arm, ipsilateral limb shortening, spina bifida, scoliosis, and pectus. Notably, in patients WITH these anomalies the usual ~6:1 male predominance is reversed to about 2:5 (female predominance). Becker melanosis itself is benign.
hy: Becker nevus syndrome = Becker + ipsilateral hypoplasia (breast/limb), spina bifida, scoliosis; sex ratio reverses (~2:5 F).
src: Bologna Ch112 · Becker Melanosis (Becker nevus syndrome)
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — hypoplastic anomalies define Becker nevus syndrome and the male:female ratio reverses in this subset.
why.B: Wrong — Becker melanosis does occur in females (and is over-represented among those with associated anomalies).
why.C: Wrong — a smooth muscle hamartoma manifests as firmness/perifollicular papules within the patch, not as breast hypoplasia.
why.D: Wrong — Becker melanosis is benign; the associated anomalies do not confer malignant potential.

section: Solar lentigo vs lentigo simplex – genetics
stem: A 70-year-old has an 12 mm medium-brown macule with a moth-eaten border on the dorsal hand and, separately, a 3 mm uniform brown macule on the trunk present since childhood. Which statement about their somatic mutations is correct?

options.A: The dorsal-hand lesion (solar lentigo) often carries FGFR3 or PIK3CA mutations, which are absent in the childhood lesion (lentigo simplex)
options.B: Both lesions carry activating FGFR3/PIK3CA mutations
options.C: Neither carries FGFR3/PIK3CA mutations; both are BRAF-driven
options.D: The childhood lesion carries FGFR3/PIK3CA mutations while the solar lentigo does not

exp: Solar lentigines result from chronic UV exposure and frequently carry somatic activating mutations in FGFR3 or PIK3CA. Lentigo simplex, by contrast, appears at an early age with little relation to sun exposure and specifically LACKS FGFR3/PIK3CA mutations — the molecular point the chapter highlights to separate the two clinically overlapping macules. The dorsal-hand, later-onset, larger lesion is the solar lentigo; the small childhood truncal macule is lentigo simplex.
hy: Solar lentigo = FGFR3/PIK3CA mutations; lentigo simplex lacks them.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — FGFR3/PIK3CA mutations characterize solar lentigines and are absent in lentigo simplex.
why.B: Wrong — lentigo simplex does not carry FGFR3/PIK3CA mutations, so "both" is incorrect.
why.C: Wrong — solar lentigines do carry FGFR3/PIK3CA mutations; they are not BRAF-defined lesions.
why.D: Wrong — this reverses the association; the solar lentigo (not the childhood macule) carries the mutations.

stem: Histology of a 3 mm brown macule shows increased basilar melanin and a slightly increased number of singly arranged melanocytes along the basal layer, without any melanocytic nests. Which diagnosis does this most support?

options.A: Lentigo simplex
options.B: Junctional melanocytic nevus
options.C: Solar lentigo
options.D: Ephelide

exp: Lentigo simplex shows increased basilar melanin plus a slight increase in singly arranged (non-nested) basal melanocytes — the combination in the stem. The defining negative is the ABSENCE of nests, which separates it from a junctional nevus (defined by junctional nests). An ephelide would show no increase in melanocyte number at all, and a solar lentigo shows elongated rete ridges with basilar hyperpigmentation in chronically sun-exposed older skin.
hy: Lentigo simplex = ↑singly arranged melanocytes, NO nests (vs junctional nevus = nests).
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — increased singly arranged melanocytes without nesting is the histology of lentigo simplex.
why.B: Wrong — a junctional melanocytic nevus is defined by junctional nests, which are absent here.
why.C: Wrong — a solar lentigo features elongated rete ridges in sun-damaged skin and carries FGFR3/PIK3CA mutations.
why.D: Wrong — an ephelide shows no increase in melanocyte number, only increased basal melanin.

stem: A 55-year-old has a mottled brown macule with irregular borders and areas devoid of pigment on the hard palate. Why does an oral mucosal melanotic macule warrant careful evaluation and documentation?

options.A: From 30% to 60% of oral-cavity melanomas are preceded by melanosis for months to years
options.B: Oral melanotic macules are obligate precursors that transform to melanoma in the majority of cases
options.C: They are caused by FGFR3 mutations identical to those of solar lentigines
options.D: A palatal melanotic macule is by itself diagnostic of Peutz–Jeghers syndrome

exp: Oral mucosal melanotic macules deserve documentation because 30–60% of oral-cavity melanomas are preceded by melanosis for months to years; an irregular, mottled palatal macule with pigment-devoid areas therefore needs baseline recording and follow-up (biopsy if atypical). They are not, however, obligate precursors — most do not become melanoma. Mucosal macules/lentigo simplex specifically lack the FGFR3 mutations of solar lentigines.
hy: 30–60% of oral melanomas are preceded by melanosis → document/follow mucosal macules.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — the 30–60% antecedent-melanosis figure is why oral melanotic macules are followed.
why.B: Wrong — they are not obligate precursors; the majority do not transform.
why.C: Wrong — lentigo simplex/mucosal macules lack the FGFR3 mutations characteristic of solar lentigines.
why.D: Wrong — a single palatal macule is not diagnostic of Peutz–Jeghers, whose lentigines favor the perioral region, lips, and hands with GI polyposis.

stem: A 25-year-old has lentigines on the lips, buccal mucosa, and fingers plus longitudinal melanonychia. Distinguishing Peutz–Jeghers syndrome from Laugier–Hunziker syndrome depends most on which feature?

options.A: The presence of hamartomatous GI polyps and visceral tumors, which occur in Peutz–Jeghers but not in Laugier–Hunziker
options.B: The anatomic distribution of the mucocutaneous lentigines, which differs markedly between the two
options.C: Longitudinal melanonychia, which occurs only in Peutz–Jeghers
options.D: An autosomal recessive inheritance pattern unique to Laugier–Hunziker

exp: Peutz–Jeghers (STK11) and Laugier–Hunziker have a nearly identical pattern of acromucosal lentigines plus longitudinal melanonychia, so distribution and nail findings do NOT separate them. The discriminator is systemic disease: Peutz–Jeghers has hamartomatous GI polyps (and increased pancreatic/ovarian/testicular tumor risk), whereas Laugier–Hunziker is a benign pigmentary disorder with no consistent internal manifestations.
hy: PJS (STK11) = GI hamartomatous polyps + cancer risk; Laugier–Hunziker = same skin/nail picture but NO internal disease.
src: Bologna Ch112 · Lentigo Simplex / associated syndromes · Table 112.2
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — GI hamartomatous polyps/visceral tumors occur in Peutz–Jeghers but not Laugier–Hunziker.
why.B: Wrong — the lentigine distributions are SIMILAR, which is exactly why the two are confused.
why.C: Wrong — longitudinal melanonychia occurs in BOTH syndromes.
why.D: Wrong — Laugier–Hunziker is not defined by recessive inheritance; the key difference is the absence of internal disease.

stem: A young adult with numerous facial lentigines is found to have cutaneous myxomas, blue nevi, and an atrial myxoma. A mutation in which gene underlies this syndrome?

options.A: PRKAR1A
options.B: STK11
options.C: PTPN11
options.D: PTEN
