exp: Carney complex (also described as the NAME/LAMB syndromes) is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by PRKAR1A mutations, featuring lentigines, mucocutaneous myxomas, (epithelioid) blue nevi, psammomatous melanotic schwannomas, atrial myxoma, pigmented nodular adrenocortical disease, and Sertoli cell tumors — the cutaneous-myxoma + blue-nevus + atrial-myxoma cluster in the stem is classic.
hy: Carney complex (NAME/LAMB) = PRKAR1A; lentigines + myxomas (skin/atrial) + blue nevi.
src: Bologna Ch112 · Lentigo Simplex / Carney complex · Table 112.2
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — PRKAR1A mutations cause Carney complex.
why.B: Wrong — STK11 mutations cause Peutz–Jeghers syndrome.
why.C: Wrong — PTPN11 mutations cause LEOPARD/Noonan syndrome (multiple lentigines).
why.D: Wrong — PTEN mutations cause Cowden disease/Bannayan–Riley–Ruvalcaba syndrome.

stem: A 6-year-old with numerous lentigines present since infancy has ocular hypertelorism and short stature and is diagnosed with LEOPARD syndrome (Noonan syndrome with multiple lentigines). Which associated abnormality is most important to evaluate for?

options.A: ECG conduction defects and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
options.B: Hamartomatous gastrointestinal polyps
options.C: Cutaneous and atrial myxomas
options.D: Renal cell carcinoma

exp: In LEOPARD syndrome the acronym is Lentigines, ECG conduction defects, Ocular hypertelorism, Pulmonary stenosis, Abnormalities of genitalia, Retardation of growth, and Deafness; it is most often due to PTPN11 mutations. The cardiac elements — conduction defects/arrhythmia and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (plus pulmonary stenosis) — are the dangerous associations to screen for. GI polyps, myxomas, and renal cell carcinoma belong to other lentiginosis-associated syndromes.
hy: LEOPARD: screen the heart (conduction defects + HCM + pulmonary stenosis); usually PTPN11.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — conduction defects and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy are the high-risk LEOPARD associations.
why.B: Wrong — hamartomatous GI polyps belong to Peutz–Jeghers syndrome.
why.C: Wrong — cutaneous and atrial myxomas belong to Carney complex.
why.D: Wrong — renal cell carcinoma is associated with BAP1/Birt–Hogg–Dubé-type predispositions, not LEOPARD.

stem: A newborn of East-Asian descent has a 6 cm blue-gray macule over the lumbosacral region. What is the most accurate counseling?

options.A: The blue color is the Tyndall effect of dermal melanin, and most such lesions regress during early childhood
options.B: The lesion is a reliable marker of inflicted injury and warrants a child-protection referral
options.C: The blue color reflects dermal melanocytes that will progress to a blue nevus
options.D: These lesions occur most commonly in fair-skinned White infants

exp: Dermal melanocytosis (Mongolian spot) is a blue-gray lumbosacral patch present at or shortly after birth, most common in Asian infants, that usually regresses during early childhood. The blue color is the Tyndall phenomenon — longer wavelengths are absorbed by deep dermal melanin while shorter (blue) wavelengths are scattered back. Because it can be mistaken for a bruise, documentation matters, but it is a benign birthmark, not evidence of abuse.
hy: Mongolian spot = Tyndall-blue, lumbosacral, Asian infants, regresses in childhood.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — Tyndall effect + early-childhood regression + Asian predilection define dermal melanocytosis.
why.B: Wrong — although it can be MISdiagnosed as a contusion/abuse, it is a benign congenital lesion, not a marker of injury.
why.C: Wrong — it typically regresses rather than progressing to a blue nevus.
why.D: Wrong — it is far more common in Asian (and other darker-skinned) infants than in fair-skinned White infants.

section: Dermal melanocytosis vs nevus of Ota
stem: Which feature best distinguishes classic congenital dermal melanocytosis (Mongolian spot) from nevus of Ota?

options.A: A lumbosacral location with regression in childhood, versus a facial (V1–V2) distribution that persists for life
options.B: A higher density of dermal dendritic melanocytes in dermal melanocytosis than in nevus of Ota
options.C: Scleral involvement, which is characteristic of dermal melanocytosis
options.D: An association with uveal melanoma, which is unique to dermal melanocytosis

exp: Classic dermal melanocytosis sits in the lumbosacral region and regresses during childhood, whereas nevus of Ota occupies the first/second trigeminal (V1–V2) facial distribution, involves the ipsilateral sclera in about two-thirds of cases, persists for life, and carries a (uveal) melanoma risk. Both share dermal dendritic melanocytes histologically, but nevus of Ota has MORE of them than dermal melanocytosis (yet fewer than a blue nevus).
hy: Mongolian spot = lumbosacral, regresses; nevus of Ota = facial V1–V2, scleral, persists, melanoma risk.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — location + natural history cleanly separate the two entities.
why.B: Wrong — nevus of Ota actually has MORE dermal dendritic melanocytes than dermal melanocytosis.
why.C: Wrong — scleral involvement is a feature of nevus of Ota, not dermal melanocytosis.
why.D: Wrong — the uveal-melanoma association belongs to nevus of Ota, not dermal melanocytosis.

stem: A woman has a unilateral blue-gray patch over the forehead, temple, and malar region with ipsilateral scleral pigmentation. Which is the most appropriate management point?

options.A: Ophthalmologic follow-up is warranted because ~10% develop glaucoma and most associated melanomas are uveal
options.B: The lesion will spontaneously regress, so no follow-up is required
options.C: Routine prophylactic excision of the cutaneous patch is recommended
options.D: The greatest melanoma risk is cutaneous, mandating wide local excision of the skin patch

exp: Nevus of Ota persists for life and carries ocular consequences: about 10% develop glaucoma, the sclera is involved in ~two-thirds, and the melanomas that arise are predominantly UVEAL (ocular) rather than cutaneous. The correct management point is therefore ophthalmologic surveillance (and biopsy of any new subcutaneous nodule). The cutaneous patch is treated with Q-switched lasers for cosmesis, not prophylactically excised.
hy: Nevus of Ota → ophthalmology follow-up: ~10% glaucoma, melanoma risk is uveal.
src: Bologna Ch112 · Nevus of Ota – Treatment
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — glaucoma in ~10% and a predominantly uveal melanoma risk mandate ophthalmologic follow-up.
why.B: Wrong — nevus of Ota persists for life and does not spontaneously regress.
why.C: Wrong — prophylactic excision is not standard; lasers are used for cosmesis.
why.D: Wrong — the principal melanoma risk is ocular/uveal, not cutaneous, so wide skin excision is not the answer.

stem: Somatic activating mutations in GNAQ or GNA11 are found in nevus of Ota. Relative to which lesions is the mutation frequency in nevus of Ota comparatively LOW?

options.A: Blue nevi and uveal melanoma, in which GNAQ/GNA11 mutations are far more frequent
options.B: Common acquired melanocytic nevi, which are also predominantly GNAQ-driven
options.C: Spitz nevi, which share an identical GNAQ mutation frequency
options.D: Café-au-lait macules, which are GNAQ-driven

exp: GNAQ/GNA11 activating mutations are found in only up to ~15% of nevi of Ota, compared with the majority of blue nevi (reported at ~50–75% across the chapter, primarily GNAQ) and ~80–85% of primary uveal melanomas — so nevus of Ota has a comparatively LOW frequency among these GNAQ-pathway dermal melanocytic lesions. Common acquired nevi and Spitz nevi do not belong to this group (BRAF/NRAS and kinase fusions/HRAS, respectively), and CALMs are not GNAQ-driven.
hy: GNAQ/GNA11: nevus of Ota ~15% < blue nevi (majority, primarily GNAQ) < uveal melanoma ~80–85%.
src: Bologna Ch112 · Nevus of Ota – Pathogenesis · Table 112.3
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — blue nevi and uveal melanoma have much higher GNAQ/GNA11 mutation frequencies than nevus of Ota.
why.B: Wrong — common acquired nevi are driven by BRAF (≈80%) or NRAS, not GNAQ.
why.C: Wrong — Spitz nevi are driven by kinase fusions or HRAS, not GNAQ, and do not share Ota's frequency.
why.D: Wrong — café-au-lait macules are not GNAQ-driven (NF1/GNAS biology).

section: Hori nevus (acquired nevus of Ota-like macules)
stem: A 45-year-old Chinese woman develops bilateral blue-gray to gray-brown macules over both zygomatic areas in adulthood, with no ocular or mucosal involvement; she was previously treated unsuccessfully for "melasma." What is the most likely diagnosis and its implication?

options.A: Acquired nevus of Ota-like macules (Hori nevus), which—unlike melasma—responds to laser therapy
options.B: Nevus of Ota; ipsilateral scleral involvement will develop over time
options.C: Melasma; continued topical hydroquinone is the best option
options.D: Bilateral dermal melanocytosis that will regress spontaneously

exp: Acquired nevus of Ota-like macules (Hori nevus) are BILATERAL blue-gray to gray-brown macules of the zygomatic area in adult (typically Chinese/Japanese) women, with NO ocular or mucosal involvement — the features that separate it from the unilateral, sclera-involving nevus of Ota. It is frequently misdiagnosed as melasma, but the distinction matters because, unlike melasma, Hori nevus responds to laser surgery.
hy: Hori nevus = bilateral zygomatic, adult women, no eye/mucosa; melasma-mimic that responds to laser.
src: Bologna Ch112 · Nevus of Ota-like macules (Hori nevus)
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — bilateral adult-onset zygomatic macules without ocular involvement = Hori nevus, laser-responsive.
why.B: Wrong — nevus of Ota is unilateral with scleral involvement, not bilateral and eye-sparing.
why.C: Wrong — the lesion is dermal melanocytic (Hori), not melasma; hydroquinone is ineffective.
why.D: Wrong — dermal melanocytosis is a lumbosacral lesion of infancy that regresses, not adult bilateral facial macules.

stem: A patient has a unilateral blue-gray patch over the shoulder extending onto the lateral upper trunk, in the distribution of the lateral cutaneous brachial and posterior supraclavicular nerves, with histology identical to nevus of Ota. What is the diagnosis?

options.A: Nevus of Ito
options.B: Nevus of Ota
options.C: Becker melanosis
options.D: Plaque-type blue nevus

exp: Nevus of Ito is a dermal melanocytosis of the shoulder/lateral upper trunk corresponding to the lateral cutaneous brachial and posterior supraclavicular nerves; its clinical appearance and histology are identical to nevus of Ota, differing only in location. Nevus of Ota occupies the face (V1–V2). Becker melanosis is a brown hypertrichotic patch (not blue), and a plaque blue nevus is a discrete pigmented plaque rather than this nerve-distributed patch.
hy: Nevus of Ito = shoulder/lateral upper trunk (brachial/supraclavicular nerves); Ota histology.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — the shoulder/upper-trunk nerve distribution with Ota-like histology is nevus of Ito.
why.B: Wrong — nevus of Ota is on the face in the trigeminal V1–V2 distribution.
why.C: Wrong — Becker melanosis is a brown, hypertrichotic patch, not a blue dermal melanocytosis.
why.D: Wrong — a plaque blue nevus is a localized pigmented plaque, not a patch following these cutaneous nerves.

stem: A child with several typical café-au-lait macules also has a few patches that are notably darker ("café-noir") than the others. In which two syndromes are café-noir macules described?

options.A: LEOPARD (Noonan with multiple lentigines) and Carney complex
options.B: Peutz–Jeghers and Laugier–Hunziker syndromes
options.C: McCune–Albright syndrome and neurofibromatosis type 1
options.D: Xeroderma pigmentosum and Cowden disease

exp: Café-noir macules are hyperpigmented patches even darker than ordinary CALMs and are described in LEOPARD syndrome (Noonan with multiple lentigines) and Carney complex. They serve as a clue that a child with "CALMs" may actually have one of these lentiginosis-associated syndromes rather than neurofibromatosis.
hy: Café-noir (darker than CALM) → LEOPARD and Carney complex.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — café-noir macules are reported in LEOPARD syndrome and Carney complex.
why.B: Wrong — Peutz–Jeghers and Laugier–Hunziker are characterized by mucocutaneous lentigines, not café-noir patches.
why.C: Wrong — McCune–Albright and NF1 produce café-au-lait (not café-noir) macules.
why.D: Wrong — xeroderma pigmentosum (photodistributed lentigines/cancers) and Cowden disease are not the café-noir syndromes.

section: Primary acquired melanosis of the conjunctiva
stem: Regarding primary acquired melanosis (PAM) of the conjunctiva, which statement is correct?

options.A: PAM with atypia can progress to melanoma, whereas PAM without atypia generally does not
options.B: PAM is almost always bilateral and symmetric
options.C: PAM with atypia regresses spontaneously and requires no follow-up
options.D: PAM is reliably distinguished from a conjunctival nevus by being thicker and more circumscribed

exp: Primary acquired melanosis (PAM) of the conjunctiva is nearly always unilateral and is divided into PAM without atypia and PAM with atypia; only the atypical form can progress to conjunctival melanoma, so it requires monitoring/treatment. It must be distinguished from conjunctival nevi (which are thicker and more circumscribed) and from complexion-associated melanosis (bilateral, perilimbal).
hy: PAM: unilateral; PAM-with-atypia can → melanoma, PAM-without-atypia generally does not.
src: Bologna Ch112 · Mucosal Melanotic Macules / PAM
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — the atypia subset of PAM carries the melanoma risk; the non-atypical subset generally does not.
why.B: Wrong — PAM is nearly always UNILATERAL; bilateral perilimbal pigment is complexion-associated melanosis.
why.C: Wrong — PAM with atypia can progress to melanoma and must be followed, not ignored.
why.D: Wrong — it is the conjunctival NEVUS that is thicker and circumscribed; PAM is flat and diffuse.

stem: A teenager presents with numerous lentigines distributed over both sun-exposed and sun-protected skin, including the palms and genitalia. Beyond reassurance, what is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Investigate for an associated systemic disorder (a lentiginosis syndrome)
options.B: Recommend daily broad-spectrum sunscreen, since these lentigines are UV-driven
options.C: Excise several representative lesions to exclude melanoma
options.D: Begin topical hydroquinone, which reliably clears lentigo simplex

exp: Multiple/generalized lentigines distributed over sun-exposed AND sun-protected skin (including palms and genitalia) should prompt investigation for an underlying systemic disorder, because such patterns are associated with the lentiginosis syndromes summarized in Table 112.2 (e.g. LEOPARD, Carney complex, Peutz–Jeghers). Lentigo simplex has little relation to sun, so sunscreen will not address it, and benign-appearing lentigines do not require excision or hydroquinone.
hy: Generalized/sun-protected-site lentigines → work up for a lentiginosis syndrome (Table 112.2).
src: Bologna Ch112 · Lentigo Simplex – Treatment · Table 112.2
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — widespread lentigines, especially at sun-protected sites, warrant evaluation for systemic syndromes.
why.B: Wrong — lentigo simplex is largely sun-independent, so sunscreen is not the key step.
why.C: Wrong — benign-appearing lentigo simplex does not require excision; only atypical acral/mucosal lesions are biopsied.
why.D: Wrong — hydroquinone does not reliably clear lentigo simplex and misses the point (excluding a syndrome).

section: Becker melanosis – associated smooth muscle hamartoma
stem: A Becker melanosis on a patient's trunk is firm to palpation and shows perifollicular papules. What does this finding most likely indicate?

options.A: A coexistent smooth muscle hamartoma
options.B: Early malignant transformation of the lesion
options.C: An underlying plexiform neurofibroma
options.D: Secondary bacterial folliculitis within the patch

exp: Within a Becker melanosis, firmness to palpation and perifollicular papules point to a coexistent smooth muscle hamartoma (proliferation of arrector pili muscle), which is commonly but not invariably associated. Becker melanosis itself is benign, so these findings do not indicate transformation; a plexiform neurofibroma is a separate (often softer, "bag of worms") lesion, and folliculitis would be inflammatory/pustular rather than firm papular.
hy: Firmness + perifollicular papules in a Becker = associated smooth muscle hamartoma.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — firmness and perifollicular papules reflect an associated smooth muscle (arrector pili) hamartoma.
why.B: Wrong — Becker melanosis is benign; malignant transformation has not been reported.
why.C: Wrong — a plexiform neurofibroma is a distinct soft "bag of worms" tumor, not the cause of these findings within a Becker.
why.D: Wrong — folliculitis is an inflammatory/pustular process, not firm perifollicular papules with a smooth muscle component.

stem: A teenage boy has the unilateral lesion of the upper trunk shown below, which thickened and grew coarse hair during adolescence. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Becker melanosis (nevus)
options.B: Large café-au-lait macule
options.C: Congenital melanocytic nevus
options.D: Plexiform neurofibroma

exp: The image shows a unilateral hyperpigmented patch of the upper trunk with overlying coarse hypertrichosis — the hallmark of Becker melanosis, an androgen-dependent hamartoma that thickens and grows hair during adolescence in boys. A café-au-lait macule is a flat tan patch WITHOUT hypertrichosis; a congenital melanocytic nevus is usually present from birth and is a true nevus (often raised, can be hairy but typically more sharply nevoid); a plexiform neurofibroma is a soft "bag of worms" subcutaneous tumor. The combination of an acquired hypertrichotic block-like truncal patch in a teenage boy is specific for Becker.
hy: Unilateral hypertrichotic truncal patch in an adolescent boy = Becker melanosis.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions
imgcap: Unilateral hyperpigmented patch with overlying coarse hair on the trunk

why.A: Correct — a unilateral hypertrichotic hyperpigmented truncal patch with pubertal onset is Becker melanosis.
why.B: Wrong — a café-au-lait macule is a flat tan patch without the prominent overlying coarse hair seen here.
why.C: Wrong — a congenital melanocytic nevus is present from birth and is a true (often more raised/nevoid) melanocytic lesion.
why.D: Wrong — a plexiform neurofibroma is a soft, compressible subcutaneous "bag of worms," not a flat hairy pigmented patch.

stem: This adult has the unilateral facial finding shown below, including pigmentation of the ipsilateral sclera. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Nevus of Ota
options.B: Melasma
options.C: Dermal melanocytosis (Mongolian spot)
options.D: Nevus of Ito

exp: The photograph shows unilateral blue-gray discoloration of the periorbital/malar skin WITH ipsilateral scleral pigmentation — diagnostic of nevus of Ota (oculodermal melanocytosis) in the trigeminal V1–V2 distribution. Melasma is bilateral, brown (not blue-gray), and spares the sclera; dermal melanocytosis (Mongolian spot) is a lumbosacral lesion of infancy; nevus of Ito occupies the shoulder/upper trunk, not the face. The scleral involvement is the decisive clue.
hy: Unilateral facial blue-gray patch + scleral pigment = nevus of Ota.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions
imgcap: Unilateral blue-gray discoloration of the periorbital and malar skin with scleral pigmentation

why.A: Correct — unilateral facial blue-gray pigmentation with scleral involvement is nevus of Ota.
why.B: Wrong — melasma is bilateral, brown, and does not involve the sclera.
why.C: Wrong — dermal melanocytosis (Mongolian spot) occurs in the lumbosacral region of infants, not the face.
why.D: Wrong — nevus of Ito has the identical appearance but is located on the shoulder/lateral upper trunk.

stem: A 70-year-old has the lesions shown below on chronically sun-exposed skin of the dorsal forearm. What are they?

options.A: Solar lentigines
options.B: Ephelides (freckles)
options.C: Café-au-lait macules
options.D: Junctional melanocytic nevi

exp: The image shows numerous tan-to-brown macules, several with irregular borders, on chronically sun-damaged skin of an older adult — typical solar lentigines. Ephelides are smaller (1–5 mm), appear in childhood, and fade in winter; café-au-lait macules are larger uniform patches usually present from early life and are typically solitary or few; junctional nevi are discrete nests-bearing lesions, not this confluent field of sun-induced macules. The age, chronic-sun site, and lesion size point to solar lentigines.
hy: Multiple tan macules on chronically sun-exposed older skin = solar lentigines.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions
imgcap: Numerous tan-to-brown macules, some with irregular borders, on sun-damaged skin

why.A: Correct — multiple brown macules on sun-damaged skin of an older adult are solar lentigines.
why.B: Wrong — ephelides are smaller, childhood-onset, and fade seasonally; they do not dominate aged sun-damaged skin like this.
why.C: Wrong — café-au-lait macules are larger uniform patches, usually solitary or few, not a field of small macules.
why.D: Wrong — junctional melanocytic nevi are discrete nevomelanocytic lesions, not UV-induced lentigines.

stem: A child has the lesion shown below: a uniformly tan patch with a fairly regular geographic border and no overlying hair or texture change. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Café-au-lait macule
options.B: Becker melanosis
options.C: Nevus spilus
options.D: Postinflammatory hyperpigmentation

exp: The image shows a uniformly light-brown ("coffee-with-milk") geographic patch with a fairly well-defined border and no surface change or hair — a café-au-lait macule. Becker melanosis would show hypertrichosis and develop in adolescence; a nevus spilus has darker "speckles" (macules/papules) superimposed on the tan background; postinflammatory hyperpigmentation follows a preceding dermatosis and conforms to its distribution. The uniform color without speckles or hair identifies the CALM.
hy: Uniform tan geographic patch, no hair/speckles = café-au-lait macule.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions
imgcap: Uniformly light-brown geographic patch with a well-defined border

why.A: Correct — a uniform tan patch with a regular geographic border and no hair/speckling is a café-au-lait macule.
why.B: Wrong — Becker melanosis is hypertrichotic and adolescent-onset.
why.C: Wrong — a nevus spilus has darker speckled macules/papules on the tan background, which are absent here.
why.D: Wrong — postinflammatory hyperpigmentation follows a prior inflammatory dermatosis and its distribution, not a congenital geographic patch.

stem: A young adult with a history of recurrent small-bowel intussusception from hamartomatous polyps has the mucocutaneous finding shown below. Which syndrome does this picture most support?

options.A: Peutz–Jeghers syndrome
options.B: Laugier–Hunziker syndrome
options.C: Addison disease
options.D: Carney complex

exp: Multiple dark macules concentrated on the vermilion lips and perioral skin, in a patient with hamartomatous small-bowel polyps causing intussusception, are diagnostic of Peutz–Jeghers syndrome (STK11). Laugier–Hunziker produces an almost identical mucocutaneous lentigine pattern but has NO internal/GI disease — so the polyp history is the discriminator. Addison disease causes diffuse mucosal/skin hyperpigmentation (not discrete lentigines) with systemic features, and Carney complex lentigines favor the broader face with myxomas/endocrine tumors.
hy: Perioral/labial lentigines + GI hamartomatous polyps = Peutz–Jeghers (STK11).
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions
imgcap: Multiple dark macules on the vermilion lips and perioral skin

why.A: Correct — labial/perioral lentigines plus GI hamartomatous polyps define Peutz–Jeghers syndrome.
why.B: Wrong — Laugier–Hunziker gives the same lip lentigines but lacks the GI polyps/visceral disease that the history provides.
why.C: Wrong — Addison disease causes diffuse hyperpigmentation, not discrete labial lentigines.
why.D: Wrong — Carney complex lentigines are more broadly distributed on the face and accompany myxomas/endocrine tumors, not small-bowel hamartomatous polyps.

stem: A young adult with the facial pigmentation shown below is found to have a cardiac myxoma and cutaneous myxomas. Which syndrome does this picture most support?

options.A: Carney complex
options.B: LEOPARD syndrome (Noonan with multiple lentigines)
options.C: Ephelides (freckles)
options.D: Peutz–Jeghers syndrome

exp: Numerous lentigines concentrated on the central face in a patient with cardiac and cutaneous myxomas indicate Carney complex (PRKAR1A; also called NAME/LAMB), which combines lentigines, mucocutaneous myxomas, blue nevi, atrial myxoma, and endocrine tumors. LEOPARD/Noonan with multiple lentigines features cardiac conduction defects/pulmonary stenosis (not myxomas); ephelides are sun-induced freckles without systemic disease; Peutz–Jeghers lentigines favor the lips/perioral region with GI polyps. The myxoma association is the decisive clue.
hy: Facial lentigines + cardiac/cutaneous myxomas = Carney complex (PRKAR1A).
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions
imgcap: Numerous small lentigines concentrated on the central face/midface

why.A: Correct — lentigines with cardiac and cutaneous myxomas define Carney complex.
why.B: Wrong — LEOPARD syndrome features conduction defects/pulmonary stenosis, not myxomas.
why.C: Wrong — ephelides are benign sun-induced freckles with no systemic associations.
why.D: Wrong — Peutz–Jeghers lentigines favor the lips/perioral skin and accompany GI hamartomatous polyps, not myxomas.

stem: A 1.5 cm firm blue-gray nodule on the buttock of a young woman is excised; a sentinel-type node sampled nearby contains small bland nests of blue-nevus cells in the capsule. Which statement is correct?

options.A: Such nodal blue-nevus deposits ("benign metastasis") are a recognized finding in cellular blue nevus and do not by themselves indicate malignancy
options.B: Any nodal melanocytic deposit defines metastatic melanoma and mandates completion lymphadenectomy
options.C: Cellular blue nevi essentially never involve regional lymph nodes
options.D: Nodal involvement here proves the lesion is an atypical cellular blue nevus

exp: Cellular blue nevi occasionally deposit bland nests of blue-nevus cells in the marginal sinus or capsule of the draining node — a passively transported or arrested-migration phenomenon termed "benign metastasis" that does NOT signify malignancy. Cellular blue nevi are larger (1–2 cm), denser tumors than common blue nevi, typically on the buttocks/sacrococcygeal area/scalp. The decisive point is that bland nodal blue-nevus cells in this setting are a recognized benign finding.
hy: Cellular blue nevus can show bland nodal "benign metastasis" — not melanoma.
src: Bologna Ch112 · Blue Nevus – Cellular blue nevus
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — bland nodal blue-nevus deposits are a described benign finding in cellular blue nevus.
why.B: Wrong — not every nodal melanocytic deposit is melanoma; bland blue-nevus cells here are benign, so reflex lymphadenectomy is not indicated.
why.C: Wrong — cellular blue nevi CAN involve regional nodes (the very point of the question).
why.D: Wrong — atypical cellular blue nevus is defined by atypical histologic features (size/atypia/mitoses/necrosis), not merely by nodal deposits.

stem: Which combination of features distinguishes an ATYPICAL cellular blue nevus (with potential for nodal metastasis or progression to melanoma) from a conventional cellular blue nevus?

options.A: Large size (>1–2 cm), asymmetry, ulceration, cytologic atypia, mitoses, and necrosis
options.B: Homogeneous blue color and a diameter under 1 cm
options.C: A dome-shaped silhouette with peripheral junctional nests and Kamino bodies
options.D: Heavy dermal melanin with abundant melanophages and a sclerotic stroma

exp: Atypical cellular blue nevi show one or more worrisome features versus conventional cellular blue nevi — larger size (>1–2 cm), asymmetry, ulceration, infiltrative growth, cytologic atypia, mitoses, and necrosis — and these lesions have been associated with lymph-node metastases and evolution to melanoma, so they are completely excised. A homogeneous small blue lesion is the conventional (low-concern) end of the spectrum, while peripheral junctional nests with Kamino bodies describe a Spitz nevus, and heavy dermal melanin with melanophages and sclerosis is the look of an ordinary common blue nevus.
hy: Atypical cellular blue nevus = >1–2 cm + asymmetry/ulceration/atypia/mitoses/necrosis → excise.
src: Bologna Ch112 · Blue Nevus – atypical cellular blue nevus
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — these are the atypia criteria the chapter lists for cellular blue nevus.
why.B: Wrong — small homogeneous blue color is the conventional (reassuring) phenotype, not the atypical one.
why.C: Wrong — dome shape with peripheral junctional nests and Kamino bodies describes a Spitz nevus.
why.D: Wrong — heavy dermal melanin with melanophages and a sclerotic stroma is the histology of an ordinary common blue nevus.

stem: A rapidly growing 2.5 cm nodule develops within a long-standing blue lesion on the scalp of a 55-year-old. Melanoma arising in a blue nevus (MBN) most characteristically has which profile?

options.A: Arises in a pre-existing cellular blue nevus and carries additional BAP1 or SF3B1 mutations on top of the GNAQ/GNA11 of the blue nevus
options.B: Arises from a common (non-cellular) blue nevus and is uniformly indolent
options.C: Is defined by an activating BRAF V600E mutation, as in superficial spreading melanoma
options.D: Occurs almost exclusively in children and never metastasizes

exp: Melanoma arising in a blue nevus (MBN) nearly always arises in a pre-existing CELLULAR blue nevus, classically as a rapidly growing >2 cm nodule on the scalp of an adult >45 years; it is aggressive, metastasizing to regional nodes and liver. Molecularly it shows the GNAQ/GNA11 mutation of the underlying blue nevus PLUS additional driver aberrations in BAP1 or SF3B1, which is the genetic signature of progression. Common (non-cellular) blue nevi rarely transform, and BRAF V600E defines superficial spreading melanoma, not MBN.
hy: MBN = cellular blue nevus, scalp, >45 yr, >2 cm; GNAQ/GNA11 + BAP1 or SF3B1.
src: Bologna Ch112 · Blue Nevus – melanoma arising from blue nevus
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — MBN arises in a cellular blue nevus and adds BAP1/SF3B1 to the GNAQ/GNA11 background.
why.B: Wrong — it arises from the cellular (not common) blue nevus and is aggressive, not indolent.
why.C: Wrong — BRAF V600E is the driver of superficial spreading melanoma, not melanoma in blue nevus.
why.D: Wrong — MBN occurs in older adults and metastasizes; it is not a childhood, non-metastasizing tumor.

stem: A pigmented epithelioid melanocytoma (PEM; formerly "epithelioid blue nevus" / "animal-type melanoma") is best characterized by which statement?

options.A: It is characterized by PRKAR1A inactivation and frequently spreads to regional nodes yet is almost never fatal
options.B: It is a uniformly benign nevus that never involves lymph nodes
options.C: It is an aggressive melanoma subtype with a high mortality rate
options.D: It is defined by biallelic BAP1 loss and an epithelioid morphology

exp: Pigmented epithelioid melanocytoma (PEM) — which absorbed the old terms "epithelioid blue nevus" and "animal-type melanoma" — is characterized by loss/inactivation of PRKAR1A and is composed of heavily pigmented epithelioid and spindled melanocytes. It behaves as a low-grade tumor: it frequently metastasizes to regional lymph nodes but rarely spreads beyond them and is almost never fatal. It was first described within Carney complex but also occurs sporadically.
hy: PEM = PRKAR1A-inactivated; frequent nodal spread but almost never fatal.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — PRKAR1A inactivation with frequent nodal but rarely fatal behavior defines PEM.
why.B: Wrong — PEM does involve regional nodes; it is not a node-sparing benign nevus.
why.C: Wrong — despite the old "animal-type melanoma" label, PEM is indolent and rarely fatal, not a high-mortality melanoma.
why.D: Wrong — biallelic BAP1 loss defines the BAP1-inactivated melanocytic nevus, not PEM.

stem: What is the most common initiating oncogenic event in a common acquired melanocytic nevus?

options.A: An activating BRAF mutation (≈80%)
options.B: An activating NRAS mutation (≈80%)
options.C: A GNAQ mutation
options.D: An NTRK3 gene fusion

exp: In common acquired melanocytic nevi the initiating oncogenic event is usually an activating BRAF mutation (~80%), with NRAS mutations in a smaller fraction (~5%); these MAPK-activating mutations drive a clonal melanocytic proliferation that then typically senesces. GNAQ drives blue nevi/nevus of Ota and NTRK3 fusions drive Reed/spindle-cell nevi, so neither is the common-nevus driver.
hy: Common acquired nevus = BRAF (~80%) > NRAS (~5%).
src: Bologna Ch112 · Common Acquired Nevi – Pathogenesis
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — BRAF mutation (~80%) is the dominant driver of common acquired nevi.
why.B: Wrong — NRAS accounts for only ~5%, not ~80%, of common acquired nevi.
why.C: Wrong — GNAQ drives blue nevi and nevus of Ota, not common acquired nevi.
why.D: Wrong — NTRK3 fusions drive pigmented spindle cell (Reed) nevi, not common acquired nevi.

stem: A 22-year-old worries that his number of moles has increased through his teens and 20s. Based on the natural history of acquired melanocytic nevi, what is the most accurate counseling?

options.A: Nevus counts typically peak in the third decade and then gradually decline with age
options.B: Nevus counts rise continuously throughout life, peaking in old age
options.C: Nevi present in infancy and steadily disappear through childhood
options.D: The total number of nevi has no relationship to melanoma risk

exp: Acquired melanocytic nevi are few in early childhood, increase through adolescence and reach a peak in the third decade, then gradually decline in number with age (e.g. mean counts fall markedly by the seventh decade). So a rising mole count in the 20s is expected. Although total nevus count DOES correlate with melanoma risk, the natural history itself is reassuring; nevi do not rise continuously into old age, nor do they simply disappear through childhood.
hy: Nevus count peaks in the 3rd decade, then declines with age.
src: Bologna Ch112 · Common Acquired Nevi – Epidemiology
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — nevus counts peak in the third decade and then decline with age.
why.B: Wrong — counts decline after the third decade rather than rising into old age.
why.C: Wrong — only a few nevi are present in early childhood; they increase, not disappear, toward puberty.
why.D: Wrong — total nevus count does correlate with melanoma risk (even though the natural history is benign).

section: Junctional vs compound vs intradermal nevus
stem: A skin-colored to light-brown soft papule is biopsied and shows nests of melanocytes confined to the dermis with no junctional component. Which clinicopathologic type is this?

options.A: Intradermal nevus
options.B: Junctional nevus
options.C: Compound nevus
options.D: Blue nevus

exp: An intradermal nevus has its melanocytic nests entirely within the dermis with no junctional (dermal–epidermal) component, and presents clinically as a soft, often skin-colored to light-brown papule. A junctional nevus is a macule with nests confined to the dermal–epidermal junction; a compound nevus has both junctional and dermal components; a blue nevus is composed of dendritic dermal melanocytes producing a blue papule.
hy: Intradermal nevus = dermal-only nests = soft papule.
src: Bologna Ch112 · Common Acquired Nevi – Clinical/Pathology
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — dermal-only nests with no junctional component define an intradermal nevus.
why.B: Wrong — a junctional nevus is a macule with nests at the dermal–epidermal junction.
why.C: Wrong — a compound nevus has BOTH junctional and dermal components.
why.D: Wrong — a blue nevus is composed of dendritic dermal melanocytes (Tyndall-blue), not ordinary nevus nests.

stem: A renal-transplant patient on azathioprine, and separately a melanoma patient started on a BRAF inhibitor, each develop a sudden crop of new melanocytic nevi. Which statement is correct?

options.A: Both systemic immunosuppression and BRAF-inhibitor therapy are recognized triggers of eruptive melanocytic nevi
options.B: Eruptive nevi occur only after intense sun exposure, not with drugs
options.C: Immunosuppression suppresses nevogenesis, so new nevi argue against it
options.D: BRAF inhibitors prevent new nevi by blocking the MAPK pathway

exp: Both systemic immunosuppression (chemotherapy, organ transplantation/azathioprine, HIV) and BRAF-inhibitor therapy are recognized triggers for the development — sometimes eruptive — of melanocytic nevi (Table 112.5), along with UV exposure, blistering processes, and pregnancy. Immunosuppression promotes rather than suppresses nevogenesis, and BRAF inhibitors paradoxically induce new nevi (and keratoacanthomas) through paradoxical MAPK activation in BRAF-wild-type melanocytes.
hy: Eruptive-nevi triggers: immunosuppression, BRAF inhibitors, UV, blistering, pregnancy (Table 112.5).
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — immunosuppression and BRAF inhibitors are both listed triggers of (eruptive) nevi.
why.B: Wrong — eruptive nevi are triggered by several non-UV factors, including drugs and immunosuppression.
why.C: Wrong — immunosuppression promotes nevogenesis; new nevi are consistent with it, not against it.
why.D: Wrong — BRAF inhibitors paradoxically INDUCE new nevi rather than preventing them.

stem: A child with recessive dystrophic epidermolysis bullosa develops large (up to 3 cm), asymmetric, irregularly bordered dark nevi at sites of prior blisters. What is the best interpretation?

options.A: These are "EB nevi" — benign nevi arising at sites of antecedent bullae that can clinically and histologically mimic melanoma
options.B: These are de novo melanomas that have arisen because of the EB
options.C: These are congenital melanocytic nevi unrelated to the blistering
options.D: These lesions exclude melanoma and never require any monitoring

exp: "EB nevi" are distinctive melanocytic nevi that arise at the sites of antecedent bullae/erosions, chiefly in recessively inherited epidermolysis bullosa; they can be strikingly large (up to 3 cm), asymmetric, irregularly bordered, dark, and even show satellitosis — closely mimicking melanoma clinically and histologically — yet they are benign (best regarded as exaggerated recurrent/persistent nevi). Because melanoma can rarely arise in EB, longitudinal observation is still warranted.
hy: EB nevi = benign nevi at sites of prior blisters; melanoma mimics needing monitoring.
src: Bologna Ch112 · Common Acquired Nevi – EB nevi
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — EB nevi arise at sites of bullae and are benign melanoma mimics.
why.B: Wrong — these are benign nevi, not de novo melanomas, although melanoma can rarely arise in EB.
why.C: Wrong — they are acquired at blister sites, not congenital.
why.D: Wrong — because they mimic melanoma and melanoma can rarely arise in EB, monitoring is advised.

stem: A vulvar nevus removed from a young woman shows architectural disorder, large junctional nests along the sides of rete ridges, and dermal lamellar fibroplasia. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Nevi of special sites (genital, scalp, ear, nipple, flexural, acral) can show atypical features overlapping with dysplastic nevi and melanoma yet behave benignly in young patients
options.B: Any architectural disorder at a special site is diagnostic of melanoma
options.C: Special-site nevi are histologically identical to ordinary acquired nevi
options.D: These atypical features confirm a congenital melanocytic nevus

exp: Nevi of "special sites" (genitalia, scalp, ear, nipple, umbilicus, flexural, acral) characteristically show architectural and cytologic features that OVERLAP with dysplastic nevi and even melanoma — asymmetry, large/irregular junctional nests including along the sides of rete ridges, discohesion, and lamellar fibroplasia — yet behave benignly, particularly in young patients. Recognizing this "special-site" pattern prevents over-diagnosis of melanoma. Melanoma can still occur at these sites (especially in older adults), so context matters.
hy: Special-site nevi mimic dysplastic nevi/melanoma histologically but are benign in young patients.
src: Bologna Ch112 · Genital Nevus and Nevi of Special Sites
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — special-site nevi show melanoma-overlapping atypia yet are benign in young patients.
why.B: Wrong — architectural disorder at a special site is expected and not by itself diagnostic of melanoma.
why.C: Wrong — special-site nevi specifically differ from ordinary acquired nevi by these atypical features.
why.D: Wrong — these features describe a special-site nevus, not a congenital melanocytic nevus.

stem: A pigmented macule on the sole shows, on dermoscopy, parallel bands of pigment running along the furrows (sulci) of the skin markings. A second sole lesion shows pigment on the ridges. Which interpretation is correct?

options.A: Pigment along the furrows (parallel-furrow pattern) favors a benign acral nevus, whereas pigment on the ridges (parallel-ridge pattern) favors acral melanoma
options.B: Pigment on the ridges favors a benign nevus; pigment in the furrows favors melanoma
options.C: Both patterns are equally benign and the distinction is not useful
options.D: The fibrillar pattern is the specific sign of acral melanoma

exp: Acral dermoscopy exploits the furrow (sulcus)/ridge anatomy of palmoplantar skin: in benign acral nevi the nests sit around the furrows, producing pigment along the furrows (parallel-furrow pattern, the most common benign pattern; also lattice-like and fibrillar). In acral melanoma the pigment instead localizes to the ridges (parallel-ridge pattern), where the intraepidermal eccrine ducts open. So furrow pigment = reassuring, ridge pigment = melanoma.
hy: Acral: parallel-FURROW = benign nevus; parallel-RIDGE = melanoma.
src: Bologna Ch112 · Acral Nevus · Table 112.6 / Fig 112.14
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — furrow pigment is the benign nevus pattern; ridge pigment is the melanoma pattern.
why.B: Wrong — this reverses the rule; ridge pigment is the worrisome one.
why.C: Wrong — the patterns carry opposite significance, so the distinction is highly useful.
why.D: Wrong — the fibrillar pattern is a benign acral-nevus pattern, not the melanoma sign (that is the parallel-ridge pattern).

stem: Which molecular profile is characteristic of a Spitz nevus, as opposed to conventional cutaneous melanoma?

options.A: Kinase gene fusions (ALK, ROS1, NTRK1/2/3, RET) or activating HRAS mutations, without TERT-promoter mutations
options.B: BRAF V600E plus a TERT-promoter mutation and CDKN2A loss
options.C: GNAQ/GNA11 mutations identical to blue nevi
options.D: Biallelic BAP1 inactivation

exp: Spitz nevi are driven by structural rearrangements producing kinase fusions (ALK, ROS1, NTRK1/2/3, MET, MERTK, RET) or by activating HRAS mutations, and they lack the TERT-promoter mutations and CDKN2A/PTEN/TP53 inactivation that accumulate in conventional melanoma (which is driven by BRAF, NRAS, or NF1). This genetic distinction underlies the difficult Spitz-versus-melanoma differential. GNAQ/GNA11 (blue nevi) and BAP1 (BAP1-inactivated nevi) define other entities.
hy: Spitz = kinase fusions or HRAS, no TERT; melanoma = BRAF/NRAS/NF1 + TERT/CDKN2A.
src: Bologna Ch112 · Spitz Nevus – Pathogenesis · Table 112.7
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — kinase fusions/HRAS without TERT mutations characterize Spitz nevi.
why.B: Wrong — BRAF V600E + TERT promoter + CDKN2A loss is the conventional-melanoma profile.
why.C: Wrong — GNAQ/GNA11 mutations characterize blue nevi, not Spitz nevi.
why.D: Wrong — biallelic BAP1 inactivation defines BAP1-inactivated melanocytic nevi.

stem: Intraepidermal eosinophilic globules (Kamino bodies) characteristically seen in Spitz nevi are composed of what, and what is their significance?

options.A: Aggregated basement-membrane material (collagen types IV and VII); common in Spitz nevi but not entirely specific
options.B: Apoptotic melanoma cells; their presence confirms melanoma
options.C: Keratin and intercellular bridges; a hallmark of squamous differentiation
options.D: Birbeck-granule–rich Langerhans cells; diagnostic of histiocytosis

exp: Kamino bodies are intraepidermal eosinophilic globules composed of aggregated basement-membrane material, including collagen types IV and VII; they are commonly, numerously, and largely seen in Spitz nevi and are a helpful (though not entirely specific) clue, since they can occasionally appear in other melanocytic tumors, including melanoma. They are NOT apoptotic tumor cells, keratin pearls, or Langerhans-cell aggregates.
hy: Kamino body = BM material (collagen IV/VII); Spitz clue, not fully specific.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — Kamino bodies are basement-membrane (collagen IV/VII) globules typical of Spitz nevi.
why.B: Wrong — they are not apoptotic melanoma cells, and their presence does not confirm melanoma (they can appear in Spitz and other lesions).
why.C: Wrong — keratin pearls/intercellular bridges are features of squamous cell carcinoma.
why.D: Wrong — Birbeck-granule–rich Langerhans cells define Langerhans cell histiocytosis, unrelated to Kamino bodies.

stem: Which histologic feature favors a Spitz nevus over cutaneous melanoma?

options.A: Maturation with depth (cells and nests become smaller deeper in the dermis) with superficial, sparse mitoses
options.B: Confluent sheets of cells that retain their size and density at the base of the lesion
options.C: Numerous deep and atypical dermal mitoses
options.D: Disorderly pagetoid spread of single cells reaching the granular layer across the whole lesion

exp: "Maturation" — a progressive reduction in cellular and nuclear size and in nest size with increasing dermal depth, plus orderly dispersion and superficial, sparse, non-atypical mitoses — favors a Spitz nevus over melanoma (Table 112.7). Melanoma instead shows little/no maturation (confluent cells retaining density and size at the base), numerous deep and atypical mitoses, and disorderly pagetoid spread of single cells reaching the granular layer across the lesion.
hy: Spitz: maturation with depth + superficial sparse mitoses; melanoma: no maturation, deep atypical mitoses.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — maturation with depth and superficial sparse mitoses favor a Spitz nevus.
why.B: Wrong — retained density/size at the base (lack of maturation) favors melanoma.
why.C: Wrong — numerous deep atypical mitoses favor melanoma, not Spitz nevus.
why.D: Wrong — disorderly full-thickness pagetoid spread favors melanoma.

stem: A spitzoid melanocytic lesion is 8 mm, asymmetric, extends deep into the dermis, lacks maturation, and shows 4 dermal mitoses/mm² including deep ones. How is this best classified and managed?

options.A: Atypical Spitz tumor — complete excision with clear margins and follow-up; sentinel-node positivity, if sought, does not carry the same prognostic meaning as in conventional melanoma
options.B: Conventional Spitz nevus — reassurance with no excision needed
options.C: Unequivocal Spitz melanoma requiring immediate wide excision and adjuvant systemic therapy
options.D: Pigmented spindle cell nevus of Reed — observation only

exp: A spitzoid lesion that is >5–10 mm, asymmetric, deeply infiltrating, non-maturing, and shows easily found/deep dermal mitoses (>2–3/mm²) is an atypical Spitz tumor (Spitz melanocytoma) — biologically between Spitz nevus and Spitz melanoma. Management is complete excision with clear margins and follow-up; if a sentinel-node biopsy is done, nodal positivity in Spitz tumors does NOT carry the same ominous prognosis as in conventional melanoma (most such cases do not progress beyond the node). It is neither a reassuring conventional Spitz nevus nor an unequivocal melanoma.
hy: Atypical Spitz tumor → complete excision; SLN positivity ≠ conventional-melanoma prognosis.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — these features define an atypical Spitz tumor; excise with clear margins, and SLN positivity is not prognostically equivalent to melanoma.
why.B: Wrong — its atypical features place it well beyond a conventional Spitz nevus needing no excision.
why.C: Wrong — it is not unequivocal melanoma; the diagnosis is intermediate, and adjuvant systemic therapy is not the standard answer.
why.D: Wrong — a Reed (pigmented spindle cell) nevus is a different, heavily pigmented superficial spindle-cell lesion.

section: Pigmented spindle cell nevus of Reed
stem: A young woman has a sharply demarcated, jet-black 5 mm papule on the thigh with a symmetric "starburst" (peripheral pseudopods/streaks) dermoscopic pattern. Which entity and driver does this best fit?

options.A: Pigmented spindle cell nevus of Reed, driven by an NTRK3 fusion
options.B: Nodular melanoma, driven by a TERT-promoter mutation
options.C: Blue nevus, driven by GNAQ
options.D: Dermatofibroma, driven by trauma

exp: The pigmented spindle cell nevus of Reed is a sharply circumscribed, heavily pigmented (jet-black) small papule, classically on the thigh of a young woman, with a symmetric starburst dermoscopic pattern (peripheral pseudopods/radial streaming); it is driven most often by an NTRK3 fusion. Although it can mimic melanoma, its symmetry and uniform pigment are reassuring. Nodular melanoma (TERT), blue nevus (GNAQ), and dermatofibroma do not fit this young-woman/thigh/starburst picture.
hy: Reed nevus = black thigh papule, starburst, NTRK3 fusion.
src: Bologna Ch112 · Pigmented Spindle Cell Nevus
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — Reed nevus is the black starburst thigh lesion driven by NTRK3 fusion.
why.B: Wrong — nodular melanoma is not the classic symmetric starburst thigh lesion of a young woman.
why.C: Wrong — a blue nevus is blue-gray (Tyndall) and GNAQ-driven, not a black starburst lesion.
why.D: Wrong — a dermatofibroma is a firm dermal nodule with dimpling, not a black melanocytic starburst lesion.

stem: A WNT-activated deep penetrating/plexiform nevus arises when a common nevus acquires a second hit. Which molecular combination defines it, and which stain supports the diagnosis?

options.A: WNT-pathway activation (usually a CTNNB1/β-catenin mutation) added to MAPK activation (usually BRAF); nuclear β-catenin and LEF1 positivity support it
options.B: A solitary BRAF V600E mutation; HMB-45 negativity supports it
options.C: Biallelic BAP1 loss; loss of nuclear BAP1 supports it
options.D: An NTRK3 fusion; pan-TRK negativity supports it

exp: A WNT-activated deep penetrating/plexiform nevus arises when a common (usually BRAF-mutant) nevus acquires a second hit activating the WNT pathway — in >90% of cases a CTNNB1 mutation affecting β-catenin (rarely APC loss). The combined WNT + MAPK activation produces the deep, wedge-shaped/plexiform, pigmented lesion; nuclear positivity for β-catenin and LEF1 supports the diagnosis (cytoplasmic staining is non-specific). It is usually benign.
hy: Deep penetrating nevus = CTNNB1/β-catenin (WNT) + BRAF (MAPK); nuclear β-catenin/LEF1.
src: Bologna Ch112 · WNT-Activated Deep Penetrating/Plexiform Nevus
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — CTNNB1/β-catenin (WNT) plus BRAF (MAPK), with nuclear β-catenin/LEF1, defines this nevus.
why.B: Wrong — a solitary BRAF mutation gives an ordinary nevus; the defining second hit is WNT activation.
why.C: Wrong — biallelic BAP1 loss defines the BAP1-inactivated nevus, not the deep penetrating nevus.
why.D: Wrong — NTRK3 fusion defines the pigmented spindle cell (Reed) nevus.

stem: A skin-colored dome-shaped papule shows dermal large epithelioid melanocytes with loss of nuclear BAP1 on immunohistochemistry. The patient has several similar lesions. What is the most appropriate consideration?

options.A: Consider testing for a germline BAP1 mutation (BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome), since multiple such nevi can be a marker of it
options.B: The loss of BAP1 staining confirms the lesion is benign and needs no further thought
options.C: These are Spitz nevi, confirmed by the BAP1 loss and Kamino bodies
options.D: Solitary or multiple, BAP1-inactivated nevi carry no syndromic implication

exp: BAP1-inactivated melanocytic nevi are dome-shaped skin-colored/reddish papules of large epithelioid melanocytes that show loss of nuclear BAP1 on immunohistochemistry (a surrogate for biallelic BAP1 inactivation), usually combined with a BRAF mutation. While solitary lesions are usually sporadic, MULTIPLE such nevi can be a marker of the BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome (BAP1-TPDS), so germline BAP1 testing should be considered. Loss of BAP1 staining is not itself proof of benignity (BAP1-inactivated melanoma exists).
hy: BAP1-inactivated nevus = loss of nuclear BAP1; multiple → test germline BAP1 (BAP1-TPDS).
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — multiple BAP1-inactivated nevi can mark BAP1-TPDS, prompting germline testing.
why.B: Wrong — BAP1 loss is not proof of benignity; BAP1-inactivated melanoma occurs.
why.C: Wrong — these are not Spitz nevi; BAP1-inactivated nevi lack Kamino bodies and the wedge silhouette and show loss of BAP1.
why.D: Wrong — multiple BAP1-inactivated nevi DO have syndromic implications (BAP1-TPDS).

stem: For which group of malignancies should carriers of a germline BAP1 mutation undergo surveillance?

options.A: Uveal and cutaneous melanoma, mesothelioma, and renal cell carcinoma
options.B: Medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, and mucosal neuromas
options.C: Medulloblastoma, basal cell carcinomas, and ovarian fibromas
options.D: Pancreatic, ovarian, and testicular tumors

exp: Carriers of a germline BAP1 mutation (BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome) are at increased risk for uveal melanoma, cutaneous melanoma, malignant mesothelioma, and renal cell carcinoma (and cholangiocarcinoma), so surveillance is directed at this spectrum. The other option sets belong to different syndromes: MEN2 (medullary thyroid/pheo/neuromas), Gorlin/basal-cell-nevus syndrome (medulloblastoma/BCCs/ovarian fibromas), and Peutz–Jeghers (pancreatic/ovarian/testicular tumors).
hy: BAP1-TPDS: uveal + cutaneous melanoma, mesothelioma, renal cell carcinoma.
src: Bologna Ch112 · BAP1-Inactivated Melanocytic Nevus – BAP1-TPDS
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — these are the core BAP1-TPDS-associated malignancies.
why.B: Wrong — medullary thyroid carcinoma/pheochromocytoma/mucosal neuromas describe MEN2B.
why.C: Wrong — medulloblastoma/BCCs/ovarian fibromas describe basal cell nevus (Gorlin) syndrome.
why.D: Wrong — pancreatic/ovarian/testicular tumors describe Peutz–Jeghers syndrome.

stem: How is the melanoma risk associated with atypical (dysplastic) melanocytic nevi best understood?

options.A: The number of nevi (e.g. >50–100) and the number of clinically atypical nevi mark an "entire skin at risk"; an individual atypical nevus is rarely the actual precursor
options.B: Each atypical nevus is an obligate precursor that will become melanoma if not excised
options.C: Atypical nevi carry no more melanoma risk than ordinary nevi
options.D: Risk depends only on a single atypical nevus, not on the total nevus count

exp: Melanoma risk in patients with atypical nevi correlates with the TOTAL number of nevi (e.g. >50 or >100) and with the number of clinically atypical nevi; thus the atypical-nevus phenotype is best viewed as a marker of "an entire skin at risk" rather than each lesion being an obligate precursor. In fact the annual transformation rate of any single nevus is vanishingly small (on the order of 1 in 100,000s). Risk is driven by the overall phenotype plus personal/family melanoma history.
hy: Atypical nevi = phenotypic marker of skin-at-risk; risk tracks total + atypical nevus counts, not one lesion.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — risk tracks total and atypical nevus counts; the phenotype marks skin at risk.
why.B: Wrong — individual atypical nevi are not obligate precursors; most never become melanoma.
why.C: Wrong — atypical nevi do confer increased risk relative to ordinary nevi.
why.D: Wrong — risk depends on total nevus burden, not a single atypical lesion.

stem: A shave biopsy returns "dysplastic nevus, moderate atypia" with a focally positive peripheral margin and no clinically visible residual lesion. Based on current evidence, what is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Re-excision is often not required, because data show re-excision of moderately atypical nevi rarely changes the diagnosis
options.B: Mandatory wide local excision with 1 cm margins, as for melanoma
options.C: Sentinel lymph node biopsy
options.D: No follow-up of any kind is ever needed for atypical nevi

exp: Independent studies show that re-excising MODERATELY atypical (dysplastic) nevi with positive biopsy margins rarely changes the diagnosis or management, so routine re-excision is often not required when no clinical residuum remains. Only "severely"/markedly atypical nevi (overlapping with melanoma in situ) are routinely re-excised. Wide 1 cm margins and sentinel-node biopsy are melanoma treatments, not dysplastic-nevus management, and these patients still warrant skin surveillance.
hy: Moderately atypical nevus, positive margin, no residuum → re-excision usually unnecessary; severe → re-excise.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — data do not support routine re-excision of moderately atypical nevi.
why.B: Wrong — 1 cm wide excision is a melanoma margin, not the management of a moderately atypical nevus.
why.C: Wrong — sentinel-node biopsy is a melanoma staging procedure, not indicated for a dysplastic nevus.
why.D: Wrong — patients with atypical nevi do need ongoing skin surveillance.

stem: Compared with common acquired nevi and with melanoma, the mutational profile of atypical (dysplastic) nevi is best described as:

options.A: A BRAF V600E driver (as in common nevi) plus additional changes such as TERT-promoter or CDKN2A alterations — an intermediate mutation burden between common nevi and melanoma
options.B: Identical to melanoma, with TERT, CDKN2A, PTEN, and TP53 all inactivated
options.C: Driven by GNAQ/GNA11, like blue nevi
options.D: Free of any oncogenic mutation

exp: Atypical (dysplastic) nevi most often carry the same BRAF V600E driver as common nevi, but additionally accumulate changes such as TERT-promoter mutations or CDKN2A loss; overall their mutation burden is intermediate — higher than common acquired or congenital nevi but lower than melanoma. They are not mutation-free, not GNAQ-driven, and not fully melanoma-equivalent (they lack the complete inactivation of TERT/CDKN2A/PTEN/TP53 seen in melanoma).
hy: Atypical nevus = BRAF V600E + TERT/CDKN2A; mutation burden between common nevus and melanoma.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — BRAF V600E plus TERT/CDKN2A changes, at an intermediate burden, fits atypical nevi.
why.B: Wrong — atypical nevi do not have the full melanoma profile (TERT/CDKN2A/PTEN/TP53 all inactivated).
why.C: Wrong — GNAQ/GNA11 drives blue nevi, not atypical nevi.
why.D: Wrong — atypical nevi are not mutation-free; they have a measurable (intermediate) mutation burden.

stem: Congenital melanocytic nevi are classified by their projected adult size. Which set of cut-offs is correct?

options.A: Small <1.5 cm; medium 1.5–20 cm; large >20–40 cm; giant >40 cm
options.B: Small <5 mm; medium 5–10 mm; large 10–20 mm; giant >20 mm
options.C: Small <1 cm; medium 1–5 cm; large 5–10 cm; giant >10 cm
options.D: Small <3 cm; medium 3–10 cm; large 10–30 cm; giant >30 cm

exp: Congenital melanocytic nevi are categorized by projected ADULT size: small <1.5 cm, medium (intermediate) 1.5–20 cm, large >20–40 cm, and giant >40 cm. (Because the body grows, a newborn lesion ≥9 cm on the scalp or ≥6 cm on the trunk is already assigned to the large/giant group.) These cut-offs matter because melanoma and neurocutaneous-melanosis risk rise with size.
hy: CMN size: small <1.5 / medium 1.5–20 / large >20–40 / giant >40 cm (adult).
src: Bologna Ch112 · Congenital Melanocytic Nevus – Classification
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — these are the chapter's adult-size cut-offs for congenital nevi.
why.B: Wrong — these millimeter figures are far too small for the congenital-nevus size bands.
why.C: Wrong — these cut-offs are incorrect (the giant threshold is >40 cm, not >10 cm).
why.D: Wrong — these values do not match the chapter's classification.

stem: How does lifetime melanoma risk vary with congenital melanocytic nevus size?

options.A: Small/medium congenital nevi carry a low risk (<1%), while large and giant nevi carry a substantially higher risk (roughly 2–3%)
options.B: All congenital nevi, regardless of size, carry an identical ~30% melanoma risk
options.C: Small congenital nevi carry the highest risk; giant nevi are essentially benign
options.D: Congenital nevi never give rise to melanoma

exp: Melanoma risk in congenital melanocytic nevi rises with size: small and medium nevi carry a low risk (most studies <1%, with prospective cohorts of medium nevi finding essentially no melanomas), whereas large and giant nevi carry a substantially higher risk — a comprehensive review reported ~2.5% (large) and ~3.1% (giant), versus ~0.7% overall. Melanoma in large/giant nevi may arise deep (dermal/subcutaneous), while in small nevi it usually begins at the dermal–epidermal junction.
hy: CMN melanoma risk: small/medium <1%; large ~2.5%, giant ~3.1% (overall ~0.7%).
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — low risk for small/medium and roughly 2–3% for large/giant matches the chapter.
why.B: Wrong — there is no uniform ~30% risk; risk is size-dependent and far lower.
why.C: Wrong — this reverses the gradient; large/giant nevi carry the higher risk.
why.D: Wrong — congenital nevi can and do give rise to melanoma.

stem: Which patient with congenital melanocytic nevi has the HIGHEST risk of neurocutaneous melanosis, warranting MRI of the brain and spine (most sensitive before ~6 months of age)?

options.A: An infant with a giant nevus in a posterior axial location plus numerous (≥20) satellite nevi, or multiple medium-sized congenital nevi
options.B: A child with a single small congenital nevus on the leg
options.C: A child with one medium-sized nevus and no satellites
options.D: An adult with a solitary giant nevus on the anterior thigh and no satellites

exp: Neurocutaneous melanosis is concentrated in patients with a giant congenital nevus in a POSTERIOR AXIAL location with numerous satellite nevi (≥20), or with multiple (≥3) medium-sized congenital nevi — the latter group actually carrying the highest NCM risk (up to ~70% in one series). At-risk asymptomatic infants should have MRI of the brain and spine (with gadolinium), most sensitive in the first ~6 months of life. A single small nevus or one medium nevus without satellites is low-risk.
hy: NCM risk: ≥20 satellites + posterior axial giant nevus, or multiple medium CMN; MRI <6 mo.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — posterior-axial giant nevus with ≥20 satellites, or multiple medium CMN, is the high-NCM-risk profile.
why.B: Wrong — a single small congenital nevus carries minimal NCM risk.
why.C: Wrong — one medium nevus without satellites is low-risk and does not mandate MRI.
why.D: Wrong — anterior location without satellites is not the high-risk pattern (posterior axial + satellites is).

stem: Which oncogenic driver is found in the majority of congenital melanocytic nevi?

options.A: NRAS (≈80%)
options.B: BRAF V600E (≈80%)
options.C: GNA11
options.D: KIT

exp: The majority of congenital melanocytic nevi carry an oncogenic NRAS mutation (~80%); BRAF mutations are found in up to ~7%, and KRAS mutations or kinase fusions are rare. This contrasts with common acquired nevi, which are predominantly BRAF-driven — a useful discriminator.
hy: CMN driver = NRAS (~80%) (vs common acquired nevi = BRAF).
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — NRAS (~80%) is the dominant congenital-nevus driver.
why.B: Wrong — BRAF (~80%) drives common acquired nevi; in congenital nevi BRAF is only ~7%.
why.C: Wrong — GNA11 drives blue nevi/nevus of Ota, not congenital nevi.
why.D: Wrong — KIT is associated with acral/mucosal/chronically sun-damaged melanomas, not congenital nevi.

section: Proliferating nodule vs melanoma in CMN
stem: A firm nodule arises within a giant congenital nevus in an infant. Array comparative genomic hybridization (aCGH) is performed. Which finding favors a benign proliferating nodule over melanoma?

options.A: Gains and losses of WHOLE chromosomes (numerical aberrations), rather than partial-chromosome gains/losses
options.B: Multiple partial-chromosome gains and losses
options.C: A TERT-promoter mutation with homozygous CDKN2A deletion
options.D: Deep dermal mitoses with marked nuclear pleomorphism alone

exp: Proliferating nodules within congenital nevi can be very hard to distinguish from melanoma, but molecular testing helps: benign proliferating nodules typically show gains and losses of WHOLE chromosomes (numerical/aneuploid changes), whereas melanomas show gains and losses of only PORTIONS of chromosomes (structural aberrations). Deep mitoses and pleomorphism alone, or a TERT/CDKN2A signature, point toward melanoma rather than a benign nodule.
hy: Proliferating nodule = whole-chromosome aberrations; melanoma = partial-chromosome aberrations (aCGH).
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — whole-chromosome gains/losses favor a benign proliferating nodule.
why.B: Wrong — partial-chromosome gains/losses favor melanoma.
why.C: Wrong — a TERT-promoter mutation with CDKN2A deletion points to melanoma, not a benign nodule.
why.D: Wrong — deep mitoses with pleomorphism raise concern for melanoma rather than reassuring for a benign nodule.

stem: When surgical removal of a giant congenital melanocytic nevus is planned in an otherwise stable infant, why is the procedure typically delayed until at least ~6 months of age?

options.A: To reduce the risk associated with general anesthesia in early infancy
options.B: Because melanoma risk is negligible until after 6 months of age
options.C: Because the nevus will spontaneously regress if removal is delayed
options.D: Because MRI screening for neurocutaneous melanosis is most sensitive after 6 months

exp: When excision of a giant congenital nevus is elective and the infant is stable, the procedure is typically delayed until at least ~6 months of age to reduce the heightened risk of general anesthesia in early infancy. Melanoma risk in giant nevi is in fact present from birth (not negligible), the nevus does not spontaneously regress, and MRI for neurocutaneous melanosis is MORE (not less) sensitive in the first ~6 months.
hy: Delay giant-CMN excision to ≥6 months — for anesthesia safety.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — the delay is to lower general-anesthesia risk in early infancy.
why.B: Wrong — melanoma risk in giant nevi is present from birth, not negligible until 6 months.
why.C: Wrong — giant congenital nevi do not spontaneously regress.
why.D: Wrong — MRI for NCM is most sensitive in the first ~6 months, so this is not the reason to delay surgery.

stem: A tan patch studded with multiple darker brown macules and papules has been present since early childhood. Which statement about nevus spilus (speckled lentiginous nevus) is correct?

options.A: It carries mosaic HRAS mutations and is classified as a type of congenital melanocytic nevus; melanoma can rarely arise within it
options.B: It is distinguished from agminated nevi by the absence of a background tan patch
options.C: It is driven by GNAQ, like blue nevi
options.D: The background tan patch reliably excludes any melanoma risk

exp: Nevus spilus (speckled lentiginous nevus) is a tan background patch studded with darker macules/papules (ranging from lentigines to junctional/compound and even Spitz or blue nevi); both the patch and the speckles carry mosaic HRAS mutations, and the lesion is classified as a type of congenital melanocytic nevus. Melanoma can rarely arise within it. It is distinguished from agminated nevi and partial unilateral lentiginosis by the PRESENCE of the background tan patch.
hy: Nevus spilus = HRAS mosaic, tan patch + speckles, a CMN variant; rare melanoma.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — mosaic HRAS, congenital-nevus classification, and rare melanoma risk define nevus spilus.
why.B: Wrong — nevus spilus is distinguished BY its background tan patch (agminated nevi lack it).
why.C: Wrong — it is HRAS-driven, not GNAQ-driven.
why.D: Wrong — the tan patch does not exclude melanoma risk; melanoma can arise within a nevus spilus.

stem: A 52-year-old man develops a new halo nevus. In contrast to the same finding in a teenager, why does new-onset halo phenomenon in an older adult warrant a careful examination?

options.A: It can be a clue to a melanoma (ocular, mucosal, or cutaneous) elsewhere, so a full skin and ocular exam is indicated
options.B: Halo formation in adults indicates the central nevus is malignant and must be excised
options.C: Halo nevi in adults are caused by B-cell lymphoma and require a marrow biopsy
options.D: The halo means the nevus has become a blue nevus

exp: A new halo (Sutton) nevus in an OLDER adult is a red flag: multiple or new-onset halo nevi after ~40 years of age can be a clue to a melanoma (ocular, mucosal, or cutaneous) elsewhere, so a complete skin and ophthalmologic examination is indicated. In teenagers, by contrast, halo nevi are common and benign. The halo reflects a CD8+ cytotoxic T-cell reaction against nevus cells; it does not itself mean the central nevus is malignant or that the nevus has become a blue nevus.
hy: New halo nevus in an adult >40 → search for melanoma (skin, mucosa, eye).
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — new-onset halo nevi in older adults warrant a search for an occult melanoma.
why.B: Wrong — the halo itself does not mean the central nevus is malignant.
why.C: Wrong — halo nevi are not caused by B-cell lymphoma; the infiltrate is CD8+ T cells.
why.D: Wrong — the halo is depigmentation around the nevus, not transformation into a blue nevus.

stem: A lesion shows two distinct melanocytic populations — a banal compound nevus admixed with a discrete focus of pigmented epithelioid/spindled dermal melanocytes. Why does this "combined nevus" pattern matter clinically?

options.A: The presence of two or more components raises the possibility of melanoma arising in a nevus, so biopsy of the whole lesion and immunostains (e.g. BAP1, β-catenin/LEF1, PRKAR1A) help classify it
options.B: Two components are diagnostic of melanoma and require wide excision
options.C: A combined nevus is a purely junctional lesion with a single cell population
options.D: Combined nevi cannot be confused with melanoma and never need biopsy

exp: A combined nevus contains two or more phenotypically distinct melanocytic populations (commonly a banal compound/intradermal nevus plus a focus of pigmented epithelioid/spindled dermal melanocytes, e.g. a blue-nevus component). Because two components in one lesion can also be the look of melanoma arising in a nevus, a suspicious lesion should be biopsied in its entirety, and immunostains (BAP1, β-catenin/LEF1, PRKAR1A) help reclassify many former "combined nevi" (e.g. BAP1-inactivated, deep penetrating, PEM).
hy: Combined nevus = ≥2 populations → exclude melanoma; IHC classifies the components.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — multiple components raise melanoma concern; full biopsy plus IHC classifies the lesion.
why.B: Wrong — two components are not by themselves diagnostic of melanoma.
why.C: Wrong — a combined nevus is defined by ≥2 distinct populations, not a single junctional one.
why.D: Wrong — combined nevi CAN be confused with melanoma, which is precisely why biopsy may be needed.

stem: Six weeks after a shave removal of a nevus, irregular brown pigment reappears strictly within the surgical scar and does not extend beyond it. What is the best interpretation?

options.A: This is a recurrent (persistent) nevus / "pseudomelanoma" — pigmentation confined to the scar favors observation over re-biopsy
options.B: Pigment within a scar always indicates residual melanoma and mandates wide excision
options.C: Recurrence at any time and extent is reassuring and never needs review
options.D: Recurrence beyond the scar or after >12 months would be MORE reassuring than this

exp: A recurrent (persistent) nevus — "pseudomelanoma" — is irregular pigmentation reappearing within a scar after incomplete (usually shave) removal, typically within ~6 months; residual nevus cells migrate up into the epidermis over the scar and can look alarming histologically. When the pigment is confined to the scar, observation is appropriate. Recurrence that extends BEYOND the scar, or appears >12 months later, is the worrisome scenario that should prompt re-evaluation of the original biopsy/re-biopsy.
hy: Pigment confined to a scar after shave = recurrent nevus (pseudomelanoma) → observe; beyond scar/>12 mo → re-biopsy.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions

why.A: Correct — pigment confined to the scar favors a benign recurrent nevus and observation.
why.B: Wrong — scar pigment confined to the scar does not always indicate melanoma or mandate wide excision.
why.C: Wrong — recurrence beyond the scar or after a long interval is NOT reassuring and needs review.
why.D: Wrong — this inverts the rule; beyond-scar/late recurrence is more concerning, not more reassuring.

stem: A young adult has the lesion shown below, a stable, firm, well-circumscribed dome-shaped papule. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Blue nevus
options.B: Nodular melanoma
options.C: Dermatofibroma
options.D: Pyogenic granuloma

exp: The image shows a solitary, well-circumscribed, steel-blue to blue-black dome-shaped papule — the classic look of a (common) blue nevus, whose color is the Tyndall effect of dermal melanin. Nodular melanoma is typically a larger, changing, often ulcerated nodule; a dermatofibroma is a firm skin-colored/brown nodule that dimples on lateral compression; and a pyogenic granuloma is a friable, rapidly growing red papule that bleeds. The stable, uniform blue papule is the discriminator.
hy: Solitary uniform blue dome papule = blue nevus (Tyndall).
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions
imgcap: Solitary steel-blue to blue-black dome-shaped papule

why.A: Correct — a stable uniform blue-black dome papule is a blue nevus.
why.B: Wrong — nodular melanoma is usually larger, changing, and may be ulcerated, not a small stable blue papule.
why.C: Wrong — a dermatofibroma is a firm brown nodule that dimples on compression, not blue.
why.D: Wrong — a pyogenic granuloma is a friable bleeding red papule, not a blue nevus.

stem: This is the dermoscopic image of a pigmented macule on the sole. Which pattern is shown and what does it indicate?

options.A: Parallel-furrow pattern — a benign acral melanocytic nevus
options.B: Parallel-ridge pattern — acral melanoma
options.C: Blue-white veil — nodular melanoma
options.D: Arborizing vessels — basal cell carcinoma

exp: The dermoscopic image shows pigment arranged along the parallel FURROWS (sulci) of acral skin — the parallel-furrow pattern, the most common benign acral-nevus pattern (nests sit around the furrows). The dangerous counterpart is the parallel-RIDGE pattern (pigment on the ridges), which indicates acral melanoma. A blue-white veil (nodular melanoma) and arborizing vessels (BCC) are different dermoscopic findings altogether.
hy: Parallel-FURROW = benign acral nevus; parallel-RIDGE = acral melanoma.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions
imgcap: Dermoscopy of acral skin with pigment along the parallel furrows

why.A: Correct — furrow-aligned pigment is the benign parallel-furrow pattern of an acral nevus.
why.B: Wrong — the parallel-RIDGE pattern (pigment on ridges) is the melanoma sign; that is not what is shown.
why.C: Wrong — a blue-white veil is a melanoma feature, not the acral furrow pattern.
why.D: Wrong — arborizing vessels are a basal cell carcinoma feature.

stem: A child has the rapidly appearing, symmetric, dome-shaped pink-red papule shown below. Which is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Spitz nevus
options.B: Nodular melanoma
options.C: Dermatofibroma
options.D: Blue nevus

exp: The image shows a solitary, symmetric, dome-shaped pink-red papule of recent onset in a child — the classic presentation of a Spitz nevus (which is often amelanotic/pink and well-circumscribed). Nodular melanoma is exceedingly rare prepubertally and is usually a larger changing nodule; a dermatofibroma is a firm dimpling nodule; a blue nevus is blue-gray, not pink. The age, symmetry, and pink dome morphology point to Spitz.
hy: Symmetric pink dome papule in a child = Spitz nevus.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions
imgcap: Solitary symmetric red-pink dome-shaped papule on a child's skin

why.A: Correct — a symmetric pink-red dome papule in a child is a Spitz nevus.
why.B: Wrong — melanoma is very rare in prepubertal children and is usually larger and changing.
