why.C: Wrong — a dermatofibroma is a firm brown dimpling nodule, not a pink dome papule.
why.D: Wrong — a blue nevus is blue-gray, not pink-red.

stem: This H&E image is from a symmetric melanocytic lesion in a child. Which feature is indicated and is characteristic (though not entirely specific) of this entity?

options.A: A Kamino body (eosinophilic basement-membrane globule), typical of a Spitz nevus
options.B: A keratin pearl, typical of squamous cell carcinoma
options.C: A Michaelis–Gutmann body, typical of malakoplakia
options.D: A Civatte body, typical of lichen planus

exp: The arrowed dull-pink intraepidermal globule is a Kamino body — an aggregate of basement-membrane material (collagen IV/VII) that is characteristic of (though not entirely specific to) Spitz nevi, in which it is typically numerous and large. A keratin pearl belongs to squamous cell carcinoma, a Michaelis–Gutmann body to malakoplakia, and a Civatte (colloid) body to lichenoid interface dermatitis.
hy: Kamino body (BM collagen IV/VII globule) = Spitz nevus clue.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions
imgcap: H&E showing a dull-pink intraepidermal globule (arrow) in a spitzoid lesion

why.A: Correct — the eosinophilic basement-membrane globule is a Kamino body, typical of Spitz nevi.
why.B: Wrong — keratin pearls are a squamous cell carcinoma feature.
why.C: Wrong — Michaelis–Gutmann bodies are seen in malakoplakia.
why.D: Wrong — Civatte (colloid) bodies are apoptotic keratinocytes in lichenoid dermatitis.

stem: A patient has the lesion shown below, displaying asymmetry and several shades of brown. Which is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Atypical (dysplastic) melanocytic nevus
options.B: Seborrheic keratosis
options.C: Dermatofibroma
options.D: Blue nevus

exp: The lesion shown is asymmetric with several shades of brown — the morphology of an atypical (dysplastic) melanocytic nevus, which simulates the clinical features of melanoma. A seborrheic keratosis is a "stuck-on" waxy verrucous plaque with milia-like cysts; a dermatofibroma is a firm dimpling nodule; a blue nevus is a uniform blue papule. The flat, color-variegated, asymmetric pigmented macule is the atypical nevus.
hy: Asymmetric, color-variegated flat pigmented lesion = atypical (dysplastic) nevus.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions
imgcap: Macular/slightly raised pigmented lesion with asymmetry and color variegation

why.A: Correct — asymmetry plus color variegation in a flat pigmented lesion is an atypical nevus.
why.B: Wrong — a seborrheic keratosis is a waxy "stuck-on" plaque with horn cysts, not a flat variegated macule.
why.C: Wrong — a dermatofibroma is a firm dimpling nodule, not a variegated macule.
why.D: Wrong — a blue nevus is a uniform blue papule, not a multicolored asymmetric macule.

stem: This newborn has the lesion shown below. What is the most likely diagnosis and its key association?

options.A: Giant congenital melanocytic nevus with satellite nevi — at risk for neurocutaneous melanosis
options.B: Extensive dermal melanocytosis (Mongolian spots)
options.C: Plexiform neurofibroma
options.D: Diffuse cutaneous mastocytosis

exp: The newborn has a giant pigmented (often hairy) plaque in an axial distribution with numerous smaller satellite nevi — a giant congenital melanocytic nevus, which is at risk for neurocutaneous melanosis (especially with ≥20 satellites and a posterior axial location) and carries an elevated melanoma risk. Extensive Mongolian spots are blue-gray and flat without satellites; a plexiform neurofibroma is a soft "bag of worms"; diffuse mastocytosis shows urticating tan papules/plaques (Darier sign), not a giant hairy nevus with satellites.
hy: Giant hairy axial nevus + satellites = giant CMN → NCM risk.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions
imgcap: Giant pigmented hairy plaque in an axial distribution with numerous smaller satellite lesions

why.A: Correct — a giant pigmented nevus with satellites is a giant CMN with neurocutaneous-melanosis risk.
why.B: Wrong — dermal melanocytosis is flat blue-gray pigment without satellite nevi.
why.C: Wrong — a plexiform neurofibroma is a soft "bag of worms," not a giant pigmented satellited nevus.
why.D: Wrong — diffuse mastocytosis shows urticating papules with a positive Darier sign, not this morphology.

stem: The lesion shown below consists of darker brown macules and papules superimposed on a uniform tan background patch. What is the diagnosis?

options.A: Nevus spilus (speckled lentiginous nevus)
options.B: Agminated melanocytic nevi
options.C: Café-au-lait macule
options.D: Partial unilateral lentiginosis

exp: The lesion shows darker brown macules and papules superimposed on a uniform tan background patch — nevus spilus (speckled lentiginous nevus). The defining feature is the tan BACKGROUND patch: agminated nevi and partial unilateral lentiginosis lack that background, and a plain café-au-lait macule has no darker speckles.
hy: Tan patch + darker speckles = nevus spilus.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions
imgcap: Tan background patch studded with multiple darker brown speckles

why.A: Correct — darker speckles on a tan background patch define nevus spilus.
why.B: Wrong — agminated nevi are grouped nevi WITHOUT a background tan patch.
why.C: Wrong — a café-au-lait macule is a uniform tan patch without darker speckles.
why.D: Wrong — partial unilateral lentiginosis is segmental lentigines without a background tan patch.

stem: The lesion shown below is a central melanocytic nevus surrounded by a sharply marginated rim of depigmentation. What is the diagnosis?

options.A: Halo (Sutton) nevus
options.B: Vitiligo without a nevus
options.C: Amelanotic melanoma with a halo
options.D: Blue nevus

exp: The image shows a central pigmented nevus encircled by a sharply demarcated, symmetric rim of depigmentation — a halo (Sutton) nevus, due to a CD8+ T-cell reaction against the nevus. Vitiligo without a nevus would be a depigmented patch with no central lesion; an amelanotic melanoma with a halo would show an atypical/changing central tumor (a concern mainly in older adults); a blue nevus is a uniform blue papule without a halo.
hy: Central nevus + symmetric white halo = halo (Sutton) nevus.
chname: Benign Melanocytic Neoplasms and Melanotic Lesions
imgcap: Central pigmented nevus surrounded by a symmetric white (depigmented) halo

why.A: Correct — a central nevus with a symmetric depigmented halo is a halo (Sutton) nevus.
why.B: Wrong — vitiligo alone has no central melanocytic nevus.
why.C: Wrong — an amelanotic melanoma with a halo would show an atypical central tumor, not a banal nevus (and is mainly a concern in older adults).
why.D: Wrong — a blue nevus is a uniform blue papule and does not have a depigmented halo.

stem: A 1.4 mm thick cutaneous melanoma is widely excised. The recommended peripheral surgical margin is:

options.B: 1.0 cm
options.D: 2.0 cm

exp: Cutaneous melanoma is excised with a margin scaled to Breslow thickness — the perpendicular depth from the granular layer to the deepest tumor cell, the dominant prognostic variable. A 1.4 mm tumor falls in the >1–2 mm band, for which a 1.0–2.0 cm margin is recommended (the exact width tailored to site and closure). Wider excision lowers local recurrence but confers no survival benefit beyond 2 cm, so margins plateau there; randomized trials underpin these bands. A and B (0.5–1 cm, 1 cm) are the in-situ/≤1 mm margins; D (2 cm) is reserved for >2 mm.
hy: Margins step up with thickness and plateau at 2 cm.

stem: Which lesion is paired with its correct standard peripheral excision margin?

options.A: Actinic keratosis — 4 mm margin
options.B: Non-morpheaform BCC <2 cm — 4 mm
options.C: Low-risk cutaneous SCC — 2 mm
options.D: Melanoma in situ — 2.0 cm margin

exp: A non-morpheaform BCC under 2 cm is cleared by a 4 mm margin in ~98% of cases — the anchor figure for keratinocyte cancers. Each tumor class has its own rule: AK is intraepidermal/precancerous and treated by field-directed therapy (no excision margin); low-risk cutaneous SCC needs 4–6 mm (not 2 mm); melanoma in situ needs 0.5–1.0 cm (2 cm is the thick-melanoma ceiling, never used for in situ). Knowing one anchor per class lets you reconstruct the rest.
hy: BCC <2 cm = 4 mm is the anchor; each class differs.

stem: For which lesion is sentinel lymph node biopsy considered standard staging?

options.A: Basal cell carcinoma larger than 2 cm
options.B: Cutaneous melanoma ≥1 mm Breslow thickness
options.C: Low-risk cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma
options.D: Hypertrophic actinic keratosis with scale

exp: Sentinel lymph node biopsy maps the first draining node to detect occult regional spread; in melanoma it is the strongest prognostic factor and is standard at ≥1 mm Breslow (and considered in thinner T1b lesions with ulceration, mitoses ≥2/mm², or LVI). BCC essentially never metastasizes, so it is never nodally staged; low-risk cSCC is not routinely sampled; AK cannot metastasize at all.
hy: "≥1 mm melanoma → SLNB"; thinner only with high-risk histology.

stem: The T1a/T1b boundary for thin melanoma is defined at a Breslow thickness of:

options.A: 0.5 mm
options.B: 0.8 mm
options.C: 1.0 mm
options.D: 1.2 mm

exp: AJCC-8 subdivides T1 melanoma at 0.8 mm: T1a is <0.8 mm without ulceration; T1b is <0.8 mm with ulceration, or 0.8–1.0 mm with or without ulceration. The 0.8 mm cut matters because it shifts SLNB discussion. The classic trap is to place the line at 1.0 mm — but that is the T1/T2 boundary, not the a/b split.

stem: The single strongest determinant of melanoma T-stage and the excision margin is:

options.A: Clark anatomic level
options.B: Breslow tumor thickness
options.C: Maximum surface diameter
options.D: Number of primary lesions

exp: Breslow tumor thickness is the dominant prognostic variable and the basis of both T-staging and margin selection; ulceration and mitoses refine it. Clark level (anatomic depth I–V) was the old system but was dropped from AJCC-8 because thickness predicts outcome better. Surface diameter and lesion number are not melanoma staging axes.
hy: Breslow drives stage and margin; Clark level is obsolete.

stem: Regarding Merkel cell carcinoma, which statement is correct?

options.A: A wide-local-excision margin of exactly 2 cm is specified as standard
options.B: SLNB is advised for most patients and may be positive in small tumors
options.C: Sentinel node biopsy is reserved only for tumors over 2 cm in diameter
options.D: Like basal cell carcinoma, it rarely warrants any nodal staging at all

exp: Merkel cell carcinoma is an aggressive neuroendocrine skin cancer; Bologna recommends excision of the primary plus SLNB for most patients because nodal spread is common — the SLN is positive in ~35% at diagnosis and in ~15% even of tumors ≤5 mm, so small size does not make it "safe." Adjuvant radiation to the primary ± nodal basin is frequently added. Option A is wrong because Bologna gives no specific cm WLE margin; C and D understate its nodal risk.
hy: Merkel: SLNB for nearly everyone — size doesn't make it safe.

stem: Mohs micrographic surgery is the preferred treatment for which of the following?

options.A: A superficial actinic keratosis located on the forehead
options.B: A recurrent morpheaform basal cell carcinoma on the nasal ala
options.C: A 1.2 mm cutaneous melanoma on the upper back with no ulceration
options.D: A low-risk 6 mm squamous cell carcinoma on the trunk

exp: Mohs micrographic surgery examines 100% of the peripheral and deep margin intra-operatively, so it is preferred for tumors that are ill-defined, recurrent, high-risk, or on cosmetically/functionally critical sites — a recurrent morpheaform BCC on the nasal ala is the textbook indication (Mohs recurrence ~1% vs ~10% for standard excision). AK is field-treated; invasive melanoma and low-risk truncal SCC are managed by wide excision, not Mohs.
hy: Mohs = ill-defined / recurrent / high-risk facial keratinocyte tumors.

stem: A melanoma in situ on the cheek is excised. The recommended peripheral margin is:

options.B: exactly 2.0 cm
options.C: 4 mm, as for BCC
options.D: none — topical therapy only

exp: Melanoma in situ is confined to the epidermis (no metastatic risk) and is excised with a 0.5–1.0 cm margin; the lentigo maligna subtype, with its broad subclinical extension, may need ≥1 cm or staged/Mohs margin control on the face. 2 cm is the ceiling for thick invasive melanoma; 4 mm is the BCC figure; topical therapy alone is inadequate for definitive treatment.
hy: In-situ margin = 0.5–1.0 cm (wider for lentigo maligna).

stem: The delay phenomenon increases the surviving territory of a flap mainly by:

options.A: dilating the choke vessels linking adjacent angiosomes
options.B: raising systemic cardiac output and flap inflow pressure
options.C: inducing new lymphatic channels within the flap
options.D: thickening the subdermal plexus and overlying dermis

exp: A flap spans several angiosomes (each the territory of one source artery); adjacent angiosomes are joined by reduced-caliber "choke" vessels. The delay phenomenon — partially raising or dividing part of the pedicle in a staged first operation — provokes these choke vessels to dilate toward true anastomoses over ~48 h to weeks, so the flap can then survive on a larger captured territory. It is a local vascular conditioning, not a systemic, lymphatic, or fat-layer effect; necrosis, if it occurs, falls at the next choke connection beyond the delayed territory.
hy: Delay works by opening choke vessels.
exptable: tissue block from one source artery
no capillary flow despite patent anastomosis

stem: The "no-reflow" phenomenon refers to:

options.A: failure of capillary perfusion after ischemia despite a patent anastomosis
options.B: venous outflow thrombosis caused by a kinked or twisted pedicle
options.C: arterial spasm that is reversed by topical papaverine application
options.D: progressive compression of the pedicle by an expanding postoperative hematoma

exp: No-reflow (May) is the failure of capillary perfusion after prolonged warm ischemia even though the arterial and venous anastomoses are patent — endothelial and parenchymal swelling plus microthrombi plug the microcirculation, so blood cannot re-enter the capillary bed. This is why ischemia time must be minimized and why a flap can fail despite a technically perfect anastomosis. B–D are mechanical or spasm causes of compromise, not no-reflow.
hy: Patent anastomosis but no capillary flow = no-reflow.
exptable: tissue block from one source artery
no capillary flow despite patent anastomosis

stem: The gold standard for postoperative free-flap monitoring is:

options.A: an implantable Doppler probe on the pedicle
options.B: clinical observation of the flap
options.C: laser Doppler flowmetry of the skin
options.D: near-infrared spectroscopy monitoring

exp: Direct clinical observation of the flap — color, capillary refill, turgor, surface temperature, and pinprick (color and speed of bleeding) — remains the gold standard for monitoring; it is cheap, continuous, and reliable in experienced hands. Implantable Doppler, laser Doppler, and near-infrared spectroscopy are useful adjuncts but supplement rather than replace serial clinical exam.
hy: Nothing beats serial clinical exam.
exptable: clinical exam (color, refill, turgor, temp, pinprick)
q30 min ×24 h → q1–2 h → q4 h (first 72 h)

stem: A surface-temperature difference of what magnitude between flap and control site is ~98% sensitive for vascular compromise?
exp: A flap-to-control surface-temperature difference of 1.8°C is ~98% sensitive and 75% predictive of vascular compromise, and an absolute flap temperature below 30°C indicates failure — temperature trend is an easy, inexpensive adjunct. Smaller gaps (0.5–1.0°C) are within normal variation; 3.0°C is past the validated cut-point.
hy: ΔT 1.8°C is the threshold; <30°C = failing.
exptable: clinical exam (color, refill, turgor, temp, pinprick)
q30 min ×24 h → q1–2 h → q4 h (first 72 h)

stem: Which adjunct can distinguish venous obstruction from arterial occlusion?

options.A: surface-temperature probe
options.B: laser Doppler flowmetry
options.C: pinprick testing
options.D: capillary refill time

exp: Laser Doppler flowmetry (and implantable Doppler waveform analysis) can differentiate venous obstruction from arterial occlusion — useful because the two emergencies look different and demand different action. A temperature probe, pinprick, and capillary refill flag that something is wrong but do not localize the problem to artery versus vein. Venous compromise (congested, blue, brisk dark pinprick bleeding) is the more common early free-flap problem.
hy: Laser Doppler tells venous from arterial.
exptable: clinical exam (color, refill, turgor, temp, pinprick)
q30 min ×24 h → q1–2 h → q4 h (first 72 h)

stem: The recommended frequency of flap checks during the first 24 hours after free tissue transfer is approximately:

options.A: hourly
options.B: every 30 minutes
options.C: every 4 hours
options.D: continuous monitoring only

exp: Standard protocols check a free flap every 30 minutes for the first 24 h, then every 1–2 h for the next 24 h, then every 4 h for the next 48 h — the first 72 h carry the highest thrombosis risk and earliest detection drives salvage (success ~96–98%). Hourly and 4-hourly are later-window frequencies; machines supplement but do not replace the timed clinical checks.
hy: q30 min in the first day; intensify early.
exptable: clinical exam (color, refill, turgor, temp, pinprick)
q30 min ×24 h → q1–2 h → q4 h (first 72 h)

stem: The arc of rotation of a muscle flap is:

options.A: inversely proportional to the number of vascular pedicles
options.B: directly proportional to the total muscle mass and bulk
options.C: determined primarily by the muscle's motor nerve supply
options.D: essentially identical across all Mathes–Nahai muscle types

exp: Arc of rotation — how far a pedicled flap can reach — is inversely proportional to the number of vascular pedicles: a muscle tethered by many segmental pedicles (Type IV, e.g. sartorius) barely reaches, while a muscle fed by one dominant pedicle near its insertion (Type V, e.g. latissimus dorsi) swings widely and may even have two arcs. It is not set by muscle mass or motor nerve, and it differs markedly across Mathes–Nahai types.
hy: Fewer pedicles → wider arc.

stem: An angiosome is best defined as:

options.A: the cutaneous territory supplied by a single perforating vessel
options.B: a composite block of tissue supplied by a single source artery
options.C: the venous drainage basin of a single named muscle belly
options.D: the zone of stasis surrounding a deep thermal burn wound

exp: An angiosome (Taylor & Palmer) is a composite three-dimensional block of tissue — skin, fascia, muscle, and bone — supplied by a single source artery and drained by its accompanying vein; the body is a jigsaw of these units linked by choke vessels. This concept underpins flap design and the delay phenomenon. A describes a single perforator's territory (a subunit); C and D are distractors (D belongs to burn pathophysiology).
hy: Angiosome = one source artery's full tissue block.
exptable: tissue block from one source artery
no capillary flow despite patent anastomosis

stem: Using the Parkland formula, the total first-24-hour fluid volume for a 70 kg adult with 40% TBSA burns is:
exp: The Parkland formula estimates the first-24-hour crystalloid need after a major burn as 4 mL × body weight (kg) × %TBSA of lactated Ringer's: 4 × 70 × 40 = 11,200 mL. It guides initial resuscitation of the massive capillary leak; the rate is then titrated to urine output, not given blindly. 5,600 halves the multiplier; 16,800 and 22,400 over-scale it (6 and 8 mL/kg).
hy: 4 mL/kg/%TBSA — memorize the multiplier.
exptable: half in first 8 h, half over next 16 h

stem: Under the Parkland formula, the first half of the calculated volume is delivered over the:

options.A: first 4 hours
options.B: first 8 hours
options.C: first 12 hours
options.D: first 16 hours

exp: Parkland delivers half the calculated volume in the first 8 hours (timed from the moment of injury, not from arrival) and the second half over the next 16 hours, because capillary leak is greatest early. The endpoint is urine output — about 0.5 mL/kg/h in adults and 1.0 mL/kg/h in children — and the rate is adjusted up or down to hit it.
hy: Half in 8 h, half in 16 h, titrate to urine.
exptable: half in first 8 h, half over next 16 h

stem: A pressure injury with directly visible or palpable bone is classified as:

options.A: Stage II
options.B: Stage III
options.C: Stage IV
options.D: Unstageable

exp: Pressure-injury staging reflects the depth of tissue loss. Stage IV is full-thickness loss with directly visible or palpable bone, tendon, or muscle, carrying osteomyelitis risk and usually requiring flap reconstruction. Stage II is partial-thickness; unstageable means the depth is hidden by slough/eschar; only Stage IV exposes deep structures.
hy: Exposed bone/tendon/muscle = Stage IV.
exptable: full-thickness; fat may show; no bone/tendon/muscle
purple/maroon intact skin or blood blister

stem: Stage III is distinguished from Stage IV pressure injury by:

options.A: the presence of an intact serum-filled blister
options.B: nonblanchable erythema of fully intact skin
options.C: whether bone, tendon, or muscle is exposed
options.D: a wound base obscured by slough or eschar

exp: The III-versus-IV distinction hinges on a single feature: whether bone, tendon, or muscle is exposed. Stage III is full-thickness with possible visible subcutaneous fat but those deep structures are NOT exposed; Stage IV exposes them. A blister is Stage II; nonblanchable intact-skin erythema is Stage I; a base obscured by slough/eschar is unstageable — each distractor is a real neighboring category.
hy: III = no bone/tendon/muscle; IV = exposed.
exptable: full-thickness; fat may show; no bone/tendon/muscle
purple/maroon intact skin or blood blister

stem: A burn that is insensate with a white, waxy, non-blanching surface is classified as:

options.A: superficial partial-thickness
options.B: deep partial-thickness
options.C: full-thickness
options.D: epidermal (first-degree)

exp: Burn depth is read off appearance, blanching, sensation, and healing. A full-thickness burn destroys the entire dermis including nerve endings, so it is insensate, with a white/waxy/charred, leathery, non-blanching surface and no capacity to heal — it needs excision and grafting. Partial-thickness burns blanch and are painful (superficial PT very painful, deep PT with sluggish refill and reduced sensation); epidermal burns are red, dry, and blanch.
hy: Insensate + non-blanching white = full-thickness.

stem: A 4.5 mm thick melanoma without ulceration is staged:

options.A: T3a
options.B: T4a
options.C: T3b
options.D: T4b

exp: Melanoma T-category is set by thickness then ulceration: >4.0 mm is T4, and absence of ulceration makes it the "a" subtype → T4a (ulceration would make it T4b). T3 covers >2–4 mm, so a 4.5 mm tumor is beyond T3a/T3b.
hy: >4 mm = T4; suffix a/b by ulceration.

stem: A 2.5 cm Merkel cell carcinoma that is node-negative with no deep structural invasion is AJCC stage:

options.A: I
options.B: IIA
options.C: IIB
options.D: III

exp: Merkel cell carcinoma AJCC-8 pivots on a 2 cm primary: ≤2 cm node-negative = Stage I; >2 cm node-negative = Stage IIA; invasion of bone/muscle/fascia/cartilage = IIB; any node-positive = III; distant disease = IV. A 2.5 cm, node-negative tumor without deep invasion is therefore IIA.
hy: Merkel size cut-point is 2 cm.

stem: Which statement about these staging systems is correct?

options.A: Melanoma T-stage is based on Breslow thickness, not Clark level
options.B: Total body surface area in burns includes first-degree burns
options.C: Stage I pressure injury involves partial-thickness skin loss
options.D: The Parkland resuscitation formula uses colloid during the first 8 hours

exp: Only A is true: melanoma T-stage uses Breslow thickness (plus ulceration), not Clark level. B is false — first-degree burns are excluded from TBSA; C is false — Stage I pressure injury is intact skin with nonblanchable erythema (Stage II is the partial-thickness one); D is false — Parkland uses crystalloid (lactated Ringer's), not colloid, in the first 24 h. Each distractor swaps a real rule from a neighboring system.
hy: One true claim per same-class set — here, Breslow over Clark.
src: Bologna Ch113; Neligan Vol IV Ch16/18

stem: A Baker grade III capsular contracture is characterized by:

options.A: a soft breast with a non-palpable implant
options.B: a firm breast with a palpable but not visible implant
options.C: a hardened breast with a palpable and visible implant
options.D: a hard, painful, and visibly distorted breast

exp: Baker grades the firmness of the fibrous capsule that forms around every breast implant: I soft (implant not palpable), II firm (palpable, not visible), III hardened (palpable AND visible distortion), IV hard, painful, and distorted. Grades III–IV are the ones generally taken back for capsulectomy/implant exchange. Option D conflates IV's pain/distortion with III.
hy: III = hard + visible; IV adds pain/distortion.

stem: In the Simon classification, grade IIa is separated from grade IIb by:

options.A: the absolute degree of breast enlargement
options.B: the presence of marked skin redundancy
options.C: a glandular rather than a fatty composition
options.D: unilateral versus bilateral involvement

exp: Simon grades gynecomastia by breast volume and skin envelope. Grades IIa and IIb are both moderate enlargement; the discriminator is skin redundancy — IIa minimal, IIb marked — because marked redundancy changes the operation (adds skin excision to gland/liposuction removal). Grade I is small with minimal redundancy; III is marked enlargement with marked redundancy. Tissue type and laterality are not Simon criteria.
hy: IIa vs IIb = skin redundancy, not size.

stem: In breast ptosis, the term "pseudoptosis" describes:

options.A: a nipple that has descended below the inframammary fold
options.B: a well-positioned nipple with a ptotic, descended lower pole
options.C: a nipple that points directly toward the floor
options.D: both the nipple and the gland sitting above the inframammary fold

exp: Pseudoptosis is a well-positioned nipple (still at or above the inframammary fold) with descent of the lower-pole parenchyma below the fold — a "bottomed-out" lower pole — and is corrected by lower-pole/parenchymal work rather than nipple elevation. True ptosis (Regnault) is graded by how far the nipple has dropped below the fold. A describes true ptosis; C is severe (grade III) ptosis; D is a non-ptotic breast.
hy: Pseudoptosis = good nipple, sagging lower pole.

stem: A Le Fort III midface fracture is also termed:

options.A: a nasomaxillary fracture
options.B: a zygomaticomaxillary fracture
options.C: craniofacial disjunction
options.D: a piriform-rim level fracture

exp: Le Fort classified midface fractures by the level of the transverse fracture line. Le Fort III separates the entire midface from the cranial base (fractures through the nasofrontal junction, orbits, and zygomatic arches), producing "craniofacial disjunction" with mobility at the nasofrontal junction and lateral orbital rims. Le Fort II is the pyramidal "nasomaxillary" fracture (central midface from lateral); Le Fort I is the low transverse fracture at the piriform-rim/buttress level. "Zygomaticomaxillary fracture" is the ZMC, not a Le Fort level.
hy: III = craniofacial disjunction; II = nasomaxillary; I = low transverse.

stem: The Seddon category defined by conduction block with no Wallerian degeneration and full recovery is:

options.A: neurapraxia
options.B: axonotmesis
options.C: neurotmesis
options.D: neuroma-in-continuity

exp: Seddon classified nerve injury by severity. Neurapraxia is a focal conduction block (usually segmental demyelination) with the axon intact, so there is no Wallerian degeneration and recovery is full, typically within ~3 months — it equals Sunderland 1st degree. Axonotmesis has axonal loss with Wallerian degeneration distally; neurotmesis is complete transection with no spontaneous recovery; neuroma-in-continuity is a 4th-degree scar block.
hy: Neurapraxia = block, no Wallerian, full recovery.
exptable: axon out, endoneurium intact → good (1 mm/d)

stem: A Sunderland second-degree nerve injury involves:

options.A: focal demyelination with conduction block alone
options.B: axonal disruption with intact endoneurial tubes
options.C: rupture of axons and endoneurium with scar formation
options.D: complete transection of the whole nerve trunk

exp: Sunderland refines Seddon by which connective-tissue layer is breached. Second-degree injury is axonal disruption with the endoneurial (basal lamina) tubes still intact — equivalent to Seddon axonotmesis — so the regenerating axons follow their original tubes and recover well at ~1 mm/day. First degree is demyelination/conduction block; third degree adds endoneurial rupture and scar (less complete recovery); fifth degree is complete transection.
hy: 2nd degree = axon out, endoneurium intact.
exptable: axon out, endoneurium intact → good (1 mm/d)

stem: In ISL lymphedema staging, Stage II differs from Stage I in that it is:

options.A: reversible with simple limb elevation
options.B: characterized by pitting edema alone
options.C: irreversible and non-pitting (spongy)
options.D: marked by warty epidermal overgrowth

exp: ISL stages lymphedema by reversibility and tissue change. Stage I is reversible — soft, pitting swelling that improves with elevation/compression. Stage II is irreversible and no longer pits (spongy, from fibro-adipose deposition); elevation does not resolve it. Stage III is lymphostatic elephantiasis with skin fibrosis, thickening, and warty overgrowth. A and B describe Stage I; D describes Stage III.
hy: I = pitting/reversible; II = non-pitting/irreversible; III = elephantiasis.

stem: Flexor tendon Zone II — Bunnell's "no man's land" — extends:

options.A: distal to the FDS insertion down to the FDP insertion
options.B: from the proximal digital sheath to the FDS insertion
options.C: beneath the transverse carpal ligament
options.D: proximal to the transverse carpal ligament

exp: Verdan divided the flexor system into zones to guide repair and prognosis. Zone II runs from the proximal reflection of the digital synovial sheath (about the distal palmar crease) to the FDS insertion — here FDS and FDP run together within the tight fibro-osseous sheath, so adhesions are common and outcomes historically poor, earning Bunnell's name "no man's land." Zone I is distal to the FDS insertion (FDP alone); Zone IV is within the carpal tunnel under the TCL; Zone V is proximal to the TCL.
hy: Zone II = digital sheath → FDS insertion.
exptable: distal to FDS insertion → FDP insertion
digital sheath → FDS insertion (no man's land)

stem: Fitzpatrick skin phototype I is defined as skin that:

options.A: always burns and never tans
options.B: burns easily and tans poorly
options.C: rarely burns and tans easily
options.D: never burns and tans readily

exp: Fitzpatrick phototype grades constitutive skin color by its UV burn-versus-tan response and predicts skin-cancer risk and laser/peel settings. Type I always burns and never tans (palest skin); II burns easily and tans poorly; IV rarely burns and tans easily; VI never burns. The scale is about the response to sun, not color alone.
hy: Type I: always burns, never tans.

stem: In a trauma patient, hypothermia is graded "severe" below a core temperature of:
exp: Sabiston grades hypothermia by core temperature, with stricter cut-points for trauma than for accidental hypothermia because cold-induced coagulopathy (impaired clotting enzymes, platelet dysfunction, enhanced fibrinolysis) worsens hemorrhage. Trauma: mild 36–34°C, moderate 34–32°C, severe <32°C; accidental tolerates lower (severe <28°C). Trauma mortality rises steeply and approaches ~100% at 32°C, so 32°C is the critical trauma threshold — 28°C is the accidental-severe cut, and 35°C/30°C are higher (milder) boundaries.
hy: Trauma severe <32°C; accidental severe <28°C.

stem: Induced (therapeutic) hypothermia after out-of-hospital VF cardiac arrest targets a core temperature of:
exp: Induced (therapeutic) hypothermia deliberately cools the patient to slow metabolism and limit ischemia-reperfusion injury — distinct from the harmful spontaneous, accidental, and trauma-associated forms. The ILCOR recommendation for unconscious adults with return of spontaneous circulation after out-of-hospital ventricular-fibrillation arrest is 32–34°C for 12–24 hours. Deeper cooling (28–30°C) is not the standard ROSC target, and 35–36°C is barely subnormal.
hy: Post-VF-arrest target = 32–34°C for 12–24 h.

stem: The latissimus dorsi muscle has which Mathes–Nahai vascular pattern?

options.A: Type I
options.B: Type III
options.C: Type V
options.D: Type II

exp: Latissimus dorsi is a broad back muscle (origin: thoracolumbar fascia, lower ribs, and iliac crest; insertion: intertubercular groove of the humerus; action: shoulder adduction/extension/internal rotation). Its dominant pedicle is the thoracodorsal artery (from the subscapular off the axillary), with secondary segmental perforators from the posterior intercostal and lumbar arteries — the one-dominant-plus-secondary-segmental arrangement that defines Mathes–Nahai Type V and gives it two arcs of rotation. Clinically it is a workhorse pedicled or free flap for breast reconstruction, chest-wall and back coverage, and functional muscle transfer (e.g., restoring elbow flexion), and the segmental supply allows a reverse turnover flap for midline back defects.
hy: Latissimus = Type V (thoracodorsal dominant + segmental).

stem: The most common muscle vascular pattern — a dominant pedicle plus minor pedicle(s) — is:

options.A: Type I
options.B: Type II
options.C: Type III
options.D: Type IV

exp: Type II — one dominant pedicle plus one or more minor pedicles — is the most common pattern in human muscle: the muscle survives on its dominant pedicle once the minor pedicles are divided during elevation, which makes these reliable flaps. Gracilis is the prototype (dominant pedicle: ascending branch of the medial circumflex femoral artery), a slender medial-thigh adductor used as a free functional muscle for facial reanimation and for perineal/vaginal reconstruction; soleus, trapezius, sternocleidomastoid, and biceps femoris share the pattern.
hy: Type II (dominant+minor) is the commonest pattern.

stem: Which muscle has a Type IV (segmental) pattern, making it the least reliable as a flap?

options.A: Gracilis
options.B: Sartorius
options.C: Rectus abdominis
options.D: Latissimus dorsi

exp: Sartorius is the classic Type IV muscle: it is fed by multiple segmental pedicles entering along its length (from the superficial femoral artery), each supplying only its own segment. Dividing more than 2–3 of them during elevation causes distal necrosis, and the arc of rotation is short — so it is the least reliable as a transfer (used mainly as a local turnover to cover the femoral vessels in the groin). Gracilis is Type II, rectus abdominis Type III, latissimus Type V.
hy: Sartorius/tibialis anterior = Type IV = worst flap.

stem: The rectus abdominis muscle has which Mathes–Nahai vascular pattern?

options.A: Type I
options.B: Type II
options.C: Type III
options.D: Type V

exp: Rectus abdominis is Type III — two dominant pedicles from separate sources (the superior epigastric from the internal mammary, and the inferior epigastric from the external iliac), so it can be split and will survive on either. This dual supply is the basis of the TRAM/VRAM and the inferior-epigastric-based free flaps; the deep inferior epigastric perforators also supply the abdominal skin used in DIEP breast reconstruction. Type I is single, II dominant+minor, V dominant+segmental.
hy: Rectus abdominis = Type III (two dominant pedicles).

stem: Which muscle is a classic Type I (single vascular pedicle) flap?

options.A: Tensor fascia lata
options.B: Gracilis muscle
options.C: Sartorius muscle
options.D: Pectoralis major muscle

exp: Tensor fascia lata is a classic Type I muscle — a single vascular pedicle (the ascending branch of the lateral circumflex femoral artery) supplies the whole muscle, so the entire unit is reliably perfused from one source. As the TFL/anterolateral-thigh-region flap it is used for trochanteric pressure sores, groin and abdominal-wall coverage. Gracilis is Type II, sartorius Type IV, pectoralis major Type V.
hy: TFL = Type I (single pedicle).

stem: The radial forearm flap is which Mathes–Nahai fasciocutaneous type?

options.A: Type A
options.B: Type B
options.C: Type C
options.D: Type II

exp: The radial forearm flap is fasciocutaneous Type B — a single septocutaneous pedicle (the radial artery and its perforators running in the lateral intermuscular septum) supplies the skin. It is a thin, pliable, well-vascularized flap with a long pedicle, the workhorse for intraoral/tongue and head-and-neck resurfacing and for phalloplasty. Type A is direct-cutaneous/axial (groin, scalp); Type C is multiple segmental perforators (deltopectoral); "Type II" belongs to the muscle classification, not the fasciocutaneous one.
hy: Radial forearm = fasciocutaneous Type B.

stem: A Type IV muscle is a poor flap choice because:

options.A: division of more than 2–3 segmental pedicles causes distal necrosis
options.B: it offers no single pedicle suitable for microvascular anastomosis
options.C: its arc of rotation is excessively wide and inherently unstable
options.D: it carries no musculocutaneous perforators reaching the overlying skin

exp: A Type IV muscle is supplied by multiple segmental pedicles, each perfusing only its own segment; dividing more than 2–3 of them during elevation causes distal muscle necrosis, and because the pedicles are many and small the arc of rotation is limited — so Type IV muscles (sartorius, tibialis anterior) are the least reliable flaps. They do have pedicles and musculocutaneous perforators (so B and D are false); the arc is too small, not too wide (C false).
hy: Type IV fails distally when segmental pedicles are divided.

stem: Which flap is correctly matched to its Mathes–Nahai fasciocutaneous type?

options.A: Groin flap — Type A
options.B: Radial forearm flap — Type C
options.C: Anterolateral thigh flap — Type A
options.D: Scalp flap — Type B

exp: Groin flap = fasciocutaneous Type A: a direct cutaneous (axial) pedicle, the superficial circumflex iliac artery, runs a long superficial course and can be palpated/Dopplered — historically a key pedicled flap for hand resurfacing. The other pairings are wrong: radial forearm is Type B (not C); anterolateral thigh is Type C (not A); scalp is Type A (not B).
hy: Groin = A, radial forearm = B, ALT = C.

stem: A superficial spreading melanoma is sent for molecular testing. Mutation of which pathway is present (BRAF and NRAS combined) in up to ~80% of cutaneous melanomas AND benign melanocytic nevi, marking it as the central driver of melanocytic neoplasia?

options.A: BRAF or NRAS (MAPK pathway)
options.B: GNAQ or GNA11 (Gq pathway)
options.C: KIT receptor tyrosine kinase
options.D: PTEN loss (PI3K–AKT pathway)

exp: The MAPK cascade (RAS→RAF→MEK→ERK) is the master proliferative pathway of melanocytes, and melanoma is fundamentally a disease of its constitutive activation: a mutation in BRAF (a serine/threonine kinase) or in NRAS (a small GTPase upstream of it) is present in up to ~80% of cutaneous melanomas. Crucially, the *same* mutations are found in ordinary benign melanocytic nevi — so MAPK activation alone drives only a proliferative burst that then enters oncogene-induced senescence (a built-in brake), and a nevus stays benign until secondary hits (e.g. CDKN2A or TERT-promoter lesions) release that brake and permit malignant progression. This shared-driver biology is the conceptual core of melanocytic neoplasia and the rationale for BRAF/MEK-inhibitor therapy. The other options are genuine drivers, but each belongs to a *different* melanocytic compartment: GNAQ/GNA11 (Gq-class G-protein α-subunits feeding into PKC/MAPK) define uveal melanoma and blue nevi; KIT (a receptor tyrosine kinase) typifies acral, mucosal, and high-CSD melanoma; and PTEN loss (a lipid phosphatase whose loss disinhibits the PI3K–AKT survival pathway) is a cooperating, not an initiating, event.
hy: BRAF/NRAS (MAPK) is mutated in up to 80% of cutaneous melanomas and nevi — the core driver axis.

stem: BRAF V600E, the commonest BRAF mutation in melanoma, drives constitutive MAPK signaling through which precise molecular change?

options.A: Substitution of glutamic acid for valine at codon 600
options.B: Substitution of lysine for valine at codon 600
options.C: An in-frame kinase fusion that deletes the autoregulatory domain
options.D: A promoter mutation that creates a new transcription-factor binding site

exp: V600E denotes substitution of glutamic acid (E) for valine (V) at codon 600 of the BRAF kinase domain; the negatively charged glutamate mimics the phosphorylation that normally activates the kinase, so it locks BRAF in its active conformation and drives the MAPK cascade independent of upstream RAS or receptor input. Because the lesion is a single point mutation in the ATP-binding kinase domain, it is directly druggable — the structural basis for selective BRAF inhibitors such as vemurafenib and dabrafenib. V600E is by far the commonest BRAF mutation (~90% of BRAF-mutant melanomas) and predominates in low-CSD/superficial-spreading tumors. The distractors are each a real alternative molecular mechanism: V600K (lysine for valine) is a genuine non-E variant enriched in high-CSD/chronically sun-damaged melanoma of older patients; an in-frame kinase fusion that excises the autoregulatory domain is how "pan-negative" (BRAF/NRAS/NF1-wild-type) melanomas activate MAPK; and a promoter mutation that creates a new transcription-factor binding site is the TERT-promoter mechanism — a distinct non-coding lesion that reactivates telomerase rather than a kinase.
hy: V600E = glutamic acid→valine at codon 600 → constitutive MAPK; V600K is the high-CSD variant.

stem: A melanoma on the sole and a separate sinonasal mucosal melanoma are tested. Mutation/amplification of which gene is most characteristic of acral lentiginous and mucosal melanomas, raising the possibility of a KIT-inhibitor (e.g. imatinib)?

options.A: KIT
options.B: BRAF V600E
options.C: CDKN2A
options.D: GNA11

exp: KIT is a type-III receptor tyrosine kinase (the stem-cell-factor receptor, also called CD117) that normally drives melanocyte development; activating point mutations or gene amplification of KIT are found in ~15–40% of acral lentiginous, mucosal, and high-CSD melanomas. These tumors arise on UV-shielded or chronically sun-damaged sites where BRAF is usually wild-type, so KIT becomes the principal MAPK driver — and because it is a kinase already targeted in other cancers, KIT-mutant melanoma can respond to KIT inhibitors such as imatinib (though responses are partial and not durable, and depend on the specific mutation). The distractors anchor to other compartments shown in Table 113.1: BRAF V600E dominates non-CSD/superficial-spreading melanoma of intermittently sun-exposed skin; CDKN2A is the high-penetrance familial-melanoma germline gene that accounts for only ~2% of sporadic tumors; and GNA11 (with GNAQ) marks uveal melanoma and blue nevi, not acral/mucosal tumors.
hy: KIT = acral/mucosal/high-CSD melanoma → potential imatinib target.

stem: Which statement about GNAQ/GNA11 mutations is correct?

options.A: They occur in 80–90% of uveal melanomas and in blue nevi, yet are absent in cutaneous melanoma
options.B: They are the single most common driver found in high-CSD cutaneous melanomas of the elderly
options.C: They are present in roughly 50–60% of superficial spreading (low-CSD) cutaneous melanomas
options.D: They are the activating mutation that defines Spitz melanomas and atypical Spitz tumors

exp: GNAQ and GNA11 encode the α-subunits of Gq-class heterotrimeric G proteins; their activating mutations lock the G protein "on," constitutively signaling through phospholipase-C/PKC and downstream MAPK. These mutations occur in 80–90% of uveal (choroidal) melanomas and in benign blue nevi — the lineage of dermal dendritic melanocytes — but are characteristically ABSENT in conventional epidermal cutaneous melanoma, which is the classic board discriminator (they mark the dermal/ocular melanocytic compartment, not the epidermal one). The wrong options misassign drivers: high-CSD cutaneous melanoma of the elderly is most often NRAS-mutant (with NF1 also common); superficial spreading melanoma is typically BRAF V600E; and Spitz tumors are defined by HRAS mutations or NTRK/ALK/ROS1 kinase fusions, not GNAQ/GNA11.
hy: GNAQ/GNA11 = uveal melanoma + blue nevi, not cutaneous melanoma.

stem: CDKN2A is the major high-penetrance familial-melanoma gene. Which statement about it is correct?

options.A: It encodes both p16 and p14ARF, and explains roughly 2% of all cutaneous melanomas
options.B: It encodes the BRAF kinase and is somatically mutated in about half of all melanomas
options.C: Its germline loss is the defining lesion underlying the BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome
options.D: It is amplified rather than deleted in the large majority of uveal (choroidal) melanomas

exp: CDKN2A is remarkable because a single locus encodes two distinct tumor-suppressor proteins through alternate reading frames: p16^INK4a inhibits CDK4/6, keeping Rb hypophosphorylated to enforce the G1→S cell-cycle checkpoint, while p14ARF inhibits MDM2, thereby stabilizing p53. Loss of CDKN2A therefore cripples BOTH the Rb and the p53 axes at once — disabling two independent guardians of the cell cycle, which is why it is such a potent melanoma-predisposition gene. It is the most common germline mutation in familial (heritable) melanoma kindreds, yet because most melanoma is sporadic it explains only ~2% of all cutaneous melanomas — a denominator trap. The distractors describe other genes: BRAF is the MAPK kinase somatically mutated in roughly half of melanomas; BAP1 (a nuclear deubiquitylase), not CDKN2A, defines the BAP1 tumor-predisposition syndrome; and uveal melanomas are driven by GNAQ/GNA11 (often with BAP1 loss), not by amplification of CDKN2A.
hy: CDKN2A → p16 + p14ARF; commonest familial gene but ~2% of sporadic melanoma.

stem: Loss of NF1, activating both the MAPK and PI3K pathways, is most characteristically found in which melanoma context?

options.A: Desmoplastic melanoma (~25%) and high-CSD melanoma (~30%)
options.B: Uveal melanoma (~50%) and melanoma arising in blue nevi
options.C: Acral lentiginous and mucosal melanoma (~15–40%)
options.D: Spitz melanoma and atypical Spitz tumors of childhood

exp: NF1 (neurofibromin) is a RAS-GAP — a GTPase-activating protein that switches RAS *off* by accelerating its GTP hydrolysis; losing NF1 therefore leaves RAS chronically GTP-bound and active, simultaneously firing both the MAPK and the PI3K–AKT pathways. This makes NF1 a third recurrent MAPK driver class alongside BRAF and NRAS, and it is characteristically enriched in desmoplastic melanoma (~25%, where its high mutational burden also reflects heavy UV damage) and in high-CSD melanoma (~30%). NF1-mutant tumors are part of the "triple-wild-type/pan-negative" group when BRAF and NRAS are absent. The distractors place the wrong driver at each site: uveal melanoma is GNAQ/GNA11-driven (with BAP1 loss in roughly half, marking metastatic risk); acral and mucosal melanoma favor KIT and NRAS; and Spitz tumors carry HRAS mutations or kinase fusions.
hy: NF1 loss = desmoplastic (~25%) and high-CSD (~30%) melanoma.

stem: A germline E318K variant in MITF is associated with which phenotype?

options.A: Light skin, high nevus count, and increased melanoma and renal cell carcinoma risk
options.B: Café-au-lait macules, axillary freckling, neurofibromas, and optic glioma risk
options.C: Numerous early basal cell carcinomas with odontogenic jaw cysts and palmar pits
options.D: Marked photosensitivity and early skin cancers from defective nucleotide-excision repair

exp: MITF (microphthalmia-associated transcription factor) is the master regulator of the melanocyte lineage, driving pigment genes and survival/proliferation. The germline p.E318K variant disrupts a SUMOylation site on MITF, removing a normal post-translational brake so the transcription factor is hyperactive — producing a phenotype of light skin and a high nevus count with an increased risk of melanoma (including amelanotic forms) and, distinctively, renal cell carcinoma (a melanoma–RCC predisposition allele). It is a moderate-penetrance susceptibility gene rather than a high-penetrance Mendelian one. Each distractor is a separate, well-defined cancer-predisposition phenotype: café-au-lait macules with axillary/inguinal freckling, neurofibromas, and optic-glioma risk is neurofibromatosis type 1; numerous early basal cell carcinomas with odontogenic jaw cysts and palmar pits is Gorlin (nevoid BCC) syndrome from PTCH1 loss; and marked photosensitivity with early skin cancers from defective nucleotide-excision repair is xeroderma pigmentosum.
hy: MITF E318K = light skin, ↑nevi, melanoma + RCC risk.

stem: A "pan-negative" melanoma (wild-type BRAF, NRAS, NF1) is found to carry an NTRK gene fusion. Which agent specifically targets this alteration?

options.A: Larotrectinib, a selective TRK inhibitor
options.B: Vemurafenib, a selective BRAF V600 inhibitor
options.C: Imatinib, a KIT/ABL receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor
options.D: Trametinib, an allosteric downstream MEK pathway inhibitor

exp: A subset of "pan-negative" melanomas (wild-type for BRAF, NRAS, and NF1) instead activate MAPK through in-frame kinase fusions — NTRK1/2/3, ALK, or ROS1 fusions — in which a partner gene replaces the kinase's autoregulatory domain, leaving it constitutively on. An NTRK fusion is therefore a true oncogenic driver and is directly actionable with the selective TRK inhibitor larotrectinib (a "tumor-agnostic" approval). Matching the drug to the exact lesion is the point of the item, and each distractor needs a *different* molecular target: vemurafenib is a selective BRAF V600 inhibitor and is useless without a V600 mutation; imatinib targets the KIT receptor of acral/mucosal melanoma; and trametinib is a MEK inhibitor given *with* a BRAF inhibitor to block MAPK downstream, not a stand-alone match for an NTRK fusion.
hy: NTRK fusion melanoma → larotrectinib (TRK inhibitor).

stem: The 2018/2023 WHO classification of melanoma is organized primarily around which axis?

options.A: Cumulative sun damage (high-, low-, non-CSD) plus site of origin
options.B: The Breslow thickness category as measured at the time of diagnosis
options.C: The presence or absence of histologic ulceration of the primary tumor
options.D: Predominance of the radial versus the vertical growth phase histologically

exp: The 2018/2023 WHO classification of melanocytic neoplasia is organized around the *pathway of evolution* — primarily the degree of cumulative sun damage (high-CSD, low-CSD, or non-CSD/UV-shielded) integrated with anatomic site of origin and the characteristic mutation pattern at each (e.g. BRAF V600E in low-CSD, NRAS/NF1/V600K in high-CSD, KIT in acral/mucosal). This framework deliberately links biology to anatomy and replaces the older purely morphologic subtype list, because it predicts the likely driver mutation and hence therapy. The distractors are all genuine, important variables — Breslow thickness and ulceration are the determinants of AJCC T-stage and prognosis, and the distinction between a radial (horizontal, intraepidermal) and a vertical (dermal-invasive, metastasis-competent) growth phase is a key histologic concept — but none of them is the organizing axis of the WHO molecular-anatomic classification.
hy: WHO melanoma classes = cumulative sun damage + site + mutation pattern.

stem: Tyrosinase, gp100/PMEL17, and MART-1/Melan-A belong to which class of melanoma antigen?

options.A: Melanocyte differentiation (lineage) antigens
options.B: Cancer/testis shared tumor-specific antigens
options.C: Mutant tumor-specific neoantigens (e.g. p16)
options.D: Viral oncoproteins from an oncogenic virus

exp: Tyrosinase, gp100/PMEL17, and MART-1/Melan-A are melanocyte *differentiation* (lineage) antigens — normal melanosomal/pigment-synthesis proteins that are overexpressed by melanoma cells and presented on MHC, so autologous cytotoxic T cells can recognize them; this is why they double as both immunotherapy/vaccine targets and diagnostic immunohistochemical markers. They are "self" antigens, which is why targeting them can also cause autoimmune vitiligo. The distractors are the other antigen categories the chapter enumerates: cancer/testis antigens (e.g. MAGE-1/-3, NY-ESO-1) are normally silenced except in germline tissue and re-expressed by tumors; mutant tumor-specific neoantigens arise from somatic mutations such as altered p16/CDKN2A and are truly tumor-restricted; and viral oncoproteins drive a *different* skin cancer — Merkel-cell carcinoma (Merkel-cell polyomavirus) — not conventional melanoma.
hy: Tyrosinase/gp100/MART-1 = melanocyte differentiation antigens.

stem: CTLA-4, the target of ipilimumab, inhibits T-cell activation by which mechanism?

options.A: An inhibitory CD28 homolog that competes for B7, removing costimulation
options.B: A receptor whose ligand PD-L1 triggers apoptosis of tumor-specific T cells
options.C: An inhibitory receptor blocked by the anti-LAG3 antibody relatlimab
options.D: A molecule presenting antigen on MHC class I to cytotoxic T cells

exp: T-cell activation requires two signals: antigen via the TCR, plus costimulation when CD28 on the T cell binds B7 (CD80/86) on the antigen-presenting cell. CTLA-4 is an inhibitory homolog of CD28 that is upregulated *after* the T cell activates and binds B7 with much higher affinity, outcompeting CD28 and thereby stripping away the costimulatory signal — a built-in negative-feedback brake that limits the early (priming/afferent) phase of the response, predominantly in the lymph node. Ipilimumab, an anti-CTLA-4 antibody, releases this brake and was the first checkpoint inhibitor approved. The distractors describe sibling mechanisms: the PD-1/PD-L1 axis drives apoptosis of tumor-specific T cells at the later effector phase in the tumor itself; LAG3 is a separate inhibitory receptor (the relatlimab target); and presenting antigen on MHC class I is the job of the tumor cell/APC, not a function of CTLA-4.
hy: CTLA-4 = CD28 homolog that steals B7 → removes costimulation (ipilimumab target).

stem: During checkpoint-inhibitor therapy, development of vitiligo-like leukoderma is notable because it:

options.A: Correlates with improved progression-free survival
options.B: Mandates immediate permanent discontinuation of immunotherapy
options.C: Signals progression to distant metastatic disease
options.D: Predicts treatment failure and a switch to chemotherapy

exp: Vitiligo-like leukoderma during checkpoint-inhibitor therapy is a melanocyte-directed immune-related adverse event: the same T-cell response unleashed against melanoma differentiation antigens (tyrosinase, MART-1, gp100) also attacks normal melanocytes, producing depigmentation. Occurring in roughly 2–25% of treated patients, it is a *marker of an active, effective antitumor immune response*, and its appearance correlates with improved progression-free (and overall) survival — so it is a favorable on-treatment sign, not a reason to stop therapy. It is typically asymptomatic and managed expectantly. The distractors invert this biology: it does not mandate discontinuation, does not signal metastatic progression, and does not predict treatment failure — quite the opposite.
hy: Checkpoint-inhibitor vitiligo = good prognostic sign (better PFS).

stem: Which is a recognized mechanism of melanoma immune escape?

options.A: Loss of MHC class I molecules from the tumor-cell surface
options.B: Upregulation of MHC class II on all surrounding keratinocytes
options.C: Increased CD28-mediated costimulation of cytotoxic T cells
options.D: Enhanced presentation of tumor neoantigens to dendritic cells

exp: Melanoma evades the immune system by becoming invisible and inhospitable to T cells. Loss of MHC class I from the tumor-cell surface removes the platform on which cytotoxic CD8 T cells recognize tumor peptides, so even a primed T cell cannot "see" the cell — a core escape mechanism listed in Table 113.2, alongside loss/downregulation of the tumor antigens themselves, secretion of immunosuppressive cytokines (IL-10, TGF-β), and upregulation of inhibitory ligands (PD-L1, CTLA-4 pathway). Each of these tilts the microenvironment toward tolerance. The distractors are all immune-*promoting* and therefore the opposite of escape: more MHC class II presentation, more CD28-mediated costimulation, and enhanced neoantigen presentation to dendritic cells would each strengthen, not weaken, antitumor immunity.
hy: Immune escape = lose MHC I/antigens, secrete IL-10/TGF-β, upregulate PD-L1/CTLA-4.

stem: Immune checkpoint blockade was first introduced clinically for melanoma. Which agent was approved first?

options.A: Ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4)
options.B: Nivolumab (anti-PD-1)
options.C: Pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1)
options.D: Relatlimab (anti-LAG3)

exp: Ipilimumab, an anti-CTLA-4 antibody, was the first immune checkpoint inhibitor ever approved, and melanoma was the disease in which checkpoint blockade was first proven — a landmark because it was the first therapy to show a durable overall-survival benefit in metastatic melanoma. The class then expanded in order: the anti-PD-1 antibodies nivolumab and pembrolizumab followed and became the preferred first-line monotherapy (better tolerated, often combined with ipilimumab for high-risk disease), and the anti-LAG3 antibody relatlimab (paired with nivolumab) is the most recent addition. The distractors — nivolumab, pembrolizumab, relatlimab — are all genuine checkpoint agents, but each reached approval *after* ipilimumab, so the discriminator is recalling that CTLA-4 blockade came first.
hy: Checkpoint blockade debuted in melanoma with ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4).

stem: The PD-1/PD-L1 interaction promotes tumor immune escape by:

options.A: Driving apoptosis of tumor-specific T cells while sparing regulatory T cells
options.B: Competing with CD28 for B7 to strip away the early costimulatory signal
options.C: Blocking the loading of antigenic peptides onto tumor-cell MHC class I
options.D: Preventing antigen-loaded dendritic cells from migrating to the draining node

exp: PD-1 is an inhibitory receptor on activated T cells; when tumor (or stromal) PD-L1/PD-L2 engages it, the resulting signal drives apoptosis of the tumor-antigen-specific *effector* T cells while sparing — even favoring the survival of — regulatory T cells, tilting the local microenvironment toward tolerance. Unlike CTLA-4, which acts early during priming in the node, the PD-1 axis operates at the later effector phase *within the tumor*, which is why anti-PD-1 antibodies (nivolumab, pembrolizumab) reactivate exhausted tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes. The distractors belong to other checkpoints/escape routes: competing with CD28 for B7 to remove costimulation is the CTLA-4 mechanism, while blocking MHC-class-I peptide loading or preventing dendritic-cell migration to the draining node are distinct (afferent-phase) ways tumors hide, not the PD-1 mechanism.
hy: PD-1/PD-L1 = apoptosis of tumor-specific T cells, sparing Tregs (effector-phase brake).

stem: The annual increase in cutaneous melanoma incidence in White populations has generally been estimated at:

options.A: 3–7% per year, predicting a doubling every 10–20 years
options.B: Under 1% per year, with essentially stable long-term rates
options.C: About 15% per year, the fastest rate of any human cancer
options.D: About 25% per year, doubling roughly every 3 years

exp: Across White populations the annual rise in cutaneous-melanoma incidence has been roughly 3–7%, a rate that compounds to a doubling every 10–20 years and has made melanoma one of the most rapidly increasing cancers in fair-skinned populations. The increase is partly real (more intense intermittent UV and tanning-bed exposure) and partly an artifact of heightened detection of thin tumors — a nuance that explains why mortality has not climbed in parallel with incidence. Knowing the order of magnitude matters because it frames the public-health rationale for sun protection and screening. The distractors are the neighbouring wrong magnitudes used as traps: a stable/<1% rate understates the well-documented climb, while ~15–25% per year (doubling every ~3 years) wildly overstates it.
hy: Melanoma incidence rising ~3–7%/yr → doubling every 10–20 yr.

stem: Which region carries the world's highest cutaneous melanoma incidence rates?

options.A: Australia/New Zealand (~54 per 100,000 per year)
options.B: Northern Europe (~25–26 per 100,000 per year)
options.C: North America (high but below Australasia)
options.D: Southern Europe (lower than Northern Europe)

exp: Australia and New Zealand carry the world's highest cutaneous-melanoma incidence (~54 per 100,000 per year) — the product of a predominantly fair-skinned, Northern-European-descended population living at low latitudes under intense ambient UV with an outdoor lifestyle. This combination of a susceptible phenotype and a high UV dose is exactly the gene–environment interaction that maximizes melanoma risk. Northern Europe is the next-highest band (~25–26 per 100,000), reflecting the same fair phenotype but far less UV. The other listed regions — North America (high but below Australasia) and Southern Europe (lower than Northern Europe, as the more pigmented Mediterranean phenotype offsets the sunnier climate) — have substantial but lower rates, so the discriminator is simply recalling that Australia/New Zealand leads the world.
hy: Highest melanoma incidence = Australia/New Zealand (~54/100,000).

stem: Which statement about melanoma mortality and tumor-thickness trends is correct?

options.A: Mortality has stabilized/declined since the 1990s, though older men still show rising rates
options.B: Mortality has fallen uniformly across every age group and both sexes in all countries worldwide
options.C: Mean Breslow thickness at diagnosis has risen steadily across recent decades in most populations
options.D: The proportion of thick melanomas at diagnosis falls progressively with each increase in patient age

exp: Melanoma incidence and mortality have diverged: since the 1990s mortality has broadly stabilized or even declined while incidence keeps climbing — a pattern driven chiefly by earlier detection of thin, curable tumors and, more recently, by genuinely effective systemic therapy (checkpoint and targeted agents). The improvement is *not* uniform, however: older men remain a group with persistently rising mortality, partly because they present later with thicker tumors. This is the correct, nuanced statement. The distractors are plausible-sounding inversions of the real trends: the decline is not uniform across all ages and sexes (older men rise); mean Breslow thickness at diagnosis has *fallen*, not risen, with earlier detection; and the proportion of thick melanomas *increases* with patient age (reaching roughly 20% by age 80), rather than falling.
hy: Mortality stabilizing (thinner tumors), but older men still rising; thick-tumor share ↑ with age.

stem: A patient has more than 100 common melanocytic nevi. Approximately what relative risk of melanoma does this confer?

options.A: ~8- to 10-fold
options.B: ~2-fold
options.C: ~3- to 4-fold
options.D: ~20- to 30-fold

exp: Having >100 common melanocytic nevi confers a relative melanoma risk of roughly 8–10-fold, making total nevus count one of the strongest *phenotypic* (non-genetic) risk markers we have. It is powerful because a high nevus count integrates two things: cumulative UV exposure and an inherited melanocytic-proliferation phenotype, and the dose–response is near-linear (each additional band of nevi adds risk). This is also why total-body photography and self-examination are emphasized in high-nevus patients. The distractors are the adjacent risk magnitudes for *other* lesion categories listed in Table 113.3 — multiple solar lentigines confer roughly 3–4×, while atypical (dysplastic) nevi reach much higher (≥5 atypical ~4–6×, up to ~32× at the extreme) — so the reader must match the right lesion type to the right number rather than recall a single figure.
hy: >100 common nevi ≈ RR 8–10×; the dose-response is near-linear.

stem: Above how many clinically atypical melanocytic nevi does relative risk rise sharply (defining atypical mole syndrome), with little further increase beyond that threshold?

options.A: 5 or more
options.B: 2 or more
options.C: 10 or more
options.D: 50 or more

exp: Five or more clinically atypical (dysplastic) melanocytic nevi marks a sharp step-up in melanoma risk and is the threshold used to define atypical mole syndrome — typically present alongside a high common-nevus count (≥50, often >100). The biology is that atypical nevi are both direct (rare) precursors and, more importantly, *markers* of a melanoma-prone phenotype with abundant active melanocytes; beyond about five atypical nevi the studies show relatively little *further* incremental rise, so five is the operational cut-point. The distractors are the adjacent counts that do not define this threshold: 2 atypical nevi is too few to define the syndrome; 10 is where the maximum reported risk (~32×) is seen rather than the syndrome threshold; and 50 refers to the *common*-nevus count, a different marker.
hy: ≥5 atypical nevi = atypical mole syndrome threshold; risk plateaus beyond.

stem: Which phenotype reflects the pheomelanin-predominant, high-risk pigmentation pattern?

options.A: Red hair and blue eyes, a tendency to burn, and an inability to tan
options.B: Black hair and brown eyes that tan readily without ever burning
options.C: Extensive congenital dermal melanocytosis over the trunk and buttocks
options.D: Constitutively dark (Fitzpatrick phototype V–VI) sun-resistant skin

exp: Red hair, blue eyes, and a tendency to burn without tanning reflects a *pheomelanin*-predominant pigmentation phenotype, classically driven by loss-of-function MC1R variants. Melanocytes make two pigments: brown-black eumelanin, which absorbs and shields against UV, and red-yellow pheomelanin, which gives little photoprotection and can itself generate reactive oxygen species under UV (pro-oxidant) — so a pheomelanin-shifted person sustains more UV and oxidative DNA damage, the basis of the elevated melanoma risk. This is among the strongest constitutional risk phenotypes. The distractors are lower-risk patterns: a dark-haired, brown-eyed phenotype that tans readily without burning has efficient eumelanin protection; congenital dermal melanocytosis is a benign deep-dermal pigment finding; and constitutively dark (Fitzpatrick V–VI) skin carries the lowest melanoma risk of all.
hy: Red hair/blue eyes/burns-not-tans (pheomelanin) = high-risk phenotype.

stem: Regarding ultraviolet exposure and melanoma, which statement is correct?

options.A: Indoor tanning before age 35 substantially raises risk and is a classified human carcinogen
options.B: Intense intermittent sun exposure lowers melanoma risk relative to chronic daily exposure
options.C: Blistering childhood sunburns bear essentially no relationship to later melanoma risk
options.D: Residence at equatorial latitudes is protective through gradual pigment adaptation

exp: Indoor (artificial-UV) tanning substantially raises melanoma risk, and the effect is greatest when exposure begins young — first use before age 35 carries the highest excess risk because early UV damage to dividing melanocytes has the longest latency to act. On this evidence the WHO/IARC classifies tanning devices as a Group 1 human carcinogen, the basis for legislation restricting minors' access. The distractors are factual reversals of established UV epidemiology: intense *intermittent* recreational sun exposure (and blistering childhood sunburns) *raises* melanoma risk rather than lowering it; sunburns — especially in childhood — are firmly linked to later melanoma; and residence at equatorial/low latitudes *increases* UV dose and risk in susceptible skin rather than being protective.
hy: Tanning beds <35 = carcinogen, ↑melanoma; intermittent sun & childhood sunburns also raise risk.

stem: Which population has increased melanoma risk specifically because of acquired immunosuppression?

options.A: Solid-organ transplant recipients and people living with HIV infection
options.B: Patients using topical retinoids long-term for chronic inflammatory acne
options.C: Patients with active, spreading, non-segmental generalized vitiligo
options.D: Otherwise healthy women taking combined oral contraceptive pills

exp: Acquired immunosuppression raises melanoma risk by weakening immune surveillance against transformed melanocytes — the same T-cell response that checkpoint inhibitors exploit therapeutically. The two paradigmatic groups are solid-organ transplant recipients on chronic immunosuppressants and people living with HIV; both show increased melanoma incidence and often worse outcomes, underscoring that intact immunity normally restrains the disease. The distractors are non-risks or even inverse associations: long-term topical retinoids for acne are not a melanoma risk factor; vitiligo, far from raising risk, actually correlates with a *better* response to checkpoint immunotherapy; and combined oral contraceptives have not been shown to increase melanoma risk.
hy: Transplant recipients and HIV = immunosuppression-related ↑melanoma risk.

stem: Why is prophylactic removal of clinically banal melanocytic nevi NOT recommended?

options.A: Single-nevus transformation is ~1/thousands (common) to 1/hundreds (atypical), and most melanoma is de novo
options.B: Banal melanocytic nevi essentially never carry any of the driver mutations that are also seen in melanoma
options.C: Excising a melanocytic nevus paradoxically raises the risk of a melanoma developing later at distant sites
options.D: Melanomas that arise within a pre-existing benign nevus are uniformly fatal regardless of complete excision

exp: Prophylactic excision of clinically banal nevi is not recommended because the arithmetic does not support it: any single common nevus has a transformation risk on the order of 1 in thousands (1 in hundreds for an atypical nevus), and most melanomas arise *de novo* in normal-appearing skin rather than within a pre-existing nevus — so removing benign moles would entail enormous numbers of excisions to prevent very few cancers, while doing nothing about the de-novo majority. The correct strategy is surveillance and excision only of clinically suspicious or changing lesions. The distractors are each false: banal nevi *can* and frequently do carry BRAF and other melanoma driver mutations (which is exactly why driver presence alone does not equal malignancy); excising a nevus does not raise the risk of melanoma at distant sites; and melanomas that do arise within a nevus are not uniformly fatal — outcome depends on stage, as for any melanoma.
hy: Single-nevus transformation ≈ 1/thousands–1/hundreds; most melanoma is de novo → don't excise banal nevi.

stem: Approximately what proportion of familial melanoma kindreds carry a germline CDKN2A mutation?

options.B: About 2%
options.D: About 5%

exp: Germline CDKN2A mutations are detected in roughly 20–40% of familial melanoma kindreds (the reported range spans about 5–70% by geographic region and how stringently "familial" is defined). CDKN2A is the major high-penetrance familial-melanoma gene, but even within selected high-risk families it accounts for only a minority — which is why multi-gene panels and counseling are still needed when CDKN2A is negative. The classic trap is the adjacent figure ~2%, which is a *different denominator*: that is the fraction of *all cutaneous melanomas* (the mostly sporadic total) attributable to CDKN2A, not the proportion of familial kindreds carrying it. The other options (70–90%, ~5%) are simply the wrong magnitudes for kindred-level carriage.
hy: CDKN2A in 20–40% of familial kindreds, but only ~2% of all cutaneous melanomas.

stem: Which statement about MC1R variants and melanoma is correct?

options.A: They are low/moderate-penetrance pigment alleles; two variants raise BRAF-mutant melanoma risk up to ~17×
options.B: They constitute the single highest-penetrance familial melanoma susceptibility gene currently known
options.C: They encode the p16 tumor-suppressor protein that brakes the CDK4/6–Rb cell-cycle checkpoint
options.D: They are somatic UV-induced mutations rather than inherited germline pigmentation gene variants

exp: MC1R encodes the melanocortin-1 receptor, the switch that, when activated, shifts melanocytes from making pheomelanin to protective eumelanin; common loss-of-function MC1R variants are inherited, low-to-moderate-penetrance pigmentation alleles (the "red-hair-color" variants). They raise overall melanoma risk only modestly (~1.5–3×), but the striking finding is a gene–gene interaction: carrying two MC1R variants raises the risk of *BRAF-mutant* melanoma specifically by up to ~17-fold — pigment genotype amplifying a somatic-driver-defined subset. The distractors misclassify MC1R: the highest-penetrance familial melanoma gene is CDKN2A, not MC1R; the p16 tumor-suppressor protein is encoded by CDKN2A (not MC1R); and MC1R variants are inherited germline polymorphisms, not somatic UV-induced mutations.
hy: MC1R = pigmentation susceptibility allele; 2 variants → up to 17× BRAF-mutant melanoma.

stem: A patient develops several small red-orange papules ("BAPomas"/MBAITs) showing loss of nuclear BAP1 staining. The associated tumor syndrome predisposes to which constellation?

options.A: Cutaneous and uveal melanoma, mesothelioma, and renal cell carcinoma
options.B: Pancreatic carcinoma, astrocytoma, and early-onset cutaneous melanoma
options.C: Medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, and mucosal neuromas
options.D: Basal cell carcinomas with palmar pits, jaw cysts, and medulloblastoma

exp: BAP1 (BRCA1-associated protein-1) is a nuclear deubiquitylase and tumor suppressor; germline loss-of-function causes the BAP1 tumor-predisposition syndrome, which predisposes to cutaneous *and* uveal melanoma, mesothelioma, and renal cell carcinoma (with lesser basal cell carcinoma, meningioma, and cholangiocarcinoma risk). Its hallmark skin lesions are BAP1-inactivated melanocytomas — variously called MBAITs or "BAPomas" — small red-orange papules whose diagnosis rests on loss of nuclear BAP1 staining on immunohistochemistry, mirroring the germline second hit. Recognizing the skin clue lets the dermatologist flag a kindred at risk for lethal internal tumors. The distractors are other tumor syndromes: pancreatic carcinoma plus neural tumors with early melanoma fits CDKN2A (familial melanoma–pancreatic cancer syndrome); medullary thyroid carcinoma with pheochromocytoma and mucosal neuromas is MEN2; and basal cell carcinomas with palmar pits, jaw cysts, and medulloblastoma is Gorlin (nevoid BCC) syndrome.
hy: BAP1 syndrome = cutaneous + uveal melanoma, mesothelioma, RCC; MBAITs lose nuclear BAP1.

stem: By the AAD "rule of 3," genetic testing for familial melanoma is indicated for an individual with:

options.A: ≥3 primary invasive melanomas, or a family with ≥3 melanoma/pancreatic-cancer diagnoses on one side
options.B: A single thin invasive melanoma in a person who has no relevant personal or family history of cancer
options.C: Any patient over the age of 40 years who presents with their first single primary invasive melanoma
options.D: Only those patients in whom a pathogenic germline CDKN2A mutation has already been formally confirmed

exp: The AAD "rule of 3" is a practical filter that identifies the individuals most likely to carry a CDKN2A (or related) germline mutation and therefore most likely to benefit from genetic testing/counseling: a *personal* history of ≥3 primary invasive melanomas, OR a *family* with ≥3 combined invasive-melanoma and/or pancreatic-cancer diagnoses among close relatives on the same side of the family (reflecting the CDKN2A melanoma–pancreatic-cancer link). The threshold concentrates testing where the pre-test probability of a high-penetrance mutation is meaningful, avoiding low-yield testing of sporadic cases. The distractors fall below this bar: a single thin melanoma in a person with no relevant history, or simply being over age 40 with a first melanoma, does not meet criteria; and an already-confirmed pathogenic mutation is the *result* of testing, not a prerequisite for it.
hy: Rule of 3 = ≥3 melanomas in one person, or ≥3 melanoma/pancreatic dx on one side of the family.

stem: For genetic risk assessment of familial melanoma, current AAD guidance prefers:

options.A: Multi-gene panel testing rather than single-gene (CDKN2A-only) testing
options.B: Single-gene CDKN2A testing alone as sufficient in essentially all cases
options.C: Routine germline testing of every patient who presents with one melanoma
options.D: Deferring counseling entirely unless a pathogenic mutation is already known

exp: Current AAD guidance prefers multi-gene panel testing over CDKN2A-only testing for familial-melanoma risk assessment, because melanoma predisposition is genetically heterogeneous: a CDKN2A-negative kindred may still carry mutations in CDK4, BAP1, MITF, POT1, or other susceptibility genes, several of which also raise the risk of *internal* malignancies (e.g. pancreatic cancer, mesothelioma, RCC) that change surveillance. A panel captures these in one assay and avoids the false reassurance of a negative single-gene test. The distractors over- or under-reach: germline testing is *not* recommended for every patient with a single melanoma (only for those meeting criteria such as the rule of 3); single-gene CDKN2A testing alone is no longer the preferred approach; and genetic counseling is recommended for appropriate patients regardless of whether a mutation has already been identified.
hy: Use multi-gene panels (not CDKN2A alone) for familial-melanoma genetic testing.

stem: The Nambour (Queensland) randomized trial provided the pivotal evidence that regular sunscreen use:

options.A: Reduced new primary melanoma incidence by ~50–73% at 10-year follow-up
options.B: Had no effect on melanoma but reduced actinic keratosis counts only
options.C: Increased melanoma risk by lowering circulating vitamin D levels
options.D: Lowered melanoma mortality but left overall incidence unchanged

exp: The Nambour Skin Cancer Prevention trial (Queensland, Australia) is the pivotal randomized controlled trial — the highest level of evidence — showing that sunscreen prevents melanoma, not merely keratinocyte cancers: regular daily application of SPF-16 sunscreen reduced new primary cutaneous melanoma incidence by roughly 50–73% when measured about 10 years after the intervention period.
