Because melanoma develops slowly, the benefit only became apparent at long follow-up, which is why this trial is so often cited as the definitive sun-protection evidence. The distractors understate or invert the result: the trial demonstrated a genuine reduction in *melanoma* (not an actinic-keratosis-only effect); it did not increase risk through lowered vitamin D; and its headline endpoint was melanoma *incidence*, not mortality.
hy: Nambour RCT: daily sunscreen ↓ new melanoma ~50–73% at 10 yr.

stem: Which is the most common subtype of cutaneous melanoma in light-skinned individuals, accounting for ~60–70% of cases and arising most often in the fourth-to-sixth decades?

options.A: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.B: Nodular melanoma
options.C: Lentigo maligna melanoma
options.D: Acral lentiginous melanoma

exp: Superficial spreading melanoma (SSM) — broadly corresponding to "low-CSD" (low cumulative-sun-damage) melanoma, the WHO molecular pathway driven by intermittent sun exposure and BRAF mutation in younger skin — is the commonest subtype in light-skinned individuals (~60–70%), diagnosed most often in the fourth-to-sixth decades. The defining biology is a prolonged radial (horizontal) growth phase: melanocytes spread laterally within and just beneath the epidermis as a slowly enlarging, flat, irregularly pigmented macule for months to years before any vertical (dermal-invasive) phase develops, which is why SSM is often caught thin and curable. Histologically this radial phase shows pagetoid (upward) scatter of atypical melanocytes across the epidermis. The frequency makes it the natural "default" answer for a typical truncal melanoma in a White patient, and recognizing the radial-then-vertical sequence is the key concept that separates it from the others. The same-class distractors are the other classic subtypes with distinct epidemiology and growth biology: nodular melanoma (~10–25%, sixth decade, de novo vertical growth with no radial phase), lentigo maligna melanoma (~10–15%, seventh decade, high-CSD sun-damaged face), and acral lentiginous melanoma (~2–5%, palms/soles/nail, not UV-driven).
hy: SSM = most common (~60–70%), low-CSD, radial then vertical growth.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Types of Primary Melanomas, Table 113.5

stem: Which statement about superficial spreading melanoma (SSM) is correct?

options.A: It favors the back in men and the legs in women, arising in a pre-existing nevus in about half of cases
options.B: It arises de novo as a vertical-growth-phase tumor without any preceding horizontal radial growth phase
options.C: It occurs almost exclusively on the chronically sun-damaged facial skin of much older elderly patients
options.D: It is the melanoma subtype most disproportionately frequent in darkly pigmented non-White populations

exp: SSM shows a sex-specific site distribution that reflects intermittent recreational sun exposure: it favors the trunk (especially the back) in men and the legs in women, the body areas burned during sunbathing. It is also the subtype most likely to arise within a pre-existing melanocytic nevus — roughly half in some series — although the absolute transformation risk of any single nevus remains tiny, so prophylactic removal of ordinary nevi is not justified; the practical point is that a *changing* nevus on the trunk/legs warrants attention. This nevus-association ties to the low-CSD/BRAF pathway, in which acquired nevi and SSM share the BRAF V600E driver. The same-class distractors describe the other subtypes: arising de novo as pure vertical growth (no nevus, no radial phase) is nodular melanoma; chronically sun-damaged facial skin of the elderly is lentigo maligna melanoma (high-CSD); and a disproportionate share of melanomas in darker-skinned patients is acral lentiginous melanoma.
hy: SSM: back (men)/legs (women); ~50% arise in a pre-existing nevus.

stem: Which feature distinguishes nodular melanoma (NM) from the other growth-pattern subtypes?

options.A: It arises de novo as a vertical-growth-phase tumor with no preceding radial growth phase
options.B: It is defined by a prolonged radial growth phase confined for years to the epidermis
options.C: It is the subtype most strongly correlated with high cumulative ultraviolet sun damage
options.D: It almost always retains heavy uniform pigmentation and very rarely ulcerates or bleeds

exp: Nodular melanoma (NM) is distinguished from every other growth-pattern subtype by arising essentially de novo as a vertical-growth-phase tumor — invasive dermal proliferation from the outset — without the prolonged intraepidermal radial growth phase that characterizes SSM, LMM, and ALM. Because there is little or no "in situ" lead-time, NM presents as a rapidly enlarging, often symmetric, dome-shaped papule or nodule and is therefore detected late and thick; the radial phase is what normally gives clinicians the ABCDE window, and NM skips it. This is also why NM contributes disproportionately to thick melanomas and to melanoma deaths despite being less common. The same-class distractors invert these facts: a prolonged epidermal radial phase describes SSM/LMM; cumulative sun damage and a facial location characterize LMM (high-CSD); and the claim that NM cannot ulcerate/bleed or be amelanotic is false — NM commonly ulcerates, bleeds, and can be pigment-poor (hence the EFG rule).
hy: NM = de novo vertical growth, no radial phase → presents thick.

stem: Although nodular melanoma represents only ~10–25% of melanomas, why does it contribute disproportionately to melanoma deaths?

options.A: It is detected late and thick — making up ~50% of melanomas >2 mm and ~40% of fatal cases
options.B: It is intrinsically more resistant to systemic chemotherapy than other subtypes at equal stage
options.C: Its melanoma-specific survival is far worse stage-for-stage than that of the other subtypes
options.D: It spreads hematogenously to viscera while the other subtypes metastasize only via lymphatics

exp: Nodular melanoma's outsized contribution to mortality is fundamentally a *detection* problem rather than a uniquely lethal cell biology: because it lacks the intraepidermal radial growth phase, it grows vertically (into the dermis, the route to metastasis) from the start and is found late and thick — accounting for nearly half of melanomas thicker than 2 mm at diagnosis and roughly 40% of ultimately fatal cases. Breslow thickness is the dominant survival driver, and NM simply presents on the wrong side of that curve. Stage-for-stage (i.e. matched for thickness, ulceration and nodal status), NM survival is not clearly worse than other subtypes — the lesson is that earlier detection, not different chemotherapy, is what would change its outcomes. The distractors propose biology the chapter does not support: there is no demonstrated stage-for-stage survival disadvantage, no special intrinsic chemoresistance, and no exclusively hematogenous (bypassing lymphatics) spread pattern unique to NM.
hy: NM kills via late/thick detection (~40% of fatal cases), not worse stage-for-stage biology.

stem: Lentigo maligna melanoma (LMM, high-CSD melanoma) characteristically:

options.A: Arises on sun-damaged face/scalp of older adults from a macular in-situ precursor (lentigo maligna)
options.B: Arises on the trunk of young adults who have had intense intermittent recreational sun exposure
options.C: Occurs on the palms, soles, and nail units largely independent of ultraviolet sun exposure
options.D: Presents abruptly as a rapidly growing amelanotic dermal nodule over a few weeks to months

exp: Lentigo maligna melanoma (LMM) is the "high-CSD" (high cumulative-sun-damage) subtype — the WHO pathway driven by chronic, year-round UV exposure rather than intermittent burns — and therefore develops on the most photodamaged skin: classically the face, and the bald scalp/ears of older men. Its natural history is slow: it begins as lentigo maligna (LM), an in-situ macular precursor that enlarges horizontally over years as a tan-brown irregular patch, and only later does an invasive (vertical-phase) papule or nodule supervene, at which point it is termed LMM. The background skin shows marked solar elastosis, and the proliferation is lentiginous (single atypical melanocytes along the basal layer) with extension down adnexal/follicular epithelium — features that make margins hard to judge clinically. The same-class distractors describe other subtypes: the trunk of younger adults with intermittent sun is SSM (low-CSD); acral/nail sites independent of UV are ALM; and a rapidly growing pigment-poor nodule is nodular melanoma.
hy: LMM = sun-damaged face/scalp of elderly; macular LM precursor.

stem: Regarding lentigo maligna (LM), which statement is correct?

options.A: LM is melanoma in situ of sun-damaged skin; about 5% of LM lesions progress to invasive melanoma
options.B: LM is by definition an invasive melanoma as soon as it becomes clinically pigmented and visible
options.C: LM is a wholly benign lentigo of photodamaged skin with essentially no malignant potential at all
options.D: Roughly half of all LM lesions progress to frank invasive melanoma within the first year of onset

exp: Lentigo maligna (LM) is melanoma *in situ* — atypical melanocytes confined to the epidermis (no breach of the basement membrane) — arising in chronically sun-damaged skin, and it is the precursor to invasive lentigo maligna melanoma. The estimated lifetime progression to invasive disease is on the order of ~5%, but the true rate is genuinely uncertain because LM is often excised once recognized and because it is hard to date its onset. Two clinical consequences follow: LM is a true malignancy (not a benign lentigo) that warrants definitive treatment, yet its slow, low-percentage progression supports less-aggressive options (e.g. topical imiquimod or radiotherapy) when surgery is impractical in elderly facial sites. The distractors misstate its nature: LM is in situ, not invasive by definition; it is not a benign lesion without malignant potential; and a ~50%-within-a-year progression figure grossly overstates the rate.
hy: LM = melanoma in situ of CSD skin; ~5% progress to invasive.

stem: Which statement about acral lentiginous melanoma (ALM) is correct?

options.A: Its absolute incidence is similar across races, but it is a far higher proportion of melanomas in Black/Asian patients
options.B: It is caused by cumulative ultraviolet sun exposure delivered directly to the palms and soles over decades
options.C: It is the single most common melanoma subtype overall among non-Hispanic White patients in the SEER data
options.D: It is biologically and genetically identical to superficial spreading melanoma despite its acral location

exp: The pivotal epidemiologic point about acral lentiginous melanoma (ALM) is that its *absolute* incidence is roughly similar across racial/ethnic groups, because ALM arises on UV-protected glabrous skin (palms, soles, nail unit) and is not driven by sun exposure. What differs is the *relative* proportion: darker-skinned populations develop few UV-related (SSM/LMM) melanomas, so ALM makes up a disproportionate share of their melanomas — up to ~36% in Black and ~22% in Asian patients, versus only ~2% in non-Hispanic Whites. This is a classic exam trap, because it is tempting (but wrong) to conclude that ALM is biologically "more aggressive in" or "more common in" dark skin in an absolute sense. ALM is also genetically distinct, frequently harboring KIT or cyclin-pathway alterations rather than the BRAF mutations of low-CSD SSM. The distractors are false on each count: ALM is not UV-driven, it is not the commonest subtype in White patients (SSM is), and it is not molecularly identical to SSM.
hy: ALM: similar absolute incidence across races, but up to ~36% of melanomas in Black patients.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Acral Lentiginous Melanoma, Table 113.5

stem: ALM is frequently diagnosed at an advanced stage. Which factor most contributes to this delay?

options.A: Primary misdiagnosis (e.g. as a wart or traumatic lesion) occurs in roughly one-third of patients
options.B: ALM grows faster than any other melanoma subtype, outpacing the interval between skin examinations
options.C: ALM is essentially invisible to dermoscopy because of the thick keratin of the acral stratum corneum
options.D: ALM never produces any pigment, so it is uniformly amelanotic and missed on routine inspection

exp: The dominant reason ALM is diagnosed late is misrecognition, not fast biology: roughly one-third of ALMs are initially mistaken for benign acral conditions — a plantar wart, a callus, a fungal or traumatic nail change, or a subungual hematoma — so the lesion is treated topically or simply observed while it thickens. Two factors compound this: low clinician and patient awareness that melanoma occurs on palms, soles and nails (especially in darker skin), and a high threshold to biopsy acral sites because of pain, slow healing and weight-bearing morbidity. The practical teaching point is to biopsy persistent acral pigmented or non-healing lesions and to use dermoscopy, where the *parallel-ridge pattern* (pigment along the raised ridges/sweat-duct lines of acral skin) flags malignancy whereas a parallel-furrow pattern suggests a benign acral nevus. The distractors are unsupported: ALM is not the fastest-growing subtype, dermoscopy genuinely helps via these acral patterns, and ALM is usually pigmented rather than uniformly amelanotic.
hy: ~1/3 of ALMs are misdiagnosed → diagnostic delay and advanced stage.

stem: By the SEER frequency data, which ordering of the four classic subtypes from most to least common (overall) is correct?

options.A: Superficial spreading > nodular ≈ lentigo maligna > acral lentiginous
options.B: Nodular > superficial spreading > acral lentiginous > lentigo maligna
options.C: Lentigo maligna > superficial spreading > nodular > acral lentiginous
options.D: Acral lentiginous > nodular > lentigo maligna > superficial spreading

exp: Per SEER frequency data (Table 113.5), the descending order of the four classic clinicopathologic subtypes is superficial spreading (~68%) > nodular (~16%) ≈ lentigo maligna (~14%) > acral lentiginous (~2%), and the accompanying median ages rise in parallel (SSM ~54 yr, NM ~61, LMM ~69), reflecting that the sun-damage-dependent subtypes appear in older skin. Two anchors fix the ranking: SSM dominates by a wide margin in light-skinned populations (the low-CSD/BRAF intermittent-sun pathway), and ALM is the *rarest overall* despite being the most prominent subtype in darker-skinned patients — an absolute-versus-relative distinction. NM and LMM are close in overall frequency, so the order hinges on getting SSM first and ALM last. The distractors simply scramble this sequence; recognizing the two endpoints (SSM highest, ALM lowest) is enough to eliminate them.

stem: A spindle-cell dermal melanoma stains negative for HMB45, MART-1/Melan-A, and MITF. Which marker is most reliably positive and key to the diagnosis?

options.A: S100 and SOX10
options.B: HMB45 (gp100)
options.C: MART-1/Melan-A
options.D: Cytokeratin AE1/AE3

exp: Desmoplastic melanoma is a spindle-cell tumor embedded in a fibrous (desmoplastic) stroma, and its defining diagnostic trap is that the standard melanocyte-differentiation markers — HMB45 (recognizes the melanosomal glycoprotein PMEL17/gp100), MART-1/Melan-A, tyrosinase, and the transcription factor MITF — are frequently *negative* because the spindled cells are poorly differentiated and contain little melanosomal antigen. The markers that rescue the diagnosis are the lineage transcription factors and S100 family: S100 (a calcium-binding protein, highly sensitive for melanocytes including spindled/desmoplastic forms) and especially SOX10, a neural-crest nuclear transcription factor that reliably labels the spindled component with crisp nuclear staining and less background than S100. So when a dermal spindle-cell lesion is HMB45/MART-1/MITF-negative, a positive S100/SOX10 confirms melanocytic origin and clinches desmoplastic melanoma. The distractors are precisely the markers that *fail* in this tumor (HMB45, MART-1) or an epithelial marker (cytokeratin) that would be negative in any melanoma and instead points to carcinoma.
hy: Desmoplastic melanoma: HMB45/MART-1/MITF often negative → rely on S100/SOX10.

stem: Which set of features is characteristic of desmoplastic melanoma?

options.A: Spindle cells in fibroblastic stroma, frequent neurotropism, NF1 mutation, and possible adjuvant radiotherapy
options.B: Pagetoid epidermal spread of large pigmented epithelioid melanocytes with abundant cytoplasmic melanin
options.C: A dermal dendritic-melanocyte proliferation driven by GNAQ/GNA11, arising as a blue nodule on the scalp
options.D: A KIT-mutant amelanotic tumor of the oral, nasopharyngeal, and anogenital mucosal surfaces

exp: Desmoplastic melanoma is defined histologically by spindled melanocytes dispersed in a collagenous (desmoplastic) stroma, and several features cluster together: it shows frequent neurotropism (perineural invasion, tracking along nerves), it carries a very high UV-mutation burden and loss-of-function NF1 mutations in about a quarter of cases (NF1 is a RAS-GTPase-activating tumor suppressor, so its loss activates RAS signalling), and pathologists subclassify it as "pure" (≥90% desmoplastic) versus "mixed." These features drive management: thick lesions with pure desmoplastic histology, extensive neurotropism, or a head/neck location are at high risk of local recurrence and may be considered for adjuvant radiotherapy, and pure DM has a notably lower sentinel-node positivity rate than conventional melanoma. The same-class distractors describe other melanoma entities: pagetoid epithelioid intraepidermal spread is SSM; a GNAQ/GNA11-driven blue-black dermal scalp tumor is blue nevus-like melanoma; and a KIT-mutant mucosal-junction tumor is mucosal melanoma.
hy: Desmoplastic = spindle cells + neurotropism + NF1; consider adjuvant RT.

section: Melanoma Arising in a Blue Nevus
stem: Blue nevus-like melanoma ("malignant blue nevus") most characteristically:

options.A: Is a blue-black scalp dermal nodule with an associated cellular blue nevus and GNAQ/GNA11 (± BAP1) mutations
options.B: Is an indolent intraepidermal lentiginous melanocytic proliferation arising on the palms and soles
options.C: Carries a BRAF V600E mutation and behaves like a low-CSD superficial spreading melanoma of the trunk
options.D: Is the single most common subtype of cutaneous melanoma encountered in prepubertal children

exp: Blue nevus-like melanoma ("malignant blue nevus") is a rare dermal melanoma that arises in or resembles a (often cellular) blue nevus, presents most characteristically as a blue-black deeply situated nodule on the scalp, and shares the molecular pathway of blue nevi: the lesion carries GNAQ or (less often) GNA11 mutations — these encode Gq-class G-protein alpha subunits that constitutively activate downstream MAPK/YAP signalling — with additional BAP1, SF3B1, or EIF1AX alterations marking the malignant transformation. The blue-black color is the Tyndall effect of melanin sited deep in the dermis. Clinically it behaves aggressively, with high rates of local recurrence and metastasis, so the deep-seated bland-looking nodule belies its danger. The distractors are the wrong subtypes/biology: an indolent acral intraepidermal proliferation describes acral lentiginous in-situ disease, a BRAF V600E truncal lesion is SSM (low-CSD), and "most common pediatric melanoma" is simply false for this rare entity.
hy: Blue nevus-like melanoma = scalp dermal nodule, GNAQ/GNA11 (+BAP1/SF3B1), aggressive.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Melanoma Arising in a Blue Nevus

stem: "Primary dermal melanoma" is distinguished from metastatic melanoma chiefly because it:

options.A: Lacks epidermal involvement/regression yet has a favorable prognosis (~80–100% 5-yr) despite thick depth
options.B: Always displays a well-developed overlying intraepidermal in-situ component above the dermal tumor
options.C: Pursues a uniformly fatal metastatic course despite its small size and limited Breslow thickness
options.D: Is defined by a Breslow depth under 1 mm with brisk regression and a prominent radial growth phase

exp: Primary dermal melanoma is a melanoma confined to the dermis (and sometimes subcutis) with *no* overlying epidermal (in-situ) component and *no* regression, so on biopsy it looks exactly like a cutaneous metastasis from an occult primary — that mimicry is the whole diagnostic problem. The diagnosis is therefore one of exclusion: it is accepted as a primary only after clinical and radiologic work-up finds no other melanoma to be the source. Despite a deceptively thick measured Breslow depth (often ~3–9 mm), it paradoxically carries a relatively favorable prognosis (~80–100% 5-year survival), much better than a true thick conventional melanoma or true metastatic disease — possibly because it is a single localized clone rather than disseminated tumor. The distractors are false: it characteristically *lacks* an in-situ (epidermal) component (the very feature that, if present, would prove a primary), it is not uniformly fatal, and it is not defined by thin (<1 mm) depth — it is usually thick.
hy: Primary dermal melanoma: no epidermal component, thick but favorable (80–100% 5-yr).

stem: A uveal (choroidal) melanoma is analyzed for prognosis. Which molecular finding most strongly predicts poor survival?

options.A: Monosomy 3 (loss of a copy of chromosome 3)
options.B: A BRAF V600E activating point mutation
options.C: A germline CDKN2A (p16) gene deletion
options.D: An NTRK1/3 receptor kinase gene fusion

exp: Uveal (choroidal) melanoma is prognosticated by cytogenetics, and monosomy 3 (loss of one entire copy of chromosome 3, which carries the BAP1 tumor-suppressor) is the dominant adverse marker: it drops 5-year survival from roughly 100% to under 50%, and the risk is worst when combined with gains of chromosome 8q. This reflects uveal melanoma's strong, almost exclusively *hematogenous* tropism to the liver, so chromosome status is used to triage metastatic surveillance. The driver mutations here are GNAQ/GNA11 (Gq-class G-proteins), not the BRAF/CDKN2A pathway of cutaneous disease, which is why those cutaneous markers do not prognosticate eye tumors. The distractors therefore do not apply to uveal prognostication: BRAF V600E and CDKN2A loss belong to cutaneous melanoma, and NTRK gene fusions characterize Spitz tumors.
hy: Uveal melanoma: monosomy 3 → 5-yr survival ~100% to <50%.

stem: Which statement about uveal melanoma is correct?

options.A: It harbors GNAQ/GNA11 mutations, metastasizes to the liver, and is treated with proton-beam radiotherapy
options.B: It responds to immune checkpoint inhibitors essentially as well as cutaneous melanoma of similar stage
options.C: It is driven by BRAF V600E and is therefore treated first-line with combined BRAF and MEK inhibitors
options.D: Its single most frequent site of distant metastasis is the lung rather than the liver or the brain

exp: Uveal melanoma is biologically distinct from cutaneous melanoma on essentially every axis: it is driven by GNAQ/GNA11 (Gq-class G-protein) mutations rather than BRAF, it metastasizes preferentially and often late to the liver via the bloodstream, and modern management favors eye-sparing proton-beam (or plaque brachytherapy) radiotherapy over enucleation when feasible. Because it carries a low mutational burden and few neoantigens, it responds poorly to the immune-checkpoint inhibitors that revolutionized cutaneous melanoma; the agent specifically approved for metastatic uveal melanoma is tebentafusp, a bispecific molecule that engages CD3+ T cells against gp100, and only in HLA-A*02:01-positive patients. The distractors invert these facts: checkpoint inhibitors do *not* work as well here, it is not BRAF-driven, and its dominant metastatic site is the liver, not the lung.
hy: Uveal melanoma = GNAQ/GNA11, liver mets, proton beam; poor ICI response.

stem: Mucosal melanoma characteristically:

options.A: Is ~1% of melanomas, is amelanotic in up to ~35%, and can carry activating KIT mutations
options.B: Is the most common melanoma subtype encountered in fair-skinned White populations
options.C: Is reliably detected at an early stage because of its accessible mucosal location
options.D: Is defined by a BRAF V600E mutation and carries an excellent overall prognosis

exp: Mucosal melanomas are rare (~1% of all melanomas) and arise on non-sun-exposed mucosal surfaces — head/neck (sinonasal, oral), anorectal, and vulvovaginal regions, typically near mucocutaneous junctions. Diagnosis is delayed because these sites are hidden and because up to ~35% are amelanotic (pigment-poor), so they masquerade as polyps, hemorrhoids, or benign mucosal lesions and are usually advanced (often nodal/visceral) by the time they are found — hence a generally poor prognosis. Molecularly, like acral lentiginous melanoma, mucosal melanoma is UV-independent and is enriched for activating KIT mutations (and amplifications), which raises the possibility of targeted therapy with KIT inhibitors such as imatinib, though clinical benefit has been limited. The distractors are false on each count: mucosal melanoma is uncommon (not the commonest subtype), is usually advanced rather than caught early, and is not defined by BRAF V600E or by a favorable outcome.
hy: Mucosal melanoma: ~1%, ~35% amelanotic, KIT-mutant, poor prognosis.

stem: A pregnant woman has a suspicious pigmented lesion. Which management statement is correct?

options.A: Biopsy/excision with lidocaine-epinephrine are safe; if SLNB is needed defer to ≥2nd trimester and avoid blue dyes
options.B: All diagnostic biopsies and definitive excisions must be deferred entirely until after the delivery
options.C: Sentinel-node biopsy should be performed immediately in the first trimester using isosulfan blue dye
options.D: Pregnancy is an absolute contraindication to any surgical treatment of a suspected melanoma at all

exp: A suspicious pigmented lesion in pregnancy must be worked up like any other — diagnostic biopsy and definitive wide local excision under local anesthesia (small amounts of lidocaine ± epinephrine are considered safe) should *not* be delayed, because deferring care risks tumor progression that is far more dangerous to mother and fetus than the minor procedures. When sentinel lymph node biopsy is indicated, guidelines advise deferring it until at least the second trimester to minimize the fetal risk of general anesthesia, and the vital-blue dyes (isosulfan blue, methylene blue) are avoided — methylene blue is teratogenic/risky — so a radiotracer (technetium) is used instead, or nodal status is followed by ultrasound. The governing principle is that maternal cancer outcomes drive management; pregnancy modifies the *technique* (anesthesia timing, dye choice), not the decision to treat. The distractors err in opposite directions: deferring all surgery to after delivery under-treats, while first-trimester SLNB with blue dye is unsafe.
hy: Pregnancy: biopsy/WLE safe; SLNB ≥2nd trimester; avoid blue dyes.

stem: Which statement about hormones, pregnancy, and melanoma is correct?

options.A: Exogenous hormones have not been shown to raise risk, and higher parity is linked to lower melanoma risk
options.B: Combined oral contraceptive pills roughly double a woman's lifetime risk of developing melanoma
options.C: Pregnancy occurring after a melanoma diagnosis markedly increases the risk of disease recurrence
options.D: Postmenopausal hormone replacement therapy is the single strongest known melanoma risk factor

exp: Despite the long-held intuition that melanocytes are hormonally responsive, the epidemiologic evidence does not support hormones as a melanoma driver: exogenous hormones — combined oral contraceptives, postmenopausal hormone-replacement therapy, and fertility treatments — have not been shown to increase melanoma incidence, and higher parity (more pregnancies) is actually associated with a modestly *decreased* risk. Pregnancy at the time of diagnosis has likewise not been shown to worsen prognosis in localized/regional (stage 0–III) disease when matched for stage. The clinical message is reassurance: a melanoma history does not, by itself, forbid contraception, HRT, or future pregnancy, and counseling should be individualized rather than fear-based. The distractors invert the data: OCPs do not double the risk, a subsequent pregnancy does not markedly raise recurrence, and HRT is not a major risk factor.
hy: Hormones/OCP/HRT not shown to raise melanoma risk; higher parity → lower risk.

stem: Because conventional ABCDE criteria often fail in prepubertal children, modified pediatric criteria were proposed. What does the pediatric "ABCD" stand for?

options.A: Amelanotic; Bleeding/Bump; Color uniformity; De novo/any Diameter
options.B: Asymmetry; Border irregularity; Color variation; Diameter >6 mm
options.C: Age young; Brisk mitoses; Caliber of vessels; Depth of invasion
options.D: Atypia; Breslow thickness; Clark level; Dermoscopy pattern

exp: Pediatric (especially prepubertal) melanomas frequently violate the adult ABCDE rule — they are often amelanotic, symmetric, well-bordered, uniform in color, and smaller than 6 mm, more closely resembling a wart, pyogenic granuloma, or Spitz nevus than a classic adult melanoma — so a modified pediatric "ABCD" was proposed to flag them. It stands for Amelanotic; Bleeding or Bump (a raised/bleeding lesion); Color uniformity (uniform color, the *opposite* of the adult "color variegation" cue); and De novo appearance / any Diameter (size is not reassuring in children). The point is that relying on adult criteria in a child causes missed diagnoses, so a different pattern of suspicion is needed. The distractors are the adult ABCDE (which uses Diameter >6 mm and color variegation) or invented mnemonics ("Age/Brisk/Caliber/Depth"; "Atypia/Breslow/Clark/Dermoscopy") that do not exist.
hy: Pediatric ABCD = Amelanotic, Bleeding/Bump, Color uniformity, De novo/any Diameter.

stem: In the ABCDE rule for melanoma, what does "D" denote per Bologna, and which letter do patients most often use to recognize melanoma?

options.A: Diameter >5 mm; "E" (evolution/change) is most often the patient-recognized clue
options.B: Depth beyond 1 mm; "A" (asymmetry) is most often the patient-recognized clue
options.C: Dermoscopy positivity; "C" (color variegation) is the main patient-recognized clue
options.D: Diameter beyond 10 mm; "B" (border irregularity) is the main patient clue

exp: In Bologna's ABCDE public-awareness rule, the letters are Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variegation, Diameter >5 mm, and Evolution — note the "D" is *diameter* (a size threshold of >5 mm in this text), not depth or dermoscopy. The clinically pivotal letter is "E": studies show patients most often recognize a melanoma because the lesion *changed* — it is new, or it grew, darkened, or began to itch/bleed — so evolution over time is the single most useful warning sign for self-detection and is the only dynamic criterion in the list. The other letters describe a single snapshot, whereas a benign nevus that suddenly changes is what prompts presentation. The distractors substitute the wrong meaning of "D" (depth, dermoscopy, or a >10 mm cutoff) or name the wrong patient-recognized letter.
hy: ABCDE: D = diameter >5 mm; "E" (change) is the key patient clue.

stem: A clinician notices one pigmented lesion on the back that looks strikingly different from all the patient's other "signature" nevi. This recognition principle is termed:

options.A: The "ugly duckling" sign
options.B: The Hutchinson sign
options.C: The "Little Red Riding Hood" sign
options.D: The "EFG" rule

exp: The "ugly duckling" sign is the principle that, because most of a given patient's nevi share a family-resemblance "signature" pattern, the melanoma is often the one lesion that looks *different from the others* — a comparative, intra-patient outlier — rather than the one that scores worst on absolute ABCDE criteria. This matters because a person with many atypical nevi may have several lesions that each look mildly "abnormal," so the discriminating move is to find the deviant, not to apply a fixed checklist to each; the sign is reproducible even among non-experts and complements ABCDE. The vignette's lesion "strikingly different from all the patient's other nevi" is the textbook trigger. The distractors are different named signs: the Hutchinson sign is periungual pigment spread in subungual melanoma; the "Little Red Riding Hood" sign refers to surrounding erythema/inflammation; and "EFG" (Elevated, Firm, Growing) aids detection of amelanotic nodular melanoma.
hy: Ugly duckling = the outlier lesion unlike the patient's other nevi.

stem: The "EFG" rule is a clinical aid most useful for detecting which melanoma presentation?

options.A: Amelanotic nodular melanoma — Elevated, Firm, and Growing
options.B: Lentigo maligna on the cheek of a sun-damaged elderly man
options.C: Subungual acral melanoma of the thumb with a pigment band
options.D: Superficial spreading melanoma in situ on the upper back

exp: The "EFG" rule — Elevated, Firm, Growing — was devised precisely for the melanoma that defeats the pigment-based ABCDE rule: the amelanotic nodular melanoma. Because that tumor has little or no pigment, it shows neither color variegation nor an irregular pigment border, so it is missed by ABCDE and by dermoscopy's pigment cues; the EFG triad instead keys on the physical behavior of a vertical-growth nodule (a firm, raised lesion that is enlarging), prompting biopsy of a fast-growing pink/flesh nodule even without pigment. This complements ABCDE rather than replacing it, covering the most lethal and most easily overlooked presentation. The distractors are presentations where pigment-based or other signs already apply: lentigo maligna melanoma (ABCDE/dermoscopy on the face), subungual melanoma (the Hutchinson sign), and SSM in situ (ABCDE on a flat pigmented macule).
hy: EFG (Elevated/Firm/Growing) → amelanotic nodular melanoma.

stem: Clinical (naked-eye) diagnostic accuracy for melanoma and the effect of dermoscopy are best described as:

options.A: ~75% by visual inspection alone, rising to ~90% with expert dermoscopy use
options.B: ~95% by visual inspection alone, with expert dermoscopy adding essentially nothing
options.C: ~50% by visual inspection alone, left entirely unchanged by the use of dermoscopy
options.D: ~30% by visual inspection alone, paradoxically falling further when dermoscopy is used

exp: Unaided (naked-eye) clinical diagnosis of melanoma is correct in at best around ~75% of cases, but in trained hands dermoscopy (epiluminescence microscopy, which visualizes subsurface pigment and vascular structures invisible to the naked eye) raises diagnostic accuracy to roughly ~90% and simultaneously reduces unnecessary biopsies of benign lesions by improving specificity. The crucial caveat is that dermoscopy is not infallible: an estimated 10–25% of melanomas are "featureless" — they lack the specific dermoscopic criteria — so a clinically changing or "ugly duckling" lesion should still be biopsied even if dermoscopy looks bland. The take-home is that dermoscopy is a powerful adjunct used by trained clinicians, not a replacement for clinical judgment or histopathology. The distractors either over- or under-state these accuracy figures or wrongly claim dermoscopy is useless or harmful.
hy: Visual accuracy ~75% → ~90% with expert dermoscopy.

stem: In the two-step dermoscopic algorithm, step 1 asks whether a lesion is melanocytic. Which finding qualifies a lesion as melanocytic?

options.A: A pigment network, aggregated globules, streaks, homogeneous blue, or an acral parallel pattern
options.B: Arborizing (branched) telangiectasias radiating across an otherwise pearly translucent papule
options.C: Multiple milia-like cysts together with comedo-like openings in a "stuck-on" verrucous plaque
options.D: Sharply demarcated red-blue lacunae corresponding to dilated vascular spaces in the dermis

exp: The two-step dermoscopic algorithm first asks "is this lesion melanocytic?" before any melanoma-specific scoring, because the malignancy criteria only apply to melanocytic lesions. A lesion is classified as melanocytic in step 1 if it shows at least one positive melanocytic clue: a pigment network (reticular pigmented lines), aggregated brown globules, radial streaks/pseudopods, diffuse homogeneous blue pigment, or — on glabrous acral skin — a parallel (furrow/ridge) pattern. Only after a lesion is deemed melanocytic does step 2 grade it for melanoma features; mislabeling a basal cell carcinoma or seborrheic keratosis as melanocytic would misroute the whole analysis. The distractors are exactly the criteria that mark *non-melanocytic* tumors and thus *exclude* melanocytic origin: arborizing vessels (basal cell carcinoma), milia-like cysts and comedo-like openings (seborrheic keratosis), and red-blue lacunae (vascular lesion/hemangioma).
hy: Melanocytic clues = network, globules, streaks, homogeneous blue, acral parallel pattern.

stem: Which local dermoscopic feature most specifically raises concern for melanoma?

options.A: A blue-white veil
options.B: Comma-shaped vessels
options.C: A regular narrow network
options.D: Multiple milia-like cysts

exp: The blue-white veil is a confluent, irregular gray-blue to whitish-blue structureless zone, and it is among the most *specific* local dermoscopic criteria for melanoma — its histologic correlate is an acanthotic epidermis with compact orthokeratosis/hypergranulosis (the white component) overlying heavily pigmented melanocytes and melanin deep in the dermis (the blue component, via the Tyndall effect). Because it reflects deep pigment plus epidermal change, it usually signals an invasive (vertical-phase) lesion, which is why it carries such high specificity even though it is not present in every melanoma. The distractors point to benign or non-melanoma diagnoses: comma-shaped vessels and a regular, narrow, evenly-spaced pigment network indicate a benign dermal/junctional nevus, and milia-like cysts indicate seborrheic keratosis.
hy: Blue-white veil = high-specificity melanoma clue.

stem: On dermoscopy, which structure-to-histology correlation is correct?

options.A: Streaks correspond to confluent junctional nests of melanocytes at the lesion margin
options.B: Blue-white veil corresponds to dilated, blood-filled vascular spaces in the upper dermis
options.C: Milia-like cysts correspond to pigmented basaloid-cell aggregates in the papillary dermis
options.D: Regression structures correspond to elongated, regular, evenly pigmented rete ridges

exp: Each dermoscopic structure maps to a specific histologic substrate, and the correct pairing here is that streaks (radial lines/pseudopods at the lesion edge) correspond to confluent *junctional nests* of melanocytes at the dermo-epidermal junction; when these are irregularly distributed around the periphery they signal radial-growth-phase melanoma. Knowing the histologic correlate is what lets you reason about, rather than memorize, dermoscopy. The distractors give mismatched correlates: the blue-white veil reflects deep pigmented dermal melanocytes with epidermal hypergranulosis (not dilated vessels); milia-like cysts are intraepidermal keratin-filled horn pseudocysts seen in seborrheic keratosis (not basaloid cell aggregates); and regression structures (scar-like white areas, peppering) reflect dermal fibrosis and melanophages (not regular rete ridges).
hy: Streaks = confluent junctional nests.

stem: On dermoscopy of a non-melanocytic lesion, arborizing (branched) vessels are the hallmark of which tumor?

options.A: Basal cell carcinoma
options.B: A seborrheic keratosis
options.C: A cherry hemangioma
options.D: Amelanotic melanoma

exp: Arborizing vessels — sharply focused, bright-red, large-caliber telangiectasias that branch like a tree into progressively finer twigs — are the dermoscopic hallmark of basal cell carcinoma; they lie in the superficial dermis under a translucent tumor, which is why they appear so crisp. Recognizing them lets you separate a pigmented BCC from melanoma even when both look brown clinically, since each non-melanocytic tumor carries its own vascular/structural signature. The distractors are those competing signatures: seborrheic keratosis shows milia-like cysts and comedo-like openings (with hairpin vessels); a hemangioma/vascular lesion shows red-blue lacunae (sharply bordered red saccules); and melanoma shows atypical, dotted or irregular polymorphous vessels together with the blue-white veil.
hy: Arborizing vessels = BCC; each non-melanocytic tumor has its own vascular pattern.

stem: Two non-invasive imaging tools are FDA-approved for melanoma. Which pairing of test and performance is correct?

options.A: RCM ~92% sensitivity/~70% specificity; electrical impedance spectroscopy >96% sensitivity/~35% specificity
options.B: Both reflectance confocal microscopy and impedance spectroscopy exceed 95% specificity and replace histopathology
options.C: Electrical impedance spectroscopy has the higher specificity of the two and rarely yields false-positive results
options.D: Reflectance confocal microscopy images only structures lying below the reticular dermis and subcutis

exp: Two non-invasive adjuncts are FDA-approved for melanoma diagnosis, and they trade off sensitivity against specificity. Reflectance confocal microscopy (RCM) uses a near-infrared laser to image cells in the epidermis and papillary dermis at near-histologic resolution *in vivo* ("optical biopsy"), giving a balanced pooled sensitivity ~92% and specificity ~70%. Electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) instead measures how skin resists weak alternating currents — malignant cells alter tissue impedance — yielding very high sensitivity (>96%) but poor specificity (~35%), because benign lesions such as seborrheic keratoses also distort impedance and generate frequent false positives. Both are triage tools that reduce unnecessary biopsies but neither replaces histopathology. The distractors are false: neither supplants histology, RCM (not EIS) is the more *specific* test, and RCM images only the superficial skin, not below the reticular dermis.
hy: RCM ~92%/~70%; EIS >96%/~35% (low specificity).

stem: Which histopathologic feature favors melanoma over a benign melanocytic nevus?

options.A: Absence of maturation — dermal nests and nuclei fail to become smaller with descent
options.B: Architectural symmetry with sharp, well-defined lateral circumscription of the lesion
options.C: Progressive maturation, with smaller nests and nuclei in the deeper dermal component
options.D: Evenly spaced, uniformly sized and shaped junctional nests along the rete ridges

exp: "Maturation" is the normal phenomenon in a benign nevus whereby melanocytes get smaller, lose pigment, and form smaller nests as they descend deeper into the dermis (so the lesion looks "calmer" at its base). The defining histologic clue to melanoma is the *absence* of this maturation — dermal nests and nuclei remain large and atypical at depth — together with overall asymmetry, poor lateral circumscription, and pagetoid (upward, single-cell) scatter of melanocytes through the upper epidermis. Other supporting features are deep/atypical mitoses and confluent, irregularly sized and spaced nests. The distractors instead list the architecture of a *benign* nevus: symmetry, sharp circumscription, progressive maturation with descent, and evenly spaced uniform nests.
hy: Melanoma = no maturation with descent (+ asymmetry, pagetoid scatter).

stem: In the two-phase model of melanoma growth, which statement is correct?

options.A: The vertical growth phase (dermal nests larger than intraepidermal cells) confers metastatic potential
options.B: The radial (horizontal) growth phase is the phase that confers metastatic potential, even when in situ
options.C: Pagetoid scatter of single melanocytes through the epidermis is what defines the vertical growth phase
options.D: Growth phase (radial vs vertical) is a required, scored component of current AJCC-8 melanoma staging

exp: In the two-phase (Clark) model, the radial growth phase (RGP) is intraepidermal and/or superficially papillary-dermal spread with no expansile dermal nodule — single cells and small nests that lack the capacity to metastasize, even when a few cells focally enter the papillary dermis. The vertical growth phase (VGP) is the biologic turning point: an expansile dermal nest or nodule whose cells are larger than, or cytologically/architecturally distinct from, the intraepidermal melanocytes (often with dermal mitoses), and it is this clone that acquires metastatic competence — which is why VGP melanomas can seed lymphatics and blood. Conceptually, RGP is "horizontal/non-metastasizing" and VGP is "deep/metastasizing." The distractors invert this (the RGP is not the metastatic phase), misdefine pagetoid scatter (which is an *intraepidermal*, in-situ feature, not the vertical phase), or wrongly state that growth phase is a formal AJCC staging parameter (it is not — staging uses Breslow thickness, ulceration, and mitotic context, not RGP/VGP).
hy: VGP = metastatic potential; RGP generally lacks it; growth phase is not in AJCC staging.

stem: Which immunohistochemical marker is most sensitive for melanocytes (including spindled/desmoplastic melanoma) and is favored for its easily interpreted nuclear staining?

options.A: SOX10
options.B: HMB45 (gp100)
options.C: MART-1/Melan-A
options.D: CD68

exp: SOX10 is a neural-crest nuclear transcription factor expressed by melanocytes (and Schwann cells), so it is highly sensitive for melanocytic lineage — crucially including the poorly differentiated spindled cells of desmoplastic melanoma, where the melanosome-based markers HMB45/MART-1/MITF are characteristically negative. It is favored over S100 (a calcium-binding protein that is also sensitive but stains many other cell types — Langerhans cells, Schwann cells, fat, eccrine glands) because its crisp *nuclear* pattern is easier to read and produces far less background staining of dermal fibrohistiocytic cells, which is exactly what helps distinguish residual desmoplastic melanoma from scar. Neither S100 nor SOX10 is perfectly melanocyte-specific, so they confirm lineage rather than malignancy. The distractors are less suitable: HMB45 (recognizes gp100/PMEL17) and MART-1/Melan-A are less sensitive and often negative in desmoplastic melanoma, and CD68 is a histiocyte/macrophage marker that does not label melanocytes at all.
hy: SOX10 = sensitive nuclear melanocyte marker (catches desmoplastic), easier than S100.

stem: PRAME immunohistochemistry is useful in melanocytic lesions because:

options.A: It is usually negative in benign nevi but diffusely nuclear-positive in most melanomas (desmoplastic/spitzoid excepted)
options.B: It is positive in essentially all benign nevi while remaining negative in the majority of true melanomas
options.C: It reliably stains desmoplastic melanoma in nearly every case, making it the marker of choice for that subtype
options.D: It is a cytoplasmic marker that is specific for keratinocytes rather than for cells of melanocytic lineage

exp: PRAME (PReferentially expressed Antigen in MElanoma) is a cancer-testis antigen first identified in melanoma-reactive T cells; on immunohistochemistry it is generally *negative* in benign nevi, solar lentigines, and melanocytic hyperplasia of sun-damaged skin, but shows *diffuse nuclear positivity* in the majority of primary cutaneous melanomas and their metastases — and this pattern correlates well with abnormal cytogenetics. That benign-negative/malignant-positive split is what makes it useful for assessing melanoma surgical margins (including lentigo maligna), distinguishing melanoma micrometastases from benign nodal nevi in sentinel nodes, and adjudicating ambiguous melanocytic lesions. Important caveats temper it: only about a third of desmoplastic melanomas are PRAME-positive, spitzoid neoplasms correlate less reliably, and a minority of benign nevi can be positive, so PRAME must always be read alongside conventional H&E. The distractors invert the staining pattern (positive in nevi, negative in melanoma), overstate desmoplastic positivity, or wrongly call PRAME a keratinocyte marker.
hy: PRAME: negative in nevi, positive in most melanoma; weak in desmoplastic/spitzoid.

stem: Which statement about molecular tests used to classify ambiguous melanocytic lesions is correct?

options.A: CGH screens the whole genome for copy-number change, whereas FISH targets a few loci (6p25/RREB1, 11q13/CCND1, 9p21)
options.B: FISH provides broad genome-wide, hypothesis-free copy-number screening across all chromosomes simultaneously
options.C: CGH reliably detects single-nucleotide point mutations as well as balanced reciprocal translocations and fusions
options.D: Gene expression profiling works by sequencing the entire genome of the tumor to identify its driver mutations

exp: The two cytogenetic tools differ chiefly in breadth. Comparative genomic hybridization (CGH) co-hybridizes tumor and reference DNA to detect DNA copy-number gains and losses across the *whole genome* in a single hypothesis-free assay — benign nevi typically show none, whereas most melanomas show characteristic gains (e.g. 6p, 7, 8q) and losses (e.g. 6q, 9p, 10q). Fluorescence in-situ hybridization (FISH) is *targeted*: labeled probes interrogate only a few preselected loci known to be aberrant in melanoma — classically 6p25 (RREB1), 6q23 (MYB), 11q13 (CCND1), and 9p21 (CDKN2A) — so it is faster and works on small samples but can miss aberrations outside those probes. Both detect copy-number change, so neither reliably finds point mutations (e.g. BRAF V600E) or balanced translocations. The distractors invert the roles (FISH is the targeted one, not genome-wide), overstate CGH's reach (it misses point mutations and balanced rearrangements), or misdefine gene-expression profiling (GEP measures mRNA expression of a selected gene set, not whole-genome sequencing).
hy: CGH = genome-wide copy number; FISH = a few targeted loci.

stem: In distinguishing Spitz neoplasms from melanoma, which molecular finding actually favors a Spitz lesion rather than conventional melanoma?

options.A: An isolated gain of chromosome 11p (HRAS) or an isolated loss of chromosome 3 (BAP1)
options.B: A homozygous deletion of the 9p21 (CDKN2A) tumor-suppressor locus by FISH testing
options.C: A TERT promoter mutation, which is associated with extranodal metastatic spread
options.D: A BRAF V600E mutation accompanied by multiple chromosomal copy-number changes

exp: When separating a Spitz neoplasm from conventional melanoma, a few cytogenetic findings are reassuring because they characterize Spitz biology and are *not* seen in ordinary melanoma: about 20% of Spitz nevi show an isolated copy-number gain of 11p (which corresponds to HRAS activation) or an isolated loss of chromosome 3 (corresponding to BAP1 loss), and either, in isolation, favors a Spitz lesion rather than melanoma. The logic is that Spitz tumors are defined by specific driver events (HRAS, or kinase fusions such as ALK/ROS1/NTRK) and a *quiet* genome, whereas melanoma accumulates many aberrations. The distractors instead point toward malignancy: homozygous deletion of 9p21 (CDKN2A), a TERT-promoter mutation (telomerase reactivation), and BRAF V600E accompanied by multiple copy-number changes all argue for melanoma over a Spitz nevus.
hy: Isolated 11p (HRAS) gain or chr-3/BAP1 loss favors Spitz, not melanoma.

stem: In spitzoid neoplasms, which molecular finding is most associated with aggressive behavior (advanced locoregional disease or death)?

options.A: Homozygous deletion of 9p21 (CDKN2A)
options.B: Gain of chromosome 11p (HRAS)
options.C: Isolated loss of chromosome 3
options.D: A kinase fusion without copy-number change

exp: Among the cytogenetic findings in spitzoid melanocytic neoplasms, homozygous (biallelic) deletion of 9p21 — the locus of the CDKN2A tumor suppressor, which encodes the cell-cycle brakes p16 and p14ARF — is the single change most strongly tied to aggressive behavior, i.e. advanced locoregional disease or death; complete loss of both copies removes a key checkpoint and de-restrains proliferation. For this reason, adding the 9p21/CEP9 probe to a melanoma FISH panel significantly increases sensitivity for spitzoid melanoma. A useful gradient: gains of 8q24 (cMYC) and 11q13 (CCND1) also predict an aggressive course but carry *lower* risk than homozygous 9p21 loss, and a TERT-promoter mutation likewise flags worse behavior. The distractors are either lower-risk or Spitz-favoring: isolated 11p (HRAS) gain and isolated chromosome-3 loss favor a benign Spitz nevus, and the 8q24/11q13 gains are real but second-tier compared with 9p21 deletion.
hy: Homozygous 9p21 (CDKN2A) deletion = aggressive spitzoid behavior.

stem: Subungual melanoma may present with longitudinal melanonychia. Pigment extending onto the proximal/lateral nail fold or hyponychium is termed:

options.A: The Hutchinson sign
options.B: The ugly-duckling sign
options.C: The Little Red Riding Hood sign
options.D: The EFG sign

exp: Longitudinal melanonychia is a brown-black pigmented band running along the nail from the matrix to the free edge; while most bands are benign (ethnic, drug-induced, or a nail-matrix nevus), the Hutchinson sign — extension of that pigment beyond the nail plate onto the surrounding skin, i.e. the proximal/lateral nail fold or hyponychium — is the worrying clue that the underlying process is a subungual (acral lentiginous) melanoma invading periungual tissue. Its presence should prompt nail-matrix biopsy. The pathophysiologic point is that pigment "spilling" past the matrix onto skin reflects melanocytic proliferation beyond the nail unit, not a contained band. The distractors are unrelated named signs: the ugly-duckling sign (an outlier nevus), the "Little Red Riding Hood" sign (surrounding erythema), and the EFG rule (Elevated/Firm/Growing, for amelanotic nodular melanoma).
hy: Hutchinson sign = periungual pigment spread in subungual melanoma.

stem: A fair-skinned 50-year-old has this flat-to-slightly-raised lesion on the back showing asymmetry, irregular notched borders, and brown-black color variation. Which melanoma subtype does the morphology best represent?

options.A: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.B: Nodular melanoma
options.C: Lentigo maligna melanoma
options.D: Acral lentiginous melanoma

exp: The recognition cue is a flat-to-slightly-raised, asymmetric plaque on the trunk with irregular notched borders and brown-black color variegation — the textbook look of superficial spreading melanoma (SSM, the low-CSD subtype and commonest melanoma in light skin). The flatness reflects its long radial (intraepidermal/superficial) growth phase, and the color variation reflects uneven melanin and partial regression. The same-class distractors are the other growth-pattern subtypes, separable here by morphology and site: nodular melanoma is a raised dome (vertical growth, not a flat plaque), lentigo maligna melanoma is a tan macule on the chronically sun-damaged face of the elderly, and acral lentiginous melanoma is on the palm, sole, or nail.
hy: Flat asymmetric trunk lesion with color variation = SSM.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Fig 113.8, Superficial Spreading Melanoma
imgcap: Pigmented lesion on the back

stem: This darkly pigmented dome-shaped papule developed rapidly over weeks. Which subtype is most consistent with the appearance?

options.A: Nodular melanoma
options.B: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.C: Lentigo maligna melanoma
options.D: Dysplastic nevus

exp: The recognition cue is a darkly pigmented, dome-shaped (often symmetric) papule/nodule that grew rapidly over weeks — the hallmark of nodular melanoma, which enters the vertical growth phase from the outset with no radial-phase lead-time, hence the fast, three-dimensional growth and a tendency to ulcerate or bleed. Because it skips the flat radial stage, it is often thick and dangerous when found, which is why "rapidly growing nodule" should trigger biopsy regardless of ABCDE. The distractors are other entities: SSM is a *flat* plaque with a radial phase, lentigo maligna is a facial macule on sun-damaged skin, and a dysplastic nevus is a flatter, more symmetric, stable benign lesion that does not balloon over weeks.
hy: Rapidly growing pigmented nodule = nodular melanoma.
imgcap: Pigmented papule

stem: This asymmetric pigmented macule/plaque is on the face of an elderly man with chronic sun damage. Which subtype does it represent?

options.A: Lentigo maligna melanoma
options.B: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.C: Acral lentiginous melanoma
options.D: Nodular melanoma

exp: The recognition cue is an asymmetric, variably pigmented macule/plaque on the chronically sun-damaged face of an elderly man — the classic presentation of lentigo maligna melanoma (LMM, the high-CSD subtype). The flat, ill-defined tan-brown patch on a background of solar elastosis reflects a slow lentiginous radial phase, and clinical margins typically extend beyond what the eye sees, complicating excision. The distractors differ by site and biology: SSM favors the trunk/legs of younger adults (intermittent sun), ALM occurs on acral/nail sites, and nodular melanoma is a raised, rapidly growing nodule rather than a facial macule.
hy: Pigmented facial macule in elderly CSD skin = LMM.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Fig 113.13, Lentigo Maligna Melanoma
imgcap: Pigmented facial lesion

stem: This irregularly pigmented lesion is on the plantar surface. Which subtype is most likely?

options.A: Acral lentiginous melanoma
options.B: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.C: Lentigo maligna melanoma
options.D: Blue nevus-like melanoma

exp: The recognition cue is an irregularly pigmented macule/plaque on the plantar (sole) surface — the classic location for acral lentiginous melanoma (ALM), which arises on glabrous palmo-plantar/subungual skin independent of UV exposure and is the subtype that predominates *proportionally* in darker-skinned patients. On dermoscopy a parallel-ridge pattern (pigment along the raised ridges) supports the diagnosis, distinguishing it from the parallel-furrow pattern of a benign acral nevus. The same-class distractors are the other subtypes by site: SSM favors the trunk/legs, LMM the sun-damaged face, and blue nevus-like melanoma the scalp.
hy: Pigmented plantar lesion = acral lentiginous melanoma.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Fig 113.15, Acral Lentiginous Melanoma
imgcap: Pigmented lesion on the sole

stem: This nail shows a pigmented band with extension of pigment onto the proximal nail fold. The periungual extension is which clinical sign?

options.A: Hutchinson sign
options.B: Ugly-duckling sign
options.C: Little Red Riding Hood sign
options.D: EFG sign

exp: The recognition cue is a pigmented nail band whose pigment spills beyond the nail plate onto the proximal nail fold/periungual skin — the Hutchinson sign, which flags a subungual (acral lentiginous) melanoma rather than a benign longitudinal melanonychia. The spread of pigment onto skin indicates melanocytic proliferation extending past the nail matrix and should prompt matrix biopsy. The distractors are unrelated named signs: ugly-duckling (an outlier nevus), "Little Red Riding Hood" (surrounding erythema), and EFG (Elevated/Firm/Growing, for amelanotic nodular melanoma).
hy: Periungual pigment spread = Hutchinson sign (subungual melanoma).
imgcap: Nail with pigmented band

stem: This skin-colored to light-pink nodule lacked obvious pigment; biopsy confirmed melanoma 4 mm thick. Which descriptor fits?

options.A: Amelanotic melanoma
options.B: Lentigo maligna melanoma
options.C: Superficial spreading melanoma in situ
options.D: Dermal nevus

exp: The recognition cue is a skin-colored to light-pink nodule with little or no visible pigment that nonetheless proves to be a thick (here 4 mm) melanoma — an amelanotic melanoma, which lacks the pigment-based ABCDE clues and is therefore easily dismissed as a benign nodule, basal cell carcinoma, or pyogenic granuloma. This pigment-poor pitfall is exactly why the EFG rule (Elevated, Firm, Growing) exists: biopsy a firm, enlarging pink nodule even without pigment. The distractors are pigmented or benign and so do not fit a non-pigmented thick nodule: LMM and SSM-in-situ are pigmented *macular* lesions, and an intradermal nevus is a benign, stable papule.
hy: Non-pigmented melanoma nodule = amelanotic melanoma.
imgcap: Skin-colored nodule

stem: This blue-black, deeply situated nodule arose on the scalp; histology showed an associated cellular blue nevus. Which subtype is most likely?

options.A: Blue nevus-like melanoma
options.B: Nodular melanoma arising de novo
options.C: Acral lentiginous melanoma
options.D: Lentigo maligna melanoma

exp: The recognition cue is a blue-black, deeply situated nodule on the scalp with histology showing an associated cellular blue nevus — blue nevus-like melanoma ("malignant blue nevus"). The blue-black color is the Tyndall effect of melanin sited deep in the dermis, and the blue-nevus component carries GNAQ/GNA11 mutations (with BAP1/SF3B1/EIF1AX changes marking malignant transformation); the entity is rare but aggressive. The same-class distractors differ by origin/site: a de novo nodular melanoma lacks the blue-nevus association, and ALM/LMM occur at acral and facial sites rather than as a deep scalp nodule.
hy: Blue-black scalp nodule + cellular blue nevus = blue nevus-like melanoma.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Fig 113.19, Melanoma Arising in a Blue Nevus
imgcap: Blue-black scalp nodule

stem: This dermoscopic image of a melanoma shows a confluent gray-blue to whitish-blue structureless area. What is this feature called?

options.A: Blue-white veil
options.B: Milia-like cysts
options.C: Arborizing vessels
options.D: Red-blue lacunae

exp: The recognition cue is a confluent gray-blue to whitish-blue *structureless* zone on dermoscopy — the blue-white veil, one of the most specific dermoscopic signs of melanoma. Its histologic correlate is heavily pigmented melanocytes/melanin deep in the dermis (blue, via the Tyndall effect) beneath an acanthotic epidermis with hypergranulosis (white), so it usually marks an invasive lesion. The same-class distractors are other dermoscopic structures with different "homes": milia-like cysts (seborrheic keratosis), arborizing vessels (basal cell carcinoma), and red-blue lacunae (vascular lesion/hemangioma).
hy: Confluent blue-white structureless area = blue-white veil.
imgcap: Dermoscopy of a pigmented lesion

stem: This H&E section shows atypical melanocytes as solitary units and irregular nests scattered at all levels of the epidermis. This pattern is termed:

options.A: Pagetoid scatter
options.B: Maturation with descent
options.C: Kamino bodies
options.D: Cowdry inclusions

exp: The recognition cue is atypical melanocytes scattered as solitary units and irregular nests at *all* levels of the epidermis (not just the basal layer) — pagetoid scatter, a hallmark of melanoma; when the atypia is confined to the epidermis it defines melanoma in situ. The name comes from the upward "buckshot" spread resembling Paget cells; normal/benign melanocytes stay along the dermo-epidermal junction. The distractors are different concepts: maturation with descent is a *benign-nevus* feature (cells shrink as they go deeper), Kamino bodies (dull-pink dermo-epidermal globules) are a Spitz-nevus clue, and Cowdry inclusions are viral nuclear inclusions.
hy: Upward (all-level) intraepidermal spread = pagetoid scatter.
imgcap: Histopathology of a melanocytic lesion

stem: This H&E section from sun-damaged facial skin shows a lentiginous proliferation of single atypical melanocytes along the basal layer with extension down hair follicles and marked dermal solar elastosis. Which subtype's histology is shown?

options.A: Lentigo maligna
options.B: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.C: Nodular melanoma
options.D: Acral lentiginous melanoma

exp: The recognition cue is a *lentiginous* proliferation of single atypical melanocytes strung along the basal layer (rather than broad pagetoid nests), with extension down hair-follicle/adnexal epithelium, on a background of marked dermal solar elastosis — the histology of lentigo maligna (high-CSD melanoma in situ on the face). The solar elastosis (degenerated, basophilic dermal collagen from chronic UV) and adnexal extension explain both the diagnosis and why margins are hard to clear. The same-class distractors are other subtypes' histology: SSM shows broad *pagetoid* (upward) spread without prominent elastosis, nodular melanoma is a dermal nodule with minimal epidermal component, and ALM shows lentiginous spread in a thickened acral epidermis.
hy: Lentiginous single-cell spread + adnexal extension + solar elastosis = lentigo maligna.
imgcap: Histopathology with dermal changes

stem: This dermal tumor shows loosely textured spindle cells within a fibroblastic stroma with scattered lymphoid aggregates; HMB45 and MART-1 were negative but S100 was positive. Which diagnosis fits?

options.A: Desmoplastic melanoma
options.B: Nodular melanoma
options.C: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.D: Blue nevus

exp: The recognition cue is a dermal tumor of loosely arranged spindle cells set in a fibroblastic (collagenous) stroma with scattered lymphoid aggregates, that is HMB45/MART-1 *negative* but S100 *positive* — the diagnostic signature of desmoplastic melanoma. The IHC profile is the giveaway and a classic pitfall: the spindled cells are too poorly differentiated to express melanosomal markers, so only the lineage marker S100 (or SOX10) lights up, and a scar or fibrohistiocytic lesion could otherwise be mistaken for it. The same-class distractors do *not* fit a spindle-cell, S100-only profile: nodular and superficial spreading melanoma are epithelioid and usually HMB45/MART-1-positive, and a blue nevus is a benign dendritic, heavily pigmented dermal lesion.
hy: Spindle cells + fibrous stroma + S100+/HMB45−/MART-1− = desmoplastic melanoma.
imgcap: Spindle-cell dermal tumor

stem: This H&E section shows a relatively well-circumscribed dermal nodule of confluent atypical melanocytes with minimal overlying epidermal involvement. Which subtype's histology is shown?

options.A: Nodular melanoma
options.B: Lentigo maligna
options.C: Melanoma in situ
options.D: Acral lentiginous melanoma

exp: The recognition cue is a relatively well-circumscribed *dermal* nodule of confluent atypical melanocytes with minimal overlying epidermal (in-situ) involvement — the histology of nodular melanoma, which by definition is essentially pure vertical growth with little or no adjacent radial (intraepidermal) component. The scant epidermal involvement is the key contrast: other subtypes leave a broad intraepidermal "shoulder," whereas NM dives straight into the dermis. The same-class distractors differ histologically: lentigo maligna and melanoma in situ are *intraepidermal* processes (no dermal nodule), and ALM shows lentiginous junctional spread under a thickened acral epidermis.
hy: Dermal nodule, scant epidermal component = nodular melanoma histology.
imgcap: Dermal nodule histopathology

stem: On the dorsal foot of an 84-year-old man, this asymmetric, poorly circumscribed light-brown macule was the "outlier" lesion compared with the patient's other nevi. The lesion is best classified as:

options.A: Melanoma in situ
options.B: Invasive nodular melanoma
options.C: Solar lentigo
options.D: Dermatofibroma

exp: The recognition cue is a subtle, asymmetric, poorly circumscribed light-brown macule on sun-damaged skin that stands out as the morphologic "outlier" among the patient's other nevi (the ugly-duckling principle) — a presentation best classified as melanoma in situ, i.e. early disease still confined to the epidermis before invasion. Such small, flat, featureless early melanomas are easily overlooked on naked-eye exam, which is exactly where comparative recognition and dermoscopy add value. The distractors differ morphologically: an invasive nodular melanoma would be a raised, palpable nodule, while a solar lentigo or dermatofibroma would be a benign, well-defined, stable lesion rather than an asymmetric outlier macule.
hy: Subtle outlier macule on sun-damaged skin = melanoma in situ.
imgcap: Subtle pigmented macule

stem: Which single histologic feature of a primary cutaneous melanoma is the strongest predictor of survival?

options.A: Breslow tumor thickness
options.B: Clark level of invasion
options.C: Dermal mitotic rate
options.D: Presence of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes

exp: Breslow thickness — the vertical extent of the tumor measured in millimeters — is the most important single prognostic factor in primary cutaneous melanoma and is the variable that defines the AJCC T-category, because the deeper the tumor invades the dermis, the greater its access to lymphatics and vasculature and the higher the risk of metastasis. This is why the entire T system is anchored on thickness breakpoints (1/2/4 mm) and why getting an accurate Breslow measurement from a complete excisional biopsy drives every downstream decision (margins, SLNB, surveillance intensity). The distractors are real prognostic concepts but weaker or superseded: Clark level grades the *anatomic* level of dermal invasion (papillary → reticular dermis → subcutis) and was largely abandoned because it is less reproducible and less predictive than thickness; dermal mitotic rate is independently prognostic (a high count lowers survival) but in AJCC-8 it no longer upstages the primary; and tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes are a secondary histologic feature reflecting host immune response, not the dominant survival determinant.
hy: Breslow depth = strongest histologic survival predictor.

stem: This schematic illustrates how melanoma thickness is measured. The double-headed arrow (the Breslow measurement) is taken from which landmark to which?

options.A: From the top of the granular layer (or ulcer base) to the deepest tumor cell
options.B: From the outer stratum corneum surface down to the dermal–subcutaneous junction
options.C: From the dermal–epidermal junction down to the deepest invasive tumor cell
options.D: By the anatomic level of invasion reached (papillary versus reticular dermis)

exp: The Breslow method measures vertical tumor thickness with an ocular micrometer from the top of the granular cell layer of the epidermis down to the deepest point of tumor invasion; if the lesion is ulcerated (no intact epidermis to start from), measurement begins instead from the base of the ulcer. The granular layer is chosen as the upper landmark because it is a fixed, reproducible epidermal reference, and the deepest tumor cell is the lower landmark because depth correlates with metastatic potential — together they yield the single number that defines the T-category. The figure shows exactly these two reference points spanning the tumor. The distractors misplace the landmarks: full-thickness skin or measuring to the dermal–epidermal junction would not capture tumor depth, and describing invasion by *anatomic levels* of the dermis (papillary, reticular, subcutaneous fat) is the separate Clark level system, which is less reproducible and has been largely supplanted by Breslow thickness.
hy: Breslow = granular layer (or ulcer base) → deepest tumor cell.
imgcap: Microstaging schematic of skin layers

stem: In AJCC-8, which Breslow thickness boundaries separate the T categories?
exp: AJCC-8 sorts every invasive melanoma into four T-categories by Breslow thickness: T1 ≤1.0 mm, T2 >1.0–2.0 mm, T3 >2.0–4.0 mm, and T4 >4.0 mm — the 1/2/4 mm breakpoints are the backbone of the entire staging system because thickness is the dominant prognostic factor. These cut-points are not arbitrary trivia: each band carries a stepwise drop in survival and, combined with ulceration, drives whether SLNB is offered and how aggressively the patient is surveilled. Knowing them lets you reconstruct the "a/b" subcategories and the stage groupings around nodal status. The distractors use adjacent but wrong cut-points (0.8/1.6/3.0; 2/4/6; 1/3/5) — the classic numeric-staging trap, where a plausible-looking sequence is wrong by a fraction of a millimeter, so only the exact recalled figures survive.
hy: T boundaries: 1, 2, 4 mm.

stem: Within T1, what distinguishes T1a from T1b in AJCC-8?

options.A: T1a = <0.8 mm no ulceration; T1b = <0.8 mm with ulceration OR 0.8–1.0 mm
options.B: T1a = <1.0 mm with any dermal mitoses; T1b = <1.0 mm without any mitoses
options.C: T1a = Clark anatomic level II invasion; T1b = Clark anatomic level III invasion
options.D: T1a = <0.5 mm thickness; T1b = 0.5–1.0 mm thickness, regardless of ulceration

exp: AJCC-8 subdivides T1 using a 0.8 mm thickness threshold and the presence of ulceration: T1a is a thin melanoma <0.8 mm WITHOUT ulceration, whereas T1b is either <0.8 mm WITH ulceration OR any tumor 0.8–1.0 mm (regardless of ulceration). This split matters clinically because it is the line at which sentinel-node sampling becomes worth discussing — T1a melanomas have a <5% node-positivity rate so SLNB is not routine, while T1b crosses into the ~5–10% range where SLNB is considered. A major AJCC-8 change was *dropping* mitotic rate as a T1 splitter (it had defined T1b in AJCC-7), because the 0.8 mm cut-point proved more reproducible. The distractors invoke criteria AJCC-8 no longer uses for this purpose — mitotic rate, the abandoned Clark level, or a 0.5 mm cut-point that does not exist in the current T1 definition.
hy: T1a <0.8 mm no ulcer; T1b <0.8 mm+ulcer OR 0.8–1.0 mm.

stem: How does ulceration affect AJCC T-staging, and how is it defined histologically?

options.A: It upstages a tumor "a"→"b" within its thickness band; defined as loss of intact epidermis + host response
options.B: It lowers the assigned T category by one full level because it represents earlier, more superficial disease
options.C: It is determined purely clinically as a visibly eroded or crusted surface, without any histologic criterion
options.D: It has no effect on T-staging in AJCC-8 and is recorded only as an optional descriptive comment

exp: Ulceration is a histologic finding — full-thickness absence of an intact epidermis over a major portion of the tumor, accompanied by a host reactive response (fibrin deposition, neutrophilic/inflammatory reaction) — and it is one of the most powerful adverse prognostic features in melanoma, on par with thickness. In the AJCC system it acts as the universal "upstaging" modifier: within every thickness band, the presence of ulceration moves a tumor from the "a" subcategory to the worse "b" subcategory (T1b, T2b, etc.), because ulcerated tumors metastasize and recur more often at any given depth. This is why pathology reports must explicitly state ulceration status, and why an ulcerated thin melanoma can warrant SLNB that a non-ulcerated one would not. The distractors are false: ulceration never lowers the T category, it is determined microscopically (not by clinical inspection), and it most definitely changes staging — that is its entire role.
hy: Ulceration (histologic) = a→b upstaging within each T.

stem: What is the current status of dermal mitotic rate in AJCC-8 melanoma staging?

options.A: It is prognostic but no longer upstages the primary (e.g. ≥11/mm² lowers 5-yr survival to ~84%)
options.B: It is a required staging component that directly defines the T-stage of every primary melanoma
options.C: A mitotic rate of ≥1/mm² now automatically reclassifies a T1a tumor as a higher-risk T1b tumor
options.D: It has been shown to carry no prognostic value at all and is therefore no longer reported by pathologists

exp: Dermal mitotic rate (mitotic figures counted per mm² in the invasive component) was a formal staging criterion in AJCC-7, where ≥1/mm² upstaged a thin melanoma to T1b, but AJCC-8 *removed* it as a T-category determinant because the 0.8 mm thickness cut-point proved more reproducible. Crucially, removal from staging did not erase its biology — a high mitotic rate still independently predicts worse outcome (for example ≥11/mm² is associated with ~84% 5-year survival in node-negative disease), so it is still reported and weighed in risk discussions. The teaching point is the distinction between a factor that *defines a stage* and one that *informs prognosis*: mitotic rate is now the latter, not the former. The distractors either overstate its current role (claiming it still defines T-stage or automatically converts T1a to T1b) or wrongly strip it of any prognostic meaning.
hy: Mitotic rate: prognostic but no longer upstages in AJCC-8.

stem: Which factor is the principal determinant of the N category?

options.A: Number of tumor-involved nodes (1; 2–3; ≥4) plus occult-vs-detected status and satellite/in-transit disease
options.B: The Breslow thickness of the primary tumor measured from the granular layer to the deepest cell
options.C: The serum lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) level measured at the time of the initial staging work-up
options.D: The specific anatomic site of any distant metastases, such as skin, lung, liver, or central nervous system

exp: The AJCC N category captures regional (lymphatic) spread and is built from three things: the *number* of tumor-involved regional nodes (1 = N1; 2–3 = N2; ≥4 or matted = N3), *how* that involvement was found (clinically occult, i.e. microscopic disease detected only on SLNB, versus clinically detected palpable/radiologic nodes — detected disease being worse), and the presence of non-nodal lymphatic deposits (satellite, in-transit, or microsatellite metastases, which carry the "c" suffix). These axes matter because nodal status — especially detected versus occult and node count — is the strongest determinant of stage III subgroup and of who needs adjuvant systemic therapy. The distractors belong to other categories entirely: Breslow thickness defines T, while serum LDH and the anatomic site of distant spread define M (stage IV).
hy: N = node number (1/2–3/≥4) + occult vs detected + satellite/in-transit.

stem: How are satellite and in-transit metastases defined and prognostically weighted in AJCC-8?

options.A: Satellites are ≤2 cm from the primary, in-transit lesions >2 cm but not past the nodes; both are lymphatic, similar prognosis
options.B: Satellites are >2 cm from the primary tumor whereas in-transit lesions lie within 2 cm of the excision scar
options.C: Both satellite and in-transit lesions are hematogenous visceral deposits and are classified as M1 distant disease
options.D: In-transit metastases carry a markedly better prognosis than satellite lesions despite both being lymphatic

exp: Satellite and in-transit metastases are both forms of regional *lymphatic* spread of melanoma between the primary site and the draining nodal basin, distinguished only by distance: satellite (and microsatellite) deposits lie within 2 cm of the primary, whereas in-transit metastases lie more than 2 cm from the primary but still proximal to the regional nodes.
