AJCC-8 explicitly treats the 2 cm boundary as somewhat arbitrary, because biologically these are the same process — tumor traveling through dermal/subcutaneous lymphatics — and they confer a similarly poor prognosis, which is why both are grouped under the N category (N1c/N2c/N3c) rather than M. Recognizing them as stage III lymphatic (not distant) disease is what changes management toward restaging, possible SLNB, and adjuvant therapy. The distractors fail by reversing the two distances, miscalling these deposits hematogenous M1 (distant) disease, or wrongly assigning them divergent prognoses.
hy: Satellite ≤2 cm, in-transit >2 cm; both lymphatic, similar prognosis.

stem: A patient has in-transit metastases but no tumor-involved lymph nodes. Which N category applies?

options.A: N1c
options.B: N0
options.C: N2a
options.D: N3b

exp: In AJCC-8 the "c" suffix on an N category specifically flags non-nodal lymphatic disease (satellite, microsatellite, or in-transit metastases), and N1c is the special case where such deposits are present but NO regional lymph node is tumor-involved. This category exists because a patient can have lymphatic spread without a positive node, yet that still represents stage III disease deserving the same restaging and adjuvant-therapy discussion as nodal stage III. Understanding the N-suffix logic (number of nodes gives N1/N2/N3; "a" occult, "b" detected, "c" non-nodal lymphatic) lets you decode any N code on a report. The distractors are different categories: N0 is no regional disease at all, N2a is 2–3 *clinically occult* involved nodes, and N3b denotes ≥4 nodes or matted nodes — none of which is the node-negative-but-satellite scenario N1c describes.
hy: N1c = satellite/in-transit disease with no involved nodes.

stem: In AJCC-8 M classification, which mapping of metastatic site to M category is correct?

options.A: M1a = skin/soft tissue/non-regional node; M1b = lung; M1c = non-CNS visceral; M1d = CNS
options.B: M1a = lung; M1b = liver; M1c = brain; M1d = bone marrow and the skeletal system
options.C: M1a = CNS; M1b = visceral organs; M1c = lung; M1d = skin and distant soft tissue
options.D: M1a = any visceral organ; M1b = skin only; M1c = node only; M1d = lung only

exp: The AJCC-8 M-category (stage IV) is organized by the anatomic site of distant metastasis, in ascending order of poor prognosis: M1a = distant skin, soft tissue, or non-regional lymph node; M1b = lung; M1c = other non-CNS visceral organs (liver, etc.); and M1d = central nervous system (brain), historically the worst site. Each is further appended with an LDH suffix — (0) for normal and (1) for elevated serum LDH — because elevated LDH independently signals high tumor burden and worse survival within any site. This site-plus-LDH structure matters because it shapes prognosis and modality choice (e.g. brain disease drives MRI surveillance and SRS). The distractors simply scramble these site-to-letter assignments, which is the precise recall trap on this item.
hy: M1a skin/soft tissue, M1b lung, M1c visceral, M1d CNS.

stem: What is the role of serum lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) in melanoma staging?

options.A: It subclassifies stage IV (the M-category suffix) and predicts survival in advanced disease
options.B: It defines the thickness boundary that separates T2 tumors from thicker T3 tumors
options.C: It is recommended as a routine baseline screening test for all newly diagnosed melanomas
options.D: It is the laboratory value that determines whether sentinel lymph node biopsy is indicated

exp: Serum LDH (lactate dehydrogenase), a marker of high cell turnover and tumor burden, has been a formal component of stage IV subclassification since the 2002 AJCC revision — it forms the (0)/(1) suffix on the M-category — because an elevated level independently predicts worse survival and poorer treatment response in metastatic disease. Its value is therefore confined to *advanced* melanoma, where it refines prognosis beyond metastatic site alone. The reason it is not used earlier is biological and statistical: in localized melanoma LDH has low sensitivity and specificity for occult metastasis, so screening with it generates false positives without changing care. The distractors misapply it — LDH does not define any T thickness boundary, is explicitly not recommended as a baseline lab in early disease, and plays no role in deciding who gets a sentinel-node biopsy.
hy: LDH = stage IV M-suffix; advanced-disease survival marker.

stem: Which description of the AJCC-8 stage groupings is correct?

options.A: Stage 0 = in situ; I–II = localized; III = regional nodal/lymphatic; IV = distant metastasis
options.B: Stage 0 = localized invasive disease; stage I = in situ; III = distant; IV = nodal metastasis
options.C: Stage I = regional nodal disease; II = in-transit metastasis; III = in situ; IV = localized
options.D: Stage II = distant metastasis; III = melanoma in situ; IV = localized invasive primary tumor

exp: The four-tier melanoma stage framework maps directly onto disease extent: stage 0 is melanoma *in situ* (confined to the epidermis, essentially 100% curable by excision); stages I–II are localized invasive cutaneous disease (node-negative, distinguished from each other mainly by thickness and ulceration); stage III is regional spread — tumor-involved lymph nodes and/or non-nodal lymphatic deposits (satellite/in-transit/microsatellite); and stage IV is distant metastasis. This progression is the conceptual scaffold for everything else: it determines whether the patient needs only excision (0–II), restaging plus adjuvant therapy (III), or systemic checkpoint/targeted therapy (IV). The distractors scramble these definitions, e.g. labeling regional disease as distant or localized disease as in situ, which collapses the very gradient the staging is meant to encode.
hy: 0 in situ · I–II localized · III regional · IV distant.

stem: Which set of AJCC-8 5-year melanoma-specific survival figures is correct?

options.A: Stage IA ~99%; thick ulcerated T4bN0 ~82%; stage IIID ~32%
options.B: Stage IA ~75%; thick ulcerated T4bN0 ~50%; stage IIID ~10%
options.C: Stage IA ~99%; thick ulcerated T4bN0 ~99%; stage IIID ~80%
options.D: Stage IA ~60%; thick ulcerated T4bN0 ~40%; stage IIID ~5%

exp: The AJCC-8 melanoma-specific survival (MSS) data trace a steep prognostic gradient that is worth anchoring with a few landmark numbers: stage IA (thin, non-ulcerated, node-negative) has ~99% 5-year MSS, a thick ulcerated but still node-negative tumor (T4b N0, stage IIC) drops to ~82%, and the worst regional subgroup IIID falls to ~32% — even before reaching distant disease. These anchors teach the two levers that drive prognosis: primary-tumor factors (thickness and ulceration) separate the early stages, while nodal tumor burden separates the stage III subgroups, so a node-negative thick tumor can actually outperform a heavily node-positive thin one. Carrying a rough mental scale (near-100% early → 80s for high-risk localized → 30s for advanced regional) lets you sanity-check any survival figure on the exam. The distractors are uniformly wrong magnitudes — too pessimistic for early disease or implausibly optimistic for advanced regional disease.

stem: Once nodal status is known, which factor is described as the strongest single predictor of survival and recurrence in primary cutaneous melanoma?

options.A: The pathologic status of the sentinel lymph node(s)
options.B: The patient's chronologic age at the time of diagnosis
options.C: The anatomic body site of the primary melanoma
options.D: The maximum clinical diameter of the primary lesion

exp: Once a melanoma is invasive enough to warrant nodal evaluation (roughly ≥1 mm, or thinner with high-risk features), the pathologic status of the sentinel lymph node becomes the single strongest predictor of both survival and recurrence — a positive node identifies occult stage III disease and roughly stratifies patients into dramatically different prognostic groups. This is the entire rationale for offering SLNB: not to treat, but to stage, because knowing nodal status is more informative than any clinical variable and now also gates eligibility for adjuvant systemic therapy. It outranks the primary-tumor and patient factors precisely because it directly samples whether the tumor has already left the skin. The distractors — patient age, anatomic site, and clinical diameter — are real, independently adverse prognostic variables, but each is weaker than the biological information carried by the sentinel node itself.
hy: SLN status = strongest survival/recurrence predictor.

stem: In localized (stage I/II) melanoma, which clinical variables independently worsen prognosis?

options.A: Male sex, older age, and a trunk/head/neck (rather than extremity) primary site
options.B: Female sex together with a distal extremity location of the primary tumor
options.C: Younger patient age combined with a scalp location, but only in women
options.D: Left-sided versus right-sided location of the primary cutaneous tumor

exp: Beyond the histologic drivers (thickness, ulceration), several *clinical* variables independently predict worse outcome in localized (node-negative) melanoma: male sex, older age, and an axial location — trunk or head/neck, with scalp melanomas notoriously aggressive — whereas female sex and extremity tumors fare comparatively better. These associations are partly biological (axial lesions may have richer or more ambiguous lymphatic drainage and present later) and partly behavioral (delayed detection on the back/scalp). They explain why the same-thickness tumor can carry different risk depending on who has it and where it sits, and why surveillance is sometimes intensified for older men with axial lesions. The distractors either invert these well-established associations (claiming extremity or female disease is worse) or invent prognostically irrelevant features such as laterality (left vs right).
hy: Worse in localized melanoma: male sex, older age, trunk/head/neck site.

stem: Among stage III patients, which nodal feature confers a worse prognosis?

options.A: Clinically detectable (palpable, macroscopic) nodal disease rather than occult microscopic disease
options.B: Microscopic disease detected only on sentinel-node biopsy rather than clinically palpable adenopathy
options.C: A single tumor-involved regional lymph node rather than four or more tumor-involved regional nodes
options.D: Clinically occult disease without satellites rather than the presence of multiple matted regional nodes

exp: Within stage III, tumor burden in the nodes is a key prognostic axis, and one of its expressions is *how* the nodal disease was found: clinically detected (palpable or radiologically apparent, i.e. macroscopic) nodal metastasis carries a distinctly worse prognosis than clinically occult disease found only microscopically on sentinel-node biopsy. The reason is that a node large enough to be felt or imaged generally harbors more tumor and reflects a more advanced biology than a microscopic deposit, which is exactly why AJCC separates the "a" (occult) and "b" (detected) suffixes. This is also why finding occult disease early via SLNB, before it becomes palpable, is prognostically meaningful. The distractors either invert this relationship (claiming occult disease is worse) or substitute the *node-count* axis (fewer vs more nodes — true on its own, but not the occult-versus-detected distinction being tested).
hy: Macroscopic (palpable) nodes worse than occult (microscopic) disease.

stem: In stage IV melanoma, which is the major prognostic factor, and what was the historical outlook before modern therapy?

options.A: Site of distant mets (visceral worse); historically ~9-month median survival, <10% 5-yr with elevated LDH
options.B: Primary tumor diameter; historically a 5-year survival of roughly 50% even with visceral spread present
options.C: Patient biologic sex; historically a median survival of about 5 years regardless of metastatic burden
options.D: The number of separate primary melanomas; historically an overall cure rate of approximately 80%

exp: In stage IV melanoma the dominant prognostic factor is the anatomic site of distant metastasis — non-visceral disease (distant skin, subcutaneous tissue, distant nodes; M1a) does best, while visceral spread (lung, then other organs, then CNS) progressively worsens outcome — and an elevated serum LDH further marks high burden and poor survival within any site. These are exactly the variables the M-subclassification encodes (M1a–M1d plus the LDH suffix), so the staging itself is built around prognosis. Historically, before modern systemic therapy, metastatic melanoma carried a median survival of only ~9 months and <10% 5-year survival when LDH was high — figures that have improved substantially in the checkpoint-inhibitor/targeted-therapy era, which is why these numbers are framed as the pre-modern baseline. The distractors cite the wrong drivers (e.g. primary thickness, which no longer dominates once distant disease is present) or wrong survival magnitudes.
hy: Stage IV: visceral worse; historical median ~9 mo, <10% 5-yr if LDH high.

stem: What do guidelines recommend regarding baseline imaging/laboratory tests in an asymptomatic patient with newly diagnosed cutaneous melanoma?

options.A: They recommend AGAINST routine baseline imaging or labs at any thickness in asymptomatic patients
options.B: They advise whole-body FDG PET-CT for every newly diagnosed patient regardless of tumor thickness
options.C: They advise a baseline contrast-enhanced brain MRI for all patients with T1 (≤1 mm) melanomas
options.D: They advise routine baseline serum LDH and a staging CT scan for all melanoma in-situ lesions

exp: For an asymptomatic patient with newly diagnosed cutaneous melanoma — at any Breslow thickness — evidence-based guidelines recommend AGAINST routine baseline whole-body imaging and broad blood panels, because synchronous occult distant disease is rare at presentation and indiscriminate testing produces far more false positives (leading to needless biopsies, anxiety, and cost) than true findings. The exception is symptom-directed or stage-directed workup: imaging is pursued when history/exam suggests metastasis, or as *surveillance* once the patient is high-stage (IIB and above), not as a reflex at diagnosis. This "don't over-test early disease" principle is one of the most testable management pearls in the chapter. The distractors prescribe exactly the routine baseline CT/PET/LDH screening that the guidelines explicitly reject for asymptomatic patients.
hy: No baseline imaging/labs in asymptomatic melanoma, any thickness.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Laboratory Investigations and Imaging

stem: Per NCCN/AAD guidance, surveillance cross-sectional imaging (e.g. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis or PET-CT) in asymptomatic patients may be considered starting at which stage?

options.A: Stage IIB and higher
options.B: Stage 0 (in situ)
options.C: Stage IA
options.D: Only after distant metastasis is confirmed

exp: While baseline imaging is discouraged in low-risk disease, NCCN/AAD guidance *does* consider periodic surveillance imaging — contrast CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis or whole-body FDG PET-CT — once a patient reaches stage IIB or higher, because the recurrence risk in that group is high enough that catching asymptomatic relapse early may change management. The stage IIB cutoff is the inflection point: below it the yield is too low to justify radiation/cost, while above it (including all stage III) the pretest probability of relapse rises substantially. This is the natural counterpart to the "no routine baseline imaging" rule — the difference is risk stratification by stage, not a blanket policy. The distractors err in the opposite directions: applying imaging to genuinely low-risk disease (in situ or stage IA) or, conversely, withholding it until metastasis is already proven, which defeats the surveillance purpose.
hy: Surveillance imaging considered from stage IIB upward.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Laboratory Investigations and Imaging

stem: Which imaging modality has the highest sensitivity for detecting distant metastatic melanoma at staging?

options.A: Whole-body FDG PET-CT (~80% sensitivity for distant disease)
options.B: Plain chest radiography of the thorax in two projections
options.C: A plain-film skeletal radiographic survey of the axial bones
options.D: Regional nodal-basin ultrasound (best for nodal, not distant, disease)

exp: Whole-body FDG PET-CT (positron-emission tomography fused with CT, which highlights metabolically active tumor) is the most accurate single modality for detecting *distant* metastatic melanoma, with the highest reported sensitivity (~80%) and specificity (~87%), so it is preferred for high-stage staging and surveillance when available. Its whole-body coverage is the advantage — it can find unsuspected visceral or soft-tissue deposits in one study — but it is not the right tool for every question, which is the testable nuance. The distractors fail because each is suited to a different job: chest radiography is too insensitive for staging, a skeletal survey is not a melanoma staging tool, and nodal ultrasound — though excellent for following the regional *nodal* basin (and the backbone of post-SLNB nodal surveillance) — is not the modality for distant disease.
hy: PET-CT = best for distant mets (~80% sens); nodal US best for nodes.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Laboratory Investigations and Imaging

stem: Why is routine serum LDH not recommended in lower-stage melanoma?

options.A: It has low sensitivity and specificity for detecting metastasis in early-stage disease
options.B: It is far too costly a test to justify ordering in routine lower-stage follow-up
options.C: It becomes elevated only in melanoma in situ and not in invasive or metastatic disease
options.D: It reliably replaces sentinel-node biopsy for nodal staging and so is redundant here

exp: Routine serum LDH is not recommended for lower-stage melanoma because, as a screening test for occult metastasis, it has poor sensitivity and specificity — it neither reliably detects early spread nor rules it out, so it does not change management in localized disease. Its one validated role is in stage IV, where the LDH level (normal vs elevated) forms part of the M-subclassification and refines prognosis; in some European centers the serum marker S100B is additionally used for surveillance of higher-risk patients. The general principle — reserve a test for the population in which it actually performs — is the teaching point. The distractors are false rationales: the issue is test performance, not cost; LDH is not a marker of in-situ disease; and it does not substitute for sentinel-node biopsy, which answers a completely different (regional-nodal) question.
hy: LDH low sens/spec early → reserved for stage IV.

stem: Which imaging modality is preferred for evaluating suspected brain metastases in melanoma?

options.A: MRI with contrast
options.B: Chest radiography
options.C: Nodal-basin ultrasound
options.D: Bone scan

exp: Brain is a relatively common and dangerous site of melanoma metastasis (the M1d category), and contrast-enhanced MRI is the preferred imaging because its soft-tissue resolution detects small parenchymal and leptomeningeal deposits that CT can miss; contrast CT of the head is an acceptable alternative when MRI is unavailable or contraindicated. Accurate brain imaging matters because CNS disease changes both prognosis and treatment (e.g. stereotactic radiosurgery, choice/sequencing of systemic agents that cross the blood–brain barrier). Matching the modality to the target organ is the broader skill being tested. The distractors are mismatched to the brain: a chest radiograph images the thorax, nodal ultrasound follows the regional lymphatic basin, and a bone scan targets the skeleton — none assesses intracranial disease.
hy: Brain metastases → contrast MRI (preferred).

stem: What is the preferred initial biopsy of a lesion suspected to be melanoma, and why?

options.A: Excisional/complete biopsy with narrow (1–3 mm) margins and adequate depth, allowing accurate microstaging
options.B: A superficial shave biopsy sampling only the single most darkly pigmented portion of the lesion
options.C: Fine-needle aspiration of the lesion to obtain cells for cytologic and immunocytochemical analysis
options.D: An incisional wedge biopsy of the thinnest portion of the lesion to minimize the resulting scar

exp: The standard for biopsying a suspected melanoma is a complete (excisional) biopsy with narrow 1–3 mm margins taken down to a depth that includes the full tumor base, because melanoma is microstaged by Breslow thickness and the pathologist needs the *entire* lesion to measure depth, assess ulceration, and judge architecture without sampling error. Narrow margins are used (rather than a wide excision up front) so that the definitive wide local excision and any sentinel-node mapping can still be planned correctly afterward based on the true thickness. Getting this first step right is what protects every subsequent staging decision. The distractors all compromise microstaging: a partial superficial shave of one area, fine-needle aspiration (yields cells but no architecture or depth), or a thin incisional wedge risk transecting the tumor base and under-measuring Breslow thickness — the classic cause of under-staging.
hy: Complete/excisional biopsy (1–3 mm margins) for accurate microstaging.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Skin Investigation and Clinical Diagnosis

stem: Why are superficial shave biopsies generally avoided for a thick or palpable pigmented lesion?

options.A: They risk transecting the tumor base and so underestimating the Breslow thickness
options.B: They cannot provide any usable tissue for routine histopathologic examination at all
options.C: They almost always seed and disseminate melanoma cells along the biopsy track
options.D: They are contraindicated mainly because of the substantial bleeding risk they carry

exp: A superficial shave biopsy removes only the upper portion of a lesion, so on a thick or palpable melanoma it risks slicing through the tumor before its deepest extent — transecting the base — which makes the measured Breslow thickness an underestimate and corrupts the staging that flows from it (margins, SLNB eligibility, prognosis). The chapter does permit a *broad* shave (saucerization) for thin, flat melanoma in situ of the lentigo maligna type, where there is little depth to lose, illustrating that the rule is depth-dependent, not an absolute ban. The unifying concept is preserving an accurate depth measurement. The distractors are false: shave specimens still yield histology, there is no evidence shave biopsy seeds melanoma along the wound, and the legitimate concern is tumor transection — not bleeding.
hy: Avoid superficial shave on palpable lesions → risks transection/under-staging.

stem: A patient has an invasive melanoma 2.5 mm thick (>2–4 mm). What is the recommended surgical (wide local excision) margin?

options.A: 2.0 cm
options.B: 0.5 cm
options.C: 1.0 cm
options.D: 4.0 cm

exp: Wide local excision margins for melanoma scale with Breslow thickness, but they plateau: a melanoma >2 mm thick is excised with a 2.0 cm clinical margin, which is the maximum recommended for any thickness because randomized controlled trials comparing wider versus narrower margins showed no survival benefit beyond 2 cm even for thick tumors — wider excisions only add morbidity (larger defects, grafts) without reducing recurrence or death. This is why the historical 4–5 cm "radical" margins were abandoned. Remembering the ladder (in situ 0.5–1 cm; ≤1 mm → 1 cm; 1–2 mm → 1–2 cm; >2 mm → 2 cm) lets you assign the correct margin for any case. The distractors are the margins that belong to *thinner* tumors (0.5–1 cm for in situ/≤1 mm) or the obsolete 4 cm margin that trials proved unnecessary.
hy: >2 mm melanoma → 2 cm margin (the ceiling).

stem: What is the recommended margin for a melanoma in situ (non-LM type), and what is the maximum WLE margin recommended for any thickness?

options.A: 0.5–1.0 cm for in situ; 2.0 cm maximum for thick melanoma (no benefit beyond)
options.B: 1.0 cm for melanoma in situ; up to a 5.0 cm maximum margin for thick tumors
options.C: 2.0 cm for melanoma in situ; up to a 4.0 cm maximum margin for thick tumors
options.D: 0.5 cm for melanoma in situ; a 1.0 cm maximum margin for the thickest tumors

exp: Melanoma in situ — tumor confined to the epidermis with no invasion — is excised with 0.5–1.0 cm margins, and across all thicknesses 2.0 cm is the *maximum* useful wide-local-excision margin, because randomized trials demonstrated no added survival or recurrence benefit from going wider. The two facts together define the boundaries of the margin ladder: the floor (in situ, sub-centimeter) and the ceiling (2 cm). The point is that margin selection is evidence-capped, not surgeon-preference: bigger is not better once 2 cm is reached, only more morbid. The distractors inflate the ceiling — 5 cm reflects historical pre-trial "radical" practice and 4 cm was the wider comparator that RCTs showed was unnecessary — or misstate the in-situ margin.
hy: In situ 0.5–1.0 cm; 2 cm is the maximum useful margin.

stem: For melanoma in situ of the lentigo maligna type on the face, which approach is favored?

options.A: Mohs/staged excision with complete margin assessment; subclinical spread may need ≥1 cm to clear
options.B: A fixed 5 mm peripheral margin, which is reliably sufficient for facial lentigo maligna in all cases
options.C: Cryotherapy applied to the visible lesion as the first-line definitive treatment of choice for the face
options.D: Observation without treatment, on the basis that lentigo maligna essentially never progresses to invasion

exp: Lentigo maligna (the in-situ phase of lentigo maligna melanoma) on the face is best treated with Mohs micrographic surgery or staged ("slow Mohs") excision that provides complete circumferential peripheral and deep margin assessment (CCPDMA), because LM characteristically extends subclinically along hair follicles and adnexa well beyond its visible edge — often requiring 1 cm or more of clearance — so standard fixed-margin excision frequently leaves positive margins. Examining 100% of the margin, rather than the random sections of routine pathology, both spares uninvolved facial tissue (cosmesis) and reduces local recurrence. The principle is margin-controlled surgery for an ill-defined tumor at a tissue-sensitive site. The distractors are false: a fixed 5 mm margin commonly fails for facial LM, cryotherapy is not first-line definitive treatment, and LM is not benign — it can progress to invasive melanoma.
hy: Facial MIS-LM → Mohs/staged excision (CCPDMA); subclinical spread may need ≥1 cm.

stem: Topical imiquimod is sometimes used for lentigo maligna. What is its mechanism, and when is it typically used?

options.A: A Toll-like receptor (TLR)-7 agonist; used as primary therapy when surgery is unsuitable, or adjunctively
options.B: An alkylating cytotoxic chemotherapy cream applied directly to the surgical tumor bed after resection
options.C: A topical BRAF-inhibitor preparation applied to V600E-mutant lentigo maligna to shrink it before surgery
options.D: A first-line replacement for wide local excision in essentially all invasive cutaneous melanomas

exp: Imiquimod is a topical Toll-like receptor (TLR)-7 agonist: it activates innate immunity (inducing interferon-α and other cytokines) to provoke a local antitumor immune response, which is why it has a niche in *lentigo maligna* (in-situ disease). It is used either as primary therapy when surgery is unattractive — elderly or poor surgical candidates, or large disfiguring facial lesions — or as adjuvant therapy when excision leaves narrow (≤1 mm) or transected margins with no clinically apparent residual tumor. Its role is strictly limited to in-situ disease because it cannot reliably eradicate an invasive tumor's deep component. The distractors mischaracterize it as alkylating chemotherapy, as a BRAF inhibitor (a targeted oral drug for mutant invasive disease), or as a substitute for surgical excision of invasive melanoma — none of which it is.
hy: Imiquimod = TLR-7 agonist; LM primary (non-surgical) or adjuvant.

stem: What is the current evidence-based status of Mohs micrographic surgery (MMS) for invasive cutaneous melanoma?

options.A: Evidence is insufficient to recommend MMS for invasive melanoma; its recognized role is mainly MIS (LM/acral)
options.B: MMS is the established, guideline-endorsed standard of care for all invasive cutaneous melanomas at any site
options.C: MMS is formally contraindicated for any melanoma whatsoever, including melanoma in situ of the head and neck
options.D: MMS has demonstrated clearly superior melanoma-specific survival in large prospective randomized trials

exp: Mohs micrographic surgery (MMS) examines 100% of the surgical margin intraoperatively, which is ideal for tumors with ill-defined subclinical extension, and in melanoma its established role is for *in-situ* disease — lentigo maligna (chronic sun-damage type) and acral types, and at most minimally invasive T1a lentigo maligna melanoma at anatomically constrained sites — where margin-controlled excision improves local control and spares tissue. For frankly *invasive* melanoma the evidence is currently insufficient for guidelines to recommend MMS as standard, partly because of historical concerns about interpreting melanocytes on frozen sections and the lack of demonstrated survival benefit. So the principle is: MMS for selected in-situ/superficial disease, conventional wide excision (± SLNB) for invasive disease. The distractors overstate it (calling it standard for all invasive melanoma or claiming a proven melanoma-specific survival benefit) or wrongly forbid it altogether.
hy: MMS: established for MIS (LM/acral), not yet recommended for invasive melanoma.

stem: Adjuvant pembrolizumab is approved for which resected node-negative (stage II) melanomas?

options.A: Stage IIB (T3b/T4a N0) and stage IIC (T4b N0)
options.B: Stage IA disease (a T1a N0 thin melanoma)
options.C: All resected melanoma in-situ (stage 0) lesions
options.D: Only resected stage IV distant metastatic disease

exp: Pembrolizumab (an anti-PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor) is approved as *adjuvant* therapy after complete resection of high-risk but still node-negative stage IIB (T3b/T4a N0) and IIC (T4b N0) melanoma, based on a phase 3 trial in which adjuvant anti-PD-1 reduced recurrence and death versus placebo. This extended checkpoint blockade — previously reserved for resected stage III and for metastatic disease — down into the highest-risk node-negative group, reflecting that thick or ulcerated primaries relapse often enough to justify systemic immunotherapy even without proven nodal spread. The teaching point is that adjuvant ICI eligibility now tracks recurrence risk, not just nodal positivity. The distractors are out of indication: stage IA and in-situ disease are far too low-risk to treat systemically, and the stage II approval is specifically for resected node-negative high-risk *primaries*, not for established stage IV (metastatic) disease, which is treated differently.
hy: Adjuvant pembrolizumab: resected stage IIB/IIC.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Adjuvant Therapy for Stage II

stem: For which clinical scenario is sentinel lymph node biopsy NOT typically recommended?

options.A: T1a melanoma (<0.8 mm, no ulceration), with SLN positivity under 5%
options.B: T2a melanoma (>1–2 mm thick, without ulceration of the primary tumor)
options.C: T3b melanoma (>2–4 mm thick, with histologic ulceration of the primary)
options.D: T4a melanoma (>4 mm thick, without ulceration of the primary tumor)

exp: Sentinel lymph node biopsy is offered when the chance of finding occult nodal disease is high enough to justify the procedure, and for T1a melanoma (<0.8 mm, non-ulcerated) that probability is <5% — too low to warrant SLNB routinely, since the staging yield rarely outweighs operative risk. The decision ladder runs: Tis/T1a → not typically; T1b → discuss/consider (~5–10% positivity); T2a and above → discuss and offer (>10%). This thickness-and-ulceration–driven gate is exactly why accurate Breslow microstaging on the initial excisional biopsy matters so much. The distractors name the scenarios where SLNB *is* indicated — T2a (>10% positivity) and the clearly higher-risk T3b and T4a tumors — so they are the inverse of the correct "not recommended" answer.
hy: No routine SLNB for T1a (<0.8 mm, no ulcer; <5% positive).

stem: Approximately what proportion of clinically node-negative patients with melanoma >1 mm thick have a positive sentinel node?
exp: Among clinically node-negative patients with melanoma >1 mm thick, roughly 15–20% turn out to harbor microscopic (occult) nodal metastasis on sentinel-node biopsy — which is the whole reason SLNB is informative: a sizable minority of "node-negative on exam" patients are actually stage III. That positivity rate is not fixed; it climbs with increasing Breslow thickness, ulceration, high mitotic rate, and lymphovascular invasion, the same adverse features that drive T-staging. Knowing the baseline figure helps calibrate counseling and explains why thicker/ulcerated tumors are the ones offered SLNB. The distractors quote rates far above or below this 15–20% range, which would either overstate or understate the procedure's yield.
hy: ~15–20% SLNB positivity for melanoma >1 mm, node-negative clinically.

stem: During SLNB, which is a criterion for identifying a sentinel node intraoperatively?

options.A: A node emitting ≥10% of the hottest node's tracer counts (and/or dyed blue or palpably enlarged)
options.B: Only the single anatomically largest node in the basin, irrespective of tracer uptake or dye
options.C: Specifically any node that fails to take up either the blue dye or the radiotracer at all
options.D: All lymph nodes lying within 5 cm of the primary tumor, removed empirically en bloc

exp: The sentinel node is the first node draining the tumor, and it is identified intraoperatively by a dual-mapping technique: preoperative lymphoscintigraphy plus intradermal blue dye and a radiotracer are injected around the primary, and the surgeon then removes any node that is dyed blue, any node that is the "hottest" by the gamma probe, any node emitting ≥10% of the counts of the hottest node, and any clinically palpable node. The ≥10% rule and the blue/hot criteria ensure that all true first-echelon nodes are captured, not just the single most prominent one, which is what makes the staging reliable. This selective harvest is the entire point — sample the nodes most likely to contain early metastasis without removing the whole basin. The distractors contradict the protocol: taking only one largest node, taking nodes that do *not* take up tracer, or empirically removing all nearby nodes (which would be a completion dissection, not a sentinel biopsy).
hy: SLN = blue, hottest, ≥10% of hottest, or palpable.

stem: What best describes the role of SLNB in melanoma?

options.A: A prognostic/staging procedure not shown to improve survival, but it finds occult stage III disease
options.B: A therapeutic procedure that, by removing the first draining node, independently cures regional nodal disease
options.C: A procedure that has now replaced wide local excision as the definitive treatment of the primary tumor
options.D: A staging step that is required in every melanoma, including thin melanoma in situ (stage 0) lesions

exp: SLNB is fundamentally a *staging/prognostic* procedure, not a treatment: in the MSLT-1 trial it did not improve overall survival across the whole population (a benefit was seen only in select intermediate-thickness subgroups), so its purpose is to detect occult stage III disease rather than to cure. That information is highly valuable, however, because identifying a positive node refines prognosis and, in the modern era, qualifies the patient for adjuvant systemic therapy that improves relapse-free survival. So the chain is: SLNB stages → positive node → adjuvant ICI/targeted therapy → better recurrence-free outcomes, even though the biopsy itself is not therapeutic. The distractors overstate it — calling SLNB curative/therapeutic or a replacement for wide local excision (it complements, not replaces, WLE) — or wrongly apply it to in-situ disease, which has essentially no metastatic risk.
hy: SLNB = prognostic, not therapeutic; enables adjuvant therapy.

stem: Following a positive SLNB, why has completion lymph node dissection (CLND) declined?

options.A: Two RCTs (MSLT-2, DeCOG) showed no overall-survival benefit versus nodal-ultrasound surveillance
options.B: Completion dissection was actually shown in randomized trials to worsen melanoma-specific survival
options.C: Completion dissection is technically impossible to perform once the sentinel node has been removed
options.D: Nodal ultrasound surveillance now provides full pathologic non-sentinel-node data, making it redundant

exp: Completion lymph node dissection (CLND) — removing all remaining nodes in a basin after a positive sentinel node — has been largely abandoned worldwide because two randomized trials, MSLT-2 (international) and the German DeCOG-SLT, both showed no overall-survival benefit from immediate CLND versus active observation with nodal ultrasound surveillance, while CLND adds real morbidity (notably lymphedema and wound complications). The modern standard after a positive SLN is therefore close ultrasound follow-up of the basin plus consideration of adjuvant systemic therapy, reserving dissection for nodal relapse. This is a landmark practice change driven directly by level-1 evidence. The distractors are false: the trials did not show CLND worsens melanoma-specific survival, CLND remains technically feasible, and nodal ultrasound — its key limitation — does NOT provide pathologic information on the non-sentinel nodes, it only monitors for clinically detectable recurrence.
hy: No OS benefit for CLND (MSLT-2/DeCOG) → nodal US surveillance.

stem: A patient develops indurated papulonodules within 2 cm of the melanoma excision scar (satellitosis). How does this change staging?

options.A: It reclassifies the disease as stage III — prompting imaging, wide excision/possible SLNB, and adjuvant discussion
options.B: It remains stage I provided the original primary tumor was thin and had been completely excised
options.C: It is automatically stage IV distant metastatic disease regardless of how close it lies to the scar
options.D: It carries no staging implication at all as long as the original surgical margins were histologically clear

exp: New papules or nodules appearing within ~2 cm of a melanoma scar are satellite metastases — a form of regional lymphatic (in-transit) spread — which by definition reclassify the disease as stage III (an N-category "c" lesion), not local scar-site recurrence to be ignored. The correct response is to treat it as new regional disease: restage with appropriate imaging, refer for wide excision of the deposits and consideration of nodal evaluation, and discuss adjuvant or systemic therapy, since stage III carries meaningful metastatic risk. Recognizing that lymphatic deposits are stage III — neither trivial nor automatically incurable — is the management pivot. The distractors either understage it (calling it stage I with no implications) or overstage it (labeling it automatically stage IV distant disease, which would require spread beyond the regional lymphatics).
hy: Satellite/in-transit recurrence = stage III → restage + adjuvant discussion.

stem: Talimogene laherparepvec (TVEC), used intralesionally for unresectable cutaneous/in-transit metastases, works by:

options.A: Being an engineered herpes virus that replicates in tumor cells and secretes GM-CSF to drive antitumor immunity
options.B: Acting as a systemically administered alkylating cytotoxic chemotherapy that crosslinks tumor-cell DNA
options.C: Combining a BRAF inhibitor with a MEK inhibitor to shut down constitutive MAPK pathway signaling
options.D: Functioning as an anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody that releases the brake on cytotoxic T lymphocytes

exp: Talimogene laherparepvec (TVEC) is an oncolytic immunotherapy — a genetically engineered herpes simplex virus type 1 that is attenuated so it selectively replicates within tumor cells, lysing them, and is armed with the GM-CSF gene so that as the cells rupture they release GM-CSF, recruiting and activating dendritic cells and T cells to mount a systemic antitumor immune response. It is injected directly into accessible lesions, so its niche is unresectable cutaneous, subcutaneous, or in-transit melanoma deposits, and it has also been studied in combination with checkpoint inhibitors. The dual mechanism — direct lysis plus an in-situ vaccine effect — is the testable concept. The distractors describe completely different drug classes: alkylating chemotherapy, BRAF/MEK targeted inhibition, and anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibodies, none of which is an oncolytic virus.
hy: TVEC = oncolytic engineered HSV expressing GM-CSF.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Skin Satellite and In-Transit Metastases

stem: For multiple in-transit metastases confined to one limb, which regional treatment may have palliative (sometimes curative) value?

options.A: Isolated limb perfusion using melphalan, with or without added TNF
options.B: Whole-brain external-beam radiotherapy delivered over several fractions
options.C: Systemic single-agent dacarbazine chemotherapy given intravenously
options.D: Prophylactic completion lymphadenectomy of the regional nodal basin

exp: When in-transit metastases are numerous but confined to one extremity, isolated limb perfusion (ILP) is the regional treatment of choice: the limb's circulation is surgically isolated from the systemic circulation and perfused with high-dose chemotherapy — melphalan, often with tumor necrosis factor (TNF) — so the tumor deposits receive cytotoxic concentrations that would be intolerable systemically, sparing the rest of the body. It offers durable palliation and sometimes long-term control (reported ~40% 5-year and ~30% 10-year survival in this setting). The principle is regional, high-dose, anatomically confined therapy for anatomically confined disease. The distractors are mismatched: whole-brain radiotherapy treats CNS disease, single-agent dacarbazine is weak systemic salvage chemotherapy, and a prophylactic lymphadenectomy addresses nodes, not the in-transit dermal/subcutaneous deposits in the limb.
hy: Limb in-transit disease → isolated limb perfusion (melphalan ± TNF).
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Skin Satellite and In-Transit Metastases

stem: For resected stage III melanoma with a BRAF V600 mutation, which adjuvant targeted regimen is approved?

options.A: Dabrafenib plus trametinib (a BRAF inhibitor with a MEK inhibitor)
options.B: Imatinib monotherapy directed against the KIT receptor tyrosine kinase
options.C: High-dose interleukin-2 immunostimulation given as inpatient therapy
options.D: Larotrectinib, the selective TRK inhibitor used for NTRK-fusion tumors

exp: For completely resected stage III melanoma that carries a BRAF V600 mutation, adjuvant dabrafenib plus trametinib is an approved option — dabrafenib inhibits the mutant BRAF kinase and trametinib inhibits the downstream MEK kinase, so the pair shuts down the constitutively active MAPK pathway driving these tumors, reducing recurrence. The mutation is the gatekeeper: this combination only works (and is only indicated) when a V600 BRAF mutation is present, which is why molecular testing precedes the choice between adjuvant targeted therapy and adjuvant immunotherapy. The unifying concept is matching the drug to the tumor's driver. The distractors target the wrong driver or era: imatinib is for KIT-mutant melanomas (acral/mucosal), high-dose IL-2 is older immunotherapy for unresectable disease, and larotrectinib treats the rare NTRK-fusion–positive tumors — none is the BRAF-mutant adjuvant standard.
hy: Adjuvant dabrafenib+trametinib for resected stage III BRAF V600+.

stem: Why are BRAF inhibitors combined with MEK inhibitors in BRAF-mutant melanoma?

options.A: The combination delays resistance (response ~10 months) and cuts the cutaneous SCCs/KAs of BRAFi monotherapy
options.B: The MEK inhibitor is required to activate the BRAF inhibitor, which is an inactive prodrug on its own
options.C: Adding a MEK inhibitor reliably eliminates all of the immune-related adverse events of the regimen
options.D: The MEK inhibitor is needed to treat the NRAS mutation that every BRAF-mutant melanoma also carries

exp: BRAF inhibitors work spectacularly at first but tumors develop acquired resistance, usually by reactivating the MAPK pathway downstream of BRAF; adding a MEK inhibitor blocks that escape route, which both delays resistance (extending the average duration of response to roughly 10 months) and — importantly — reduces the cutaneous toxicities of BRAF-inhibitor monotherapy. Those toxicities (squamous cell carcinomas and keratoacanthomas) arise from paradoxical MAPK activation in BRAF–wild-type keratinocytes, so suppressing MEK downstream prevents that paradox. Hence BRAF + MEK combination, not BRAF alone, is standard for BRAF-mutant disease. The distractors offer false mechanisms: BRAF inhibitors are not prodrugs that require MEK to activate, the combination does not eliminate immune-related adverse events (those belong to checkpoint inhibitors), and not every BRAF-mutant tumor harbors an NRAS mutation.
hy: BRAFi+MEKi: delays resistance (~10 mo) and cuts cuSCC/KA.

stem: BRAF-inhibitor monotherapy induces cutaneous squamous cell carcinomas/keratoacanthomas in up to ~25% of patients because of:

options.A: Paradoxical MAPK pathway activation in wild-type-BRAF (often RAS-mutant) keratinocytes
options.B: Direct chemical mutagenesis of keratinocyte DNA by the small-molecule drug itself
options.C: Drug-induced local immunosuppression of the skin permitting keratinocyte outgrowth
options.D: Reactivation of latent oncogenic human papillomavirus within the epidermal keratinocytes

exp: BRAF-inhibitor monotherapy induces cutaneous squamous cell carcinomas and keratoacanthomas in up to ~25% of patients by a *paradoxical* mechanism: in keratinocytes that have wild-type BRAF (and frequently a pre-existing RAS mutation), the drug actually drives, rather than blocks, the MAPK pathway — so the same inhibitor that suppresses signaling in BRAF-mutant melanoma cells stimulates proliferation in BRAF–wild-type skin cells. This explains both why the lesions appear quickly on therapy and why adding a downstream MEK inhibitor sharply reduces them (it interrupts the paradoxically activated pathway). The concept is class-defining for these drugs. The distractors offer mechanisms the chapter does not support — direct DNA mutagenesis by the drug, generalized immunosuppression, or HPV reactivation — none of which is the paradoxical-MAPK explanation.
hy: BRAFi → paradoxical MAPK in RAS-mutant keratinocytes → cuSCC/KA (~25%).

stem: Which statement about first-line systemic therapy for unresectable/metastatic melanoma is correct?

options.A: ICIs are generally preferred first-line regardless of BRAF status; ipilimumab is largely supplanted by anti-PD-1 agents
options.B: BRAF inhibitors are recommended first-line even for tumors that are confirmed to be BRAF-wild-type on testing
options.C: Single-agent ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) is preferred over anti-PD-1 monotherapy because it is less toxic overall
options.D: Cytotoxic chemotherapy with dacarbazine remains the standard first-line treatment of metastatic melanoma

exp: For unresectable or metastatic melanoma, immune checkpoint inhibitors are generally the preferred first-line systemic therapy regardless of BRAF status, because they produce durable, sometimes years-long responses across mutation types — an advantage over targeted therapy, whose responses, while fast, tend to be time-limited. Within the checkpoint class, the original anti-CTLA-4 agent ipilimumab (which releases the early "priming" brake on T cells) has been largely supplanted as monotherapy by the anti-PD-1 antibodies nivolumab and pembrolizumab (which release the "effector" brake in the tumor), as anti-PD-1 is both more effective and less toxic; the two are still combined in selected high-risk patients. The teaching point is that ICIs are mutation-agnostic and first-line. The distractors are false: BRAF inhibitors require a BRAF mutation (not all-comers), single-agent ipilimumab is inferior to anti-PD-1, and cytotoxic chemotherapy is now reserved for salvage after immunotherapy and targeted options.
hy: ICIs first-line (any BRAF status); anti-PD-1 > ipilimumab.

stem: Approximately what proportion of cutaneous melanomas carry an activating BRAF mutation, and what fraction of BRAF mutations are V600E?

options.A: ~45% are BRAF-mutant; ~90% of BRAF mutations are V600E
options.B: ~80% are BRAF-mutant; about half of those mutations are V600E
options.C: ~15% are BRAF-mutant; only about 10% of those are V600E
options.D: ~95% are BRAF-mutant; nearly all of those are V600K, not V600E

exp: Roughly 45% of cutaneous melanomas harbor an activating BRAF mutation and about 15% carry an NRAS mutation — both drive the same MAPK signaling pathway — and of the BRAF mutations, approximately 90% are the V600E variant (a valine-to-glutamic-acid substitution at codon 600), with V600K and others making up the remainder. These proportions are clinically load-bearing because BRAF V600 status is precisely what determines eligibility for BRAF/MEK targeted therapy, so they explain why nearly half of patients have a targetable driver. Carrying the "45% BRAF, 90% of those V600E" figures lets you reason about how many patients are candidates for dabrafenib/trametinib-type regimens. The distractors swap in wrong proportions (80%/50%; 15%/10%; or claiming 95% are V600K), which would misrepresent how common the targetable mutation is.
hy: ~45% BRAF-mutant; ~90% of BRAF mutations are V600E.

stem: Why is caution needed when sequencing a BRAF inhibitor shortly after an immune checkpoint inhibitor?

options.A: Severe drug hypersensitivity (DRESS) occurs in ~20% when a BRAFi follows ICI priming, versus ~5% without
options.B: The overlapping combination predictably triggers an immediate, life-threatening tumor lysis syndrome
options.C: Immune checkpoint inhibitors permanently and irreversibly inactivate the BRAF inhibitor molecule
options.D: The sequence reliably cures essentially all treated patients, so no special caution is actually needed

exp: Drug sequencing matters in melanoma because severe hypersensitivity — including DRESS (drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms, a delayed multi-organ reaction with rash, fever, eosinophilia, and hepatic/renal involvement) — occurs in about 20% of patients when a BRAF inhibitor is started soon after immune-checkpoint-inhibitor "priming," compared with only ~5% without prior ICI. The leading explanation is that checkpoint blockade leaves the immune system sensitized, so a subsequent targeted drug is more likely to trigger an exaggerated reaction. This is a practical reason to be deliberate about the order and timing of immunotherapy versus targeted therapy. The distractors invent unrelated mechanisms (tumor lysis syndrome, inactivation of the BRAF inhibitor) or wrongly claim the ICI-then-BRAFi sequence is uniformly safe or curative.
hy: BRAFi soon after ICI → ~20% DRESS (vs ~5%).

stem: Which is an appropriate use of radiation therapy in melanoma?

options.A: Stereotactic radiosurgery for brain mets; primary RT for unresectable MIS-LM; adjuvant RT for high-risk desmoplastic tumors
options.B: Routine first-line definitive treatment of all localized cutaneous melanomas in place of surgical excision
options.C: Routine adjuvant irradiation of the nodal basin after lymphadenectomy, on the basis of a proven survival benefit
options.D: As a standard substitute for wide local excision in the management of thin (T1) cutaneous melanomas

exp: Melanoma is relatively radioresistant, so radiation therapy is used only in specific niches rather than as primary curative treatment: stereotactic radiosurgery for brain metastases (focused high-dose treatment of discrete lesions), primary RT for unresectable melanoma in situ of the lentigo maligna type (when surgery is not feasible), and adjuvant RT for high-risk desmoplastic melanoma, particularly the neurotropic variant prone to local/perineural recurrence. The unifying logic is that RT is reserved for control where surgery is impossible or recurrence risk is locally high, not for ordinary cutaneous disease. The distractors misstate this: RT is rarely used for localized cutaneous melanoma, routine adjuvant radiation of the nodal basin improved local control but showed no survival benefit at the cost of significant toxicity (e.g. lymphedema), and RT does not substitute for the wide local excision that cures thin melanomas.
hy: RT niches: SRS for brain mets, unresectable MIS-LM, adjuvant for desmoplastic.

stem: Which statement about melanoma recurrence and second primaries is correct?

options.A: About 90% of recurrences occur within 5 years (greatest in the first 2); second-primary risk is ~3.5–4.5%
options.B: Recurrences are spread evenly across the first 20 years of follow-up, showing no early post-diagnosis peak
options.C: Late recurrence occurring beyond 10–15 years from diagnosis is by far the most common pattern of relapse
options.D: The lifetime risk of developing a second primary cutaneous melanoma exceeds 50% in most treated patients

exp: Melanoma recurrence is heavily front-loaded — roughly 90% of recurrences happen within the first 5 years and the greatest density is in the first 2 — which is exactly why surveillance schedules are most intensive early and then taper. Separately, a melanoma survivor's lifetime risk of developing a *second primary* melanoma is about 3.5–4.5%, well above the general-population baseline, and that risk persists indefinitely, justifying continued (lifelong) skin surveillance even after the recurrence window narrows. The two facts together explain the surveillance design: frequent early visits to catch relapse, lifelong periodic skin checks to catch new primaries. The distractors are false: recurrences are not spread evenly over time, late recurrence beyond 10–15 years is uncommon rather than the dominant pattern, and the second-primary risk is single-digit, not greater than 50%.
hy: 90% of recurrences in first 5 yr; second-primary risk ~3.5–4.5%.

stem: A typical recommended dermatologic follow-up schedule after melanoma diagnosis is:

options.A: 1–4 times/year for 2 years, then every 6–12 months through year 5, then at least annually
options.B: A single comprehensive skin examination visit at the 5-year mark, followed by discharge from care
options.C: Weekly dermatologic visits continued indefinitely for the remainder of the patient's lifetime
options.D: Whole-body imaging every month for life, regardless of the original stage at diagnosis

exp: Melanoma follow-up cadence is deliberately front-loaded to mirror the recurrence risk: most experts recommend dermatologic visits 1–4 times per year for the first 2 years (frequency scaled to stage/risk), then every 6–12 months through year 5, then at least annually for life. The early intensity targets the window when ~90% of recurrences occur, while the indefinite annual checks address the ongoing elevated risk of a second primary melanoma. Visits combine total-body skin examination and regional nodal palpation (with stage-directed imaging for higher-stage patients), not blanket imaging for everyone. The distractors are implausible extremes — a single visit at 5 years (misses the high-risk early period), weekly visits (no rationale), or monthly lifelong imaging (excess radiation/cost without benefit).
hy: Follow-up: 1–4×/yr ×2 yr → q6–12 mo to year 5 → annually.

stem: Neurofibromas are benign nerve-sheath tumors composed of a proliferation of which mix of cells?

options.A: Schwann cells, perineurial cells, fibroblasts, and mast cells, plus residual axons
options.B: A pure clonal proliferation of encapsulated Schwann cells forming Verocay bodies
options.C: Dendritic melanocytes set within a fibrotic, heavily pigmented dermal stroma
options.D: Storiform CD34-positive fibrohistiocytic spindle cells infiltrating the fat

exp: A neurofibroma is a benign nerve-sheath tumor formed by proliferation of the entire "neuromesenchyme" — Schwann cells, perineurial cells, endoneurial fibroblasts, and mast cells — together with residual interspersed axons. That mixed-lineage composition is the defining contrast with a schwannoma, which is a *pure* Schwann-cell tumor (encapsulated, with Antoni A/B areas and Verocay bodies). Because axons traverse the tumor, the axon-to-Schwann-cell ratio is lower than the normal 1:1, and the mast-cell component is biologically important (it drives growth in NF1 — see B115.3). Solitary cutaneous neurofibromas are common and have no age or sex preference; finding *multiple* neurofibromas should raise the possibility of a neurofibromatosis. Each distractor is a different real dermal tumor: a pigmented dendritic-melanocyte proliferation in fibrotic stroma is a blue nevus, and a storiform CD34-positive spindle-cell tumor that infiltrates fat is dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans.
hy: Neurofibroma = mixed neuromesenchyme (Schwann + perineurial + fibroblast + mast cells, plus axons); schwannoma = pure Schwann cells.

stem: A patient has a soft, pendulous "bag of worms" mass. This lesion (a plexiform neurofibroma) is:

options.A: Pathognomonic of neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1)
options.B: A characteristic skin finding of tuberous sclerosis complex
options.C: Diagnostic of NF2, which features vestibular schwannomas
options.D: A hallmark of Carney complex with its cardiac myxomas

exp: A plexiform neurofibroma is a tortuous, rope-like proliferation of neurofibromatous tissue along a nerve and its branches, producing a soft, pendulous "bag of worms" mass; it is considered congenital and is *pathognomonic of neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1)*. Recognizing one is clinically important because it should trigger a search for the other NF1 features — Lisch nodules (iris hamartomas), axillary/inguinal freckling, and café-au-lait macules — and because plexiform neurofibromas are the lesions at risk of malignant transformation to an MPNST (B115.4). Extensive involvement of a limb can cause "elephantiasis neuromatosa." The distractors are different genodermatoses with their own hallmarks: tuberous sclerosis (angiofibromas, shagreen patch, ash-leaf macules), NF2 (bilateral vestibular schwannomas), and Carney complex (lentigines plus cardiac myxomas).
hy: Plexiform neurofibroma ("bag of worms") = pathognomonic of NF1, and the precursor at risk for MPNST.

stem: Which statement about the pathogenesis of a cutaneous neurofibroma is correct?

options.A: Only a Schwann-cell subset is neoplastic (biallelic NF1 loss), aided by NF1+/− mast cells
options.B: Every cell in the tumor carries the same somatic BRAF V600E activating point mutation
options.C: It is a monoclonal proliferation driven by an inherited germline RET receptor mutation
options.D: Its growth depends on a GNAQ-mutant melanocytic clone that expands within the dermis

exp: The NF1 gene encodes neurofibromin, a RAS-GAP tumor suppressor that switches off RAS signaling; in a neurofibroma only a *subpopulation* of Schwann cells is truly neoplastic, carrying clonal biallelic NF1 inactivation (NF1−/−, "nullizygosity"). Crucially, that loss is necessary but not sufficient — tumor formation also requires a permissive NF1-haploinsufficient (NF1+/−) microenvironment, classically the NF1+/− mast cells, which is why neurofibromas are part-neoplasm, part-reactive. (Sporadic solitary neurofibromas often retain neurofibromin, so their pathogenesis is less clear.) The distractors assign drivers of other tumors: BRAF V600E drives melanoma and melanocytic nevi, a germline RET mutation drives MEN2 (medullary thyroid carcinoma + pheochromocytoma), and GNAQ/GNA11 drive uveal melanoma and blue nevi.
hy: Neurofibroma = biallelic NF1 (neurofibromin/RAS-GAP) loss in a Schwann subpopulation + a promoting NF1+/− mast-cell microenvironment.

stem: By what route does a malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor (MPNST) most characteristically arise from neurofibroma?

options.A: From a plexiform neurofibroma via deletion of the CDKN2A/CDKN2B locus
options.B: From a solitary cutaneous neurofibroma via acquired BRAF V600E mutation
options.C: From a diffuse neurofibroma via amplification of the MDM2 oncogene
options.D: From an intraneural neurofibroma via a GNAQ/GNA11 activating mutation

exp: A malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor (MPNST) — an aggressive S100/SOX10-variable spindle-cell sarcoma with a "herringbone" fascicular pattern, marked atypia, abnormal mitoses, and necrosis — most characteristically arises from a *pre-existing plexiform neurofibroma* through deletion of the CDKN2A/CDKN2B locus (loss of the p16/p14ARF and p15 cell-cycle brakes). This is why plexiform neurofibromas (and NF1 patients) warrant monitoring: rapid growth or new pain in a long-standing plexiform lesion suggests transformation. High-grade MPNSTs also frequently lose PRC2 activity (EED/SUZ12), reflected as loss of H3K27me3 staining — a useful diagnostic clue. The distractors misassign the route or driver: solitary and diffuse neurofibromas are not the usual precursors, and BRAF V600E, MDM2 amplification, and GNAQ/GNA11 are drivers of other tumors (melanoma/nevi, sarcomas/liposarcoma, and uveal melanoma/blue nevi, respectively).
hy: Plexiform neurofibroma → MPNST via CDKN2A/CDKN2B deletion (high-grade tumors also lose H3K27me3).

stem: Within the spectrum of malignant change in neurofibroma, the intermediate step "ANNUBP" denotes:

options.A: Atypical neurofibromatous neoplasm of uncertain biologic potential — atypia, ↑cellularity, ↑mitoses
options.B: A wholly benign "ancient" neurofibroma whose scattered nuclear atypia carries no clinical concern
options.C: A frankly malignant high-grade sarcoma with herringbone fascicles and loss of H3K27me3
options.D: A hybrid neurofibroma/schwannoma tumor showing nuclear palisading and Verocay bodies

exp: ANNUBP — "atypical neurofibromatous neoplasm of uncertain biologic potential" — is the recognized *intermediate* lesion on the pathway from benign neurofibroma to MPNST, defined by a variable combination of nuclear atypia, increased cellularity, and increased mitotic activity. The concept matters clinically because it separates worrisome change from harmless change: cytologic atypia *alone* in a neurofibroma is degenerative and NOT concerning (so-called "ancient neurofibroma," analogous to ancient change in other benign tumors), whereas atypia *plus* hypercellularity *plus* mitoses (ANNUBP) signals a lesion that may progress and warrants closer management. The distractors are the neighbours ANNUBP sits between: the frank high-grade sarcoma is MPNST (herringbone fascicles, H3K27me3 loss), and nuclear palisading with Verocay bodies is a hybrid neurofibroma/schwannoma.
hy: ANNUBP = atypia + ↑cellularity + ↑mitoses (pre-malignant); atypia alone = benign "ancient" neurofibroma.

stem: On histology, which feature is characteristic of a localized cutaneous neurofibroma?

options.A: An unencapsulated dermal spindle-cell proliferation under a grenz zone, S100-positive and mast-cell-rich
options.B: A well-encapsulated tumor with Antoni A/B areas and prominent Verocay-body nuclear palisading
options.C: Sheets of small round blue cells with abundant mitoses and perinuclear dot-like CK20 staining
options.D: Nests of large polygonal cells with granular eosinophilic cytoplasm and pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia

exp: The localized cutaneous neurofibroma — the common variant — is an *unencapsulated* dermal proliferation that infiltrates surrounding tissue (it lacks a capsule even when grossly circumscribed), characteristically separated from the overlying epidermis by an uninvolved "grenz zone." It is built of a fine fibrillary lattice of haphazardly arranged slender, wavy/buckled spindle cells with a variably myxoid-to-fibrotic stroma, is mast-cell-rich, expresses S100 (Schwannian marker), and shows rare or absent mitoses. The "no capsule + grenz zone + slender S100+ cells + mast cells" pattern is what separates it from its mimics. The distractors are those mimics, each with its own histology: an *encapsulated* tumor with Antoni A/B areas and Verocay-body palisading is a schwannoma; sheets of small round blue cells with perinuclear-dot CK20 are Merkel cell carcinoma; and nests of large cells with granular eosinophilic cytoplasm under pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia are a granular cell tumor.
hy: Neurofibroma histology = unencapsulated, grenz zone, slender S100+ spindle cells, mast-cell-rich, scant mitoses.

stem: The clinical differential diagnosis of a solitary cutaneous neurofibroma most classically includes:

options.A: Intradermal melanocytic nevus, schwannoma, soft fibroma, fibrolipoma
options.B: Basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, cutaneous horn, sebaceous hyperplasia
options.C: Pyogenic granuloma, cherry hemangioma, glomus tumor, angiokeratoma
options.D: Dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans, atypical fibroxanthoma, keloid, scar

exp: A solitary cutaneous neurofibroma usually presents as a soft, skin-colored, dome-shaped or polypoid papulonodule that often "buttonholes" (invaginates) on pressure, so its clinical differential is the set of other soft, skin-colored dermal/polypoid lesions: intradermal melanocytic nevus, schwannoma, soft fibroma (skin tag/acrochordon), and fibrolipoma. Knowing this differential matters because it is resolved by simple excision with histology (the treatment for a symptomatic or uncertain lesion). The distractors are coherent differentials for *other* presentations: scaly/pearly keratinocyte lesions (BCC, actinic keratosis, cutaneous horn), red vascular papules (pyogenic granuloma, cherry hemangioma, glomus tumor), and firm fibrohistiocytic/scar lesions (dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans, atypical fibroxanthoma, keloid).
hy: Solitary NF clinical DDx = intradermal nevus, schwannoma, soft fibroma, fibrolipoma; treat by simple excision.

stem: Merkel cell carcinoma is best characterized as:

options.A: An aggressive primary neuroendocrine carcinoma of skin (Toker's trabecular carcinoma)
options.B: An indolent low-grade adnexal tumor of eccrine origin with an excellent overall prognosis
options.C: A benign neuroendocrine hamartoma derived directly from epidermal touch-sensing Merkel cells
options.D: A locally aggressive but essentially non-metastasizing keratinocytic carcinoma of the elderly

exp: Merkel cell carcinoma is an aggressive primary neuroendocrine carcinoma of the skin — originally named "trabecular carcinoma" by Toker — that shares features with other neuroendocrine tumors and metastasizes readily (≈40% of patients). The distractors invert its biology: it is neither indolent/eccrine, nor a benign hamartoma (its cell of origin is actually unclear, not the normal Merkel cell), nor a non-metastasizing keratinocytic carcinoma.
hy: MCC = aggressive neuroendocrine carcinoma; Toker's "trabecular carcinoma."

stem: Which epidemiologic statement about Merkel cell carcinoma is correct?

options.A: It is far more common with age (≈10,000× more likely at 80 than at 20 years), with a male predominance
options.B: It occurs predominantly in young women, with incidence falling steadily as the population ages
options.C: It affects the sexes equally and shows no relationship between patient age and tumor incidence
options.D: It is most frequent in darkly pigmented skin and is unrelated to ultraviolet sun exposure

exp: MCC is a tumor of the elderly: incidence rises strikingly with age — an 80-year-old is roughly 10,000× more likely to develop it than a 20-year-old — with a male predominance (M:F ≈ 1.5:1), and its overall incidence is climbing as populations age. The steep age dependence reflects the two forces that drive MCC: lifetime cumulative UV damage and the decline of immune surveillance (immunosenescence), both of which accumulate with age. This is also why the tumor is rare but important — early recognition is critical given its aggressive behavior. The distractors invert the epidemiology: it is not a young-woman tumor, it is strongly age-related (not age-independent), and it arises in lightly pigmented sun-exposed skin rather than dark skin unrelated to UV.
hy: MCC: ↑ steeply with age (≈10,000× at 80 vs 20), male predominance, rising with the aging population.
src: Bologna Ch 115 · Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Epidemiology

stem: Which set of risk factors is most associated with Merkel cell carcinoma?

options.A: Lightly pigmented skin, extensive sun exposure, chronic immunosuppression
options.B: Darkly pigmented skin, chronic arsenic ingestion, prior ionizing radiotherapy
options.C: Human papillomavirus infection, heavy smoking, chronic limb lymphedema
options.D: Chronic scarring, deep burns, long-standing venous stasis leg ulcers

exp: The three major MCC risk factors are lightly pigmented skin, extensive sun (UV) exposure, and chronic immunosuppression — the last including solid-organ transplant recipients and patients with hematologic malignancies (e.g. CLL). These map onto the disease biology: UV both damages DNA (driving virus-negative, UV-signature tumors) and locally immunosuppresses, while systemic immunosuppression lets MCPyV-driven clones escape immune control — which is also why MCC responds so well to checkpoint immunotherapy. The distractors are risk factors for *other* skin cancers: arsenic ingestion, prior ionizing radiotherapy, and chronic scars/burns/stasis ulcers predispose to keratinocyte (especially squamous) carcinomas, and HPV/smoking/lymphedema belong to other tumors.
hy: MCC risk = lightly pigmented skin + UV exposure + immunosuppression (transplant, CLL).
src: Bologna Ch 115 · Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Epidemiology

stem: In the United States, what fraction of Merkel cell carcinomas are driven by Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV), and what characterizes the rest?

options.A: ~80% are MCPyV-driven; the other ~20% arise in sun-damaged skin with UV-signature mutations
options.B: ~20% are MCPyV-driven; the other ~80% arise from inherited CDKN2A germline mutations
options.C: ~50% are MCPyV-driven; the other ~50% are caused by human papillomavirus integration
options.D: Essentially 100% are MCPyV-driven; UV exposure plays no role in any geographic region

exp: MCC has two distinct etiologies. In the US about 80% are driven by Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV) — a virus that is ubiquitous on normal skin but very rarely integrates clonally to cause cancer — while the other ~20% are virus-negative, arising in chronically sun-damaged skin and carrying very high UV-signature mutation burdens. The split is geographic, tracking UV exposure and skin pigmentation: only ~40% are virus-driven in high-UV Australia (most are UV-driven there), versus ~90% in more pigmented Asian populations. This dichotomy is clinically meaningful — virus-negative (UV-driven) tumors tend to carry a somewhat poorer prognosis. The distractors reverse the fractions, invoke a CDKN2A germline syndrome, or wrongly credit HPV (the virus of cervical/oropharyngeal cancer, not MCC).
hy: US MCC ≈ 80% MCPyV-driven / 20% UV-signature; Australia ~40% viral, Asia ~90% viral.
src: Bologna Ch 115 · Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Pathogenesis

stem: How does MCPyV cause Merkel cell carcinoma at the molecular level?

options.A: Clonal viral integration plus a truncating mutation inactivating the large T-antigen helicase
options.B: Episomal viral persistence that silences the host CDKN2A locus by promoter hypermethylation
options.C: Viral insertion that directly activates a BRAF V600E mutation within the infected Merkel cell
options.D: An E6/E7-like protein that degrades both p53 and Rb without any genomic viral integration

exp: Two events are needed for MCPyV to cause MCC: (1) a clonal integration event splices the virus into a host chromosome, and (2) a mutation truncates the large T-antigen so that its helicase (DNA-replication) domain is inactivated. The truncation is essential because an *intact* helicase would fire viral DNA replication at the integration site and kill the cell — so only viruses that have lost helicase activity (but retained the Rb-binding oncogenic portion of large T and the small T-antigen) can transform. Critically, the tumor stays "addicted" to ongoing viral oncoprotein expression — silencing the viral proteins halts proliferation — which is the rationale behind targeting these tumor antigens immunologically. The distractors are other oncogenic mechanisms: CDKN2A promoter silencing, BRAF V600E activation, and the HPV E6/E7 pathway (which degrades p53 and Rb without genomic integration) are not how MCPyV works.
hy: MCPyV: clonal integration + truncating large-T mutation (dead helicase) → oncoprotein-dependent tumor.
src: Bologna Ch 115 · Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Pathogenesis

stem: Merkel cell carcinoma typically presents clinically as:

options.A: A pink-red to violaceous, firm, rapidly growing solitary nodule, often on the head and neck
options.B: A slowly enlarging pearly papule with rolled borders and arborizing telangiectasias
options.C: A chronic scaly erythematous plaque with overlying adherent hyperkeratotic scale
options.D: A pigmented macule with an asymmetric irregular border and multiple shades of brown

exp: MCC classically presents as a pink-red to violaceous, firm, dome-shaped, *rapidly growing* solitary nodule (it may ulcerate), favoring sun-exposed head and neck skin, then the extremities and buttocks. Its look is nonspecific — easily mistaken for a cyst, a vascular lesion, or a benign nodule — so diagnosis is frequently delayed; at presentation roughly 65% have local disease, 26% nodal, and 8% distant. The clinical lesson is a low threshold to biopsy a rapidly growing, painless, firm reddish nodule in an older, sun-damaged or immunosuppressed patient. The distractors are other tumors with different morphology: a pearly papule with arborizing telangiectasias is BCC, an adherent scaly plaque is SCC/Bowen disease, and an asymmetric, multi-colored pigmented macule is melanoma.
hy: MCC = rapidly growing pink-red/violaceous firm nodule, head/neck; 65% present localized.
src: Bologna Ch 115 · Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Clinical Features

stem: On histopathology, Merkel cell carcinoma is composed of:

options.A: Monotonous small round-to-ovoid blue cells with scant cytoplasm, abundant mitoses, and necrosis
options.B: Nests of atypical keratinocytes with intercellular bridges and keratin pearl formation
options.C: Palisaded basaloid cells with peripheral clefting (retraction) from a mucinous stroma
options.D: Sheets of large epithelioid melanocytes with prominent nucleoli and pagetoid scatter

exp: MCC is a "small round blue cell" tumor: a dermal (often deeply infiltrating) proliferation of monotonous small, round-to-ovoid basaloid cells about 2–3× the size of mature lymphocytes, with scant cytoplasm, finely dispersed ("salt-and-pepper" neuroendocrine) chromatin, inconspicuous nucleoli, abundant mitoses, and extensive single-cell and zonal necrosis. A sheet-like growth pattern is most common (followed by nested and the original "trabecular" patterns), and characteristic crush/drying artifact plus frequent intralymphatic tumor are typical clues. Because the cells are small and bland, the differential is the whole small-round-blue-cell family — hence the central role of immunohistochemistry (B115.15–16). The distractors are other tumors' histology: keratinocytes with intercellular bridges and keratin pearls are SCC; peripheral palisading with stromal retraction is BCC; and large epithelioid cells with prominent nucleoli and pagetoid scatter are melanoma.
hy: MCC = monotonous small round blue cells, scant cytoplasm, brisk mitoses, necrosis, sheet-like growth.
src: Bologna Ch 115 · Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Pathology

stem: Which immunostaining pattern is considered pathognomonic of Merkel cell carcinoma?

options.A: A perinuclear dot-like pattern of cytokeratin 20 (CK20)
options.B: Diffuse strong nuclear staining for thyroid transcription factor 1
options.C: Strong cytoplasmic and nuclear positivity for the S100 protein
options.D: Membranous staining for CD31 and ERG endothelial markers

exp: The pathognomonic immunostain of MCC is a perinuclear *dot-like* pattern of low-molecular-weight cytokeratins (classically CK20, also CK5/6, CK7), which corresponds ultrastructurally to paranuclear whorls of intermediate filaments — a paranuclear keratin "dot" you can recognize on the slide. MCC also expresses neuroendocrine markers (chromogranin, synaptophysin, neuron-specific enolase) reflecting its neuroendocrine nature, and CK20 positivity is additionally useful for spotting tumor at margins and detecting micrometastases. The distractors are markers that are wrong for MCC: TTF-1 is characteristically NEGATIVE (it is positive in small cell lung carcinoma — the key mimic), S100 is characteristically NEGATIVE (positive in melanoma), and CD31/ERG mark endothelial/vascular tumors.
hy: MCC = perinuclear-dot CK20 (pathognomonic) + neuroendocrine markers; S100 and TTF-1 negative.
src: Bologna Ch 115 · Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Pathology

stem: A small-round-blue-cell dermal tumor is TTF-1–negative and S100–negative but CK20-positive. How does this help?

options.A: TTF-1 negativity argues against metastatic small cell lung carcinoma, and S100 negativity against melanoma
options.B: TTF-1 negativity confirms a melanoma, whereas CK20 positivity confirms a basal cell carcinoma
options.C: S100 positivity would be expected in Merkel cell carcinoma and rules the diagnosis out here
options.D: TTF-1 positivity is the defining marker of Merkel cell carcinoma and is required for the diagnosis

exp: Because MCC is morphologically just one of several "small round blue cell" tumors, the diagnosis is made by a *pattern of exclusion* with immunohistochemistry. TTF-1 negativity argues against a cutaneous metastasis of small cell carcinoma (pulmonary or extrapulmonary) — the single most important mimic, which is characteristically TTF-1-POSITIVE — and S100 negativity argues against melanoma; meanwhile perinuclear-dot CK20 positively supports MCC, and MCPyV large-T-antigen staining can add confirmation. This combined "rule-out the dangerous mimics, rule-in with CK20" logic is exactly how the panel is used in practice. The distractors invert the facts: S100 and TTF-1 are negative (not positive) in MCC, and CK20 does not mark basal cell carcinoma.
hy: MCC panel: CK20+ (rules in) / TTF-1− (vs small cell lung ca) / S100− (vs melanoma).
src: Bologna Ch 115 · Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Pathology

stem: In AJCC-8 staging of Merkel cell carcinoma, a 3 cm primary tumor with no nodal or distant disease is which stage?

options.A: Stage IIA
options.B: Stage I
options.C: Stage IIB
options.D: Stage III

exp: In AJCC-8 staging of MCC, a primary tumor >2 cm with no nodal or distant disease is stage IIA (a 3 cm node-negative tumor therefore falls here).
