options.C: Always elevate flaps in a subfascial plane, regardless of the vascular quality of the tissue
options.D: Avoid undermining entirely, because scarred tissue tolerates tension better than normal skin

exp: Regardless of design, care must be taken to widen the tip of flaps to avoid tip necrosis, and because scars often have distorted, unreliable blood supply, thicker flaps are necessary in scarred tissue (in unscarred tissue, elevate the subdermal plexus to include the cutaneous microvasculature). Sharpening tips and using the thinnest flaps invite necrosis; a mandatory subfascial plane and "never undermine" are not stated.
hy: Widen flap tips; use thicker flaps in unreliable scar vasculature.

why.A: Correct — widen tips; thicker flaps for scarred, unreliable blood supply.
why.B: Wrong — sharp, thin tips increase necrosis risk.
why.C: Wrong — a mandatory subfascial plane is not the stated rule.
why.D: Wrong — undermining/advancement is used, not avoided.

stem: The senior authors use a mechanical silicone shielding device (Embrace) after closure. What is its rationale and evidence level as described?

options.A: It blocks perpendicular tension across the incision and cut scarring in a level-1 randomized trial
options.B: It delivers intradermal corticosteroid continuously and was validated only in uncontrolled case series
options.C: It works by freezing the incision line and to date has no published comparative evidence at all
options.D: It replaces all of the sutures at closure and was reported to increase scar width in a level-1 trial

exp: The senior authors apply a mechanical silicone shielding device (Embrace, Neodyne Biosciences) ~2–3 weeks after closure to block perpendicular tension across the incision line for at least 2 months; in a 12-month prospective, randomized, multicenter (abdominoplasty) trial it significantly improved scar appearance versus control — level-1 evidence. It does not deliver steroid, freeze the incision, or replace sutures, and it decreased rather than increased scar formation.
hy: Embrace offloads perpendicular incisional tension; level-1 trial evidence.
src: Neligan I.16 · Wound dressings and postoperative care

why.A: Correct — blocks perpendicular tension; level-1 randomized evidence.
why.B: Wrong — it is not a continuous intradermal steroid device.
why.C: Wrong — it does not work by freezing and does have comparative evidence.
why.D: Wrong — it supplements, not replaces, sutures and reduces scarring.

section: Scar revision · Principles of tissue rearrangement
stem: When local-flap scar revision on the face conflicts with an anatomic landmark, what priority does the chapter set?

options.A: Restoring landmarks (e.g., vermilion border, eyelid position, alar base) takes priority over minimizing scar features
options.B: Minimizing the overall length and visibility of the scar always takes clear priority over the position of any landmark
options.C: The scar should always be widely excised and skin-grafted rather than locally rearranged when a landmark is nearby
options.D: Landmark distortion is acceptable provided the final scar lies within a relaxed skin-tension line

exp: In tissue rearrangement, restoration of anatomic landmarks — such as the vermilion border, eyelid position, or alar base — should take priority over minimizing scar characteristics; using a local flap to prevent landmark distortion (accepting a somewhat larger scar) exemplifies this. Prioritizing scar length, mandating graft-over-rearrangement, or accepting landmark distortion for an RSTL scar all contradict the stated priority.
hy: Landmark restoration outranks scar minimization.
src: Neligan I.16 · Principles of tissue rearrangement

why.A: Correct — landmark restoration takes priority over scar minimization.
why.B: Wrong — scar minimization does not outrank landmark position.
why.C: Wrong — rearrangement (e.g., local flap) is often preferred over grafting.
why.D: Wrong — landmark distortion is not acceptable merely for RSTL placement.

section: Scar revision · Principles of tissue rearrangement
stem: Why does local tissue rearrangement deliberately convert a long straight scar into an irregular or lengthened one?

options.A: A long straight scar contracts along one axis, worsening deformity; irregularizing it spreads forces to aid camouflage
options.B: An irregular scar is said to heal faster because each of the shorter segments re-epithelializes fully on its own
options.C: Straight scars can never be placed within the relaxed skin-tension lines, whereas irregular scars supposedly always can be
options.D: Irregularization is said to increase tension along the scar axis, which is required to flatten a raised hypertrophic scar

exp: A long, straight scar contracts uniformly along one axis (myofibroblast contracture within the scar axis), causing greater surrounding deformity, depression, and possible movement limitation. Local tissue rearrangement reorients and lengthens the scar and breaks it into segments, dispersing contractile forces into multiple axes — yielding less tissue deformation and a more easily camouflaged scar. The distractors invent faster segmental healing, an RSTL impossibility, or claim irregularization raises axial tension (it disperses it).
hy: Irregularization disperses contractile force across axes → better camouflage.
src: Neligan I.16 · Principles of tissue rearrangement

why.A: Correct — disperses uniform axial contracture into multiple axes.
why.B: Wrong — segmental "faster healing" is not the rationale.
why.C: Wrong — straight scars can lie in RSTLs; that is not the reason.
why.D: Wrong — irregularization disperses, not increases, axial tension.

stem: A patient presents with the raised, well-demarcated lesions shown. They remained confined within the borders of the original wounds. What are they?

options.A: Hypertrophic scars
options.B: Keloids
options.C: Mature normotrophic scars
options.D: Dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans

exp: The lesions are hypertrophic scars: raised and itchy but, crucially, confined within the borders of the original wounds (here, surgical/chest incisions). This within-boundary behavior is the defining clinical difference from keloids, which overgrow the original margins. Mature normotrophic scars are flat, and DFSP is a dermal sarcoma, not a post-incisional scar pattern.
hy: Hypertrophic scar = raised but within the original wound margins.
src: Neligan I.16 · Conditions of excessive scarring · Fig 16.3
imgcap: Raised scars confined within original wound margins

why.A: Correct — raised scars confined to the original wound = hypertrophic.
why.B: Wrong — keloids extend beyond the original margins.
why.C: Wrong — mature normotrophic scars are flat, not raised.
why.D: Wrong — DFSP is a sarcoma, not this post-incisional scar.

stem: The lesions shown extend beyond the boundaries of the original injuries, including a pedunculated earlobe and sternal lesion. What are they?

options.A: Keloids
options.B: Hypertrophic scars
options.C: Immature scars
options.D: Acne icepick scars

exp: These are keloids: raised scars that have grown beyond the boundaries of the original injuries (the classic pedunculated earlobe and expansile sternal lesions), the defining feature separating them from hypertrophic scars. Hypertrophic scars stay within the wound, immature scars are red and only slightly elevated, and icepick acne scars are pitted depressions, not exophytic overgrowths.
hy: Keloid = scar overgrowing the original wound boundary.
src: Neligan I.16 · Conditions of excessive scarring · Fig 16.4
imgcap: Raised scars extending beyond original wound margins

why.A: Correct — overgrowth beyond the wound margin = keloid.
why.B: Wrong — hypertrophic scars remain within the wound.
why.C: Wrong — immature scars are flat/slightly raised and red.
why.D: Wrong — icepick acne scars are depressed, not exophytic.

stem: The scar-revision technique diagrammed here interdigitates two triangular flaps of equal angle, transposing each into the other's defect to lengthen the scar. Which technique is shown?

options.A: Simple Z-plasty
options.B: W-plasty
options.C: V–Y advancement flap
options.D: Geometric broken-line closure

exp: The diagram shows a simple (classic) Z-plasty: two triangular flaps of equal angle (traditionally 60°) transposed into each other's defect to lengthen the scar along its axis while redirecting tension. A W-plasty uses a zigzag excision without transposition, a V–Y advancement does not transpose flaps, and geometric broken-line closure uses assorted small reciprocal shapes — none matches equal-angle triangular transposition.
hy: Simple Z-plasty = transposition of two equal triangular flaps.
src: Neligan I.16 · Scar revision techniques · Fig 16.11
imgcap: Transposition of two equal triangular flaps

why.A: Correct — equal triangular flaps transposed = simple Z-plasty.
why.B: Wrong — W-plasty is a zigzag excision, no transposition.
why.C: Wrong — V–Y advancement does not transpose flaps.
why.D: Wrong — geometric broken line uses mixed reciprocal shapes.

stem: The technique diagrammed uses a zigzag excision of small interdigitating limbs with no net lengthening of the scar. Which technique is shown?

options.A: W-plasty
options.B: Simple Z-plasty
options.C: Four-flap Z-plasty
options.D: V–Y advancement flap

exp: The diagram shows a W-plasty: a zigzag pattern of excision with reapproximation of small interdigitated limbs and no net lengthening of the scar (tissue is removed). It is distinct from a Z-plasty (transposes triangular flaps and lengthens), a four-flap Z-plasty (web-space release), and a V–Y advancement (non-transposing). The "RSTL" label reflects Borges's use of W-plasty for facial scars not lying in relaxed skin-tension lines.
hy: W-plasty = interdigitated zigzag excision, no lengthening.
src: Neligan I.16 · Scar revision techniques · Fig 16.17
imgcap: Zigzag interdigitating limb excision

why.A: Correct — zigzag interdigitating excision = W-plasty.
why.B: Wrong — a simple Z-plasty transposes flaps and lengthens.
why.C: Wrong — four-flap Z-plasty is a web-space release design.
why.D: Wrong — V–Y advancement is non-transposing, not a zigzag excision.

stem: The camouflage technique shown incises randomly distributed small triangles, squares, trapezoids, and semicircles at the wound edge with reciprocal shapes opposite. Which technique is it?

options.A: Geometric broken-line closure
options.B: A standard W-plasty closure
options.C: A skew Z-plasty variant technique
options.D: Planimetric Z-plasty

exp: The figure shows geometric broken-line closure: 5–6 mm randomly distributed triangles, squares, trapezoids, and semicircles incised at the wound edge with reciprocal shapes planned opposite, to camouflage (especially facial) scars crossing aesthetic subunits. It is more irregular/random than a W-plasty's uniform zigzag, and is neither a skew nor a planimetric Z-plasty (both transposition designs).
hy: Geometric broken line = random reciprocal small shapes for camouflage.
src: Neligan I.16 · Scar revision techniques · Fig 16.18
imgcap: Irregular geometric interdigitations at wound edge

why.A: Correct — random reciprocal geometric shapes = geometric broken line.
why.B: Wrong — W-plasty is a regular zigzag, not mixed random shapes.
why.C: Wrong — skew Z-plasty is a transposition variant.
why.D: Wrong — planimetric Z-plasty is a coplanar transposition variant.

stem: The multiple-flap design shown is frequently used to release a first-web-space contracture. Which technique is it?

options.A: Four-flap Z-plasty
options.B: Simple (single) Z-plasty
options.C: W-plasty
options.D: Y–V advancement flap

exp: The design shown is a four-flap Z-plasty (a multiple-flap Z-plasty), frequently used — like Mustardé's "jumping man" five-flap — for release of first-web-space contractures; note the multiple triangular flaps drawn at 60° that transpose to lengthen and release the contracture. A simple Z-plasty has just two flaps, a W-plasty is a zigzag excision, and a Y–V advancement is non-transposing.
hy: Four-flap Z-plasty = multiple-flap design for first-web-space release.
src: Neligan I.16 · Scar revision techniques · Fig 16.15
imgcap: Multiple-flap Z-plasty design with four triangular flaps

why.A: Correct — multiple transposing flaps = four-flap Z-plasty.
why.B: Wrong — a simple Z-plasty has only two flaps.
why.C: Wrong — W-plasty is a zigzag excision, not transposing flaps.
why.D: Wrong — Y–V advancement does not transpose flaps.

stem: The advancement technique shown incorporates adjacent tissue without transposition and therefore does not require undermining or threaten the flap's vasculature the way a Z-plasty does. Which technique is it?

options.A: V–Y / Y–V advancement
options.B: Four-flap Z-plasty
options.C: A zigzag W-plasty
options.D: Geometric broken-line closure

exp: The figure shows V–Y / Y–V advancement flaps, which incorporate adjacent tissue without transposition and therefore require no undermining and do not threaten the vasculature the way a Z-plasty does; they can be carried out in tandem with careful design. A four-flap Z-plasty and W-plasty both rearrange tissue differently, and geometric broken line is an irregularization excision.
hy: V–Y/Y–V = non-transposing advancement, no undermining.
src: Neligan I.16 · Scar revision techniques · Fig 16.16
imgcap: Advancement flap converting between V and Y limb configurations

why.A: Correct — non-transposing advancement = V–Y/Y–V flap.
why.B: Wrong — a four-flap Z-plasty transposes multiple flaps.
why.C: Wrong — W-plasty is a zigzag excision technique.
why.D: Wrong — geometric broken line is an irregularization excision.

stem: The staged scar-revision strategy illustrated across these panels removes a portion of a large forehead scar at each sitting, undermining and advancing normal tissue before the next stage. Which strategy is shown?

options.A: Serial excision
options.B: Single-stage direct excision
options.C: Tissue expansion
options.D: Four-flap Z-plasty

exp: The panels illustrate serial excision: a large forehead scar is reduced in stages, circumferentially excising a portion and then undermining and advancing bordering normal tissue before the next stage, so that two or three sittings accomplish what would be hazardous in one. It is not single-stage direct excision (one sitting), tissue expansion (expander placement to grow skin), or a four-flap Z-plasty (transposition design).
hy: Serial excision = staged partial excision + advancement over multiple sittings.
src: Neligan I.16 · Scar revision · Planning · Fig 16.10
imgcap: Staged reduction of a large scar across sequential operations

why.A: Correct — staged partial excision with advancement = serial excision.
why.B: Wrong — a single-stage excision removes it all at once.
why.C: Wrong — tissue expansion grows skin with an implant first.
why.D: Wrong — a four-flap Z-plasty is a transposition design, not staged excision.

stem: A surgeon tangentially shaves a deep dermal burn down to a bed of punctate capillary bleeding and immediately applies a split-thickness graft in the same sitting. This "excise-and-graft in one stage" approach to burns is credited to which surgeon?

options.A: Zora Janžekovič
options.B: Karl Thiersch
options.C: Jacques-Louis Reverdin
options.D: James Tanner

exp: Zora Janžekovič is credited with tangential (sequential shave) excision of the burn eschar followed by immediate split-thickness grafting — a one-stage technique that became the modern standard of care for deep dermal burns, because early removal of nonviable tissue and immediate coverage limits infection, fluid loss, and hypertrophic scarring. Thiersch described preparing the recipient bed to enhance revascularization and lends his name to the thin STSG; Reverdin introduced the pinch graft; Tanner devised mechanical meshing (1964). Each is a real skin-graft pioneer, so the specific attribution must be known.
hy: Janžekovič = tangential excision + immediate STSG (deep-burn standard).
src: Neligan I.17 · History of the graft technique

why.A: Correct — tangential excision + immediate grafting is Janžekovič's contribution.
why.B: Wrong — Thiersch prepared the recipient bed and names the thin STSG, not one-stage burn excision.
why.C: Wrong — Reverdin introduced the pinch (Reverdin) graft.
why.D: Wrong — Tanner developed the mesh graft (1964).

stem: Which technical development, introduced in 1964, first let a single split-thickness graft be expanded to cover a wound far larger than the harvested area with minimal added donor morbidity?

options.A: Mechanical meshing of the graft (Tanner)
options.B: The introduction of the electric powered dermatome
options.C: Tangential eschar excision (Janžekovič)
options.D: Refrigerated banking of autologous skin

exp: James Tanner's systematic mechanical meshing (1964) cut regularly spaced slits into a split-thickness graft so it could be stretched to cover several times its harvested area, which is why one modest donor site can resurface a large burn. The electric/mechanical dermatome (introduced in the 1940s) enabled uniform-thickness harvest but does not itself expand the graft; tangential excision prepares the wound rather than expanding the graft; refrigerated banking (early 1990s) stores rather than enlarges skin. All are real milestones, so the date and the expansion function pin the answer.
hy: Tanner 1964 = mechanical meshing → graft expansion.
src: Neligan I.17 · History of the graft technique

why.A: Correct — Tanner's 1964 mesher is the expansion technique.
why.B: Wrong — the dermatome (1940s) harvests uniform skin but does not expand it.
why.C: Wrong — tangential excision prepares the burn bed, it does not enlarge the graft.
why.D: Wrong — refrigerated banking stores skin, it does not increase coverage area.

stem: Which epidermal layer is present only in the glabrous skin of the palms and soles, contributing to their extra thickness?

options.A: Stratum lucidum
options.B: Stratum granulosum
options.C: Stratum spinosum
options.D: Stratum basale

exp: The stratum lucidum is a translucent zone found deep to the stratum corneum only in thick glabrous skin (palms and soles), adding to the mechanical thickness of those high-wear sites. The granulosum (keratohyalin-rich transition zone), spinosum (desmosome-anchored keratinocytes plus Langerhans cells), and basale (proliferative keratinocytes, melanocytes, Merkel cells) are present throughout the integument regardless of region, so they cannot be the site-restricted layer. All four are real epidermal strata, so the distinguishing glabrous-only feature is the discriminator.
hy: Stratum lucidum is found only in glabrous palm/sole skin.
src: Neligan I.17 · Anatomy and physiology of skin

why.A: Correct — the stratum lucidum is restricted to palmar/plantar glabrous skin.
why.B: Wrong — the granulosum is present across the whole integument.
why.C: Wrong — the spinosum (Malpighian layer) is universal, not glabrous-restricted.
why.D: Wrong — the basal (germinal) layer is present everywhere.

stem: The proliferative keratinocytes that renew the epidermis, together with melanocytes and Merkel mechanoreceptors, reside in which layer?

options.A: Stratum basale
options.B: Stratum corneum
options.C: Stratum granulosum
options.D: Stratum lucidum

exp: The stratum basale (germinal layer) houses the proliferative, self-renewing keratinocyte stem cells, the neural-crest-derived melanocytes, and the Merkel cells (epithelial mechanoreceptors) — it is the generative floor from which the more superficial layers progressively differentiate. The corneum is terminally differentiated anuclear corneocytes; the granulosum is an intermediate keratohyalin zone; the lucidum is a thin glabrous-only band. Because keratinocyte proliferation begins here, damage sparing the basale (or the appendageal reservoirs) allows re-epithelialization. All are real strata, so the proliferative-plus-melanocyte-plus-Merkel combination locates it precisely.
hy: Stratum basale holds proliferative keratinocytes, melanocytes, and Merkel cells.
src: Neligan I.17 · Anatomy and physiology of skin

why.A: Correct — basal layer holds proliferative keratinocytes, melanocytes, and Merkel cells.
why.B: Wrong — the corneum is dead, terminally differentiated corneocytes.
why.C: Wrong — the granulosum is a differentiation transition zone, not the germinal layer.
why.D: Wrong — the lucidum is an acellular translucent band in glabrous skin.

stem: Roughly how long is the normal keratinocyte differentiation cycle, from production at the basal layer to shedding at the surface?

options.A: 40–60 days
options.B: 7–14 days
options.C: 3–6 months
options.D: 24–48 hours

exp: Keratinocytes produced in the stratum basale take approximately 40–60 days to differentiate upward and complete the cycle to the surface — a turnover rate whose disruption (either hypo- or hyperproliferation, as in psoriasis) is pathologic. The neighbouring values are plausible but wrong: small superficial wounds re-epithelialize in about 2–14 days (not the 7–14 shown), 24–48 hours is when graft revascularization begins, and 3–6 months overshoots epidermal turnover. The item requires the exact figure rather than an order-of-magnitude estimate.
hy: Keratinocyte turnover ≈ 40–60 days.
src: Neligan I.17 · Anatomy and physiology of skin

why.A: Correct — the chapter cites ~40–60 days for the full keratinocyte cycle.
why.B: Wrong — 7–14 days is not the turnover figure; small wounds re-epithelialize in ~2–14 days.
why.C: Wrong — 3–6 months overstates epidermal turnover.
why.D: Wrong — 24–48 hours is when graft revascularization starts, unrelated to turnover.

stem: Within the basement membrane, which component is the collagen IV–rich layer that lies just deep to the integrin/laminin-rich lamina lucida?

options.A: Lamina densa
options.B: Lamina lucida
options.C: Basal reticularis
options.D: Papillary dermis

exp: The basal lamina is divided into a superficial lamina lucida (rich in integrins and laminins) and a deeper lamina densa (rich in collagen IV); beneath the basal lamina lies the basal reticularis, a collagen III meshwork linked to the overlying lamina densa by collagen VII/fibrillin anchoring fibrils, while basal keratinocytes anchor upward via hemidesmosomes. Grafts lacking a robust dermal–epidermal junction are mechanically unstable, which is clinically why thin grafts blister and shear. All options are real junctional strata, so the collagen-IV attribution is the discriminator.
hy: Lamina densa = collagen IV; lamina lucida = integrins/laminins.

why.A: Correct — the lamina densa is the collagen IV–rich layer.
why.B: Wrong — the lamina lucida is superficial and rich in integrins/laminins, not collagen IV.
why.C: Wrong — the basal reticularis is a collagen III meshwork below the basal lamina.
why.D: Wrong — the papillary dermis is dermal, above the basement membrane, not within it.

stem: The resting tension of skin (Langer's lines) is primarily determined by the elastic and collagen fibers of which dermal layer?

options.A: Reticular dermis
options.B: Papillary (subpapillary) dermis
options.C: The hypodermis (subcutis)
options.D: Basal lamina

exp: The reticular dermis — the thicker, deeper, collagen- and elastin-rich compact layer beneath the subpapillary plexus — is the primary elastic determinant of skin and is responsible for Langer's resting lines of tension; pathologic loss of its elastic fibers produces tissue stiffness. The papillary dermis is the thin superficial layer that anchors the dermal–epidermal junction and handles metabolic exchange; the hypodermis is subcutaneous fat/stroma; the basal lamina is part of the junction. All are real skin strata, so the elastic-determinant/Langer's-lines cue points specifically to the reticular dermis.
hy: Reticular dermis is the elastic determinant (Langer's lines).

why.A: Correct — the reticular dermis sets skin's elasticity and Langer's lines.
why.B: Wrong — the papillary dermis anchors the DEJ and does metabolic exchange, not bulk elasticity.
why.C: Wrong — the hypodermis is subcutaneous fat, not the elastic tension determinant.
why.D: Wrong — the basal lamina is a junctional sheet, not the source of resting tension.

stem: Approximately what proportion of the epidermal cell population is made up of the neural-crest-derived cells that produce melanin?

options.A: About 10%
options.B: Approximately 1%
options.C: About 25%
options.D: About 50%

exp: Melanocytes make up approximately 10% of the epidermal cell population; they are neural-crest-derived, reside in the basal layer, and package melanin into melanosomes that they transfer to keratinocytes. Melanin is highly photo-absorptive and can block in excess of 99% of UV light. The distractors are adjacent plausible fractions but wrong — 1% understates, and 25% or 50% overstate the melanocyte share (keratinocytes dominate the epidermis). The exact figure matters because graft thickness determines whether functional epidermal melanin units transfer, affecting later pigmentation and UV vulnerability.
hy: Melanocytes ≈ 10% of epidermal cells; melanin blocks >99% of UV.

why.A: Correct — melanocytes are ~10% of epidermal cells.
why.B: Wrong — 1% understates the melanocyte fraction.
why.C: Wrong — 25% overstates it.
why.D: Wrong — 50% is far too high; keratinocytes are the dominant epidermal cell.

stem: A cutaneous receptor responsible for sensing vibration is best matched to which encapsulated structure?

options.A: Lamellar corpuscle of Pacini
options.B: Bulbous corpuscle of Ruffini
options.C: Tactile corpuscle of Meissner
options.D: Nerve ending of Merkel

exp: The lamellar (Pacinian) corpuscle is the deep, onion-layered mechanoreceptor tuned to vibration and rapid pressure change. The other three are real cutaneous mechanoreceptors with distinct modalities: Ruffini (bulbous) corpuscles sense skin stretch, Meissner (tactile) corpuscles detect light touch and are concentrated in glabrous skin, and Merkel endings signal sustained pressure. Because all four are genuine receptors in the same class, the item is answered only by knowing the specific modality mapping.
hy: Pacinian (lamellar) corpuscle = vibration.
src: Neligan I.17 · Neuronal supply of the skin

why.A: Correct — Pacinian (lamellar) corpuscles transduce vibration.
why.B: Wrong — Ruffini (bulbous) corpuscles sense stretch, not vibration.
why.C: Wrong — Meissner (tactile) corpuscles sense light touch.
why.D: Wrong — Merkel endings sense sustained pressure.

stem: From superficial to deep, which sequence correctly orders the four zones of the hair follicle?

options.A: Infundibulum → isthmus → stem → bulb
options.B: Bulb → stem → isthmus → infundibulum (deep-to-superficial)
options.C: Isthmus → infundibulum → bulb → stem
options.D: Stem → bulb → infundibulum → isthmus

exp: The hair follicle is divided, superficial to deep, into the infundibulum (surface to the apocrine/sebaceous entry), the isthmus, the stem, and the bulb (deepest, containing the follicular matrix and papilla that regenerate the hair). The bulge, just below the sebaceous unit, is the resident stem-cell niche, and the presence of epidermal cells down to the bulb explains rapid re-epithelialization from retained follicles after split-thickness harvest. The distractor orders scramble real zone names, so the exact superficial-to-deep sequence must be recalled.
hy: Follicle superficial→deep: infundibulum, isthmus, stem, bulb.

why.A: Correct — infundibulum, isthmus, stem, bulb is the superficial-to-deep order.
why.B: Wrong — this reverses the sequence.
why.C: Wrong — the infundibulum is most superficial, not second.
why.D: Wrong — the bulb is deepest, not second.

stem: Which cutaneous gland type is restricted mainly to the axillae, areolae, nipples, perineum, and genitalia and secretes a proteinaceous product?

options.A: Apocrine glands
options.B: Eccrine glands
options.C: Sebaceous glands
options.D: Meibomian glands

exp: Apocrine glands are regionally restricted to the axillae, areolae/nipples, perineum, genitalia, and parts of the eyelid, nostril, and ear canals, and their secretion is proteinaceous. Eccrine (merocrine) glands cover most of the body surface and secrete clear hypotonic sweat under autonomic/emotional control; sebaceous glands are holocrine, associated with hair follicles, and release lipid-rich sebum (the acid mantle); meibomian glands are specialized holocrine sebaceous glands of the eyelid. All are real glandular structures, so the distribution-plus-proteinaceous pairing identifies the apocrine gland.
hy: Apocrine glands = axillae/areolae/perineum/genitalia, proteinaceous.

why.A: Correct — apocrine glands are region-restricted and proteinaceous.
why.B: Wrong — eccrine glands are widespread and secrete hypotonic sweat.
why.C: Wrong — sebaceous glands secrete lipid sebum around hair shafts.
why.D: Wrong — meibomian glands are specialized sebaceous glands of the eyelid.

stem: The skin is the primary site of the UVB-driven conversion of which precursor toward vitamin D?

options.A: 7-dehydrocholesterol → cholecalciferol
options.B: Ergosterol → ergocalciferol
options.C: 25-hydroxyvitamin D → 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D
options.D: 7-dehydrocholesterol → 25-hydroxyvitamin D

exp: In the epidermis, UVB radiation converts 7-dehydrocholesterol to cholecalciferol (vitamin D3), the critical first cutaneous step in vitamin D production; subsequent hydroxylations to 25-OH-D (liver) and 1,25-(OH)₂-D (kidney) occur elsewhere. Ergocalciferol (D2) derives from plant ergosterol, not cutaneous synthesis. The distractors name real vitamin-D-pathway conversions but place them at the wrong organ or wrong step, so the item tests which reaction the skin actually performs.
hy: Skin converts 7-dehydrocholesterol → cholecalciferol under UVB.
src: Neligan I.17 · Metabolic functions of the skin

why.A: Correct — skin converts 7-dehydrocholesterol to cholecalciferol under UVB.
why.B: Wrong — ergosterol→ergocalciferol (D2) is a dietary/plant pathway, not cutaneous.
why.C: Wrong — 1α-hydroxylation to 1,25-(OH)₂-D occurs in the kidney.
why.D: Wrong — 25-hydroxylation is a hepatic step, not the cutaneous reaction.

stem: The Mathes and Nahai classification of the cutaneous perforators that supply fasciocutaneous flaps divides them into which three types?

options.A: Direct cutaneous, septocutaneous, musculocutaneous
options.B: Axial, random, and reverse-flow cutaneous vascular patterns
options.C: Type I, Type II, Type III muscle pedicles
options.D: Choke, true, and oscillating anastomoses

exp: The chapter cites the Mathes and Nahai classification of fasciocutaneous perforators as direct cutaneous, septocutaneous, and musculocutaneous — describing whether the perforator reaches the skin directly, travels through an intermuscular septum, or passes through a muscle. This underlies donor-site perfusion planning. Axial/random describes cutaneous flap vascular patterns generally; Types I–V is Mathes–Nahai's separate classification of muscle pedicles; choke/true anastomoses belong to Taylor's angiosome work. All are real vascular concepts, so the exact three-way fasciocutaneous scheme is the discriminator.
hy: Mathes–Nahai fasciocutaneous: direct cutaneous, septocutaneous, musculocutaneous.
src: Neligan I.17 · Vascular supply of the skin

why.A: Correct — direct cutaneous, septocutaneous, musculocutaneous are the three perforator routes.
why.B: Wrong — axial vs random describes flap design, not this perforator classification.
why.C: Wrong — Types I–V is the muscle-pedicle classification, a different Mathes–Nahai scheme.
why.D: Wrong — choke/true anastomoses are from Taylor's angiosome studies.

section: Restoration of barrier function after injury
stem: In a small, uncomplicated wound in a healthy patient, cutaneous integrity is typically re-established over what time frame?

options.A: 2–14 days
options.B: 2–14 hours
options.C: 6–12 weeks
options.D: 6–12 months

exp: For small, less-complex wounds in healthy individuals, re-epithelialization restores cutaneous integrity in roughly 2–14 days, driven by keratinocyte migration from wound edges and from residual eccrine and pilosebaceous appendages. In large, ablative injuries (burns, polytrauma) or in patients with comorbidities, poor nutrition, or infection, this process is delayed or fails, which is precisely when surgical coverage such as grafting becomes necessary. The distractors are wrong orders of magnitude — hours is too fast, and weeks or months describe complex or grafted-scar timelines.
hy: Small healthy wounds re-epithelialize in ~2–14 days.
src: Neligan I.17 · Restoration of barrier function after injury

why.A: Correct — small healthy wounds re-epithelialize in ~2–14 days.
why.B: Wrong — hours is far too fast for re-epithelialization.
why.C: Wrong — 6–12 weeks reflects complicated or large-wound healing.
why.D: Wrong — 6–12 months overlaps scar maturation, not initial closure.

stem: By thickness, which split-thickness graft classification is correctly matched to its eponym and range?

options.A: Thin STSG (0.15–0.3 mm) — Thiersch–Ollier
options.B: Thick STSG (0.45–0.6 mm) — Thiersch–Ollier
options.C: Intermediate STSG (0.3–0.45 mm) — Wolfe
options.D: Thin STSG (0.15–0.3 mm) — Blair–Brown

exp: Split-thickness grafts are classified by thickness as thin (0.15–0.3 mm; the Thiersch–Ollier graft), intermediate (0.3–0.45 mm; the Blair–Brown graft), and thick (0.45–0.6 mm; a deep graft). The distractors mis-pair the eponym or range: the Thiersch–Ollier name belongs to the thin graft (not the thick), Blair–Brown to the intermediate (not the thin), and Wolfe denotes a full-thickness graft, not an intermediate STSG. Because every option is a real graft descriptor, the exact eponym-to-range match must be known.
hy: STSG: thin 0.15–0.3 (Thiersch–Ollier), intermediate 0.3–0.45 (Blair–Brown), thick 0.45–0.6 mm.

why.A: Correct — thin STSG 0.15–0.3 mm is the Thiersch–Ollier graft.
why.B: Wrong — Thiersch–Ollier is the thin (not thick) graft.
why.C: Wrong — Wolfe denotes a full-thickness graft; Blair–Brown is the intermediate STSG.
why.D: Wrong — Blair–Brown is the intermediate graft, not the thin one.

stem: A thin split-thickness donor site in a healthy adult is generally healed enough to permit a repeat harvest from the same area after approximately how long?

options.A: About 2 weeks
options.B: About 2 days
options.C: About 6 weeks
options.D: About 6 months

exp: Because a thin split-thickness harvest leaves the deeper dermal appendages intact to re-epithelialize the donor bed, that site is usually healed and adequate for a repeat harvest after about 2 weeks — a major reason thin grafts are chosen when serial harvests from limited donor skin are needed (e.g. large burns). The distractors bracket the true value: 2 days is too short for donor re-epithelialization, while 6 weeks and 6 months greatly overstate it. Exact recall separates the adjacent options.
hy: Thin STSG donor site is reharvestable at ~2 weeks.

why.A: Correct — a thin STSG donor site is reharvestable at ~2 weeks.
why.B: Wrong — 2 days is too soon for donor healing.
why.C: Wrong — 6 weeks overstates thin-graft donor healing time.
why.D: Wrong — 6 months is far too long.

stem: Compared with a thick split-thickness or full-thickness graft, a thin split-thickness graft is expected to show which recipient-site behavior?

options.A: Greater secondary contracture at the recipient site
options.B: Less secondary contracture at the recipient site
options.C: More reliable hair regrowth in the graft
options.D: Better long-term restoration of sweat-gland function

exp: The general rule is that the thinner the graft, the more secondary contracture it undergoes at the recipient site — so thin STSGs contract most, while thicker grafts (carrying more dermis) resist contraction and are chosen over joints and high-friction areas. Thin grafts contain few or no whole cutaneous appendages, so they rarely grow hair or restore sweating; those functions return, if at all, with thick STSGs and full-thickness grafts. The distractors state the opposite of each true relationship, so the direction of the thickness–contracture and thickness–appendage rules is the discriminator.
hy: Thinner graft → MORE recipient contracture, fewer appendages.

why.A: Correct — thinner grafts contract more at the recipient site.
why.B: Wrong — less contracture is a property of thicker grafts.
why.C: Wrong — thin grafts rarely regrow hair (appendages left behind).
why.D: Wrong — sweat-gland function is not restored by thin grafts.

stem: A reconstruction over the dorsum of a mobile joint needs coverage that will resist later contraction. Regarding graft thickness, which choice best fits this goal?

options.A: A thicker graft, because more transferred dermis limits contraction
options.B: A thinner graft, because it revascularizes faster over the joint
options.C: A thinner graft, because thin grafts are said to resist contraction best
options.D: A meshed thin graft expanded 1:9 to maximize coverage

exp: Contraction is inversely related to the dermal component of a graft, so a thicker graft (thick STSG or full-thickness) is preferred over joints and other high-mechanical-friction areas precisely because the extra dermis limits secondary contraction that would otherwise restrict joint motion. A thinner graft takes more readily on marginal beds but contracts more — the opposite of what a joint needs — and a widely meshed graft contracts even more and leaves a cobblestoned surface. The pivotal modifier is the joint location, which shifts the preference toward thickness over ease of take.
hy: Over joints use a thicker graft (more dermis = less contraction).

why.A: Correct — thicker grafts carry more dermis and contract less, ideal over joints.
why.B: Wrong — faster take is a thin-graft trait but it does not address contraction.
why.C: Wrong — thin grafts contract more, not less.
why.D: Wrong — wide meshing increases contracture, worsening joint outcome.

stem: Why are thinner split-thickness grafts often preferred in young children and elderly patients?

options.A: Their skin is thinner, so a thin harvest avoids prolonged donor-site healing
options.B: Thin grafts transfer more melanocytes, improving color match in these groups
options.C: Thin grafts contract less, protecting growth in children and lax skin in elders
options.D: Thin grafts reliably restore sweating, which these groups tolerate poorly to lose

exp: Children and the elderly have inherently thinner skin than non-elderly adults, so taking a thin split-thickness graft avoids harvesting too deeply and prevents prolonged or troublesome donor-site healing in those age groups. The distractors invert real graft physiology: thin grafts transfer fewer (not more) appendages and melanocytes, they contract more (not less), and they do not restore sweating. The pivotal fact is the age-related thinness of the donor skin, not any contraction or appendage advantage.
hy: Children/elderly → thin graft (thinner native skin protects donor healing).

why.A: Correct — thinner native skin means a thin harvest protects donor healing.
why.B: Wrong — thin grafts carry fewer melanocytes, not more.
why.C: Wrong — thin grafts contract more; contraction is not the rationale.
why.D: Wrong — thin grafts do not restore sweat-gland function.

stem: At what angle to the skin is a powered dermatome typically held to harvest a split-thickness graft?

options.C: 90° (perpendicular)

exp: The powered (electric or air-driven) dermatome is pressed to the skin at roughly a 45° angle and advanced with uniform pressure and speed; the set guard determines nominal thickness, but the operator's downward pressure also influences actual depth. The adjacent angles are wrong: too shallow (15°) or too steep (90°) would produce an uneven or too-deep cut. Because the numbers are neighbouring plausible values, the exact figure is required.
hy: Powered dermatome is held at ~45°.

why.A: Correct — ~45° is the standard dermatome approach angle.
why.B: Wrong — 15° is too shallow for a uniform harvest.
why.C: Wrong — 90° (perpendicular) would gouge rather than shave.
why.D: Wrong — 75° is steeper than the described technique.

stem: Meshing a split-thickness graft carries which trade-off the surgeon must weigh?

options.A: It enlarges coverage but increases contracture and can leave a cobblestone relief
options.B: It enlarges coverage while reducing contracture and improving final texture
options.C: It speeds donor healing but weakens the graft against shear at the donor site postoperatively
options.D: It improves color match but requires a lower expansion ratio to take reliably

exp: Meshing enlarges a graft's coverage area (templates from 1:1 up to about 1:9), invaluable when wound area exceeds available donor skin, but the interstices heal by secondary keratinocyte migration and the graft contracts more, often leaving an uneven cobblestone relief; higher expansion ratios also raise the risk of hypertrophic scarring. That is why surgeons frequently limit expansion to about 1:1.5–1:2 and avoid meshing over functional or aesthetic areas. The distractors reverse the contracture effect or attach meshing to the wrong outcome (donor healing, color match).
hy: Meshing expands coverage but ↑contracture and cobblestoning.

why.A: Correct — meshing expands coverage at the cost of more contracture and cobblestoning.
why.B: Wrong — meshing increases, not reduces, contracture.
why.C: Wrong — meshing concerns the graft/recipient, not donor-site shear.
why.D: Wrong — meshing does not improve color match; ratio choice is about contracture/scarring.

stem: For a full-sheet (non-meshed) split-thickness graft over a functional area such as the hand, why is the graft "pie-crusted" with multiple small perforations?

options.A: To let seroma or hematoma drain so fluid does not lift the graft off the bed
options.B: To expand the graft's surface area up to nine times before insetting
options.C: To seed keratinocytes into the wound bed for faster re-epithelialization overall
options.D: To decrease graft contracture across the underlying joint

exp: Pie-crusting places multiple small perforations in an unmeshed graft so that blood and serum can escape, preventing a fluid collection (seroma or hematoma) from separating the graft from its bed and destroying take — the paramount concern over functional areas where meshing's cobblestoning is undesirable. It is not an expansion technique (that is meshing, up to ~9×), does not seed cells, and does not reduce contracture. All options describe real graft manoeuvres, so the fluid-egress-without-expansion purpose is the answer.
hy: Pie-crusting = fluid egress on a non-meshed graft (protects take).

why.A: Correct — pie-crusting drains fluid to protect graft take without meshing.
why.B: Wrong — expansion up to 9× is the function of meshing, not pie-crusting.
why.C: Wrong — cell seeding describes RECELL/keratinocyte spray, not pie-crusting.
why.D: Wrong — pie-crusting does not address contracture.

stem: A burn surgeon divides harvested skin into many small squares that are spread apart on a carrier to cover a much larger area when donor skin is scarce. This is which technique?

options.A: Meek technique
options.B: Tanner meshing
options.C: Reverdin pinch grafting
options.D: RECELL spray application

exp: The Meek technique divides the graft into small skin squares that are transferred onto a carrier and spread apart, achieving high expansion when donor skin is scarce — useful in massive burns. Conventional Tanner meshing slits a continuous sheet (up to ~1:9); Reverdin pinch grafting places small individual pinch grafts; RECELL delivers a sprayed suspension of autologous cells rather than skin squares. All four are real coverage/expansion methods, so the small-squares-spread-on-a-carrier description identifies the Meek technique specifically.
hy: Meek technique = skin squares spread on a carrier.

why.A: Correct — dividing skin into spread squares on a carrier is the Meek technique.
why.B: Wrong — Tanner meshing slits a continuous sheet, not squares on a carrier.
why.C: Wrong — Reverdin grafting uses small pinch grafts, not a squared carrier.
why.D: Wrong — RECELL sprays a cell suspension, not skin squares.

stem: RECELL, applied over a widely meshed split-thickness graft, contributes to healing by delivering what?

options.A: An aerosolized suspension of autologous epithelial cells
options.B: A sheet of cultured allogeneic keratinocytes on a carrier mesh
options.C: A bovine collagen–silicone bilayer dermal template
options.D: A recombinant growth factor–soaked gauze

exp: RECELL delivers an aerosolized (spray-on) suspension of the patient's own epithelial cells prepared at the bedside from a small donor sample; sprayed over widely meshed grafts it can shorten healing time and improve contracture and hypertrophic-scarring outcomes by seeding the open interstices with autologous cells. The distractors are real but different products/approaches: cultured allogeneic keratinocyte sheets, a bovine collagen–silicone dermal template (e.g. Integra), and growth-factor dressings are each a distinct technology. The autologous-cell-suspension-spray definition is the discriminator.
hy: RECELL = aerosolized autologous epithelial-cell spray.

why.A: Correct — RECELL is an autologous epithelial-cell spray.
why.B: Wrong — allogeneic cultured sheets are a different, off-the-shelf product.
why.C: Wrong — a bovine collagen–silicone bilayer describes a dermal template like Integra.
why.D: Wrong — RECELL delivers cells, not growth-factor gauze.

stem: A full-thickness graft is elliptically designed to allow primary closure of the donor site; the chapter states this is generally achieved when which length:width ratio is followed?

options.C: Roughly 1:5

exp: Designing the full-thickness donor ellipse with a length:width ratio of about 1:3 usually permits tension-free primary closure without dog-ears or distortion of surrounding tissue — the same geometric principle as an elliptical excision. A 1:1 design is too short and would not close cleanly, 1:5 is longer than needed, and 3:1 simply restates the ratio in reverse orientation. Because the options are neighbouring geometric ratios, the exact figure and its orientation must be recalled.
hy: FTSG donor ellipse ≈ 1:3 length:width for primary closure.

why.A: Correct — a ~1:3 length:width ellipse allows primary donor closure.
why.B: Wrong — 1:1 is too short and would pucker on closure.
why.C: Wrong — 1:5 is longer than the stated ratio.
why.D: Wrong — 3:1 mis-states the length:width orientation.

stem: Before inset, a full-thickness graft is "defatted." Why is removing the subcutaneous fat important?

options.A: Fat is poorly vascularized and impedes revascularization, worsening graft take
options.B: Retained fat carries melanocytes that cause unpredictable graft hyperpigmentation
options.C: Fat adds bulk that must be matched to recipient thickness for contour
options.D: Removing fat exposes hair bulbs so the graft can regrow hair

exp: The subcutaneous fat on the deep surface of a full-thickness graft is relatively avascular and acts as a barrier to imbibition and revascularization, so trimming it flush with the dermis (spreading the graft over a finger and cutting tangentially) improves graft take and reduces fat necrosis. The distractors misattribute the reason: melanocytes reside in the epidermis (not the fat), contour-matching is not the defatting rationale, and hair bulbs are within the dermis/follicle, not exposed by defatting. All statements sound plausible, so the vascular-barrier mechanism is the discriminator.
hy: Defat FTSG: avascular fat blocks revascularization and causes fat necrosis.

why.A: Correct — avascular fat blocks revascularization, so it is removed to aid take.
why.B: Wrong — melanocytes are epidermal, not carried in the trimmed fat.
why.C: Wrong — defatting is about vascularity, not contour matching.
why.D: Wrong — hair follicles sit in the dermis; defatting does not expose bulbs for regrowth.

stem: A composite graft differs from a full-thickness skin graft in that it also includes which additional component, making optimal revascularization especially critical?

options.A: A layer of subcutaneous fat (and sometimes cartilage)
options.B: Only the epidermis, harvested very thin
options.C: A vascular pedicle kept continuously attached to the donor site
options.D: An engineered dermal matrix substitute

exp: A composite graft carries epidermis, dermis, and an additional layer such as subcutaneous fat (or cartilage, as in alar reconstruction) as a single free non-vascularized unit; because fat is less vascular and more prone to ischemia, optimal recipient-bed revascularization is critical for survival, and composite grafts are size-limited. It is not thinner than an STSG, has no attached pedicle (that would be a flap), and is autologous tissue rather than an engineered matrix. The distractors describe different constructs, so the extra-fat/cartilage-layer, no-pedicle definition is the answer.
hy: Composite graft = skin + fat/cartilage (needs excellent revascularization).

why.A: Correct — a composite graft adds fat/cartilage to skin as a free graft.
why.B: Wrong — that describes a thin split-thickness graft, not a composite graft.
why.C: Wrong — an attached vascular pedicle would make it a flap, not a graft.
why.D: Wrong — a composite graft is autologous tissue, not an engineered matrix.

stem: Composite grafts are most frequently used to reconstruct which region?

options.A: Nasal tip, ala, and columella
options.B: Dorsal hand and web spaces
options.C: Anterior lower leg over the tibia
options.D: Weight-bearing plantar heel

exp: Composite grafts (skin plus cartilage/fat) are most frequently used for the nasal tip, ala, and columella — including in cleft-lip patients — because these small, contoured subunits need the structural support of the extra layer and tolerate a small graft's revascularization demand. Larger, poorly vascularized, or weight-bearing sites (leg over tibia, plantar heel, broad hand surfaces) exceed the survival limits of a composite graft and are managed with flaps or split-thickness grafts. All are real reconstructive sites, so the small-contoured-nasal-subunit indication is the discriminator.
hy: Composite grafts reconstruct nasal tip/ala/columella.

why.A: Correct — nasal tip/ala/columella are the classic composite-graft sites.
why.B: Wrong — broad hand surfaces are grafted with STSG/FTSG or flaps.
why.C: Wrong — the pretibial region is poorly vascularized for a composite graft.
why.D: Wrong — a weight-bearing heel needs durable flap coverage, not a composite graft.

stem: For resurfacing a facial defect, which donor sites are specifically recommended for the best color and texture match?

options.A: Supraclavicular, postauricular, eyelid, or scalp
options.B: Anterior lateral thigh, lateral trunk, or lower back skin
options.C: Plantar foot or palmar glabrous hand skin
options.D: Lower abdomen, groin, or buttock skin

exp: The chapter recommends supraclavicular, posterior auricular, upper eyelid, and scalp skin for facial resurfacing because skin from similar or adjacent sites gives the best texture and color match (like replaces like). The thigh and trunk are standard split-thickness donor sites but give a poorer facial color match; glabrous palmar/plantar skin is specialized and mismatched to the face; abdomen/buttock skin is used for body defects. All are legitimate donor regions, so the facial color-match indication points to the head-and-neck sites.
hy: Facial donors: supraclavicular/postauricular/eyelid/scalp (best color match).
src: Neligan I.17 · Functional and aesthetic considerations

why.A: Correct — head/neck-region skin best matches facial color and texture.
why.B: Wrong — thigh/trunk are STSG donors with a poorer facial match.
why.C: Wrong — glabrous palm/sole skin is specialized and mismatched to the face.
why.D: Wrong — abdomen/buttock skin is for body, not facial, matching.

stem: A graft sits in a concave defect where a flat pressure dressing cannot maintain contact. Which fixation method is specifically suited to this situation?

options.A: A tie-over bolster dressing
options.B: Absorbable interrupted sutures alone
options.C: Skin staples alone
options.D: Fibrin glue alone

exp: A tie-over bolster — a bulky dressing held down by long peripheral sutures tied over it — is specifically useful in difficult-to-dress areas and in grafts sitting in a depression relative to surrounding tissue, because it presses the graft into the contour and eliminates the dead space and micromotion that cause failure. Sutures, staples, or fibrin glue each secure a graft's edges or surface but do not, on their own, conform a dressing into a concavity. All are real fixation options, so the graft-in-a-depression cue selects the bolster.
hy: Tie-over bolster suits a graft in a depression / hard-to-dress area.
src: Neligan I.17 · Skin fixation and dressings

why.A: Correct — a bolster conforms pressure into a concave graft bed.
why.B: Wrong — sutures fix edges but do not press the graft into a depression.
why.C: Wrong — staples secure edges but leave dead space in a concavity.
why.D: Wrong — fibrin glue adheres but does not provide conforming pressure.

stem: Surgical dressings over a fresh skin graft are usually left undisturbed for how long, to minimize shearing during engraftment?

options.A: 3–8 days
options.B: 12–24 hours
options.C: 3–4 weeks
options.D: 2–3 months

exp: Graft dressings are typically left in place for about 3–8 days to minimize shearing and micromotion while the graft imbibes serum and begins revascularizing — the window during which the fragile new vessel connections are most easily disrupted. Removing the dressing at 12–24 hours risks shearing an unvascularized graft, while 3–4 weeks or months is unnecessarily long for the initial engraftment dressing. Adjacent time frames make the exact window the key.
hy: Graft dressings are left ~3–8 days.
src: Neligan I.17 · Skin fixation and dressings

why.A: Correct — graft dressings stay ~3–8 days through early engraftment.
why.B: Wrong — 12–24 hours is too early and risks shearing.
why.C: Wrong — 3–4 weeks exceeds the engraftment-dressing window.
why.D: Wrong — months is far too long for the initial dressing.

stem: Fibrin glue is sometimes sprayed onto the recipient bed just before graft placement. What is its proposed mechanism?

options.A: It forms a provisional extracellular matrix that adheres and stabilizes the graft
options.B: It delivers a slow-release antibiotic to sterilize the wound bed
options.C: It supplies exogenous collagen that the graft's own fibroblasts subsequently remodel
options.D: It provides a vascularized pedicle substitute until inosculation

exp: Fibrin glue is theorized to act as a provisional extracellular matrix beneath the graft, both adhering it to the bed and stabilizing it against shear — complementing sutures/staples and helping obliterate dead space. It is not an antibiotic-delivery vehicle, is fibrin rather than exogenous collagen, and cannot substitute for a vascular pedicle (grafts revascularize from the bed regardless). The distractors are plausible-sounding adjunct mechanisms, so the provisional-ECM/adhesion rationale is the answer.
hy: Fibrin glue = provisional ECM that fixes/stabilizes the graft.
src: Neligan I.17 · Skin fixation and dressings

why.A: Correct — fibrin acts as a provisional matrix that fixes and stabilizes the graft.
why.B: Wrong — fibrin glue is not an antibiotic carrier.
why.C: Wrong — it is fibrin, not an exogenous collagen scaffold.
why.D: Wrong — it does not vascularize the graft; the bed does.

stem: Which recipient bed is LEAST likely to support skin graft take?

options.A: Bare cortical bone stripped of periosteum
options.B: Healthy granulation tissue over muscle
options.C: Vascularized peritendon overlying tendon
options.D: A freshly excised, bleeding dermal burn bed

exp: A skin graft depends on a recipient bed with abundant superficial blood vessels for imbibition and revascularization; bare cortical bone stripped of periosteum (like bare tendon without paratenon, or irradiated/ischemic/scar tissue) lacks that supply and will not support take. In contrast, healthy granulation, intact vascularized peritendon/periosteum, and a freshly excised bleeding burn bed all provide the needed vascularity. This is the exception-format question: three beds are graftable and only the avascular bare bone is not.
hy: Bare bone/tendon stripped of periosteum/paratenon will not take a graft.
src: Neligan I.17 · Recipient site · Wound bed preparation

why.A: Correct — bare bone without periosteum is avascular and will not take a graft.
why.B: Wrong — healthy granulation is well vascularized and graftable.
why.C: Wrong — intact vascularized peritendon can support a graft.
why.D: Wrong — a bleeding excised burn bed has the capillary supply a graft needs.

stem: Before grafting a chronically open, colonized wound, why is debridement to bleeding tissue performed first?

options.A: Necrotic debris and high bacterial load block new-vessel ingrowth
options.B: Debridement thins the dermis so a thinner graft can be used
options.C: Bleeding tissue signals that the wound no longer needs a graft
options.D: It converts the wound into a deeper full-thickness defect suitable for an FTSG

exp: Infected or debris-laden wounds will not support a graft: necrotic tissue and a high bacterial burden physically impede the ingrowth of new blood vessels and provoke infection that lifts the graft, so open wounds are debrided (sharp, laser, water-jet, or ultrasound) down to bleeding, healthy tissue and hemostasis is regained before grafting. The distractors misstate the goal — debridement is about a clean vascular bed, not thinning the dermis, avoiding grafting, or changing the defect's thickness class. All read plausibly, so the clear-debris-to-allow-vascular-ingrowth mechanism is the discriminator.
hy: Debride to bleeding tissue: necrosis/bacteria block new-vessel ingrowth.
src: Neligan I.17 · Recipient site · Wound bed preparation

why.A: Correct — removing necrotic/infected tissue lets vessels grow into the graft.
why.B: Wrong — debridement is not done to thin the dermis for graft choice.
why.C: Wrong — bleeding tissue indicates a graftable bed, not that grafting is unneeded.
why.D: Wrong — debridement prepares the bed; it does not reclassify the defect for FTSG.

stem: A split-thickness graft donor site generally heals over what time frame, depending on depth, size, and patient age?

options.A: 7–21 days
options.B: 1–3 days
options.C: 6–10 weeks
options.D: 4–6 months

exp: A split-thickness donor site re-epithelializes from the retained dermal appendages over roughly 7–21 days, the exact time depending on how deep and large the harvest was and on patient age (slower at the extremes of age). The distractors bracket this: 1–3 days is too fast for donor re-epithelialization, while 6–10 weeks or months would indicate impaired healing rather than the norm. The adjacent ranges make the exact figure the key.
hy: STSG donor site heals in ~7–21 days.

why.A: Correct — STSG donor sites heal in ~7–21 days.
why.B: Wrong — 1–3 days is far too short.
why.C: Wrong — 6–10 weeks would signal delayed healing.
why.D: Wrong — months indicates a complication, not normal donor healing.

stem: What are the three phases of skin graft "take," in the correct temporal order?

options.A: Serum imbibition → revascularization → maturation
options.B: Revascularization → serum imbibition → then maturation
options.C: Inflammation → proliferation → remodeling
options.D: Imbibition → maturation → revascularization

exp: A skin graft is transferred without a blood supply, so take proceeds in three phases: (1) serum (plasma) imbibition — diffusion of oxygen/nutrients from the bed over the first few days; (2) revascularization — new vascular connections beginning ~24–48 h; and (3) maturation — remodeling and contraction over many months. The inflammation/proliferation/remodeling option names the general stages of wound healing, a different framework; the other orders scramble the true sequence. Because all options use real terminology, the exact ordered triad of graft take is required.
hy: Graft take: serum imbibition → revascularization → maturation.
src: Neligan I.17 · Physiology of skin graft survival

why.A: Correct — imbibition, then revascularization, then maturation.
why.B: Wrong — imbibition precedes revascularization, not the reverse.
why.C: Wrong — inflammation/proliferation/remodeling are wound-healing phases, not graft-take phases.
why.D: Wrong — maturation is last, after revascularization.

stem: During the first phase of graft take, how does the graft obtain oxygen and nutrients before it has a blood supply?

options.A: Diffusion of plasma from the wound bed (serum imbibition)
options.B: Immediate arterial anastomosis with the recipient's vessels
options.C: Autonomous glycolysis independent of the recipient bed
options.D: Neovascular sprouting completed within the first hour

exp: In the first few days a graft survives by serum (plasma) imbibition — passive diffusion of plasma-borne oxygen and nutrients across the graft–bed interface — which sustains the cells until vascular connections form. Direct arterial anastomosis and neovascular ingrowth belong to the later revascularization phase (starting ~24–48 h, not the first hour), and grafts are not metabolically autonomous of the bed. All options describe real or pseudo-plausible nutritive routes, so the plasma-diffusion-before-vessels mechanism is the discriminator, and it is why a fluid collection under the graft (which blocks diffusion) is fatal to take.
hy: Early graft nutrition is by serum (plasma) imbibition.
src: Neligan I.17 · Physiology of skin graft survival

why.A: Correct — early survival is by plasma diffusion (imbibition).
why.B: Wrong — vascular anastomosis is the later revascularization phase.
why.C: Wrong — the graft is not metabolically independent of the bed.
why.D: Wrong — neovascularization is not complete within an hour.

stem: Revascularization of a skin graft is generally described as beginning at approximately what time after grafting?

options.A: 24–48 hours
options.B: Within 4–6 hours
options.C: 7–10 days
options.D: 3–4 weeks

exp: Revascularization typically begins around 24–48 hours after grafting and proceeds by a combination of anastomosis (inosculation) of graft and bed vessels, neovascularization, and endothelial-cell ingrowth using the graft's basal-lamina scaffold. Earlier (4–6 hours) the graft is still purely dependent on imbibition; 7–10 days or 3–4 weeks would fall into later maturation. Because the distractors are adjacent time points on the take timeline, the exact onset window is the key.
hy: Revascularization begins ~24–48 h after grafting.
src: Neligan I.17 · Physiology of skin graft survival

why.A: Correct — revascularization starts ~24–48 h post-grafting.
why.B: Wrong — at 4–6 h the graft relies on imbibition only.
why.C: Wrong — 7–10 days is beyond the described onset.
why.D: Wrong — 3–4 weeks falls into the maturation phase.

stem: During graft revascularization, "endothelial cell ingrowth" specifically refers to which process?

options.A: Host endothelium grows into the graft's basal lamina as graft endothelium degenerates
options.B: Graft vessels stay intact and dilate to accept host flow without host endothelial ingrowth
options.C: Graft and host vessel lumens directly reconnect end-to-end by simple inosculation
options.D: Graft lymphatic channels transform into new arterioles feeding the graft

exp: In endothelial-cell ingrowth, recipient endothelial cells proliferate and use the graft's pre-existing empty vascular basal lamina as a scaffold while the original graft endothelial cells degenerate — a distinct mechanism from anastomosis (inosculation), which is the direct reconnection of graft and host vessel lumens. Simple dilation of intact graft vessels and lymphatic-to-arteriole conversion are not described mechanisms. All options are phrased as plausible revascularization routes, so the host-endothelium-replacing-graft-endothelium definition is the discriminator.
hy: Endothelial ingrowth = host endothelium repopulates the graft's basal lamina.
src: Neligan I.17 · Physiology of skin graft survival

why.A: Correct — host endothelium repopulates the graft's basal-lamina scaffold.
why.B: Wrong — graft endothelium degenerates rather than persisting and dilating.
why.C: Wrong — direct lumen reconnection is anastomosis (inosculation), a separate mechanism.
why.D: Wrong — lymphatics do not convert into arterioles here.

stem: The maturation phase of a skin graft — involving myofibroblast-driven contraction and collagen remodeling — typically continues over what time frame, especially in burns and children?

options.A: More than a year
options.B: About 2 weeks
options.C: About 6 to 8 weeks
options.D: About 3 months

exp: Graft maturation — repopulation by fibroblasts that develop alpha-smooth-muscle contractile fibers (myofibroblasts), collagen deposition and cross-linking, and regression of the early immature vessels — takes over a year and can continue for several years, particularly in burn patients and children. This prolonged remodeling is why graft/scar appearance keeps changing and why definitive revision is deferred. The shorter options correspond to earlier take/healing milestones, not full maturation, so the over-a-year figure is the key.
hy: Graft maturation continues over a year (burns/children).
src: Neligan I.17 · Physiology of skin graft survival

why.A: Correct — maturation extends over a year or more.
why.B: Wrong — 2 weeks is far too short for maturation.
why.C: Wrong — 6–8 weeks is still early remodeling, not full maturation.
why.D: Wrong — 3 months underestimates the maturation timeline.

stem: Roughly how much blood loss is expected for each 1% of body surface area excised during burn/graft surgery?
exp: The chapter cites an expected blood loss of about 100–200 mL for every 1% of body surface area excised — a figure critical for planning transfusion and staging large burn excisions. The distractors are order-of-magnitude neighbours: 10–20 mL grossly underestimates surgical burn blood loss, while 500–750 mL or 1000–1500 mL per 1% would predict catastrophic loss for even modest excisions. Exact recall is needed because the values are all internally plausible for surgery.
hy: Blood loss ≈ 100–200 mL per 1% BSA excised.
src: Neligan I.17 · Management of complications · Hematoma/seroma

why.A: Correct — ~100–200 mL is lost per 1% BSA excised.
why.B: Wrong — 10–20 mL grossly underestimates the loss.
why.C: Wrong — 500–750 mL per 1% is too high.
why.D: Wrong — 1000–1500 mL per 1% would be catastrophic and is incorrect.

stem: When sweat-gland function does return after skin grafting, it generally does so only after which event, and around what time?

options.A: After neural reconnection, usually around 3 months
options.B: Immediately on revascularization, within the first week
options.C: After epidermal maturation, at about 2 weeks
options.D: Only after a year, coincident with full maturation

exp: Sweat glands, transferred mainly with thick split-thickness and full-thickness grafts, can become reactivated only after neural reconnection occurs — usually around 3 months after grafting — and even then remain abnormal, so thermoregulation and sweating stay impaired for years. The distractors give the wrong trigger or timing: revascularization within a week does not restore secretion (it needs reinnervation), 2 weeks is too early, and full maturation over a year is later than the described ~3-month reactivation. The neural dependence and ~3-month figure together are the key.
hy: Sweating returns only after reinnervation, ~3 months.
src: Neligan I.17 · Skin appendages and other structures

why.A: Correct — sweating returns after reinnervation, at ~3 months.
why.B: Wrong — revascularization alone does not restore secretion.
why.C: Wrong — 2 weeks is too early for reinnervation-dependent sweating.
why.D: Wrong — reactivation is at ~3 months, not delayed to a year.

stem: Shearing is a major cause of graft loss because it does what?

options.A: Disrupts the fragile newly forming vascular connections
options.B: Dissolves the fibrin adhesive bonding graft to bed
options.C: Triggers an immune rejection of the autograft
options.D: Accelerates serum imbibition beyond any sustainable rate

exp: Shearing motion between graft and bed physically tears the fragile, newly forming blood-vessel connections during the revascularization window, which is why immobilization (sealants, secure fixation, bolsters/splints, careful postoperative handling) is central to success. Shear is a mechanical, not immunologic, cause of failure — an autograft is not rejected — and it does not work by dissolving fibrin or by over-accelerating imbibition. All options are plausible-sounding failure mechanisms, so the disruption-of-forming-vessels explanation is the discriminator.
hy: Shearing kills grafts by disrupting new vascular connections.
src: Neligan I.17 · Management of complications · Instability

why.A: Correct — shear disrupts the new vascular connections, causing graft loss.
why.B: Wrong — shear tears vessels mechanically, not by dissolving fibrin.
why.C: Wrong — an autograft is not immunologically rejected.
why.D: Wrong — shear does not fail a graft by speeding imbibition.

stem: Which of the following is listed as a systemic factor that negatively affects skin graft take?

options.A: Corticosteroid therapy
options.B: Mild iron-deficiency anemia
options.C: Controlled essential hypertension
options.D: A remote history of appendectomy

exp: The chapter lists poor nutrition, corticosteroids, vasculitis, malignancies, and immunosuppressive medications as systemic factors that impair graft take by impairing inflammation, angiogenesis, and healing. Mild anemia, well-controlled hypertension, and a remote appendectomy are not cited as meaningful risks to take. All are real clinical conditions, but only corticosteroids appear on the chapter's list of factors that negatively affect engraftment, so recognizing the named risk factor is the task.
hy: Steroids, poor nutrition, vasculitis, immunosuppression impair take.
src: Neligan I.17 · Management of complications · Other factors

why.A: Correct — corticosteroids are a listed factor impairing graft take.
why.B: Wrong — mild anemia is not among the cited take-impairing factors.
why.C: Wrong — controlled hypertension is not listed as a graft risk.
why.D: Wrong — a remote appendectomy has no bearing on graft take.

stem: Integra, a widely used dermal regeneration template, is composed of what?

options.A: Bovine tendon type I collagen on a silicone sheet (xenogeneic)
options.B: Human acellular lyophilized cadaver dermis, an allogeneic product
options.C: Autologous cultured epidermis expanded from a biopsy
options.D: A synthetic biodegradable polymer dermal matrix

exp: Per Table 17.1, Integra is a xenogeneic dermal substitute made of bovine tendon type I collagen bonded to a silicone outer sheet; it is applied to the wound bed and is typically vascularized and ready for a thin skin graft in about 3 weeks (after which the silicone is removed). The distractors are the other real products in the same table: Alloderm is allogeneic human acellular cadaver dermis, Epicel is an autologous cultured epidermal autograft, and NovoSorb (BTM) is a synthetic biodegradable polymer matrix. Because all are genuine skin substitutes, the exact composition/origin pairing is the discriminator.
hy: Integra = bovine type I collagen on silicone (xenogeneic; ready ~3 wks).

why.A: Correct — Integra is bovine collagen on silicone, a xenogeneic template.
why.B: Wrong — acellular cadaver dermis describes Alloderm.
why.C: Wrong — autologous cultured epidermis describes Epicel.
why.D: Wrong — a synthetic polymer matrix describes NovoSorb (BTM).

stem: Which product is an off-the-shelf allogeneic acellular human dermis onto which a skin graft is placed after it has vascularized?

options.A: Alloderm (LifeCell)
options.B: Epicel (Genzyme)
options.C: RECELL (Avita Medical)
options.D: Integra (Integra Lifesciences)

exp: Alloderm is an allogeneic product — human acellular lyophilized cadaver dermis — kept on the shelf and applied to the bed, with a skin graft placed on top once it has vascularized; being decellularized, it minimizes antigenicity. Epicel (autologous cultured epidermal autograft) and RECELL (autologous cell spray) are patient-derived, and Integra is a xenogeneic bovine-collagen/silicone template. All four are real skin substitutes from Table 17.1, so the allogeneic acellular-dermis definition selects Alloderm.
hy: Alloderm = allogeneic acellular cadaver dermis.

why.A: Correct — Alloderm is allogeneic acellular cadaver dermis.
why.B: Wrong — Epicel is an autologous cultured epidermal autograft.
why.C: Wrong — RECELL is an autologous cell suspension spray.
why.D: Wrong — Integra is a xenogeneic bovine-collagen/silicone template.

stem: Cellularized nonhuman (e.g. porcine) skin applied to a burn in an immunocompetent patient functions fundamentally as what?

options.A: A temporary biologic dressing rather than a true engrafting graft
options.B: A permanent replacement that becomes vascularized and persists indefinitely
options.C: An autologous graft after enzymatic decellularization
options.D: A cultured epithelial autograft substitute

exp: Cellularized xenogeneic skin is immunologically incompatible with an immunocompetent host, so used topically it acts fundamentally as a biologic dressing — protecting the wound and promoting host healing — rather than permanently engrafting. Its benefits are availability, cost, lower infectious-transmission risk, and native-like architecture, but it is ultimately not incorporated. It is neither autologous nor a cultured epithelial autograft, and it does not persist as a permanent take. The distractors overstate its permanence or misclassify its origin, so the temporary-biologic-dressing role is the answer.
hy: Cellular xenograft skin = temporary biologic dressing (not a true graft).
src: Neligan I.17 · Non-autologous alternatives and adjuncts

why.A: Correct — cellular xenograft skin serves as a temporary biologic dressing.
why.B: Wrong — it is immunologically incompatible and does not permanently engraft.
why.C: Wrong — it is xenogeneic, not an autologous decellularized product.
why.D: Wrong — it is not a cultured epithelial autograft.

stem: The micrografting approach originally described with a Meek–Wall microdermatome generates ~0.8 × 0.8 mm skin pieces and can achieve expansion up to what ratio?

options.A: Up to 1:100
options.B: Up to 1:9
options.C: Up to 1:3
options.D: Up to 1:1000

exp: Micrografting with the Meek–Wall microdermatome cuts skin into roughly 0.8 × 0.8 mm pieces that can be expanded up to about 1:100, far beyond the ~1:9 ceiling of conventional sheet meshing; more extensive dissociation (pixel grafting, ~0.3 × 0.3 mm pieces) pushes expansion further. The distractors are real expansion ratios but for the wrong technique — 1:9 is the conventional sheet-mesh ceiling and 1:3 sits well below micrografting (surgeons often limit sheet meshing to ~1:1.5–1:2) — or an overstatement (1:1000). Because the numbers are on the same scale, the exact micrografting ratio must be recalled.
hy: Meek–Wall micrografting expands up to ~1:100.
src: Neligan I.17 · Beyond the graft · Graft expansion

why.A: Correct — Meek–Wall micrografting expands up to ~1:100.
why.B: Wrong — 1:9 is the ceiling of conventional sheet meshing.
why.C: Wrong — 1:3 is far below micrografting expansion; sheet meshing tops out near 1:9.
why.D: Wrong — 1:1000 overstates the described micrografting expansion.

stem: Simple hypothermic (refrigerated) storage was originally described as maintaining autologous skin viable for up to how long?

options.A: Up to 14 days
options.B: Up to 24 hours
options.C: Up to 3 months
options.D: Up to 1 year

exp: Early work (Wentscher, then Carrel) showed hypothermic refrigerated storage could maintain autologous skin for up to about 14 days; later studies preserved viability, albeit with diminished engraftment efficacy, for as long as 3–8 weeks, and cryopreservation in liquid nitrogen has been described for longer storage. The distractors are wrong on the same time scale: 24 hours understates it, and 3 months or 1 year exceeds what simple refrigeration achieves. Exact recall separates the adjacent options.
hy: Refrigerated skin storage ≈ up to 14 days (viability to 3–8 wks).

why.A: Correct — refrigerated storage maintains skin for up to ~14 days.
why.B: Wrong — 24 hours understates hypothermic storage capacity.
why.C: Wrong — 3 months exceeds simple refrigeration (approached only with longer/cryo methods).
why.D: Wrong — 1 year is not achievable by simple refrigeration.

stem: Without immunosuppression, what is the ultimate fate of an allogeneic (cadaveric) skin graft placed on an immunocompetent patient?

options.A: Immunologic rejection
options.B: Permanent incorporation as living skin
options.C: Conversion into autologous tissue over months
options.D: Calcification without any immune response

exp: Allogeneic tissues are antigenic and, in the absence of immunosuppression, ultimately elicit an immune response and are rejected; tissue matching can prolong survival but does not prevent the eventual outcome, which is why cadaveric skin serves as a temporary biologic dressing (valuable in acute large burns to reduce mortality and enhance healing) rather than a permanent graft. The distractors describe outcomes that do not occur — allograft skin is neither permanently incorporated, converted to self, nor inertly calcified. The immunologic-rejection endpoint is the key clinical fact.
hy: Unsuppressed allograft skin is ultimately rejected.
src: Neligan I.17 · Allogeneic and nonhuman grafting

why.A: Correct — unmatched/unsuppressed allograft skin is ultimately rejected.
why.B: Wrong — it is not permanently incorporated without immunosuppression.
why.C: Wrong — allograft does not convert into autologous tissue.
why.D: Wrong — the fate is immune rejection, not inert calcification.

stem: The cross-section diagrams two graft depths. Graft (A) captures epidermis plus only partial-thickness dermis, whereas graft (B) captures epidermis plus the full dermis. Which pair is correctly identified?

options.A: A = split-thickness graft; B = full-thickness graft
options.B: A = full-thickness graft; B = split-thickness graft (reversed)
options.C: A = composite graft; B = split-thickness graft
options.D: A = full-thickness graft; B = composite graft

exp: A split-thickness skin graft (A) includes the epidermis and only a partial thickness of dermis, so deeper appendages are largely left in the donor bed (enabling donor re-epithelialization); a full-thickness graft (B) takes the epidermis and the entire dermis, giving better function, sensitivity, and less contraction but requiring a donor site that can be closed primarily. A composite graft would additionally include fat/cartilage, which is not depicted here. All options name real graft types, so reading the harvest depth in the figure is the task.
hy: STSG = epidermis + partial dermis; FTSG = epidermis + full dermis.
imgcap: Cross-section of skin showing two harvest depths

why.A: Correct — partial dermis = STSG (A); full dermis = FTSG (B).
why.B: Wrong — this reverses the two depths.
why.C: Wrong — a composite graft includes fat/cartilage, not shown; A is split-thickness.
