why.D: Wrong — B is full-thickness, not composite (no fat/cartilage layer shown).

stem: What harvesting step is shown — a powered instrument advanced across the anterior thigh at a shallow angle to shave a uniform sheet of skin?

options.A: Split-thickness graft harvest with a powered dermatome
options.B: Full-thickness elliptical graft excision with a scalpel knife
options.C: Tangential (Janžekovič) burn eschar excision
options.D: Meek microdermatome micrografting technique

exp: The image shows split-thickness graft harvest with a powered (electric/air) dermatome advanced across the anterior thigh — the standard donor site — producing a uniform partial-thickness sheet. A full-thickness graft is cut as an ellipse with a knife for primary closure; tangential excision removes burn eschar rather than harvesting donor skin; a Meek microdermatome produces small squares for micrografting. All are real operative steps, so recognizing the powered sheet-harvest device and thigh donor site is the task.
hy: Powered dermatome shaving the thigh = STSG harvest.
imgcap: Powered instrument advanced across the thigh

why.A: Correct — a powered dermatome shaving the thigh is STSG harvest.
why.B: Wrong — FTSG uses knife elliptical excision, not a dermatome sheet.
why.C: Wrong — tangential excision debrides burn eschar, not donor harvest.
why.D: Wrong — a Meek microdermatome makes small squares, not a continuous sheet.

stem: This split-thickness graft has been passed through a device that cut regular rows of staggered slits, letting it expand and drain. What has been done to it?

options.A: It has been meshed
options.B: It has been pie-crusted
options.C: It has been defatted
options.D: It has been tangentially thinned

exp: The regular staggered pattern of slits that lets the sheet stretch is meshing, which expands coverage (templates 1:1 to ~1:9) and allows fluid egress, at the cost of more contracture and a cobblestone final appearance. Pie-crusting makes a few scattered perforations in a non-meshed graft for drainage without expansion; defatting trims fat from a full-thickness graft; tangential thinning describes harvest depth, not a slit pattern. All are real graft manoeuvres, so the systematic slit/expansion pattern identifies meshing.
hy: Regular staggered slits = a meshed graft.
imgcap: Split-thickness graft with regular staggered slits

why.A: Correct — the regular staggered slit pattern is a meshed graft.
why.B: Wrong — pie-crusting is scattered perforations without an expansion pattern.
why.C: Wrong — defatting removes fat from an FTSG, unrelated to slits.
why.D: Wrong — tangential thinning is about depth, not a mesh pattern.

stem: The full-thickness graft is draped over the surgeon's finger and its deep surface is being trimmed with scissors before inset. Which preparatory step is shown?

options.A: Defatting the graft
options.B: Meshing the graft
options.C: Pie-crusting the graft
options.D: Tangential harvest of the graft

exp: Draping a full-thickness graft over a finger and trimming its deep subcutaneous fat with scissors is defatting, done to remove the relatively avascular fat that would impede revascularization and cause fat necrosis, improving take. Meshing cuts expansion slits, pie-crusting makes drainage perforations, and tangential harvest is the shaving technique used to obtain a split-thickness graft — none matches trimming fat off a full-thickness graft. All are genuine steps, so recognizing the fat-trimming manoeuvre is the task.
hy: FTSG trimmed over a finger = defatting.
imgcap: Graft draped over a finger, deep surface being trimmed

why.A: Correct — trimming deep fat off an FTSG over a finger is defatting.
why.B: Wrong — meshing makes expansion slits, not fat trimming.
why.C: Wrong — pie-crusting makes drainage holes, not fat removal.
why.D: Wrong — tangential harvest obtains an STSG; this graft is already harvested.

stem: In this fixation method, long peripheral sutures are tied over a bulky cotton/gauze pad to press a graft into contact with the bed. What is it?

options.A: A tie-over bolster dressing
options.B: A negative-pressure (vacuum-assisted) dressing
options.C: A quilting suture technique
options.D: A splint immobilization

exp: Long sutures placed at the graft periphery and tied over a bulky pad form a tie-over bolster, which presses the graft into the bed (especially in concave or awkward areas) and controls dead space and micromotion. A vacuum-assisted dressing uses continuous suction through foam under an adhesive seal — a different mechanism shown elsewhere in the same figure; quilting sutures tack the graft centrally without a bolster pad; a splint immobilizes an adjacent joint. All are real fixation methods, so the sutures-tied-over-a-pad description identifies the bolster.
hy: Sutures tied over a bulky pad = tie-over bolster.
imgcap: Long peripheral sutures tied over a bulky pad

why.A: Correct — peripheral sutures tied over a pad is a tie-over bolster.
why.B: Wrong — a VAC dressing uses suction through foam, not tied-over sutures.
why.C: Wrong — quilting sutures tack the graft without a bolster pad.
why.D: Wrong — a splint immobilizes a joint, it is not this fixation.

stem: This schematic shows a graft initially nourished by plasma from the wound bed (panel 1) before vessels connect (panel 2) and blood flow is restored (panel 3). Which process is illustrated?

options.A: Revascularization of a skin graft
options.B: The inflammatory phase of wound healing
options.C: Angiosome choke-vessel dilation in a flap
options.D: Lymphatic regeneration after node dissection

exp: The three-panel schematic — plasma imbibition, then vessel connection, then restored perfusion — depicts skin-graft revascularization (take), the process by which a non-vascularized graft becomes perfused via anastomosis, neovascularization, and endothelial ingrowth. It is not the generic inflammatory phase of healing, not flap choke-vessel physiology (which concerns pedicled/free flaps, not free grafts), and not lymphatic regeneration. All options are real vascular/biologic processes, so reading the imbibition→connection→perfusion sequence identifies graft revascularization.
hy: Imbibition→vessel connection→perfusion = graft revascularization.
imgcap: Three-panel graft nutrition and perfusion schematic

why.A: Correct — imbibition→vessel connection→perfusion is graft revascularization.
why.B: Wrong — this depicts graft perfusion, not the inflammatory healing phase.
why.C: Wrong — choke-vessel dilation concerns flaps, not free grafts.
why.D: Wrong — no lymphatic regeneration is shown.

stem: The chapter opens with a surgeon working in India c. 600–1000 BCE who reconstructed noses amputated as punishment for adultery and taught that a student "must prepare a dead body and carefully examine all its parts." Who is described?

options.A: Aulus Cornelius Celsus, the Roman medical encyclopedist
options.B: Gaspare Tagliacozzi, the Bolognese Renaissance surgeon
options.C: Sushruta, working in India roughly 600–1000 BCE
options.D: Sir Harold Gillies, the World-War facial-reconstruction pioneer

exp: Sushruta, working in India c. 600–1000 BCE, is the dawn-of-surgery figure who reconstructed noses amputated as punishment for adultery and taught that anatomical dissection of the dead is prerequisite to safe surgery — the earliest expression of the "understand the science first" ethos the chapter applies to tissue engineering. His forehead-flap nasal reconstruction is the ancestral plastic-surgery operation.
hy: Sushruta (600–1000 BCE, India) = punitive-amputation nasal reconstruction + dissection ethos.

why.A: Wrong — Celsus was a first-century Roman encyclopedist, not the BCE Indian nasal-reconstruction figure described.
why.B: Wrong — Tagliacozzi is the 16th-century Bolognese surgeon who dissected executed prisoners, not the ancient Indian one.
why.C: Correct — Sushruta is the Indian antiquity figure who reconstructed amputated noses and championed cadaveric dissection.
why.D: Wrong — Gillies is a 20th-century wartime facial-reconstruction pioneer, not an ancient surgeon.

stem: "We restore, rebuild and make whole those parts which nature hath given, but which fortune has taken away… that it might buoy up the spirit" — this credo, cited as a summary of plastic surgery's aims, is attributed to whom?

options.A: Gaspare Tagliacozzi, who reconstructed noses injured in duels
options.B: Sushruta, who reconstructed noses amputated as punishment
options.C: Sir Archibald McIndoe, who reconstructed burned RAF airmen
options.D: Joseph Murray, who performed the first human kidney transplant

exp: Gaspare Tagliacozzi, the 16th-century Bolognese surgeon who dissected executed prisoners to learn the anatomy behind his delayed-pedicle nasal reconstructions for duel injuries, authored the famous credo the chapter uses to capture the shared aspiration of plastic surgery and tissue engineering — restoring form *and* spirit.
hy: "We restore, rebuild and make whole…" = Tagliacozzi.

why.A: Correct — the quotation is Tagliacozzi's summary of plastic surgery's aims.
why.B: Wrong — Sushruta's cited line is about dissecting a dead body to learn anatomy, not this restorative credo.
why.C: Wrong — McIndoe is a Guinea-Pig-Club surgeon, not the author of the quote.
why.D: Wrong — Murray is the kidney-transplant Nobel laureate, unrelated to the quotation.

stem: Whose experimental facial reconstructions on badly disfigured RAF airmen earned their patients the nickname "The Guinea Pig Club"?

options.A: Joseph Murray and Harry Buncke, the transplant/replantation pioneers
options.B: Gaspare Tagliacozzi and Sushruta, the historical nasal reconstructors
options.C: G. Ian Taylor and Bernard O'Brien, the Melbourne microsurgeons
options.D: Harold Gillies and Archibald McIndoe, the wartime burn surgeons

exp: Sir Harold Gillies (a New Zealander) and his cousin Sir Archibald McIndoe, aided by Kilner and Mowlem, pushed the experimental limits of facial reconstruction on badly burned airmen, whose willingness to undergo pioneering procedures earned them the "Guinea Pig Club" moniker — the chapter's emblem of surgical brinkmanship driving innovation.
hy: Guinea Pig Club = Gillies + McIndoe (RAF airmen).

why.A: Wrong — Murray (kidney transplant) and Buncke (replantation) are microsurgery-era figures, not the wartime facial pair.
why.B: Wrong — Tagliacozzi and Sushruta are the antiquity/Renaissance figures, centuries earlier.
why.C: Wrong — Taylor (angiosomes) and O'Brien (lymphatic microsurgery) are the Melbourne microsurgeons.
why.D: Correct — Gillies and McIndoe are the Guinea Pig Club surgeons.

stem: The chapter credits the first successful human-to-human kidney transplant (1954), later recognized with the 1990 Nobel Prize, to which surgeon?

options.A: Bernard O'Brien, pioneer of lymphatico-venous anastomosis
options.B: Joseph Murray, working in Boston in 1954
options.C: Harry Buncke, pioneer of experimental replantation
options.D: G. Ian Taylor, who characterized the body's angiosomes

exp: Dr Joseph Murray performed the first successful human-to-human kidney transplant in Boston in 1954 and received the 1990 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine — the chapter's exemplar that plastic-surgical innovation could reshape the whole of medicine.
hy: First human kidney transplant (1954) + Nobel 1990 = Joe Murray.

why.A: Wrong — O'Brien pioneered lymphatic microsurgery, not organ transplantation.
why.B: Correct — Murray did the first human kidney transplant and won the 1990 Nobel.
why.C: Wrong — Buncke is credited with experimental replantation/microsurgery, not the kidney transplant.
why.D: Wrong — Taylor mapped angiosomes and did the first free flap, not the transplant.

stem: Who is credited with the first experimental replantation of a rabbit ear (1964) and the first primate toe-to-hand transfer (1966)?

options.A: Harry Buncke, working in the USA
options.B: G. Ian Taylor, working in Melbourne
options.C: Joseph Murray, working in Boston
options.D: Bernard O'Brien, working in Melbourne

exp: Dr Harry Buncke reported the first experimental replantation — a rabbit ear — in 1964, then the first primate toe-to-hand transfer in 1966 and omentum-to-scalp transfer in 1969, establishing the experimental foundation of clinical microsurgery.
hy: Buncke = rabbit-ear replantation (1964) + primate toe-to-hand (1966).

why.A: Correct — these experimental microsurgical firsts are Buncke's.
why.B: Wrong — Taylor's landmark was the first composite free flap and the angiosome map, not the rabbit-ear work.
why.C: Wrong — Murray's landmark was the kidney transplant.
why.D: Wrong — O'Brien's contributions were in lymphatic microsurgery.

stem: Mapping the body's vascular territories in the Melbourne morgue, which surgeon performed the first composite fasciocutaneous free tissue transfer in 1971 (a groin flap to an open ankle fracture)?

options.A: Bernard O'Brien, who restored lymphatic physiology microsurgically
options.B: Harry Buncke, who replanted a rabbit ear experimentally
options.C: Ralph Millard, who advanced cleft-lip reconstruction principles
options.D: G. Ian Taylor, who then defined the angiosome concept

exp: Professor G. Ian Taylor, mapping the body's vascular territories in the Melbourne morgue, performed the first composite fasciocutaneous free tissue transfer in 1971 (a groin free flap to an open ankle fracture) and articulated the "angiosome" concept — dividing the whole body into vascular territories permitting tissue transfer from almost any donor site.
hy: Taylor = first free flap (1971, groin→ankle) + angiosome concept.

why.A: Wrong — O'Brien, Taylor's Melbourne rival, worked on lymphatic microsurgery, not the first free flap.
why.B: Wrong — Buncke's firsts were experimental replantation, not the free groin flap.
why.C: Wrong — Millard is associated with cleft principles, not the angiosome map or first free flap.
why.D: Correct — Taylor did the first composite free flap and defined angiosomes.

stem: Taylor's cross-town Melbourne rival is credited with microsurgical restoration of lymphatic physiology — lymphatico-venous anastomosis (1976) and later vascularized lymph node transfer. Who was he?

options.A: Robert Acland, of microvascular-instrument fame
options.B: Bernard O'Brien, working in Melbourne
options.C: Joseph Murray, working in Boston
options.D: Harry Buncke, working in the United States

exp: Dr Bernard O'Brien, Taylor's cross-town Melbourne rival, applied microsurgery to the lymphatic system — performing lymphatico-venous anastomosis (1976) and later free vascularized lymph node transfer — extending microsurgery from bulk tissue transfer to physiologic restoration.
hy: O'Brien = lymphatico-venous anastomosis (1976) + vascularized lymph node transfer.

why.A: Wrong — Acland is known for microvascular instruments/anastomosis technique, not the lymphatic firsts named here.
why.B: Correct — O'Brien pioneered lymphatic microsurgery.
why.C: Wrong — Murray did the kidney transplant.
why.D: Wrong — Buncke's firsts were experimental replantation and toe-to-hand, not lymphatic surgery.

stem: Which sequence gives the chapter's chronology of composite tissue allotransplantation milestones correctly?

options.A: First full face transplant 1998 → first hand transplant 2005 → first partial face transplant 2010
options.B: First partial face transplant 1998 → first hand transplant 2005 → first full face transplant 2010
options.C: Hand transplant 1998 → partial face transplant 2005 → full face transplant 2010
options.D: First hand transplant 1998 → first full face transplant 2005 → first partial face transplant 2010

exp: The chapter dates composite tissue allotransplantation milestones as: first hand transplant 1998 (French surgeons), first partial face transplant to a living recipient 2005, and first full face transplant 2010 (Spain) — after which the field's challenge shifted from technique to the judgment of recipient selection.
hy: CTA order: hand 1998 → partial face 2005 → full face 2010.

why.A: Wrong — the full face transplant was 2010, not first in 1998.
why.B: Wrong — the hand (1998), not a partial face, came first.
why.C: Correct — hand 1998, partial face 2005, full face 2010 is the chapter's chronology.
why.D: Wrong — the partial face (2005) preceded the full face (2010); this reverses them.

stem: Y.C. Fung introduced the term "tissue engineering" in 1985, and the 1987–1992 "first biotech boom" that followed was centered where?

options.A: In the state hospital laboratories of Japan
options.B: Around the MIT and Harvard precincts in the United States
options.C: Within the National Health Service institutes of the United Kingdom
options.D: At the O'Brien Institute in Melbourne, Australia

exp: Y.C. Fung coined "tissue engineering" in 1985; the 1987–1992 "first biotech boom" that followed was centered on the MIT–Harvard precincts in the United States, with a heavily academic (≈90%) and privately funded (≈90%) make-up and little of today's regulatory governance.
hy: First biotech boom epicenter = MIT/Harvard, USA.
src: Neligan I.18 · Origin of the term

why.A: Wrong — Japan's early cell work was confined to state hospitals with no start-ups, not a boom epicenter.
why.B: Correct — the MIT/Harvard precincts were the boom's epicenter.
why.C: Wrong — the UK/NHS environment was slower and more conservative, not the epicenter.
why.D: Wrong — the O'Brien Institute was an Australian microsurgery/lymphatic center, not the biotech-boom epicenter.

stem: Langer and Vacanti's landmark definition frames tissue engineering as an interdisciplinary field applying biology and engineering to do what?

options.A: Transfer vascularized 3D blocks of a patient's own tissue between body sites
options.B: Edit the genome of somatic cells to correct inherited developmental disorders
options.C: Deliver systemic drugs that modulate a patient's own regenerative processes
options.D: Build biological substitutes that restore or improve tissue function

exp: Langer and Vacanti defined tissue engineering as an interdisciplinary field applying biology and engineering to develop functional biological substitutes that restore, maintain, or improve function in damaged tissue and diseased organs — the substitute-building aim that distinguishes it from purely pharmacologic or surgical approaches.
hy: Langer–Vacanti: engineered biological substitutes to restore/maintain/improve function.

why.A: Wrong — transferring vascularized autologous tissue blocks is free-flap microsurgery, not tissue engineering.
why.B: Wrong — somatic genome editing describes gene therapy, a tool used within TERM, not the definition.
why.C: Wrong — systemic modulation of a patient's own regenerative processes describes regenerative medicine.
why.D: Correct — this is the Langer–Vacanti definition of tissue engineering.

stem: Within the combined field abbreviated TERM, which statement best distinguishes regenerative medicine from tissue engineering as the chapter frames them today?

options.A: Regenerative medicine assembles cell-seeded scaffolds ex vivo, while tissue engineering delivers systemic promoting drugs
options.B: Regenerative medicine modulates the body's own healing, while tissue engineering delivers structural tissue components
options.C: Regenerative medicine belongs to the surgeon and engineer, while tissue engineering belongs to the physician and cell biologist
options.D: Regenerative medicine builds whole replacement organs, while tissue engineering only accelerates epithelial wound closure

exp: The chapter frames regenerative medicine as harnessing and modulating the body's own pre-existing regenerative processes (often systemically — cell, gene, immune, or nutritional therapy), while tissue engineering focuses on the physical arrangement, delivery, and implantation of structural tissue components, with or without cells; TERM is the integration of the two.
hy: Regenerative medicine = modulate native processes; tissue engineering = build/deliver structural components.

why.A: Wrong — this reverses the two (scaffold assembly is tissue engineering; systemic promotion is regenerative medicine).
why.B: Correct — this matches the chapter's regenerative-medicine-vs-tissue-engineering distinction.
why.C: Wrong — the surgeon/engineer-vs-physician/biologist split is the *older* demarcation the chapter says has blurred.
why.D: Wrong — regenerative medicine is not defined by building whole organs, nor tissue engineering limited to epithelial closure.

stem: Modern tissue-engineering constructs are conceived, per the chapter's schematic, as the integration of which three core elements to yield an engineered tissue?

options.A: A vascular pedicle, a nerve coaptation, and a supporting bony strut
options.B: Layered autograft, allograft, and xenograft in sequence
options.C: Cells, a biomaterial scaffold, and signaling growth factors
options.D: Cultured cells, systemic antibiotics, and a pressure garment

exp: The chapter's schematic conceives engineered tissue as the convergence of cells, a biomaterial scaffold, and signaling growth factors (often delivered via a bioreactor) — the classic tissue-engineering triad; surrounding disciplines (nanotech, genomics, developmental genetics) refine each element.
hy: Tissue-engineering triad = cells + scaffold + signals (growth factors).

why.A: Wrong — pedicle, nerve coaptation, and bony strut describe free-flap/reconstructive components, not the triad.
why.B: Wrong — autograft/allograft/xenograft are graft-source categories, not the cells-scaffold-signals triad.
why.C: Correct — cells, scaffold, and growth-factor signals are the three core inputs.
why.D: Wrong — antibiotics and a pressure garment are perioperative adjuncts, not the engineering triad.

expimgcap: The tissue-engineering triad: cells + biomaterial scaffold + growth factors/bioreactor → engineered tissue

stem: At the turn-of-century crash, the total capital value of tissue-engineering companies collapsed by roughly what magnitude, per the figures cited?

options.A: About 50% — from ~US$2.5 billion in 2000 to ~US$1.25 billion two years later
options.B: About 30% — from ~US$4.5 billion in 2000 to ~US$3 billion two years later
options.C: About 75% — from ~US$300 million in 2000 to ~US$75 million two years later
options.D: About 90% — from ~US$2.5 billion in 2000 to ~US$300 million two years later

exp: The sector's total capital value fell ~90%, from about US$2.5 billion in 2000 to about US$300 million two years later — the "tech bubble"-style correction the chapter uses to frame the collapse of tissue engineering Mark-I.
hy: TE crash ≈ 90% ($2.5B → $300M in 2 yr).

why.A: Wrong — the decline was ~90%, not ~50%; $2.5B did not fall only to $1.25B.
why.B: Wrong — $4.5B was the cumulative R&D invested, not the 2000 market value, and the drop was steeper than 30%.
why.C: Wrong — the starting value was billions, not $300M; $300M was the *end* value.
why.D: Correct — a ~90% drop from ~$2.5B to ~$300M.

stem: By the chapter's accounting at the moment of collapse, which statement about the early tissue-engineering industry is correct?

options.A: Of 20 products in FDA trials, four were approved yet none succeeded commercially
options.B: Of 20 products in FDA trials, the majority reached the market and turned a profit
options.C: Cardiac and neural tissue represented well over half of the sector's research effort
options.D: No tissue-engineering product ever entered an FDA trial before the sector collapsed

exp: Of 20 products that reached FDA trials, six were abandoned or failed and four were eventually approved — yet none achieved commercial success and no viable portfolio emerged, despite >US$4.5 billion invested; skin and cartilage work (not cardiac/neural) accounted for over half the effort.
hy: 20 in FDA trials → 4 approved, none commercially successful.

why.A: Correct — four of twenty were approved but none succeeded commercially.
why.B: Wrong — the products did not reach market profitably; that is the opposite of what happened.
why.C: Wrong — skin and cartilage, not cardiac and neural tissue, comprised >50% of the effort.
why.D: Wrong — twenty products did enter FDA trials before the collapse.

section: The fall of Mark-I — root causes
stem: Which factor is identified as a central, structural reason the first-generation model was commercially unviable?

options.A: A worldwide shortage of surgeons trained to implant the constructs
options.B: Patent law that banned all commercialization of cell products before 2000
options.C: The complete absence of any unmet clinical need for soft-tissue reconstruction
options.D: Reliance on living cells that were costly, scarce, and heavily regulated

exp: The core structural flaw was reliance on living cells — by definition hard to source, costly to maintain, and burdened by regulatory and ethical complexity — which prevented commercial scale, kept costs above the conventional solutions being superseded, and produced a low rate of FDA approval and payer adoption.
hy: Mark-I failed on unscalable, costly, regulated *living-cell* handling.
src: Neligan I.18 · Root causes of collapse

why.A: Wrong — the bottleneck was commercialization and cell biology, not a shortage of implanting surgeons.
why.B: Wrong — no blanket pre-2000 patent prohibition is described; the barriers were cost, scale, and regulation.
why.C: Wrong — an unmet clinical need existed (and paradoxically shrank as microsurgery matured); the problem was viability, not absence of need.
why.D: Correct — the living-cell handling problem is the chapter's central structural cause.

stem: Between 1980 and 2001, the intellectual property filed in early tissue engineering (N = 567) was dominated by which region?

options.A: Europe, holding roughly 71% of all filings
options.B: Japan, holding roughly 71% of all filings
options.C: The United States, with about 71% of filings
options.D: Australia, holding roughly 71% of all filings

exp: Of 567 early tissue-engineering IP filings (1980–2001), the United States held ~71%, with Europe ~18%, Japan ~6%, and others ~5% — reflecting the US (MIT/Harvard) epicenter of the field.
hy: Early TE IP: US ~71% dominant.

why.A: Wrong — Europe held ~18%, not 71%.
why.B: Wrong — Japan held ~6%, not 71%.
why.C: Correct — the US held ~71% of early TE IP.
why.D: Wrong — Australia was a minor contributor, not the 71% leader.

stem: Per Table 18.1, which scaffold characteristic is paired with the rationale "absorption rate compatible with the tissue regeneration rate"?

options.A: Biocompatible — generating an appropriate host immune response
options.B: Structural integrity — strength to tolerate applied physical forces
options.C: Biodegradable — matching resorption to new-tissue formation
options.D: Mechanical resilience — elasticity enduring long-term deformation

exp: In Table 18.1, "Biodegradable" is paired with the rationale that the scaffold's absorption rate must be compatible with the tissue regeneration rate — degrading no faster and no slower than the new tissue forms.
hy: Biodegradable = absorption rate matched to tissue regeneration.

why.A: Wrong — biocompatibility's rationale is generating an appropriate host immune response.
why.B: Wrong — structural integrity's rationale is strength to tolerate physical forces.
why.C: Correct — biodegradability's rationale is absorption matched to regeneration rate.
why.D: Wrong — mechanical resilience's rationale is elasticity to endure deformation long-term.

stem: In Table 18.1, which scaffold property is specifically required so that cells and vasculature can grow *into* the construct?

options.A: Porosity — a porous structure of adequate dimensions for ingress
options.B: Biocompatibility — generation of an appropriate host immune response
options.C: Mechanical resilience — elasticity enduring deformation without compromise
options.D: Structural integrity — strength to tolerate forces and preserve cell integrity

exp: Table 18.1 lists porosity — a porous structure of adequate pore dimensions — as the property that permits cellular and vascular ingress into the scaffold; without it, cells and vessels cannot populate the construct's interior.
hy: Porosity = pathway for cellular + vascular ingress.

why.A: Correct — porosity enables cellular and vascular ingrowth.
why.B: Wrong — biocompatibility governs the host immune response, not physical ingress.
why.C: Wrong — mechanical resilience concerns elastic deformation, not cell entry.
why.D: Wrong — structural integrity concerns load tolerance, not the porous pathway for ingrowth.

stem: The chapter defines a scaffold, in tissue engineering, principally as a material engineered to do what?

options.A: Provide a permanent, non-degradable strut that host tissue never remodels
options.B: Deliver a fixed systemic dose of antifibrotic drug to remodel scar
options.C: Serve as a temporary occlusive dressing that never interacts with host cells
options.D: Create a favorable environment for cells to survive and form tissue

exp: A scaffold is defined as a material engineered to create a favorable environment for cells to survive and form functional tissue — supporting the cells that are the "engine rooms" of tissue growth — and ideally re-creating the tissue-specific microenvironment to regulate long-term cell behavior.
hy: Scaffold = engineered pro-cell environment supporting tissue formation.

why.A: Wrong — an ideal scaffold interacts with and is remodeled by host tissue, not a permanent inert strut.
why.B: Wrong — a fixed systemic antifibrotic dose describes drug therapy, not a scaffold.
why.C: Wrong — a scaffold is not merely an occlusive dressing; it actively supports cellular ingrowth.
why.D: Correct — this is the chapter's definition of a scaffold.

stem: Early "epidermal" substitutes such as cultured epithelial autografts (CEA) were limited in clinical translation chiefly by what problem?

options.A: Fragility of the cultured sheets, underscoring the need for a dermal layer
options.B: A high rate of malignant transformation of the transplanted keratinocytes
options.C: An inability to be grown from the patient's own cells, forcing allograft use
options.D: Immediate hyperacute rejection because the keratinocytes were xenogeneic

exp: Skin substitutes are classified as epidermal or dermal replacements. Early epidermal substitutes — cultured epithelial autografts (CEA), expanded keratinocyte sheets — were limited clinically by the structural fragility of the cultured sheets, which highlighted how essential a dermal-like layer is to replicate skin.
hy: CEA fragility → dermal layer is essential.

why.A: Correct — fragility of the epidermal sheets, and the resulting need for a dermal layer, was the limiting problem.
why.B: Wrong — malignant transformation of the keratinocytes is not the cited limitation.
why.C: Wrong — CEAs are autologous (cultured from the patient), so allograft dependence is not the issue.
why.D: Wrong — autologous keratinocytes are not xenogeneic, so hyperacute rejection does not apply.

stem: Integra's Dermal Regeneration Template (FDA-approved 1996; Yannas and Bourke) is fabricated from which characteristic materials?

options.A: Esterified hyaluronic acid seeded with autologous fibroblasts
options.B: Bovine collagen with shark chondroitin-6-sulfate
options.C: A fully synthetic polyurethane bilayer foam
options.D: Decellularized human dermis retaining a collagen–elastin frame

exp: Integra, one of the first dermal matrices to market (FDA-approved 1996; developed by Yannas and Bourke), is built from bovine collagen and shark chondroitin-6-sulfate under a temporary silicone epidermal layer — the archetypal collagen-based xenogeneic dermal template.
hy: Integra = bovine collagen + shark chondroitin-6-sulfate.

why.A: Wrong — esterified hyaluronic acid seeded with autologous fibroblasts describes Hyalomatrix, not Integra.
why.B: Correct — Integra's matrix is bovine collagen plus shark chondroitin-6-sulfate.
why.C: Wrong — a fully synthetic polyurethane bilayer describes Novosorb BTM.
why.D: Wrong — decellularized cadaveric dermis describes Alloderm/Glyaderm, not Integra.

stem: Across the collagen-based dermal templates (Integra, Matriderm, Pelnac), which single material predominates, and why is it favored?

options.A: Hyaluronic acid — because it is the stiffest available glycosaminoglycan
options.B: Elastin — because it is the most abundant extracellular-matrix protein
options.C: Polyglycolic acid — because it is fully synthetic and non-immunogenic
options.D: Collagen — because it is the most abundant extracellular-matrix protein

exp: Although the collagen-based templates (Integra, Matriderm, Pelnac) vary in co-polymer composition, collagen predominates because it is the most abundant extracellular-matrix protein — the natural structural backbone for a biomimetic dermal scaffold.
hy: Collagen = most abundant ECM protein → predominant scaffold material.

why.A: Wrong — hyaluronic acid is used (e.g. Hyalomatrix) but is not the predominant material or "stiffest GAG" rationale.
why.B: Wrong — collagen, not elastin, is the most abundant ECM protein.
why.C: Wrong — polyglycolic acid is a synthetic scaffold polymer (e.g. lymphatic work), not the predominant dermal-template material.
why.D: Correct — collagen predominates as the most abundant ECM protein.

stem: Alloderm and Glyaderm differ from Integra in that they are produced how?

options.A: As esterified hyaluronic-acid matrices seeded with the patient's fibroblasts
options.B: As decellularized human allografts keeping a collagen–elastin frame
options.C: As bovine-collagen and shark-chondroitin xenografts beneath a silicone layer
options.D: As electrospun synthetic polyurethane foams with super-macroporous chambers

exp: Alloderm and Glyaderm are decellularized human allografts: a de-cellularization process removes donor cells while preserving the native collagen–elastin framework, contrasting with the xenogeneic (bovine/shark) polymers of Integra. Glyaderm additionally uses a three-step process with allograft priming of the wound bed.
hy: Alloderm/Glyaderm = decellularized allograft, collagen–elastin scaffold retained.

why.A: Wrong — esterified HA seeded with fibroblasts is Hyalomatrix, not Alloderm/Glyaderm.
why.B: Correct — both are decellularized allografts retaining a collagen–elastin framework.
why.C: Wrong — bovine-collagen/shark-chondroitin xenograft is Integra.
why.D: Wrong — electrospun synthetic polyurethane foam is Novosorb BTM.

stem: Novosorb Biodegradable Temporizing Matrix (BTM), developed through John Greenwood's work, represents a scaffold paradigm shift because it is:

options.A: A decellularized allogeneic dermis harvested from human cadavers
options.B: A cultured epithelial autograft delivered as a keratinocyte cell spray
options.C: A bovine-collagen hydrogel seeded with autologous fibroblasts and keratinocytes
options.D: A synthetic polyurethane scaffold whose super-macroporosity resists infection

exp: Novosorb BTM (John Greenwood; PolyNovo, Australia) is a fully synthetic polyurethane scaffold whose "super-macroporosity" (large interconnected pores/chambers) and acidic breakdown products (e.g. lactic acid) both drive cellular infiltration and vascularization and create an environment inhospitable to infection — a paradigm shift away from biologically derived scaffolds.
hy: Novosorb BTM = synthetic polyurethane, super-macroporous, infection-resistant.

why.A: Wrong — decellularized cadaveric dermis is Alloderm/Glyaderm.
why.B: Wrong — a keratinocyte cell spray describes RECELL, an epidermal product.
why.C: Wrong — a bovine-collagen hydrogel seeded with autologous cells is Denovoskin.
why.D: Correct — BTM is the synthetic polyurethane scaffold with super-macroporosity.

stem: When synthetic polyurethane BTM (Novosorb) was compared with collagen-based Integra in a mouse model, what was observed about integration?

options.A: The synthetic scaffold gave greater infiltration, vascularization, and larger vessels
options.B: The collagen scaffold produced far faster vascularization and larger vessels than the synthetic
options.C: Neither scaffold supported any measurable cellular infiltration or new vessel growth
options.D: The synthetic scaffold provoked hyperacute rejection and was extruded within a day

exp: In a mouse comparison, the synthetic polyurethane BTM provoked an augmented inflammatory response that drove *greater* cellular infiltration and vascularization than the collagen-based Integra, and produced a significantly larger average neo-dermal vessel diameter — the counterintuitive finding underpinning BTM's reliable integration.
hy: BTM > Integra for infiltration/vascularization; larger vessels.

why.A: Correct — the synthetic scaffold outperformed collagen on infiltration, vascularization, and vessel diameter.
why.B: Wrong — this reverses the result; the synthetic (not collagen) scaffold gave larger vessels and more vascularization.
why.C: Wrong — both scaffolds supported cellular infiltration; the point was a difference in degree.
why.D: Wrong — no hyperacute rejection/extrusion occurred; integration was reliable.

stem: Denovoskin (Cutiss), a dermo-epidermal skin substitute that completed phase I trials, is built from what, and produced over roughly what timeframe?

options.A: A cell-free synthetic polyurethane foam, ready within about 24 hours
options.B: Decellularized cadaveric dermis with no seeded cells, ready after 6 months
options.C: A bovine collagen hydrogel with autologous skin cells, over 3–4 weeks
options.D: A shark-chondroitin and bovine-collagen xenograft under silicone, within 48 hours

exp: Denovoskin (Cutiss, Zurich) is a bovine collagen hydrogel seeded with the patient's own dermal fibroblasts and keratinocytes, expanded into graftable stable skin within the 3–4 week neodermis-establishment window; its first compassionate use (2021) helped close a 95% TBSA burn in a 14-year-old alongside split-thickness autograft and CEA.
hy: Denovoskin = bovine-collagen hydrogel + autologous fibroblasts/keratinocytes, 3–4 wk.

why.A: Wrong — a cell-free synthetic foam ready in 24 h describes Novosorb BTM, not Denovoskin.
why.B: Wrong — Denovoskin is seeded with cells, not an acellular cadaveric dermis.
why.C: Correct — autologous fibroblasts/keratinocytes in a bovine-collagen hydrogel, ready in 3–4 weeks.
why.D: Wrong — a shark-chondroitin/bovine-collagen xenograft under silicone is Integra.

stem: Better antifibrotic delivery matters partly because hypertrophic scarring still complicates what proportion of burn survivors, per the chapter?

options.A: Roughly 5–10% of burn survivors
options.B: Roughly 30–70% of burn survivors
options.C: Roughly 90–100% of burn survivors
options.D: Roughly 1–3% of burn survivors

exp: Hypertrophic scarring still complicates 30–70% of burn survivors, which is why engineering precise antifibrotic delivery is a live problem; current management spans non-operative adjuncts (pressure garments, silicone), procedures (steroid injection, laser, micro-needling), and surgical revision.
hy: HTS after burns = 30–70%.

why.A: Wrong — 5–10% understates the cited burn-scar burden.
why.B: Correct — the chapter gives a 30–70% incidence in burn survivors.
why.C: Wrong — 90–100% overstates it; not every burn survivor develops HTS.
why.D: Wrong — 1–3% is far below the cited figure.

stem: Which intervention is described as a current mainstay of antifibrotic therapy for hypertrophic scar, reducing height, itch, and pain while increasing pliability?

options.A: Intradermal Flightless-I-neutralizing antibody injection
options.B: Nefopam acting on the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway
options.C: Intralesional corticosteroid (triamcinolone) injection
options.D: Infusion of a cryptic laminin-β1 antifibrotic fragment

exp: Intralesional corticosteroid (triamcinolone) injection is described as a current mainstay of antifibrotic therapy, reducing hypertrophic-scar height, itch, and pain while increasing pliability — the benchmark against which newer molecular therapies are measured.
hy: Triamcinolone = mainstay antifibrotic (↓height/itch/pain, ↑pliability).

why.A: Wrong — Flii-neutralizing antibody is an experimental novel target, not the established mainstay.
why.B: Wrong — nefopam is an emerging Wnt/β-catenin-targeting therapy, not the mainstay.
why.C: Correct — triamcinolone injection is the cited mainstay.
why.D: Wrong — the cryptic laminin-β1 fragment is an experimental antifibrotic, not standard care.

stem: Flightless I (Flii), studied as a novel antifibrotic target, is best described as:

options.A: A corticosteroid pro-drug that concentrates in scar and raises its pliability
options.B: A hyaluronic-acid dissolving microneedle loaded with steroid for delivery
options.C: A VEGF isoform that drives lymphatic endothelial cell migration and leakiness
options.D: A gelsolin-family actin-remodeling protein raised in poor wound healing

exp: Flightless I (Flii) is a gelsolin-family actin-remodeling protein overexpressed in states of poor wound healing and fibrosis; intradermal Flii-neutralizing antibodies reduced scarring in a murine model by modulating the TGF-β1 pathway, lowering myofibroblast activity and improving collagen deposition.
hy: Flii = gelsolin-family actin remodeler; anti-Flii ↓scar via TGF-β1.

why.A: Wrong — Flii is not a corticosteroid pro-drug; triamcinolone is the steroid mainstay.
why.B: Wrong — a steroid-loaded dissolving HA microneedle is a delivery device, not the Flii protein.
why.C: Wrong — a VEGF isoform driving LEC migration is VEGF-C, a lymphatic molecule, not Flii.
why.D: Correct — Flii is a gelsolin-family actin-remodeling protein and an anti-fibrotic antibody target.

stem: Engineered microneedle arrays for intradermal delivery generally fall within which length range and structural categories?

options.A: About 2–5 mm long; only solid stainless-steel types
options.B: About 200–900 μm; solid, hollow, dissolving, hydrogel
options.C: About 10–50 μm long; only dissolving sugar types
options.D: About 5–10 mm long; solid, hollow, and balloon-tipped types

exp: Engineered microneedle arrays generally range from 200 to 900 μm in length and fall into four structural categories — solid, hollow, dissolving, and hydrogel — enabling painless, reproducible intradermal delivery of vaccines, insulin, immunotherapies, and antifibrotics.
hy: Microneedles = 200–900 μm; solid/hollow/dissolving/hydrogel.

why.A: Wrong — 2–5 mm is far too long and omits three of the four categories.
why.B: Correct — 200–900 μm with solid/hollow/dissolving/hydrogel categories.
why.C: Wrong — 10–50 μm is far too short to reach the intradermal space reliably.
why.D: Wrong — 5–10 mm exceeds the cited range and the "balloon-tipped" category is invented.

stem: VEGF-C recurs in lymphatic tissue engineering as a double-edged molecule. Which pairing captures its role as described?

options.A: A structural collagen crosslinker that stiffens scaffolds but blocks cell adhesion
options.B: An immunosuppressant that prevents allograft rejection but halts angiogenesis
options.C: Essential for keratinocyte differentiation, yet toxic to dermal fibroblasts at dose
options.D: Essential for lymphatic endothelial growth, yet a cause of vessel leakiness

exp: VEGF-C is a key molecule for lymphatic endothelial cell proliferation and migration (central to lymphangiogenesis in embryogenesis and to re-generating lymphatics), yet it also contributes to lymphatic leakiness and, when added to scaffolds like BioBridge, produced vessel disorganization without functional drainage gain.
hy: VEGF-C: drives LEC growth *and* causes leakiness.

why.A: Wrong — a collagen crosslinker (e.g. genipin) stiffens scaffolds; that is not VEGF-C.
why.B: Wrong — VEGF-C is not an immunosuppressant; it is a lymphangiogenic growth factor.
why.C: Wrong — VEGF-C acts on lymphatic endothelium, not keratinocyte differentiation.
why.D: Correct — VEGF-C both promotes LEC proliferation/migration and causes leakiness/disorganization.

stem: In Marino and colleagues' prevascularized dermo-epidermal grafts, lymphatic-capillary formation specifically required what, beyond VEGF-C?

options.A: Complete removal of every blood endothelial cell from the co-culture
options.B: A fully synthetic polyurethane scaffold in place of a hydrogel
options.C: Fibroblasts present, without which capillaries failed to form
options.D: Systemic corticosteroid to suppress the host immune response

exp: In Marino and colleagues' prevascularized dermo-epidermal grafts, bioengineered lymphatic-capillary formation required the presence of fibroblasts: capillaries failed to form in their absence even when VEGF-C was supplied, highlighting the necessity of endothelial–stromal cell interaction in lymphatic engineering.
hy: Lymphatic capillary formation needs fibroblasts, not VEGF-C alone.

why.A: Wrong — the finding was about needing fibroblasts, not removing blood endothelial cells (which co-existed in the matrix).
why.B: Wrong — the constructs used collagen/fibrin hydrogels, not a synthetic polyurethane scaffold.
why.C: Correct — fibroblasts were required; VEGF-C alone was insufficient.
why.D: Wrong — systemic corticosteroid was not the requirement described.

stem: In designing lymphatic constructs, the chapter contrasts capillary and collecting lymphatics. Which description of the *collecting* lymphatics is correct?

options.A: Blind-ended, superficial, single-LEC vessels with scant basement membrane
options.B: The secondary lymphoid organs where antigen-presenting and immune cells meet
options.C: Non-endothelialized interstitial clefts that have no defined vessel wall
options.D: Vessels with a definitive wall and intraluminal anti-backflow valves

exp: Collecting lymphatics are structurally developed vessels with a definitive basement membrane and mural architecture and contain intraluminal valves that prevent backflow into the capillaries and interstitium — distinguishing them from the blind-ended, single-LEC-layer capillary lymphatics with scant basement membrane.
hy: Collecting lymphatics = valves + definitive wall.

why.A: Wrong — blind-ended, superficial, single-LEC, scant-BM describes the *capillary* lymphatics.
why.B: Wrong — secondary lymphoid organs (nodes/spleen) are a separate category, not the collecting vessels.
why.C: Wrong — collecting lymphatics are endothelialized vessels with a defined wall, not non-endothelialized clefts.
why.D: Correct — collecting lymphatics have valves and a developed wall/basement membrane.

stem: Tissue engineering is imagined as an aspirational new rung on the classic reconstructive ladder. Which option lists genuine rungs in the correct simple-to-complex direction?

options.A: Free tissue transfer → pedicled flap → skin grafts → primary intention
options.B: Tissue expansion → secondary intention → free tissue transfer → skin grafts
options.C: Secondary intention → skin grafts → local flap → free tissue transfer
options.D: Skin grafts → primary intention → secondary intention → local flap

exp: The reconstructive ladder runs from simple to complex — healing by secondary intention, primary intention, delayed primary closure, skin grafts (split- and full-thickness), tissue expansion, local flap, pedicled flap, local tissue transfer, and free tissue transfer — with tissue engineering imagined as an aspirational rung above the top. Only the third option ascends in the correct direction.
hy: Reconstructive ladder ascends secondary intention → grafts → local flap → free flap.

why.A: Wrong — this lists the rungs in reverse (complex-to-simple) order.
why.B: Wrong — the order is scrambled (tissue expansion does not precede secondary intention; free flap is not third).
why.C: Correct — secondary intention → skin grafts → local flap → free tissue transfer ascends simple-to-complex.
why.D: Wrong — the order is scrambled (primary/secondary intention sit below skin grafts, not above them).

expimgcap: The reconstructive ladder, simple (bottom) to complex (top)

stem: A trainee is shown this widely-published image from the early history of tissue engineering. The construct pictured is popularly known as:

options.A: Dolly the sheep
options.B: The Vacanti mouse
options.C: The Oncomouse (Harvard mouse)
options.D: CC, the first cloned cat

exp: This is the "Vacanti mouse" (1997): a cartilaginous, human-ear-shaped (bovine cartilage) scaffold grown beneath the dorsal skin of an immunodeficient (nude) mouse by Charles and Joseph Vacanti. It became a global media icon of tissue engineering's promise — and, the chapter notes, later quietly involuted into an amorphous ball of scar, symbolizing the field's over-promise.
hy: Vacanti mouse = ear-shaped cartilage scaffold on a nude mouse (TE icon).
imgcap: What is this construct?

why.A: Wrong — Dolly was the first mammal cloned from an adult somatic cell (a sheep), not an ear-scaffold mouse.
why.B: Correct — the pictured ear-on-mouse construct is the Vacanti mouse.
why.C: Wrong — the Oncomouse is a genetically engineered cancer-prone mouse (a patent landmark), not a cartilage-scaffold construct.
why.D: Wrong — CC ("CopyCat") was the first cloned domestic cat, unrelated to tissue-engineered cartilage.

stem: The colored whole-body map shown here divides the body into discrete surface/muscle territories. Which anatomical concept, central to flap design, does it illustrate?

options.A: Dermatomes (spinal sensory territories)
options.B: Lymphosomes (lymphatic drainage territories)
options.C: Angiosomes (vascular territories)
options.D: Mathes–Nahai muscle circulation patterns

exp: The colored whole-body map depicts angiosomes — the discrete three-dimensional vascular territories supplied by a source artery and its accompanying vein, characterized by G. Ian Taylor. Angiosomes underpin flap design by defining which block of skin, fascia, and muscle can be reliably transferred on a given pedicle.
hy: Angiosome = vascular territory of a source artery/vein (Taylor).
imgcap: Which concept is mapped?

why.A: Wrong — dermatomes are areas of skin innervated by a single spinal nerve root, a sensory map, not a vascular one.
why.B: Wrong — lymphosomes are lymphatic drainage territories, a different (lymphatic) mapping concept.
why.C: Correct — the map shows the body's vascular (angiosome) territories.
why.D: Wrong — Mathes–Nahai classifies a muscle's vascular-pedicle pattern (I–V), not the whole-body surface map shown.

stem: The chapter opens by contrasting the three histologic cartilage types. Which type composes the auricle (external ear) and gives it the flexibility exploited in ear-framework grafting?

options.A: Elastic cartilage
options.B: Calcified cartilage
options.C: Fibrocartilage
options.D: Hyaline cartilage

exp: The three histologic cartilages are hyaline (articular surfaces, costal cartilage, nasal septum), fibrocartilage (intervertebral discs, menisci, TMJ), and **elastic cartilage** (auricle, epiglottis) — whose elastin-rich matrix gives the external ear its springy flexibility, the very property that lets an auricular framework bend without fracturing.
hy: Auricle = elastic cartilage (elastin → flexibility).
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Correct — the auricle is elastic cartilage, whose elastin fibers confer flexibility.
why.B: Wrong — "calcified cartilage" is a degenerative/aged state, not one of the three primary histologic types of the ear.
why.C: Wrong — fibrocartilage forms load-bearing shock absorbers (discs, menisci, TMJ), not the elastic auricle.
why.D: Wrong — hyaline cartilage forms articular surfaces, costal cartilage, and septum, but is less elastic than auricular cartilage.

stem: Harvesting the *entire* conchal cartilage without a preserving strategy characteristically produces which donor-ear deformity?

options.A: An overprojected, cup-shaped ear deformity
options.B: A collapsed conchal bowl, short ear
options.C: A constricted, cup-shaped (lop) helical rim
options.D: A prominent, laterally set antihelix fold

exp: Removing the whole concha without leaving supporting struts collapses the conchal bowl (cymba and cavum concha) and shortens the ear horizontally, because the concha is the structural floor of the auricle. Han's strut-preserving harvest was devised specifically to avoid this.
hy: Whole-concha harvest → collapsed conchal bowl + short ear.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — overprojection/cupping is the *composite-graft donor* problem (helical base), not whole-concha harvest.
why.B: Correct — collapse of the conchal bowl with a horizontally short ear is the described deformity.
why.C: Wrong — a constricted/lop helix is a congenital auricular deformity, not this harvest complication.
why.D: Wrong — a prominent antihelix is unrelated; conchal over-harvest collapses, not projects, the ear.

stem: In Han and colleagues' technique for maximal conchal cartilage harvest, which structures must be left behind to prevent collapse of the donor ear?

options.A: A 1 cm cuff of cavum concha along the meatus
options.B: The whole cymba concha with a 3 mm scapha margin
options.C: ≥5 mm helical crus + 2 mm conchal rim
options.D: The entire antihelix and its superior crus

exp: Han and colleagues harvest the cymba and cavum concha separately but leave **≥5 mm of the helical crus** (with a lateral extension acting as a strut) and a **~2 mm outer rim** along the conchal wall, plus a tie-over bolster as a mould — preserving the bowl's contour while still taking a large graft.
hy: Han harvest: keep ≥5 mm helical crus + ~2 mm outer conchal rim.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — no 1 cm meatal cuff is specified; the key struts are the crus and outer rim.
why.B: Wrong — the cymba concha is largely *harvested*, not left as a 3 mm scapha margin.
why.C: Correct — ≥5 mm helical crus plus a ~2 mm outer conchal rim are preserved.
why.D: Wrong — the antihelix/superior crus is not the structure Han preserves for conchal support.

stem: Beyond ear-framework use, single-layered conchal cartilage grafts are described for reconstruction at which set of sites?

options.A: Tracheal, laryngeal, and pharyngeal reconstruction
options.B: Digital joint, phalanx, and wrist reconstruction
options.C: Orbital floor, malar, and mandibular reconstruction
options.D: Nasal, tarsal (eyelid), and nipple reconstruction

exp: Single-layered conchal cartilage grafts are versatile thin grafts used for **nasal, tarsal (eyelid), and nipple** reconstruction, in addition to serving as ear-framework material and laminated grafts for saddle-nose correction.
hy: Conchal single-layer graft → nasal, tarsal, nipple.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — tracheal/laryngeal/pharyngeal reconstruction typically use costal cartilage, not thin conchal grafts.
why.B: Wrong — digital-joint/phalangeal reconstruction is an engineering/osteochondral target, not a conchal-graft use.
why.C: Wrong — orbital floor/malar/mandibular reconstruction uses bone or alloplast, not conchal cartilage.
why.D: Correct — nasal, tarsal, and nipple reconstruction are the cited applications.

stem: What chiefly limits the usable size of a *free* (non-vascularized) auricular composite chondrocutaneous graft?

options.A: No immediate blood supply after transfer
options.B: The risk of hyperacute rejection of the cartilage
options.C: Donor-site pneumothorax during graft elevation
options.D: The tendency of the graft to calcify early

exp: A free composite chondrocutaneous graft has **no immediate blood supply**, so it survives only up to the size that can be revascularized by the recipient bed — limiting free composite grafts to small defects. Microvascular (vascularized) transfer removes this ceiling, allowing large nasal reconstructions.
hy: Free composite graft size limited by lack of immediate blood supply.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Correct — the absence of immediate perfusion caps free-composite-graft size.
why.B: Wrong — these are autologous grafts, so immune rejection is not the limiting issue.
why.C: Wrong — pneumothorax is a *costal*-harvest risk, not a limit on auricular composite grafts.
why.D: Wrong — early calcification is not the described size limitation.

stem: In Singh and Bartlett's scheme, the largest composite grafts (>1.5 cm from the helical base) require which added maneuver to prevent cupping/overprojection of the donor ear?

options.A: Primary closure with the dog-ear into the hairline
options.B: A V-wedge with half-star cartilage excision
options.C: A full-thickness skin graft applied to the scapha
options.D: A simple helical rim advancement, done alone

exp: For grafts wider than 1.5 cm from the helical base, a simple helical advancement distorts and overprojects the ear; Singh and Bartlett instead take a **V-shaped skin wedge with a "half-star" cartilage excision at its apex and add a scaphomastoid suture** (plus a postauricular incision) to prevent cupping.
hy: >1.5 cm graft → V-wedge + half-star cartilage + scaphomastoid suture.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — running the dog-ear into the hairline is the small-graft (Technique 1) closure.
why.B: Correct — V-wedge skin + half-star cartilage excision + scaphomastoid suture is Technique 5.
why.C: Wrong — a scapha skin graft is not the described maneuver for large helical-base grafts.
why.D: Wrong — a helical advancement alone causes the very overprojection this technique avoids.

stem: Zhang and colleagues reconstructed large nasal defects with vascularized preauricular and helical-rim flaps. On which vascular pedicle are these flaps based, and what survival did they report?

options.A: The facial artery; about 70% flap survival
options.B: The occipital artery; about 90% flap survival
options.C: Superficial temporal vessels; ~97% survival
options.D: Posterior auricular artery; ~80% survival

exp: Zhang and colleagues based vascularized preauricular and helical-rim flaps on the **superficial temporal vessels** and reported a **~97%** total flap survival across 63 nasal-defect reconstructions — a reliable option where free composite grafts would be too large to survive.
hy: Vascularized preauricular/helical-rim flap = superficial temporal vessels, ~97% survival.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — the facial artery is not the basis of these preauricular/helical-rim flaps.
why.B: Wrong — the occipital artery supplies posterior scalp, not this flap.
why.C: Correct — superficial temporal pedicle with ~97% survival.
why.D: Wrong — the posterior auricular artery is not the cited pedicle, and survival was ~97%, not ~80%.

stem: When harvesting septal cartilage, preserving an "L-shaped" strut is essential chiefly to accomplish what?

options.A: Preserve the olfactory mucosa and smell
options.B: Protect the anterior ethmoidal bundle
options.C: Keep the nasolacrimal duct patent and open
options.D: Maintain nasal support, prevent collapse

exp: The septal L-strut — a caudal plus dorsal band of quadrangular cartilage — is the keystone of nasal support; preserving it maintains dorsal and tip support and prevents saddling/collapse after harvest. Its required width varies with septal thickness and the surrounding nasal framework.
hy: L-strut preserved → nasal dorsal/tip support, prevents collapse.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — olfaction depends on olfactory epithelium high in the vault, not the cartilage strut.
why.B: Wrong — the anterior ethmoidal bundle is protected by careful dissection, not by the L-strut per se.
why.C: Wrong — the nasolacrimal duct is lateral, unrelated to the septal strut.
why.D: Correct — the L-strut maintains support and prevents collapse.

stem: Per the described technique, septal cartilage is accessed through which incision, with which part free to harvest once an adequate L-strut is preserved?

options.A: Hemitransfixion incision; posterior quadrangular cartilage free
options.B: External columellar incision; only the caudal strut is free
options.C: Intercartilaginous incision; only the ethmoid perpendicular plate
options.D: Killian incision; the whole quadrangular plate with caudal margin

exp: Septal cartilage is reached through a **hemitransfixion incision** with dissection around the caudal margin of the quadrangular cartilage and elevation of both mucoperichondrial flaps; once an adequate L-strut is left, the **more posterior/dorsal quadrangular (nonsupportive) cartilage is free to harvest**.
hy: Hemitransfixion incision; harvest posterior quadrangular cartilage, keep L-strut.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Correct — hemitransfixion access; the posterior nonsupportive quadrangular cartilage is harvestable.
why.B: Wrong — an external columellar approach and "only the caudal strut" misstates both access and what is preserved.
why.C: Wrong — the perpendicular plate of the ethmoid is bony, not the primary quadrangular graft source described.
why.D: Wrong — harvesting the caudal margin would sacrifice the L-strut and cause collapse.

stem: The nasal-septal chondromucosal graft for eyelid (posterior lamella) reconstruction was first published in 1962 by which surgeon?

options.A: Converse
options.B: Millard
options.C: Mustardé
options.D: Tessier

exp: **Millard** published the nasal-septal chondromucosal graft for eyelid (posterior-lamella) reconstruction in 1962; Mustardé later described the technique as well. The graft supplies both lining (mucosa) and structural support (cartilage) in one.
hy: Millard 1962 = septal chondromucosal graft for eyelid.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — Converse is associated with the auricular "tunnel procedure," not this 1962 septal graft.
why.B: Correct — Millard's 1962 report is the cited first description.
why.C: Wrong — Mustardé described it later, not first in 1962.
why.D: Wrong — Tessier (1979) is credited with the *upper lateral* cartilage chondromucosal graft, a different donor.

stem: Scuderi's pedicled nasal chondromucosal flap for upper-eyelid reconstruction harvests the upper lateral cartilage on a pedicle from which artery?

options.A: A terminal branch of the infraorbital artery
options.B: A terminal branch of the supratrochlear artery
options.C: A terminal branch of the dorsal nasal artery
options.D: A terminal branch of the angular artery

exp: Scuderi's pedicled nasal chondromucosal flap for total/subtotal upper-eyelid reconstruction is raised on the **terminal branch of the dorsal nasal artery** and carries the cranial portion of the upper lateral cartilage; it was viable in all 15 reported patients as a one-stage lining-plus-support reconstruction.
hy: Scuderi flap = dorsal nasal artery terminal branch + upper lateral cartilage.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — the infraorbital artery supplies the midface, not this nasal-dorsum flap.
why.B: Wrong — the supratrochlear artery bases forehead flaps, not the nasal chondromucosal flap.
why.C: Correct — the flap is based on the dorsal nasal artery's terminal branch.
why.D: Wrong — the angular artery is nearby but not the named pedicle for this flap.

stem: Costal (rib) cartilage is described as the best donor site for cartilage grafting primarily for which reason?

options.A: It uniquely resists calcification and warping
options.B: It can be harvested with no donor-site morbidity
options.C: It carries an axial blood supply for perfusion
options.D: It offers the most tissue volume and strength

exp: Costal cartilage offers the **greatest tissue volume and mechanical strength** of any cartilage donor and can be carved into any shape while retaining form and bulk — which is why it is the workhorse for total ear frameworks and major dorsal/structural grafts.
hy: Costal cartilage = most volume + strongest.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — costal cartilage can in fact warp and calcify with age; that is not its selling point.
why.B: Wrong — it carries real donor morbidity (pain, pneumothorax), so "no morbidity" is false.
why.C: Wrong — it is a non-vascularized graft with no intrinsic axial blood supply.
why.D: Correct — volume and mechanical strength are its defining advantages.

stem: In the classic Tanzer/Brent approach to building an auricular framework, which costal cartilages are harvested?

options.A: The 6th/7th synchondrosis plus 8th cartilage
options.B: The 4th and 5th cartilages with the manubrium
options.C: The floating 11th and 12th ribs only, harvested
options.D: The 9th and 10th cartilages with the costal arch

exp: The classic Tanzer/Thomson/Brent framework is carved from the **synchondrosis of the 6th and 7th costal cartilages together with the 8th costal cartilage** (usually with perichondrium) — providing a single strong block plus a helical rim.
hy: Classic ear framework = 6th/7th synchondrosis + 8th cartilage.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Correct — the 6th–7th synchondrosis plus the 8th cartilage is the classic harvest.
why.B: Wrong — the 4th/5th and manubrium are not the described auricular-framework donors.
why.C: Wrong — the floating 11th/12th ribs are too small/weak for an ear framework.
why.D: Wrong — the 9th/10th cartilages are not the cited framework source.

stem: Nagata's method for total auricular reconstruction requires harvesting how much costal cartilage across its two stages?

options.A: All six true-rib cartilages in a single stage
options.B: Four then one or two, across two stages
options.C: A single costal cartilage at each of two stages
options.D: Two at the first stage and four at the second

exp: Nagata's two-stage total reconstruction harvests **four costal cartilages at the first stage** (to build the framework and tragus) and **one or two more at the second stage** (for elevation) — a larger cartilage demand that raises the risk of chest-wall deformity and pneumothorax.
hy: Nagata = 4 cartilages (stage 1) + 1–2 (stage 2).
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — Nagata is explicitly two-staged, not a single-stage six-cartilage harvest.
why.B: Correct — four at the first stage and one or two at the second.
why.C: Wrong — a single cartilage per stage understates Nagata's larger demand.
why.D: Wrong — the counts are reversed; the bulk (four) is taken at the first stage.

stem: In Uppal and colleagues' study of costal-cartilage donor morbidity, what were the commonest complaints and their typical course?

options.A: Chronic pleuritic pain worsening over the first year
options.B: Immediate flail-chest needing operative fixation
options.C: Chest-wall pain and clicking, easing by ~3 months
options.D: Permanent intercostal neuralgia in most at one year

exp: In Uppal's 42-patient series the commonest complaints were **chest-wall pain and clicking**, which peaked in the first postoperative week and gradually settled over about three months; scar and chest-wall deformity were the other issues.
hy: Costal donor morbidity = pain + clicking, peak wk 1, ease ~3 mo.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — the pain eased over months rather than steadily worsening.
why.B: Wrong — flail chest requiring fixation is not a described outcome.
why.C: Correct — pain and clicking peaking early and resolving over ~3 months.
why.D: Wrong — persistent neuralgia in the majority at a year is not what was reported.

stem: Which intraoperative complication is singled out as the most serious hazard of costal cartilage harvest, especially with Nagata's cartilage-hungry method?

options.A: Cardiac tamponade
options.B: Hemothorax from the internal mammary artery
options.C: Diaphragmatic rupture
options.D: Pneumothorax

exp: **Pneumothorax** — from inadvertent pleural puncture during dissection — is the most serious intraoperative hazard of costal harvest, together with resulting chest-wall contour deformity, and is more likely with Nagata's cartilage-hungry technique.
hy: Most serious costal-harvest risk = pneumothorax.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — cardiac tamponade is not a described costal-harvest risk.
why.B: Wrong — internal-mammary hemothorax is not the emphasized hazard.
why.C: Wrong — diaphragmatic rupture is not a reported complication of this harvest.
why.D: Correct — pneumothorax is the singled-out serious complication.

stem: Kawanabe and Nagata's modified costal harvest reduced infection and pneumothorax to under 1% and eliminated chest-wall deformity mainly by doing what?

options.A: Leaving perichondrium intact; returning blocks
options.B: Harvesting only floating ribs to avoid the pleura
options.C: Replacing cartilage with a porous-polyethylene frame
options.D: Using thoracoscopic guidance for every harvest

exp: Kawanabe and Nagata harvest the costal cartilage **en bloc while leaving the perichondrium completely intact**, then cut the leftover cartilage into small blocks and **return them into the closed perichondrial pocket** as spacers — dropping infection and pneumothorax to <1% and eliminating chest-wall deformity in 270 cases.
hy: Kawanabe–Nagata: intact perichondrium + returned cartilage blocks → <1% complications.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Correct — perichondrium left intact with cartilage blocks returned is the modification.
why.B: Wrong — the true ribs (6th–8th), not floating ribs, are used; the pleura is protected by perichondrium, not avoided.
why.C: Wrong — no synthetic porous-polyethylene substitution is described here.
why.D: Wrong — routine thoracoscopy is not part of the modified harvest.

stem: Leaving the donor perichondrium intact during costal harvest is said to give two benefits. Which pairing is correct?

options.A: It sterilizes the field, removing the need for antibiotics
options.B: It avoids pleural injury and aids cartilage regeneration
options.C: It abolishes postoperative pain and prevents scarring
options.D: It gives immediate vascular inflow, preventing resorption

exp: The retained perichondrium serves two purposes: it **shields the pleura (avoiding pneumothorax)** and, because it harbors chondrogenic (perichondrial) stem cells, it **promotes donor-site cartilage regeneration**, ultimately allowing a second harvest.
hy: Intact perichondrium = pleural protection + chondrogenic regeneration.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — it is not an antibiotic substitute.
why.B: Correct — pleural protection plus regeneration via chondrogenic stem cells.
why.C: Wrong — it does not abolish pain or prevent all scarring.
why.D: Wrong — perichondrium does not supply immediate vascular inflow to the graft.

stem: After perichondrium-preserving costal harvest, what does the reported histologic follow-up show about donor-site regeneration?

options.A: Only fibrous scar, with no cartilage ever reforming
options.B: Complete ossification into mature bone by 3 months
options.C: Mixed at ~6 mo, hyaline by ~12 mo, re-harvestable
options.D: Full hyaline regeneration within 2 weeks, re-harvestable

exp: Follow-up histology showed regenerated donor cartilage that was **mixed hyaline and fibrocartilage with visible margins at 6 months**, maturing to **homogeneous hyaline cartilage by 12 months** with hardness resembling native costal cartilage — enough to permit a **second harvest**.
hy: Costal regen: mixed at 6 mo → hyaline at 12 mo → re-harvestable.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — cartilage did reform; it was not merely fibrous scar.
why.B: Wrong — the donor bed regenerated cartilage, not bone.
why.C: Correct — mixed at ~6 months, homogeneous hyaline by ~12 months, re-harvestable.
why.D: Wrong — regeneration takes months, not 2 weeks.

stem: For postoperative analgesia after costal harvest, the described intercostal block uses which agent and volume per rib?

options.A: Lidocaine 2%, 20 mL per rib block
options.B: Ropivacaine 0.5%, 15 mL per rib block
options.C: Procaine 1%, 10 mL per rib block
options.D: 0.25% bupivacaine, 5 mL per rib

exp: The described intercostal block uses **0.25% bupivacaine, 5 mL per rib**, given under direct vision to reduce postoperative pain after costal harvest.
hy: Intercostal block = 0.25% bupivacaine, 5 mL/rib.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — 2% lidocaine 20 mL/rib is far too high a dose and wrong agent.
why.B: Wrong — 0.5% ropivacaine 15 mL/rib is not the specified regimen.
why.C: Wrong — 1% procaine 10 mL/rib is not what was described.
why.D: Correct — 0.25% bupivacaine at 5 mL per rib.

stem: Rib cartilage graft is listed as a treatment for maxillonasal dysplasia, which is better known by which eponym?

options.A: Binder's syndrome
options.B: Treacher Collins syndrome
options.C: Goldenhar syndrome
options.D: Apert syndrome

exp: Maxillonasal dysplasia — midface hypoplasia with a short nose and acute nasolabial angle — is eponymously **Binder's syndrome**, and rib cartilage graft is a listed reconstructive treatment (alongside saddle-nose repair, nipple reconstruction, septorhinoplasty, and tracheal reconstruction).
hy: Maxillonasal dysplasia = Binder's syndrome.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Correct — Binder's syndrome is maxillonasal dysplasia.
why.B: Wrong — Treacher Collins is mandibulofacial dysostosis, a different entity.
why.C: Wrong — Goldenhar (oculo-auriculo-vertebral) is a distinct hemifacial spectrum.
why.D: Wrong — Apert is acrocephalosyndactyly, not maxillonasal dysplasia.

stem: Chin and colleagues' modified auricular frameworks reduce the amount of harvested costal cartilage. During ear elevation, what did they use as a support material to avoid harvesting extra cartilage?

options.A: Porous polyethylene
options.B: Bone cement
options.C: Titanium mesh
options.D: A dermal-fat graft

exp: Chin and colleagues' individualized frameworks (helix, base frame, Y-shaped antihelical complex, tragus + cartilage cube) use less costal cartilage, and they used **bone cement** as the support material at the ear-elevation stage — so no extra costal cartilage need be harvested.
hy: Chin framework: bone cement support at elevation → no extra costal harvest.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — porous polyethylene (Medpor) is an alternative *framework*, not what Chin used for elevation.
why.B: Correct — bone cement was the elevation-stage support material.
why.C: Wrong — titanium mesh is not the material described here.
why.D: Wrong — a dermal-fat graft is not the cited elevation support.

stem: The capacity of transplanted perichondrium to form neocartilage was first reported in 1959 by whom?

options.A: Engkvist
options.B: Nagata
options.C: Lester
options.D: Brent

exp: **Lester (1959)** first reported neocartilage formation from transplanted perichondrium; later observers confirmed the phenomenon, though clinical cartilage regeneration from perichondrium remains limited and unproven at scale.
hy: Lester 1959 = first perichondrial neocartilage report.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — Engkvist (1975) studied perichondrium for joint cartilage, after Lester.
why.B: Wrong — Nagata is a total-ear-reconstruction figure, unrelated to this 1959 report.
why.C: Correct — Lester's 1959 report is the first cited.
why.D: Wrong — Brent (with Ott) described the perichondro*cutaneous* graft in 1978, not the 1959 first report.

stem: Brent and Ott (1978) described the perichondrocutaneous graft for facial reconstruction. To prevent donor deformity, the donor source should be restricted to which site?

options.A: The nasal septum
options.B: The costal margin
options.C: The tragus
options.D: The concha

exp: Brent and Ott (1978) introduced the perichondrocutaneous graft for facial reconstruction, restricting the donor source to the **concha** to avoid producing a donor-ear deformity.
hy: Perichondrocutaneous graft (Brent & Ott 1978): donor = concha.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — the nasal septum is not the perichondrocutaneous donor site.
why.B: Wrong — the costal margin is not used for this composite perichondrium-skin graft.
why.C: Wrong — the tragus is not the specified donor.
why.D: Correct — the concha is the specified donor source.

stem: What is the chapter's overall verdict on perichondrial and perichondrocutaneous grafts as cartilage regenerators?

options.A: No solid evidence it regenerates significant cartilage
options.B: Randomized trials prove reliable full-thickness regeneration
options.C: They regenerate hyaline cartilage superior to costal grafts
options.D: They are contraindicated because they always ossify

exp: Despite decades of enthusiasm since Lester, the chapter concludes there is **no solid clinical evidence that transplanted perichondrium regenerates a significant amount of cartilage** — its reliable regenerative capacity remains unproven, which is why it stays a niche technique.
hy: Perichondrium: no solid evidence of significant cartilage regeneration.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Correct — the evidence for significant regeneration is not solid.
why.B: Wrong — no randomized proof of reliable full-thickness regeneration exists.
why.C: Wrong — perichondrium does not reliably outperform costal grafts.
why.D: Wrong — routine ossification is not the stated reason for its limited use.

stem: For articular-cartilage (joint) repair, which graft ultimately became the major tissue graft for restoring injured *digital* articular surfaces?

options.A: Periosteal free flaps
options.B: Costal osteochondral grafts
options.C: Perichondrial free grafts
options.D: Meniscal allograft transfers

exp: Because perichondrial and standalone grafts proved unreliable, **costal osteochondral grafts** became the major tissue graft for restoring injured *digital* articular cartilage — providing both a subchondral bony base and a hyaline cartilage cap.
hy: Digital articular repair → costal osteochondral graft.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — periosteal flaps are not the cited digital-joint solution.
why.B: Correct — costal osteochondral grafts became the mainstay for digital joints.
why.C: Wrong — perichondrial free grafts were the *less* reliable option they replaced.
why.D: Wrong — meniscal allografts are for the knee meniscus, not digital articular surfaces.

stem: The chapter defines the cartilage tissue-engineering process as the integration of which three major components?

options.A: Autograft, allograft, and one growth factor
options.B: Perichondrium, periosteum, and a bioreactor
options.C: Seed cells, a scaffold, and an environment
options.D: A vascular pedicle, a nerve, and a bony strut

exp: Cartilage tissue engineering integrates three components: **seed cells** (produce matrix), a **scaffold** (a 3D place for cells to reside/proliferate), and the **tissue-formation environment** (culture conditions under which matrix deposits and the scaffold degrades).
hy: TE triad = seed cells + scaffold + tissue-formation environment.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — autograft/allograft/growth-factor are graft categories, not this triad.
why.B: Wrong — perichondrium/periosteum/bioreactor are tools, not the defined three components.
why.C: Correct — seed cells, scaffold, and the tissue-formation environment are the three components.
why.D: Wrong — pedicle/nerve/bony strut describe a composite flap, not the engineering triad.
