stem: Per Stock and Vacanti's concept, what is the scaffold's role until the seeded cells produce adequate matrix?

options.A: It permanently remains as the load-bearing core
options.B: It delivers a systemic dose of chondrogenic drug
options.C: It serves only as an antibacterial barrier
options.D: It gives temporary architecture, then degrades

exp: Per Stock and Vacanti, the scaffold supplies the **architecture and the initial biomechanical profile** on which seeded cells organize, and it is then **degraded/metabolized** as the cells lay down adequate extracellular matrix — leaving a living tissue that restores function.
hy: Scaffold = temporary architecture + mechanics, then degrades as ECM forms.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — the ideal scaffold degrades rather than permanently remaining.
why.B: Wrong — a scaffold is a structural template, not a systemic drug depot.
why.C: Wrong — it is not merely an antibacterial barrier.
why.D: Correct — it provides temporary architecture/mechanics and then degrades.

stem: In the landmark human-ear-shaped cartilage study, which cell source, scaffold, and host model were combined?

options.A: Calf chondrocytes on PGA, in a nude mouse
options.B: Human stem cells on collagen, in a rabbit
options.C: Swine tenocytes on fibrin gel, in a rat
options.D: Bovine osteoblasts on titanium mesh, in a pig

exp: The landmark Cao/Vacanti "mouse ear" combined **calf (bovine) chondrocytes seeded on a PGA (polyglycolic acid) scaffold** shaped like a human ear, implanted subcutaneously in a **nude mouse**, producing an ear-shaped cartilage after 12 weeks — the proof-of-concept that cartilage engineering could serve plastic surgery.
hy: Cao/Vacanti ear = calf chondrocytes + PGA + nude mouse.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Correct — calf chondrocytes on PGA in a nude mouse.
why.B: Wrong — human stem cells on collagen in a rabbit is not the described study.
why.C: Wrong — the flexible-ear (Xu) study used swine *chondrocytes* on fibrin, not tenocytes; tenocytes appear in the engineered-phalanx work (Isogai), not this landmark ear study.
why.D: Wrong — bovine osteoblasts on titanium describes bone, not this cartilage study.

stem: In the ear-cartilage engineering studies, what device was used to preserve the fine three-dimensional contour of the construct during maturation?

options.A: Rigid intermaxillary fixation wires
options.B: An external stent over the implant
options.C: A continuous negative-pressure dressing
options.D: An internal rigid titanium endoskeleton

exp: Because engineered ear constructs tend to distort during maturation, an **external stent fixed over the implant** was used to hold the fine 3D contour; constructs with stent support kept a crisp ear shape while unsupported ones lost definition.
hy: External stent maintains engineered-ear 3D contour.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — intermaxillary fixation is a jaw technique, irrelevant here.
why.B: Correct — an external stent preserved the contour.
why.C: Wrong — negative-pressure dressing was not the shape-maintenance method.
why.D: Wrong — a rigid titanium endoskeleton was not used in these constructs.

stem: Using CAD/CAM to fabricate an ear-shaped scaffold from a CT scan, what shape fidelity to the original ear mold was achieved for the scaffold?

options.A: A similarity level of about 25%
options.B: A similarity level under 10%
options.C: A similarity level above 97%
options.D: A similarity level near 50%

exp: Liu and colleagues scanned a patient's ear by CT, built positive/negative molds by CAD/CAM, and fabricated a PGA/PLA scaffold that matched the original ear mold with a **similarity above 97%** — showing CAD/CAM can produce an accurately mirror-symmetric ear scaffold.
hy: CAD/CAM ear scaffold similarity >97%.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — ~25% is not the reported fidelity.
why.B: Wrong — <10% is the opposite of the high fidelity achieved.
why.C: Correct — the scaffold reached >97% similarity to the mold.
why.D: Wrong — ~50% would be far too low for accurate mirror symmetry.

stem: Xu and colleagues conferred native-like flexibility (tolerating torsion and bending without fracture) on an engineered ear by doing what?

options.A: Cross-linking the scaffold with genipin first
options.B: Seeding the construct with pluripotent stem cells
options.C: Culturing at supraphysiologic oxygen tension
options.D: Laminating it with lyophilized perichondrium

exp: Xu and colleagues **laminated the chondrocyte–fibrin construct between two layers of lyophilized perichondrium** to create a tri-layer ear that tolerated torsion and bending without fracturing (mimicking native elastic ear cartilage), whereas the non-laminated control fractured.
hy: Perichondrium lamination → flexible engineered ear.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — genipin cross-linking is a stiffening strategy, not what conferred flexibility here.
why.B: Wrong — iPS-cell seeding was not the method used.
why.C: Wrong — supraphysiologic oxygen was not the flexibility mechanism.
why.D: Correct — lyophilized-perichondrium lamination conferred the flexibility.

stem: Kamil and colleagues compared microtia-derived chondrocytes with normal chondrocytes. What did they conclude about using microtia cells for ear engineering?

options.A: Similar proliferation and cartilage formation
options.B: They failed to proliferate and were unusable
options.C: They formed only fibrous tissue, never cartilage
options.D: They required gene correction before any use

exp: Kamil and colleagues found microtia-derived chondrocytes had **proliferation and cartilage-forming ability similar to normal chondrocytes**, supporting the use of the patient's own microtia cartilage remnant as a cell source for engineering an auricular framework.
hy: Microtia chondrocytes ≈ normal → usable cell source.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Correct — similar proliferation/formation, so microtia cells are viable.
why.B: Wrong — they did proliferate, not fail.
why.C: Wrong — they formed cartilage, not merely fibrous tissue.
why.D: Wrong — no gene correction was required in the study.

stem: Yanaga and colleagues injected cultured autologous auricular chondrocytes for nasal augmentation. What was the key outcome over follow-up?

options.A: The grafts needed silicone reinforcement to persist
options.B: The cartilage kept its shape without resorption
options.C: The cartilage fully resorbed within three months
options.D: The injected cells ossified into bone tissue

exp: Yanaga injected cultured autologous auricular chondrocytes (as a gel-like cell mass) for nasal augmentation and facial contouring; over 6 months to 2 years the **injected cartilage kept its shape without resorption** across the reported series.
hy: Injected autologous chondrocytes → shape maintained, no resorption.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — silicone reinforcement was not required.
why.B: Correct — the injected cartilage maintained shape without resorption.
why.C: Wrong — it did not resorb within 3 months.
why.D: Wrong — the cells formed cartilage, not bone.

stem: Which materials were used as early injectable scaffolds to explore the feasibility of injectable engineered cartilage?

options.A: Decellularized dermis and silicone
options.B: Titanium microbeads and bone cement
options.C: Calcium alginate gels and Pluronic gel
options.D: Polymethylmethacrylate and hydroxyapatite

exp: Early injectable-cartilage feasibility was explored with slowly polymerizing **calcium alginate gels** and **Pluronic gel**, which suspend chondrocytes in an injectable matrix that sets in situ.
hy: Early injectable scaffolds = calcium alginate + Pluronic gel.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — decellularized dermis and silicone are not the cited injectable scaffolds.
why.B: Wrong — titanium microbeads and bone cement are not injectable cartilage matrices.
why.C: Correct — calcium alginate and Pluronic gels were the early injectable scaffolds.
why.D: Wrong — PMMA/hydroxyapatite are rigid bone-type materials, not injectable chondrocyte carriers here.

stem: Farhadi and colleagues engineered human nasal cartilage by seeding septal chondrocytes onto which scaffold?

options.A: A decellularized tracheal cartilage allograft
options.B: A calcium-phosphate bioceramic scaffold block
options.C: An acellular porcine dermal matrix sheet
options.D: Esterified-hyaluronan (Hyaff-11) mesh

exp: Farhadi seeded nasal-septum chondrocytes onto **nonwoven meshes of esterified hyaluronan (Hyaff-11)**; the constructs formed well-structured cartilage with good mechanical properties after in-vivo implantation, offering a potential graft for septal/alar reconstruction.
hy: Farhadi nasal cartilage = septal chondrocytes on Hyaff-11 (esterified HA).
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — a decellularized tracheal allograft was not used.
why.B: Wrong — a calcium-phosphate ceramic is a bone scaffold, not this one.
why.C: Wrong — acellular porcine dermis was not the scaffold here.
why.D: Correct — esterified hyaluronan (Hyaff-11) mesh was the scaffold.

stem: Weng and colleagues engineered a mandibular condyle by molding a PLA-coated PGA scaffold and seeding it with which two cell types?

options.A: Osteoblasts and chondrocytes
options.B: Fibroblasts and keratinocytes
options.C: Tenocytes and myoblasts
options.D: Endothelial cells and pericytes

exp: Weng molded a PLA-coated PGA scaffold into a mandibular-condyle shape and seeded it with **osteoblasts and chondrocytes**, producing a condyle-shaped construct with trabecular bone capped by hyaline articular cartilage after 12 weeks — a proof of concept for engineered TMJ reconstruction.
hy: Weng TMJ condyle = osteoblasts + chondrocytes on PLA/PGA.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Correct — osteoblasts plus chondrocytes built the osteochondral condyle.
why.B: Wrong — fibroblasts/keratinocytes build skin, not an osteochondral condyle.
why.C: Wrong — tenocytes/myoblasts were not the condyle cell pair.
why.D: Wrong — endothelial cells/pericytes build vasculature, not the condyle surface.

stem: When native chondrocytes are limited, which adult stem-cell sources are named as the most feasible for cartilage engineering?

options.A: Amniotic and umbilical-cord epithelial cells
options.B: Bone-marrow (BMSC) and adipose (ASC) MSCs
options.C: Embryonic and induced pluripotent stem cells
options.D: Hematopoietic and neural-crest stem cells

exp: When native chondrocytes are scarce, adult **mesenchymal stem cells from bone marrow (BMSC) and adipose tissue (ASC)** are named the most feasible sources — they proliferate strongly and can be driven down the chondrogenic lineage by growth factors, co-culture with chondrocytes, or chondrogenic matrices.
hy: Cartilage stem-cell sources = BMSC + ASC.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — amniotic/cord epithelial cells are not the cited feasible sources.
why.B: Correct — bone-marrow and adipose MSCs are the cited feasible sources.
why.C: Wrong — embryonic/iPS cells carry ethical/tumorigenic constraints and are not the "most feasible" here.
why.D: Wrong — hematopoietic/neural-crest stem cells are not the named chondrogenic sources.

section: In vitro vs in vivo cartilage
stem: Compared with in-vivo-engineered cartilage, in-vitro-engineered cartilage is described as having what shortcoming, linked to which matrix molecules?

options.A: Superior strength but poorer shape retention
options.B: Faster resorption from excess metalloproteinase
options.C: Weaker strength, low collagen IX/pyridinoline
options.D: Excess calcification from high alkaline phosphatase

exp: In-vitro-engineered cartilage is **mechanically weaker** than in-vivo-engineered cartilage, because the in-vivo microenvironment better promotes maturation and expression of key matrix molecules such as **collagen IX and pyridinoline** (cross-links), which strengthen the tissue.
hy: In vitro cartilage weaker → less collagen IX/pyridinoline.
src: Neligan I.19 · In vitro vs in vivo
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — in vitro was weaker, not stronger.
why.B: Wrong — the issue was maturation/cross-linking, not MMP-driven resorption.
why.C: Correct — weaker mechanics tied to lower collagen IX and pyridinoline.
why.D: Wrong — excess calcification/alkaline phosphatase is not the cited in-vitro problem.

stem: Engineering articular cartilage in a bioreactor under continuous dynamic loading was shown to do what?

options.A: Decrease matrix synthesis and soften it
options.B: Trigger ossification of the neocartilage
options.C: No measurable effect on its properties
options.D: Raise its stiffness and matrix output

exp: Culturing engineered articular cartilage in a bioreactor under **continuous dynamic loading raised its Young's modulus and increased cartilage oligomeric matrix protein (COMP) and collagens II and IX** — a mechanical-conditioning strategy to strengthen constructs.
hy: Bioreactor dynamic loading ↑ Young's modulus, COMP, collagen II/IX.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Wrong — loading increased, not decreased, matrix synthesis.
why.B: Wrong — it strengthened cartilage rather than triggering ossification.
why.C: Wrong — the effect was significant, not absent.
why.D: Correct — dynamic loading increased stiffness and matrix (COMP, collagen II/IX).

stem: In the first clinical application of in-vitro-engineered ear cartilage for microtia (Zhou et al., illustrated in Algorithm 19.1), which combination was used to fabricate and then cover the engineered framework?

options.A: Microtia chondrocytes on a compound scaffold, then expanded skin + fascia flap cover
options.B: Donor cadaveric chondrocytes on a permanent silicone core, covered with a free radial-forearm flap
options.C: Bone-marrow stem cells on a titanium frame, covered by primary closure alone
options.D: Autologous costal cartilage carved conventionally, with no engineered component

exp: In the first clinical application (Zhou et al.), a **human ear-shaped cartilage was engineered from the patient's own microtia chondrocytes on a compound biodegradable scaffold** (a 3D-printed PCL core sandwiched by PGA and coated with PLA); the matured framework was then covered by an **expanded skin flap, a post-auricular neurovascular fascia flap, and a skin graft**, giving a detailed auricle stable at 30-month follow-up.
hy: Zhou 2018: microtia chondrocytes + PCL/PGA/PLA scaffold, covered by expanded skin + fascia flap + skin graft.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage

why.A: Correct — patient microtia chondrocytes on a compound biodegradable scaffold, covered by expanded skin + neurovascular fascia flap + skin graft.
why.B: Wrong — donor cadaveric cells on a permanent silicone core with a forearm flap is not the described method.
why.C: Wrong — bone-marrow cells on a titanium frame closed primarily is not what was used.
why.D: Wrong — conventional carved costal cartilage has no engineered component and is not this trial.

expimgcap: Algorithm 19.1 — auricular reconstruction from in-vitro tissue-engineered ear cartilage

stem: This schematic shows harvest of nasal septal cartilage. The striped region that must be preserved (rather than harvested) represents what?

options.A: The vomerine bony septum below
options.B: The L-strut that maintains nasal support
options.C: The freely harvestable posterior cartilage
options.D: The ethmoid perpendicular plate above

exp: The striped area is the **L-shaped septal strut** (a caudal + dorsal band of quadrangular cartilage) that must be preserved to maintain nasal support; the nonsupportive quadrangular cartilage posterior to the dashed line can be harvested freely.
hy: Striped region = L-strut (preserve for support).
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage
imgcap: Which region is preserved?

why.A: Wrong — the vomer is bony septum, not the striped quadrangular-cartilage strut shown.
why.B: Correct — the striped L-strut is what must be kept for nasal support.
why.C: Wrong — the freely harvestable cartilage is the region *posterior* to the strut, not the striped strut itself.
why.D: Wrong — the perpendicular plate of the ethmoid is a separate bony structure, not the striped cartilage.

stem: This gross specimen from a landmark tissue-engineering study shows a construct implanted subcutaneously in a nude mouse. What is it?

options.A: A decellularized tracheal cartilage scaffold
options.B: A perichondrial neocartilage nodule
options.C: A tissue-engineered ear-shaped cartilage
options.D: An autologous costal cartilage framework

exp: This is the **tissue-engineered human-ear-shaped cartilage** grown by seeding chondrocytes on a PGA ear-shaped scaffold and implanting it subcutaneously in a nude mouse (Cao/Vacanti) — the iconic proof that an anatomically shaped cartilage could be engineered.
hy: Ear-shape cartilage on a nude mouse = engineered auricular cartilage.
chname: Repair, grafting, and engineering of cartilage
imgcap: What is this construct?

why.A: Wrong — a decellularized tracheal scaffold is tubular tracheal cartilage, not an ear shape.
why.B: Wrong — a perichondrial neocartilage nodule is a small amorphous nodule, not this detailed ear form.
why.C: Correct — the ear-shaped construct on the mouse is tissue-engineered cartilage.
why.D: Wrong — a carved autologous costal framework would be harvested from a human rib, not grown on a mouse.
